column121: First day back after being off for two weeks for COVID-19 quarantine; we don't even have half of our normal staffing which is already low due to COVID.  Total number of CPC's for the day is only 6 when normally it would be at least XX.I get put on the LC1 position with no assist or LC2; I was told that there was a CFR [Call For Release] in effect for ZZZ.  It ended being super busy and it seemed like there was no flow put in place; we were pretty much operating business as usual.  I did not receive much spacing on final and I even had to send an aircraft around because of an overtake and lack of runway separation.  Approach also sent in an uncoordinated early turn IFR jet from the north east; I had to adjust his flight path to de conflict him from the [aircraft in the] traffic pattern and from a departure I had rolling on [Runway] XXR.  This created another conflict with another arrival they had doing 180 kts that they never slowed down.It was pretty overwhelming to work that volume/complexity of traffic after two weeks off and not being able to ease back into the work flow; especially when we have limited staffing/positions open and with TRACON not following the LOA's and traffic flow directives.We are going to be operating in this limited capacity working 6 days a week 10 hours a day for the next week.  There needs to be better communication and an understanding of the unfortunate circumstances that we will be working under for the next week.  It is completely unsafe to operate business as usual and I'm hoping there will be better coordination going forward.
column125: Tower Controller reported an airborne conflict resulting in an aircraft having to execute a go-around. Controller cited staffing and traffic volume as a contributing factors.

column121: On route to the airport the First Officer's Multi Function display failed. We decided to continue destination airport because it was not required equipment. Then we were informed the weather at the airport was deteriorating unexpectedly. We completed all the required tasks and checklist before the we were cleared for the approach. On approach we realized that we were too high and our descent profile was going to be unacceptable for a stable approach. Around 11;000 ft. the Captain called for the Missed Approach. We executed the Missed Approach with no issues and cleaned up the aircraft and started climbing to the missed approach altitude. We were at the missed approach altitude and Captain called for the auto pilot on. During this time there was a bit of confusion and the right turn and swapping the navigation displays and setting the new course for the back course was slow to be executed. We turned to the heading too slow and never intercepted the back course. Once we figured out that we didn't intercept the back course we had the Course Deviation Indicator full-scale deflection. ATC gave us a new altitude and a new heading to start the restart the approach. We got vectored around to the Initial Approach Fix to restart the approach and completed the second approach stabilized without any issues. Failure to comply with the approach profile and the missed approach profile.
column123: I neglected to call for the First Officer to start our descent. The First Officer noticed my mistake; but by this time we were very high. My initial reaction was to deploy the boards and steepen the descent in an attempt to get back on profile; however I quickly realized it wasn't going to work and we were unstabilized. I called for a missed approach when we were at roughly 11;000 ft.  Because we went missed early; I called for the autopilot on once we reached the published missed approach altitude of 14;200 ft. This caused confusion as to who should complete the tasks of spinning the heading knob to the 300 heading; swapping the RTU's; and setting the course to 303. I decided to have the First Officer complete these to stay as on-profile as possible. The first two tasks; the 300 heading and swapping the RTU's; were completed however the task of setting the 303 course was not. This led to ATC querying us about our progress on the missed approach and soon after issuing an altitude alert with an instruction to climb to 15;000 ft. By the time we figured out our mistake we were showing full-scale deflection on the Course Deviation Indicator. In addition to not setting the course; I made the mistake of accelerating to 200 kts. and calling climb sequence once level at 14;200 ft. instead of waiting until outbound per the profile. I should also add that the First Officer MFD failed enroute and this most likely caused a reduction in situational awareness throughout the rest of the flight. Failure to comply with the LOC/DME 15 approach profile; especially the missed approach segment.

column121: On Date; I planned a flight to land at 23M Clarke County (a non towered field); then on to ZZZ. I obtained a full flight briefing through Foreflight prior to departure and confirmed that the only airport NOTAM for 23M was that the PAPI for runway 34 was unusable. I departed and had an uneventful flight en route to 23M. About 6 miles from 23M; I announced my position and intention to land on runway 16 on the CTAF. I made position reports again on turning left downwind; left base; and final. Abeam the numbers on downwind leg; I reduced throttle to normal MAP for landing. As I turned from base to final; I went to reduce throttle to idle; but the throttle stuck at about 12 inches MAP. As I approached the runway; I tried very diligently to get the throttle unstuck by turning the throttle tension knob and firmly pulling on the throttle. I was able to get the throttle to pull back to idle just before crossing the runway threshold and proceeded to land on runway 16 at 23M. Once on the ground; a voice came over the radio that said 'You can't land here; the airport is closed; didn't you see the yellow X on the runway?' I had not seen any X's on the runway on approach. At this point I reached the entry to the taxiway and saw that it was blocked off. I responded that I was having a semi-emergency and was troubleshooting a stuck throttle. They asked if there was anything they could do to help. I replied no and that I think I had the problem resolved and would immediately back taxi and take back off. I told them I checked NOTAMs before I departed and that I hadn't seen any NOTAMS about a closure; to which they replied 'Well I don't know how that happened.' He stated that they had no fuel and something about that being why the airport was closed; which did not completely make sense to me at the time. I cycled the throttle several times to ensure it was not sticking and the engine responded properly. There were no obstructions on the runway that would prevent takeoff and there were no workers visible around the runway. As I taxied back to the takeoff end of runway 16; I was thinking about how I did not see any X's on the runway and how there was no NOTAM for the airport being closed when I did my briefing just an hour or so prior. I felt like a real bonehead for missing this. When I got to the end of the runway; I saw that there was in fact NO X ON THE PAVED RUNWAY INDICATING IT WAS CLOSED. There was a yellow X laying on the grass leading up to the runway threshold; but there were no markings on the runway itself. I departed without issue and returned to my home airport. I checked the NOTAMs for the airport again and confirmed that there were no NOTAMs for 23M being closed on this date. Three issues stand out to me in this situation. First; if a runway is to be closed; the yellow X needs to be ON the runway; not in the grass leading up to the runway. All examples of runway closure markings I have ever seen have been ON the runway surface. Second; if an airport or runway is to be temporarily closed; there should be a NOTAM for that closure. Third; while there was an X on the grass leading up to the runway; I did not see it on final approach due to being preoccupied troubleshooting the stuck throttle. In the future; I will do a more thorough job of looking AROUND the runway for yellow X's while on downwind leg and on final approach. I am not sure if an unauthorized individual may have placed the X's in the grass and the airport wasn't really officially closed. The person on the CTAF did not identify themselves so I do not know if they were airport officials or someone else. The lack of a NOTAM and improper placement of the X's leads me to think this may not have been an official airport closure. Nevertheless; I will do a better job of scanning the area around the runway from now on for any indication of problems.
column125: Single Private Pilot reported landing at a closed airport; but there was no Notam or 'X' on runway indicating 'closed' but preceding the runway in the grass was the X.

column121: Aircraft X departed JNU for ZZZ; but changed destination shortly after to return to JNU because the pilot reported that ZZZ winds would not allow for an approach. Aircraft X initially climbed to 090; but requested descent to 080 for moderate icing. I cleared Aircraft X back to the JNU airport via direct SSR direct; to expect the LDA Z 8 approach; as requested.I cleared Aircraft X for the LDA Z 8 approach; to cross SSR at or above 060. Shortly after progressing SSR; the aircraft transmitted that they were back in the icing and had 'quite a bit of ice on the wing' and said they were unsure if they would be able to maintain the transition altitude of 056 until LYNNS intersection. The aircraft then descended to 052; below the approach altitude. I issued a low altitude alert; and instructed Aircraft X to climb to meet the transition altitude. Aircraft X reported they would attempt to climb; but were unable and appeared to level off at 050 in a 060 MIA (Minimum IFR Altitude); and requested to transition over to the RNAV W 8 approach via JIGMI; as the approach had a lower transition altitude. Seeing as the aircraft had already progress JIGMI; I asked if they would be able to establish themselves on to a published portion of the approach if I provided a heading to join. the pilot said affirmative; so I issued a 10 degree turn to the right to join the RNAV W 8 approach; and to steer the aircraft away form higher terrain to the NW; as it was clear they would be unable to climb. I then informed the pilot that I was providing priority handling for them. Aircraft X was able to maintain 050 until joining the RNAV W 8 approach; at which time I issued the approach clearance and confirmed that the aircraft would be able to maintain the new transition altitudes of that approach. The pilot confirmed that they would; and was able to safely fly the approach to landing.

column121: We were on a visual approach to Runway XXL at ZZZ at 3;000 feet. Approach cleared us for a visual approach about 7 miles out and we were giving a heading towards the FAF following an airbus. PF set 2;000 feet in the MCP and we began a slow descent heading inbound to the airport. ATC issued a 'low altitude alert' at about 2;300 feet. We leveled off and intercepted the G/S just past the FAF. In addition; we had to rapidly slow since the airbus in front slowed rapidly and ATC wanted us to slow to approximately 150 knots or less. They really did not leave us much lateral room. We slowed and configured while descending. We were never near an obstacle; nor were we a threat to another aircraft. We were still above the 1;000 feet stable approach criteria (1;300 to 1;500 feet) and met the criteria proof to reaching 1;000 feet. We were lower than what was normally flow for that area but still seamed safe. Again on a visual approach. PF stated that he read 2;000 feet at the FAF and misread the altitude. The FAF altitude is 2;800 feet. I did not reverify it since we were visual and I was working on setting up radios and configuration for landing. I assumed he saw it correctly.I can't find where we violated anything. We were a little low for the area but still in a safe position to land and had visual clearance from all obstacles. I know next time to verify the altitude in the MCP from the chart myself. That is that only thing that may have stopped the lower alt in the first place.
column123: On receiving landing clearance; I set 2;000 feet in the altitude window and planned to descend to that altitude at ZZZZZ; the FAF.  I misread the approach plate. The correct altitude at ZZZZZ is 2;800 feet. The Tower gave us a Low Altitude Alert at 2;200 MSL in VMC and we maintained that altitude (we were nearly at glideslope intercept) until glideslope intercept.  We landed uneventfully.

column121: On the transition to an ILS Approach. Once we were handed over to Approach we were given different speeds along the approach which is typical. It was IMC with a reported ceiling of 700 ft. We were given 160 kts. and handed over to Tower frequency. The aircraft we were following at the time was 5 miles ahead of us. Approximately 3 miles outside of the final approach fix I saw on the TCAS an aircraft turning a right base and appeared to be sequencing in front of us. Tower shortly after instructed us to slow to approach speed which was 135 kts. We were then told we were following Aircraft Y. The Aircraft Y was approximately 2 miles ahead of us. I told the First Officer to plan on a go-around. I had fully briefed the approach prior to Top of Descent. Tower then asked the Aircraft Y to do a midfield landing due to separation. Around 1;400 ft. we were told to go-around. It was a normal go-around profile and we were vectored back for a normal landing.
column123: Crew was on approach. Initial ATIS reported ceilings 400 feet and 1/2 mile visibility. Crew elected to transfer controls to the Captain and execute an auto-land approach for currency and to increase safety margin. Approach was commencing normally with positive airspeed control by ATC. Approximately 4 miles from Final Approach Fix ATC vectored an aircraft onto final ahead of us. Aircraft rolled out approximately 2 miles ahead on final as indicated by TCAS. We were handed off to Tower and given clearance to land number two. Preceding aircraft was instructed to expedite turnoff after landing due to close interval. At approximately 800 feet. and at the top of the IMC layer; Tower canceled landing clearance and directed a climb to 2;000 feet and track localizer inbound. Crew conducted a normal go-around. Aircraft was vectored to a second approach and a normal landing ensued.

column121: Cleared for ILS XXR approach into ZZZ outside of ZZZZZ maintain 3;000 until established.  VMC but too hazy to see runway at 12 miles final.  Autopilot was engaged and LOC was armed but the aircraft did not intercept and we flew thru LOC course. I turned off autopilot and autothrottles to attempt re-intercept of localizer however flight directors were giving far right correction with LOC indices correcting slightly left.  We were far right of course; so FD guidance was completely inaccurate and LOC indices should have been making far correction to left which they were not.  We attempted to determine source of the problem but were unsuccessful doing so. We cycled the ILS radio panel; but no help.  We asked for Go-around and were given instructions by ATC.  On second approach attempt LOC and GS captured and an uneventful landing was made.

column121: While approximately 2 hours from our destination; we discovered that ZZZZ1 weather would not allow us to land at destination. We made a decision with fuel and time to hold and then subsequently divert to ZZZZ2. The communication barrier was difficult with the controllers and while that took place we were also using the radio for Dispatch. I was the PM (Pilot Monitoring) and was coordinating the divert with Dispatch and the handling in ZZZZ2. Task saturation after a long flight was certainly a challenge. I also was having freezing issues with Jepp FD pro. After being handed to ATC; we had the approach and plan in place for the approach Runway XX. On the decent the controllers left us very high even after multiple requests for lower. The PF (Pilot Flying) was using FLCH and max speed brakes most of the time. We were able to get to an appropriate altitude safely. I had a brief moment of thought about still being in FLCH on decent and through saturation; I failed to mention it. Inside the IAF I set the TDZE as normal and confirmed with crew; as we were cleared the approach. We had still been descending constantly from events before; we never really stopped descending since just after the divert was made. As we were in the arc; we were completely configured for landing and I noticed that we had descended below the published altitude on the ARC. I quickly realized we had been still in FLCH and immediately said to stop decent. We were approximately 400-500 ft. Low. And after arresting settled at about 600 below. The PF and crew were in VMC conditions. CAVOK. We were approximately 1/2 to 3/4 of the way through the arc once I noticed. We had the airport in sight and the PF elected to stay at the approximate pattern altitude as we were over water in VMC and had the airport in sight and about to turn final. The approach continued visually and the PF made a normal landing and taxi to hard stand.

column121: In cruise I messaged Dispatch and asked the RVR for [Runway] XXR.  They told me 1400 for [Runway] XXR.  About 30 minutes from arrival we started our brief. We briefed the arrival and the approach plate for [Runway]. We then briefed the CAT2 checklist. We both put 705 feet in our DH and verified it. We did the decent check and verified it again. Did the approach check and was getting vectors to [Runway] XXR. Gear down flaps 45 before the marker.  I called 1;000 feet cleared to land when I knew something was wrong. I couldn't grasp why we only had 300 feet left to go missed. Told the First Officer something wasn't right. We hit minimums at 705 feet AGL and I called the missed. Did the missed approach; cleaned up aircraft and did the checklist. Didn't take me long to figure out what I did. The RA for [Runway] XXR is 116 feet. We put the new DH in and took our time to get set up for approach.  We got vectors on the LOC and shot the approach to a landing.We both missed the right number for the DH. I knew the approach would be to minimums so I was looking at chart in cruise so I wouldn't miss anything.  Ended up on a go around at 700 feet AGL because we had the MDA in. It was first flight of day we both felt good. Just the low visibility at destination caused a threat.Even after the lengthy briefing and checklist usage the DH was still put in there wrong. What I have learned over my career is not to get upset at the moment you realize you did something wrong.  Great training over the years and good CRM has trained me to fly the airplane. Run the appropriate checklists and then discuss what happened at a safe time later.
column123: We incorrectly set the 705 feet in the DH for a CAT 2 approach and logically got the mins aural alert more than 500 feet early. We executed a go-around; set up the DH properly and flew the second approach normally.
column125: CRJ--900 flight crew reported setting the wrong DH for the approach and executing a missed earlier than required.

column121: A little while after reaching cruise altitude; the flight attendants contacted us to let us know that there was a passenger who was not complying with the mask policy.  They were handling it per their guidance and keeping us informed.  Later in the flight there had been some other problems that they had informed us about. The First Officer and I had completed a bathroom rotation just before starting down into ZZZ when we got a call from the flight attendants informing us they were [having problems with this passenger]. They just wanted to be sure we knew this. I asked them to let us know if there were any problems.  Several minutes later; we received a call that this passenger had not taken kindly to the warning and had become 'verbally combative.'  I thanked them for letting us know; and asked if everyone was okay.  They replied yes; they were.  I asked them to keep us informed if there were any changes of if anything escalated; they said they would.  At this point we had begun the descent on the arrival into ZZZ; the First Officer had been flying the whole leg.  He had been managing the radios as well while I was talking to the flight attendants.  I considered that while the situation currently was Level 1; I felt it was most prudent to get the aircraft on the ground to protect my crew and the other passengers.  Additionally; felt it would reduce my workload as well as my First Officer's if we requested priority handling.  I told the First Officer what I was considering and why; and asked if he had any considerations.  He agreed that was a good choice.  I then requested priority handling with ZZZ approach and informed them of SOB and fuel remaining.  I had sent an ACARS message to Dispatch informing them of our situation.  I called ZZZ ops and informed them of the same.  I then checked in with the flight attendants to ensure things were okay; which they were.  I informed them we were accepting priority handling and would be on the ground as soon as possible.  As we passed through 10;000 feet we kept the speed up around 315 knots to expedite our arrival.  We were given radar vectors to a visual Runway X and landed uneventfully.  We had to wait a few minutes for our gate as it was occupied.  We pulled into the gate.  The passenger disembarked and the situation was over.  There were no injuries.  After the plane was empty the crew got together in the front of the airplane for a debrief of the situation.  At the end of the debrief I asked the flight attendants if they were okay and would be comfortable with continuing.  They all felt that they were okay to continue.  When the First Officer and I debriefed; I asked him the same and he was good.  I took a moment to assess myself and determined I was good to continue as well.It is worth noting that the flight attendants did a very good job of keeping us informed as to the status of the situation.  This was helpful and good crew CRM.  They were clear in their communication; calm and professional.  My First Officer also was a model of CRM and performed exceptionally to include his aircraft handling to expedite our arrival.
column123: Passenger wasn't complying with mask usage after being repeatedly requested to by flight attendants. Flight attendants issued warning after which passenger became verbally aggressive.
column125: Air carrier flight crew reported a passenger misconduct incident during flight that initiated due to non-compliance with face mask policy.

column121: On approach to ZZZ; Initially the weather report was 3/4 SM visibility; so I informed the controller that I needed 1SM to shoot the approach due to my high minimums status. The Controller gave me direct ZZZ VOR and Hold northeast as published at 6;000 feet. I began heading direct to the VOR; when the controller came back and gave me the updated visibility at 1SM. So I accepted vectors to the approach. As I neared the FAF; I knew that I would need to be able to see the approach lights and runway in a low visibility situation; so I attempted to move the sun visor back to its stowed position. The visor became stuck; which is normal for these airplanes so I pushed harder. As I did that; the visor suddenly released itself and hit me in the face; causing a dull pain and immediate swelling. I touched the area and came back with a lot of blood on my hand; so I concentrated on flying the approach using standard procedures and call-outs and getting back on the ground. Unfortunately; I reached mins and did not see the runway environment; so I called out 'minimums; missed approach' and began the missed approach procedure according to standard procedures and call-outs. The controller attempted to give me a hold over the ZZZ VOR; but as I was bleeding and didn't know how long I would need to hold I chose to divert back to ZZZ1. I confirmed this decision after reaching cruise and having center call company Dispatch. Center informed me that I was a [priority] aircraft and would be treated as such as I had initially requested paramedics upon arrival. Upon reaching cruise however; I used my phone (which was off) as a mirror and concluded that I did not need medical assistance. So I informed Center of this change. I completed the flight back to ZZZ1 with no further issues. Paramedics met me at the ramp anyway; along with Airport OPS. They did not request my information and sent me on my way after cleaning the blood off of my face and neck. I then called dispatch and the Chief Pilots office to inform them of the situation.When the sun visor hit me in the face and I found blood on my hand. The sun visor in this airplane is in poor condition and gets stuck on the rail constantly. This in addition to the fact that I was very task saturated and didn't realize that the bleeding wasn't as bad as I had initially thought. I made the division to divert as I was performing a missed approach and wasn't aware of how severe the injury was. I knew the other airports in the vicinity were not suitable so I decided to go back to ZZZ1 as I knew it was 800-1000 foot OVC and I would have no issues. If I had moved the sun visor earlier that would have been beneficial; aside from that it could definitely use some TLC.

column118: Flight Crew Executed Go Around / Missed Approach
column121: While executing the ILS to [Runway] XXR at ZZZ; ATC informed us that we were following a heavy and instructed us to slow to 170kts. The Captain and I saw the other aircraft on TCAS and judged the distance to be six or seven miles. We discussed the possibility of wake turbulence but we didn't think the spacing was an issue. Then; after switching the tower; at around 3;000 feet; we were instructed to start slowing to our final approach speed. We then configured the aircraft for landing and started running the landing checklist. While listening and responding to the Captain's checklist callouts; I thought I heard someone say 'approach clearance canceled; maintain 3;000'; but I was unsure of the call sign. This was partly because the Captain and I were in the middle of running a checklist and partly because there was another aircraft on frequency that had a very similar call sign. We were Company X Callsign ABBC and the aircraft ahead of the heavy was Company X Callsign ADDC (there were at least three instances during the arrival where an instruction was given and we weren't 100% sure who it was for). I interrupted the Captain and told him that I thought tower had just canceled someone's approach clearance and asked him to query ATC to see if it was for us. The controller responded with a very urgent tone and said 'That is affirm; Callsign ABBC your approach clearance is canceled; maintain present heading and climb to 3;000. I'll have a turn for you in just a second.' As the captain was responding to ATC; I pressed the heading button to maintain our present heading as instructed; but I was confused as to what to do next. At this point we were around 2;400 feet (about 2;000 feet AFE); so a hard go-around using the TO/GA button was not required. I was not really mentally prepared for the go-around and was having trouble deciding what to do next; but ATC sounded like they wanted me to start climbing immediately to 3;000 feet; which was already set in the altitude window; so I reacted to his instruction by hitting FLCH. This caused the aircraft to apply full throttle and pitch up to maintain our current approach speed of about 145kts. We maintain this climb for several seconds until I was able to dial the speed up and get the aircraft accelerating. With everything happening so fast; and not really being mentally prepared for a go-around; I called gear up while the flaps were still at 5 which caused the landing gear warning to sound. It was a very sloppy go-around but I don't think the aircraft was ever in an unsafe state.This was my first go-around in the actual aircraft. However; I have mentally rehearsed and practiced enough hard go-arounds in sim training that the procedure is pretty much automatic and I can execute it without much thinking required. However; I can only recall doing a soft go-around once during FTD training and; as I recall; in that scenario we were not yet configured for landing and the altitude assigned was below us; so it was a simple matter of stopping the descent and flying the assigned heading. We also discussed soft go-arounds vs hard go-arounds in training and I had recalled that using TO/GA was optional but not really preferred during a soft-go around. I think there needs to be more training on soft go-arounds and the SOP should give more guidance as to preferred methods in various scenarios. Currently; the SOP does not give much guidance on the subject other than reminding the pilot to retract flaps at the F-bug or XX knots above green dot. After landing; the Captain and I discussed what I could have done better. He suggested; and I agreed; that the best course of action would have been to press ALT to stop the descent and then start accelerating before starting our climb. However; after replaying it in my head all night; I came to the conclusion that; given the fact that ATC wanted me to climb; it would have been perfectly acceptable to switch to heading mode and then press TO/GA and execute my normalgo-around actions and callouts; even though we were well above 1;000 feet AFE. This would ensure the aircraft was maintaining a safe speed while we began to reconfigure the aircraft and would have eliminated a lot of confusion that I created while I pondered what technique I should use to get us to 3;000 feet while also cleaning up the aircraft. Perhaps this should be the preferred method to execute a soft-go around when fully configured for landing and a climb is required. But perhaps there's another aspect of it that I'm not considering. The technique of pressing ALT and accelerating aircraft prior to starting the climb also seems like it would work in most situations. That's why I think the SOP should give more guidance on the subject. If there were a preferred method spelled out in the SOP; then it would eliminate a lot of the confusion and both pilots would be on the same page instead of debating on a technique for a procedure that is rarely used when there isn't really time to stop and think about it.This experience has also taught me to be prepared for a go-around at all times; not just when below 1;000 feet on an approach. Although; the spacing behind the heavy looked good to us; we should have been more prepared for the possibility that ATC would call for a go-around unexpectedly.
column125: Air carrier First Officer reported having to do an unexpected go-around on final approach that may not have been correctly executed and cited confusing communications and unclear SOP procedures as contributing factors.

column121: At touchdown; aircraft started to pull right and right wing started to drop. I heard a noise and thought I had blown a tire. Next I flew back to centerline and in doing so I may have added slight power. I slapped the gear handle. I never heard a Gear Warning tone. When the Crew was asked; they said they didn't either.Settling down again; I kept the wings level and anticipated what I thought might be the inevitable right tendency.The aircraft however remained level; but I informed tower I had an issue and would roll out to the end. I appeared to have good control; and the noise was gone. I elected to taxi the short distance to the FBO with an escort of Airport Operations vehicles.By shutdown; all indications appeared to be normal again until post-flight inspection. At post-flight; damage was observed to the prop; the flap cowlings (subsequently the flap linkages); and rear avionics antennas.The approach to landing felt normal. I was sequenced between two aircraft and behind another Pilatus. Flaps were extended 7-8 miles from the airport and gear was extended on a final vector to the FAF approximately abeam the FAF. Landing checklist was completed via the IPAD; and a 'Last Finals' check was performed by myself at 500 foot call. (Last Finals: gear down-three green; flaps 15- final setting; lights on; landing clearance; go-around altitude set). Yaw Damper (YD) was disconnected at DH announcement.On GP for the ILS; Torque was normal at 6-7 inches with the AOA indicating middle or slightly lower. Idle power was set between 70-50 AGL. Initial touchdown was at TDZ or slightly beyond.ATC was typical for the airport and time of day.Maintenance and company management are investigating. And I have been reviewing my actions and performance in this event.
column125: Pilot reported that at touchdown the aircraft pulled to the right and the right wing started to drop; however; the wings remained level during rollout. At postflight; damage was found to parts of the aircraft.

column121: Aircraft X came to an abrupt stop on Runway XX at ZZZ due to snow on the runway. We were the only occupants of the craft. Neither of us were injured; nor was the plane damage. There was no damage to any other persons or property.A low approach was made to assess the condition of the runway before attempting a soft field approach and landing. We decided to make an approach to land after judging that the snow looked to be at or less than 2 inches. It looks like two layers of very thin snow. We could see tracks from another tricycle gear airplane. What we didn't realize was that that plane had landed in much shallower snow and that it had then snowed more on top. Being able to see grass protruding through the snow also gave the notion that the snow was not deep. I briefed my student on precautions to take with landing on snow before we made the approach.When we landed; the plane's mains and nose wheel sunk in causing us to come to an abrupt halt. We were able to taxi the aircraft most of the way up the runway. We agreed to attempt a soft field take off back down our tracks; which didn't get very far.Chief; Assistant Chief; Owner; and Lead Pilot were notified. We contacted the Airport Manager; who was able to come to our aid with a backhoe. We took off from the county road next to the runway as decided between myself; the Student and Chief Pilot; and as advised by the Airport Manager. A successful short field takeoff and climb out was performed by myself; the instructor and my student and I returned to ZZZ1.14 CFR 91.103; Pre-flight preparation; was not observed or fulfilled to its fullest. I will not get into the possible reasoning behind this; aside from that we decided to go to #X# during taxi.Lessons learned are abundant by our main takeaways that [we] I discussed were as follows:-New or unfamiliar airports need better preparation and scrutiny.-Snow depth should not be judged from the air. Until properly equipped and properly trained; no approach and landing should be made to a snowy runway that one is not sure they could take off from again.-I now have a new example of things not to do to use as a teaching tool with my students.We were extremely lucky pilots today. but we as pilots do not want to rely on luck. Lessons learned.

column121: I was working Approach Control and received a call over the landline from ZNY Center about an emergency aircraft that was diverting that had an engine failure and was going to be landing. They checked on normally with me and said they were expecting a normal approach and landing at the airport and were getting set up to receive the approach clearance. They were given vectors to help them descend and they advised when they were ready to accept the approach clearance for the ILS approach. They then flew the approach and landed safely. Emergency vehicles were standing by; but not needed. It was brought to my attention that the ILS approach was not authorized in the NOTAMs. I had been briefed that changes were made to the approach plates for the ILS approach and was informed that the changes went into effect four days prior. It was not clear and I was not aware that the ILS approach was not authorized at the time the emergency aircraft used the approach.Clearer guidance is needed from the FAA once changes are made to approach plates.

column121: Prior to the event; I came up from break and took a LC (Local Control) position that controls a satellite Class D airport with a gravel strip and water lanes. This position was combined with both CIC and Clearance Delivery/FD. This was never trained as a standard; but some work crews have made this a normal practice when staffing is short. Quickly after taking position; and before the event occurred; I did call up someone to split off the position but I should not have allowed it to remain de-combined in the interim. LC and GC (Ground Control) were de-combined; and while working I heard GC bring a potential conflict to LCs attention (good tower teamwork from GC). As I examined the situation I saw the same conflict- a heavy aircraft had taxied into departure position (Runway 33) with a large aircraft arriving behind on a non-intersecting runway (Runway 7L). With the spacing of the aircraft it did not appear that there was enough space to ensure compliance with our facility order. Our facility order details spacing required with aircraft arriving Runways 7R/L and departing Runway 33. I advised the LC that I did not believe adequate spacing would exist and suggested; but did not instruct; alternate courses of action to avoid a situation where the arriving aircraft might encounter jet blast. The LC insisted it would work and did not take any action. With the proximity of the aircraft; I don't believe a safety issue existed; however lack of compliance with facility guidelines with no standard for separation is an unacceptable risk to the users of the system.This event highlights many issues.As CIC during the event; I take responsibility for my own personal performance. I should not have taken CIC combined with an LC position. I should have had more engagement in the operation - if this situation had been detected earlier there may have been more time to correct it. I also should have given the LC instruction instead of suggestion when I saw action was not going to be taken.1. Lack of staffing. Perpetual lack of staffing leads this facility down the path of using what should be contingency practices into day to day practices. Positions are too often combined and because this has become the norm; controllers do not understand that they are providing a reduced level of service.2. Lack of safety culture. LC had two controllers address concern over this action and did not appear to consider an alternate course of action. This is not a behavior to this specific Controller but throughout this facility.3. Lack of training. The training culture and training at this facility has fallen short on ensuring that the staff of the facility have a thorough understanding of the rules we are expected to apply.4. Lack of supervision. Much like our Controller workforce; our supervisory staff is frequently understaffed and asked to do an excessive amount of duties that take them away from the operation. There was not a supervisor present at the time of the incident.5. Bad procedures. The facility; and region as a whole have done a poor job at creating procedures that are not easily followed by pilots or controllers. Many of these procedures don't have reasonable 'outs'; which put pilots and controllers in dangerous situations in a recovery situation.Consider revising ANC jet blast procedures.Provide clear expectations to CPCs on services we are expected to provide pilots.Provide adequate staffing to accomplish existing goals.
column125: ANC Tower Controller reported Local Controller failed to follow facility policy nor take action to comply. Reporter states facility policies are unclear and difficult to follow.

column121: I was notified about a report of a wrong surface landing. I was the Controller on the midnight shift.  I cleared Aircraft X for Runway XXC; but the Aircraft landed Runway XXR. Approach switched the aircraft on an extended base for the visual approach [to] Runway XXC. The pilot checked on the frequency for Runway XXC. I cleared the aircraft to land for Runway XXC. After I cleared the plane to land the janitorial guy comes to the Tower cab to vacuum the Tower and empty the trash. I usually go opposite of him so we can keep our space (we had our mask on) for social distancing. When he finished cleaning the Local 3 position I recognized the aircraft was short final to Runway XXR. I knew the runway had no vehicles on it because my strip bay showed no vehicle above Runway XXR bay header. I let the aircraft land Runway XXR due to the critical phase in flight. I taxied the aircraft to the ramp after the aircraft landed safely. In the past Approach would call me on the shout line to remind me that this aircraft is set up on the wrong approach. By me not being at the position walking around the Tower cab I did not recognize the aircraft on approach for the wrong runway.The recommendations I would use to prevent this wrong surface landing is to observe an aircraft turn onto the right approach for the right runway. If that pilot makes a mistake turning on to the wrong approach I can either send the aircraft around or correct the aircraft to land on the proper runway; or I can help the pilot get visual for the runway the pilot is lined up for and clear the plane to land for that runway. The key is catching the aircraft turning on the approach early enough so I can correct the pilot.
column125: ATC Tower Controller reported an aircraft landed on the wrong runway and cited social distancing distraction during a janitorial service in the Tower as a contributing factor.

column121: On a flight on a VMC day; all was going smoothly. On short final to Runway XXR; below the 1;000 foot call; I saw amber 'STEERING'; on the wheels system page and pointed this out to the FO (First Officer) who was PF (Pilot Flying); We then got an ECAM; WHEEL N/W STRG FAULT. Quickly discussed the situation and decided to continue on to land the aircraft. The FO was flying and continued to do so. After a beautiful landing; on roll out I took control of the aircraft; bellow 80 knots. I had no control of steering with the tiller. I used rudder and differential in-braking to exit the runway on the high speed exit and hold short Taxiway 1. Notified ATC that we could not taxi and were stopping on taxiway; clear of XXR and short of taxiway. Talked with ZZZ Maintenance and reset the A/SKID & N/W STRG; this cleared the ECAM. When I tried to taxi the plane the ECAM returned. Maintenance said they would send a tug to tow us to the gate. Sent a message for the fault. Notified Dispatch of our situation. After about 30 minutes; Maintenance arrived and talked with us. The mechanic said the nose wheel was canted. They asked us to try and reset a couple of circuit breakers. Discussed the situation With the FO and decided on no more trouble shooting and just be towed to the gate to get passengers off the plane. Maintenance agreed and we were towed uneventfully to the gate.

column121: We were assigned the RNAV Rwy 35; and given direct MEYRS.  We briefed the approach and confirmed altitudes in the FMS.  ATC gave us the clearance 'Cross MEYRS at or above 14;000 [feet]; cleared for the RNAV 35.'  I was the Pilot Monitoring; and I dialed 14;000 into to the autopilot pre-select.  The Pilot Flying entered 14000 in the FMS at MEYRS and executed a direct VNAV descent to MEYRS.  This caused the altitude to turn yellow in the FMS because the next altitude on the approach; at YARUB; was 14;800 [feet].  We discussed this for a moment and I deleted the 14;800 from the FMS.  The Pilot Flying then stated; and I agreed; that we should follow the charted altitudes and we entered 15;000 for MEYRS so that we would be above the published MEA of 14800 between MEYRS and YARUB.  With my intention being to put the YARUB altitude back into the FMS; I referenced the chart and then entered 12000 for YURUB (which was an error).  After crossing MEYRS at 15;000 [feet] we began a slow VNAV descent for 12;000 [feet]; but we should have been level at 14;800 [feet] all the way to YARUB.  While cross checking our position between the map display and the Jepp chart; I began to suspect that we had made an error.  Passing about 13;000 [feet]; I asked the Pilot Flying to level off; which he did; and then I stated that we were not at the correct altitude and that we should climb.  At that moment we received a GPWS caution that gave us a one time audible alert; and the Pilot Flying began a climb.  Shortly afterwards we passed the YARUB fix; and the MEA was now 12;000 so we continued the descent.While monitoring our progress on the approach; I noticed that our altitude was below the charted MEA.  I was confused for a minute while I tried to determine if we had made an error and then realized that I had deleted an altitude; and then re-enter it; but had in fact programmed the incorrect altitude.  Looking back I can't quite explain my thought process; and after the fact my mistake seems very obvious and amateur.  I did reference the chart for the YARUB altitude; and looking at it again '12;000' is written very close to YARUB; and that's my only explanation for how I matched that altitude to that fix; but of course 12;000 is the altitude after YARUB.  While I was monitoring our progress I remember thinking that something didn't seem quite right; and it took me a minute to piece together the ATC clearance; our ensuing actions; and to come to the conclusion that I had made a mistake.  The ATC clearance to an altitude that was already below a charted MEA caused a moment of confusion and second guessing.  I removed an altitude from the FMS that was auto loaded with the approach.  A few minutes later; when I realized I had made an error; I attempted to correct it but I put the wrong altitude back in the FMS after incorrectly reading the chart.  I asked the Pilot Flying to level off while I cross checked the chart and; FMS; and our position.  I then stated that we should climb; which we did.I should not have been so quick to delete an altitude from the FMS which was loaded by selecting the approach.  Also; when entering an altitude into the FMS I need to be more careful while referencing the chart and also confirm with the Pilot Flying that I am entering the correct altitude.
column123: While descending into MJT we were cleared by ATC to pass over MEYRS at or above 14;000 feet and cleared the RNAV 35 approach. We had already briefed the approach and checked the altitudes in the FMS. The first segment between MEYRS and the next point on the approach YARUB is 14;800 feet. Just before reaching MEYRS we decided to stop our descent to pass over MEYRS at 14;000 [feet]and hold at 15;000 [feet]. After YARUB the next step down is 12;000 feet. We had been descending in VNAV to make the cross at Myers. My First Officer put 12;000 feet at YARUB and I confirmed it. In VNAV we then started a slow descent to pass over YARUB at 12;000 feet. This was a mistake on my part. We realized at about 12;700 we where wrong and below a published minimum. I stopped the descent and started a climb. We did get a momentary GPWS terrain and responded according. We climbed about 20 feet and it was gone. We had already stopped descending when we got the GPWS. Realizing we where doing the wrong thing by descending in VNAV to cross YARUB at 12;000 [feet].Allowing the Pilot Monitoring to put 12000 feet n the FMS at YARUB. Immediate once we realized our mistake.. Autopilot off. Start a climb. We never did get to 14;800 [feet] because of how we where close to YARUB. I realized that we where low but also safe and never in any danger. I feel like I should have queried ATC about the descent clearance. I should have been more vigilant with the cross at YARUB.

column121: ZZZ to ZZZ1; visual approach into Runway XX; VMC weather conditions. At approximately 300 feet AGL two messages populated on the EICAS; PTRIM (Pitch Trim) MAIN INOP and PTRIM BACKUP INOP. Both crew members initially looked to see if half of the trim control on either side yolk had been accidentally held; which would cause the effected main control to become INOP; neither side was observed being pressed or stuck. The crew then quickly tested each main control and back up control; one at a time; each control switch failed to activate/ control the elevator trim. The airspeed was 145 KTS IAS. The crew briefed that the controls were trimmed for landing speed; 145 KTS; and due to the short final; now near 200 feet AGL; the landing would be continued. The crew also quickly briefed that in the event of a go-around special attention would need to be paid attention to airspeed due to the INOP trim and the traffic pattern and second landing would be flown at/near 145 KTS. The flight terminated safely and maintenance was called. Maintenance investigation found that when the autopilot disconnect button was pressed to disengage the autopilot it became stuck in the pressed position. This stuck position lead to the dual; main and backup; trim inoperative condition.I would very much like to know if this is a replicable condition with the AP disconnect button or if this failure was an anomaly. If this is a programming fail safe designed into the system; then awareness training might be beneficial to the rest of the group.

column121: DEN ground instructed us to taxi to RWY 17L from E4 via mike; hold short of ED. Once on Mike northbound Ground instructed us to turn right at ED and hold short of the approach end of Runway 17R. The FO (First Officer) was in the process of completing his delayed engine start flow as I started the turn on ED. At this point I noticed traffic on short final to Runway 17R and thinking to myself that it didn't seem right to turn on ED. I stopped the aircraft as soon as it was straight and pointed east on ED. I asked the FO if he saw a sign or hold short line on his side. I saw no signage in front of us and now felt extremely uncomfortable with our proximity to the Runway 17R approach path. At this point the Ground Controller told us we were supposed to stop 50 ft. further back. I looked behind us and noticed the left wing tip was over the east edge of Taxiway Mike. I could not see a hold line below the wing. We were switched to Tower. The Tower Controller gave us a number to call for a possible pilot deviation. Shortly after writing down the number we were cleared for takeoff. As we rolled down the runway I realized we failed to complete the final items.I spoke to the Tower supervisor later that day and explained the events as they occurred. He admitted that this hot spot is an ongoing issue; the hold area is not well marked and the Controller's intent was to have us pointed east to help move aircraft through the Runway 17R approach path faster. Unfortunately the Tower had to send around the landing traffic I was distracted with during the turn because they were not sure if we would stop. I have flown in and out of DEN since the day it opened and have never been issued a clearance like this. I have always been instructed to hold short of ED when aircraft are landing Runway 17R. I believe this type of clearance should be avoided; specially in an area that is a hot spot. The aircraft behind us which received the same clearance had to confirm the clearance and the intent to hold short immediately after turning.
column123: We had a leg planned for DEN-ZZZ with Runways 17L; 17R; and 8 being used for departure.  Going westbound; I programmed and planned and briefed a 17R departure. The captain briefed a single-engine taxi.  After block out we were advised of a 17L departure.  For me that is several threats; a runway change requiring FMC programming; retrieving new performance; single-engine taxi ( increased workload; 2 engine-start flows etc); and unavoidable time when only the Captains eyes are outside the cockpit during FO single-engine duties during hi intensity ground operations @ DEN. On taxi out we were instructed to taxi to Runway 17L via Taxiway M and hold short of ED.  At some point northbound on Taxiway M with a hold short instruction in hand;  I initiated start of ENG 1.   During the engine start; the ground controller gave an additional instruction to turn right on to ED and hold short of the Runway 17R approach area.  Due to my required duties during an engine start which aren't easily paused/stopped; I could not write down the instruction; nor could I refer to my taxi diagram so that I could act as a backup-another set of eyes in the cockpit; I was only able to say-back the instruction and nothing else. The captain had a response to me that conveyed that he heard and understood the instruction.  I pressed on completing the engine-start.   I was heads down completing my duties during our expected right hand turn onto ED.  Shortly thereafter I heard the captain make a comment about an aircraft landing on Runway 17R and he brought the airplane to stop.  It was several moments before the ground controller came on the radio to say that we were to hold short of the approach area which was now behind us.  We were then instructed to continue taxi; switched over to tower; given a phone number and cleared for takeoff.

column121: First Officer and I flew our first leg of the day from ZZZ to Ashville Regional Airport. Most of the flight was normal and uneventful. About 15 minutes into the flight; we were cruising at FL190 and decided to prepare for the approach. We got the ATIS; landing performance information; set up the approach and First Officer briefed the approach. We noticed and briefed that AVL was on the FOQA list of top 10 airports for unstable approaches and briefed that they were for high speeds and descent rates. ATC then cleared us to descend with multiple step down altitude assignments (11;000; 7000; 6000; 5500; and 4700). We completed the descent and approach checklists when they were needed. Upon descending through 10;000 feet; ATC started vectoring us for the visual approach for runway 35. We continued the set up and called the runway in sight. We were vectored a bit high because of the surrounding terrain. While we were descending on our turn from base to final into Ashville Regional Airport; we got a GPWS warning (terrain; terrain; whoop; whoop; pull up). I decided to react first; since this was unexpected and immediate action may have been necessary with a GPWS warning. I started the recovery by leveling the wings; adding full power and starting a climb (simultaneously pulling back on the yoke and adding power). Then I called for a go-around. We notified ATC of the go-around and they asked for the reason. We told them it was due to the GPWS warning and they said okay and gave us an initial altitude and heading to rejoin the approach. After reconfiguring; calling for the autopilot; and After-Takeoff checklist; I transferred controls back to First Officer and we discussed what had happened and briefed how we would execute the next approach to avoid another GPWS warning. I made a P/A explaining the go-around to the passengers so that they would feel comfortable and safe. We then requested an ILS 35 approach and flew into to AVL without further incident.My First Officer and I concluded that the GPWS warning was likely due to the high closure rate in which we were descending to join final. We were joining final and descending right at the final approach fix (UMUXE); and the charts show multiple obstacles and rising terrain in the immediate area. Also; the FOQA alert page mentioned the obstacles/terrain in the report sent to us via ACARS.As a crew; it would help to always request an ILS approach when it is available. As the pilot flying; I would have slowed down earlier and planned a descent to be level while crossing the FAF. This would reduce the chance the that the aircraft EGPWS will calculate a high rate of closure between the aircraft and terrain that results in a GPWS warning.

column121: Better monitor FO actions. Increase awareness of calculating descent points using distance from the runway to approximate a 3 degree descent path to the runway in order to avoid unnecessary and lengthy low level segments.  Flying at 3;000 feet about 15 miles WSW of IND direct to BIGRD on the RNAV Y 5R. Approach asked if we had the field in sight; we responded that we did; and then we were given a left turn pretty much direct to HUUPS; which is the final approach fix on the RNAV Y 5R; and cleared for the visual approach. Tower then handed us off to Tower. During this time while I was switching to Tower I became task saturated with my PM flying duties from the frequency change; getting a landing clearance; and the FO requesting flap configuration changes. After we were cleared to land I noticed we were just about to level off at 2;100 feet. Well below a 3 degree descent path given our current position to the runway. I had not noticed the FO had set 2;100 feet; which is the altitude at HUUPS; and started a descent as soon as we were cleared for the approach. We leveled off at 2;100 feet and continued for some time before reaching HUUPS and continuing the descent for the runway. We were level at approximately 1;300' feet AGL for a while before descending for landing. I don't think this was technically illegal or in violation of an FAR; as we were cleared for the visual and in the process of making a descent for landing; but I do feel it's not a good technique to simply set the FAF altitude; dive and drive to that altitude; level off for several miles; then continue descent once over the FAF. We were not on a published portion of an approach; and well below a 3 degree descent path to the runway. This wasn't an issue for this particular approach; but it could become an issue at night in mountainous terrain or if there are obstacles in the area.

column121: After landing I maintained center line; braking action after landing I noticed was nil. The plane was near stopping and had started to veer to the right; at the time in which I input left rudder to counter the right turn and no avail the aircraft began to slide sideways. After attempts were made to completely stop; the engines were shut down to prevent further damage as it slid into the snowbank to which after coming to a complete stop; ATC was notified once we were allowed to exit the aircraft noticed the runway was icy.
column123: Upon touchdown on runway 25 at Merrill airport it was very apparent that there was NIL braking action available. Tower informed us prior to landing that there was no braking action reports. As we were decelerating through approximately 35-40 knots ground speed; the student in the left seat (pilot flying) commented that we had no brakes. I immediately placed my feet on the rudder pedals with him and applied brakes. There was no braking available. I released pressure from the brakes to aid in steering and asked the student to cease braking. My intentions at this time was to allow the aircraft to decelerate using aerodynamic braking and roll to the end of the runway. Shortly thereafter the aircraft began a slide to the right. I immediately applied full left rudder without any action. I then applied left brake to aid in the directional control; which also did not assist. My last resort was to increase power on the right engine to attempt to correct the aircraft towards center line; but at this point the aircraft was too far gone in the slide. As it was apparent the aircraft was going to exit the runway I immediately pulled both mixtures to the idle cutoff position in an effort to stop the props from rotating and causing any damage. The aircraft ended up approximately 110 degrees rotated to the right of the center of the runway and the aircraft came to a rest approximately 5-10 feet off of the runway edge in approximately 6 inch deep snow. At this point I informed the tower of our position and we would need assistance to get the aircraft back on the runway. I called the owner of [the FBO and he was able to remove and tow the aircraft without further incident. After further inspection it was determined that there was absolutely no damage to the aircraft or any airport property; including the runway edge lights. The aircraft has since been turned over to Angel Aviation maintenance for further inspections.

column121: I was flying with a student pilot; practicing simulated engine failure emergency procedures. Student selected Frazier Lake airport (1C9) as the landing point. We positioned the airplane directly towards 1C9 for runway 5 which is a grass runway. I scanned the area in front and right of us to make sure there was no other traffic nearby. We descended towards runway 5 and ended the practice by going around above runway 5 at about 800 ft AGL. The next day we received a phone call from another pilot who was operating in traffic pattern at 1C9 attempting to land on the hard surface taxiway parallel to runway 5. He told us that he saw us on final flying next to him only approximately 200 ft above. He made few radio calls trying to reach us but no response; because we were not on 1C9 CTAF at the time. What have caused this event was that the student was busy performing the maneuver; forgot to change frequency to 1C9 CTAF and announce our intention. I was too focused on the training but overlooked the safety aspect of the flight. The corrective action is I should have changed frequency to 1C9 CTAF and monitor it when the student decided to head over there.

column121: Aircraft X departed Runway 30 on an assigned 270 heading. Shortly after switching the aircraft to departure; I scanned the radar and saw Aircraft X turning through a 270 heading and continuing a left turn to the east directly into the arrival corridor for aircraft landing Runways 1L and 1C. At that moment; a controller at PCT; I think Departure Control contacted me. My initial thought was that Aircraft X had misunderstood my assigned heading and had not switched to Departure Control and was still on my frequency. While the controller was speaking to me other aircraft were talking on the frequency. The cross-talk prevented me from clearly understanding the information that was being relayed in the coordination. I heard the controller say climb the aircraft to 3;000 ft.; and turn him to the west immediately.Because I did not clearly hear the call sign of the aircraft he was referencing my mind jumped to Aircraft X as the aircraft needing the instructions. I immediately issued a climb and turn to Aircraft X and began issuing traffic alerts. Midway through issuing the traffic alerts I recognized that Aircraft X was descending and turning to join final for Runway 1L. In that instance I recognized what Departure Control was trying to coordinate and I sent Flight Number X around and vectored him away from Aircraft X and issued a traffic alert.Aircraft X checked onto my frequency on a mile final. I cleared him to land. Immediately after clearing him to land; the ASDE-X alerted with a taxiway proximity alert. It appeared to me that Aircraft X was correcting back toward Runway 1L. I asked the pilot if they requested priority handling. The pilot said no. The aircraft appeared aligned with the runway and the pilot confirmed they were; but because he was not a priority. I sent the plane around as required by the 7110.65. Coordination would have been easier and clearer had the radar data tag scratchpad entry for Aircraft X been changed to '01L' which would have indicated that he was inbound to land. The scratchpad showed the SID he filed; which contributed to my initial thinking that he was off course and not a priority. Additionally; had Departure Control requested priority handling in the coordination; I believe I would have immediately keyed onto the fact that Aircraft X was returning.The Airport Surface Detection Equipment (ASDE-X) ASDE Taxiway Arrival Prediction Software (ATAP) has had five alerts since its implementation on Date. In every case; the aircraft have been properly aligned with the runway and the alerts have been the result of poorly designed taxiway capture zones. The ATAP enhancement should be temporarily disabled and a full review of the ATAP system should occur.
column125: A Tower Local Controller reported a departure deviated from its clearance and turned back to the airport to land and into confliction with an arrival on final approach.The Tower received an alert from an automated system (ASDE X ATAP) warning that the aircraft was aligned with a taxiway and not the runway although the aircraft had corrected back to course. The reporter states that false alerts from the ASDE X ATAP (Airport Surface Detection Equipment Taxiway Arrival Prediction Software) is a recurring issue.

column121: I was working LC/GC (Local Control/Ground Control) combined during LADP (Local Airport De-Icing Plan) operations. An aircraft called ready for departure on Runway 6 and was instructed to hold short for traffic landing Runway 33. Several Airport-Operations vehicles were on the frequency at the time and one of them stated on the frequency that Runway 33 was closed. The landing aircraft was issued go-around instructions on a two-mile final and subsequently landed on Runway 6. The Tower CIC (Controller in Charge) checked the NOTAMs and verified that Runway 33 had been closed for approximately 30 minutes. No notification had been received from Airport Operations that Runway 33 was closed.Similar incidents of this nature have happened in the past. Coordination via automation; or recorded landline; may help prevent these situations from happening in the future.

column121: Working Radar East at ZZZ. I had approximately 14 aircraft. Aircraft X was on an arrival to ZZZ. I had many other over flights and 3 more arrivals into ZZZ. I tried to turn the Aircraft X back to ZZZ and was late in seeing Aircraft Y that was being vectored south of ZZZ to a nearby airport. When I saw Aircraft Y; I told Aircraft X to give me a tight turn to heading 020; if unable I'll need to turn to the south. I called traffic and the aircraft were really close. Aircraft X reported Aircraft Y insight and was climbing. They didn't report and RA at the time. Aircraft Y was also called traffic and told to maintain visual separation after they reported Aircraft X insight.Aircraft X was cleared for visual approach shortly afterwards.Due to the COVID staffing my crew continues to work short staffed. I allowed another controller to allow a VFR aircraft to be flying to conflict with my arrivals that drew attention away from the final at the time. I also should have handed off the arrivals to the West Controller that also had the finals at the time. The West Controller was the one that allowed the VFR traffic that I was watching in conflict with the arrival into ZZZ conflict.

column121: The Captain briefed that he would fly the ZZZ ILS 27R. On the arrival abeam the FAF we called the airport in sight and were subsequently cleared #1 for the visual approach. In the turn towards the final approach I lost sight of the runway. I was expecting the Captain to maneuver the aircraft to align the airplane with the approach course prior to; or at; the FAF. He turned well inside the FAF to intercept a 3 mile final. I was not expecting this and felt behind the aircraft. While on the base leg we received a radio call from Tower 'low altitude alert; below minimum vectoring altitude.' This left me even more uneasy during the approach. I expressed my concern to the Captain. The Captain was able to re-cage my situational awareness and continued the visual approach. We rolled out on final; stabilized on glide path and made an uneventful landing. We thoroughly debriefed the flight; with much attention on the approach.
column123: The FO (First Officer) had never been into ZZZ; and I hadn't been there for years. Briefing the approach; I handled the terrain" item by saying something silly like "I don't know of any." In fact there was no high terrain; but there were close-in obstacles. I mentioned that we might well get a slam dunk visual but I did not communicate to the FO my idea of normally being comfortable joining the final on visual approaches inside the FAF; about 3 miles out at 1;000 feet AGL. If I had done that fuller briefing; he would not have been surprised when I later pointed the aircraft inside the FAF. Descending on the arrival; we noted that the procedure put us on a long final for the ILS to 27R; but we both got the impression that we would be #1 to the airport and slam dunked. On downwind abeam the airport we were descended to 3;000 feet and then cleared for the visual approach. I headed over toward the ILS FAF and descended toward its crossing altitude; 2;100 feet; while I evaluated the position and energy. I saw that we were in a position to join a 3-mile final in a stabilized condition. Focusing visually on the right base leg and the airport; we were at about 1;700 feet on about a three mile base. At this time ATC transmitted; "Low altitude alert; minimum vectoring altitude is 2;100 feet." Our path looked clear ahead and on final to the right; and I did not consider ATC's radar vectoring altitude to exactly apply to our operation. Still; they were trying to tell us something! I looked to the left and saw downtown; laterally closer than desired but certainly more than 2;000 feet horizontally. I shallowed the descent as much as I could without going too high to be stabilized on final. We joined the final and landed normally.By inadequately studying and briefing the airport's visual flying environment; I did a night visual approach that was safe but that (1) concerned ATC; (2) concerned my flying partner; and (3) worst of all; surprised me about our proximity to downtown because I had not identified the high buildings as a threat."

column121: Inbound to ZZZZ on ZZZZZ Star.  Taken off STAR early and cleared direct to ZZZZ1 VOR.  We were instantly 1;800 feet above the path.  Proactively used speed brakes and planned to slow and extend flaps when below 20;000 feet; but were then told to keep speed at 250 KTS.  Lowered landing gear shortly after passing ZZZZ1 to get back on profile.  Was able to regain the VNAV path just prior to ZZZZZ1 and at Vref +10 per procedure.  Flaps were at 25. Frequency was very busy and sky hazy. Very late hand off to Tower.  Localizer did not correctly ID until just prior to FAF.  FO was very busy with frequency changes; configuration changes and checklist.  Tower did not clear us to land; but instructed us to continue as an aircraft was in position on our Runway; XXR. Another aircraft was holding short of XXR waiting to cross.  At 1;000 feet I was at approach speed and called 'stable;' but was distracted by no landing clearance and aircraft on runway.  Aircraft in position was cleared for immediate takeoff; we were cleared to land; and other aircraft was given clearance to cross XXR.  Departing aircraft was slow to accelerate.  Passing 500 feet FO made 'stable' call.  Shortly after we received an aural alert indicating we did not have landing flaps selected.  Flaps indicator showed flaps at 25 degrees verses the planned and briefed of 30 degrees.  I realized I had not asked for flaps 30 and was now below 400 feet. Elected not to make a configuration change to 30 degrees below 1;000 feet per procedure.  Hesitant to go-around per procedure because I wasn't sure if the crossing aircraft had not just departed the inboard parallel Runway XXL; and the late departing XXR aircraft had just rotated.  With the very hazy sky I was concerned about a conflict and /or overtaking the other aircraft during the go-around to increase speed to Vref+10 and land with flaps 25. Landing and rollout were normal.Despite adequate and recent experience into ZZZZ; these particular controllers were hard to understand.  FO had to ask for clarification a few times in the last 1;500 feet FAF. FO had just returned from 7 months of leave; was called out on reserve; and had only flown once earlier; and 4 times in the last 7 months.  I briefed the arrival and approach thoroughly and early; but it was a Max effort with the direct clearance.  I believe he got distracted from completing the checklist when the localizer did not ID until just before the FAF.  I was distracted from not calling for final flaps with the aircraft on the runway and the hazy conditions; and the late localizer ID.Add to the note of being at Vref +10 by the ZZZZZ1 fix; to be at final landing flaps. This may have helped me if I had a goal to complete that by the fix.

column121: Prior to Final approach in ZZZ; approach vectored us very close to the final approach course with a 30 kt tailwind on base and at 4;000 ft just outside ZZZZZ for the ILS XXR; assigned 190 kts. As we intercepted the course the autopilot flew past the localizer at close to half scale deflection; as it would not correct course timely. We also had to descend and slow down to configure for final approach within 3 miles of final approach fix. As the autopilot was correcting for that we set 3;000 ft for the missed approach altitude. A CAS MISCOMP status message appeared and the altitude alerter came on would not stop ringing. When we changed navigation source to green needles; the localizer would not capture with the autopilot; therefore the glide slope would also not capture. During the navigation source change; my flight director on the first officer's side became erratic and I lost the localizer transmission. We decided to execute a missed approach due to being unstable along with the flight instrument errors. Tower assigned us a heading and altitude and we reconfigured the aircraft for the go around all while the altitude alerter was blaring. During straight and level flight; we decided to reassign the FCP to heading and altitude. Once established; we turned the autopilot on and all instruments worked correctly. The altitude alert ringer was still buzzing so we decided to silence using Data Control Unit (DCU) audio buttons to declutter the situation. After completing proper checklists; we decided to continue on with another ILS XXR and executed approach and landing normally. After shutdown the altitude alerter still continued to ring until AC power was switched from the APU GEN to EXT AC. We wrote up the problem and notified maintenance.ATC giving us tight vectors with a tailwind on base along with our equipment malfunctions. The altitude alerter sounding loudly along our flight directors being erratic caused task saturation to the captain who was PF. As PM I helped the captain de clutter the situation and to reminded him to just fly the airplane which he did well as we tracked the localizer and conducted the go around. Once we silenced the altitude alerter it became much easier to fly the aircraft and we were able to conduct the next approach successfully.Possibly asking for a slower intercept speed or lower from ATC. The equipment malfunctions lead to the approach to become unstable and that's why we conducted a go around. I could have possibly contacted tower a few seconds earlier to ask for a vector and altitude but it was quite difficult with the task saturation being as high as it was; especially with the altitude alerter loudly sounding. Overall it was a great lesson in always being able to maintain control and fly the aircraft regardless of the state of automation it is in which I thought we did a great job of despite the system malfunctions during a high work load environment.
