column121: In preparation for our arrival into DEN we briefed for the CLASH 3 RNAV arrival and the ILS to 35R as this was the expected runway from our direction of arrival. We also included in our brief the threat in DEN of runway changes and how we'd handle that especially during the descent phase on the arrival. About 20 NM from the waypoint CLASH on the arrival ATC informed us to expect runway 35L. We then re-set up for that runway and re-briefed. There was still plenty of time for this and there was little time compression. One point of confusion; however; was that the CLASH 3 has a transition for RWY 35R; but doesn't for 35L; which only has a point at which to expect vectors. We spent some time studying the arrival and approach plates to ensure that was the case to make sure we weren't missing something. I asked my F/O to double check as he was the PM and I wanted to concentrate on the descent as the PF. This has always been confusing as some arrivals and approaches have transition points and some don't. There should be some consistency. This was the beginning of some of the time compression which would later result in some workload management issues.Some point after this ATC broke us off of the arrival and instructed us to descend to 9000 feet and gave us a heading. They then assigned us to RWY 35R. We related that we'd just been assigned 35L and they reiterated we were now reassigned to 35R. This resulted in a very rushed set up for 35R as we were now pretty close in to the approach environment. I asked the F/O to set it all up so that I could continue to fly. I quickly dialed in what I thought was the 35R LOC; but it turned out not to be. I could see that he was struggling and tried to help him out. In hindsight; this was a mistake in workload management because I should have let the F/O do all of the set up. I verbalized that we didn't need to re-brief for 35R as we'd already briefed it and so had a plan. Again; in hindsight; I'm not sure this was a correct call; but we literally did not have time to brief at that point.Shortly after this we were cleared the visual approach to 35R.  We were roughly 7 miles from FRONZ and discussed not descending too quickly so as not to bust the floor of the Class B airspace. At about the same time ATC asked us to slow to approach speed for traffic ahead of us for 35R. After slowing we ended up just about 2.5 miles in trail.  At this point we were apprised of traffic for 35L and received a TCAS TA because of the assigned heading we were given to intercept the LOC for 35R. We both saw the traffic and I decided to hand fly from this point on as we had an overshooting crosswind and given our heading there would have been a good chance of an overshoot of the LOC.At FRONZ I asked for gear down and flaps 15. At about this point I noticed that neither the LOC or GS were engaging but I had the runway and the PAPI and decided to continue as we had been cleared for the visual. The FO asked if I'd like him to try and dial in the correct LOC and I said yes if he had the bandwidth to do so. Soon after we passed through 1;000 feet and we both missed the call out as I think he was still trying to dial in the LOC frequency. Also we never accomplished the Landing Checklist as we never got to final flaps. We got to 500 feet and we made those call outs. At around 400 feet AFL we received a Too Low Flaps GPWS warning and my knee jerk reaction was to call for FLAP 30 once I realized we were still at FLAPS 15. I don't know if the FO ever selected them; however. I quickly realize this was the wrong call and called for us to go around and the FO called for it at about the same time. We executed the missed approach with no issues and were vectored expeditiously for a second approach and landing without further incident.The FO is not submitting a report as far as I'm aware as we had no ATC deviations or non-compliance with FARS.
column123: This is a report regarding Flight X ZZZ-DEN regarding the changes and ATC clearances we received during our descent and approach to land in DEN.When we reviewed the flight plan in ZZZ we loaded the CLASH3 STAR with runway 35R being our selected landing runway. The flight plan had 17R; but DEN was landing North so that was the only change made to the plan on our part versus what dispatch had planned.Prior to TOD the CA fully reviewed and briefed the STAR and approach that we were expecting. We were fully prepared to execute this plan. Upon checking in with a close in DEN center controller we were advised to expect runway 35L with the EBBLR transition. There was some discussion regarding the EBBLR transition by myself and the CA as we were attempting to figure out if the transition and how the transition linked up with the approach. However; we did this again prior to TOD and were able to be prepared for the new arrival transition and approach. As we descended into Denver; we were handed off to DEN APP. This controller changed our runway back to 35R. We were well into the descent at this point and fairly close to the airport. The CA instructed me to load up the new approach and as I was attempting to do so ATC took us off the arrival for vectors and speed changes. At this point we were South of DEN but still fairly close in. I was still working on getting the FMC setup when ATC vectored us due West and then turned us on to final for 35R. The vector was vey poorly planned by the controller and both the CA and I could see that we were going to blow through the localizer if we allowed the A/P to handle the intercept. The CA disengaged the A/P and made a manual turn to join the localizer. At this point we could both see that the vector had us close in behind another airplane in final. ATC requested that we slow to 160 knots. The CA asked for me to deploy the gear and the flaps to down and 15. Shortly after ATC then requested us to slow to the slowest possible speed. Both the CA and I were closely monitoring the separation between us and the preceding aircraft. At this point I noticed there was a message on the FMC scratchpad that I had not seen before and did not recognize. I don't remember exactly what the message was but it did indicate that there was a conflict between the FMC selected approach and what the VHF radios were tuned to. I was rapidly trying to get the correct frequency on the NAV radios but my JEPP app on the iPad was not responding quickly as I was attempting to zoom in to view the frequency box. At this point we were somewhere around 1;000 feet AGL. The CA announced that he had the runway and the PAPIs in sight and was going to land and just disregard the FMC He was busy attempting to slow and again we were both concerned about the separation between us the preceding aircraft. That was what are focus was on at this point. I cannot recall if either the 1;000 ft or the 500 ft call outs were made. We were both totally task saturated at this time. At some altitude below 500 ft we received a warning message 'too low-flaps'. We both looked at the flap position and realized we were still at flaps 15. The CA called for flaps 30 and I monetarily selected flaps 30 before advocating that we needed to go around. The CA also realized we had to go around at almost the same instant I was stating that we needed to go around. So we executed the go around. We did have the 10;000 ft foot missed approach altitude selected in the MCP so we were climbing to that altitude. However; while just climbing through 7;000 the tower told us to maintain 7;000. I advised tower that we were at 7;200. Tower instructed us to maintain 7;100. We descended back to 7;100 feet and flew runway heading and completed the rest of the go around profile. Tower asked us for the reason for the GA and I replied 'configuration'. They handed us off to approach and we were vectored back around for another ILS to runway 35R. We ensured everything was properly setup and landed without further incident.Some comments about this incident. Both the CA and I had discussed the threat of Denver and how they often make changes to arrivals and approaches during cruise. In fact the CA had been reading in the flight ops newsletter on his iPad about the problems we have been having with arrivals into Denver. We were both fully aware that Denver presented a threat and thought that we had planned and were prepared for our arrival. Had we been cleared as planned per our original plan we would have been fine. Even after the first change to runway 35L we had time to plan and brief. It was only after the final switch back to 35R that we both began to become task saturated with the speed changes; the off route vectors; the FMC programming; the NAV radios; the poor vector to join final; and finally the separation between us and the preceding aircraft and the speed changes made while on final in regards to that aircraft. The amount of items needed to be accomplished while this was occurring in the terminal area was long and complex. We both became so overcome that we must have failed to accomplish the landing checklist; which would have had both of us realizing that we were not in the landing configuration.We made errors along the way; failing to get some of the call outs accomplished; FMC programming; and NAV radio frequency selection among some of the errors. However; many of these errors were driven by the nature of the changes being made by ATC in regards to arrival vectors; speed changes; runway and approach changes among other demands being made by ATC in a very time compressed period while flying within (or near) the terminal area.One more note about the runway changes in Denver. I know Denver and am aware of its threat even on departure. So while we were getting ready to depart and head to ZZZ2 I asked the ramp controller which runway was in use for the ZZZZZ SID. He stated XXR. Yet; when we received our push clearance 15-20 minutes later he told us to expect runway XY. I don't know if he was given bad information by the ATC tower or what happened; but yet again here we are in a high workload situation having to make changes to the FMC and request new TO data.Because of our short turn time in Denver the CA decided to debrief the entire arrival event into Denver while in cruise flying back to ZZZ2. We spent a good 30-40 minutes discussing the flight. Threat and errors were discussed; we used the briefing cards and identified what had occurred on the flight. I am not going to make excuses for the errors that we made; even if they occurred during a task saturated situation. However; a couple of my observations from this event were that we had talked about Denver ATC being a threat. We thought that we had addressed this threat by a good solid brief for the originally planned arrival; even after the change to the 35L arrival; we had time to plan and brief. We were ready; but ATC in Denver threw us some many changes and curve balls that we both were task saturated to the point leading to an undesired aircraft state. Fortunately; both the CA and I followed our training and SOP. safely executed a missed approach; and subsequently landed safely and uneventfully on the next approach.
column125: Air carrier flight crew reported a high workload environment on approach into DEN airport; which included several runway changes; confusion over arrival procedures; and an unstable approach resulted in a go-around.

column121: The RE [RADAR East] was working a busy session and Finals was combined to RW [RADAR West].  However; when Finals is combined; usually the two controllers will each work their own finals traffic.  The RE had Aircraft X on a base leg to Runway XXR at 2;000 feet.  The RW Controller had a VFR Aircraft Y; southbound at 1;800 feet; west of the Runway XXR final approach course.  The RE controller was distracted by another aircraft and forgot to turn Aircraft X.  He went through the final approach course and conflicted with the VFR Aircraft Y.  Both controllers issued traffic and Aircraft Y was able to maintain visual separation from Aircraft X.  However; Aircraft X responded to a TCAS RA and climbed.  Although separation was not lost; the situation was still concerning.I was working the desk and combined with AD [Arrival Data].  I had numerous duties on AD and wasn't able to provide consistent oversight.  However; I observed that it was getting busy on RE and I suggested the controller give his finals traffic to the RW Controller who wasn't as busy.  I did not direct the controller to do so; and so he declined my suggestion.  At the time of the incident; I was coordinating a release off of a satellite airport; as part of my AD duties.I believe that it's getting too busy for the staffing that we currently have.  We only had one Controller on break; so it would have been very difficult to split off another position.  I am consistently having to work AD from the desk; and have been encouraged to do so as much as possible to minimize the number of controllers in the operation during COVID-19.  The need for the CIC to work AD is also because we have so few controllers on the shift.I think I could have been more direct with the Controller; forcing him to hand off his finals traffic instead of suggesting it; and he could have used better techniques; such as not descending the arrival to the same altitude as the VFR aircraft.It should be noted that [personal reason] was the day before this incident.  I'm not sure that that contributed; but I was more tired than normal.

column121: The Satellite position had just been combined with Departure during a relatively slow traffic period. Shortly after assuming control of their traffic an aircraft went NORDO on a heading for an approach. While trying to reestablish communications with the aircraft; Aircraft X was on a vector for the ILS approach into a different airport. The data tag had already been accepted by Tower; so when the new controller assumed the traffic; the tag had already been turned a separate color than usual. Aircraft X was given a descent to 2;100 feet by the previous controller. During the attempts to reestablish communication with the NORDO aircraft; Aircraft X ended up skirting the boundary of a 2;600 foot Minimum Vectoring Altitude at 2;100 feet before making the turn back for the approach. No other aircraft were affected. After an aircraft data tag is highlighted green; there should be a way to turn the color of the aircraft back to white.

column121: I was the pilot monitoring and my First Officer was the pilot flying on Aircraft X from ZZZ1 to ZZZ.  We were rather high and fast at 6;000 feet with 250 knots when ATC cleared us for a visual approach for [Runway] XXL (backed up by RNAV XXL).  Approach clearance included the altitude restriction to cross ZZZ2 VOR at or above 2;500 feet which is normal when landing south at ZZZ.  However; in our effort to lose excess altitude and airspeed quickly we got busy; and that created a distraction in the cockpit.  The result was our crossing the VOR at around 2;200 feet (RNAV FAF altitude).During our post flight de-brief; the First Officer told me that he was not aware of the safety alert memo on the chart page; which cautions pilots on being high and fast when landing south.  He also told me that he was not sure of the location of ZZZ2 VOR on his navigational display.  None of that was communicated while in flight.  My being very familiar with operating in and out of ZZZ; and the past experience of working well with this particular First Officer; led me to assume that he was well aware of the uniqueness of the local procedures.  I should know better than to make assumptions about my crew's awareness.  What I should have done was bring that alert memo to his attention when he did not include that in his briefing.  I should have realized that the First Officer's recent flight experience might have been lacking due to COVID pandemic induced schedule reductions.  Again; a thorough briefing without my assuming First Officer's knowledge and awareness could have prevented the altitude deviation.  I will be careful going forward.
column123: We were vectored off of the ZZZZZ arrival (landing south) for the visual [Runway] XXL. At this point; we were still holding 250 KIAS at 4;000 feet. We were given a heading to intercept and a clearance for the visual approach with the restriction to 'cross the VOR above 2;500 feet.' Although we had the aircraft stabilized by 1;500 feet AGL; we may have missed the crossing restriction by approximately 300 feet. During my approach briefing; I failed to notice the safety alert box located on the chart page. When we received the crossing restriction in the clearance; I struggled to find the VOR on the approach plate (RNAV XXL). First; I did not know which 'VOR' the clearance was referencing. Second; the ZZZ VOR is not charted on the RNAV XXL approach plate. This caused a brief moment of confusion allowing me to get 'behind' the aircraft. Instead of arming NAV mode; I engaged APPR mode. The Captain objected and NAV mode engaged. Realizing that we were fast; high and becoming task saturated with automation; I elected to disconnect the aircraft and fly the approach manually. We used gear and flaps to reduce airspeed before I lowered the nose to put the aircraft onto a glide path. We intercepted the glide path from above and managed to establish a stabilized approach by approximately 2;200 feet AGL.  However; in my assumption that we were high on the approach anyway; I did not verify that we crossed the VOR at 2;500ft. The Captain made mention of this after the flight ended in the debrief. He believes that we may have actually crossed the VOR at 2;200 feet (300 feet low).I realize that my failure to thoroughly review and brief all necessary approach plates resulted in missing critical information that would have helped us avoid the threat of this unusual crossing restriction.

column121: Suffice it to say that after touching down on a good 3-point landing with full control of the aircraft; on the rollout my left sneaker briefly got stuck on the left rudder pedal and then it started to lose the footing as I did the left-right; left-right continuous tapping that is required to control a tailwheel on the ground. So; with the tapping that is required in a tailwheel; with each tap after getting stuck; the left foot came more and more off the pedal; but I had to keep tapping to control the aircraft. While I slowed the aircraft down a lot; when my foot finally popped off the rudder pedal; the plane did about a ¼ loop to the left and the left landing gear snapped (as it is supposed to do when it receives excess side stress) and the left aileron scraped the runway. There was no real other damage [to the aircraft]; or to the runway; or any other aircraft. There were no injuries at all. As soon as the plane stopped; I turned the master and mags (magnetos) off and rotated the fuel lever into the off position.

column121: Inbound to FXE on the FISEL SEVEN ARRIVAL. We were given a vector off of the arrival track and cleared to 3000 ft. Several miles Northwest of FXE on approximately a left downwind for the approach to Runway 09; as we were approaching level off at 3000 ft. we received a 'Traffic' warning from TCAS. Target was noted on the display at about 10 o'clock less than 1000 ft. below our altitude and well within the 3 mile circle; with a climbing indication (up arrow). As we continued on assigned heading at 3000 ft. the target appeared to be closing on our track and continuing to climb. Shortly after level off the TCAS issued a 'Climb' command. At this point I noted that the target was indicating 300 ft. below our altitude. I initiated the climb and called for the PM (Pilot Monitoring) to advise ATC of TCAS CLIMB. ATC acknowledged by calling out VFR traffic to us and stating that it was 'never above 2000 ft.' I acquired the target visually (low wing piston single) and observed it to be converging with our track from the left and close to our altitude. It is difficult to judge distance in this context; but we routinely pass aircraft that are 1000 ft. above/below our altitude. In my judgement the target in this case was much closer. We reached about 3600 ft. before TCAS went to a neutral vertical speed command; and the target aircraft was still indicating less than 1000 ft. below. By this time target was behind our aircraft and distance was increasing. Shortly after that TCAS issued a 'Clear of Conflict' and we returned to 3000 ft. The rest of the flight was uneventful; except to note that the same controller put us on a vector for the left base and then sent us beyond the final for Runway 09 while dealing with multiple VFR aircraft.

column121: This involves 3 near misses and being cutoff by the same pilot three times. I was at 5;500 feet with flight following coming south to north to reach ZZZ Airport from the ZZZ VOR. The wind was out of the north and the runway in use was Runway XX. As I was descending to fly past the airport and join the pattern with a 45 degree entry to downwind; I heard at least 3 planes in the pattern. Then; I heard a Bonanza behind me announce a straight in. I advised him that it was unwise given a full pattern; which would soon include me. I flew east of the airport and joined the Runway XX right downwind pattern with a standard 45 degree entry. The Bonanza then announced he would not go straight in. As I was in the pattern; downwind abeam the numbers; the Bonanza flew directly across my path. I was flying south in the downwind; and he was flying east. The Bonanza crossed about 200 feet above me and this was way too close to overfly me in the pattern. I announced downwind; base; and then final as I completed my pattern. I even called a redundant short final. As I was on short final; the Bonanza cut in front of me by taking a base entry. I was only about 400 feet above the ground; on short final. He was directly in front of me turning base to very short final. I called out for him to avoid and that I would be doing a go around. The Bonanza had overshot his approach and couldn't make the turn to the runway. Instead of landing; he then started to go around; pulling up directly into my path. I made an avoidance abrupt turn to the west to avoid colliding with the Bonanza. I then flew north; away from the field; and waited for the Bonanza to rejoin the pattern to land before rejoining the pattern well behind him and eventually landing. I have never turned in another pilot for making a mistake; but three mistakes in the pattern like this. Cutting me off; who had made multiple announcements to avoid a deviant pilot like this; still did not avoid an almost mid-air collision. I highly recommend investigation of the offending pilot. Among other things; he should know better than to try to fly a straight in to an extremely busy airport like ZZZ with multiple planes in the pattern and he should know the proper procedures for entering and flying a full pattern.

column121: Established on the localizer; we received a terrain warning from the GPWS. We received vectors and altitudes from Approach. We were monitoring our position and altitude with VOR/DME; GPS with radio altitude chart 10-1R; approach plate; localizer; terrain displayed on ND (Navigation Display) and visual reference. Our position and altitude of 8;200 ft. were certain with a current altimeter setting and all equipment was operational when we received the warning. I have been to this airport numerous times and had never received a terrain warning on this approach. After receiving the warning; I immediately initiated the escape maneuver as per company procedure. The warning went away immediately after initiating the maneuver. We reestablished automation and continued the approach. Being familiar with the area; I was familiar with the hill on approach but should never have received a terrain warning as we were at the appropriate altitude and position. I believe the EGPWS data base should be updated to prevent this from happening in the future.
column123: While being radar vectored for the  ILS approach at 8;200 ft.  received a very short GPWS warning 'pull up'. The Captain complied; but since the message only lasted about 1 second the aircraft never went above 8;300 ft. I'm not really sure why it happened. We were at the Minimum Vectoring Altitude assigned by ATC with terrain on ND (Navigation Display) selected and verified with the GPS symbol on the minimum vectoring chart. The correct altimeter setting was used. A normal approach and landing followed. We never informed ATC since we barely left the assigned altitude.We were at the correct altitude assigned by ATC with the correct altimeter setting. The only thing I can thing is that a hill with an elevation of about 7;100 ft. was just below us and just before intercepting the localizer for the ILS. Possibly warn pilots about the possibility of false GPWS warnings on the airport charts.

column121: Upon checking on with ATL approach; we were told to expect Runway 9R. Our initial brief was for Runway 8L because that is the typical Runway assignment when flying the CHPPR arrival. The FO (First Officer) briefed the Runway change. A subsequent Approach Controller cleared us to descend via the arrival and also that we were cleared for the ILS PRM [Precision Runway Monitor] Runway 9R approach. We had to again brief for the PRM approach because they were not advertising PRM's in the ATIS. We got a little behind the airplane and approach asked to start our descent and then handed us off to Tower. I can't recall if we were given a speed to maintain to the 5-mile fix. I checked on with ATL Tower and we were cleared to land on approximately 8-10 mile final. I recall the Approach Controller on the monitor frequency asking the aircraft behind us to slow to final approach speed. Less than a minute later; at about 1;200 feet. AGL; Tower instructed us to discontinue the approach; climb to 4;000 feet; and gave us headings. We maintained normal and stable approach at all times. We were told that the reason was because we slowed to our final approach speed at around a 6-mile final. I assumed then that we had insufficient spacing with the aircraft behind us. We were vectored for the same approach which concluded normally.We had an apparent loss of spacing on final approach which resulted in ATC cancelling our approach clearance. We were task saturated with Runway and approach changes; and with ATC instructions. We began to slow to our final approach speed sooner than ATC anticipated. We could have better anticipated the sequence of events when transitioning from the arrival to the approach.

column121: On the CHPPR 1 RNAV arrival assigned 180 knots after NAVVY. Shortly thereafter was cleared for the approach ILS 9R. Captured glide slope and continued the approach at 180 knots. Told to contact Tower and we were cleared to land. At 1 mile prior to final approach fix began to configure; slow down to 160 knots; and then VREF (Approach speed). Fully configured and approach checklist complete; stable approach. I recall a Controller asking the traffic behind us to slow down. At 1;200 feet AGL Controller told us to cancel landing clearance; fly runway heading; and then 15 right turn shortly thereafter.Go-around procedure completed and we asked reason for go-around. Controller said we slowed down 6 miles prior to runway. Note: Final approach fix is 5 miles from the runway and we do not recall a 180 knot assigned speed to the final approach fix. We were vectored back to the ILS 9R and landed; all else normal.Tower to request go-around; task saturation.

column121: While transitioning VNY Airport from the North I was told by ATC to remain on the east side of the 405 freeway while pattern traffic was restricted on the west side of the 405 (standard practice). The aircraft opposite direction never read back to remain on the west side of the 405 freeway and remained on the east side where I was causing a loss of separation. I took evasive action and immediately broke right around Aircraft Y which continued straight ahead. Had I not taken evasive action it would have been a midair collision. The pilot was a solo student pilot as well. I called the tower to ensure they spoke with the pilot and asked how to best mitigate this from happening again; we agreed the controller should have verified the read back and maybe in the future they will pay closer attention to closely separated planes.

column121: I was the PM (Pilot Monitoring) and the CA (Captain) was the PF (Pilot Flying). I believe the CA was hand flying. We were descending southbound on a base leg for a visual to RWY 26R in ATL; we encountered a drone at close range. We were 13 miles or so from the ATL VOR; just outside of the 12.0 DME arc of class B airspace; which exists between 3;000 - 12;500 ft. MSL. We were descending out of 5;200 ft. MSL or so; when I saw a dark-colored drone; northbound; at approximately 4;900 ft. We both saw it once it was at our 2:00 - 3:00 position; so evasive action was unnecessary; but it was within a wingspan or two from us. It was also 200 - 300 ft. below our altitude and we were descending. We reported it to ATL TRACON. They asked the pertinent questions and notified aircraft behind us; but; from what I heard before leaving the frequency; there were no other sightings.

column121: After landing Runway XX in ZZZ1; the flight attendants reported a 'smell' in the cabin. I continued taxing to the gate. Thinking the smell was from a normal source; engine exhaust; brakes; tires...  The APU bleed was opened to discount the engine bleed. The taxi in was of minimum time. After shut down was complete; I contacted the cabin the smell was then described as a 'dirty sock'. I had the flight attendant open an aft door; after verifying it was disarmed; and a cockpit window was opened to increase air flow through the cabin. The light passenger load was deplaned in minimum time. Maintenance was contacted and Smoke; Odor and Fumes Report was compiled with and sent to maintenance control via duty pilot due to IT issues.Short taxies to a gate are a high threat phase. I was focused on getting the plane to the gate safely. [Reporter suggested] accurate communication between cabin and cockpit crewmembers [during fume events].

I was turning left base for Runway XX; before I noticed that an aircraft was inbound for left base for the opposite runway which is Runway XY. I tried to communicate with him that I was turning for the final of Runway XX but I did not hear any respond. Then I continued for the final of Runway XX. Then I called out for the short final of Runway YY then I corrected twice that I was on the short final of Runway XX. Upon the completion of the touch and go I called out that I was on the upwind of Runway XX and then the aircraft called out for left base of Runway XY. I pitched down to try to avoid the aircraft that was on the left base of Runway XY. I tried to see and avoid but I still did not have the aircraft in sight. I pitched up to climb and I saw the aircraft approximately 150 feet above me and I avoided the midair collision. I communicated with the other pilot and asked him if he had seen me and he replied that he thought I was landing on Runway YY.

column121: Altitude report deviation. Doing the approach ILS [Runway] XX; we were cleared by ATC to descend to 3;500 ft. We set 2;500 ft. by mistake in the altitude window during instructions. While descending ATC contacted us due to the mistake. We realized we began to correct the altitude again to 3;500 ft.; so ATC gave us again a clearance to stay at 2;500 ft. We continue the approach without any incident from present position.Instruction were for the approach to maintain heading and altitude; after that we were cleared for the approach for ILS [Runway] XX. At that time the wrong altitude was set in the altitude window and we didn't realize the error until ATC notified for the deviation.We started to climb immediately climbing to the correct altitude; but ATC asked to stay at the altitude we were of 2;500 ft. without any problem.
column123: On approach for ILS [Runway] XX; ATC cleared us to descend to 3;500 ft. 2;500 ft. was mistakenly set in the altitude window when given our instructions. We descended to 2;500 ft. and ATC contacted us regarding the error. We started to correct our altitude to 3;500 ft. when ATC gave us a new clearance to remain at 2;500 ft. The ILS approach to [Runway] XX was executed without further incident.We received our instructions for the approach to maintain heading; altitude; and were cleared for the ILS [Runway] XX. During that moment the wrong altitude was placed in the altitude window and was not caught until ATC notified us of the deviation.As stated our reaction was to correct immediately by climbing to the appropriate altitude of 3;500 ft.; but we were given a new clearance to stay where we were at 2;500 ft.

column121: On arrival into ZZZ; we were instructed to turn to a heading and descend from 11;000 ft. to 3;000 ft. We began a descent using speed mode set at 250 kts. Just below 4;000 ft.; we received an Obstacle Caution message. During the process of turning the descent into a climb; which removed the caution message; we were instructed to climb to 4;000 ft. I was able to identify the obstacle; an antenna; below and to the right of the aircraft. We passed the antenna above and to the left. We leveled off at 4;000 ft. From there; we were vectored to a visual approach to [Runway] XX and proceeded with a normal visual approach and landing.Descending in speed mode likely caused the aircraft to approach the level off altitude earlier than ATC anticipated thus causing the higher closure rate to the obstacle; generating the caution message. Better terrain awareness could have prompted a shallower descent rate; perhaps using vertical speed mode. Querying ATC about the level off altitude; suggesting a higher bottom altitude; could also have reduced or eliminated the occurrence of the caution message.

column121: I was providing On the Job Instruction during the event. I had previously cleared Aircraft X to 17;000 feet prior to the trainee taking position. Another aircraft; Aircraft Y; was also receiving vectors for the approach and was given a descent to 14000 feet and a speed reduction to 210 knots. Aircraft X read back the instructions and then immediately Aircraft Y read back the instructions. On position; I did not hear the call sign of Aircraft X and thought that Aircraft Y read back the instruction a second time. It was clearly a different voice so I just thought that the pilots were confused as to who was talking and who was flying. A few moments later the Developmental recognized that Aircraft X was at 15;200 feet in a 15;500 foot Minimum Vectoring Altitude (MVA) and issued a low altitude alert. The pilot responded that it was blocked and the trainee gave an immediate climb to 16;000 feet. I recognized the the trainee did not use the full proper phraseology by not saying the 'MVA in your area is XXX;' but decided at this point the solution had been acknowledge and it was not worth correcting the phraseology in a third transmission. The aircraft continued to descend to 14;400 feet until we observed a climb. During that time the aircraft crossed into a 14;900 foot MVA; but being in a climb I decided it was not necessary to issue another safety alert since we knew it was being corrected.There was a bit of frequency congestion due to confusion about the weather; and by the time I was ready to issue a brasher warning the aircraft was on a 10 mile final. I felt it was best to let the aircraft fly and have Local Control or Ground Control issue the brasher warning; so I coordinated that with Ground Control.

column121: On Approach into ZZZ [airport] at night on the ZZZZZ arrival requested Runway XX for landing from ZZZ Approach. ZZZ Approach then instructed a 180 heading for the Runway XX. We could see we were getting on descent that was already going to be high to start with and requested a descent from ZZZ Approach. ZZZ let us down to 5;200 feet due to terrain in the area. This was not how we had briefed or planned to enter the visual approach. We had discussed and gone over the pages enroute and decided based on weather and winds and go-around factors that the coded visual to 15 would be the wisest choice. However; we thought that would start from ZZZZZ1. The vector ATC gave us pointed the plane toward the final approach fix for the Coded Visual Approach ZZZZZ2 bypassing the rest of the waypoints on the approach. The FO was flying the approach and used a high rate of descent to get down. Got a descent rate call on the approach but that was also expected due to the 3.2 degree glide slope. At 1;000 feet AGL I was 10 knots fast right at the edge of the stable and decent rate was 1350 ft/min. I thought I had til 500 feet to get stable since it was a visual approach. I should have either gone around; requested Runway Y or maybe executed an S-Turn. The plane landed uneventfully in about 3;500 feet using a Flaps 40 landing.This was my first time flying into ZZZ and the Captain had only flown in there once before in the previous year also has not flown much during the year due to leave. ATC gave us a vector toward the Final Approach Fix on the Coded Visual Approach which was not expected from reading the pages. Also; the Captain and I were under the belief we had until 500 feet to be stable due to the old SOPs. Furthermore; on the descent into ZZZ using the ZZZZZ3 arrival you are on with ZZZ1 Approach and do not get handed off to ZZZ Approach until 6;000 feet so you don't get a runway assignment very far out. Being at night first time into a short field with mountainous terrain was a cause as well.I think on the company pages it would help to paint a better picture if there were notes to not accept a vector to the visual and proceed direct to ZZZZZ for the visual approach to Runway X if that is the runway of choice. If entering on the ZZZZZ3 arrival a note letting the crew know they won't talk to ZZZ Approach til below 10;000 feet. Perhaps at night to not accept a visual approach to Runway X and use the ILS to Runway Z.

column121: We checked on with ZZZ Approach and requested the RNAV Z XX via ZZZZZ.  ATC advised unable due to traffic for Runway YY and told us to expect a visual to either [Runway] XYR or YY. The ATIS was reporting a high OVC and a low scattered layer; I think at 700 feet; we did not think we could accept a visual so we requested the RNAV Z YY. The Controller said we could expect that but we would need to be vectored; I said that was fine and also offered direct ZZZZZ1. We were vectored pretty far to the south east; over the river and past ZZZZZ1. We were finally given a vector to intercept final at or above 3;000 and cleared the approach. The CA tried to activate the approach and we discussed not activating RNPs; we had descended some while on the final course in VMC (I recall about 1;900 feet) and decided to cancel approach. We flew the missed and were vectored for the RNAV Y YY; that approach was uneventful.The CA and I had a full debrief and we learned a few things.The first issue was the Controller seemed unaware that we are unable to accept vectors to final on an RNP. After the go-around he asked the reason why and I told him that we can accept direct to or an intercept on a published leg but not vectors to final. He said thanks and I think he just did not know; but perhaps this should be known by all the controllers that work company aircraft.The second thing I learned is that I need to trust my gut feeling in these situations; once we were vectored past ZZZZZ1 I was anticipating that the Controller was going to vector us on to final; perhaps I could have asked him or requested the RNAV Y.The CA was ready to accept vectors to final and activate the approach; he began descending on the final course and I reminded him that we cannot accept that as it may cause issues with VNAV. The CA is a good pilot and sharp but is a newer captain and hadn't flown in over a month so the rustiness probably contributed.Overall I think we handled the situation as best we could and did not compromise safety in any way; especially with a missed approach not at the MAP which is something we rarely do on the line.

column121: After transitioning from an RNAV IMC approach to daytime visual references; stable; on-speed and glide-path; I briefly became distracted by an unexpected appearance of what turned-out to be a paved runway environment prior to the actual threshold. This resulted in a momentary increase in vertical speed outside stable parameters; which was corrected immediately upon verification of the PAPI and touch down zone. Limited lighting equipment in operation; and with that limited intensity of said lighting during daytime. Pavement surface area markings also difficult to discern.  The preceding aircraft made several broadcasts to the controller regarding the lighting availability and intensity. There is limited reference to a displaced threshold nor to any significant extension of such surface area published in either the 10-9 or company pages. Updating this might be considered helpful for pilots to properly frame the runway environment when transitioning from lower-visibility or lower-ceiling conditions.
column123: FO was PF. Flight and approach were normal and paced. We followed another aircraft that asked; maybe 3-4 times; if the 'lights were up' (relevant later) but that they saw the airport @ 1000'. 1000' was stable. Of note; I did not make the 500' call due to the proximity to mins but he did (possibly thinking I forgot it?). At 500' he was stable (sink 800 roughly). Minimums were set at 530' MSL (MDA + 50'; 434' AGL). Weather was worse than reported - right at minimums with rain. FO called 'runway in sight' and proceeded to push the nose over. I saw a sink rate on the VSI of approx 1350'. I immediately called it out to his attention which he then corrected; while getting a 'sink rate'. Since he was correcting and now had the (actual; more on that later) runway in sight; we proceeded to land in the touchdown zone. At the gate the review showed 1498' vertical speed(!).The night before this flight I commuted two legs and spent the night in a hotel. On my commute/hotel stay I reviewed several items and the SYCJ airport plates on Jepp FD. I even pulled up the NOTAMs for the airport so as not to be caught off guard. I cannot overstate how much prep work was done with this. No where in any document did I see information about a deconstructed runway threshold extending from R/W 06 @ SYCJ. On the JeppFD pro AMM 10-9 there is a LARGE blob of red; prior to R/W 06; but no mention what it is. There was mention of 'elevated' threshold lights and cables but nothing to indicate that a large runway threshold was prior to the R/W 06 threshold. This is important because what I believe happened is due to approaching minimums; the FO was 'antsy' to see the runway (as we all are); so he began looking outside. In his haste to 'find' the runway; he saw the deconstructed threshold (there were white X's; albeit degraded; from what I remember; as we got closer). Quite frankly; had he not called it; I most likely would have as well. Due to the weather conditions what one could make out was asphalt that looked like a runway; except we looked very high. He then pushed the nose over in an attempt to 'correct' for his perceived inaccurate vertical guidance occurring from this visual illusion which is what precipitated the increased vertical speed. R/W 06 solely has a pair of REILs and a PAPI...not your traditional ICAO lit approach so no threshold lights or a rabbit delineating the runway.The reason for submitting this report is to bring attention to the large displaced runway threshold in front of R/W 06. During visual conditions; this is immaterial; but due to the weather conditions being literally at minimums this can cause an inadvertent flight path correction. Of interest; I have found online some mention that the runway was expanded roughly 975m (3200') to 10;500. From what I can piece together it's possible the runway was expanded and now is being torn up again (or redone). So if true that (what looks like a displaced threshold) is 3000+' long. Again; in visual conditions that is not an issue...but with rain and low CIGs; it can pose a problem.

column123: While being vectored for a Visual Approach to Runway 21 at CHO on a left downwind; level; at an assigned altitude of 3;000 MSL we received a GPWS caution annunciation of: 'terrain pull up'. The flight conditions were day VMC; so both the Captain and I had the terrain we were overflying in sight.  Our vectored flight path took us over steep rising terrain. Upon hearing the GPWS annunciation; we verbally acknowledged the annunciation and the visual steep rising terrain we were overflying.  We maintained straight and level flight because we could visual ensure we were clear of all obstacles and/or terrain. Request ATC assigned an altitude of 4;000 MSL instead of 3;000 MSL when vectoring on this flight path for a visual approach to Runway 21 into CHO.

column121: Descending into ZZZ on a visual approach to Runway XXL; but following the guidance of the ILS.  A stable and uneventful approach. Upon landing we had a 6-7 knot tailwind. We landed longer than normal. I wouldn't say that we were outside of the touchdown zone; but it was very close. From my perspective the main wheels touched down prior to the end of the touchdown zone. We had floated a bit and I had to wait for the aircraft to lose that lift and start sinking into the runway before executing my landing flare. Elected not to go around and continue because nothing was unsafe we had a 10;996 foot runway and I elected to use max thrust reversers upon landing to slow us down safely.There was a few contributing factors. The main one being the tailwind on landing. Second was because of less travel due to COVID we sometimes have very light airplanes. Our landing weight was under 36;000 lbs being very light for the aircraft. So the lightweight along with the tailwind wasn't ideal. Lastly; company has recently within the last year or so updated our procedures where we add X knots to our straight in landing speeds regardless of the situation. Not having to add that extra X knots would have helped us carry less momentum into our final approach. We do bleed off the airspeed before touching down; although it's never perfect.The airport should have been landing in the other direction with the wind favoring the other runway; and it would also be more safe because of less terrain that we can't see at night. Also I should have thought about our extra light airplane and possibly requested the other runway. Also; It has been my opinion since it has started that airline go back to the normal landing numbers without adding extra 5 knots. And let the pilots decide when the extra airspeed is necessary depending on environmental factors.
column125: Air carrier First Officer reported a long landing possibly outside of the landing zone.  Reporter attributed discrepancy to possible company policy of adding speed to straight in landings and the aircraft being light in weight.

column121: Arriving Denver on the CLASH 3 STAR. Initially planning on 35R. We were given Runway 35L descending through 15;000 feet. Immediately after getting runway change; we were given a heading and cleared to 11;000 feet. ATC then cleared us for 9;000 feet. ATC asked if we had the airport in sight (we did) and turned us to a heading of 325 degrees and cleared us for the visual approach to 35L. That heading essentially aimed us directly at the FAF DYMON. I selected 7;000 feet in the altitude alert and continued the descent visually. Shortly after leveling at 7;000 feet; ATC advised us altitude alert; minimum altitude our sector 7;500 feet. I climbed up to 7;500 feet and remained there until glideslope interception. Flight continued uneventfully to DEN.I should have stayed at 9;000 feet until glideslope intercept.
column123: Arriving DEN on the CLASH 3 STAR. Initially planning on 35R was given Runway 35L descending through 15;000 feet MSL. Immediately after getting runway change; we were cleared to 11;000 feet. ATC then cleared us for 9;000 feet. ATC asked if we had the airport in sight; we responded in the affirmative; and ATC turned us to a heading of 325 degrees; and cleared us for the Visual Approach to 35L. That heading essentially aimed us directly at the FAF DYMON on the ILS for Runway 35L.The Pilot Flying (PF) selected 7;000 feet in the altitude window and continued his descent visually. Shortly after arriving at 7;000 feet; ATC advised us Altitude Alert and stated minimum altitude was 7;500 feet in our sector. The PF immediately corrected our altitude to 7;500 feet and we stayed at that altitude until glideslope intercept for the ILS to 35L. We landed uneventfully in DEN.When cleared for the visual; stay at your last safe altitude assigned until intercepting glideslope; or have better awareness of the minimum altitude in the sector you are flying. Another option would be for visual clearance to land including a minimum altitude if required.

column121: Aircraft X in ZZZ with pilot discrepancy 'F/CTL FLAPS FAULT. ECAM OCCURRED ON APPROACH WHEN MOVING FLAP LEVER FROM 1 TO 2. AFTER RECYCLING FLAP LEVER PER ECAM; FLAPS OPERATED NORMALLY.' Post flight report indicated flap jam in flight phase 6 cruise. 4th shift started troubleshooting per TSM and I was assigned to continue. TSM stated both flap actuators were required to be changed in the event of a jam with no faults due to known icing problem. Concurred with Maintenance Control that this was desired and safest step to continue. Gave all information to my crew chief; and supervisor and that the plane needs to be taken out of service until the parts are removed and replaced. Supervisor outlined that his boss argued that it's not the problem and proceeded to find any way to keep aircraft green. Both myself and work partner were pressured for well over the night to not ground Aircraft X and call it good. We raised the fact the TSM explicitly states with no faults both actuators must be changed. Due to ongoing peer pressure I reluctantly agreed to sign Aircraft X airworthy on the technicality that although the aircraft is reporting a flap jam; the phase of flight it occurred was in cruise and probably an indication error. However; prior to Date on Date-1 the exact fault and exact write up was entered. Corrective action from ZZZ2 base was a reset of LH #1 torque limiter. The TSM for this faults states that when resetting a flap torque limiter; the TSM refers to lubricate drive system; do a detail inspection and replace rotary actuator which have a tripped torque limiter. This was not done in ZZZ2 when an indicator was tripped; reset; and signed off. See [Logbook Entry]. I advised Maintenance Control and supervision of this oversight and advocated for replacing flap actuators.Event occurred because management doesn't take un airworthy as acceptable.Keep management where it belongs. Managing their supervisors and leave the technical aspect to the mechanics with the proper steps and actions from the manuals.
column123: Plane arrived in ZZZ with Flight CTL flap slat fault; was written up previous day for same thing. Contract maintenance with Maintenance Control guidance troubleshot fault - no faults where found; we ran engines and cycled the flaps per Maintenance Control request; no faults occurred. Approaching ZZZ1 at 2;000 ft. at flaps 2 selected we had a ECAM FLT CTL FLAPS FAULT message. Did go around and followed vectors to 3;000 ft.; advised ATC and followed QRH; We did all steps but were both misinterpreted the checklist. Should have put lever to flaps 3; instead of flaps full and calculated the wrong approach speed as we now believe we misinterpreted the max speed chart for approach speed. 190 kts. instead of 165 kts. Report said we were 185 kts. I believe. On the approach I knew something was not right; thought about going around but was worried about the fuel time. During approach we had full slats no flaps. We requested longer runway (12;000 ft.); Landing was normal I let plane slow to 100 kts. before applying breaks. Never heated up beyond 150 [degrees] more or less. After firefighters looked over the airplane we proceeded to gate. Should have referred to SOP in performance for added info but thought we were right and worried about fuel. I have done this procedure in training; I ran the checklist and made the mistake that I was warned about. I believe frustration with a reassignment; irritated with a broken airplane (multiple problems) that was delivered to us and worried about fuel during checklist all added to my distraction and lead me to misinterpreting the checklist.Slow down slow read checklist slower; have more fuel to run all the way through.

column121: Flying from ZZZ to ZZZ1 in VMC/VFR. I was tracking the RNAV (GPS) RWY XX approach from ZZZZZ to the airport. I made 3 position reports that I was on final at various other distances using GPS provided information to Runway XX.I heard Aircraft Y approaching from the north making his announcements; from airport; downwind and then right base for a short approach. At this time I was 3 miles from the runway and made my 3rd report. I expected Aircraft Y to appear ahead of me and that I would spot on base to final or earlier.I am looking for this airplane; but I am not seeing it. At about 1 mile from the airport at approximately 700 feet MSL; I see the other aircraft just to the right; behind; and above my aircraft when he was reporting being on final (looked like he hadn't finished rolling out on final). I said that 'I am ahead of you'; then he replied 'I got you'; and then announced he would do a '360 turn.'I landed uneventfully taxied to the fuel island and shutdown; and a bit pissed at this point. Aircraft Y landed and taxied behind me for fuel too. He never exited his aircraft so we did not speak.I believe the other pilot was trying to beat me to the fuel island with his 'short approach' which must not have been all that short; considering where we met up at. I think he should have used better judgement and extended his downwind. My airplane is equipped with Lo Presti Boom Beam landing and taxi lights; and a precise flight pulse light. The effect is that even during day these bright lights which are alternating on the wings makes my aircraft highly visible. I always fly with these lights on; and they were on.My mistake was trusting the other pilot's judgement; that his short approach would have gotten him to the airport much sooner than reality. I should be more proactive and suggested that he extend his downwind; or just abort my approach.The other factor is another airplane was in the pattern; and was turning crosswind to downwind around the time of the near miss.My aircraft was a bit faster than he may have anticipated; as I was still slowing down with speed brakes deployed; but at time of incident was around 80 knots indicated.

column121: It has been hard to fly during the pandemic; and the aircraft had been in for annual inspection; so I had not flown for two months. I went out for practice. I chose the back-course approach at ZZZ; as it can be challenging. Also; during recurrent training in Month; we had noticed some odd autopilot behavior when flying this approach. My aircraft has Garmin avionics but these are mated to an older analogue autopilot.By the time I was told to switch to ZZZ1 Approach; I was still at 12;000 feet and quite close to ZZZZZ intersection on the approach. When I gave my request for the approach; the controller gave me vectors to circle back to rejoin the course while descending to 4000 feet. She then cleared me for the approach. I believed she said 'Maintain 4000 until established...'; but in our later discussion she said her instruction was 'Maintain 4000 to ZZZZZ.' I believe I read back the former. In any case; when back on extended approach course; but a short distance before ZZZZZ; I began to descend further. The chart showed 3000 feet for the segment between ZZZZZ and ZZZZZ1; which is the FAF and 5.8NM from ZZZZZ. My GPS displayed an altitude of 3100 at ZZZZZ; which I believe is its indication of a safe altitude. So; I had set altitude capture for 3100 feet and was descending. The controller alerted me that she had expected 4000 feet to ZZZZZ. While we were discussing this; the autopilot failed to capture and the descent continued to 2900 feet. The controller told me to switch to tower.On my first attempt to call tower at ZZZ; I had not hit the switch correctly and was still with ZZZ1 approach. At the same time; I noticed the autopilot taking me off course for the approach and interrupted it while calling the tower. We were in the descent within the FAF by this time. The tower controller told me I was low for the course and left of course; and he asked if I was flying the approach visually instead of using the Back Course. Due to the autopilot issue; I was by that time flying visually. It was a clear VFR day; and at no time was I in close proximity to terrain or other aircraft. The problem arose for several reasons. The pilot (me) was rusty and needed practice. It was a mistake to do the more challenging BC approach as the first one. (After this event; I did the missed approach as planned at ZZZ and did two fine approaches at ZZZ1 and ZZZ2.) Also; I believe there anomalies with my avionics set-up and back-course approaches; and I should not have been trying to troubleshoot that as a single pilot; especially being a bit out of practice. Another contributor was my misunderstanding of the controller's instruction; along with using the 3000 foot altitude indicated on the chart for that segment ZZZZZ to ZZZZZ1) and the 3100 foot altitude indicated as the target for ZZZZZ on my GPS for the altitude I was trying to attain. All of these issues increased workload; which is better handled by an in-practice pilot (which is why I had gone out to practice). A final recommendation from this experience is that I will avoid flying BC approaches with my autopilot until I can do a flight with a competent pilot and avionics technician aboard. The BC approaches are quite rare; and almost all have GPS overlays nowadays. So; they are not really a high priority; I actually chosen this one to be challenging and give me practice monitoring the autopilot and taking over if it acted strangely. But given the altitude misunderstanding; resulting discussion; and then the strange autopilot behavior; it was a bit choppy. I flew the airplane in a safe manner throughout (there was no terrain issue along my path); but my adherence to published instructed and published altitudes on the approach was poor.

column121: There was a pair of arrivals staggered by about 0.5-0.75 miles; with the Runway 28 Right arrival preceding Runway 28 Left arrival.  There was a solid 16-32 knots crosswind from the north depending on what altitude they were at.  Runway 28 Left glide slope is 2.85 degrees while Runway 28 Right is 3.00 degrees.  In this very rare set of circumstances; the left arrival is getting a rough ride form the right arrival. Aircraft X  [the first E175] was cleared to land Runway 28 Right and Aircraft Y [the second E175] was cleared to land Runway 28 Left. Aircraft Y was about 2.3 mile final when they advised going around.  I gave them runway heading and 3;000.  I advised them of traffic that departed north and stopped climbing in their southbound turn; an A320; and got visual separation transferred to the pilot.  I then coordinated the go around and was asked the reason.  I said I assumed unstable approach but was not sure.  The aircraft was coming up on level flight and so I confirmed the reason.  The pilot said they were hit by wake turbulence.  I shipped them to Departure; then coordinated with their coordinator the reason.  We logged it in the Tower log; but did not write an MOR (mandatory occurrence report). When the airplane came back around and landed; I inquired about the event.  They said that the airplane yawed to the right and then began rolling to the left so they went around.  That sounds very unsettling to me; however they made it out to fly another approach. No recommendations; it sounds like the pilots did what they were trained to do to save the ship.

column121: Landing [Runway] XXL on final; aircraft in front of us reported a 10 knot loss. The FO was pilot flying at the time and over corrected for the wind; causing us to descend below glideslope very quickly on short final.  As soon as I was calling go-around the GS aural warning came on.  The FO executed a go-around. Because of the difficult wind conditions and seeing we were already at 2;100-2;200 LBS; I decided that I would land the second attempt as I did not want to get into a fuel situation. We were in agreement with this.  On short final for the second attempt; with me as pilot flying; at about 150 feet; we had the wind-shear caution alert and proceeded to execute our second go-around. ATC gave us instructions to set up for [Runway] XXR but due to fuel concerns I said 'Unable' and declared min fuel. Also; I added that if we did have to take any delays we would be declaring emergency fuel. ATC set us up immediately for a close pattern for [Runway] XXL. On this attempt; I stayed slightly high as the wind shear seem to be the worse right at the tree line at about 150 feet.  On this third attempt; we landed successfully and taxied back to the gate.Unstable approach recognized by me and a wind shear caution message for the second attempt.Inexperienced FO who had only flown once since May 2020 and challenging weather conditions with wind shear.Two go-arounds and declared min-fuel.Have pilots who have been out a while receive more recurrent training.  It was evident due to lack of recency the FO was struggling with the conditions that day.  Also; be vigilant on [Runway] XXL as I have had issues on windy days before where the tree line ends and the field begins. With the second attempt we had a 15 knot increase with the wind shear caution message.
column123: I was the Pilot Flying. The Wind shear changes in combination with the amount of time since I've been flying in the airplane were contributing factors. There were changes of +/- 15kts and on short approach I went slightly below glide slope and then the Wind shear happened which caused the Glideslope aural warning to sound. The Captain called for a Go-Around which we executed. Once we were at a safe altitude and being vectored back for another approach the we exchanged controls and the Captain became the Pilot Flying. As we were coming in on short approach the Wind shear Aural Caution message sounded so we executed a second go-around. ATC told us that they were going to vector us around for Runway XXR; but were now low on fuel and declared min fuel. They instead kept us close pattern for Runway XXL and we landed safely. Upon landing we had 1;450 LBS of fuel. We taxied to the gate with no issues.Glideslope Aural Warning System on first attempt and Wind shear warning system on second attempt.Wind shear affecting experience level.Go-Around executed twice.Keep crews more recent on flying or more time in simulator if it's been a while.

column121: I departed ZZZ at ZA:10 local on an IFR flight plan to ZZZ1 in [a rented aircraft]. A preflight inspection at ZZZ revealed no items of concern; and the brakes responded as expected when I tested them upon first movement from the tie down spot and throughout my taxi. The weather was VMC for the duration of the flight; and the weather I picked up on the ZZZ1 ATIS 15 minutes prior to arrival was winds 320@7kts; 9SM visibility; sky clear. I informed ZZZ Approach I would take the visual approach to Runway XX; at which point they cleared me for the visual approach and handed me off to ZZZ1 Tower. Tower requested me to continue on course to fly a left base for Runway XX and report three miles west of the field. Upon my reporting three miles west of the field; they cleared me to land runway XX. Upon nearing the extended centerline; I turned 90 degrees left and aligned myself on an approximately three mile final for Runway XX while at approximately 1;200 feet MSL and 90 KIAS. I completed my final approach and before-landing checks; including pumping the brakes to check for pressure; and crossed the runway threshold at approximately 65 KIAS with 30 degrees of flaps. Immediately upon touching down; the aircraft yawed violently to the left. I applied full right rudder deflection; which somewhat arrested the left yawing and increased the radius of the turn. However; the left main wheel by that time had entered the grass adjoining the runway; further aggravating left yaw. The aircraft continued to rotate about the left wheel until it came to a stop facing approximately due east approximately 75 feet from the west edge of the runway. After ensuring there was no immediate danger of fire or structural damage; I checked the main gear tires out the windows to see if one had blown; but they appeared to be OK. I advised Tower I would try to taxi back to the runway and proceed to the ramp. Upon applying throttle; the aircraft pivoted about its pitch axis into a slight nose-down attitude; which ceased upon decreasing throttle. I tried twice more to increase throttle to induce forward movement; but the aircraft continued to pitch forward when throttle was increased and pitch backward when throttle was decreased. At that point; I informed Tower I would need a tug. An airport crew came with a tug and attached a towbar to the aircraft. Upon attempting to pull the aircraft forward; the tug was not able to move it and subsequently became stuck in the ground. The airport crew then tried using a pickup truck to pull the tug while the tug pulled the aircraft; but the pickup truck became stuck. A 4wd pickup truck came and was able to remove the tug and the first pickup truck back to the runway. The airport crew used a shovel to dig the front wheel of the aircraft out of the ground; as it had become stuck during the previous attempts to taxi back onto the runway. They then attached the towbar to the 4wd pickup truck and were able to get the aircraft moving and onto the runway. I was then able to taxi the aircraft to the ramp under its own power; but noticed it had difficulty tracking in a straight line without additional brake inputs. Upon reaching the ramp; I did a quick inspection of the brake assemblies and discovered a metal-on-metal scraping noise when moving the aircraft back and forth by hand and no movement of the calipers when the toe brakes were actuated. At this point; I felt it was unsafe to continue operating the aircraft and arranged for a mechanic to inspect it the following morning. I called the owner; and he told me; 'that is why you shouldn't take that airplane places on a [weekend] afternoon - no one is around to fix it.' The mechanic inspected the brakes and found that the caliper slider pins were void of lubricant and rusted frozen; one brake lining was missing its rivets due to them rusting away; and several fasteners were loose. He replaced the linings on both slides; lubricated the assemblies; and torqued all the fasteners. I visually inspected the brakes; tested them on the ramp and during runup; and was satisfied that the aircraft was safe to operate. I departed ZZZ1 and had an uneventful IFR flight and landing back at ZZZ.In the future; I will add more thorough visual inspection of the brake assemblies to my preflight inspections; along with checking to make sure the calipers move when the brakes are actuated. I will also land prepared for a problem such as stuck brake; blown tire; or FOD and be more ready to immediately lift back off if safe to do so.

column121: On flight XX; ZZZ to ZZZZ FO pre- loaded FMC with arrival ZZZZZ XA with Runway XXR.  Before descent checklist (6 hours) later briefed STAR and arrival into ZZZZ for both XXL and XXR. ZZZZ ATIS stated both XXL and XXR were in use. Obtained landing data for both runways. Approaching ZZZZZ we got vectors to ZZZZZ1 with expect XXL. On descent ATIS was calling for gusty winds on final from moderate to severe broken clouds at 2;500 feet. We talked about the winds and before we got to ZZZZ1 we got vectors and a decent to XXL we talked about the winds and forgot to change the FMC to XXL. On vectors to XXL with the approach mode armed we intercepted the LOC to XXR. We still were not aware of the error until the controller told us we were lined up for XXR and wanted to give us a heading of 160. We knew traffic was very light so asked if we could continue to XXR and was cleared to continue and land XXR. Landed without incident.
column123: Upon returning to the flight deck from break; I was briefed on the ZZZZZ XA arrival and ILS XXL approach. ZZZZ ATIS broadcast that both XXL and XXR were in use. Runway data for both XXL and XXR were had been obtained earlier. Prior to descent we completed the descent checklist. At the FMC's; radios; item on the descent checklist; I intended to check the FMC to verify the approach and checked the item complete; but was interrupted by a radio call and never completed it. I did not catch the error that the FMC had ILS XXR programmed instead of ILS XXL; which had been briefed and anticipated. On the descent we were cleared the ZZZZZ XA Arrival to ILS XXL. Having briefed the ILS XXL; and believing we had ILS XXL programmed; no changes were made to the FMC. We did not catch the error until receiving radar vectors to the final approach. We were given a base leg and shortly after a vector to intercept. The turn to final seemed late and the aircraft but captured and lined up on the centerline. At that point; ATC gave us a 'right turn to 160' and then stated we were lined up for XXR and that we had been cleared for ILS XXL. He said there was no traffic for XXR and asked if we wanted to continue. We were at least 4 miles from the FAF so we accepted the approach clearance. We were able to review the approach noting the LOC identifier; changing the minimums and reviewed the Missed Approach procedure prior to descent on the GS. A stable approach was flown from the FAF to the runway for landing and was uneventful; as was the rollout and taxi-in.On the ATIS; gusty winds were noted and moderate; possibly greater; turbulence mentioned. This was a topic of discussion during the briefing and after. We briefed the cabin crew to have the cabin secured early and to take their seats as soon as possible after the chimes for the sterile cockpit notification. The aircraft was slowed early and we had begun configuring early as well so as to be stable early. The discussion was possibly an additional distraction during the briefing and Descent checklist.While we were able to comfortably get set up for the approach that was incorrectly loaded in and able to fly a stable approach; we should have asked for vectors to a new approach in order to properly brief prior to the initiation of the approach phase. We were lucky there was no traffic conflicts as there was little if any traffic due to time of day and the current state of airline traffic due to COVID.

column121: Fumes on flight ZZZ-ZZZ1.  This report is to highlight the procedures used to resolve fume events. It isn't to report the fume event. In summary; after the flight and cabin crew smelled fumes during flight and after landing; Maintenance worked to resolve the problem. After performing various procedures; Maintenance said the problem was fixed three separate times. Each time; the flight and cabin crew pointed out that the fumes were still present. Even so; a manager from another department was brought in to see if she smelled any fumes-she did not. So; as the maintenance policy allows; the aircraft was returned to service; overruling six professional and very experienced flight and cabin crewmembers' specific and detailed descriptions of the characteristics of the still present fumes' smell; the locations in the flight deck and cabin where the fumes were detected and the air supply vent locations from which the fumes were emanating. Approximately an hour later; a flight crew reassigned to ferry the aircraft to ZZZ2 started the #1 Engine; smelled the fumes and refused the aircraft. The fume event began during descent below at 5;000 ft.; at 15 miles from ZZZ1 airport when I noticed musty smelling fumes on the flight deck coming from the eyeball vents on both the overhead and instrument panels. I recognized the smell from previous experience (along with information supplied by both Air Carrier and the Pilots Association); as being from petroleum based fluid being heated and entering the air conditioning system. Being close to the ground and landing; I wanted to find a solution quickly so I closed the flight deck vents to see if there was any change. There was not; so I reopened them thinking that if the fumes were transient that air flow might move the fumes through and off of the flight deck. I also started to feel slightly nauseated so I donned my O2 mask. After donning my O2 mask the nauseated feeling subsided somewhat. Landing was made without incident. At taxi speed I opened my window; turned off the air conditioning packs and taxied to the gate. I then spoke with the flight attendants to see if any of them had smelled the fumes. The flight attendant at Door 3L said that shortly before landing some passengers asked him about a smell in the area. Once they had pointed it out; he said he had then smelled the fumes. No other flight attendants reported smelling the fumes at their jumpseat locations during flight. I wrote up the fumes in the AML (Aircraft Maintenance Logs); then completed and submitted a smoke; odor; fume form. When Maintenance arrived I described the smell of the fumes to them. None of the mechanics with whom I spoke (four I believe); smelled the fumes. From then until when the flight was cancelled at XA:00; one mechanic (of the four); acknowledged that he smelled the fumes 'slightly' and only one time. During this time; Maintenance completed procedure(s) three separate times and after each time said that they didn't smell the fumes and wanted to sign off the AML. Each time; my flight and cabin crew pointed out the smell of the fumes in the cabin and flight deck; so Maintenance continued their procedures. At some point after those three attempts to resolve the problem; one mechanic told me that the aircraft was going to be there until at least XB:00 that day. Soon after that; a manager from another department was asked to walk through the cabin and see if she smelled the fumes.  After a bit I checked my schedule and saw that we were reassigned to take that same aircraft back to ZZZ at XC:00 on [Date]. That was consistent with the last I heard from the mechanic who said the aircraft was going to be there until at least XB:00 that night. Around XD:00; I saw that another crew had been reassigned to fly our aircraft to ZZZ2 at XE:00. I texted the Captain on that flight to be sure he was aware of the seriousness of the problem. He replied; stating that after they had started the #1 Engine; the fumes returned. He did not accept the aircraft. I then called [Pilots Association] Safety for some guidance. I am concerned because; despite all the procedures in place to determine whether the source of the fumes is eliminated; the methods to determine whether the fumes are present or remain:1. Expose maintenance personnel and possibly other people to the fumes and their potential harmful effects (isn't that what we're trying to avoid?).2. Allow using the olfactory senses of random individuals that; (a) may not be able to detect the smell of the fumes; (b) have never smelled the fumes and/or; (c) have no knowledge of the seriousness of the smoke; odor and fume events and their potential and documented health threats.3. Allow the complaints and input of those that do smell the fumes to be ignored or overruled by any randomly chosen person that does not smell them. It seems that the goal should be that if just one person still smells the fumes; the problem isn't solved and the aircraft won't be released to flight status.

column121: I took the plane out to practice maneuvers etc. and was out for about an hour and a half.  There were a lot of planes in the area when I returned from the north and was asked to fly down the east side of the valley to enter a right downwind for Runway XX and to report midfield.  I repeated the instruction and entered the right downwind for Runway XX.  As I was about to report at mid-field; the controller stated that he saw me and cleared me to land on Runway XX.  A couple of minutes later; I believe he also stated that a plane was holding short for takeoff until I landed.I passed around the back of the landmark; made the right turn to the runway heading and saw the runway.  I then finished my landing checklist and proceeded to land on what I thought was Runway XX. Just as I was about two feet off the runway a second or so before the wheels touched; I heard the tower say to 'go-around' just as my wheels touched the ground. This was immediately followed by the tower stating-- [aircraft call sign] cleared to land Runway XX.I have no one to blame; nor any condition to blame for my mistake--I was shocked beyond belief that I had landed on the wrong runway and as many times as I've heard other pilots talk about it happening to them; I honestly thought it would never happen to me; as I have not had a single incident in 10 years of flying.  I have only one possible answer for my error and that is the 'confirmation bias' I've read about and heard discussed in the FAA courses and webinars I've taken.I take full responsibility for my error.  To say I'm embarrassed is an understatement; but one good thing has come of this:  I'm now acutely aware of the reality of confirmation bias from this first-hand experience and will definitely be intensely focused on confirming and reconfirming my runway prior to landing forever more.

column121: We had planned on doing a touch and go at 33V on Runway 22. After we had touched down we raised the flaps and began to add power. While we were rolling at about 65 mph; a pickup truck with a snow plow on the front of it pulled out onto the runway; and started coming down Runway 4 head at us. I didn't believe we had enough room to stop; so I kept the throttle in for full power and tried to get airborne so we could side step the runway. The airplane was not able to break ground effect due to the high density altitude. The vehicle drove off the side of the runway and we were able to continue down the runway for takeoff. We had made standard radio calls on the CTAF frequency.
