column121: Corporate flight to ZZZ [Airport]. Approximately one hour from arrival airport we had multiple route changes. Final route change was ZZZ [VOR] VXXX ZZZ1 [VOR] ZZZ [Airport]. Had difficulty programing Proline Fusion with new route so I built each intersection on VXXX and reconfirmed route.Given a descent to 7;000 ft. by TRACON arrival. We had discussed and programed RNAV (GPS) Y Runway XX. We didn't ask TRACON for the approach we planned. TRACON cleared us direct to ZZZZZ [Intersection] for the ILS Runway XX. I had reviewed the ILS and knew ZZZZZ Intersection wan on the approach. I had the ILS programed in the number two Navigation unit under the number two FMS RNAV.I gave Captain a heading left to intercept the ILS as I had raw data up. Captain was not set up for the ILS and tried to load frequency and inbound course. Now we are high. I pointed the airport our to Captain. Captain had set up on his ILS navigation display 065 inbound.Speed was 250 kts. I told Captain to slow down. ATC asked if we were too high on approach. Captain advised we could make the field. Switched to Tower. I saw we were too high in altitude and speed to make a stable approach. I advised the Tower that we would like a visual approach with a right down wind for Runway XX. Captain was in agreement. Twice more I advised Captain to slow down. I put my hand over his hand and pulled throttles to idle. Captain brought throttle back up. I believe we were at 3;000 to 4;000 feet descending. Tower instructed us to make a right down wind to Runway XX. Again; I advised we are way too fast. Captain started his right turn. Tower advised us about an aircraft ahead of us. I am not positive; but I thought Tower said to climb to 4;000 feet. I did hear tower state pattern altitude was 2;000 feet. We picked up aircraft visually coming right to left as we were making the right turn to downwind. We received a RA but had visual sighting. I think the aircraft was about 1;000 feet horizontal and about 200 vertical feet above us. I advised Captain twice I had the aircraft. He handed me the aircraft and I slowed the aircraft and started to configure the plane for landing. Once configured for landing I gave aircraft back to Captain. I took aircraft back a second time when I did not think he was quite aware of where we were in relation to Runway XX. We were advised of VFR aircraft and cleared to land. Captain was preoccupied looking for VFR traffic. I brought aircraft around to final on the PAPI. Gave the aircraft back to Captain and asked if he was OK to land aircraft. Normal landing.I have been a Captain for a Major airline and told pilot and mechanic I wished to have debrief to address the errors we made with loss of situational awareness and getting behind on the aircraft.The proline fusion avionics system I believe has bugs to be worked out. Almost all numerical fonts are the same size. with all the color enhanced background if you do not know specifically where to look nothing stands out. None of the National flight training facilities have a simulator that has the Fusion avionics system. Flight Safety ZZZ2 has a stand-alone practice system that has no real time capability. We are pretty much self-taught to use the Fusion Proline Avionics system. To change an approach with the Fusion system also take time to set up an instrument approach means if you are in Navigation FMS mode you need to go to heading select. go to the VOR/ILS. set up the frequency and inbound course. then go back to FMS/NAV mode. I believe Captain made his error in the heat of the moment by putting the reverse course inbound with the tail of the VOR/ILS needle.My errors where I knew we were getting behind the aircraft and thought I could talk the Captain through what we needed to do verses taking the aircraft sooner.I have learned in Corporate flying that standardization in aircraft management means there is no standardization if you work for a small fight department. Especially as a contract pilot. This was without a doubt the most difficult flight I have had in many years. Pilots have great attitudes and always seek to learn and improve. I certainly learned but wish to not learn by making and compounding errors.

column121: Coming to ZZZ on the ILS [Runway] XXR; we were continually slowed by ATC for spacing of traffic ahead and behind.  At about 1;200 MSL on glide path still IMC Tower told us to go-around.  I was PF and hit GA button and advanced power levers.  We went through missed approach procedure; but as flight guidance was attempting to be set the aircraft was also capturing the level off of 2;000 MSL so took a little longer to complete.  Meanwhile we were a little late on turning to 195 heading.  ATC queried us as we were in the turn.  We had also climbed to 2;180 feet before stopping and returning back to 2;000 immediately.  After we got settled ATC vectored us back to ILS with no further issues.  ATC told us they had to send us around because aircraft on final for parallel Runway XXL behind us had gotten .99 miles from us.  Spacing needed to stay a min of 1 mile.I should have paid more attention to altitude and started turn to 195 even if flight guidance wasn't quite set yet.  Also pulled back power sooner or not add as much to avoid going through altitude.
column123: During our arrival into ZZZ; when told to run base (heading approximately toward ZZZZZ [Intersection]); we were slowed to 170 KIAS.  We were maintaining 4;000 feet throughout base turn and intercept turn onto final.  Once established on final (approximately 20 NM from ZZZZZ1 [Intersection]) we were slowed further to 160 KIAS; with traffic approximately 4 NM in front of us.  Once we intercepted the glideslope; we maintained approximately 1;000 feet above the preceding traffic; but gained to within 3 NM distance.  Meanwhile; ATC vectored a airline flight onto final for [Runway] XXL close behind us.I told the First Officer (PF) to potentially fly a half-dot high on the glide slope to avoid any potential wake from the preceding aircraft.  Once we reached approximately 10 NM from ZZZZZ1 [Intersection] we were slowed further to 150 KIAS.As we continued down the approach; the TCAS showed us to be approximately 2.5 NM from the preceding traffic; with the traffic behind us close in trail (unable to tell the specific distance).  At approximately 600 AGL; ZZZ Tower issued a Go-Around; with nonstandard missed approach instructions (Right turn heading 195; climb and maintain 2;000).  The First Officer initiated the Go-Around and the Captain (PM) proceeded to re-program the Flight Guidance.Due to the light passenger load; the subsequent climb rate caused the Flight Guidance to immediately switch from a Climb indication to an Altitude Capturing indication; which caught the Captain off-guard; and he tried to re-configure the Flight Guidance back to a Climb indication.  The Flight Guidance again sequenced to an Altitude Capture indication.  At this point; the Captain (PM) noticed the aircraft was off course to the left approximately 35 degrees and was rapidly approaching 2;000 feet.  The Captain brought this to the attention of the First Officer while also simultaneously acknowledging ATC stating 'Turn right heading 195.'  The Captain also prompted the First Officer to reduce the power setting; as the airspeed was approaching maximum flap speeds.  The missed approach up to leveling off at 2;000 feet was all hand-flown (i.e. not with the autopilot).Once the aircraft was established on the appropriate heading; altitude; and configuration; ZZZ Approach vectored the flight around for a second attempt at the ILS 16R; which occurred without further incident.  In researching the First Officer's seniority; he has been here less than X years (Date).  After debriefing the flight; he stated that that was his first Missed Approach / Go-Around in the actual aircraft EVER.Since we had been slowed down significantly early during the approach; we knew we could expect to be sandwiched in between other aircraft.  However; we did not know that ATC needed only 1 mile separation between aircraft on parallel ILS approaches (told to us by ATC during our second attempt).  There was no mention of spacing requirements on our first approach.Due to 'COVID loads;' the aircraft type climb rates are very good; requiring steeper than normal attitudes so as to not exceed flap speeds.  This factor; combined with the First Officer's relative inexperience with missed approaches; caused a momentary loss of situational awareness by the First Officer.This required the CA to not only re-configure the flight guidance for a missed approach; but also ensure the First Officer did not over speed the flaps; communicate with ATC; and ensure the aircraft was following appropriate flight guidance (which was a non-standard missed approach).Also contributing to the loss in situational awareness was the altitude at which ATC issued the Go-Around:  600 AGL.  This caused a significant startle factor in both the Captain and First Officer.  Had ATC mentioned anything about a potential missed approach; we would have been much more prepared.Last-minute ATC-issued Go Around instructions (below 1;000 AFE); caused by inadequate spacing with simultaneous parallel approach aircraft.RECOMMENDATIONS:1.  ATC NEEDS to let crews know 'be ready for a missed approach' if there is potential for a spacing issue.  We were given our Go-Around instructions at 600 AGL.  This is a very high-risk area to issue a Go-Around; when spacing throughout the entire approach was tight.  Some sort of notification to us would have primed for a potential Missed Approach and therefore could have avoided any loss of situational awareness and subsequent heading / altitude deviation.2.  STOP ISSUING NON-STANDARD MISSED APPROACH INSTRUCTIONS.  The ILS [Runway] XXR's Standard Missed Approach instructions are to fly runway heading to 900 feet; then climb on heading 195 to intercept the ZZZ 210 degree radial; maintain 2;000 feet.  This is VERY similar to what we were assigned (heading 195; climb to 2;000 feet).  However; when we're given nonstandard missed approach instructions; not only do we have to interpret what it is ATC actually told us; but also program that into the Flight Guidance.  This programming is CONTRADICTORY to what our normal Flight Guidance programming procedures are.  Had ATC simply said 'fly the published missed approach;' that could've aided in our appropriate handling of the aircraft.LESSONS LEARNED:Don't trust ZZZ ATC to not screw up.  If spacing looks tight; brief a missed approach; even if it's done on the Final Approach Course.  Also; do not trust your fellow crew member to fly a perfect Missed Approach.  Watch them like a hawk for course; airspeed; and altitude management; and IMMEDIATELY speak up if there is even a minor deviation.

column121: The problem arose when I went to log the flight and found out that I was not current. Contributing factors (beside my inattention) could be that with COVID; both in general and having had it; I have not flown the amount that I normally would. However; I should have been more alert to my 'currency.' I file IFR for every trip whether VMC or IFR and so I filed IFR; again without checking for currency. For corrective actions I have set dates in my calendar quarterly to make sure I am on track for currency.Concerning my medical currency; I have two years 'under my belt' with Basic Med. I went in for my annual physical and gave the forms to my doctor. However; when it was discovered that I was not under Basic Med; I called the Doctor's office and his response was that he remembers the conversation but not filling out any forms. He had done my last Basic Med so I left it in his hands to complete. I understand that it was/is my responsibility to follow up on this; but I did not. Again; I have placed a reminder in my calendar both to renew my Basic Med in two years and then a week later to follow up with my doctor.

column121: I was sitting front seat; doing my tail wheel training in the Citabria practicing touch and goes. I was practicing my second ever short field landing in this aircraft and as I was applying breaking to the aircraft I heard my instructor telling me to press harder on the brakes; as we were nearing the end of the landing. I then applied brakes to the point of the tail wheel rising up and then the propeller struck the runway.
column123: We were practicing short field landings for training. After landing the student started applying brakes very lightly. I prompted the student to increase braking action and the student suddenly applied aggressive braking action. The tail began to rise and I applied full aft elevator to help slow the rise but was unable to prevent the prop from striking the runway. From the time aggressive brakes were applied until the prop struck the ground was less than .5 seconds. I had previously briefed with the student what the appropriate short field landing and braking action should look like on the ground and in flight just before the demonstration. I then demonstrated how the maneuver was to be performed before allowing the student to execute the maneuver. Total distance covered from the time the prop impacted the runway was only approximately 5 feet. After the aircraft came to a stop I shut everything down besides the radio; made a radio call to traffic in the area to temporarily close the runway and then had the student monitor the radios while I contacted airport management to notam the runway closed and get assistance with the aircraft. A preliminary inspection revealed damage to the prop; spinner; and lower cowling. No injuries were incurred and not further damage is known at this time.

column121: [Aircraft X] was being vectored for an ILS approach into BFI RWY (Runway) 14R by the Arrival West (1W); Arrival East (1E); Boeing Arrival (1D); and Paine (1P) sectors combined. As [Aircraft X] was being vectored traffic was issued for two VFR aircraft on 1200 codes transitioning both north and south through the localizer for Runway 14R. Due to this traffic I left [Aircraft X] at 3;000 ft. and obtained a point-out with SEA Final (1F). Despite this altitude; [Aircraft X] responded to a TCAS RA and; in an effort to protect the sea final approach course; I turned [Aircraft X] right to a heading 220. As [Aircraft X] turned southbound I observed a C172; [Aircraft Y]; being vectored for the ILS Runway 20 approach into PWT. I turned [Aircraft X] further right to a northwest heading; and measured the distance between [Aircraft X] and [Aircraft Y]. I observed approximately 3.3 miles between the two and since I had already issued the turn; I judged that sufficient separation and attempted to continue working to not fall further behind. [Aircraft X] turned slower than anticipated and caused a separation error between themselves and [Aircraft Y]. The root of this issue is the continual constant conflict between IFR jets into BFI and VFR aircraft transitioning through the localizer in both north and south flow. It is a well-known; persistent issue that has been thoroughly reported for several years. Several solutions have been proposed; but none have been adapted.The only fix for this issue is a restructuring of the Seattle Class B airspace to protect the finals in both directions at BFI. I have personally submitted redesigns for this issue that both protect BFI; and in doing so protect SEA; and allow VFR operations in and out of BFI; and are not unduly hindering to the general aviation community. If safety is truly the priority of the FAA and the Seattle region; changes will be initiated immediately. I understand the process for airspace changes take time; however; the changes will never take place if the process is not started. When the inevitable aircraft incident finally occurs; I hope that no one dies and I am not on position.
column123: [Aircraft Y] was on vector; at 2;500 ft.; to ILS Runway 20 approach at PWT. [Aircraft X]; controlled by W Sector; was on final to Runway 14R at BFI and experienced a TCAS RA with an uncontrolled VFR aircraft. The W Controller was already at a high level of complexity and volume as sectors were combined. The BFI Runway 14R final is in close proximity to PWT 20 mile final and is also underneath the Runway 16 finals at SEA. With limited options [Aircraft X] was turned to the southwest towards [Aircraft Y] with only 500 ft. between them. There are many factors that lead to this situation. These include airport proximities; sector overload; traffic complexity. There is also the issue of lack of controlled and protected airspace in and out of BFI. This is an issue controllers have been bringing to local management for years. The workforce is metaphorically beating our heads against a wall as we are asking for help and are given zero assistance in return. The final to BFI is underneath the SEA finals so we are forced to descend large; fast turbojet aircraft out of Class Bravo airspace into a very high VFR traffic area. This leads numerous TCAS situations. Many suggestions have been brought up through established reporting procedures so I will not waste my time typing them out again. The feeling of a lack of support for change is disheartening. There needs to be change made into airspace and procedures for aircraft landing at BFI; and this should be done for both arrival directions at BFI.
column125: Two S46 Approach controllers reported an aircraft on an ILS Approach to BFI had to be vectored away from two conflicting VFR aircraft not in communication with ATC which resulted with the BFI aircraft losing separation with a fourth aircraft on an ILS approach to another airport. Both reporters emphasized these situations are a recurring issue due to the insufficient Class B protected airspace in the SEA/S46 airspace.

column121: While approaching to land in ROA Runway 24 we were being vector by ATC for a straight in approach. At 4;000 feet in level flight.  The aircraft was approximately 3.0 NM southeast of the final approach fix of HIBAN. ATC issued us a turn 'To a heading of 290 to intercept the GPS course. Cleared for the GPS Y Runway 24 approach.' Shortly afterwards the aircraft GPWS system activated with 'TERRIN TERRAIN PULL-UP.' Pilot flying disconnect the autopilot and climbed to approximately 4;700 feet. Then turn to the assigned heading and intercepted the GPS course and continued the decent to land into ROA. On the approach flight crew verified altitude and position and told what had occurred. ATC verified that the aircraft was at the appropriate altitude and position. They also added that their minimum vector altitude in the area that the aircraft was at was 4;000 feet. The flight continued the approach and landed safely on Runway 24.Still unsure on what the cause of the GPWS.

column121: We were entering the terminal area with given heading descending from 11;000 feet down to 4;000 feet from the South west of the airport for RNAV 28. Passing through 5;000 feet we called the 'airport insight' and received the clearance for the visual approach for Runway 28. By the time we were abeam the airport flap 1 was down and altitude was 2;400 feet descending to 2;200 feet which was final approach segment altitude. Even though I was fully aware of the presence of obstacle (1;687 feet) on the path of our downwind I was confident that 500 feet margin and few thousand feet right of the course would give us good margin for safety. As we were getting close to the tower crane 'terrain' aural message came and after 2-3 second later 'terrain! pull up!' message came. I applied immediate max power and established pitch between 15-20. The message lasted few seconds and we climbed about 2;700 feet. After the conflict was resolved I intercepted the final course and follow the PAPI to the runway as normal operation.When we have received the visual clearance about 20 miles away I bugged the altitude down to 2;200 feet assuming within the 10 mile radius to the airport there cannot be anything that can pose threat to our path. Even though I was aware of the Tower; I didn't mention it during my briefing and considered it a big threat. When the Captain brought me an attention about the Tower once we were getting closer to it and asking me to turn inside the final approach point I delayed his suggestion wanting to intercept the final course outside the FAP. I could have been clear to ask him his intention or worries as we are nearing the obstacle and get the concurrence from both of us that 500 feet is enough or not.1. Better understanding of EGPWS logic could have given me the conservative altitude margins. 2. Detailed briefing; I could have planned better plan by flying higher or going around the tower instead of over flying it. 3. Rushing; I could have gone further out and fly higher to intercept instead of tightening my base turn to join right at the FAP.

column121: I was switched from ZHU Center to Kingsville Approach and I requested direct to the IAF CELIV for the COPTER RNAV 22 into the Alice Hospital. Kingsville Approach stated that they could not clear me for the approach; because they did not have the procedures; yet in the past we have had other controllers clear us. This has been an on-going issue with this particular facility. The approaches are certified and have been established for this hospital for some time. A short time later Kingsville came back and told me to maintain altitude until reaching CELIV and established; switch to advisory and report IFR (Instrument Flight Rules) cancellation on the frequency; or call their number when I'm on the ground. In hindsight; I'm not sure if they were finally able to locate their copy of the procedure; and I may have confused their last set of instructions as the clearance. When I contacted Kingsville TRACON by phone to coordinate my departure from the hospital; I was given the designated Obstacle Departure Procedure (ODP) for that particular location; adding further confusion; because there must be a copy of the procedure.

column121: I was on the ground at JNX with my student pilot getting ready for a soft field takeoff. The pattern was very congested with 5 aircraft in the pattern in various places. An aircraft had just landed; and there were a few other aircraft on downwind. A downwind aircraft stated that they were extending their downwind for departing traffic. I told my student that after the landing aircraft had exited the runway to be ready for immediate departure. The exiting aircraft called clear at this point and I told my student to make the radio call and take us out. As we were taxing out the aircraft that had said they were extending downwind was now on a 1.5 mile final. I told the student to accelerate and a radio call over the CTAF said go-around. I took the controls from my student to avoid a confliction with the aircraft going around. The aircraft going around side stepped to the right to avoid conflict. At no time was a G1000 traffic advisory issued; and no aircraft came close to contact. The overpopulated pattern with 4 aircraft doing stop and goes and my aircraft trying to find a spot to get out was a contributing factor to this issue. After the aircraft sidestepped; they immediately departed the pattern; and we continued our takeoff and entered left closed traffic to continue landing practice.
column123: Myself and a student who is currently working toward his CFI certificate went out for a training flight. After an hour of practicing maneuvers we approached JNX to practice takeoffs and landings. During our final approach into landing on Runway 21 at JNX my student made a call to let the other traffic in the pattern know of our position and our intentions; shortly after this call another plane pulled out on to the runway in frontof us. We made the decision to go-around and commenced a turn to the west so not to be on a collision course with the downwind traffic. We then proceeded to climb and leave the area.

column121: I departed ZZZ East ramp in [a] R22 with a private pilot student for pre-solo training at ZZZ1. We practiced emergency procedures en-route. After listening to ZZZ1 ASOS we determined to set up to enter the traffic pattern for Runway XX. 8 miles west from ZZZ1 we announced our location on CTAF and identified there were two airplanes in the pattern. We entered a 45 degree left downwind for Runway XX at ZZZ1 at 1;100 ft. and 75 kts. We announced and turned left base for Runway XX at 900 ft. and 60 kts. during which I could see Aircraft Y in a close left downwind. We announced turning final and turned final on Runway XX rolling out at 300 ft. AGL (900 ft. MSL) and 60 kts. I instructed the student to perform a normal approach to the approach end of Runway XX and terminate to a 3 ft. hover directly over the numbers (just as would be required during solo or a check ride). The student initiated his normal approach at the normal 10 degree angle required. I then heard Aircraft Y announce he was on final for Runway XX and had the helicopter in sight. I assumed the airplane had misjudged his spacing in the traffic pattern would either adjust his airspeed; perform turns for spacing; or go around. This is not uncommon and simple courtesy to the lower aircraft. On short final for Runway XX I looked out the left rear of the helicopter and could see Aircraft Y on short final right behind us. Aircraft Y asked me my intentions on CTAF. I replied that I intended to complete a landing to a hover at the approach end of Runway XX and then conduct a normal takeoff. Three things were on my mind. First; an aircraft on final and at a lower altitude always has the right of way. Second; I wanted the student to have the opportunity to conduct a normal approach to a hover just as he will be required to do during upcoming solo flight and his PPL checkride. Third; I need to access the student's ability to terminate the landing to a stationary hover. I asked Aircraft Y if he intended to 'go around' as it was clear either we would have to rush off the runaway; before completing the landing; or Aircraft Y could go-around. Aircraft X immediately replied with a complaint over CTAF stating that 'you little black helicopters are hogging the runways here and as a local pilot I can tell you that we are sick of you'. Just as I decelerated to a 3 ft. hover over the numbers for Runway XX I heard the sound of an airplane at high rpm overhead. I looked up to see Aircraft X directly over my helicopter about 20-30 ft. As he overflew me; he stated; 'he intended to report me to the FAA' and requested my name. I provided my tail number for his use as I did not want to provide my name over CTAF. I then contacted Aircraft Y over CTAF and requested that he not over fly my aircraft (for fear of collision) and that I will work with him to gain proper spacing in the pattern. Aircraft Y insulted me over CTAF and challenged me to a physical altercation. I instructed the student hover taxi to the parallel taxiway for Runway XX. I then noticed a pickup truck [surveying] our helicopter. I instructed the student to land on a nearby taxiway intersection near parking and go to idle. I exited the helicopter and approach the truck. I greeted the driver of the white truck with a handshake; introduced myself; and asked if there was an issue. I was told by the driver that they are reporting me to the FAA and they are just 'sick of our little helicopters that are difficult to see and tie up the runways for airplanes.' I sympathized with the driver but told him this is a public airport and that we have a right to conduct flight training here just as much as anyone; provided we all adhere to the rules. Furthermore; I told him that the airplane made an error in spacing by turning downwind; base; and final too quickly and essentially attempted to push me off the runway while conducting a landing. I told the driver that I am sorry this happened; but I feel the airplane did not adhere to Part 91 Right of Way rules to a lower aircraft on final. I gave the driver my number and told him I'd be more than happy to work with him on any future issues. We shook hands and went separate ways. I returned to the helicopter and returned directly to ZZZ. My chief concern with this incident is that the airplane should not have rushed or crowded another aircraft on final approach. A change in speed; turns for spacing; or a go-around were all options for the airplane pilot. Furthermore; executed a go around directly over and in close proximity to another aircraft is dangerous. What if I had directed the student to execute a go around or immediate takeoff (because we were being rushed on final and had climbed up into the airplane. As a military aviator and civilian helicopter and airplane pilot of over XX years I had always striven to fly with the utmost safety and friendliness. I preach this to my students; and I hope that all Aviators practice this as well. My goal is simply to encourage friendly; smart; and safe flying by all pilots at both ZZZ and ZZZ1.

column121: Holding short of Runway XX at ZZZ; our aircraft was given a departure clearance void time of 60 seconds from ZZZ Center. We initiated an immediate departure on Runway XX. After climbing via the obstacle departure procedure; we proceeded enroute and contacted ZZZ Center again. We then heard another aircraft call back to ZZZ Center; and stated they were forced to initiate a go-around as they were on final approach to Runway XX. It is my guess that our departure is what caused that aircraft to go-around. I however; do not recall seeing the aircraft on final approach; or hearing a radio call that the aircraft was on final approach. Conditions were VFR at the airport; but IFR traffic load was heavy. I do not recall ATC advising us of the aircraft on final approach. It again is responsibility of us as a crew to ensure the approach/runway was clear for takeoff. If this incident was caused by our departure; we should have more clearly made sure it was safe for takeoff; and will do so in the future.

column121: I was conducting a training flight with a student and entered the pattern at PWT on the 45 for a full stop landing on Runway 20; making all radio calls in the pattern. As I was on short final (100 ft. AGL); the aircraft holding short makes a radio call that he is 'taking the active [Runway] 20'. He did not move his aircraft and continued holding short as I crossed the threshold; at which point I assumed he saw me as I passed right in front of his windshield in my roundout. I could not make any radio calls within those few seconds due to other traffic making calls in the pattern. As I am completing my landing roll to exit on taxiway A3; I hear a plane takeoff right on top of me. I asked on CTAF if he took off while I was still on the runway; to which 3 different aircraft concurred that he did. After a few minutes; as I'm departing the airspace; the aircraft in the pattern (along with myself) were discussing the events that transpired over radio. At that point; the pilot in question keyed up saying 'I'm still on frequency guys; what's the problem?'. I told him that he took off while I was still on the runway; to which he replied 'yeah; what's the problem with that?'. Another pilot noted that immediately after lift-off; he executed a steep right turn and exited the airspace at low level. In my professional opinion; the actions of the PIC of Aircraft X is a textbook example of a reckless operation; and a clear demonstration of several different hazardous attitudes. He obviously showed no remorse for his actions; believing that they were considered 'normal' things to do. There was absolutely nothing I could have done to prevent this from happening; because it is clearly all happening from the other guy's end. If I had to execute a go-around; it would almost definitely have resulted in a midair collision. The only way to prevent something like this from happening again is remedial training for this guy; or even a reevaluation of his certificate; because the way he operates his aircraft is clearly without regard for anyone's safety.

column121: I was on top of scattered clouds at 5;500 ft. arriving at HAF from the south. Weather reports from the airport all indicated the airport was VFR. As I approached the IF for the approach I had to decide between coordinating a pop up approach with Approach Control or descending through a large opening in the cloud layer and flying up the beach. I chose to remain VFR and fly up the coast due to the fact that it was getting dark and I had no idea how long it would take Approach Control to coordinate an approach. I descended uneventfully below the clouds and proceeded to the airport at between 1;000 and 1;500 ft. over hills that vary from 100 ft. to 300 ft. high. Apparently there is some drone operator who operates off a hill that gets upset when aircraft fly over at low altitude. He called me right after I had landed. He told me he 'I can not enforce this but I am requesting that we not fly over his drone operation'. I never saw a drone and am not sure if he was operating one at the time. Talking to other pilots it appears he calls every pilot he sees fly over at 1;500 ft. or below. I typically fly higher but on days when the ceilings are 1;500 to 2;000 ft. it is unavoidable to fly low. I am concerned about the drone operator calling pilots and possibly influencing their decisions when sacrificing safety of flight of the aircraft vs. inconvenience of the drone operator.

column121: During this incident I was on a checkride and we ran a partial panel VOR Runway X approach with a circle to Runway XY. This is normally a Class D airspace but the Tower at ZZZ is currently closed due to COVID and this has presented some challenges to the arriving and departing process given we share the airport with airline operations. We made it to the left downwind for XY and there was a carrier aircraft holding short of XY getting ready to take the runway and they requested over CTAF for us to extend our downwind so they could takeoff. We extended our downwind about a mile or so and decided to turn base. About the time we were turning base there was another carrier that called a 10 mile final for XY. With the position report given; we were under the impression we had the appropriate spacing for us to get on the ground and off the runway before they reached the field. We continued and turned in for final and the carrier taking off was on the roll. I made position reports and heard nothing back that would indicate an issue. When we were on short final carrier made a 6 mile final call. I then made a position call for short final and we were within a half mile of landing. About the time I was roughly 100 feet over the runway threshold; the carrier aircraft called and stated; 'Aircraft on short final; we are going around and will be flying above you.' We landed safely and had to extend our ground roll down the runway to exit because another carrier aircraft was taxiing to Runway XY via Taxiway A and we had to taxi beyond where they were. That concludes the incident. I do not know how accurate the position reports were from carrier or their speed but there didn't appear to be an issue on spacing and if they knew they were catching us that fast they could have let us know and I would have executed a go-around. This was not a near-miss event and we never saw the aircraft near us on ADS-B radar or receive any traffic notification. It seems to be more lack of communication in a non-towered environment with busy airspace as a root cause. I think they were well aware of our location in front of them and didn't speak up to let us know how quick they were catching us. I feel like there should be some additional measures in place when a tower is closed at an airport like this with heavier volume and many different types and sizes of aircraft operating. Sharing an airport between GA and airline ops in a non-towered environment is setting the stage for incidents like this to happen and truly presents some added challenges and hazards.

column121: I was approaching L52 from the north/west to enter a left downwind for Runway 29. As I was crossing the shoreline at 1;000 AGL (one mile out) and decelerating; my passenger and I spotted two powered gliders crossing the departure end of 29 at approximately 900 feet AGL.They seemed to be transitioning from the southern part of the dunes to the norther part of the dunes through the upwind part of the traffic pattern. I veered the aircraft slightly more to the south to avoid them when I spotted another powered glider transitioning from the south to the north by intersection the downwind leg of the left pattern to 29. He was coming directly toward my aircraft in the opposite direction of traffic pattern. I then had to veer slightly to the left (north/east) to avoid that vehicle. I then landed without incident on 29.When parked at transient space we witnessed another powered glider takeoff from the tarmac and join the other gliders.  They continued to overfly the airport at pattern altitude and not seem to adhere by any observable pattern.

column121: I released Aircraft X for departure from BFI in a south flow. Aircraft X reported climbing to 2;000 ft. on the NIRVANA1 SID. I radar identified Aircraft X and climbed them to 6;000 ft.. As Aircraft X was climbing through 1;800 ft. I noticed that Aircraft X appeared to be making a right turn towards the SEA VOR and not following the NIRVANA SID. I instructed Aircraft X to 'turn left immediately; left turn heading 130.' At this point; Aircraft X was starting to converge with Aircraft Y who was on final approach into SEA descending through 1;100 ft. on the ILS Approach to Runway 16R. Aircraft X was about 1.5 miles laterally separated and 700 ft. vertically separated from Aircraft Y and converging. I observed Aircraft X achieve vertical separation from Aircraft Y; then saw the turn to heading 130 start occurring. I gave Aircraft X the Brasher statement. The pilot of Aircraft X stated that it 'looks like the activation for the autopilot for the NIRVANA1 did not take place.'I honestly don't know how to fix this problem; but it is a recurring; systemic issue. This is not my first event with almost identical circumstances; and many of my co-workers have had very similar events. Attempts have already been made to alter the SID; or issue an initial heading for aircraft departing BFI; but these events still occur. The typical issue is that pilots are filing SEA.J5 and though BFI is issuing the NIRVANA1 SID and a Preferred Departure Route that would join J5 from TAOMA or SUMMA; pilot error including unchecked autopilot actions that result in a turn to SEA VOR will immediately cause convergence at the same altitude as aircraft inbound to SEA within the final approach fix; as well as any aircraft departing SEA during that time. There is very little and very subtle warning; and immediate action is necessary to recover separation and end the rapidly worsening danger of the situation. I believe in most cases it is a result of an individualized pilot deviation; and I don't know how to end those completely. The only other solution I can think of would be to completely remove/decommission the SEA VOR.

column121: Flying C172 on an IFR flight from ZZZ to ZZZ. Was flying a RNAV approach to ZZZ. Soon after hand off to ZZZ Approach; I disconnected the autopilot when the aircraft was at about 4;000 feet AGL. At that time ATC advised to turn left to 220 heading to intercept initial approach fix. Right after disconnecting the auto pilot; the aircraft banked left steeply to about 60 degrees roll and started descending while the ATC keep advising to turn left and cleared me for the RNAV approach. The conditions were IMC and aircraft was hard to control. It took a few minutes to get the aircraft under control. The ATC workload obviously increased while not knowing the exact situation and even doubted at one point if the pilot (me) was IFR rated and current. Once I was finally able to get the aircraft stabilized; I informed ATC that I was having trouble with the controls and apologized. The flight ended safely after all at ZZZ. The probable cause was having an iPad mounted on the yoke. Will never mount the iPad on the yoke again.

column121: For the ILS Runway 04R at ZZZ; performance numbers were run for a Flaps 30 landing with a good stopping margin at Autobrakes 3. ATIS winds were calling approximately 070/13 - no gusts. Upon switching to Tower at ZZZZZ on the localizer course and tracking the glideslope; Tower cleared us to land with winds now gusting to 20. As PF (Pilot Flying); I elected to add 4 knots to the Vtarget and as an added Safety measure at ZZZ; I selected Autobrakes MAX to compensate for a slightly increased approach speed. The PM (Pilot Monitoring) attempted to quickly re-run the performance data; but the gusts were not yet accounted for in the METAR. Rather than going heads down any longer inside 1;000 feet AGL; we agreed a 4-5 knot addition to VTarget and using Autobrakes MAX would be more than sufficient to account for the gust factor.At approximately 500 feet AGL; we received a GPWS Too Low Flaps Alert. The PM immediately called for the go-around and it was initiated. A quick scan of the flap handle and I realized that I had neglected to call for Flaps 30 from a Flaps 15 setting; and the Before Landing Checklist was not run. We were quickly vectored around for another uneventful ILS. The minor distraction of adding a gust factor and changing the Autobrakes setting sent me out of a normal approach configuration sequence and we ended up both missing the final flap setting and subsequent checklist.With the normal ZZZ procedure of not contacting (and often not switching to) Tower until reaching the fix at about a 5-mile final; it's possible for us to miss out on cues to changing weather on the ground. We were number 3 or 4 in a sequence of landings on 04R. Had we switched to Tower five or more miles sooner; we may have caught the gusting winds earlier and had more time to type in the adjusted winds and send away for the performance numbers prior to 1;000 feet AGL. This was another series of events combined with mild fatigue that led to poor task prioritization on our parts.
column123: The short version is that we got 'Too Low; Flaps' at 500 feet because we were at Flaps 15. We went around. Planned winds were 070/7. Tower winds were 070/12G20. I re-ran the performance landing numbers at 1;500 feet; but did not override the METAR and got back the numbers for 070/7. The PF (Pilot Flying) chose to add 5 knots to VTarget and we agreed to go from Medium to Max braking and continue.There is no guidance I am aware of as to when we would be required to re-run our landing numbers. I should not have tried to re-run the landing numbers at that point. That would have eliminated the distraction that led to not selecting our planned final flaps or running the Before Landing Checklist.

column121: TCAS RA on arrival into ZZZ [airport]. Assigned descent to 7;000 feet and vectors to final by ZZZ Approach. We reached 7;000 feet and noted approach was extremely busy where the Controller was being stepped on seemingly more than half of her transmissions. While level at 7;000 feet the TCAS noted a proximate target converging from 1 o'clock; 400 feet below. We attempted to contact Approach but there was zero opportunity to get a word in due to the sheer amount of communication on frequency. I advised the Captain PF to 'get ready' while we attempted to locate the aircraft visually. A few seconds later the TCAS noted traffic and ordered a very shallow climb. I announced to ZZZ Approach that we were responding to an RA; but I believe I was blocked; the frequency still jammed with constant communication. A very short time later the controller issued traffic to us and I again advised 'Climbing on RA.' Controller replied 'Roger' I believe and the event was over shortly after. We returned to 7;000 feet and were vectored for the visual; but the controller still had to vector us around multiple VFR targets on our way to the runway. Most significantly we were cleared for the visual approach; but were restricted to flying and intercepting between 5 and 7 NM from the airport due to area traffic. This caused a perceived limitation that pilots are not used to. The CA/PF then overflew the Localizer slightly attempting to maintain these limitations. The aircraft was clearly stable by 1000 feet and we continued in to land; but this was following an incident due to the same cause.Cause was due to the intense level of traffic in ZZZ Approach's airspace. I spoke with a contact at ZZZ [Approach] later who noted the Controller had 15 active aircraft not including the VFR targets attempting to get clearance from the closed airspace; Practice IFR traffic; and VFR flight following requests. The Controller was doing an excellent job and maintained a very professional demeanor throughout. There was just no reasonable way for Controller to maintain communications with the volume of aircraft. I am not sure; but this could have been a result of reduced staffing due to COVID. Maybe there was an East and West ZZZ [Approach] sector available; but no staffing to accommodate. My suggestions would be to either split ZZZ Approach or to draw a new arrival for ZZZ airport to reduce the amount of communications and possible routes needed for the controllers.

column121: ATC turned us onto the approach while still at 4;000. Told us to 'maintain 3;000 till established cleared ILS [Runway] XXL. FO (First Officer) put in 3;000 in the altitude selector and I acknowledged it. Once established; instead of selecting 2;700 feet for the FAF altitude he rolled the altitude selector to 3;300 and started a slow descent. Stated he was going to just control the altitude himself. I thought that was strange; but let him do it figuring it was just a technique he liked to do.As we approached 2;700 feet; he was still descending at ~700 FPM; so I realized he was going to blow through the altitude. I told him he needed to stop descending and return to the FAF altitude until we intercepted the GS. The first time I said this he seemed confused and didn't really do much. I said more sternly that we needed to climb back to the proper altitude. This time he initiated a climb back to the proper altitude and we were still stabilized and fully configured by 1;000 feet AGL. ATC did issue us a low altitude alert as we were climbing back to the proper altitude and we got a single 'Glideslope' callout from the aircraft.The cause was the FO's improper use of the altitude selector and VS (Vertical Speed) mode. Contributing was me allowing him to do the method he did instead of asking him to just put the FAF altitude in the altitude selector till GS intercept.I should have insisted that the FO put the FAF altitude in the altitude selector; especially in IMC. I also told him afterword's that he could have just stayed at 3;000 once established and intercepted the GS from there since at that point we were already close to GS intercept.
column123: Cleared ILS [Runway] XXL; we were given an intercept heading and told to maintain 3;000 feet. The Controller kept us close and fairly high before clearing us for the approach. We were at 4;000 feet when cleared. Captain was PM (Pilot Monitoring); First Officer was PF (Pilot Flying). Once established; PF started down with vertical speed to capture glide slope by 2;700 feet at ZZZZZ. PF moved altitude knob up to 3;300 feet from 3;000 feet instead of to 2;700 feet causing us to go below published altitude by almost 500 feet. Was immediately corrected and aircraft was stabilized. Given a close proximity intercept; task saturation was high with intercepting; radio communications; and configuring aircraft. Should have cross checked better and stated intentions more clearly (PF to PM).If crew starts to feel rushed or task saturation is high; ask for longer final or a more stable intercept from ATC. Never let cross checking suffer due to task saturation; should always come first. PM should state approach intention more clearly while getting established. Don't have tunnel vision (Example Focusing on getting configured and not having correct altitude set).

column121: While descending into LRU airport; I heard ATC call to another aircraft (Aircraft Y) that was also inbound. I was arriving from the west; and Aircraft Y from the north; North West area. I could see him on ADS-B. While Aircraft Y was behind me; he was much faster; however ABQ (Albuquerque) center had him climb and then hold as I continued my descent. Leveling off at my assigned altitude; I was still unable to see the airfield; despite the field reports of better weather. I was; however; still several miles away. At about the same time; Aircraft Y called in; cancelling his IFR stating that he had the airport in sight. ATC cancelled his IFR as he stated he was now in VMC. I then asked ATC for a report from the Aircraft Y; as he was able to see the field and I was not; despite me being at a lower altitude. (I asked hoping I would break out of the clouds soon; or find an area where I could; such as where Aircraft Y came from). Quickly after; I did get the airport insight below me and was able to descend VFR. I also cancelled my IFR at that time.Being almost over the airport; I extended away from it and then I called entering the Left downwind for runway 26. (The winds at the time were reported as being 240 at 22 - 27; making runway 22 or 26 the best options.) I then called a base turn; and then final. At that time I also heard Aircraft Y call in for the RNAV for runway 12. Being as he cancelled IFR with no mention of practicing approaches; I anticipated his landing before me; with no conflict. As I came on short final for runway 26; I heard Aircraft Y call out that he was going missed from 12; which would then be intersecting my landing path. At that moment I looked up and saw Aircraft Y just ahead and to my right; above me; but very close. I would estimate within 200-300 feet. Already being close to the ground; no evasion action could have been taken. I landed and taxied into the ramp. Shortly after; Aircraft Y landed on a different runway and parked as well. I called the center to report the incident and express my concern and safety issue that had occurred.

column121: I was cleared to transition underneath JFK's Class B Airspace; along the shoreline below 500 feet. while flying a westerly heading; I encountered wake turbulence from a plane landing on Runway 04R. Roughly 30 seconds before the encounter; I was cleared to climb; not leaving me adequate time to climb above the turbulence. The wake we experienced was stronger than expected. It shook our plane and dislodged the battery onboard my aircraft. I first noticed the acidic smell and shortly after observed smoke in the cockpit. I decided that I would need to land the plane immediately at JFK. After contacting Kennedy Tower; I got clearance to land on [Runway] 13R. Once acknowledging my clearance; I informed the Tower that I was shutting down my battery to prevent the fire from expanding and that we may lose further communication. We made a safe return to the airport and I stopped the plane on Taxiway P. I shut the motor down and deplaned my passengers. [Ground personnel] put out the fire and towed the plane to the FBO. Both myself and my passengers were evaluated by medical staff and were thankfully unscathed.

column121: In our initial climb to 6;000 feet we were instructed by Departure to turn off course and given a fix to fly to. I confirmed the fix with the CA (Captain) and pressed Nav Mode on the FMS. I looked over to my PFD to set the new altitude assigned by Departure and the CA looked away at the same time. Shortly thereafter; Departure called and asked us if we were turning on course. We both looked up and realized that the Nav mode had not captured the course. I immediately turned the heading bug towards the course line on the display; while the CA notified the Controller that we were correcting and turning on course.While compensating for winds and flying via the heading bug; we started to analyze the problem. Shortly after changing the AP to the CA's side; we were able to correct the Nav issue; as the AP was functioning properly on the CA's side; I continued as the pilot flying. About 45 minutes out; the CA left the flight deck to use the LAV. As he returned; he noticed that we had about 8 status messages that popped up sometime after he had left the flight deck. I was engaged with a short conversation with the FA (Flight Attendant) and did not notice the display of messages until he returned. The status messages were flight control related; noting flight spoilers; rudder; etc. The CA referred to the QRH for landing data as a precautionary measure; in the event of a total system failure to those systems. The CA Then notified ATC of our potential systems status and requested deleting the LAHSO restrictions. ATC was unable to comply.Later; as we were given an intercept angle to intercept the final approach course; I selected the NAV button and was waiting for the intercept. We had a [heavy aircraft] on our left and we were paying close attention to that traffic; when we noticed that the aircraft had blown through the center line and was deviating right. The CA then asked me what was going on and while trying to process the information; I replied; 'I have no idea.' At that time Tower called to query our actions; It was as if the aircraft had a mind of its own and before I was able to kick off the autopilot; the CA then asked for the flight controls. We landed without incident and all flight control systems were operational throughout the landing phase; as all the spoilers had deployed. The AP had remained on the CA's side throughout the entire approach.Hindsight; I told the CA; that the one course of action we could have taken at the onset of discovering that the AP/Nav was not recognizing my side; we should have changed roles and allowed him to be the Pilot Flying. With the unexpected behavior of the FMS during the critical phase of flight; I was in total agreement with the CA's actions and decisions; I believe his actions were appropriate and timely while operating in the essence of safety for the passengers and crew.

column121: On DATE; Aircraft X was scheduled to depart on a two leg cross county trip from ZZZ to ZZZ1.  The trip was slated to depart at XA:30 but was delayed approximately 50 minutes due to unforeseen complications dealing with one of the aircraft preflight checklist items.  While checking the tire pressure of the landing gear tires; it was discovered that the left main landing gear tire valve stem was inaccessible due to the hub cap being installed incorrectly.  There is a small access door that allows access to the valve stem through the hub cap and the hub cap was clocked in the wrong position rendering the valve stem inaccessible.  A local A&P Service Center was contacted and on-site service was requested to remove the hubcap and re-clock.  An A&P responded to the location and removed the hub cap.  He asked to confirm the POH air pressure requirements; and continued to top off the air pressure per the POH.  The hub cap was replaced in the correct position.  All tire air pressures were serviced per the POH.  This was the last remaining check list item to be completed; and Aircraft X departed ZZZ2 at XB:20 en route to ZZZ3 for the first leg of the trip.  The taxi; take off; landing; and post landing taxi phases were normal.  On DATE; Aircraft X departed ZZZ2 on the second leg of this cross country trip to ZZZ1.  Taxi and takeoff were normal.  The trip was uneventful.  IMC conditions existed over ZZZ1 during our arrival time; with unknown tops and 4;400 ft. ceilings.  VMC conditions existed below the ceiling with winds reported variable at 3 kts.  The RNAV GPS approach was requested and granted into ZZZ1.  Following the approach; the main landing gear touched down with the aircraft near center line just beyond the extended threshold. The aircraft responded as expected upon main gear touchdown.  When the nose landing gear touched down the plane veered hard to the right.  Left rudder pedal was used to counter the uncommanded right turn and the plane veered left and immediately began skidding to the left.  The plane did not respond to input and skidded off the runway just short of taxiway F; into the landscaped area immediately adjacent to the runway.  The nose wheel collapsed while the engine was still running and dropped the spinning prop to the ground.  It was immediately obliterated.  The turbine engine abruptly quit running.  When the plane swerved to the right following the nose wheel touch down; the plane immediately felt unstable.  It felt as if the runway was icy and without good traction.  However; it was later determined there was no ice on the runway.  I advised the tower when the plane came to a complete stop that I had just hit ice.  This event duration happened extremely quick.  There were no injuries.  There was no known airport property damage observed.  There is no other property damage.  Emergency crews showed up quickly.  The aircraft did not catch on fire.  Emergency crews did not deploy any foam.  The ELT activated.  The airbag seat belts did not deploy.  Environmental observation:  Prior to decent; and while in cruise at FL270; I had been observing the OAT slowly creep down from around -45c to a max low of -51c.  The minimum in-flight temperature limitation of this aircraft is -54c.  When the temperature dropped to around -49c; I became acutely aware and concerned of the temperature as I knew I was approaching the maximum lower limit.  I advised ATC I needed lower due to temperature limitations and they advised expect lower in three minutes.  I was then instructed to descend to FL250.  The temperature increased to above -49c.  Shortly thereafter; the decent into the terminal area required a rapid descent of roughly 2;000 FPM.

column121: Aircraft X was on an ILS Approach to Runway 16L. A VFR aircraft was skirting around AFW's Class D airspace; not in communication with AFW or D10. The VFR aircraft was eastbound then turned north and crossed over WIGZU the FAF for ILS RWY 16L at 2;600 FT MSL. Aircraft X was at the same altitude. The Local Controller called the traffic and Aircraft X reported the traffic in sight. Aircraft X reported that they had to turn off the ILS then turn back once they were clear of the traffic. The two aircraft were less than 1/2 mile apart at the same altitude head on to each other. This area is dangerous and this continues to be an issue. The airspace design is outdated. AFW airport has grown and is continuing to grow. The facility has recently been upgraded from a Level 5 Tower to a Level 6 Tower. In the last year three new air carriers have all started operating at AFW.  A contractor for the US military has relocated their maintenance base to AFW. Aerospace company X has also begun construction at their new facility here as well. Both runways were extended from 9;000 feet to 11;000 feet in the last few years. Everything at this airport has been growing and expanding except the airspace. The Class Bravo airspace should be expanded to include WIGZU the FAF for RWY 16L. This would allow us the ability to talk to VFR aircraft that constantly cross this area around 2;500 feet. We have conducted pilot outreach at surrounding airports. It has not been successful. We just want the ability to talk to these VFR aircraft to provide traffic advisories. The airspace needs to be updated.

column121: During the DINGO 5 arrival into TUS; after waypoint MAVVA; I continued on the charted 075 degree heading passing through the Runway 11L localizer course; not complying with the textual instruction to intercept the I-TUS localizer course. ATC gave me vectors back around to re-intercept the localizer course; and a normal landing was made.The cause of the event was a failure to cross-check the textual instructions with what is displayed on the chart. Contributing factors were as follows: 1) A 'Descend Via' clearance was not issued. ATC instructed to 'Descend and maintain 8;000 feet; and EXPECT a visual approach.' I misinterpreted that to mean he would provide vectors to the final approach course; or clear me for a visual approach passing MAVVA.  2) I found the verbiage on the chart confusing where it states 'Runway 11L EXPECT approach clearance'. I interpreted that to mean once established on the 075 heading from MAVVA; not upon intercepting the localizer; as normally you would not intercept a localizer without ATC clearance or prior to accepting a visual approach.To avoid a recurrence of this event; a thorough briefing covering all noted and textual information should be conducted and cross checked prior to every landing. While my briefing did cover all of the information on the plan-view of the arrival; I missed a crucial piece of information solely located in the textual description that was not well depicted nor defined on the plan-view. It would have been helpful if ATC had offered us a visual approach earlier; directed us to intercept the localized after MAVVA and THEN expect approach clearance; or have the instruction to Intercept I-TUS localizer clearly printed on the plan view. It is worthy to note; the ATC controller stated 'Yeah; you're not the first airliner to do this' both over the frequency and on the telephone while speaking with him after landing. This leads me to believe that this could be a common mistake that could be corrected easily with a revised; emphasized; note on the arrival chart.

column123: Clearance by Approach Control - Intercept the Runway 06 approach course and expect the Runway 06 ILS; circle Runway 01. The pilot flying began a climb from 820 ft. MSL to approximately 1;300 ft. MSL. Message cleared and the approach was continued to a full stop landing. The aircraft was flown too close to obstructions during the circling to land approach. 2 contributing factors: Unfamiliarity with the procedure at TEB and reliance on autopilot.With the frequency that [our company] operates in and out of TEB; it may be advisable to add some type of briefing to the pilot [group]. This is a common procedure at TEB; but it doesn't occur frequently enough to build pilot confidence in the circle to land maneuver.

column121: In the traffic pattern at ZZZ; my CFI and I were on the right downwind for Runway XXR when we were told to Continue for Runway XXR. We then continued and a few moments later was told to turn right base for XXR. When turning right base we noted traffic; but I didn't think it was a factor at first because we assumed it was for the parallel because we still were told to turn right base.  As we got established on base we saw the traffic was close and my CFI took controls to avoid a mid-air collison. We got no warning from the Tower and later found out that the tower told the other aircraft to continue for Runway XXR. Shortly after the Tower told them to continue for XXR that's when the tower said to turn base. I didn't think that traffic that was on final would be a factor for us and thought it was for Runway XXL. In the future; I think the Tower should be more clear and understand the traffic spacing better because if he did then he could've told us to extend a couple of miles so we could've passed behind the traffic instead of passing in front and coming within 1;000 feet horizontally of each other. I believe the talking between me and my CFI also contributed because maybe we would've heard the call for the other aircraft to continue and could've challenged that with the Tower but even then we still were told to turn base. I believe the tower should've noted the other aircraft and looked at the spacing; and told us this isn't going to work and this near-miss could've been avoided altogether instead we got turned directly into the path of another aircraft.
column123: I was flying with my student in the pattern at ZZZ. When we were downwind; Tower didn't clear us for a touch and go; but told us to continue. We continued downwind and waited for further instructions when Tower then told us to turn base.We turned base. There was some hesitation but then the Controller instructed us to turn directly towards the numbers. At this point; we were on right base unaware we were being turned onto an aircraft that was on final.   Before my students turn for final; I always have them look on final to make sure it is clear of traffic. At this point; I noticed the traffic on the left hand side and saw it on my iPad. I took the controls from my student and climbed to offset to the right where we avoided a near miss collision of about 1;000 feet horizontal distance and 300 feet vertical distance over a densely packed neighborhood of City. We never were told to extend or follow this aircraft.Prior to the Controller telling us to turn base; he had spoken to the aircraft on the straight in final and told them to continue. He realized the aircraft was there; but did not communicate the location and told us to turn base onto him. This situation demonstrates that pilots must exercise extreme vigilance at airports where ATC is so busy to provide clear instructions.The term 'continue' can be vague in that…does it mean we're supposed to fly the pattern as regularly; or are we supposed to continue downwind? In that case; the term 'extend' would make more sense.When I talked to the Controller on the phone later; I was told that this situation occurred due to a late runway switch by the other aircraft. However; in examination of the track log; the aircraft was on a straight in approach. The pilot also called up that they were approaching a point on the approach for XXR.  The Controller didn't acknowledge the mistake that had been made and the possible deadly consequences that could have occurred from this near miss.This is a serious near miss.

column121: Aircraft X; was a training flight with a student performing a practice ILS Approach to FDK Runway 23; authorized and approved by FDK Tower.  Aircraft X was 10 miles from Runway 23; approaching the NUMBE intersection on the 295-degree radial from the EMI VORTAC; the authorized feeder-route to the FDK ILS Runway 23 Approach.After receiving such approval; Aircraft Y; requested the same practice ILS Approach and self-announced they were 2 miles from NUMBE; but based on the ADS-B display in Aircraft X; Aircraft Y was approaching the inbound localizer course from the northwest; from which there is no authorized transition or feeder route.Aircraft Y told FDK Tower they would follow behind Aircraft X and slow down but at various points and times between NUMBE; the IAF (10 nm from Runway 23); and Runway 23; Aircraft Y was as close as 500 horizontal feet behind Aircraft X and was proceeding at a higher rate of speed than Aircraft X.Twice during the practice ILS Approach; the ADS-B-based Traffic Alerting System in Aircraft X (the leading aircraft) sounded an aural and visual caution (yellow); meaning Aircraft Y was closing in on the tail of Aircraft X.The flight student in Aircraft X was spooked by these warnings; as well as the radio calls from Aircraft Y saying they were 'too close'; and had difficulty controlling the aircraft and/or experiencing productive learning.Because of this high risk; and the student's unease with the entire situation; Aircraft X aborted the practice ILS 23 Approach and took evasive action (executing the missed approach 1.5-miles from Runway 23 and side-stepping to the left); to get out of the way of the closing aircraft; Aircraft Y.Subsequently; while on the ground; Aircraft Y's Certificated Flight Instructor and co-pilot approached the crew of Aircraft X and laughed at Aircraft X for 'flying too slow.'The crew of Aircraft X felt this was an inappropriate and unsafe display of arrogance; especially in light of Aircraft Y's violation of FAR Section 91.113 (f).

column121: During a practice RNAV (GPS) [Runway] 35 approach to Colorado Air and Space Port (CFO); we had just crossed the Final Approach Fix (FAF) at ARRES at the charted altitude of 7;500 feet MSL. The student at the controls was 2-3 seconds late in beginning the glideslope intercept; and our glideslope indicated slightly high. Knowing that we had to descend 500 feet in order to be below the upcoming 7;000 feet MSL Class B shelf; I reduced power to increase our rate of descent. There was also an air carrier [Aircraft Y] at our 1 o'clock; approximately 1;000 feet above our altitude according to ADS-B; which was making a right turn from a southerly heading to a northwesterly as it make its approach to DEN. Denver Approach contacted us saying; 'I need you below the Bravo shelf at 7;000 feet.' I replied 'roger; we are increasing our rate of descent; currently indicating 6;800 feet.' The Aircraft Y crossed above and ahead of us; right to left; approximately 1;000 feet above and 200 feet ahead. I did not hear any of the communications between the Aircraft Y and Denver Approach; nor did I notice any maneuvering by the Aircraft Y indicating it was reacting to our presence. The complex airspace around DEN is extremely challenging; and this approach's FAF is approximately 1.4 NM from the 7;000 feet MSL Class B shelf. Descending 500 feet in less than 1.4 NM at small aircraft approach speeds does not require excessive rates of descent; but it does require a timely descent. Any delay; or crossing the FAF above the 7;500 feet MSL altitude; quickly makes it likely that an airplane may violate Class B airspace during the Final Approach Segment. In our case; the timing of the arriving Aircraft Y was coincidentally exactly the same time we were at this critical place. As a CFI-I; I am reluctant to use this approach for training ever again. One suggestion may be to cross ARRES at an altitude lower than the charted minimum of 7;500 feet MSL - if; and only if; it's a practice approach under VFR. The potential negative training this would cause would have to be weighed against the extra margin of separation from the Class B shelf. Additionally; in the future; if I use this approach for practice again; I will have the student execute a missed approach and turn away from the 7;000 feet MSL Class B shelf if the aircraft is high at ARRES; does not initiate a timely descent; or if I notice DEN traffic that could cross above us as happened on this flight.

column121: On the 3rd touch and go landing after touch down on Runway 28 I could see a Aircraft Y approach our runway from left to right at Taxiway C. I commented to the student to keep an eye on him to make sure he stops. Aircraft Y proceeded to cross the runway in front of us without stopping. I immediately took control of the airplane and began a full brake application. We were getting close enough to Aircraft Y that I was considering turning the aircraft off the runway to avoid contact. We were able to avoid hitting the airplane and remain on the runway. We were slightly past Taxiway D when we came to a full stop; I do not recall hearing any radio transmission from the Aircraft Y announcing his intentions. I commented to Aircraft Y 'Thanks for crossing in front of a landing aircraft and you can buy us a new set of tires.' He commented that 'he did not see us.' This is just the latest of several incursions I have witnessed on this runway. Most are caused by pilots unfamiliar with our airport; this is the first from an what I would assume to be a highly experienced pilot and was told by the lineman that aircraft is here fairly often. I feel there is a need for more emphasis and training in making pilots aware of ground procedures and traffic pattern etiquette especially at uncontrolled airports with multiple runways. Having to cross a runway to get to the designated calm wind runway which also is the longest runway (10;000 ft.) presents a huge potential for an accident.

column121: I was conducting a discovery flight a requested flight following on the ground with a departure out to the southeast of the field near City X to fly over the house of the client. I was cleared to takeoff of Runway XX. On the climbout I was not sure if Tower wanted me to make a left or right turn towards the south east so I asked for clarification. He said 'you're going to the east right?' And I repeated stating we were going to the southeast. He replied back with instructions to make a right turn to head that way. Right as I turned onto the downwind; Tower switch me to Approach. I switched frequencies and began explaining something to the student as I started to cruise the plane out at 1;500 MSL. About 15 seconds after I switched frequencies to Approach; I gave them a call to check in telling them the altitude I was at and the direction I was headed; southeast. Immediately after I called in; I saw a King Air to my right that was supposedly on final for XX. They were on a track towards us but were in a climb. I saw they were climbing so I immediately started descending. After that is when Approach called in to tell me about the traffic saying that I was intercepting the final approach course for XX. When I looked at my ground track after the flight; I did drift slightly right when I was on the extended downwind; but I never intercepted the final approach course. I did not receive notice from the Tower about the King Air on final before being switched to Approach and it wasn't until after I checked in with Approach that they gave me the advisory. The King Air's TCAS system showed that we reached within 100 feet of each other but myself and the pilots in the King Air agreed that it was much more that 100 feet. I overall feel like there was a lack of communication from all parts that could have possibly prevented this occurrence. I personally could have been more specific with tower on where I was going. I could have been more watchful of my track to stay clear of final. The winds were from the northwest so that could have been a factor in why I was slightly drifting. That is why it is important to know where your winds are coming from to maintain a constant ground track along the ground and prevent drifting.

column121: Landed on a runway with snow removal equipment on it. While on rollout; the low visibility obscured runway activity until no other choice was available.Onboard equipment failed; distracting crew from making radio positions reporting to ZZZ traffic. Event occurred after Tower had closed for the night.Good accident prevention was conducted by all vehicles and aircraft involved; and no other problems occurred.This could have been a classic example of 'Chain of events causing a accident.'  A late departure because of icing conditions at origin. Weather at arrival airport causing snow removal equipment to be on runway for extended periods of time. A minor problem during the approach to landing phase distracted the crew; and company procedures required crew to be playing the role of both flying and non-flying pilot at the same time.      In this case; I believe; safety would have been better served if once the decision to hand off control was made; we didn't change back 600-400 feet above the ground. Once the decision was made our roles would have been clearer; and airplane radio communication would have been made at the critical trigger points.
column123: On approach to ZZZ Approach vectored my flight to join the ILS [Runway] XX. They cleared us for the approach and told us to contact ZZZ on the CTAF frequency. The vector brought us in at 2;000 feet MSL and outside of ZZZZZ final fix. We had tuned and identified the ILS frequency. I was flying the aircraft and had intercepted the localizer. The glide slope needle on the Captain's ADI flagged. The FO's instruments were still working so control of the aircraft was transferred just outside of the final approach fix.  As we descended on the approach; I was monitoring the FO's progress. I had forgotten to make the needed radio call outs; (in the blind) on the CTAF frequency; to announce our presence on the approach and our intentions to land. On the landing roll; we saw the snow removal equipment clearing the runway at the far end.We were alerted to the presence of the snow removal operation upon our roll out on the runway. We also realized that the radio; although properly tuned into the stand-by position on the #1 radio; had not been switched over.The weather at ZZZ was a ceiling of 1;200 feet and visibility of 1 3/4 miles with blowing snow according to the ASOS. (There was nothing to advise us of snow removal activities in progress.) The instrumentation problem caused an unexpected transfer of control of the aircraft to a student pilot who was on his OE training. The transfer was completed successfully; but I was concerned about monitoring the progress of the student on the ILS approach. My attention was on the approach and I failed to make the needed callouts on the CTAF frequency to announce our arrival at ZZZ. The visibility prevented us from seeing the snow removal in progress as we approached the runway.We switched over to the proper frequency and proceeded to taxi in to the FBO.We could have used an alert from Approach control as to the presence of the snow removal equipment working on the surface at ZZZ or have that information broadcast on the ASOS.

column121: I have two questions/concerns: 1) We could use some clarification regarding minimum altitudes to begin turns after departure from MIA. The problem seems to be centered upon the different altitudes in the Obstacle DP on the [Page] 10-9A and those on the SIDS. The differences are significant and need to be reconciled. 2) Can anyone shed light on the Note #5 in the header section of the 11-3 ILS or LOC RWY (Runway) 9 chart; 'Autopilot coupled approach not authorized'? As per the AIM and Company's FOM definitions; a coupled approach refers to any approach performed by the autopilot. This is obviously not the same definition as an auto landing. There are currently exist 2 NOTAMs concerning autopilot usage; one for ILS RWY 9 and one for the ILS RWY 26L. Neither refer to auto landings. If the FAA meant to restrict auto landings on Runway 9 it should be clearly stated in the header notes or in a NOTAM. The way it is currently written leads one to believe ILS or LOC approaches to Runway 9 must be hand flown.Poorly written Jeppesen notes.Clarify with succinct verbiage.
