column121: While on visual approach to COS [Runway] 35L; crew both identified a very large building under construction with crane offset between 1.7 to 2 miles from the runway threshold. At approximately 1.5 miles from threshold; received EGPWS (Electronic Ground Proximity Warning) 'Obstacle; Pull Up!' warning. Aircraft was stable and on visual glide path as verified by FMS 3 degree visual glide path indicator and also visually with Runway 35L PAPI. Sink rate at time of incident was 500 ft. per minute. As the building was either directly underneath or just behind aircraft; no other obstacles were noted and aircraft was stable and on glide path; PIC (Pilot-in-Command) decided this was an erroneous warning and elected to continue flight. Landing was uneventful. During debrief; both crew members agreed that the message was erroneous and must be an anomaly caused by the new construction and a go-around at that time would be futile. If the weather was not CAVU (Ceiling and Visibility Unlimited) an immediate go-around would have been performed. There is a current NOTAM (01/037) regarding a crane at this location but the crane was offset from the flight path and at 300 ft. AGL. An issue exists with the EGPWS system and this new building construction that will trigger false warnings and may cause aircraft to go-around unnecessarily.

column121: Aircraft X was originally going to land at ZZZ; however; 2 new NOTAMs entered the system just before starting an instrument approach regarding standing water and ice on Runway 20 and the taxiways. Due to the wind and previous icing encountered; he decided to not land at ZZZ. I put him into the published holding at JADGU at 120. We discussed his options for many other close by airports; but due to forecast and known icing and moderate to severe turbulence throughout the area we were having a hard time coming up with a good airport. Aircraft X was also not fuel critical; but did not have a lot of reserve fuel. During this; I was working departures and arrivals into/out of DEN with moderate turbulence. I advised Aircraft X that PUB was 60 miles northeast; and had VFR weather with no known icing. I advised him that he would have to climb to 160; but only for a few minutes and they could descend to 140. Aircraft X decided to go to PUB airport. I climbed Aircraft X to 160 and he asked if it was about a 050 heading. I concurred; but he needed to climb first due to terrain before I could turn him there. He accepted the climb and then asked again about the heading. I told him to remain in the published hold and climb to 160 and I would have the turn on course above 130. He advised me that he was already turning to a 050 heading but was climbing to 130. At this point the MSAW alert was going off; but he was through130 in the 130 MIA. I reiterated the climb to 160 and told him the terrain climbed rapidly and to expedite as much as he could. At this point; Aircraft X was climbing through 140 heading into the 160 MIA. I advised Aircraft X the minimum IFR altitude is 160 and to report reaching. I should have issued a heading south as soon as he advised me he was already turning to PUB. I know the highest mountains in that area are not above 140; but the terrain climbs so rapidly in that area I should have known he would not have topped the 160 MIA even though he did climb above the 130 MIA. He also reported turbulence and icing at this time.The pilot should not have exited the published holding without a clearance to do so. I should have issued a south heading as soon as he told me he was turning out of hold. It was poor service on my end. I know all of the moderate reports and constant transmitter changes did not help the situation.

We were given a visual approach and he [turned] off the auto pilot at approximately 1;000 feet. At the 500 feet call he was stable; I don't recall the exact altitude after 500 feet as I was heads up looking outside and I noticed three red and one white on the PAPI. I did observe he was getting low on the glideslope. We had the ILS backed up and when I looked up; I saw four red and instructed him ' four reds; level off' we also received the oral warning glideslope at the same time. He did respond and leveled off approximately two to three seconds later and I saw the three red one white again. Landing was uneventful.It is also worth noting that we were into a 13 hour duty day due to weather delays and rescheduling.Also; worth noting was both Approach Control and Tower stated that they were receiving PIREPs of 40 knot headwinds until a 4 mile final. In actuality the winds were gusting up to 40 all the way to about a 1 mile final.

column121: While on an OE Refresher flight; I was the PM and my FO student was on his first PF leg in about 9 months due to being non-qualified during the COVID crisis.We were on a right down wind with a clearance to descend 2;000 feet. We were told to stop our descent and we leveled off at 2;600 feet. ATC alerted us to a helicopter crossing our path at 2;000 feet. We reported him in sight and ATC turned us for a right base. When we were clear of the traffic we were cleared to 2;000 feet and then for a visual approach with the airport in sight.  The FO called for gear down just outside the marker and managed the speed at the marker. We were at 170 knots at the time.  We had planned for a flaps 3 landing. With our encounter/interruption with the close traffic we didn't take into account that the marker was 3.4 miles from the runway and at 1;200 AGL. With the glide slope captured and a planned flaps 3 landing the airplane was having a hard time slowing down. I pulled the speed brakes to assist the aircraft and the FO was slowing the airplane down.  I pulled them just inside the marker and stowed them a few seconds later when the airspeed was near Vref. What I didn't realize was I had left them extended below 1;000 feet.  I think part of the issue is the approach seems a little nonstandard (standard: 5 NM; 1;500 feet AGL) and sneaks up on you with the marker at 3.4 NM from the runway at 1;200 feet.

column121: We operated Aircraft X from ZZZ to MDW; and the majority of the flight was uneventful. Mr. Y was my Captain and PF; and we had setup well in advance for the RNAV RNP Y approach for Runway 22L at MDW according to their latest ATIS. Later we received an ATIS update showing that the ILS for Runway 31C was active; so we re-configured and briefed that; still with plenty of time. We had discussed the possible need of diverting to ZZZ1 rather than our filed ALT1 of ZZZ2 and ALT2 of ZZZ3; but the visibility at MDW stayed above the minimums we needed for either the RNAV or ILS approaches to 22L/31C respectively.Fast forward to our first approach attempt: we're established on the ILS to 31C; fully configured well before the FAF and I was thinking we've got it made. We broke out at around 1;700 MSL and inflight visibility was not an issue and better than reported. From around 2;000 feet and down we experienced constant light and moderate turbulence; but no icing or precipitation. Captain had turned the autopilot and autothrottles off to help deal with the winds that were gusting from 260 degrees at 21 to 39 knots. We were also landing with stall protection ice speeds; adding 10 knots to our Vref and Vapp speeds.Everything was going great until somewhere between 1/2 to 1 mile from the approach end of 31C we got multiple 'WINDSHEAR' automated call outs from the airplane. Captain elected to go-around which we did; re-configuring as needed and I let MDW Tower know at around 400 feet AGL per the SOP. They gave us a heading and altitude to fly; asked us the reason for the go-around and handed us back off to Chicago Approach. We cleaned up the airplane and got vectored in for another ILS approach to 31C behind an Air Carrier Y.The second ILS approach was almost identical to the first; albeit with slightly less wind and turbulence. This time; even though we were ready for the conditions and knew more of what to expect; we managed to catch a gust of wind right in the flare and didn't touch down on Runway 31C until between the first third and the midpoint of the runway. Captain did a great job of thinking quick and performed a rejected landing; setting and calling 'TOGA' almost immediately after touchdown and off we went. We reconfigured again; got vectored around and handed off to Chicago Approach again and came around for a third attempt at Runway 31C.The third time Captain was able to land normally and well within the touchdown zone; but when he applied the brakes we found out that the '5/5/5' runway condition codes advertised for 31C were optimistic at best with the airplane noticeably sliding and skidding as we rolled out. We also noticed that there was blowing snow obscuring at least 50 percent of the runway surface. Captain advised MDW Tower of the braking action we experienced; calling it 'poor' and; to their credit; they immediately directed the broom and plow teams out to clear Runway 31C again. The rest of the flight was normal as we taxied into our gate.If there's anything I learned tonight; it's that just because you're on short final that doesn't mean you're going to land. Also: just because the Air Carrier Y flight that landed ahead of you reported 'braking action good' doesn't mean you will. I remember being on short final during our first ILS approach to 31C feeling complacent that we 'had it made' being on the ILS rather than RNAV RNP approach with its higher minimums and having broken out early at 1;700 feet. My go-around call outs also could have been crisper and more prompt; but that was mostly due to the 'startle' factor which was my own fault.My one suggestion would be for the airport operations crews at MDW to conduct their own braking action tests more often and with a more pessimistic eye. When the ATIS states RCCs are '5/5/5' the Air Carrier Y flight that landed ahead of you reports 'braking action good' and yet you experience significant wheel slip/skid due to icy runway surface conditions upon landing; thatindicates someone is being either dishonest or overly optimistic on their own braking action reports. If we or our Dispatch had known that the 'real' RCCs for 31C at MDW were actually 3/3/3 or even 2/2/2; we most likely would not have had the required landing performance numbers that we needed to land at MDW's shorter runways and would've gone to ZZZ1.
column125: Air carrier First Officer reported having to go-around/missed approach several times due to weather at MDW airport.  When they finally landed the crew experienced some sliding and skidding due to braking action being 'poor' contrary to the reported 'good braking action.'

column121: Descending via the NIIXX 2 arrival for the ILS Runway 35R in CAVOK conditions. Around the BSTON Intersection we were given a westerly to northwesterly radar vector and cleared to descend and maintain 10;000. As we got closer to the final approach course (somewhere between DEANE and DRUMM) we were cleared to descend and maintain 8;000 and cleared direct to the FRONZ Intersection (FAF for the ILS). The Controller asked if we had 35R in sight; after a pause; we had the runway in sight and advised ATC. We were then cleared for the visual approach to 35R. The FAF altitude at FRONZ is 7;000 so that is what we set in the MCP while descending through approximately 8;800 in FLCH. We had just gotten the '2;500' call from the Radio Altimeter and made our call outs. As we descended through 8;000 I noticed the Radio Altitude around 1;800 and descending. A quick look at JeppFD PRO showed our aircraft position just south and east of the DRUMM Intersection. I called out the altitude and the fact that we need to go back up to 8;000 (our last cleared altitude prior to the approach clearance) until we are north of DRUMM. I reset the MCP to 8;000 and verbalized that we need to climb back up. The Captain selected a V/S climb back to 8;000. We joined the approach course and followed the G/S and landed without incident.ATC never mentioned anything to us about violating airspace or going below the DEN Class B. Though it is nice to get directs; etc. However; I believe the direct to FRONZ clearance; if not really familiar with the DEN Class B; can set crews up to possibly violate airspace and even lead to an unstable approach.Once parked at the gate with all checklists complete; we debriefed and there certainly was some confusion as to exactly where we were when we descended below 8;000. We had a jump seat observer who thought we might have been a little low as he has a lot of General Aviation experience in the DEN area.

column121: Coming in to ROA we were cleared for the visual approach for Runway 34; I was PM and Captain was PF. We were set up for a left base and kept our descent coming down to 4;000 feet. On the descent down we received a GPWS warning for terrain and Captain took immediate action and we climbed out of the terrain we were near. Captain and I both had our terrain mode on; but it was not showing up on the MFD for both sides. ATC also reported to us if we saw the terrain ahead before we received the GPWS.I believe this started from the beginning of our trip both Captain and I were deadheading from ZZZ to ZZZ1 and the aircraft had a hydraulic issue which made our flight delayed. Which got rid of 2 of our 3 flights that we were supposed to operate and made this flight our first flight of the day together. This was also my first time at ROA and Captain told me that he has been there a couple times before so I was unfamiliar with the airport and the surrounding terrain. With it also being a moonless night it was very hard to see the terrain in front of us and with terrain mode on but it wasn't showing us anything made it very difficult to determine exactly where we were at on the ILS approach plate when we were in VFR conditions. I also should have spoken up when I was looking on the approach plate that the highest terrain was 3;508 and Captain was setting the altitude bug to 4;000 feet. I believe our vertical speed was also a factor but I don't remember exactly what Captain had it set to. Captain and I also debriefed together to figure out what we did wrong and what we did right and learn from our mistakes.Add terrain photos or jepp plates on the 10-7 pages to help with situational awareness.
column123: Cleared for ILS 34 on an extended left base and received the ground proximity warning for terrain on decent to the final approach fix altitude. Initially cleared to altitude on our route to 9;000 feet crossing Pulaski (PSK) [VOR]. After PSK on rout to ROA we were then given a decent down to 6;000 feet. Clear night we called airport in sight for the sight of the rotating beacon we set up for ILS 34. On an extended left base approach control gave us heading 090 and we were cleared for the visual to 34. After our clearance I gave a further turn to heading 100 and tried to widen out the base for Runway 34. With our clearance I descended down to 4;000 initially keeping in mind we had a tailwind until turning on final for Runway 34. On our descent to 4;000 we both switched to terrain mode on the PFD and saw nothing. Initially I was still on my legs page on my IPAD and my First Officer on the approach plate. We got a call from Approach asking us if we know the terrain around us and while he asked leveling at 4;000 we got a a TERRAIN TERRAIN PULL UP WARNING. We exercised the escape maneuver and leveled at 6;000. We replied to the approach that we exercised the escape maneuver and discussed that we thought we were south of the terrain further back. At night it is hard to distinguish that but exercised extreme caution until back on final.Cause - No initial knowledge of the area of terrain at night even in terrain mode on PFD. Cross reference with iPad to select altitude until final. Suggestion - I will hold the last cleared altitude at 6;000 feet and go further out towards the IAF before turning for final and descending to final altitude before glide slope capture.

column121: We were flying into GUC in a non RNP aircraft. Winds were 250/11; SKC; elected to fly [Runway] 06 approach per ops pages; then enter downwind via Plato to GU24D. Used RNAV for SA (Situational Awareness); but did not Nav due to UNABLE RNAV RNP. When level at 9200 feet in valley on down wind; received 3 separate GPWS 'terrain; terrain' erroneous indications. There was no terrain and we were not descending. Additionally; there was no red GPWS indications on ND; just the aural cue one time with red warning light. I was the PF (Pilot Flying); and we both were perplexed by the indication. We continued the approach; as there was no reason to react to this partial warning. This event did make us question our flight path; but we also agreed that all was performed correctly. We didn't see any warnings about erroneous GPWS; and the Aircraft appeared to have no issues. We both also agreed that flying a Basic airplane that is not RNP capable into Gunnison was unsatisfactory. Are we to land with a 10 kt. tailwind to avoid indications such as this when performing the pattern as prescribed by the company?Flying a Basic; non-RNP AC into a challenging mountainous terrain airportOur original aircraft was swapped out for some reason. Non RNP airplanes should go to basic destinations.

column123: My student and I took off in his airplane from ZZZ and flew to ZZZ1 where we added some fuel to the airplane. Then we took off and flew to ZZZ2 where we began an ILS practice approach. On final the gear would not extend. We went missed and flew around for an hour and forty five minutes trying to get the gear and flaps to extend. Only the nose gear came down. We decided to land at ZZZ2 so called approach for landing. They immediately turned us over to the tower where we explained our situation. WE then landed on [Runway] XXR on the nose wheel and the tail tiedown ring. It stayed on the tiedown ring until it was almost stopped then came to rest on the left wing. We departed the airplane with no issues. There was very minimal damage to the plane. Later we discovered the ELT battery was due for replacement 60 days earlier.

column121: Description of Event: The aircraft with PIC as above and one passenger was returning to ZZZ from a 1-hour cross-country flight that originated at ZZZ1. The flight was uneventful in VFR weather; with altitude from 2;000 feet to 1;600 feet after takeoff and climbout until approach. Flight path was as depicted in the charted course; following VFR altitude restrictions as required within ZZZ2 Mode C airspace; and with ADS-B Out active on 1200 a required. Visibility was observed to be 10+ miles with a solid cloud cover (overcast) at 9;000-12;000 feet. Winds were variable 6-9 knots at approximately 230-260 degrees from surface to flight altitude. Within 10 nm on the approach to ZZZ on a vector of 220; while monitoring ZZZ voice radio on 123.00; little traffic within the airport operations area was observed or noted on the radio. At approximately 8 nm from ZZZ; an inbound aircraft was announced on my ForeFlight display through the ADS-B In signal at my 7 o'clock; 400 feet below and overtaking. That Cessna 152 pilot announced his intention for a straight-in approach to ZZZ runway XX. Communicated directly with that PIC and told him that we would slow down to distance behind him on my own straight-in approach to ZZZ runway XX; since he already had a 25+ mph speed advantage. Within 2 minutes; the Cessna 152 had passed us by on our left side and descended ahead of us to line up for Runway XX. In the process; I lost visual contact with that aircraft; and since there was no other traffic in the area; and ForeFlight did not show a blip for its position; I decided to perform a slight S-turn to the left to further delay our landing until the 152 announced he was clear of Runway XX. At approximately 4 nm from the threshold; with our speed down to 62 mph (58 mph Ground Speed); the Cessna 152 PIC announced 'clear of Runway  XX ZZZ'; after which I immediately announced a straight-in approach for Runway XX ZZZ and began to adjust our speed and trim to line up with the two VASI lights on the glide path. At approximately 2 nm; we were high (two white lights) but gradually settled to a nominal (white/red) glide slope on the final approach. We completed visual and verbal cockpit checks and I glanced outboard at the right Flaperon position (there being no flap setting indicator in this CH750 STOL; and no stall warning annunciation); but did not notice anything incorrect; even though I had earlier made a mental note that we would be making a flaps-up; standard landing instead of the full-flaps; STOL landings I usually make. We settled slightly low of nominal (two red lights) as we approached the threshold and at that moment; I made a mental 'flip' or attitude adjustment that we were about to land STOL; and I decreased power for a short-field landing. Our sink rate increased; but mentally; I still had confidence that the STOL nose-up attitude was safe; and did not add power but instead retarded it to idle as we crossed the threshold of Runway XX. Without flaps; our stall speed was 48 mph instead of 42 mph; and we stalled out at approximately 12' above the numbers and fell in a slightly nose-high attitude until impacting on the main and then nose gear. The aircraft bounced up and then impacted slightly nose-down; collapsing the nose strut; blowing out the nose tire; and stopping the engine as the fiberglass prop blades impacted and flexibly shattered.We immediately checked for fuel leaks even though the aircraft came to rest tail-high. I switched off the ignition and removed the key; exited the cockpit; checked again for fuel leaks (none); then shut down the avionics manually with the master switch; and turned the Fuel Selector valve to OFF. After ascertaining that the aircraft was not an immediate hazard and that both Pilot and Passenger were able to move about without difficulty; we called other airport residents; and cleared the CH750 from Runway XX.Conclusion: In summation; distracted flying due to traffic avoidance caused energy mismanagement resulting in a stall upon landing flare with flaps retracted and subsequent nose gear collapse without injuries.

column121: On the downwind landing at ZZZ; I was #2 in the pattern when abeam the touchdown point. The plane that was #1 had just touched down; and around the same time my engine began stuttering and soon after failed. At that point; I had begun my descent and was at [approximately] 800 ft. AGL. I began to attempt to restart the engine and was unable to do so. I then decided to execute a power off 180 degree turn and land on the runway. Perhaps as a result of attempting to restart my engine and triggering the master on/off; I lost communication with ZZZ Tower (I had been previously communicating with them). The plane that was previously #1 was already quite far down the runway and not a factor. I ultimately ended up landing safely; although never received clear to land instructions from Tower due to communications failure. I attempted to advise ATC when the engine failed; however I don't believe this was communicated.Upon landing; I noticed that both of the fuel selector valves were turned off. I am not sure if these were inadvertently disabled when I was attempting to restart the engine; or perhaps I had accidentally turned both of them off before engine failure. I am skeptical that this would be the case; as I remember ensuring that they were both enabled as part of my descent checklist ~15 minutes before the engine failed. It is indeed possible that I may have turned both to the 'off' position accidentally during the descent checklist; but I do not believe the engine would have continued to operate for this long were they to be off.I do not have a clear recollection of what actions I took to restart the engine in the air as it is frankly quite a blur. After being on the ground; I enabled both fuel selector valves and attempted to restart my engine without success. I tried a second time; and it started and I taxied off the runway (at that point 2 fire trucks and a police car had arrived; and I taxied off in convoy with them).A takeaway for me is that; regardless of the cause of the engine failure; my previous flights had been in another model of airplane; and while I was very familiar with Aircraft X; I would say that I should have spent more time familiarizing myself with the emergency procedures checklist. Also; earlier in the day while pre-flighting; there were some irregularities with the communications systems in the plane; and also it was very difficult to start the engine. I did not feel that these issues were enough to warrant aborting the flight; but in hindsight perhaps I should not have flown that plane that day and had it serviced instead.

column121: Landing Runway 26 Using Visual approach guidance from GTN 650(Garmin Navigation/Display). No glideslope guidance was provided by the GTN. I crossed what appeared to be the threshold with very faded marking in significant turbulence. I taxied to the ramp without incident. Only after reviewing the track log from foreflight on google earth did I realize I may have actually landed on the displaced threshold. I believe better markings on the displaced threshold would help prevent this in the future. In addition; a more positive identification of runway markings on my part and a go around would have prevented this event. Contributing was a runway change from our planned arrival to Runway 35R due to changing winds.

column121: On a flight from ZZZ to ZZZ1 in my TBM 700 (Aircraft X); I experienced moderate icing and severe turbulence and wind shear. On the approach to ZZZ1 moderate icing was reported to ATC which allowed me to descend to 2;000 ft. MSL. This got me out of IMC temporarily. I visually flew directly over ZZZ2. Shortly after I reentered IMC and experienced severe turbulence and wind shear. I had difficulty maintaining heading and altitude. It also caused me to be disoriented. I got heading and altitude warnings from ATC. I reported the severe turbulence and requested a vector twice. The second time registered and by then I was in moderate turbulence; icing and intermediate wind shear. ATC suggested an immediate landing at ZZZ2. I wanted no part of going close to whatever I had just passed thru and proceeded to ZZZ1. The airport came into view and I landed hot with one notch of flaps because of the wind shear and the fact that the plane was iced up. ATC was very helpful.

column121: On final [Runway] XXL ZZZ we were paired up with traffic on another runway in visual conditions. I thought the FO had selected TA on the transponder. After debriefing on the ground he told me that he was telling me to set TA on the transponder; but what I heard was transponder is set to TA. Inside ZZZZZ [Intersection] at about 1;200 feet we got an RA Climb. I verbalized 'We got to go.' I surmised that the TCAS RA would be followed by a go-around. My communication was unclear. The traffic was in sight throughout the approach. The FO continued the approach and landed uneventfully. We should have gone around; but after debriefing I understood that the FO thought it might be unsafe to climb and go-around with the other traffic so close.
column123: With the A.P. and A.T. off; I was P.F. on the visual approach to Runway XX.  ATC calls out traffic at our 2 o'clock that will be close to us for another runway.  We call the Aircraft Y traffic in sight and proceed inbound.  Both the Captain and I are wearing masks (COVID); which I believe muffled my request to 'Set to TA only.'  The Captain later said he thought I said 'We're set to TA only.'  The approach continued normally with us going to managed speed; gear down; as we started down the glide slope inside the FAF.  Just after I called for flaps 3 around 1;400 feet AGL; I noted the Aircraft Y a little above us and the appropriate distance to the right joining up with us.  I was still slowing the aircraft at that moment; and the RA went off 'Climb; Climb.'  I asked the Captain 'We are not in TA only?'  He responded 'Well we are going to have to go.'  I momentarily considered going around; but thought continuing the approach was actually the safest course of action when the RA immediately stopped.  We continued the approach and landed uneventfully.  The RA happened the moment the Aircraft Y was joining the other localizer course.  What I should have done is gone around.  Following the RA is the right thing to do unless it is unsafe to do so.

I noted that aircraft was 3 miles from the runway and 600 feet AGL. The drone was slightly below us (estimate 400 feet) and East of the extended centerline (estimate 2;500 feet). At first; I thought it might be a balloon; but recognized it as a drone as it passed abeam and behind us. The FO (First Officer) was flying and given the phase of flight and that I did not feel that it was an immediate threat; I elected to wait until we were clear of the runway to inform the FO and Ground Control.

column121: We were on the ILS 09R at PHL approximately 15 DME when the Localizer exhibited erratic deviations on both the Captain and First Officers  Primary Flight Displays. The Flight Director and Auto Pilot was engaged and attempted to 'chase' the course which started to worsen the situation. ATC advised us that we appeared to be about a mile south of the localizer course; slightly less than 1/2 dot deflection at this point. The First Officer disengaged the autopilot and hand-flew the approach with no further issue. The deviation appeared to subside as we got closer to the field and we were within stable approach parameters from the Final Approach Fix until touchdown. Also; the NAVAID had been positively tuned/identified per company SOP prior to commencing the approach. Course deviation on the HSI followed by ATC advising us that we were right of course. Unreliable Localizer signal at PHL which is apparently a known issue.The First Officer disconnected the autopilot and hand flew. I notified maintenance and was told by multiple maintenance personnel both on the phone and in person at the aircraft that this was a known issue with runway 09R; it happens all the time; and that there's a NOTAM in effect regarding the issue. I searched all of the NOTAMs  and could not find anything remotely related to unreliable LOC/ILS on 09R. I do not believe that the FAA would allow a malfunctioning NAVAID to remain in service and do not think there is any merit in the maintenance department's claim that this is a known problem that we should just accept and learn to deal with. However; if there is a known problem with this or any other NAVAID that necessitates a procedure change; then the company should disseminate that information to the pilot group with an FIL; etc.
column125: An Air Carrier pilot reported the PHL Runway 09R Localizer is unreliable and caused a course deviation. The reporter stated maintenance personnel told him it was a known issue and a NOTAM was published but their are no NOTAM's addressing this issue.

column121: While on approach to ZZZ in day VMC conditions; crew was instructed to fly direct 'ZZZZZ' - the FAF for ILS XX - for a visual approach to [Runway] XX. The crew had been left high by both Center and Approach; but was able to reach the 5;000 ft. and slow prior to intercept. The PIC/PF (Pilot Flying) briefed the SIC/PM (Pilot Monitoring) on speed/energy management and airspeed 'windows' prior to the approach. After reporting runway in sight and receiving the approach clearance; the PF initiated descent to 4;500 ft. (the FAF altitude) and began to lessen the intercept angle; and PM prompted gear down and flaps 22. Aircraft still slightly overshot the localizer but was able to capture both LOC and GS which happened simultaneously. 4;500 ft. is within 1;300 ft. AGL of field elevation; and due to the short distance both vertically to the runway and laterally (just outside 4 miles); the PM/PIC anticipated an unstable approach and called a go around. The PF pressed the TOGA (Takeoff Go-Around); disconnected the autopilot; and called 'check thrust flaps nine' and the PM set flaps 9 and checked the thrust to discover the PF had not yet increased thrust to detents. Concurrently with the 'check thrust flaps 9 actions'; and 'Sink Rate' EGPWS warning occurred; and momentary hesitation by PF prompted PM/PIC to take control and initiate the immediate action in response to the 'sink rate' AURAL as required for the EGPWS escape; with a 'Pull Up' Aural warning occurring concurrently with the initiation of the EGPWS escape procedure. (At the time of initiation the EGPWS escape; PIC noted an 1;800 fpm descent rate). Upon reaching a safe altitude; the aircraft configuration was cleaned up in line as normal; and flight continued back for ILS without further incident. One error was made during EGPWS escape; which was PIC only increasing thrust to detents and not to max. Upon completion of the flight; crew debriefed on a number of key items. These included energy management and the approach profile in the AOM; go around procedure and ensuring power is set concurrently with pitch and callout when conducting a missed; and the EGPWS escape maneuver.Crews should act very early to mitigate unstable approach situations; including in a situation like this requesting a lower altitude earlier and a vector out to the IAF in lieu of the FAF; this can alleviate the chances of an unstable approach long before the issue manifests. Missed approaches should be initiated immediately upon identification of an unstable approach. In the event of a missed approach; crews should ensure the action is taken decisively including the increase of power; and both pilots should remain involved in the process. Should a GPWS warning be received; decisive; immediate action should be taken - as per the AOM - superseding normal missed approach actions due to the time critical nature of high vertical speed close to the ground. Captains especially should keep a close eye on FOs conducting operations at outstations - in this situation; having approach offer a better vector to join or taking an actual ILS clearance may have prevented the situation from manifesting at all.

column121: While instructing a student in the pattern at ZZZ; just before turning onto our base leg; we were advised to make a wide left 360 and re enter the pattern on the right downwind for Runway XX. As we began our turn to the left we noticed an incoming airplane on a left downwind flying at same altitude in our path. I took controls from my student and we rapidly began to descend. If wasn't for our quick reaction it surely would have been a mid air collision. The incoming traffic began a climb in order to avoid our path. The incoming traffic was out of our line of sight but ATC still made the call giving us clearance to turn after I voiced that we did not have the traffic behind us insight. This event was noticed by another controller and he promptly relieved his coworker and handled the situation. This controller has been known around flight schools operating at ZZZ to decrease safety in the sky. I felt it was imperative to report this event.

column121: At 10;000 ft. cleared direct ZZZZZ and cleared visual RWY XXL ZZZ. We both agreed 8;000 ft. was a safe altitude due to terrain then 7;000 ft. after clearing the hills. Flying pilot; FO (First Officer); set 8;000 ft. then 7;000 ft. in the altitude window. It seemed we were high since we delayed the descent so we were trying to get down. I did not notice the altitude was set to 6;000 ft. Approaching ZZZZZ I realized we were low and told the FO to stop the descent. We climbed slightly and joined the Glideslope to continue the approach. We did not get any terrain warnings but it was concerning that we descended lower than anticipated at night into a high altitude airport.The FO was low time in the airplane and had just came back from a 5 year absence. I did not realize he was behind a bit on the automation. While I was trying to figure out what he was doing I lost situational awareness even though we had discussed safe altitudes. It was also the third leg we had flown that day so we were both tired.I was overly depending on the automation to help reduce the work load for both of us. The automation will only help if it is programed correctly so a person has to be very careful to cross check all available information. From now on I think it is best to request an instrument approach when it's dark going into airports you're not 100 percent familiar with. From now on I will at the very least request to join the visual further out on the instrument approach course in order to get more stabilized.

column121: While on final approach into Seattle on a visual approach to [Runway] 16R; after Capturing glide path with the auto pilot; we were configuring the last of our flaps through 1;000 ft. and the Captain called for below the line; while running the checklist the Captain made the remark we were looking low. As I came up the plane was in the flight director; however for some reason the auto pilot had gotten knocked out of GS mode and defaulted to pitch hold. As the Captain started to correct back to the PAPIs we received a low alt alert from Tower; along with a glide slope warning from EGPWS. The Tower saw us correcting during the call however and noted disregard. As we were able to re established on glide path and were stable before 500 ft. we continued the approach to land. Not sure why the flight director disconnected from the glide slope and defaulted to pitch hold; and if was something we did while configuring; or if it was a fault of the plane or ground equipment.I was distracted by the running of the checklist and did not see the entities deviation from glide path; more why the autopilot changed modes. After returning to the displays I was getting conflicting information from the flight director and the glide slope indication. It would have been easier to just look outside and get the indications from the PAPI as to our current airplane state.
column123: ILS [Runway] 16R into Seattle; visual conditions; LOC and GS captured with autopilot engaged. After aircraft configuration complete I felt like we were low. I saw that we were now in pitch hold and below glide path. LOC was still captured. I disconnected autopilot and leveled off; heard 'Glide-slope' as well. ATC was informing us about low altitude alert; then immediately said disregard. I regained glide-slope utilizing PAPI.Not sure why the GS disconnected; if it was something we did wrong or if it was a fluke or something to do with the system. I talked to the Captain taking the airplane about the event and suggested to use the ILS. Lesson learned; at all times monitor glide path and lat/vertical modes. The transition from GS to pitch hold was very subtle.

The crew regained control following a ground proximity warning and low altitude alert from air traffic control.

column121: Upon reaching clearance for ILS XX circle Runway XY at ZZZ from Approach; we were switched over to ZZZ Tower frequency. Established on the approach between the IAF and FAF; I keyed the mic to establish contact with Tower inbound. The entire avionics turned off completely; losing intercom; transponder; GPS/Nav/Comm 1; and Nav/Comm 2. ZZZ was currently under IFR due to 2SM visibility; but we were in visual conditions on the approach course. I broke off from the approach and turned to the south; since we were no longer receiving navigational guidance along the approach; and I had no communications to contact Tower or Approach. I knew the weather towards the south was looking clearer so the plan was to continue south towards ZZZ1 or ZZZ2. We troubleshot the avionics; checking circuit breakers and cycling the avionics switch to no avail. No indications of ammeter discharge or low voltage illuminated; and the turn coordinator was still operable as well as engine instruments; so I presumed that the failure was based solely on avionics and not complete electrical failure. We descended lower; maintaining VFR so that we could receive cell signal; and I contacted ZZZ Tower via cellphone; since they gave us instructors their phone number another time when they had an ATC/instructor meeting. I told them we were okay; and had complete avionics failure. I asked for the phone number for ZZZ1 Tower so I could communicate with them if I decided to continue south to ZZZ1. At that time; the visibility and clouds around ZZZ began to clear up and we could start to see towards ZZZ. I asked ZZZ Tower if the field was still IFR; and they replied that it just went VFR. We proceeded inbound toward ZZZ; maintaining VFR; and were able to receive landing and taxi clearances for our aircraft through the cell phone. The aircraft was repaired; with the problem being a short-circuited avionics switch.
column125: C172 flight instructor reported a complete failure of communication and navigation equipment while on an instrument approach and in reduced visibility. The instructor broke off the approach; remaining in visual conditions; and was eventually able to contact ATC on a cell phone and make a successful approach and landing.

column121: This was my third solo XC (Cross Country). Flight planning was approved by the instructor. Weather and NOTAMs were reviewed by both myself and instructor and flight was deemed safe to proceed. There were no NOTAMs or field condition reports for the destination airport. One pattern was flown at departure airport with an instructor to update currency prior to departure of solo flight. Takeoff; climb; en route; and descent were uneventful. During a visual approach to Runway 02 at the uncontrolled airport; I noticed patchy snow on the runway. I noticed other aircraft had landed recently. I was expecting a normal landing to a touch and go. ASOS indicated the winds were from the northwest at 8 kts. I touched down on centerline at 67 kts. Before I set the nose down; the aircraft started veering to the left. I made the attempt to correct and the airplane continued to slide to the left. I believe due to the unresponsiveness of my control inputs; I was on ice. I made the decision to full stop and applied brakes. The aircraft continued to slide on the icy portion of the runway with no response to my braking or control inputs. The aircraft struck a snow bank on the left side of the runway. The aircraft tipped forward and to the right; resulting in damage to the propeller and the right wingtip. I'm not sure if this is a reportable incident/accident at this time. The following day; a NOTAM was issued for 30 PCT patchy snow and ice.	I believe I landed on a patch of ice. There were no NOTAMs published at the time of my departure. Had a NOTAM been published for wintery conditions; I would not have attempted a solo XC to this airport. I have not yet been trained to land in wintery conditions. Based on previous landing markings on the snow in the runway; I assumed it was safe to land. My relative inexperience resulted in not recognizing the ice under the snow. I should've gone around. In the future; I will take a harder look at my experience level and the conditions present in order to make a better decision. I have learned a valuable lesson to always err on the side of caution.
column125: Student Pilot reported on landing losing control due to ice; which resulted in loss of control; a runway excursion; and damage to the aircraft.

column121: Enroute ZZZ-San Salvador at FL390 we were able to ascertain through ATC Control that Runway 25 was in use and that we could expect to proceed via CAT VOR and execute the ILS Y 25. The FO (First Officer) was the Pilot Flying and thoroughly briefed the approach and his expectations for altitudes; automation modes and aircraft configuration throughout the approach. We were turned over to San Salvador Approach approximately 70 miles from the airport descending to FL200. San Salvador Approach cleared us direct CAT VOR; cross VOR above 5;000 [ft.] cleared ILS Y Runway 25. Both pilots confirmed clearance; altitudes and automation as we proceeded. As we neared CAT we were around 8;000 [ft.] at 240 kts.; still on the VNAV path. Although the FMC appeared to like the profile we both agreed it was time to slow down to meet the briefed approach profile. The FO selected vertical speed and dialed the speed back in the MCP window. With the selected vertical speed we weren't slowing down so I recommended Level Change. We slowed; configured and continued the approach passing CAT VOR. I set 2;500 [ft.] in the altitude window to fly outbound from the VOR as we completed the landing checklist. Once crossing the fix at [8.0 nm] we turned inbound via LNAV and set the FAF crossing altitude of 1;600 [ft.]; approach mode selected. We were in VFR conditions with the airport in sight on my side of the aircraft. As we turned towards the airport and descended I noticed we seemed low since we were more than 8 miles away and below the glide path. I looked back at the FMC and noticed fix [7.8 nm] with a crossing altitude of 2;500 [ft]. Shortly afterwards ATC asked what our altitude was and I replied 1;600 [ft.]. At that point both the FO and I realized we had missed [7.8 nm] on the procedure and should have crossed it at 2;500 [ft.]. [7.8 nm] is not clearly depicted on the approach plate with a crossing altitude nor is there an altitude depicted for the turn inbound. Also; we flew the procedure properly if the automation was in VNAV; but it wasn't. Although it was nighttime we were in VFR conditions with the airport in sight throughout the procedure. No GPWS cautions. At no time did I feel the procedure unsafe.
column123: I was the PF.  CENAMER approach cleared us to descend out of FL390 to FL 200.  We were expecting the ILSY Rwy 25.  We briefed the approach thoroughly and I explained I would stay in VNAV until lined up on final approach where I would select APP for the ILS.   Well above FL200; we were handed off to ElSal approach.  They told us to proceed to CAT VOR and cleared us for a full approach ILS Y Rwy 25 approach with procedure turn.  HOWEVER; we were high before arriving at CAT so I selected to use vertical speed and open the speed window to control speed.  The CAPT suggested I use LVL CHG instead since it would do a better job getting down and controlling speed.  I did so; while  flaps and gear helped slow us.  I was still well above 3500'; over the VOR beginning the procedure turn outbound to D8.0.  Still in LVL CHG; We were able to fully configure and be on the VNAV PATH at 2500' at D8.0.  We completed the LANDING CHECKLIST.  At this point we had the airport in sight and never lost contact the whole time.  We were both using TERR MODE for obstacle clearance.  After passing D8.0 I asked the CAPT to select the next lower altitude of 1600' as depicted on the approach chart; and he did so.  In my haste to get at the correct altitude; slow down and  configure for landing; I did not  realize I had not reselected VNAV.  The autopilot descended us to 1600' as we turned toward final.  We crossed D7.8 at 1600' vice 2500'..  There was no GPWS warning and we had the airport in sight the whole time.  On final approach I selected APP and we noticed we looked low and at the same time; the control tower queried us about our altitude.  That was when we noticed our error but were already capturing the glideslope at that time.  We continued the approach; landed safely and taxied to our gate without further incident.

column121: I departed ZZZ after topping off my aircraft fuel tanks. I flew VFR in VMC conditions to PDK. The traffic pattern very congested so much so that several aircraft had difficulty obtaining clearance to enter the controlled airspace at PDK. After monitoring the tower frequency; I elected to call ATL Approach to request a VFR ILS to PDK for a full stop landing. This would help me to practice a VFR approach; setting up the approach; capturing the localizer and glide-slope; Because I was solo I remained alert to visually remain clear of all aircraft nearby.I contacted ATL Approach and received a discreet transponder code as well as VFR vectors for the approach; I was vectored through the ILS localizer for sequencing and then cleared for the approach just outside of the FAF CHAMB and was transferred to the PDK tower frequency.The radio traffic was so heavy that I was not able to transmit before the tower called my call sign and cleared me for landing number 2 behind jet traffic. Because I was cleared to land #2 behind the jet; this contributed to my understanding I would land on Runway 21L; the longer runway used by Jets. The parallel Runway 21R is shorter and primarily used by piston and training aircraft.I continued on approach on the Localizer and Glide slope until short final. When I was approximately over the Runway 21 L approach end I observed Aircraft Y fly over head at approximately 75 ft. at a faster speed. I immediately called the tower and reported the aircraft overhead while side stepping to the right to go around. The tower sequenced me to land on the parallel Runway 21R and I continue to a safe landingThe issue was I was cleared for the ILS by ATL approach and expected to land on Runway 21L. Also I was cleared initially by the tower to land #2 behind Jet traffic However; the tower said that they cleared me to land on [Runway] 21R.I have been flying from this airport for over XX years and I as well as many other pilots regularly receive runway landing changes often on short final. In this case I may have misunderstood or did not hear correctly the landing clearance and as a result had a near miss with another aircraft.Contributing factors was the radio frequency congestion as well as the high volume of aircraft in the airspace; and my expectation to land on the longer runway behind jet traffic. Also the Aircraft Y pilot did not see me as he approached from above and behind my aircraft; which also contributed to the near miss. I have on board Aircraft X an L3 active TCAS as well as ADSB traffic advisory. I do not believe I heard or saw a traffic alert on my avionics.

column121: While on approach into CHO we were being vectored by Potomac Approach on frequency 126.4. We were on a right downwind being vectored for the ILS Runway 03. It was IMC conditions with light to moderate rime ice accumulating on the aircraft. At 3;000 ft. MSL we were given a right turn; a base turn; toward the final for Runway 03. We were just outside; south of the fix WULDU. I noticed were getting close to the localizer course for the final approach; and Potomac had not issued a turn for us to intercept the approach. I prompted Potomac approach and tried to establish communication. There was no answer on 126.4. I prompted again; and still there was no answer from Potomac. At this time we crossed over the localizer course without a turn to intercept given from Potomac. I had guard (121.5) already tuned in on comm 2. I heard Potomac approach trying to hail us on 121.5 on guard and advised us to switch to frequency 132.85. I switched to 132.85 and they gave us an immediate turn to the right to reestablish us on the localizer course. The coverage on 126.4 for the area was lost and I confirmed that with Potomac that we were trying to contact them. Apparently a Learjet overheard us trying to transmit on 126.4 and relayed it to Potomac. Once we re-established communication; Potomac gave us that right turn to intercept but it was 'too late'; The aircraft GPWS detected terrain and gave us a 'Terrain; Terrain' 'PULL UP' audio warning. I directed the First Officer to Climb the aircraft and disregard the instruction from Potomac Approach. We climbed up to 3;500 ft. MSL and got the aircraft clear of the terrain. I advise Potomac what had happened and that we were unable to intercept the approach from the vector that they gave us. Potomac than gave us more vectors to get re-established on the approach. From there we landed with no further issues. I conversed with Potomac about the drop in radio coverage on frequency 126.4. They agreed and confirmed that they could not hear us and we could not hear them on 126.4.[Problem was identified by] flight crew scan. No communication from ATC have prompting them several times. Having Guard (121.5) on comm 2 and have our audio on to re-establish communication. Being aware of the terrain in the area and seeing the aircraft GPS position on The Jepp charts. [Cause was] loss of two-way communication on 126.4 between our aircraft and Potomac approach lead us to not receiving an instruction to intercept the localizer course. Without that instruction we continued on our heading and had to re-establish communication via guard and switch to a different frequency that has radio coverage in the area. All of this lead to vectors in an area with terrain and caused us to be in a position where the aircraft GPWS was activated. [We] contacted ATC. Followed the aircraft GWPS system and climbed to avoid terrain. Advised ATC that we were responding to a GWPS annunciation. [Suggest] better radio coverage equipment is needed for the area around CHO. Suggest the importance of ALWAYS monitoring 121.5 on Comm 2 for flight crews.

column121: During taxi to Runway 31L; ground control told us to hold short of the approach end of Runway 22 at Taxiway Z. As another aircraft was landing; ground told us to cross Runway 22 In front of the aircraft on landing roll and told that aircraft to give way to us. As we began to cross in front of the aircraft on landing roll; there was no indication that the aircraft was slowing down to give way to us. So I stopped our aircraft and the aircraft on landing roll passed in front of us. Ground said that we shouldn't have stopped.This event was caused by ground control setting up a disaster scenario by wanting us to cross in front of the aircraft on landing roll. If anything went wrong; like brake failure of the landing aircraft; then there would have been a potentially catastrophic aircraft accident.In the future; ground shouldn't cross aircraft in front of other aircraft on landing roll unless they are positive that the aircraft on landing roll has slowed down sufficiently to be safe.
column123: We were instructed to taxi from FBO to '[Runway] 31L via Zulu; hold short approach end [Runway] 22 at Zulu'. We exited the FBO ramp onto Zulu; turned left towards [Runway] 22 and noticed that [Taxiway] Zulu terminates before the approach end of [Runway] 22; and it becomes [Taxiway] Yankee. We discussed this on the flight deck that the taxi instructions should have been clearer because you can't really hold short of [Runway] 22 on Zulu; you would actually either hold short of [Runway] 22 approach on Yankee; or hold short of Yankee on Zulu. In any case; the Captain as PF (Pilot Flying) exercised some good judgement and decided to stop a bit back from Yankee; at least from the sign anyways. It's hard to figure where Zulu actually ended. That way we could respond to Ground if they modified their instructions to hold back further. Whatever is the case with Zulu or Yankee; we stopped and awaited further instructions. We noticed a bizjet (Aircraft Y) landing on [Runway] 4 so we suspected we were being held from crossing the approach end of [Runway] 22 (same as rollout end of [Runway] 4) due to the landing traffic. After a short wait; we heard ground instruct Aircraft Y which was now rolling out to 'give way to Aircraft X; he will be crossing at the end of the runway in front of you'. I cannot recall if the other aircraft acknowledged. The next thing we both heard was 'Aircraft X; cross Runway 22 and continue via Kilo'. I turned all the lights on and the Captain started to move forward. I looked right to clear and noticed the landing Aircraft Y was very close; it seemed to me he was past Bravo and Kilo 2 where I presumed he would have turned off; that his reversers were still deployed; and he was rolling very fast for so close to the end of the runway. I said 'STOP; we're not clear' or words to that effect and the Captain immediately stopped as he was now seeing Aircraft Y appear large in my windshield. It may have been hidden beside my windshield side pillar previously. We both agreed it would not have been pretty had we gone across the runway as it clearly appeared Aircraft Y was landing fast and rolling out long; and more importantly; evidently not giving way to us. After just a few seconds; Ground said 'Aircraft X; you're not moving' and we responded that it was not safe to do so. Ground replied; 'Aircraft Y is giving way to you' or words to that effect; and we replied that wasn't the case. After a few more seconds Aircraft Y exited onto Kilo in front of us; and Ground then instructed us to cross the runway and continue onto Kilo on our taxi to [Runway] 31L. Ground and Tower frequencies were combined per ATIS onto tower frequency 118.7; we were always using it; but when Aircraft Y cleared in front of us and we followed him until we turned left onto Charlie; we never heard him. Our subsequent taxi and departure was uneventful.The confusing or incorrect taxi instructions provided (i.e.; is it Zulu or Yankee to hold short) started us off with noticing ATC peculiarities as we spoke to Ground. The other aircraft not giving way to us; or appearing to do so; was the major cause of this averted conflict. Combining Tower and Ground on one frequency may also have caused some confusion; because the tower cab can hear everything but in the aircraft we can only actively hear the frequency we're currently using.First off; I think our alertness was the link in the chain that prevented an incident (or worse). I think that ATC made a procedural error in directing us to cross in front of the landing traffic without adequate time/distance to do so. I recommend [Company] request an official copy of the tape from the FAA if possible for review and development of lessons learned.

column121: Descending into ZZZ my First Officer (FO) and I noticed there was an unusual sound but we couldn't tell where it was coming from; I called the flight attendant and asked her if she could hear anything and she said no everything sounded normal to her in the back. We then pulled up the engine page and looked at the engine system followed by checking the electrical page but we couldn't see anything abnormal. We continued our descent and started our approach; it was visual conditions so we were on a visual approach to Runway XX; we had already canceled IFR. I called for flaps 12 and the FO put flaps 12 in; we noticed that the sound had changed a little so we again went through the engine instruments and everything still seemed normal. As we continued to add flaps we noticed the sound would get a little louder each time and when we went flaps 32 we could feel a small vibration coming through our feet. We checked the hydraulic page and everything looked fine; we then checked the flight control page and everything still looked normal. I was hand flying and did not notice any change to the handling of the aircraft; the only thing we noticed was the sound and then the small vibration. We continued to land with no issues; just a normal landing. After parking the aircraft and deplaning; I asked my First Officer to take a little extra time with his post flight to see if he could see anything that would have made the noise and vibration on the approach. After his postflight he came in and said he couldn't see anything unusual outside. I finished up my tasks and then decided to walk around the aircraft myself. When I got to the left side I noticed something sticking up on top of the wing maybe a foot or two from the fuselage. I grabbed a ladder and climbed up to look at it; I found that the trailing edge of the flap was cracked right next to the fuselage and then again about a foot or two away there was another crack. The top trailing edge of the flap had a piece that had come off and was sticking up. I took pictures and called the mechanic on call. I sent him the pictures and he called me back with a plan to send a mechanic down on a different aircraft that night. I went off duty at that point and when I got back to the airport the next morning my aircraft had been swapped out with a different aircraft for us to continue our day.

column121: We were being vectored from the northwest to the ILS approach and descending to 2;000 ft. when Tower asked if we had the airport in sight. This was my first time in ZZZ and although I thought I saw the airport I was not in a hurry to call it in sight; just to be sure. I verbalized that with the First Officer but he had been there before and was comfortable calling the airport in sight; so I did. Our initial vector would have taken us just outside the Final Approach Fix. However; when Tower issued clearance for the Visual Approach; the First Officer turned to a heading that would take us inside the Final Approach Fix. At the same time; he continued to descend while configuring with flaps (I think at either 10 or 15) and landing gear; and the speed brake extended. As the descent continued; Tower issued an 'Altitude Alert' and instructed us to adjust immediately. I cannot remember the exact verbiage; but there was a mention of our altitude being 1;200 ft. and perhaps something about 'minimum vectoring altitude.' The First Officer immediately adjusted his attitude to level off at around 1;200 ft. and continued to fly towards the runway center line. We were approximately abeam the Final Approach Fix at this point. He then asked for more flaps; ultimately Flaps 30; for the landing. I realized at about that point that he still had the speed brake extended and told him. He immediately stowed the speed brake and intercepted the visual glide path to the runway. I think he asked me at about that point if I was okay to continue. He was on the glide path and stabilized; so I said 'yes.'On landing rollout I noticed what sounded like an open microphone on the Tower frequency. Since there didn't seem to be any other aircraft on the frequency; I suspected it was coming from our aircraft. The Tower made a broadcast to all stations on the frequency that they were broadcasting on an active ATC radio frequency; and that they should cease immediately. When I made the '80 kts.' call out on landing roll; I heard myself over the radio. That confirmed that the First Officer was holding down the microphone button in his grip on the yoke. As soon as he loosened his grip; the transmission stopped.
column123: I was the pilot flying. Based on the weather reported we briefed the RNAV Arrival to be followed by the ILS Approach. We made special note that we were not to utilize the Autothrottle for the approach due to MEL(Minimum Equipment List) restriction. The arrival went as planned until we were given a vector off the arrival and then direct to the Final Approach Fix and a descent to 2;000 ft. As we were a bit high and fast; the speed brake was being utilized to expedite descent. While proceeding to the Final Approach Fix; the weather was acceptable to proceed visually so we reported field in sight and were cleared for the Visual Approach. Due to the high intercept angle to the Final Approach Fix I turned to establish a more acceptable intercept angle to capture the localizer. My perception looking outside was that we were quite high so asked the Pilot Monitoring to set Touchdown Zone Elevation. I failed to engage Vertical Nav for better situational awareness in relation to my vertical path and relied solely on the visual sight picture until on final. While on a dogleg to final we received an ATC Altitude warning alert telling us that we were below the sector altitude. We were approximately 1;100 ft. just abeam of the Final Approach Fix on a dogleg to final. I then stopped the descent until approaching final approach course and engaging Approach mode for glideslope capture. Continued the Visual Approach to a landing.

column121: We had been vectored off of the WAZKO1 RNAV Arrival toward the final approach course for the RNAV Runway 4 at ELP. We broke out of the overcast around 9;000 ft. and were approaching KANTR at 7;500 feet. There was a lot of ambient light from the city below and the reflection of that light from the overcast above as well as the fresh layer of snow on the surface. Approach cleared us for the approach and gave us 'Direct KANTR; Maintain 7;200 ft. until KANTR;' which I read back; with an emphasis on the '200'. The FO (PF) selected vertical speed mode after I set 7;200 ft. in the altitude preselector and called '7;200 ft. set!'. The published altitude at KANTR is 7;500 feet; which is located above terrain at 5;851 ft. We leveled at 7;200 ft. and she called for Flaps 18; and we joined the final approach course with the runway; and the terrain below in sight since descending out of 9;000 ft. Just then the GPWS System chimed in with 'Terrain! Pull Up!'. The FO dutifully disconnected the autopilot and I told her NOT to go around; as we were on a published segment of the final approach in good VMC (ELP was reporting 10 SM Vis) and I had the terrain in sight the whole time. We were not descending; and we had been level for some time. We landed without incident and taxied to our gate. Upon docking; I asked Ground for the phone number to the folks at approach control. The fellow who answered offered that he was indeed the Controller. I told him what had happened and he said that it was not at all uncommon; and that 7;200 ft. was their Minimum Vectoring Altitude in that sector. I told him that I would be filing a report with respect to the incident; for his own information.If there are other similar incidents here a communique should be distributed to the pilot group.

column121: As I was setting up cruise at 11;500 ft. I noticed my speed was not what it should be with a bit of a tailwind and the plane was yawing severely to the right. I used a fair amount of left rudder to keep the ball centered for the whole flight. I tried to figure out what could be wrong with the plane to cause this; but could not come up with a plausible explanation. Checked the trim; looked at the flaps; ailerons; elevator where visible and could see nothing. [On a previous flight] there was pretty substantial turbulence and I thought maybe something happened to the rudder that I missed on the preflight. Other than being slow and requiring the constant pressure on the left rudder pedal; the flight was uneventful. Approach cleared me through the Bravo airspace utilizing the west transition to get to the south side of the Bravo airspace. Once clear of Bravo; they terminated VFR Flight Following and I contacted Tower about 10 miles west and indicated inbound for landing with ATIS. I believe Tower had me #2 with a 2 mile final. I had nice stable approach utilizing a fair amount of left rudder to align with the center line at a 90 kt. approach. I crossed the numbers at about 84 kts. and mains touched down at about 76 kts. When the nose gear settled; the aircraft immediately shot straight right off the side of the runway. I worked the left rudder pedal and brake to try and regain control and got the plane back on the runway fairly quickly. Tower asked if I needed assistance and I told them no. I tried to explain what happened but really did not know. Tower said to contact ground. I did so and they said the pilot waiting to depart said my nose wheel was canted 45 degrees to the right as I came by them. That would explain the yaw and the plane shooting off to the right on landing. I contacted a mechanic and he will be looking at the plane to [determine] what the issue is and what it will take to repair that issue. The mechanic said there is a pin on the strut that can break and cause this issue. He said I was very fortunate to have regained control and the nose gear released and was able to track again. He said when that pin breaks; very often the nose gear will not release and allow straight forward movement. I had no idea that this is a not uncommon occurrence with these aircraft. I had googled a description of what happened and got nothing coming up that would explain what the mechanic told me.
column122: Reporter stated that the mechanic said strut pin failure is not uncommon. Reporter added that the pin slides into a V groove to center the nose gear upon retraction. The aircraft has since been repaired at an authorized Cessna shop. Reporter stated that the shop would not supply the mechanic with details on the failure.

column121: I was flying with a student practicing approaches. We were flying an ILS approach when we were given a traffic advisory regarding another aircraft. I saw this aircraft from several miles away and kept an eye on it. This aircraft was flying laterally through the approach corridor at altitudes published on the ILS approach we were flying. The aircraft did not deviate from its path and continued towards us. I took controls from my student (then using a view limiting device) when the aircraft was close enough to warrant evasive action; which resulted in a 300 foot climb. I advised approach that I was climbing to avoid the traffic. I looked down and saw the aircraft at the altitude that we had just vacated with little to no lateral separation from where we just were. I believed that this aircraft would see where we were given that I saw them; however this aircraft either did not see us; or did not choose to deviate from their current trajectory despite having seen us. I should have deviated sooner to avoid this aircraft and prevent what ultimately turned into a near miss.

column121: I am a CFI and I was flying a training mission with my client who is a licensed private pilot working on transition training to a new model aircraft (Aircraft X). This was my client's first flight in a Aircraft X. The lesson started with a detailed preflight; followed by a similarly detailed run-up. Once ready; we departed Palo Alto Airport for a familiarization flight in a practice area south and west of the airport. During our time in the practice area we maintained visual separation from another aircraft in the training area (each trying to stay in our own section of the training area; while we also maintained appropriate distances from scattered clouds in the area; and staying below the Bravo which starts at 4;000 in that area. During this time; we stayed in contact with the Tower having asked them for advisories during our training mission.After approximately 1 hour of Hobbs time (including the taxi and detailed run-up); we copied ATIS Quebec and informed the Tower that we would like to return to the field for the option; and closed traffic. We were instructed to fly over Stanford and enter the pattern for left traffic for Runway 31. We made our way as instructed and entered the left downwind for Runway 31. The Tower then told us that we were number 4 cleared to land; and that the traffic that we were following was roughly abeam us in the opposite downwind. We acknowledged the instruction and reported negative contact with the traffic to follow. Meanwhile; the Tower was very busy with numerous other traffic entering; leaving; and in the pattern. We were then told to extend our downwind; that we had extensive extensions into Moffett Field's airspace (it is very common for Palo Alto to have and use extensions into Moffett's airspace - this extension was even further than usual) and that the Tower would call our base. While extending; we saw our traffic to follow turn base and then final. But we continued downwind since the Tower said that they would call our base.Moments later; the Tower instructed us to fly 'straight towards the 31 numbers' which was a bit strange since we were so far from the runway (probably at least 3 miles at this point). But the frequency was very busy and it didn't seem to make sense (or even possible) to ask to verify the instruction. We turned left onto a long final and proceeded towards the runway. We were holding approximately 1;000 feet (+/- since my student was flying and this was their first time in the aircraft) this entire time on downwind; base and long final.Not too long after we turned long final; the Tower told us to climb 500 feet immediately for traffic. I immediately took the controls of the plane; quickly (re)checked nearby traffic on the G1000 MFD (with ADS-B traffic) and did a visual scan for traffic as I went full forward on the throttle; prop; and mixture and pitched the nose back for the climb. The quick scan of traffic (visual and ADS-B) did not indicate any traffic below or next to us; but it did indicate one aircraft above our altitude but not in the immediate vicinity. None-the-less; we tried to visually acquire the above traffic as we climbed.Moments later; the tower told us to stop our climb immediately and to turn right and fly directly towards Leslie Salt. Again; we immediately complied with the Tower's instructions as we continued to try to acquire and track any immediate threats visually. Just then; we saw another aircraft pass directly above us; slightly off to our left side; in very close proximity to our aircraft. The Tower then instructed us to make a right turn back to final for landing.I've been flying at PAO for nearly XX years; so I am familiar with how busy it can get. But even with that experience; it was particularly busy at this time. One of the things that I think was a factor in this situation was a partial callsign conflict. Our aircraft callsign ended in [--] while the callsign of another aircraft on frequency at the time ended in [--]. In fact; it is possible that the aircraft above us when we were instructed to climb; and the one that nearly collided with moments later; was the aircraft ending in [--]. My theory is that the instruction to climb was actually intended for Aircraft Y and not Aircraft X; and that the controller tried to stop our climb as soon as he noticed the error. Unfortunately; these problems were all compounded by the heavy radio traffic making it virtually impossible to verify instructions; especially in a timely fashion.My client and I replayed this whole incident both while still in the air; and during debrief on the ground; and we are both quite certain that we responded to our correct callsign and that this wasn't a case of us improperly (and unintentionally) responding to someone else's callsign. I wanted to use the G1000 PLAYback feature to check/verify the radio call; but; unfortunately; the radio traffic was so heavy that there was no way to use the replay feature successfully. I have replayed this entire situation a number of times trying to analyze what I could have / should have done differently to avoid getting this close to another aircraft. Unfortunately; I haven't really come up with a good answer given the details of the situation.Given that the only nearby traffic that I could observe at the time was the one above us; I could have held my attitude of 1;000 feet; in defiance of the Tower Controller's instruction; until I could verify that the climb instruction was for us; or until I could verify another nearby traffic threat. But this seems like a very bad idea. I have always operated that when ATC gives you an immediate traffic avoidance instruction that you follow that instruction immediately. There could have very well been another nearby aircraft that I was unaware of that was an even more of a threat that I was currently unaware of. Hence my immediate response as instructed.I believe that the similar sounding callsigns; heavy radio traffic; and large number of aircraft operating in the class Delta; all contributed to the confusion for all parties. I know that it made our task (mine and my client) much more difficult; and I believe that you could hear that in the voice and instructions of the Tower Controller and the other pilots. After landing; we opted to terminate rather than continue pattern operations. Once back in the flying club; we talked with another CFI and student who was flying (and returning to the airport) at the same time. I asked them if they heard the Tower 'trying kill us' and the CFIs response was 'yeah; the Tower Controller was clearly overwhelmed!' We asked him if he heard any indications that we responded to the wrong callsign. His response was 'no' and that; again; he thought that whole problem was that the Tower Controller was simply overwhelmed by the overall situation.I know that Palo Alto is an FAA Training Tower; and I'm fully understanding and supportive of that. But perhaps in this case maybe there should have been additional oversight of Tower operations; or at least a senior controller stepping in before the controller got too overwhelmed.

column121: At 800 feet AGL; clicked autopilot off for a visual landing. At 500 ft.; FO called 'Stable; cleared to land Runway XX Center.' I replied 'Landing.' Between 500 and 400 feet AGL tried to make small lateral correction with aileron but aileron movement was limited. Used a touch of rudder for correction. Clicked autopilot button again and crossed checked then confirmed that 'Flight Director' was displayed in PFD. Landed uneventfully but elevator movement was very heavy in flare. After landing at taxi speed; I tried to move the yoke left and right and there was very limited movement. The First Officer tried as well with the same result. We checked the Flight Control and Hydraulic Displays and both were normal.I considered a go-around but quickly [determined] a go around would be extremely difficult with big power changes and limited flight control capability. The flight path was set up to touch down in the landing zone on speed so I elected to land.Unsure what caused the flight controls to be limited. After landing; I debriefed Maintenance in the cockpit and put a detailed write up in the logbook.
