column121: I was enroute to ILM. As I began my descent into the Wilmington terminal area the ATIS and Approach Controller indicated ILS Runway 06Z was the approach in use. Weather was reported as 400 overcast and one mile visibility. Winds 030 degrees at 4 knots. A cold front and convective activity was approaching the airport and I requested a short vector to final. I was cleared for the approach and began my descent. I ended up too high on the approach and executed a missed approach at minimums. As I was being vectored back for another approach I was advised that the aircraft following me also executed a missed approach due to being too high. I looked at the wind vector on my PFD and realized that I had a 50 knot tailwind at 1;500 feet on my last approach. I advised the Controller about the strong winds at altitude and asked if Runway 6 was still the best runway.I also asked the Controller if he had any real time winds on the ground and was reminded that the Tower was closed; he would try to get me an update from the automatic reporting system. I was told that the automatic reporting system still indicated winds out of the NE (northeast) at 11 knots. I continued for a second approach but at 700 feet AGL my wind vector was showing a 50 knot tailwind and my groundspeed confirmed that. I deemed it unsafe to land and executed another missed approach.The Controller suggested ZZZ as an alternate. I began vectors to an approach to RNAV GPS Runway XX. I quickly realized that I would have the same 50 knot tailwind situation at ZZZ and began questioning the Controller about any real time wind information on the ground. He said the only info he had still indicated winds on the ground were out of the NE about 7 knot. He said the convective activity situation at Wilmington was improving and asked if I would like to go back for another approach. I was beginning vectors to another ILS Approach 06Z. I was still seeing the same 50 knot winds at altitude and began doubting the wind reports on the ground. I asked for an approach to Runway 24. I was cleared for the RNAV approach 24. As I was descending on final approach; I had a 50 knot head wind at 1;000 feet.When I reached minimums and began to go visual; I encountered a windshear. I increased power to MCT (Max Continuous Thrust) trying to climb out; but to no avail. As I was continuing to recover from the windshear; my landing gear contacted a treetop about 1/4 mile from the runway. At this point I deemed the safest course of action was to continue to land. I made a normal landing to a full stop; turned off the runway and taxied to the FBO. After exiting the airplane minor damage was noted. At this point the winds on the ground had shifted to the SW (southwest)) and were gusting to 35 kts indicating to me that I had landed during frontal passage.The biggest contributing factor to safety I noted; was the lack of any real time winds on the ground. If I had real time wind data from the Controller as I was conducting my approach; I would have been advised of the wind shift occurring; and might have been able to take preventive action before I encountered the windshear. My recommendation is to not have a system that solely relies on the automatic reporting system which is not real time. I would have each Approach Control have the ability to see and give real time winds just as an operating Tower would have.

column121: On approach; hitting sterile cockpit an unusual odor described by many crew members of dirty socks; feet; filled the cabin for the remainder of our flight as we landed. As I was sitting in my flight attendant jump seat a rancid like smell filled the forward galley; I then spoke to the Lead Flight Attendant who noticed it as well. After the detection I called the back to ask if the odor was in the back as well. The entire cabin smelt of dirty socks. As for the cause we are not sure. After informing the Captain during approach; I was then informed on why it smelt after all passengers were in the process of deplaning. He informed me it was due to the ozone layer. After the detection of the smell; I informed the lead as well as contacting 4 doors confirm if they are smelling it as well. The Lead then informed the pilots about the smell. The aroma of dirty socks; may typically be the notice of engine oil vapors seeping in. Which is usually known as 'bleed air'. If at high enough concentration flight crew as well as passengers can grow ill.

column121: The aircraft was on a normal approach to land at ZZZ RWY XX. During the final approach; the aircraft was within standards: the airspeed and glide path were stable; and the aircraft was aligned with the runway's extended centerline. Idle power was set about 20ft AGL causing the plane to touch down firmly on the main landing gears. This was followed by a slight bounce which caused the aircraft to lift approx 3ft off the ground. Following the bounce; I maintained aft elevator/yoke pressure to prevent the nose gear from impacting the ground first. The main landing gears then contacted the ground smoothly after this correction. As the airspeed decreased and the nose contacted the runway; I immediately felt a strong vibration from the nose followed by the nose continuing to fall to an abnormally nose-low attitude where I could see the runway directly. Seeing this happen; I immediately shut down the engine. Once the aircraft came to rest; I secured the rest of the cockpit. After exiting the plane; I was informed by the airport operations staff that they had retrieved the nose gear from somewhere on the runway (they did not specify where they found it). ZZZ Tower then closed the runway and airport for operations until both the runway and aircraft were secured. I do not believe any human performance considerations negatively affected this incident. As the events unfolded; I performed to the best of my ability to ensure that I was safe while also ensuring that the aircraft was not damaged further. while this incident ended safely; it has definitely inspired me to further my proficiency. I will focus on modules that will improve my knowledge on maintaining stabilized approaches; ADM; and preventing and recovering from bounced landings. As a flight instructor; I hope to use this experience as a lesson to any future students.
column125: Flight Instructor had a bounced landing and while attempting to land the aircraft again; the nose impacted the runway. Postflight; the pilot learned the nose gear had separated during landing and airport operations had retrieved it from the runway.

column121: Aircraft X was uneventful leading up to the approach at ZZZ. We had been cleared for the approach to Runway XX and were established on the localizer and glideslope. The autopilot and autothrust had been disengaged earlier in the approach and the aircraft had been configured to Flaps 3 when Gear Down was requested. When the gear lever was selected down the three indicators on the landing gear panel indicated red UNLK (UNLOCKED); no noise from the landing gear doors opening was heard. Captain [Name] and I communicated and confirmed on the Landing Gear Systems page that the landing gear had not extended. We decided to level out at 6;000 feet MSL while we confirmed the situation and attempted to cycle the gear. We communicated to the tower we would be going around for a landing gear failure approximately 3mi prior to the runway. Tower provided vectors and a climb to 9;000 feet; we initiated a go around over the threshold per ATC instructions and cleaned up the aircraft per SOP. The autoflight was re-engaged after the go around. During this time after the failure of subsequent attempts to cycle the gear and with the lever still down; we eventually got the L/G GEAR NOT DOWNLOCKED ECAM. The ECAM was run according to SOP by Captain [Name] while I flew the aircraft and handled radio calls. We [received priority]; stated our intention to return to land on Runway XYR and requested CFR (Crash Fire Rescue Equipment). Gravity extension was successful and we were vectored to the approach. Approach and landing were normal with the exception of the gear doors remaining open. We landed with 8000 lbs. of fuel. We stopped on the runway and Operations was standing by to tow the aircraft back to the gate.

column121: While on final approach the PF was having difficulty slowing the aircraft and continuing the descent. The headwind we had been fighting quickly turned to a crosswind. The PF elected to re-bug and use flaps 40 well above 1;000 feet. As the PF I selected the new flap setting in the FMC and verbally called out the Vref and target speeds. As we were slowing past our previous target speed the PF called for flaps 40. We had no amber band visible for flaps 40 placard speed; so I verified the placard; 162 knots; and selected the flaps seeing 158 knots and slowing on my tape. As I verified the speed brake was armed he again called for 40. The flaps were still at 30. At this point we were now below 1;000 feet but above 500 feet. We discussed the non-movement and load alleviation. As the PM; I suggested; pitch up and slow; the flaps moved to 40 as we suspected they would. The checklist was completed and we were able to be stable by 500 feet AGL; so we continued the landing. We did not observe any limitations being exceeded on the flaps or in procedure as the PM was able to manage the approach prior to the 500 foot gate.In discussing this event during our debrief we believe that we did meet our approach gate criteria. We were in our first 'heavy' approach in many months as the flight was full. The weight may have contributed. The wind was also a contributing factor as it shifted down the final enough that by the time we departed the traffic flow had reversed.Neither of us were averse to going around but it did not seem necessary in the moment as it seemed we met our criteria to continue. Having said that I'm certain eyes further from the instrument panel may have captured data we missed but I believe we trapped and corrected the situation.
column123: While on final approach I; PF; called for final landing flap (flaps 40) and flaps did not move to commanded position (they were at 30); despite being slower than max speed for flaps 40. Since we were within a few knots of max flaps 40 speed; I slowed further and flaps extended to commanded position. I believe the flap extension occurred right around 500 feet.  The final approach speed was within stabilized approach criteria and the outcome of the approach and landing were not in doubt. Landing was uneventful.We have been flying mostly empty airplanes for almost a year and this flight was almost completely full. I believe the higher landing weight coupled with a significant loss of headwind on final contributed to the airplane taking a while to slow down to an acceptable speed for final flaps. When I called for final flaps; we were 3-4 knots below the max flaps speed and I believe the aircraft's safety system was restricting flap movement until I was a little slower. As we return to Pre-COVID passenger loads; I will have to adjust lead time for decelerating the jet. Today was a good reminder.

column121: On initial approach to TRM I was announcing the arrival quite often and very diligently; in a descent of approximately 1;600 feet per minute another plane was observed on the ADS-B and was in sight. I continued the descent to fly well under the plane which was obviously VFR and as I approached the plane it turned into me with a sharp turn. The TCAS had a TA but not an RA as I had descended well below the other plane. The other plane must not have been monitoring the UNICOM or monitoring their traffic ADS-B.I realize now that I should have changed course at an earlier time; to give a much wider birth to the crossing plane. Also; I can not take into account other pilots listening to the CTAF or UNICOM frequency for area traffic. In addition; as I approached the airport to fly over and enter a left downwind; another plane was spotted that had just flown over the field with no announcements. VFR traffic in this area are not listening or announcing their intentions in the airport environment.

column121: My student and I were conducting a dual pattern flight at ZZZ. We were cleared for the option number 2 for Runway XX. Number 1 was on final and number 3 was student pilot solo who was behind us in the downwind after going around after his first landing attempt. We turned based and then to final being number 1. On about a 3/4-mile final from the runway; the student pilot solo turned base and then proceeded to turn final right in front of us; and slightly below us. My best guess is that he thought he was following number 1 and that he was number 2. At best glance; they were probably 400 feet in front of us; and 200 feet below us on final. I took flight controls; queried Tower and initiated a go around left side. The student landed and was given a phone number to copy down. After we completed our lesson a few laps around the pattern later and taxied off the runway; Tower asked if we were okay with the go around. It was certainly a little close for comfort; but we had enough space to recognize the threat and execute a go around.
column123: Was the 2nd solo flight in my pilot career. I had arrived back into ZZZ traffic pattern into Runway XX. While on downwind; I was cleared by Tower as number 3 and cleared to land on Runway XX. I had identified the traffic in front of me as number 2 without any doubt. I followed that traffic and landed safely. Once I called up ground; I was notified that I had cut off the actual number 2 traffic and that traffic was forced to go around. In reality; I had incorrectly identified the number 1 traffic as number 2 and failed to see any other traffic making me believe I had the correct number 2 in sight.When I am in a congested pattern like ZZZ; I will use my multi function flight display map page and make sure there are no planes I have failed to identify. I will also start counting out traffic to make sure I have accounted for all of them.

column121: Training flight for a student who needed a 90 day solo endorsement. After doing maneuvers; I gave him a partial power loss and he decided to divert to ZZZ about 10 NM from our position. I had already checked NOTAMs and we got AWOS information twice on the way. Student announced every couple miles where we were and that we were inbound with a simulated loss of power for a straight in on Runway XX. I saw when we were about 2 NM away that the runway looked good but some of the taxiways were snowy and told my student we were not doing a full stop so we planned for a touch and go with a go-around possibility. About 1 NM away I saw a snowplow working on the taxiways. As we were in the flare; I saw the snowplow turning towards the runway. I put the flaps up and my student added power and as we climbed over the runway the snowplow turned onto the approach end of Runway XY and in our direction; we flew over the plow about 100-200 feet overhead.I believe the weather may have contributed as it was hazy on the ground; we could clearly see things on the ground but the haze may have affected the ability of the plow to see us.I think we could have made a safer decision to only do a low approach.I mostly think the situation would have not been an issue if there was any communication from the airport. There was no mention of work being done or the runway being closed in either NOTAMs or the AWOS; nor did the plow ever say anything on CTAF about their intentions. We were broadcasting our location and intentions regularly but heard no one else. We did a double check and volume was good; frequency was correct.

column121: During flight testing of an experimental; retractable gear aircraft; the landing gear failed to extend.  The failure was first noted at 2;500 feet AGL while configuring for landing stalls.  The left main gear failed to lock down; as noted by the left main gear indicator light remaining red.  The gear would retract normally.  Several attempts at extension were made; including during pitch and yaw; to no avail. Manual extension of the gear was attempted following POH (Pilots Operating Handbook) checklist procedures; but the manual pump handle would not move. The decision was made; as recommended by the POH emergency procedure; to land with all gear retracted.  The aircraft returned to the airport of origin; which also had a grass runway.  Landing was made with full flaps at stall speed; and slid about 250 feet in the grass before stopping. There were no injuries to the single occupant of the aircraft.

column121: We were flying; from ZZZ to QRO (Queretaro; MX). During preflight inspection; the FMC database was current as well as both pilots' Electronic Flight Kits. While in-flight; the weather was reporting clouds at 1;200 feet 5 mile visibility and light winds. The runway in use was [Runway] 09. While in cruise flight; Mexico Center informed us to expect VOR DME 2 for RWY (Runway) 09 approach in QRO. The aircraft was set up; and the Pilot Flying (Captain) completed all briefings. While in cruise flight; Mexico Center gave us a late descent clearance which is very common on this flight that required LVL/CHG mode and speedbrakes to get back on path. While we were descending to our first cleared altitude (around 14;000 feet MSL); Mexico switched us to QRO Approach. Upon contact with QRO Approach about 15-20 miles out; they advised of a traffic on a ten mile final; and informed us to now expect the VOR DME 1 RWY 09 Approach. We were recleared direct to QRO VOR; provided another descent; and then shortly thereafter; Approach cleared us for the approach. The First Officer went into the FMC and configured it to fly direct to QRO VOR. After the aircraft was established on course to the VOR; the First Officer selected the QRO VOR fix in the FMC and inserted the VOR DME 1 RWY 09 approach after verification with the Captain. Because of the angle the aircraft was approaching the VOR (straight in Runway 09); and relative to the approach procedure (an outbound heading 292 followed by a left procedure turn inbound); an additional procedure turn was required just to begin the approach. The procedure turn would normally be done at the VOR. Due to the FMC not having a procedure turn at the VOR to begin the approach for arrivals from the west; it immediately started auto-sequencing fixes to fly the aircraft directly to the outbound procedure turn DME 10 from the QRO VOR. The First Officer had to re-enter the VOR into Direct To and build a holding pattern into the FMC. There was a missed approach holding pattern depicted on the approach chart; therefore; the First Officer built a hold based on the holding pattern published. The First Officer informed ATC that a procedure turn was required at the VOR and also notified approach that the aircraft would enter holding as published to begin the approach. ATC advised us to descend to 11;000 feet and cleared for the approach. ATC seemed confused as to what we were doing. It appears that they were expecting the aircraft to make a hard left after crossing the VOR (almost a 180 degree turn) into unprotected airspace that contained mountainous terrain. The minimum safe altitude for that sector is 12;800 feet and if the aircraft would have flown that; there could have been a CFIT event. Once the aircraft entered the holding pattern; making a procedure turn to enter the crew selected IMM EXIT and the aircraft completed the current holding pattern; just a procedure turn; and exited the hold to fly the entire approach as planned and expected. There seems to be an issue regarding the Jeppesen Charts; and FMC database for this specific approach. The FMC did not contain any proper procedure to join the approach legally. Also the FMC sequencing the approach could have presented multiple issues including CFIT; TCAS; or lost of Situational Awareness.
column123: We were heading to MMQT to shoot the VOR 1 approach from the west. We were headed direct to the VOR with the approach loaded after that. Approximately 9 miles prior to the VOR the airplane started a left turn to join the outbound portion of the approach. This was not appropriate or legal. We turned the airplane back towards the VOR and realized there was nothing loaded to turn the airplane around to join the approach legally. There is also no procedure turn depicted on the chart. There was a holding pattern at the VOR and a missed approach procedure associated with the approach. We decided to enter the holding pattern at the proper altitude and told the controller we were doing that. That allowed the airplane to turn around and we were able to join and commence the approach that way. The rest of the approach was uneventful
column125: Air carrier flight crew reported they were cleared for a VOR approach which required a procedure turn. There was no procedure turn published on their charts or contained in the FMC.

column121: This is a report to bring to light a potential issue with a NOTAM; no pilot deviation from ATC instructions or company procedures is believed to have occurred. References you will want: Approach Chart 11-3 ILS or LOC [Runway] 24 BHM; possibly the low IFR chart for that area; and NOTAM BHM A0233/21. This NOTAM has you take a turn to [heading] 219 to intercept the VUZ radial 067. This heading does not intercept that radial; I do not know how to fly this MAP (Missed Approach Procedure) legally. We caught this and requested amended MAP from ATC before commencing the LOC [Runway] 24.This is either due to me not understanding the NOTAM or it being written poorly.

column121: We were left base descending at 1;800 feet turning final for a visual landing 15 into MOB. As it all happens so fast there was no time to react in the moment; but as we turned to what looked like a single lone bird turned out to be a drone quadcopter zipped by me on my left at eye level and went directly over our left wing within 15 feet. I immediately reported this to MOB Tower described it as quadcopter at our altitude and within 15 feet of the aircraft and that If it had been any closer it would've struck us or potentially went into our left engine possibly causing failure. This drone was in the critical approach corridor in our path on final to land. Luckily after we landed we did a thorough pre-flight and  there was no damage nor evidence of a collision. I saw the drone as we quickly passed it in a split second.Cause - Careless drone operator operating a drone illegally in vicinity of airport.Always be vigilant of the airspace and treat them as if they were birds even if not always avoidable. Report any such activity to hopefully avoid an emergency in the future.
column123: While being vector for final approach to runway 15 in MOB we experienced a near miss was a drone at approximately 1;800 feet.  It was spotted at the last second because of the few clouds in the vicinity of aircraft at the time of the incident.  The drone was within 15 feet of the left side of the aircraft  moving in the opposite direction.  ATC was immediately notified of the location and description.  The aircraft was inspected for a possible strike only in parking; but nothing was detected during the post inspection.Cause - Someone was operation a drone in close proximity of MOB airport at a unsafe altitude.We did not have time to react to the drone because of the location. [We should] be cognizant of the possibility of drones operating illegally in airspace in and around airports.  Report the incidents to ATC.
column125: Air carrier flight crew reported a NMAC with a drone during approach to MOB airport.

column121: After an uneventful approach and touchdown with Captain as Pilot Flying; the aircraft was decelerating normally with speed brakes and reversers deployed. The crew accomplished a normal exit of the runway when smoke could be seen in the flight deck and sirens sounded in the cabin. Purser was immediately contacted and told flight crew that there was smoke in the cabin and detectors going off. The Purser was asked to assess the situation in the cabin as simultaneous contact was made with the Tower. Cabin was asked for any signs of a fire or source of smoke and none were present. The Tower advised crew that a runway treatment had been used two days prior and that upon crew request no signs of smoke or fire were visible coming from aircraft. Crew determined it was safe to exit the runway taxiway due to smoke being from possible ingestion into the packs or reversers from chemicals in the runway area and proceed to the ramp with constant contact with the cabin to ensure no escalation or fire warnings.A short time later an evacuation message and siren was triggered in the flight deck. Once again we verified with cabin crew that no fire or source of smoke could be assessed. We requested that the emergency equipment be available and accompany the aircraft to the gate out of an abundance of caution and increasing warnings in the flight deck. A normal shut down was accomplished and the flight mechanic entered the flight deck and informed crew that he had inadvertently set off the evacuate warning while trying to cancel the smoke detectors in the cabin.Crew was met by local airport authorities and emergency personnel. During the interaction with crew we were informed that two compounds 'Sodium Formate' a liquid treatment and 'Potassium Acetate' mixed in with 'aviation grade sand' had been used two days prior and could possibly have been ingested by reverser system and packs leading to smoke. A giant smoke cloud could also be seen at the midpoint of the runway at least 200 feet tall but centered in one location; the possible site where chemicals interacted with the aircraft. Crew taking over the flight was briefed on the situation and Maintenance was in the process of examining the aircraft. By all logic with no indications of a fire and smoke being generated inside the aircraft an external source was deemed the most likely cause. The pebble sized compound that may have been present along runway edge and some portions of runway; even though runway appeared perfectly clean and clear were brought by airports OPS Crew after an inspection of touchdown and mid runway areas.With the compounds mentioned not being familiar to crews not much comment can be given; I would suggest familiarizing crews with this event to help discern an actual fire with a foreign contaminant causing indications of smoke and fire. I would also point out that procedures need to be followed in regards to on board mechanics response to dealing with abnormalities; rather than helping in trying to cancel an alarm after it was deemed non-threatening the issue was compounded which could have forced an evacuation had flight deck and cabin crew had poorer communication.
column125: Air Carrier Captain reported smoke in the flight deck and cabin due to the reversers or packs ingesting chemicals that had been used to treat the runway surface.

column121: On IFR flight plan I was cleared for an RNAV Approach. I was monitoring the CTAF while still with ATC.  There was someone ahead of me on the RNAV Approach; so I was second to land.  With the airport in sight; I canceled IFR and changed frequency; kept transponder number.  Another plane announced it would enter downwind and land after me.  Another plane announced in area and would be number four.  With each new plane arriving to the area; the Fixed Base Operator announced winds and preferred runway.  The plane ahead of me cleared the runway.  On short final; I saw a headlight at the far end of runway rapidly approaching. I announced that I was going around for plane on opposite side of runway. I climbed and turned slightly right.  The plane from the other direction was rolling on the ground and about midfield took off and turned slightly right. I then announced that I would be staying in the pattern.  I then landed uneventfully.  He came and found me to apologize. He said that he had keyed in the wrong frequency and had been announcing his intentions to land on the opposite direction runway.

column121: Flight was all normal until we were cleared for a visual approach. Approach vectored us about 2 miles outside of the Final Approach Fix. When cleared for the a visual approach the First Officer turned inside of the Final Approach Fix. We had briefed the towers and their height and had them visually. The First Officer turned off the flight directors and turned directly towards the runway; about 20 degrees angle.  At 1;000 feet Tower called and asked if we had the towers in sight and said he had gotten an altitude alert.  At that point we were still visual with the towers and a half dot low on the glide slope. I directed the First Officer to come left and get on final. We continued the approach and landed. Found out afterwards this was the First Officer's first time to this airport and that he really didn't have a good visual on the runway. I felt his turning off the flight directors added to his lack of situational awareness. We discussed this approach afterwards and he felt I should have directed a go around. I believe he was waiting for me but I was in the green.  I didn't know he was lost. I explained to the First Officer that there is a reason we brief instrument approaches at night; to use them.  I shouldn't have allowed him to make the turn directly towards the runway but told him to intercept closer to the Final Approach Fix. The First Officer needed to communicate he wasn't in the green and execute a go around if he felt it was needed.
column123: Cleared visual approach after I stated to the Captain that I had the beacon in sight. I turned toward the airfield and began my descent referencing the glideslope. I only saw the beacon; not the runway. Tower issued a low altitude alert as I flew too close to the towers west of the field. I had the towers in sight and the Captain gave me verbal commands to help me orient the runway. The landing and rollout were uneventful. I accepted a visual approach based on the beacon alone. I was also distracted by the airspeed going below the hook for reasons I didn't understand. After landing; the Captain and I thoroughly debriefed the approach and landing.
column125: A Flight Crew conducting a Visual Approach at night reported the Pilot Flying turned towards the runway prior to the Final Approach Fix and was issued a Low Altitude Alert from the Tower.

column121: I was conducting a stage check for my student and we were practicing engine out approaches. On the first attempt a slip was attempted but the nose was too high and the airspeed dropped dangerously low. I took over and attempted to initiate a go around but the aircraft landed on all 3 gear to bounce sharply. I proceeded back to our base and the student and I attempted to get his power off approaches cleaned up and we did multiple touch and goes. After several landings we decided to end the lesson but I noticed that the left brake lost all authority when trying to stop. I took over and prevented a [runway] excursion and tried to slow down in a controlled manner. When I tried to exit [the] taxiway the wind pushed my tail around faster than expected and the plane skidded to a stop just before the hold short line with the nose gear and left main in the grass and snow. No damage to the plane or taxiway lights occurred. No injuries occurred.

column121: Was cleared visual approach [Runway] 23 / asked to turn short final inside of GS Intercept Altitude for traffic. Changed Alt Select from 2;300 feet to 1;500 feet and selected VS (Vertical Speed) descent of 700 rpm to get below GS. Descending below 1;800 feet or so; received Low Altitude Alert from ATC / Tower. Deactivated AutoPilot; added power and initiated climb to intercept GS.ATC Low Altitude Alert followed later (approximately 5-10 sec of 'Glideslope' call-out from GPWS.Two causal items: short turn to final / cleared for visual approach at altitude that would put us above glide slope; so selected lower altitude and initiated descent. Second causal item was over aggressive selection of Altitude Select to 1;500 feet . . . In retrospect should have selected something like 1;900-2;000 MSL given field elevation of 700 feet.De-selected AP and climbed to 2;000-2;100 feetWe actually discussed not accepting a short turn to final as part of approach briefing . . . When cleared for the visual; initially turned right to extend final leg; but then was asked by ATC to turn to a left heading given other arriving traffic behind us. In retrospect; could have indicate unable; but that seemed unnecessary given overall regime; weather; etc. Second suggestion would be to double-think / exercise caution in selecting an altitude above the DH but below the FAF / GS Intercept Altitude.

column121: Entering the traffic pattern for the airport I turned to enter final which positioned my aircraft directly into an extremely bright setting sun.  The sun then blinded me at that point and I lost visual contact with the airport and runway and altitude information.  After regaining sight from the sun and while establishing my position along with altitude in relation to the airport and runway I observed another aircraft that appeared to be flying in the opposite direction about 200 feet away.  I then took an evasive maneuver and cleared the traffic.  At that point I was no longer in position to continue the landing and I executed a VFR go around and re entered the traffic pattern and landed without further incident.  What caused this incident was turning on final directly into the extremely bright setting sun which blinded me. To prevent this from happening again I will use a runway if available that is not in line of sight with the sun or fly to an alternative airport without conflict with the setting sun or wait until sunset.

column121: We were flying from ZZZ to ASE and I was the pilot flying. As we approached Aspen; which at that time was reporting VFR; we were given vectors to intercept LOC DME E into ASE. I looked over and noticed the LOC approach was not properly set up. I warned the monitoring pilot of this but at that point we made visual contact with the airport. We accepted clearance to maintain 13;000 ft until established and clearance to execute LOC DME E approach. Having visual contact with the airport we started our descent. A layer of clouds moved in below us as we made the final turn inbound. At the entire time we had full visual contact with all the terrain around. We lost sight of the airport due to the cloud layer; at the same time ATC issued an altitude alert. I emphasize that we were in full visual contact with all the terrain and that visibility at our flight level was unrestricted. The pilot monitoring advised that we had terrain but lost a visual with the airport and said that we would be going missed and starting a climb. We got another altitude alert from ATC. We advised we are in a climb and asked for vectors to give time to decide what to do. We proceeded to ZZZ1 airport afterwards. We were NOT in IMC conditions at any phase of the flight and we maintained visual clearance from all the terrain. The mistake was communication breakdown with ATC and in part CRM in regards to the intentions to execute a visual approach. At no time aircraft was in IMC conditions and we were maintaining clearance with terrain at all times. The aircraft was never put in any unsafe condition. I learned from this how CRM and communication has to be mastered the entire time.

column121: I was 10 miles north of ZZZ and flying heading 220 at 1;500 ft. MSL and 105 knots in a Mooney inbound for a VFR traffic pattern entry on a right downwind to Runway XX. Three airplanes had just landed and I was maneuvering for the 45 degree entry to Runway XX. I heard the Cessna announce it was 4 miles ahead and also maneuvering for the same entry to Runway XX. The sun was setting to the west which made it difficult to spot aircraft to the southwest and west of my flight path. I announced on CTAF that I was 10 miles north and maneuvering for the 45 degree entry right downwind Runway XX. At 5 miles northwest of ZZZ; I announced that I was 5 miles northwest maneuvering for the 45 degree entry; right downwind; for Runway XX.At that point; I heard the Cessna was on the 45 entry to right downwind Runway XX. I turned to a heading of 180 at 1;300 ft. MSL; 113 knots; and remained 5 miles west of ZZZ; while visually scanning ahead; left and right; for the Cessna. It was very difficult to see southwest and west due to the sun setting. At 5 miles west of ZZZ; I announced Mooney is 5 miles west of ZZZ maneuvering for the 45 entry; right downwind; Runway XX".Within a minute; I spotted the Cessna approximately 100 ft. below and 400 ft. to the right. The CFI onboard called on the radio; and said "did you see us?" I responded; "Yes I just did. Would you like to go ahead of me or do you want me to proceed on the 45 entry?" He responded that they would continue the entry and go ahead of me. I continued to fly a heading of 180 and then executed a turn to the west followed by a 360 degree turn to the east to enter the pattern after the Cessna. I landed and did not see the Cessna on the ramp.Afterwards; I called the CFI in the Cessna; whom I've known for years. I listened to his remarks and explained that I had no intention of overtaking him in The Cessna. This is not something I would ever attempt nor have I ever done in 18 years of flying. He mentioned they had requested my position in the air (around 5 miles out) and I said that I never heard that request nor that they were in the Cessna. I was only monitoring CTAF and visually scanning for traffic. I heard they were already on the 45 degree entry to Runway XX. I believe that 1 or 2 of their position reports or requests for my position were not transmitted or heard because we may have both pressed the mic button at the same time to give or request position reports. One of the Cessna's communications may have been incomplete which could explain why I heard that the Cessna was on the 45 degree entry to Runway XX; as I was 5 miles west northwest of ZZZ."

column121: My second report regarding aircraft from the Palm Springs area performing erratic maneuvers in the face of IFR arrivals.  Aircraft Y departed Palm Springs and terminated services outside of the PSP Delta. They proceeded to fly just north of UDD and start performing aerobatic maneuvers between 050 and 060. This aircraft climbed right in front of Aircraft X; who was cleared for the RNAV visual 31L approach.  Traffic alert was issued to Aircraft X and while the pilot did not say 'near midair'; they did say 'he's right in front of us!' and the altitudes were the same.The pilot for Aircraft X said they had to respond to two RAs for that one aircraft.The pilot of Aircraft Y may have also been in violation of FAR Sec. 91.303 which prohibits aerobatic flight within 4 miles of a federal airway (V137) and over an area of congested population.I am requesting; again; for the PSP airspace to have a Charlie or Bravo airspace.  My colleagues and I deal with dangerous situations every day that could easily be avoided by having non-voluntary protected airspace.  This is the second event in one day where PSP Tower has terminated a VFR aircraft who ultimately got much too close to an IFR arrival. A Class C/B airspace and a requirement for Tower to hand off departures to approach; would allow the radar Controller to determine if a dangerous situation is imminent.As of now; the only controlled airspace in the Palm Springs valley is the Tower's Class D. Class E airspace exists around TRM; but only prohibits flight for poor weather. This airspace is a free-for-all with half of the aircraft talking to ATC and the other half are not; and just blasting through everyone else. This needs to change.Also; the local pilots should know better than to be flying in these congested areas doing aerobatics.  The safety culture of these pilots is questionable and should be discussed.
column125: SCT TRACON Controller reported re-occurring airborne conflicts between VFR aircraft executing aerobatic maneuvers and IFR arrivals into PSP.  Reporter advised they deal with these types of situations every day; which could be easily avoided by having non-voluntary protected airspace.

column121: Aircraft X arrived from the south east at 110 where ZZZ has a MVA (Minimum Vectoring Altitude) at the highest point of 115. I do not know if ZZZ1 has the same or lower MVA in that area. Per the LOA with ZZZ1 any arrivals in this area 130 or lower; must be on an airway and verbally coordinated. There was no coordination from ZZZ1.  OJTI was being conducted at the Approach position; I was the OS in the TRACON. Due to COVID social distancing requirements the OJTI was working from the 'B' scope; the trainee was working from the 'A' scope and I was at the OS desk. There is no radar scope or secondary monitor available at the OS desk to observe traffic; so I was unable to see the altitude of the aircraft. A regional jet that was about to be switched to the Tower told approach that an aircraft was trying to call approach and at that point I was able to observe Aircraft X at the altitude below the MVA. Aircraft X eventually was able to reach approach on radio and landed without incident.-Review ZZZ1-ZZZ LOA-Add a non-certified radar display at the ZZZ TRACON OS desk-Check the MVAs to the south east of ZZZ to see if there are any discrepancies between ZZZ1 and ZZZ MVAs.
column123: I am training the trainee on approach control. Three arrivals scheduled with close arrival times. Trainee accepted all three handoffs back to back well outside of our airspace boundary; further away than normal. He was not aware that Aircraft X was flying a wrong altitude for direction of flight as well as 500 feet below the MVA we have depicted on our maps until after accepting the handoff.  Neither of these two issues were coordinated between either facility. Aircraft landed without issue.We need to have matching MVA maps with ZZZ1. This could be a real issue in the future. That aircraft was 500 feet below our MVA and could not reach us on radio due to the terrain.

column121: I was working Local Control and had four aircraft waiting to depart  with Aircraft X on short final to an intersecting runway and Aircraft Y still about 10 mile final for the same runway  on an instrument approach for a option back to radar pattern. There was also two VFR picture taking aircraft around the Tower airspace at 2700 feet. I asked Aircraft X if he could land to hold short of the intersecting runway. He said he could; though hesitantly. I cleared him for LAHSO(Land and Hold Short). I began to depart the other aircraft of the crossing runway with turns and to also have them stop at 2000 feet for the picture takers. When Aircraft Y checked on; I cleared him for the option. My biggest mistake was putting expeditiousness over safety. I overlooked Aircraft X in two important ways.  I had an expectation bias that he would exit the runway before the intersection and  even the I scanned the runway; I missed him sitting at the intersection. The color of the plane and the concrete made it difficult to notice him. Not until Aircraft X keyed up to talk did I remember he was there but by then Aircraft Y had touched down; saw the aircraft and was stopping to avoid.This situation very poignantly reminded me (because it was a very close call and so thankful it was not more serious) that safety is our number one priority. As a facility we need to have a clear reminder that a runway is occupied as opposed to one single light that carries multiple meanings.  We should clearly define a procedure for LAHSO as well.

column121: I was working a sector and Controller in Charge combined during the event. I cleared an aircraft for the approach to maintain 13;000 feet until established on the localizer.  As I was about to switch the aircraft to Tower I noticed that they were at 12;100 feet on the approach.  The crossing altitude at the fix they were cleared to is 12;900 feet so I issued a low altitude alert and the crossing altitude.  The aircraft responded that they did not have the runway in sight and wanted to go around.  Since they were on an instrument approach I told them to execute the missed approach.  I noticed the aircraft continued to descend; so I issued another low altitude alert; this time using the 12;100 foot Minimum Vectoring Altitude as a reference since they were at 11;700 feet and advised they climb to 14;000 feet  immediately. Once the aircraft was above MVAs I issued a heading and eventually notified them of a possible pilot deviation and gave them the tower number.

column121: ZZZ ATIS advertising ILS Runway XY; winds 24008 favoring [Runway] ZA but it is normal for ZZZ to use 12 until winds are 10 KTS or greater due to terrain and approach undesirability of Runway ZA.Aircraft checked on with correct ATIS descending from Center and asked for RNAV ZA. I only heard RNAV.  The common request from airline is for the RNAV Z Runway XY when we are advertising ILS's because it saves flying miles/time.I should mention that the cleaning/disinfecting person was starting my station so I had to move out of the way and move items away for her cleaning process. This is a nightly routine and is highly distracting.I cleared Aircraft X for the RNAV Z Runway XY approach. To which they read back 'Cleared RNAV' (pause) 'Zulu; Runway XY' (the XY was dropped and due to the cleaning I only heard Y which I assumed was part of X-Y) [Note: there is NO Runway YA at ZZZ].I observed the aircraft making a right turn at ZZZZZ intersection instead of the left turn for Runway XY this alerted me the aircraft was following the Runway ZA approach path. I immediately canceled the approach clearance; assigned an appropriate altitude and heading and informed the pilot it appeared he was on the wrong approach and I was now vectoring for the correct approach. The pilot was still above all MVA's so there was no immediate danger. There was a short discussion with the pilot and he set up for the correct approach which was completed with no issue.1st; Cleaning/Disinfection of control position and its immediate vicinity by contract staff should be done only when the position is not being worked at that station. Our facility closes the TRACON at XA:00 and has a 5hr XX min window this could be done in with no distractions and eliminate the danger to the NAS is currently poses.2nd; this was a clear hearback/readback issue with expectation bias on both the controllers and pilots part.A- Because of distraction; I failed to hear the initial request for Runway ZA and followed up by issuing a standard clearance for the RNAV Z Runway XY with no other warning to the pilot such as 'Unable Runway ZA' as I would normally do with an opposite direction request I was unable to approve.B- The pilot readback a completely wrong clearance in many ways; The RNP approach he wanted to Runway ZA is called 'RNAV Runway ZA'. It does not have a Z and is to ZA not YA. By reading back 'Cleared Rnav Z Runway YA' it greatly increased the chances of my hearing it as I expected.C- In listening to the recording the pilot obviously was unsure of the clearance he got as there was a long pause between RNAV and Zulu; which leads me to believe the pilot had a flag going off in his head that he neglected to properly follow up on.  Had I not been involved with dancing around the cleaning person to be able to see my scope I probably would have picked up on this better. As it was I must have had a flag of some kind in my own mind because I did give extra vigilance to ensure the correct turn at ZZZZZ so there must have been something warning the back of my mind as well.In the end the system did work as it ultimately should and the error was caught and corrected with no risk. I know a last minute correction like this can cause stress on a flight crew and this crew handled it with high professionalism and safety was insured by both them and myself.

column121: Aircraft X; was on the CORRL STAR landing SFB.   Aircraft X checks in level at 5;000.  There is an un-tagged target Aircraft Y in their proximity at 5;900.  At that time; they are not in conflict; and the targets do not appear to be on a course to merge.This area is Class E airspace; there is no requirement to be in contact with ATC; and it is a practice area for flight schools.  Yes; this seems extremely unsafe; and it is!  We have numerous commercial aircraft; loaded with potentially over 100 human lives; going through a VFR practice area all day; every day and nothing is done to stop it.In this practice area; you can expect to see aircraft doing such things as stalls; steep turns; rapid altitude changes; just to name a few.After assigning Aircraft X a runway assignment and seeing no traffic that they are in immediate conflict with I move onto another task.  Aircraft Z; cold calls me requesting VFR practice approaches.  I get them a code and assign them the code of XXXX.  I wait a few seconds; and they aren't tagging up.   So now; I ask the call sign of the Aircraft Z requesting approaches; the aircraft tells me and that's when I see the primary target change to a code of XXXX.  I identify the aircraft; however; it won't tag up. I'm diagnosing why the target won't tag up and discover it's because they filed an IFR flight plan to be picked up in the air.  So now there are 2 codes for this aircraft in my tab list.  While I'm trying to delete the IFR code; Aircraft X keeps up and states they are responding to a TCAS/RA.  I immediately did issue a traffic alert; but it was too late.After looking at the replay; I watched the targets merge; and what the playback showed was the in-tagged target made a hard-left hand turn; and descended rapidly causing the TCAS/RA. It appears the the target was almost diving at the aircraft and 'buzzing' it.The Aircraft X company itself; has identified the area between OMN and SFB as the area they receive the most TCAS/RA's in.  But yet; we still fly them through there knowing this!  Why?  We issue multiple traffic advisories and traffic alerts in the area throughout the day.  It isn't a secret this is a dangerous area.  What would you expect; a major VFR practice area placed right in the middle of a published STAR; and at the published STARs altitude.  With no airspace (Class C; Class B) protecting the aircraft.This isn't the first time this has happened; and if nothing is done it won't be the last time.  The risk for a mid-air catastrophe in the area is extremely high.  Something needs to be done. My recommendation is either move the practice area or change the STAR.  We have 2 STARs into SFB; one over OMN and one over OCF.  This event happen on the OMN STAR.  The other STAR (OCF); is much safer; but yet we continue to receive multiple Aircraft X aircraft over the OMN STAR.  In fact; the majority of the Aircraft X [company aircraft] are assigned the OMN STAR!  I know; it doesn't make sense; but this is done all day every day.Now the provider (F11); the user Aircraft X [Company] has identified a significant safety risk to the FAA; and yet we are still in this predicament.Stop using the OMN STAR; that is my recommendation!  Unless weather or other safety factors are more dangerous than this. I would be hard pressed to find a more dangerous spot than this.Also; flight schools should be picking up their airborne IFR with us and telling us on initial contact they have one on file.  This will prevent 2 codes being generated for the same aircraft and aircraft automatically tagging up when assigned a code.  We are going to work with them locally to see if they will start doing this.

column121: I was working Aircraft X as a visual approach to PSP. They were cleared about 15-20 miles NE of the airport. I noticed a 'Z' tag (PSP Tower) on a downwind departure that was moving quickly; so it caught my attention. I clicked on it to see that it was the local [aircraft type]; Aircraft Y.  This aircraft is notorious in our area for getting in the way of IFR arrivals to PSP; so I kept my eye on the data tag.Aircraft Y dropped off the Z tag and turned southbound after passing an arrival to 31L. This put them on a converging course with Aircraft X. I still had some altitude separation to work with; so I called traffic to Aircraft X and told them to stop at 045 since Aircraft Y appeared to be momentarily level at 040. I thought maybe Aircraft Y saw the arrival. After I stopped Aircraft X; tower called and said Aircraft Y had Aircraft X in sight. I then saw Aircraft Y start climbing; and issued a traffic alert to Aircraft X. At the same time Aircraft X started descending.Aircraft Y was never pointed out to me; and no coordination was performed by the tower. Aircraft Y pilot is notorious in our area because they fly around at high speed; in highly congested areas; doing aerobatic maneuvers without talking to anyone. Then they fly back in the PSP; skipping approach every time; avoiding any sequencing from us. They force us to move around them; since we don't know what their intentions are; and then call the tower right before the Delta to get pattern entry instructions. This is all legal; but unsafe. The plane is from a local area; and they are very aware of the procedures and traffic in the area. However; they continue to avoid talking to approach control because it is not required in a TRSA.The few times the pilot has been on approach frequency; they are argumentative and obviously frustrated with our instructions.  The safety culture has obviously left this pilot and they feel they are above the rest of us and our mission of safe and efficient flow.The issue in this event is the conflict happened right outside the delta; with no coordination; and probably some pressure from the pilot to get on course and on their way. The Tower probably felt pressure to terminate the aircraft; as they were already outside of their delta airspace; and the TRSA has no requirements for participation.PSP airspace needs a Class Charlie or Bravo airspace around it. We provide no protection to a steady stream of arrivals by having a voluntary participation airspace surrounding it. The airspace is BUSY. The controllers have no safe airspace to assume separation. Aircraft fly through our arrival and departure corridors at leisure; stopping departures; corrupting sequences and causing unstable or aborted approaches.UDD is located about 10 miles east of PSP and has VFR jet departures launching all day long; right at aircraft in the downwind for PSP. What major airport allows for aircraft to fly into the radar downwind?  This creates an incredibly confusing situation for the controller; as we have no idea what the intentions are of any of the aircraft. When you look at the amount of traffic; the speed; and the lack of maneuvering room; the Springs/Desert sector is a time bomb waiting to explode. We as controllers do the best we can to alleviate these situations; but with mountains surrounding almost all quadrants of the valley; there are few routes to take in and out; which results in many head-on situations.I have a similar report that I filed a few years ago where a YAK converged with an arrival for PSP 13R and the pilot reported a NMAC with the aircraft.  Protected Class Charlie or Bravo airspace would have avoided both of these situations. The controllers and pilots deserve to be afforded some level of protection when close into the airport. RA's and and NMAC's 5-10 miles away from a major airport are unacceptable.

column121: Aircraft X entered the airspace for 4 miles south of HIO at 020 heading eastbound. I used the ADS-B function on the radar display to determine the call sign. It appeared as if Aircraft X was going to join the pattern at ZZZ; a nearby airport. Aircraft X exited the airspace and was not in conflict with any aircraft. No attempt at communication was made. Aircraft X turned north and re-entered the airspace at 2100 ft.; 4 miles southeast of HIO. Aircraft X then made a right turn and joined a 5 mile final at 1800 ft. Aircraft X broke off of final and flew north; then suddenly rejoined a 3 mile final. This was the first attempt at communication with Aircraft X. I attempted communications and Ground Control attempted communications on their frequency; 121.5; and the practice area frequency.  Aircraft Y was in right traffic for the right side runway and Aircraft X looked like he was joining final for the same runway I changed Aircraft Y to the left runway and issued a full stop landing clearance until Aircraft X was safely on the ground. Aircraft X overflew the right runway and began circling in the north portion of the airspace. Aircraft X was given the green light gun signal multiple times but never landed. In total; 6 different aircraft had their flight paths adjusted to avoid Aircraft X. Aircraft Y called ready for departure; IFR on a SID. I called Approach and requested a 5 minute release to attempt to avoid Aircraft X's erratic flight paths. Aircraft X was on a left downwind heading for the opposite direction runway and had been circling for approximately 15 minutes. I cleared Aircraft Y for takeoff assuming that Aircraft X would continue their right turns and not be a factor for Aircraft Y. Aircraft X suddenly turned a left base and descended just at Aircraft Y was lifting off. I issued a traffic alert for Aircraft Y; who reported the traffic in sight and maneuvered to avoid Aircraft X. It appeared that the closest proximity was 200 ft. lateral and 0 ft. vertical. Aircraft X overflew the runway at 500 ft. and made a left turn northbound. Aircraft Y rejoined the SID and was shipped to Departure Control. Ground Control received a phone call from ZZZ1 Tower; who has been in contact with Aircraft X since he entered HIO airspace. Ground Control advised that Aircraft X is cleared to land on any runway. Aircraft X intercepted a left base for an intersecting runway but flew through final. Aircraft X transitioned outside of the airspace at 1000 ft. Aircraft X was tagged up as 'VIOLTR' and Approach advised that Aircraft X landed at ZZZ1.The backup radios in the Tower are difficult to use. Having a newer; digital; handheld version similar to the ones that pilots use might make it easier to establish communications with a lost pilot. A shout line connected to the other nearby towers might also help.
column123: As the pilot in command; I departed in my airplane on a VFR cross country flight to ZZZ1. The aircraft has an installed GNS 430W GPS and GTX-330ES transponder providing ADSB capability. The route of flight followed a basically southerly course direction; with route guidance provided by the G430W direct to my destination. Approximately 15 to 20 miles from my destination; I announced my intention to land. The GPS map course indicated that I was closing in on what was indicated to be my destination; and I made preparations to land; with a call to Tower; indicating my intentions. Having received no response; I maneuvered the aircraft west of the airport in an attempt to gain some time for working out the communication problem. Eventually; I received a broken communication that provided clearance to land. I set up my approach and noticed that the runway markings did not match up to what I expected and did not land. I found out later that I was at a nearby airport however; and this did not correspond to my Garmin 430W readout. Given the communications issues I was having; I contacted my son with my cell phone; who was on the ground having traveled there independently. He contacted the Tower; indicating there was a problem with my flight. The Air Traffic Controller on duty; at that time; was able to locate my aircraft almost immediately and provided vectors. Communications between my aircraft [and ATC] improved as I flew closer to the airport. I made a safe; uneventful landing.To summarize; it is apparent that there were significant issues with medium to long range communications and erroneous GPS information that directly caused concerns with this flight. There was nothing obvious indicating that there was equipment failure on board my aircraft; at that time or subsequently. The return trip was perfectly normal; with support from ATC flight following the entire way; later that evening.The real concern comes down to bad GPS tracking data; which placed me in the wrong airport airspace unintentionally; whereas; I had reason to believe; based on erroneous information; that I was receiving correct information pursuant to an airport arrival at my intended destination There have been published reports of military GPS interruptions for testing and training purposes in the area that are known to have impacted civilian aircraft. Perhaps such jamming activities disrupted my GPS on board; thus would have provided inaccurate location guidance to my Garmin 430W.

column121: An Airport Vehicle asked if they could get onto Runway XXL to clear signs. I clarified if they needed go to the approach end of Runway XYR or the approach end of Runway XXL. They stated the approach end of Runway XYR. I instructed the Airport Vehicle to proceed onto the approach end of Runway XYR; and hold short of Runway XZC at all times. I received a correct read back of the hold short instructions for Runway XZC. A short time later; I attempted to reach out to the Airport Vehicle to reiterate the hold short instructions and ensure they would give way to an Airbus that I was initially going to take down Runway XYR. I did not get an answer from the Airport Vehicle. I then changed the taxi instructions for Aircraft Y to stay on the taxiway; cross Runway XZL and hold short of Runway XZC. Once I ensured that Aircraft Y was holding short of Runway XZC; and the Airport Vehicle did not appear to be moving forward; I looked down to finish processing some flight progress strips. At that time; the Supervisor on the Watch Desk asked to verify that the Airport Vehicle was holding short Runway XZC. I said he was supposed to be; keyed up and reiterated the hold short instructions. The Supervisor instructed Local Control to issue a go around for Aircraft X. The Airport Vehicle appeared to just cross the runway edge of Runway XZC on Runway XYR. They immediately reversed and exited Runway XZC; remaining on Runway XYR. Soon afterwards; I issued the brasher warning.Next time I will ensure I reiterate the hold short instructions when a vehicle is in close proximity to the active runway.

column121: I was flying into AWO. The winds favored Runway 29 as they were out of 270 at 10 knots with peak gusts of 21 knots. All aircraft had been landing [Runway] 29 all day. I was making all of my turn calls doing laps in the pattern and noticed that there was an aircraft in the patter behind me. They had no lights on; so I checked my Foreflight running on Sentry to see if they had an ADSB beacon and they did not show up. I made a few calls asking if anyone else was in the pattern and repeatedly made my calls Crosswind; Downwind; Base; and Final all landing Runway 29. I incorrectly assumed that anyone else with common sense would also be landing [Runway] 29 given the strong gusty conditions. On Final approach; out of the corner of my eye; I noticed an aircraft to my left coming in on Runway 34. At AWO there is a hot spot on the final approach where incoming airplanes could converge. We were less than 100 feet off a collision course if evasive action was not taken. The other aircraft made no modifications to their course and I immediately climbed 100 feet to avoid a potential collision. I decided to land long to taxi over and see who was flying the aircraft and under what conditions.The aircraft which had two people inside both wearing headsets. While taxiing past them I called their tail number on the radio and received no response.I believe that whoever was flying the aircraft was intentionally avoiding awareness that they were indeed flying and were doing so in a way that endangered me and others in the area.

column121: I was pilot monitoring on this flight. ATC was giving us vectors for a visual approach to RWY XX at ZZZ. We were at 2;000 feet on a right base between waypoints ZZZZZ and ZZZZZ1 (1;500 feet MSL) when we told Approach we had the airport in sight. We were cleared for the visual and told to contact Tower. I said 'glide slope alive' and the First Officer asked for gear down flaps 15. I looked down to select the Tower frequency (which I didn't have preselected) and told them we were on the visual for RWY XX. We were cleared to land. When I saw we were capturing the localizer I noticed that the glide slope was now full scale deflection (low). The First Officer had selected a lower altitude (1;100 feet) on the MCP without telling me and so we were below the final approach fix altitude of 1;500 feet at ZZZZZ1. I called for a go around and we were vectored back around for a successful landing. The cause was a long flight across the country with last minute vectors to final for a visual approach led to the First Officer thinking we were high nearing the FAF and incorrectly setting an altitude lower than glideslope intercept altitude without verbally advising the Captain.

column121: My student and I were returning to the home airport from the local practice area after a training flight. Since this was the student's 2nd training flight; I was explaining the correct traffic pattern entry and radio calls to him. I made the initial radio call 10 miles away from the airport and stated location; altitude and intentions. A short time later; a Mooney checked on to the CTAF and announced his location and intentions. I noted to myself that the Mooney was near us; but behind and decided to be vigilant. The wind favored landings on Runway XX that day; which meant that we would have to fly from the practice area to the northwest of the airport to an area southwest of the airport in order to make the 45 degree entry to the right traffic. I was a bit concerned as the student and I were flying in a Cessna 150 which is very slow and was worried about being overtaken by the faster Mooney as we both set up for the traffic pattern entry. I made my next radio call at a location approximately 4-5 miles northwest of the airport and stated that we intended to maneuver for the 45 degree entry to the traffic pattern. I also directed the student to descend to traffic pattern altitude. I continued to visually scan for the Mooney. Since I expected the Mooney to overtake us and since the right of way rules state that overtaking aircraft should pass on the right; I spent most of my time searching on our right. The pilot of the Mooney made a radio call and stated a position and distance from the airport that was very similar to ours. I became even more concerned about the possibility for a collision and made an additional radio call with our revised position and stated that we were in a Cessna 150; in an attempt to inform the Mooney pilot of our speed and the possibility for collision. The pilot of the Mooney made another radio call and stated his position; which was very similar to ours; 5 miles west of the airport. I became very concerned about the situation and began to aggressively scan for them. I didn't feel that it would be appropriate to divert towards the airport; as it would cause me to conflict with the traffic pattern. I also didn't feel that it would be appropriate to divert away from the airport; as I expected the Mooney to pass on the right. A very short time later; the Mooney passed by us on our left and slightly above us. It appeared to me that we were vertically separated by less than 200 feet and laterally separated by less than 200 feet.I realized after the fact; that I had allowed myself to continue into the possibility of a collision because I felt I had the right of way as the slower aircraft and because I did not want to cause my student an undue expense of maneuvering away from the airport. I noted that the other pilot made three radio calls that expressed essentially the same position and distance from the airport; yet I made no attempt to escape the situation. In the future; I will be more aggressive about maneuvering out of or away from potential traffic pattern conflicts.

column121: In my aircraft (Aircraft X) I was positioned at the approach end but off Runway XX at ZZZ Airport; in preparation for taking Runway XX for my second takeoff of the morning. An aircraft (Aircraft Y) with a male voice pilot called inbound for Runway XY. Shortly; a female voice pilot in a Hawker aircraft (Aircraft Z) called 10 miles out for Runway XX. I radioed and advised I would wait for landing traffic Runway XY. Then; Aircraft Z called her turn to base for Runway XX. Shortly; Aircraft Y radioed he was on a 5 mile approach for Runway XY. I visually saw the incoming Aircraft Z on final approach for Runway XX at the same time she called final for Runway XX. The pilot of Aircraft Y radioed he would change to downwind for Runway XX. Aircraft Z passed visually by me as I was perpendicular to the Runway XX. I watched her land; complete her taxi and turnoff off the runway on XX taxiway; about midfield; when she called clear of Runway XX. I checked to my sides and scanned for Aircraft Y; but did not see that aircraft. I felt that with the passage of time that occurred Aircraft Y was downwind Runway XX and behind my field of vision. I called taking Runway XX with a left turnout to leave the area. I rolled; lifted off; maintained Runway XX heading for 3/4 to 1 mile; then turned left while continuing to climb. Then I heard the pilot of Aircraft Y say an aircraft just crossed under him. I looked back and saw his aircraft above; perpendicular to and ahead of my flight path. I radioed that I had just departed Runway XX having given a prior announcement that I was making a left turnout and that I thought he was much further downwind. He repeated that I crossed underneath him. I said I was sorry; but that I calculated him much further downwind based upon his call of change to downwind [Runway] XX. There was no other comment from either of us.

column121: I am a private pilot; multi-engine student. I was working with my instructor on a variety of things that morning in the practice area. We came back to the airport and proceeded to perform some touch and go landings on Runway XX; left traffic. We performed two touch and go landings; then a full stop landing. We taxied back and performed two more touch and go landings before the tower asked us to switch to right traffic (five landings in total). On downwind; the nose gear light didn't come on immediately. The hydraulic lever would kick back telling us it was pressurized and I had visual confirmation that the front gear was down.We performed three more touch and go landings to do our best to confirm that the gear was down and locked. I recall the nose gear light would flicker from time to time. We performed the checklist items accordingly but could not get the nose light to stay on when extending the gear. Every time the hydraulic gear lever would return to neutral and the gear was visible in the mirror.  My instructor requested to the tower that we switch to the non active Runway (XY) so that he could land and get out to see what was going on with the gear. With the instructor flying; he chose to feather the left engine after the gear was lowered. No flaps were used. Upon landing; he shutdown the right engine. The plane initially acted normal but started to veer to the right as we continued down the runway. I could feel that he had very little directional control. He also has no brakes on the right seat position so he yelled 'left brake!; left brake!' I pushed the left brake as hard as I could and the plane turned left enough to stay on the runway; however shortly thereafter the nose gear collapsed. We skidded to a stop; shut everything down and exited the plane. The right engine hadn't stopped in time so the prop was damaged from contacting the runway. The left engine was feathered so there didn't appear to be any damage to it. The plane was removed from the non active runway and brought back to the hangar. I have no idea what we could have done differently as we had hydraulic pressure and visual confirmation that the gear was down (bad switch?). The previous touch and go landings were fine with no adverse steering issues. Perhaps the nose gear wasn't getting a lot of contact during the touch and goes but I still had directional control prior to him landing. Feathering the left engine obviously saved that from damage. The intent was to only gently land and inspect prior to taxing back to the hangar; but due to other circumstances the plane veered right; was corrected with left brake and the nose gear still ended up collapsing.

column121: During a routine training session of multi-engine student instruction on DATE; we experienced nose gear indication problems which terminated with a collapsed nose gear and right engine/propeller damage.Numerous maneuvers had been performed involving several landing gear extension retraction cycles; and 5 takeoffs and landings; all normal until last 3 patterns during which nose gear initially showed locked then unsafe. Several touch and go landings(3) were performed during which we attempted to lightly touch nose wheel to move into locked position and get the safe indication. Some of those attempts were firm enough to confirm gear was actually down and locked. Landing gear hydraulic lever had returned to center and gear was visible in mirror every time. Checklist items for a single light out; handle returning to neutral; gear warning horn active was followed and in addition three touchdown taps indicated to me gear was extended. Discussed with student all actions and elected to land on Runway XY instead of active [Runway] XX so we could land; roll out gently; stop; and shut down so I could visually inspect the gear. After we were cleared to land on Runway XY I feathered left engine on base; continued approach; conducted gump's and final checks. As main gear contacted runaway I pulled mixture on number two engine and although initial touchdown was normal; aircraft began a slight yaw to the right along with some unusual noise like rubbing or scuffing. Felt similar to right main tire going flat quickly followed by greatly increasing yaw; much worse than a flat tire. We applied left full rudder pedal but yaw was increasing dramatically aircraft was nearly 30 degrees to centerline and about to leave runway. As there are no wheel brakes on my side of aircraft so I loudly instructed student 'left break; left break; left brake; left brake 'which he did; straightening out aircraft parallel to centerline approximately 6 ft. from edge of runway. Shortly after this nose gear collapsed and remaining 1 to 200 ft. of runway was done on the nose of the aircraft and the main gear. Once stopped; we shut off master; generators; avionics; fuel and vacated aircraft. Myers aviation removed airplane and put in our hanger. Upon inspection and disassembly it was discovered that nose wheel was turned past the stops to the right; tire scuff marks; and hydraulic actuator bent 90deg in fully extended position most likely due to excessive side load. Upper drag link assembly tubing cracked; separated; other parts bent or damaged and all located except one turn limit stop pin.

column121: I was inbound to ZZZ from the southeast at 1;500 feet MSL; making entry for a left-hand downwind for Runway XX. Listening on CTAF; there was a Helicopter flying the practice ILS [Runway] XY into ZZZ; about 5 miles out. There were two aircraft ready to go on Runway XX; a Cirrus Aircraft X and an experimental Aircraft Z. The Helicopter continued to make position reports; e.g. 'ZZZ Traffic; Navy helicopter on 4 mile final; ILS [Runway] XY'The Cirrus started to roll on Runway XX; while the Helicopter was now on about a 0.5 mile final for runway XY. As I overflew the field; I watched as the Helicopter and the Cirrus were on a collision path headed towards the intersection of runways at ZZZ. While the Cirrus was on the roll; he inquired on CTAF if the Helicopter had the Cirrus in sight. The Helicopter said it did and being a helicopter; was able to immediately with a swift pitch up maneuver; reduce airspeed and come to a hover before the runway intersection; thus preventing a collision. The Cirrus continued to take off and fly the upwind leg up and out of the airport. I believe the issue stemmed from the Helicopter making radio calls including IFR terms while the pilot in the Cirrus may have been a VFR only pilot. The inclusion of 'ILS' may have caused confusion with the Cirrus pilot; making them feel that the helicopter was farther away than reality.

After maneuvering to avoid them I changed the pattern to a non standard right pattern and still encountered them on that side of the airport at different altitudes. They do not have radios; and being slow are hard to see; especially the light of a low; soon to be setting sun.This is a very unsafe airport ; especially for training; when these PPC's are operating. 7F3 is a high student pilot training facility and this needs to be addressed ASAP.
