column121: On vectors from Houston Approach for the ILS 14 at GLS. Cleared to intercept the localizer from current heading maintain 2;000 feet. Tracking the localizer inbound at 2;000 feet was given a handoff to Tower. I wasn't cleared for the approach so I asked if I was cleared ILS. Was given clearance and switched to GLS Tower.The glideslope needle went active and I lowered the gear to follow the glide slope down. 400 feet later the glide slope needle flagged out and came in and out. I initiated a climb and realized I was outside of the final approach fix. This was a false glideslope.I climbed back to 1;800 feet and approached the FAF. The real glideslope came in and I completed the approach.I reported the issue via telephone to the Tower after landing.The late clearance from Center for the approach [was a contributing factor]. I didn't brief myself clearly enough on the minimum altitudes until the FAF. I had a big headwind so my internal timer felt right to be descending but the reduced ground speed I was still outside of the FAF.My reaction to realizing the issue was fast. I stopped my descent and started a climb quickly upon seeing the glideslope problems. I then identified the problem and what happened. Resumed the approach when everything was correct.

column121: While on approach to landing in ZZZ; a gust of wind caused me to abort that landing. I was able to get enough altitude to take the gondola through the tops of trees just ahead of me. Once through the trees; I immediately saw power lines at eye level approximately 20 feet ahead of me. I immediately shut off the propane tank and turned off the burner; attempting to minimize any probable excessive explosion. After hearing the power line break; but not having an explosion and discovering that we were still aloft; I quickly assessed the state of passengers and equipment. Finding absolutely no damage; I turned on the propane tank and relit the burner. My ground team contacted me by radio and I informed them that we are okay and planning to land at the next available opportunity. I flew on and had a safe landing approximately 1/4 mile away. There was no aircraft damage and there were no injuries.

column121: Aircraft X; cleared Visual Approach  off a downwind turn behind a Heavy. Aircraft X was pointed away and behind Heavy when cleared to follow the traffic and issued caution wake turbulence. The pilot turned to follow the Heavy at his discretion towards the traffic and the field to a dog leg at about 5.5 miles in trail of the Heavy. Aircraft X joined final approach behind the traffic and I; the Final Radar Controller; observed what looked like a quick radar track anomaly. Quickly followed was an observed turn and quick descent and climb by Aircraft X back towards the field. The pilot promptly advised of a 'complete upset' and that he was back on the turn towards the traffic and on the Visual Approach with out a chance for me to issue any instructions.Later to find out this verbiage meant the aircraft had been sent into a complete roll to the right in which the pilot continued to recover the aircraft. No official cause but I can only guess wake turbulence from the Heavy traffic sent him into an uncontrolled roll. I acknowledged; inquired if any assistance was required to which the pilot responded in the negative; and I continued to work the traffic to include breaking out a Heavy behind him to allow him to continue the Visual Approach. The aircraft continued on the approach and double confirmed that everything was okay and no assistance was required before sending him to the Tower for landing.No recommendation; but awareness for all pilots of potential wake turbulence induced events. I personally could have ensured the aircraft was at a higher altitude to possibly prevent the wake trajectory; but I was originally setting up Aircraft X for an ILS Approach to follow at the appropriate  wake turbulence minimums. Only after the pilot called traffic and field in sight was he given the Visual Approach clearance to follow. If the pilot did not feel comfortable with this; I did not know or could have recommended a different method of approach.

column121: Turning for a 7 to 8 NM final from left base to Runway XX with aircraft configured flaps 20; PF established aircraft on ILS Runway XX GS and LOC for visual approach. PM made a VFR traffic call for an 8 NM final landing Runway XX. Another jet on the ground at ZZZ made multiple calls for a radio check and the PM responded he could hear the jet loud and clear. PF called autopilot off; and request the visual approach for Runway XX in the FMS. The PM entered the Runway XX visual approach into the FMS.  PM made a 5 NM final call on CTAF. The jet on the ground informed the PM they were taxiing on Runway XX and would clear the runway prior to aircraft landing and then engaged the PM in a conversation about their inability to contact ATC Clearance. PF called flaps 30 and flaps 45 and slowed the aircraft on speed and Glide Slope. PM read the before landing check and at the LANDING GEAR DOWN call both the PF and PM noted the lack of DOWN 3 GREEN on the display panel and then visually checked the gear handle and noted it was in the up position. This occurred at about 1;100 feet HAT and was follow by the gear horn going off. PF immediately began a go-around and made call outs PM preformed G/A TO actions and traffic calls. The aircraft descended to 900 to 1000 HAT before entering the upwind for Runway XX and performed an uneventful left traffic pattern landing on Runway XX.This was my first day back from a month of leave and my Captain had also recently returned from leave. Rust may have been a factor. Additionally; we were flying at a high altitude airport with terrain at night with no Tower. There was definitely a communication break down and I was tasked saturated and should have let the Captain know I was in the red; and I could have missed the gear call. The final distraction was the jet on the ground taxiing on Runway XX where they should not have been; and fixation on them clearing the runway and engaging in a conversation about contacting ATC to get their IFR clearance was also a factor taking my awareness outside the aircraft.In the future I need to be more aware of my saturation level and communicate this correctly to my captains and avoid engaging in extraneous ground communications with other aircraft. Also; I need to visually and verbally verify my actions and confirm call outs by myself and other pilots regardless if I am PM or PF and verbally verify ambiguous calls or situations.

column121: We were cleared approach RNAV Z XXR.  I looked at the navigation display and saw a solid unbroken magenta line from our position to the runway.  LNAV and VNAV displayed in FMA's.  Prior to this we were given delete speeds and at some point; I think we were assigned 210 knots.  Weather was clear VMC.  We were looking for traffic.  At some point traffic was called to us.  I did not see the discontinuity at ZZZZZ in the MCDU.  Approaching ZZZZZ I felt the plane was not turning to follow the path as it should.  I disengaged the autopilot and hand flew the rest of the procedure.  ATC asked us if we were turning and then asked if we could land.In the 10 months preceding this incident; I have 18 hours of block time.  I have 2 hours of block time in the last 90 days.  I was an X type aircraft Captain but was retrained as a First Officer (FO).  I have 0 hours as an FO in this aircraft prior to training.  My training consisted of one 4-hour sim period and two OE legs.  I believe the Captain was just back from a medical issue; had done a short training course and this was his second trip on the line.
column123: Arrival into ZZZ during daytime VFR conditions with excellent visibility. Our FMC was programmed for the arrival as well as the RNAV RNP Z to XXR with the transition at ZZZZZ intersection. Our primary error was that we did not close the FMC discontinuity between the arrival and the approach. The result was that we overflew ZZZZZ1 intersection and corrected immediately after recognizing that we were not turning at the appropriate time. In hindsight it appears that multiple distractions contributed to our failure to remove the discontinuity. The change in speed assignments as well as a request by the Controller to watch for Aircraft Y traffic on a parallel runway and the timing associated with the issuance of our approach clearance.We were able to visually identify the Aircraft Y parallel traffic and kept visual separation. Once established on the extended runway centerline we received TA/RA warnings associated with the proximity of the Aircraft Y on XXL.  Also; there was never any indication that our aircraft deviated laterally of the extended runway centerline that would have compromised separation.Immediately after arrival at the gate I called approach control to discuss the late turn on the approach. (I was not asked to call.) I requested to speak with a Supervisor and we had a 5-10 minute discussion. I was told there was 'nothing on their end' in the way of a complaint. We also had an additional discussion about the traffic advisories associated with our approach given the close proximity of parallel traffic.One of the challenges of flying into ZZZ is that we typically are issued our runway assignment; approach; and transition in the latter stage of our arrival. Also; once issued; it seems to be relatively common to be reassigned to yet another runway; approach and transition. It is also common to receive speed modifications on the arrival.We were prepared for the RNAV Z to XXR and per company procedure we had the default arrival transition programmed. In hindsight I believe that if our clearance to fly the approach had come earlier in the arrival phase (when we had less distractions) that we would have been more likely to close the link between the arrival and approach and avoid our error.

column121: We; a C172S G1000; were flying to ZZZ under VFR radar vector with RNAV Y RWYXX for practice approach. While approaching to FAF; right after Approach told us to contact Tower; we found a Piper Cherokee on our 11 o'clock less than 500 feet horizontal above us and descending and approaching to us. We could not see them until last moment because they were above our wing 'blind spot.' Approach never mentioned about this traffic and our G1000 display never displayed its ADS-B location.  After I found the traffic; I took over the control from student who was under the hood and started descent in about 500-700 feet per minute. We reported to Approach after the traffic passed behind us. Approach responded that; it was showing 1;000 feet vertical separation with us so Approach did not mentioned that traffic. Also; that traffic was not talking to Approach; so Approach did not know their intention. After that; we continued the approach and landed at ZZZ.

column121: Winds were moderate; variable; and gusty; but within limits for the aircraft. In accordance with accepted technique; half the gust factor; approximately 6 knots; was added to the final approach and landing speeds. Just after touchdown on the centerline; with all three gears established on the runway; a gust was felt and corrected for. As the correction was made with what was perceived as moderate aileron and rudder input; the airplane veered sharply to the left. With the thought that over-controlling back to the right could lead to the left wingtip dipping and then digging into the runway resulting in the aircraft tumbling (worst case) or a ground loop at high energy (best case); I elected to input enough control to keep the aircraft tracking as straight ahead as possible. This resulted in departing the runway surface to the left of the runway. Once in the gravel that was lining the left side of the runway; my entire focus was on maintaining enough control not to hit obstacles or dig in gear or wingtips so as to cause tumbling. In the process of dissipating energy; I crossed two high-speed taxiways at almost a perpendicular angle and at a still relatively high-speed resulting in the aircraft becoming lighter on the struts. The struts compressed again on the other side of the taxiways resulting in enough energy depletion to safely steer back to the runway surface.Upon re-establishing safe contact with asphalt; the aircraft was taxied a short distance to parking. Upon the post-flight walk-around inspection; there was visible damage to two of three propeller blade tips (of composite material made by Hartzell) from contact with gravel likely occurring when the struts compressed after crossing the taxiways. No other visible damage to the aircraft was observed nor were fluids observed to be leaking. It is very likely the root cause of this event was the application of too much rudder for the capabilities of the aircraft in countering the cross-wind gust after landing.The PA-46 line of aircraft is known to have very sensitive nose wheel steering; especially at higher speeds; and while being aware of it and managing it were trained to extensively; it's entirely possible that too much rudder was applied as was appropriate for this aircraft type. Habit pattern transference is a likely contributing factor in that I have several thousand hours in other type aircraft; to include very recent and continued professional flying experience in the Pilatus PC-12; and all of which do not exhibit the level of sensitivity in nose wheel steering that the PA-46 does. In instinctively applying the crosswind gust correction I'm used to; it is very possible that I applied an amount that was too much for safe control of the PA-46 at the time of occurrence.I do not feel training was a contributing factor as I received very thorough Piper-authorized training and initial conditioning in the aircraft with a Piper-authorized instructor. However; overall low time in type is definitely a contributing factor. Up to the time of this event; I had logged 20 landings in the PA-46 and several of which were in gusty conditions and or crosswinds similar to what was experienced during this event. In the future; the biggest take away from the event for me for flying the PA-46 is to concentrate on keeping the nose wheel off the runway as long as possible as energy depletes so that when the nose wheel does contact the runway; it's at the lowest speed practical. During this event the nose wheel was centered upon initial contact with the runway; as was emphasized in training. Any application of nose wheel steering at higher speeds should always be done very lightly at first to ensure the aircraft remains under control.

column121: Turning final ZZZZ fully configured at 150 knots I armed the Localizer first than the Approach mode. First Officer inquired and received the landing clearance. I noticed that the glide slope didn't capture and that we were going above the path. I tried to descend in Vertical Speed; but it didn't come active; so it wouldn't work. Approach mode light still on. I disconnected the Autopilot; lowered the nose and started a descend using the VGSI (Visual Glideslope) path indicator and the outside PAPI (Precision Approach Path Indicator). During the descent we tried to reset the Approach/Glideslope unsuccessfully. The approach was pretty bumpy with some good downdrafts. The aircraft went below the glide slope and I started to correct back when the pull up warning came on then stopped right away. Then the glide slope warning followed. I was just under the path recovering and had 3 red lights and 1 white on the PAPI. Speed around 10 knots above Vref and decreasing toward Vref. I was very focused on the approach and continued to an uneventful normal landing.We were configured early; ready for approach; but the Glideslope not capturing and the uncertainty concerning the landing clearance created a distraction that put me behind the aircraft and I developed some tunnel vision trying to remedy the approach. I became fixated and continued when I should have discontinued the approach and gone around. After landing the First Officer said to me that she had mentioned going around; but it hadn't registered with me during the approach. I tell First Officers that there is no mission; but today I didn't follow my own advice. It's easy to become over focused without realizing it. When in doubt; go-around.
column123: The event began while beginning the ILS Approach. The company notes on the approach dictate not to arm the ILS until reaching a certain position because of the possibility of false localizer signals. The Captain was the Pilot Flying and I was the Pilot Monitoring.  I remember thinking that the Pilot Flying armed the approach later than I would have since we were well beyond the designated point. My best guess on what happened is that because of the delay the localizer armed but the glideslope never captured because we were already too high. Whatever the reason for the automation not doing what we had expected; the result was that we realized that the Glideslope never captured and were trying to get the aircraft to come down.Not only were we distracted with being above the intended glide path; but we were handed over to tower late and I was busy with getting clearance to land while the Pilot Flying was flying the plane trying to get back to a stabilized approach. As the aircraft was getting back to a normal glide path (3 red; 1 white on PAPI) around the 500 foot mark I gave my stabilized approach callout and realizing we were slightly above speed and correcting; but the vertical speed exceeded what is acceptable. At this point I said 'I think we should go around'. I can't remember if it was just before or after I said this that we got a 'PULL UP' alert. The Captain elected to continue the landing. Later after discussion; I learned he may have had tunnel vision to the point where my go around call didn't register with him.My unfamiliarity with ZZZZ did not help our situational awareness as well as being distracted with getting switched over to tower (late) and attempting to get landing clearance around the same time we realized the automation did not perform as expected. As a FO/PM not being assertive enough when a go-around was needed.Abiding by company SOP to the letter. No matter what; at 500 feet if stabilized approach criteria aren't met; it's an automatic and firm 'GO AROUND'.

column121: Inbound to ZZZ from the south. Reported on CTAF 5 nm south of airport inbound for landing on Runway XX. A flight of 3 RVs reported taxiing out for XX. Approaching the airport RVs reported their takeoff roll; just prior to entering the downwind leg RVs reported they would be departing southbound. I then reported I was 2 minutes south. At that time; I observed the RVs over the departure end turning left. At this time; I could see them on a direct course toward me; they were climbing; I was level at pattern altitude (900 feet MSL). They were basically climbing or about to climb through my altitude; my best option was to descend underneath them; as they were climbing. At that point one of the RVs; I assume the leader reported seeing me headed underneath them. After we passed; I climbed back to pastern altitude and turned downwind; the RVs continued southbound.I saw the RVs from their x-wind turn. I thought they would make a standard 45 departure from the x-wind leg prior to turning southbound but they continued south. I should have been a little more to the East so that I joined the downwind at mid-field. They should have departed on a 45 to the x-wind. Fortunately; I was watching for them and saw them in time to avoid them. Everybody being on frequency was a positive.

column121: Aircraft X was southbound at 030 for a visual approach to Runway XX at ZZZ. I accepted a VFR point out from ZZZ1 facility on a XXXX code just a few miles east of ZZZZZ intersection going a few miles west of ZZZZZ to turn back to ZZZ1 airspace along the coastline at 015.  I did not tag up the point out because there was no traffic in that vicinity; and I was busy messing with my COVID mask. I was trying to put my mask on which I had in my pocket.  I cleared Aircraft X into Runway XX for the visual approach. I switched Aircraft X to the Tower when the point out aircraft was 7NM East of Aircraft X at 015. The tower controller issued traffic to Aircraft X three times. The first traffic call Aircraft X reported seeing the aircraft on TCAS. The third traffic call Aircraft X reported the traffic insight. No traffic alert was given to Aircraft X and no TCAS RA was performed. Aircraft X continued inbound and landed without further incident.  I lost track of the point out because when I took it I was working planes while trying to fix my COVID-19 mask.  When I decided to just work without one; I had moved on to other traffic and forgot to tag up the point out.The fact that I was more concerned with getting in trouble for not having my mask on rather than doing a completely efficient job is the FAA's fault.  The FAA is making us fear the repercussions of the mask mandate; so much its eroding the safety of the NAS...DO NOT REQUIRE MASK TO BE WORN ON POSITION IF WE ARE ALREADY SIX FEET APART; which we are based on the distance of scope to scope. Whether its distracting; [probably] muffles transmissions; the mask mandate on position is depleting the safety of the NAS.
column123: I was working LC. Runway XX was the active Runway; advertising RNAVs.Aircraft X; checked on Tower frequency on an 11 mile final at an altitude of 1;700 feet.  I noticed a target moving east to west at a high rate of speed (240 knots) and was not tagged up.  I called traffic to Aircraft X at his 2 o'clock 3 to 4 miles moving westbound at 1;500 feet.  Aircraft X said he had it on his TCAS and he was looking for traffic.  I continued to watch the westbound traffic realizing it was going to potentially become a conflict for Aircraft X and continued to call traffic to them.  I advised Aircraft X to try and maintain 1;700 feet based on the fact the westbound traffic appeared to be holding 1;500 and Aircraft X was on an instrument approach.  I also told Aircraft X it appeared the traffic was going to pass underneath. Aircraft X reported the traffic in sight right as my Ground Control and I realized the target was making a hard 180 degree turn back to the east while climbing up into Aircraft X's flight path and altitude. I responded back to Aircraft X to maneuver at his discretion to avoid the aircraft. The target continued eastbound.  Aircraft X landed without incident.  I asked Aircraft X if he would give me a call so that I may ask questions.  I told him he was not in trouble; he obliged and I gave him the tower number.  I then attempted to call the Supervisor from our communications counsel to ask what was going on with the target to no avail; the Supervisor did not pick up. Once on break I was able to speak with the pilot: The Pilot said it was a [description].  He caught a glimpse of it while it passed underneath then Aircraft Y did the 180 degree turn back east and passed right off the nose of his aircraft at his altitude.  The pilot also mentioned that his passengers had asked about the aircraft; leading me to believe in fact Aircraft Y was extremely close and causing this to be a significant event.  The pilot said he was also going to file a report.I took the information down to my Supervisor.  My Supervisor called the ZZZ [TRACON] Supervisor who said he didn't know anything of the event and that he was too busy to look into at the moment.  Seeing as how Aircraft Y came from ZZZ's TRACON airspace; my Supervisor gave them a call to see if they knew anything about Aircraft Y and what it was doing.  ZZZ4 said they were talking to Aircraft Y and had coordinated with ZZZ with a point out; ZZZ accepted the point out on Aircraft Y.  There was never any coordination from ZZZ to myself on LC in reference to Aircraft Y.Note: I did not issue a Traffic Alert because I had no clue what Aircraft Y was doing; I was focused on calling the traffic from the beginning;  and with the 180 degree high speed turn I felt like giving the pilot discretion to maneuver as needed (since he had him in sight then) was the best course of action for the pilot to be able to keep his passengers alive.Note:  Aircraft Y was never tagged up on the scope.  They appeared to be VFR but I'm really unsure if they were.Events like this in the ZZZ [TRACON] airspace and ZZZ [Tower] environment in my opinion are a consistent problem.1.  ATC 101: coordination and positive control. ZZZ1 [TRACON] coordinated with ZZZ [TRACON] as a point out.  ZZZ [TRACON] accepting the point out.  In all reality both facilities should be monitoring what Aircraft Y was doing.  The original point out coordination was that Aircraft Y was going 6 miles west of the fix ZZZZZ.  Once Aircraft Y passed 6 miles there were 2 possible controllers to notice and do something about it.  ZZZ [TRACON] should have questioned ZZZ1 [TRACON] and myself reference Aircraft X; ZZZ1 [TRACON] should have reached out to Aircraft Y and ZZZ [TRACON].  Because no one did Aircraft Y was 15 miles west of ZZZZZ and crossing through the final of Runway XX at ZZZ.2. Take responsibility when you have accepted a point out by keeping an eye on the target as well as looking at your current traffic and what may be aconflict between the point out and current traffic. Do NOT lose track of your traffic and or point outs (which was the excuse of the ZZZ [TRACON] Controller). If you're busy do not accept the point out potentially causing yourself to 'lose track' of traffic.3.  CARE! This job is not a game.  There is not time to not care when human lives are at stake.For my side:1.  I have re-played this 1000 times over in my head.  I think maybe I could have called a traffic alert but what I can't see is what I would have suggested to Aircraft X.  I called the traffic early and continued too; once he got it I felt like the pilot knew how to fly that plane better than me; and could see what Aircraft Y was doing better than me as well.  If I had given him a suggestion based on how erratic Aircraft Y was I truly think it would have ended badly.   The pilot's RA never went off; and speaking to the pilot he wasn't sure why but he said it probably had no idea what to do based on what that Aircraft Y was doing.2.  Suggestion; call the pilot if able or the airport and question said pilot.  Potentially file a deviation or ask what was going on.3.Stop the 'holes in the swiss cheese'.  This was always my favorite CBI [Computer Based Instruction].  In order to not have the holes line up you have to be vigilant always.  I try and will continue to try. Maybe I could have even been more vigilant today?  Saw it coming earlier?4.  Encourage pilots to file reports so this information can be turned into learning experiences.Thank you for reading some recommendations; and there are probably 100 more things that could be done.  This event took a toll on myself the rest of my shift.  Some things you just can't un-see; but for myself it makes me better and care even more for our pilots in the sky and their passengers.
column125: TRACON Controller reported being distracted by attempting to put on a mask and did not issue important traffic to an air carrier on final. Tower Controller reported issuing traffic to an arrival about an unknown aircraft that was pointed out to the TRACON; but the TRACON Controller never advised the Tower Controller.

column121: Flying a steeper than usual glide path to a runway with displaced threshold. During transition to visual I inadvertently descended below the glide slope to achieve a normal sight picture. Received a Ground Proximity Warning and Glide Slope. Too much focus outside the aircraft. We were over the touchdown zone and a normal landing was completed. Attention inside and outside the airplane during transition to landing flare.
column123: We were in the flare over the runway and received the 'Glideslope' EGPWS advisory immediately prior to touchdown. Aural Glideslope advisory while we were in the landing hover/flare. The First Officer hand flew the Visual Approach and maintained the Glideslope until we were approaching the threshold and he shifted his attention to the external environment. The Glideslope and PAPI (Precision Approach Path Indicator) for this runway may have been the cause when he transitioned to flying by outside reference and attempting to achieve a sight picture closer to our typical 3.00 degree glide path. We were over the touchdown zone and completed a very normal and uneventful landing.

column121: We were level 5;000 feet 250 knots approaching ZZZ VOR on the ZZZZZ2 Star. Approach gave a heading 280 and slow to 180 and asked if we had the field in sight.  We were heading 330 and I was thinking a turn to 350 was coming so 280 heading caught me a little off guard. I (FO) was pilot flying. The sun was just above the horizon as I looked west and across the cockpit for the field.  Simultaneously I was looking cross-cockpit and programing the speed and heading change into the FMC. I could make out the field so I said to the Captain (PM); 'I can see the field; you can call it in sight.'  I noticed another aircraft on TCAS at our altitude on TCAS and then saw them visually less than ten miles away; also headed west toward ZZZ [Airport]; I noted the  paint scheme and relayed to the PM all that information and that they were probably headed to the north side of the airport.  In my head I was thinking that our airplane was not slowing down as fast as I would like when the PM said 'where is that airplane; I think we need to turn.' I pushed the localizer button on the FMC but the airplane did not turn so I turned the autopilot off and manually turned to intercept final for [Runway] XXR. As I did; we received at TCAS TA then a few seconds later a TCAS RA to climb.  The ADI showed a red boxed area from level flight down (I was descending) so I just leveled off until we heard 'clear of conflict.'   Then Approach gave us a 180 heading; paused; and then said 'you guys can't do that;' and switched us to Tower.  I was able to correct the high energy level of the airplane using gear; flaps; and speed brakes and continued the approach and landed uneventfully.Fast close to the airfield; sun in eyes; failure on my part to use automation.Something from ATC that I was going to turn towards the runway instead of downwind in the [Runway] XXR pattern or an advisory where we were in the sequence.  My recency of experience led to not arming the localizer; while I did brief that at the start of the first leg I could have reemphasized it during the approach brief.

column121: Arriving in gusty crosswind conditions. VMC. I was hand flying the ILS. At runway threshold; I inadvertently descended below the glide slope during transition to outside and received a glide slope warning. As we were in the flare over the runway landing was the safest option. [Identified by] GPWS warning; glide slope. Began transition to outside too early preoccupation with winds. Failed to continue cross checking indications. I heard the annunciation in the landing flare and completed a normal landing. The flight director indications guide the airplane to the touchdown zone use this information in combination with outside.

column121: Intended to land flaps 30 in windy conditions with add speed of 14 knots.  Visual approach on Visual Approach Slope indicator and slightly below ILS glide slope. We received a Ground Proximity Warning alert for 'too low terrain'. Rechecked landing gear was down and landed. We realized on very short final that flaps were actually set to 25 instead of 30. We did not select flaps to 30 since we were so low. With the extra speed we had added for the gusty winds; I felt we were safer to leave them set at 25 and land.
column123: While on final approach  we experienced a Ground Proximity Warning 'Too Low Terrain' aural warnings. Knowing that we were on glide slope and Precision Approach Path Indicator and the runway was in sight in VFR conditions; we decided to continue the landing. We ensured that the landing gear was down and that we were free of terrain; however; on short final the Captain noticed we were at flaps 25 instead of 30. It was decided to safely complete the landing since we were stable on speed and path. We already had built in a 14 knot wind additive for the windy conditions. The entire approach was stable including at the 500 foot 'Stable' call out point. I take responsibility for misreading the flap gauge; and will certainly give it more focus in the future. One potential factor; not an excuse; is there was the distraction of balloons going by our aircraft around the time of the landing checklist. I reported the balloons to Tampa Tower.

column121: Private pilot; instrument student flying ZZZ direct to ZZZ1. Flew the RNAV Runway XX; circling approach to Runway XY for circling practice as well as winds were favorable to Runway XY. Before the Final approach Fix we performed our pre-landing checklist which included gear selector down. Gear selector switch came down at the Final approach fix; and showed 3 green lights indicating gear is down and locked. Student and instructor confirmed 3 green lights indicating gear down.  Landing was very smooth; lightly touched down and began sliding.  We were unaware that the gear was not down and locked.  There was no impact or sparks.  The plane remained on the runway; veered slightly to the left due to the left gear collapsing more than the right.
column123: I was with my instrument student who was flying ZZZ direct to ZZZ1. Upon completion of the descent checklist; we confirmed we would fly the RNAV XX approach and circle to land Runway XY as winds were favoring XY. Prior to the Final Approach Fix; we performed the pre-landing checklist. Upon arrival at the Final Approach Fix; the landing gear selector was set to the down position. Shortly after putting the gear down; both my student and I confirmed 3 green lights indicating the gear was down and locked. We later started our circle to Runway XY and performed the landing checklist and confirmed landing gear indicated down and locked. Shortly after flaring; the airplane touched down then we began sliding to the left of the runway. Once realizing the propeller stopped spinning and was bent; we promptly performed shutdown procedures and exited the aircraft. Upon exiting the aircraft; the right main was partially extended while the nose gear and left main appeared mostly collapsed. Neither myself nor my student experienced any injury.

column121: Returning to 7B3 (Hampton Airfield) late at night; turned on the runway lights; and landed in-between them. However; the lights border the grass runway rather than the asphalt runway that I meant to land on. As a result; I landed on the grass runway; when I expected to land on asphalt. This was potentially dangerous; if the grass runway was blocked with snow (it was not; fortunately). The grass runway was not operational at the time (NOTAM to that effect).No adverse effect happened; but it was not ideal.It's not clear that in order to land on the asphalt; I'd have to land OUTSIDE the two sets of runway lights which border the turf runway. Adding a third row of runway lights (on the other side of the asphalt runway) would make it much clearer for a night landing.

column121: I was practicing air taxiing and quick stops with a 31-hour student pilot on a taxiway. During the recovery phase of the quick stop; the student did not raise enough collective to arrest the descent. I took controls at around 30 feet AGL and increased the collective to our 5-minute limit; but we continued to descend and impacted the ground hard enough to spread the skids. A piece of non-structural sheet metal detached from the underside of the helicopter and landed beside the taxiway. I reported what happened to the tower and they sent someone to retrieve the sheet metal. I flew the helicopter back to the flight school's ramp and did not observe any damage other than the spread skids. I should have taken the controls earlier and not allowed the descent rate to develop to an unrecoverable state. I was trying to talk the student through the correct control inputs when I should have just abandoned the maneuver and recovered.

column121: Upon second lap around the pattern on ZZZ; I heard Aircraft Y calling 5 miles away with intend to join pattern behind downwind traffic in sight. That radio call came right came in between my Student Pilot call from crosswind to downwind. And there was another aircraft that was just passing the numbers. I made a second left downwind RWY XX call in case my Student Pilot's position call wasn't heard.I was passing mid-runway point and couldn't see the airplane that was joining pattern. Aircraft Y reported 45 degree entry ; left downwind [Runway] XX behind downwind traffic. After I passed the abeam point; I instructed Student Pilot to not turn base nor descend until ahead traffic passed our wing; in which we had momentarily lost sight as they were doing a very wide pattern. At that moment; Aircraft Y and I were about to collide. Aircraft Y was positioned/ entering traffic on left base instead of downwind; behind us; as they called previously. I took controls from Student Pilot executing an evasive maneuver; steep left turn from base to final; while trying to not collide with aircraft on final that had just passed us. Aircraft Y made a turn to their right. No one was hurt nor lost aircraft control.

column121: Another aircraft was cleared through ZZZ airspace en route to ZZZ1 and was approved to do so via ZZZ Tower; which indicated that it did not have a visual on that aircraft. ZZZ does not have radar.I was cleared for takeoff on ZZZ Runway XX around the same time. Tower gave a frequency change to the other aircraft approximately one minute later and advised them of the aircraft taking off (me). The other aircraft responded that they were looking for traffic. Tower gave me a frequency change one to two minutes after that and advised me of the other aircraft.I was unable to spot the aircraft; but saw them on my traffic system and used the visual display and audio traffic alerts to avoid the other aircraft. I kept searching for the plane; climbing away from their flight path during this time; but even so; the traffic system indicated that we came within 100 vertical feet of each other.I believe that ZZZ Tower should have visually identified the transiting aircraft prior to clearing me for takeoff; or required me to identify the other aircraft prior to clearing me for takeoff. Both aircraft were in ZZZ airspace at the time of the event; and yet I am fairly certain that the controller did not have a visual on either of us (but certainly not on the other aircraft).If in this situation in the future; I will delay my takeoff until I have identified any aircraft that could become a hazard during my takeoff and climb-out. In addition; I will execute a turn away from the other aircraft; even when in controlled airspace.

column121: I was pilot in command of a tail wheel airplane. The purpose of the flight was to maintain tail wheel proficiency. I was seated in the forward pilot station with a passenger seated in the aft pilot station. We departed to proceed to ZZZ with the intention of performing upper airwork maneuvers enroute to a landing at ZZZ to exchange pilot stations. Winds at takeoff were reported SW at 6 knots.On our arrival at ZZZ the automated weather was reporting winds as SW 8G16. The main Runway XX/XY [at] ZZZ is closed for resurfacing and the taxiway 'A' is marked as the temporary runway. The midfield windsock indicated the winds were generally SW 8G16 with some intermittent change to W and continuing intermittent gusts. On final approach to Runway XX the apparent winds were significantly more westerly. The aircraft touched down on the right main gear first. I used forward elevator to keep the horizontal stabilizer and rudder in an airflow that kept them aerodynamically effective. As the left main gear touched down I applied additional right aileron to arrest sideway drift and left pedal to maintain runway longitudinal alignment.  As the tail wheel was about to touchdown the rudder became ineffective for maintaining runway alignment I applied left braking action but was unable to maintain runway heading and the nose of the aircraft yawed right and the aircraft exited the runway. As the aircraft weathervaned further nose right it slid sideways a short distance and the left wing tip contacted the ground. The aircraft was partially on the runway. Both tires were inflated and there was no apparent damage to the left wing. I elected to apply power and maneuver the aircraft clear of the runway and taxi into the ramp area of ZZZ to secure and inspect the aircraft.

column121: We conducted the ZZZZZ4 arrival and joined the ILS XX approach from ZZZZZ [Intersection] as briefed. When the Captain contacted the Tower; the Controller cleared us to land XX Right. The Captain read back the clearance. I said; 'Can you confirm Controller wants us to land on the right? We're on the ILS XX Left.' The Captain confirmed with the Controller cleared to land on the right. The Controller responded 'Affirmative. XX Right; Cleared to land.' At the time; we were well above 1;000 feet in visual conditions with the runway in sight and; so I had no objection. I clicked off the autopilot and side-stepped to XX Right and we landed. No issues arose with ATC or any other traffic. The next day; the Captain reached out to me and we spoke over the phone. Captain learned that we had actually been cleared for the ILS XX Right approach and Captain intended to file a report. A couple of days later I was finally able to confirm that this was correct. We aren't able to determine if it was being read back correctly or incorrectly; but there was never a clarification type of response from ZZZ Approach. It's also important to bring up that the Captain was wearing a face mask throughout the flight operations which did contribute to difficulties in communication with myself and with ATC at various times during the trip. This likely was a contributing factor to our deviation. Another contributing factor was the violation of sterile cockpit below 18;000 feet by the Captain and myself. Personally; I was distracted by our conversation as well as the fact that Captain was wearing a mask during the flight.Complacency is also a factor as the ZZZZZ4 Arrival usually results in the ILS or visual to XX Left.I think our company needs to take a harder stance on mask-wearing during flight operations in the flight deck.  I recommend that the company forbids the use of masks by pilots during flight operations to ensure that communications are clear and effective. In this case; it can be assumed that the Captain was repeating the clearance saying 'XX Left' but the Controller did not hear it clearly and assumed Captain was saying XX Right. Therefore; Captain was never corrected and we continued with what we had expected in our heads. There were multiple points throughout our trip that ATC did not understand the Captain and asked who was calling or needed clarification on what Captain was saying because it was difficult to understand with the mask. I thought on several occasions throughout our trip that it was the Captain's choice to wear it but if we have an emergency situation that I really hope Captain would take it off so that effective communication could be established and maintained. An emergency situation did not occur; but clearly an issue did arise with our approach to our destination.
column123: ZZZ had a broken layer at 1;700 feet and we were expecting to do an approach. Descending into ZZZ on the ZZZZZ4 arrival we had set up for the ILS [Runway] XXL. We were assigned to join the ILS [Runway] XXR at ZZZZZ [Intersection] and then later cleared for the ILS XXR approach. We did not fly the ILS XXR. We flew the XXL approach and when we were on with Tower; we told them we were inbound on the ILS XXL. Tower cleared us to land XXR. By the time we contacted Tower we were out of the clouds and could see the runway. We verified two times that we were cleared to land XXR and not XXL. And Tower confirmed that we were to land XXR. Since we had visual on the runway; we side stepped and landed on XXR. We were probably at 1;500-1;600 feet AFE when we side stepped. We were confused as to why they had changed runways on us; but we knew that XXR was longer than XXL and that the landing speeds were the same and we were familiar with the airport. As we side stepped; I programmed in the ILS XXR so that we had a backup source even though we visually had the airport in sight. Later in the evening it still didn't sit right with me that we had a runway change that late; especially since we had been on an IFR approach. I realized we had in fact been assigned ILS XXR all the way back on with Approach. They never corrected me. So; I either read it back correctly but we were really hearing what we wanted to hear (ILS XXL) or they never corrected me and we all missed it. The approach was stable all the way through for XXL and the sidestep was done in a safe manner.I attribute not being sterile in the descent as a factor even though we were assigned to join ILS XXR at ZZZZZ prior to FL180. I would say complacency and expectancy were the primary factors. XXL wasn't closed and we were set that we would be doing the ILS XXL. It was also the last leg on the last day of our trip and we had had a long day. I had been up since 8 or 9am and we had flown 4 legs. This flight landed around XApm.I can say that I once again am reminded how serious our jobs are and that miss hearing a simple 'L' vs 'R' can be life changing. I can only say that I am thankful that this took place during a low traffic period and that we were able to maintain a safe condition. I'm honestly not too sure how to avoid this situation. I believe that writing down my clearances would assist in allowing me to verify and that maybe I need to make sure to turn my headset up so that I won't miss ATC.
column125: Air Carrier flight crew reported a track heading deviation during approach due to communication issues with ATC.  The First Officer cited the Captain wearing a face mask on the flight deck may have contributed to the communication problems with ATC and between them.

column121: After completing a fuel stop at PEO; I taxied from the fuel pump near the FBO to Runway 28 for departure. Prior to taxiing I completed my pre-taxi checks and self announced my taxi on CTAF. The wind was 220@17G23 and I wanted to complete my run up into the wind. Runway 28/10 doesn't have a parallel taxiway to the East requiring a back taxi for approximately the half the runway length. The runway; departure and arrival corridor appeared clear and I announced taking the runway for back taxi with no other traffic heard. I completed a short run up; scanned the runway and announced on CTAF I was taking off Runway 28. I applied power; checked instruments and accelerated down the runway. Shortly I noted something in the distance down the runway; a second later noticed engines rise above the crest of the the runway and I immediately aborted takeoff. I was close to rotation speed; pulled power back and aggressively braked. Once at a safe speed I announced I was aborting and vacating the runway. I was close the the north/south taxi and uneventfully exited the runway at that point. At that time I saw a Falcon jet exit runway 10/28 via the next taxiway about 800 feet down the runway heading to the fuel pumps. I turned my aircraft around announced my back taxi and departure. Concerned that I had an equipment issue or was on the wrong frequency I asked for a radio check while I was departing downwind and received a good check. Due to a significant crown/slope in the runway it is not possible to see the opposite ends of the Runway 28/10. The other aircraft operator should follow the best practices identified in AC 90-66b par 10.1 self-announce their intentions and monitor the frequency for other traffic as well. Additionally; the aircraft was taxiing from the west hangars to the fuel pumps and if there's sufficient wingtip clearance they should use the parallel taxiway instead of the runway as an increased measure of safety.

column121: I was wrapping up a long training flight with my instructor and we were practicing approaches and landings before wraping up for the day. On the third landing approach there was a Cessna 150 ahead of us in the pattern.  I extended the downwind to make room and turned base and final much further out than typical. One of the things we had been practicing is making sure to be low enough on the approach to not overshoot since it is such a short runway.  On this approach I was quite low and setup a little low on final when I realized that while I had seen the C150 land I wasn't certain it had cleared the runway. I thought it might still be on there and asked my instructor if we should go around. He said no and to go ahead and land since we didn't see the other aircraft on the runway anymore and we would be landing very short. The previous loop around the pattern I initiated a go around because it didn't feel right but was assured that we were setup just right for landing and should've landed.  I think that I was reluctant to do another go around 'for no reason' if I didn't need to according to the instructor.Shortly after landing and rolling out several feet and up the incline in the runway we saw the other airplane at the far end. At just about the same time we heard frantic calls for the other airplane to get off the runway as soon as possible.  I was setup for a short field landing and exited the runway after a very short landing roll about 1;700 feet from where the other aircraft had exited the runway.  We were shaken from the realization that we had shared the runway and scared the other training aircraft.Contributing factors:1. Fatigue- This was one of the first training sessions after a 4 month break and my instructor and I were exhausted.2. Human Factors - I deferred to my instructors experience and decided against the go around.  We have since discussed this issue and agreed that if either of us feel a go around is a good idea we will go around.3. Airport -  The runway X landing zone is at a significantly lower elevation than the XX end of the runway. because of the geography is difficult to see aircraft at the other end.

column121: Uneventful flight to ZZZ. On final ILS XXR at ZZZ; below 2;500 feet; inside ZZZZZ but prior to ZZZZZ1; LOC and GS Captainured; flaps 20 set; Captain called for leg gear down and disconnected AP and A/T. At this moment; the L HYD SYS PRESS Master Caution Lt illuminated. At first I thought the light was associated with the AP and A/T being disconnected. It took a couple seconds to recognize that it was the HYD pressure light. At this time also we were checking in with  XX_1 r outside ZZZZZ1 and were cleared to land. Very shortly after I accepted the leg clearance; the Captain alerted to Go Around and made the Go Around call to Tower. We accomplished the normal go around procedure and shortly received a LDG GEAR DISAGREE caution as the gear did not retract due to the initial emergency. We repositioned the gear handle to the down position and that caution extinguished. After reaching 3;000 feet and completing the GA procedures; the Captain continued to fly the AC and I accessed the Non Normal procedure through the FM on my EFB. The HYD section has a large index and initially I inadvertently selected the HYD SYS PRESS L and C non-normal and after the first two steps realized we were in the wrong checklist and switched to the HYD SYS PRESS L (only) Checklist. I ran the checklist aloud as the Captain continued to fly. Step 7 instructed not to use auto brakes. When completing the deferred items; the flaps and gear were already in the proper configuration and due to the post Go Around altitude and box pattern vectors; though not unduly rushed; I inadvertently failed to re-run the descent checklist as part of the deferred items; missing the last reminder to not use autobrakes. We did run the deferred landing checklist; and the Captain flew the approach to landing. After touchdown the auto brakes engaged firmly and the Captain immediately moved the speed brake lever slightly fwd to disengage the autobrakes and proceeded to manually brake the AC along with use of the RT engine reverser. We slowed to a gentle stop with approximately 1;500 feet remaining on Runway XXR. Due to the lack of runway steering we remained on the runway; coordinating with ground personnel; and monitored brake temps. After an initial external check by ground personnel; we secured the engines; remaining on APU power for the remainder of the ground ops evolution and tug to our gate.
column123: Communicating with ZZZ approach on the ILS XXR to ZZZ between ZZZZZ and ZZZZ1; with the flaps at 20 and after lowering the landing gear; we received an EICAS message L HYD SYS PRESS. I was Pilot Flying; hand flying with the A/P and A/T off. The First Officer was Pilot Monitoring. At ZZZZZ1 we switched to ZZZ Tower and informed them we would abandon the approach. We were given a northerly heading and a climb to 3;000 [feet]. I decided to abandon the approach using V/S mode as we were at approximately 1;600 feet; and to reduce my workload; engaged the right autopilot. I inadvertently called for 'Gear Up' which did not occur due to the loss of the left system. We realized this error when a 'GEAR DISAGREE' EICAS message appeared and returned the gear handle to the down position. The gear remained down from the prior approach through the eventual landing.I stated the I had the airplane and the radios and directed the First Officer to run the L HYD SYS PRESS non-normal checklist.We also pulled up the status page and noted the loss of quantity from the left system; less than .2 with RF showing.We initially started the L and C HYD SYS PRESS checklist but caught the error when the checklist called for an action regarding the center system.We then proceeded to run the L HYD SYS PRESS checklist; but made an error; missing the 'do not use autobrakes' step. I would eventually select Autobrakes 4 for landing.ATC asked me at this point if I needed the equipment standing by and I declined.Upon completion of the checklist up to deferred items; I [advised] with ATC.ATC asked us which runway we would like; and I inquired about [Runway] XYL. ATC said it would be available as a visual approach. I decided to re-use the ILS to XXR as we were set up for it already; would benefit from the precision guidance at night; and it had an approach light system that [Runway] XYL did not. The length of [Runway] XXR provided our required landing distance; with margin; plus around another 4;000 feet.I sent a brief ACARS message to Dispatch stating I had [advised with ATC] and would be landing in ZZZ. I should have included the nature of the [event] and our need of a tow in the body of the message. Despite being somewhat concerned about the time available as I was stuck in a high drag configuration; I still had enough time to send a more complete message and should have done so- it would have been a few more keystrokes. I also had enough time to re-run the non-normal checklist. Doing so would have caught the autobrake restriction. The First Officer and I discussed this in our debrief.I then briefed the purser using the TEST acronym; advising that we would NOT need to prep the cabin for evac.I do not recall asking for the equipment to be standing by; other than stating we would need to be towed off the runway. The equipment was in fact standing by for us at landing and detected hydraulic fluid that contaminated the runway.ATC had provided a modified box vector for us; and as we were approaching base I exchanged roles with the First Officer and assumed aircraft control as Pilot Flying. I called for the deferred items. Another opportunity exists in the deferred items with a descent checklist step stating the autobrakes should be off. In our debrief; we discussed that the were a number of items in the deferred items checklist that were not applicable to our situation such as alternate gear and flap extension. This may have led to the error and missing the descent checklist step. We committed to running future checklists step-by-step.The flaps 20 approach was uneventful; and I used the autopilot and autothrottle until short final. Approach had given us a discrete frequency for ZZZ Tower and the rescue equipment. At landing; I felt a sensation similar to anti-skid activation and immediately disengaged the autobrakes by slightly advancing the speed brake lever; and came to a stop using the operable reverser and manual braking.ZZZ Fire and Rescue assessed our aircraft and we set up for a tow-in. ZZZ Fire and Rescue noted hydraulic fluid contaminated the runway.We proceeded under tow to gate. At the gate; I discussed the event with Dispatch and the [Chief Pilot].In my debrief with the First Officer; we went through the debrief card. The items that seemed to go well included workload management; automation management; and our planning and decision making. The humbling items that could have gone better would include my monitoring and cross check of the items on the non-normal checklist. In a future event; with time available; one of us will independently re-run the non-normal checklist before committing to the approach. Calling for the gear up as we abandoned the approach was a rote response. With a hydraulic issue on arrival in the terminal area; next time we will maintain the existing configuration unless performance reasons dictate otherwise.Communication with Dispatch should have been more thorough; next time I will spend an extra two minutes to send a more informative message to Dispatch.

column121: We were 3rd in line for takeoff at ZZZ and it was particularly busy. There was a lot of rotary traffic present in the right pattern as I believe there was a helicopter event present. We had been having breakfast at the cafe and witnessed 4-5 Cessna 172's doing power off approach so they were in the left pattern. When we were holding short of [Runway] XX @ [Taxiway] A we could hear the Controller on tower was busy and we waited for around 10 minutes. In that time; myself and the PIC who was left seat agreed that it was standard traffic pattern; I checked the sectional to confirm. We made a comment that perhaps rotary traffic was using the right pattern for separation. We got called by Tower and the Controller thanked us for our patience and said Runway XX clear for takeoff. We proceeded to takeoff and climbed in the upwind to around 650 feet before making a turn to the left. Whilst we were in the crosswind segment; still climbing we noticed a plane that appeared to be 200-300 feet below us in right downwind for Runway XY off to our left; on its way to pass underneath us. It was probably 600 feet away when we first noticed it and it passed underneath our nose. We were both puzzled as to what they were doing. Once we were clear of them; we made another left turn into left downwind for XX the Controller came on the radio and said to us that we had come very close to 'my' ILS traffic for ZZZ1 and that they had it written down that we had requested right traffic; which was incorrect. Our inactions were verifying left or right traffic; assuming left traffic was standard especially due to all the rotary traffic using the right downwind for XX. The Controller was particularly busy and perhaps as we were heading to ZZZ1 they figured it was more direct to make right traffic. We climbed to 200 feet of TPA [Traffic Pattern Altitude] on the upwind so to make sure we were as close to TPA before being in Downwind at the wrong altitude.
column123: We were cleared for takeoff for Runway XX.  Prior to takeoff; I was monitoring entry of all aircraft entering the traffic pattern.  Helicopters were on right downwind Runway XX; while fix-winged were entering left downwind Runway XX.  I never communicated which way I was going to turn crosswind since I was following the other aircraft that have been in the pattern throughout the morning.  After departure; I climbed to about 700 feet and started to monitor other traffic in the area prior to making my crosswind turn.  I saw a small aircraft to the left of me that was descending about 200-300 feet below.  I then started to make a left crosswind turn from XX0 degrees to approximately YY0 degrees.  I flew over the traffic and continued heading south for ZZZ2.  ZZZ Tower Controller then addressed me on the radio that they had me down for a right downwind turn.  I never once communicated I was making a right downwind turn nor heard the Controller mention; 'make right crosswind upon departure'.  If I wasn't supposed to make a left crosswind because of other traffic on an ILS for ZZZ1; why didn't the Controller mention that to me prior to departure?  I would have gladly complied and made a right crosswind turn and continued my course to ZZZ2.

column121: While on the visual approach into HOU on the ILS 4. The FO (First Officer) was Pilot Flying and while he was flying the approach I noticed what I thought were two birds just to the right of the aircraft at around 9 o'clock but outside the course. I made the FO aware of this and continued to keep scanning for more birds in the area since the first one I saw was well clear of our course. However at 2;300 ft. and 0.5NM past ELREN on the ILS 4 the two objects were actually drones that past off our right hand side that were the size of two big birds. I noticed the two were indeed drones because I saw the propellers on two of them and they had a shiny black finish on them. I also checked the TCAS to see if they were there; but it didn't show them on the screen. I relayed this information to HOU tower and told them what altitude and where they were. Upon landing and taxing off the runway and contacting ground; I was given a phone number to call the tower to give them more information on the description of the drones; and where I spotted them on the approach.My suggestion is to always keep scanning the area for any hazards while flying even if the TCAS system doesn't show anything in the area.

column121: BWI Approach Control was vectoring us for a visual to RWY 10 and keeping us at 3;000 ft due to traffic below us at 2;000 ft.  Approach turned us in on a short approach between COLUM and JEANS fix to Runway 10 and cleared us to 2;000 ft.  On right base we were asked if field was in sight and we acknowledged it was and we were cleared for a visual to RWY 10 and contact tower.  Crossing altitude at JEANS is 1;500 ft so pilot flying selected 1;500 to catch the glide path.  At 1;800 ft tower said they were getting a low altitude alert and cleared to land.  Pilot flying disconnected the autopilot and leveled which was just about on glide path 6.4 miles from runway. We were never below the 1;500 ft min altitude inside of COLUM and joined the glide slope normally and landed.  The approach was stable at all times.Possible short approach clearance by ATC.Better vectors by ATC or requesting extended downwind.  Possibly alert was issued due to leaving us high and us having to increase rate of descent.
column123: On arrival to BWI; the Approach Controller kept us at 3;000 ft due to a Cessna at 2;000 ft. As we were turned toward the final approach segment between COLUM and JEANS on the approach; the controller gave us 2;000 ft and asked if we had the field at 1;230 and 8 miles.  We confirmed the airport in sight and we were cleared for the visual approach and told to contact Tower. Crossing alt at JEANS is 1;500 ft. We started to descend to 1;500 as we were rolling into the final at 1;800 ft in the descent the tower called a low alt alert.  We leveled the aircraft at 1;800 ft and 6.4 miles from the runway. GS was captured and normal landing accomplished no other coms about low alt alert.We were cleared for a visual approach and were descending to the Altitude at the Marker. Apparently our rate of descent may have triggered the low alt alert.  The approach controller could have extended us further out in order to give more time to descend at a slower rate since they had held us up high so long.

column121: We were on the TIPTOE Visual Runway 28L. We were paired up with a heavy that was on [Runway] 28R. Due to the gusty winds from the north we ended flying on their wake which caused our plane to roll; creating an upset. We followed upset recovery procedures and we aborted landing and went around. Factors are gusty winds from the north and also night time. Being paired with a heavy caused the winds to push the heavy airplane wake in our flight path. During strong winds from the north; they need to provide greater spacing between planes to help mitigate wake.
column123: On final approach in SFO we were paired up with a heavy jet on the right side with very gusty cross winds from the north.  On 5 mile final we encountered wake from the heavy and it induced a roll upset. The recovery procedure was utilized and aircraft control was regained at which point we encountered the wake again and it induced a second roll upset. Proper upset recovery techniques were utilized and we went around without issue. The cause of this event was due to high gusty winds out of the north and being paired too closely with a heavy jet upwind. The resulting wake was blown directly into our flight path. Having known about the winds and the pairing of the heavy jet closely spaced upwind; I would suggest staying a minimum of 1 dot high on the glide slope and requesting a long landing so as to avoid potential wake in the future.

column121: I fly X type missions; for which I'm wearing a mask; which I knew harms the sealing of my active noise reduction headset (and as a result reduces the sound quality; bringing in some ambient noise). It was not until yesterday that I realized how disruptive this is.I was flying on the last leg of my IFR flight plan; from ZZZ to ZZZZZ (from there to my destination - ZZZ1). ATC called me and said something about heading 290 with ZZZZZ in it; I assumed they're telling me I need to fly 290 to ZZZZZ (and from there proceed as usual). That's what I did; ATC got (rightfully) upset and told me that I was asked to fly 290; which I then did and later cleared for a visual approach to Runway XX at ZZZ1.Listening to the recording on liveatc.net; I could clearly hear the ATC telling me to depart ZZZZZ at heading 290. That 'depart' is the one piece of the puzzle I didn't hear during the flight.On my way back - no passengers; no mask - all of a sudden ATC sounded much clearer. It made obvious to me this has to change; and I need to find a mask that does not interfere with my headset.

column121: I picked up Aircraft X from [Company] after annual inspection. The flight home was uneventful until reaching pattern altitude. Upon putting down the landing gear I noticed that I only had 2 green lights. The light for the right main gear was not illuminated. I informed Tower of the potential problem and asked for assistance is noting the status of the landing gear. Multiple aircraft; along with Tower; indicated that all landing gear were extended. I extended the flaps fully and continued a stabilized approach. The aircraft was allowed to gently touch down on the mains; at which point a felt the right main begin to give way. I began to slow the aircraft while the right wing tip made contact with the pavement. The drag from the wing pulled me to the right; and I eventually ended up coming to a stop in the grass to the right on Runway XX. I then shut the engine down and exited the aircraft. There was no evidence of any fuel leaks or other problems. Initial inspection of the plane revealed a mechanical failure of a pin that connects 2 portions of the landing gear system. The only known damage was to the right wing tip; right flap; and right side rear door step. A recovery company was contacted; and we moved the plane into my hangar for the night.

column121: Aircraft X checked on approximately a 6 mile final on the Visual Approach and I cleared him to land.  At approximately a 4-mile final Aircraft Y asks for an 'S turn to the North' to which I state 'Approved'.  About 30 seconds later; Aircraft X asks for another s-turn to the south to which I also state 'approved as requested' and inform him that there is no traffic in his vicinity and also issued the wind. Aircraft X is now on a 1.5 mile final and well east of the airport and asks 'Tower; Aircraft X we are still plenty high; can we do a left tun and a left 360 around?' I state 'Unable' due to traffic in trail and ask Aircraft X if he needs to go around. Aircraft X replies 'affirmative'.At this point Aircraft Y is approximately 2 miles well east of the airport and it appeared that they were turning toward the airport. Not wanting Aircraft Y to overshoot finals; I issue 'Aircraft Y; fly heading 195; climb and maintain 2;000'.  When Aircraft Y becomes established on the heading; I issue 'fly heading 210 and maintain 3;000'. I did this without coordinating with Approach first because things were rapidly developing as Aircraft Y was indicating 190 knots and they appeared to be headed toward terrain east of the airport.  I realize that this was a vector below the Minimum Vectoring Altitude (MVA). The MVA east of the airport is 2;400 feet but at this point; I wanted to get Aircraft Y away from terrain as quickly as possible without issuing any vague or confusing instructions to the pilot. I did coordinate with Approach with the 210 heading when I got the chance and it was approved.As I stated in my above narrative; I realize that the vector that I issued was below the MVA but from my perspective from the Tower; I needed to get Aircraft Y away from terrain as quickly as possible. This was a unique situation where I had to utilize my best judgment with the circumstances that were presented. I realize that I was breaking the rule of vectoring an aircraft below the MVA but what was the alternative at this point? Let Aircraft Y head toward terrain and possible result in a catastrophe? It was the best of the two bad choices that were presented to me at that point and I had to quickly make a judgment call.

column121: Very light 757; 16.7 on the fuel; almost empty payload; operating from ZZZ to ZZZ1; daytime VMC. High winds in effect. Vref was 111; Vapp is 126. I was PF (Pilot Flying); and decided to fly faster on final since we were in a light 757; and my limited experience with this situation led to this. Missed approach procedures were reviewed multiple times as other jets had to go around several times. Runway was dry; over XX;000 feet long; plenty of distance. I decided to fly a new Vapp of 135; as the jet was having problems controlling the airspeed since the winds were about 060/22G30. Our divert was ZZZ1; and we knew that several aircraft attempting to land had to execute missed approaches. All this into account; I decided to fly around 135 KIAS; as this felt like a more stable approach. The airspeed wouldn't stabilize safely below this number; as it was all over the place and I had to fight with the autothrottles to maintain within 15 kts of the initial Vapp of 126--it just wouldn't stabilize at a speed below 135. My reasoning is that since I was light; in a 757; long runway; daytime VMC; and super gusty winds; I had plenty of room to stop safely without encountering hot brakes or running out of concrete. We landed uneventfully; although long; as I touched down past the touchdown zone--this was the only cost of flying faster; and I felt it was worth it; all things considered. We landed and were able to taxi clear on taxiway; no problem.  If I was in a 767 or a heavier 757; I wouldn't have even thought of this; and we would have flown the Vapp given; for better or worse.  Were we technically unstable? Yes; I believe we were. Were we safer; in my eyes; flying a more stable approach with the winds we had? Yes; I also believe this to be the case. We never had a windshear warning; and we never felt unsafe; but looking back; I was in the wrong; even though I felt more stable; which led to a safer approach. However; the AOM procedures don't allow for this; so I was technically in the wrong; even though the airplane flew better and safer; in my opinion. Next time; I will fly the Vapp given; and if we have to divert; then so be it.

column121: While on visual approach to Runway XX and configuring; we had configured normally through gear down. The Captain called 'flaps 30' and I reached for the handle while looking at the speed. The trend indicator was well below 185 and I was too focused on the trend rather than the actual speed. I was too quick to move the flap handle. We were actually about 187 knots. We got the flap overspeed warning for about one to two seconds as our speed continued to dissipate. It was my first flight from furlough and recurrent training. I was allowing myself to rush; even though there was no need to rush at the time. I made an error of referencing the trend before the actual speed.  I also need to refresh my habit of not touching the flap handle until I have stated 'speed checks.'

column121: Arriving from ZZZ; myself and the Captain were preparing for an ILS to Runway 12 based on available weather at hand. Once handed off to Approach Control; we were instructed to expect the RNP 30. Given our close proximity to the airport; I scrambled quickly to load the FMS and review the changes. I failed to notice the available IAFs available and was instead hastily looking for one of the IFs in the FMS. Given our plan to conduct a visual approach and use the RNP for guidance; I elected to select 'no approach VIAs' and to add the additional fixes requested by ATC. As we progressed on the approach; we got multiple GPWS alerts while in VMC; including 'pull up.' The Captain discontinued the approach and elected for a go-around followed by the published hold to troubleshoot. We later completed an ILS to Runway 12; circle 30 without event. Thoroughly review approach plate and recognize the appropriate approach VIA option. If needing additional time; request delay vectors.

column121: On approach to landing at ORD Runway 9C at approximately 200 feet we received a GPWS (Ground Proximity Warning System) alert 'TOO LOW; TERRAIN.' The alert repeated itself several times until touchdown. It was day VMC; some light rain falling. We were on glide slope and in a safe position to land so we continued the approach and considered the alert erroneous. It's possible that since the runway is fairly new at ORD; that the GPWS database had not been updated. Perhaps a message to pilots operating into ORD should be made aware of possible erroneous GPWS alert associated with Runway 9C.

column121: Normal departure and flight enroute to ZZZ with thunderstorm approaching ZZZ airport on arrival from the west. Radar vectors for ILS XXL for approach; multiple speed adjustments from ATC on approach and no reports of wind gusts on initial arrival from ATC with winds 180/6 from ATIS. Storm had moved over field when on approach. On short final there was an RVR (Runway Visual Range) update from recall of greater than 5;000 RVR and moderate to heavy rain but no winds passed from recall. VMC around 1;100 feet and could see right and center runway but then went into torrential rain downpour around 400-500 feet on short final where Captain made an EFIS lights call and then I shortly after picked up runway lights and landing environment just prior to minimums and was a little high but was adjusting in close. Around 30-40 feet we had an instantaneous airspeed loss of around 10kts and at that time I had decided to go-around but with the airspeed loss and instantaneous loss of lift we touched down as go-around decision was made. We had a semi-hard landing and bounced and go-around selected all simultaneously. We were slow to put the speed brake back in after touching down but did eventually secure it and maintained attitude as we executed go around. Go-around procedures were completed airborne. Vectored back around by ATC for uneventful approach; landing and taxi in. The Captain and I both did a walk around of the aircraft post flight to ensure integrity of aircraft and check tail skid as a precaution but didn't notice the skid was ground down as we later were informed from the Director of Operations and Maintenance. We debriefed the event. Windshear/airspeed loss at low altitude while simultaneously executing go-around and didn't maintain proper go-around pitch once touching down as indicated from tail skid damage. Quicker reaction from crew with go-around with instantaneous airspeed loss and loss of lift and maintain proper attitude of aircraft on touchdown when executing go-around. Follow rules to live by; do not make large control inputs when in flare/go-around close to ground.

column121: While on approach to ZZZ we were cleared to descend to 3;000 feet at which time we had the runway in sight. Upon reaching 3;000 feet the controller gave us a low altitude alert and wanted to verify we were level at 3;600.  The PNF (Pilot Not Flying) responded that we were level at 3;000 and had the airport in sight. The controller advised he had only cleared us to descend to 3;600 not 3;000. We then called the airport in sight and he cleared us for the visual approach and we initiated a successful landing. After landing the Tower Manager contacted the flight crew via telephone at the FBO and advised there was a possible pilot deviation.
column123: We were in contact with ZZZ Approach. We were to expect radar vectors for the ILS XX. During the descent there were thunderstorms in the area. The approach controller instructed to descend to 3;000. As we leveled at 3;000 the controller ask to confirm our altitude. I informed the controller we were level at 3;000. He informed us we should have been at 3;600. We informed him we thought he had said 3;000. By this time we were clear of clouds and has the airport in sight. We climbed to 3;600 and received radar vectors as we were cleared for the approach.

column121: On vectors 6 SM northwest of TRI airport at 4500; ATC was vectoring us around weather for the approach. We were level; given a left turn from 270 to 090. On about a 180 heading; we got the GPWS 'caution terrain' followed by 'terrain terrain woop woop pull up' one time. I leveled the wings and executed the terrain escape maneuver. We climbed up to 5;300 feet from 4;500 feet. As soon as I started the escape maneuver; the indications went away. We notified ATC and flew the approach without disruption. This seemed like an erroneous activation as we were above what ATC said was the MVA. But; it was night; so not 100% sure.Either a false warning or a terrain clearance issue. It seemed that we were too high to get this in a level turn. ATC indicated we were 100 feet above their MVA. Responded and leveled the wings and executed the escape maneuver. Suggest to fly at or above MSA rather than the minimum vectoring altitude.
