column121: We made a UNICOM frequency call; then taxied out to Runway X; called ZZZ Center to get clearance to takeoff; then on takeoff roll; a private aircraft calls and says that they are landing on Runway YY; resulting in a sidestep and go-around by the private aircraft. While monitoring UNICOM frequency and ZZZ center; both me and the First Officer missed calls made on the UNICOM frequency about potential traffic in the area due to a short taxi; high workload calling and obtaining departure clearance; running checklists; and making UNICOM frequency calls.In order to prevent this in the future; I could have made a 'traffic report' call on the UNICOM frequency; asking if there was any traffic in the area and what runway they were using. I could have also taxied slower to allow more time to monitor the UNICOM frequency in-between checklists. In the future I will be more diligent and alert to any potential communications on a UNICOM frequency in all phases of flight at an untowered airport.

column121: We were approaching BZN; planning RNAV RNP 30 Approach. When we checked in with Big Sky Approach they assigned us the RNAV A Approach and cleared us direct SURPE. I typed it in manually and we proceeded direct. In my search in the FMS for that approach; it was not in the database. We had it in the Jeppessen FD Pro; but not in the database. Prior to reaching SURPE we became VFR; called the field and completed a visual approach. Having it in the Jeppessen charts and not in the database in the FMS is not cool. Leads one down a path to red. ATIS is not digital so our awareness of what approach is being used was late; late; late in the flight.  Brought us into the red. Into the red at an incredibly critical phase of flight is also not cool. Set up a liaison to make the airports like this aware of our fleet capabilities. Example: we have charts in the Jeppessen; but not in the FMS database so don't assign said approach.
column123: We were inbound to BZN VOR expecting vectors to the final portion of RNAV (RNP) Runway 30.  We were planning to request direct JAPER intersection for the balance of the approach.   The airport was reporting VFR conditions but we were still IMC in the descent.  We were handed off to Big Sky Approach about 20 DME from the airport.  Without given a chance to request JAPER we were directed to proceed to SURPE intersection on the RNAV (GPS)-A. That's when our situational awareness went red.   We accepted the approach as it was listed in our FD Pro.  I manually put SURPE in the legs page and descended to 10;000 feet as directed.  The First Officer tried to pull up the RNAV (GPS) - A in the FMC but it was not there.  Luckily we broke out into VMC approaching SURPE and completed a visual approach.I can think of three. First; the controllers need to be aware of which approaches they can and cannot assign.  Secondly; if an approach is published in FD Pro but not available in the FMC that discrepancy should be clearly spelled out. Finally; I made an assumption that if a procedure is in the FD Pro it would also be in the FMC.Big Sky needs to know which of their approaches are not in our FMC's.  This would help them immensely to plan for our arrivals.  We also need to complete an internal audit of all airports we serve to either remove or annotate in the  pages which FD Pro approaches are not found in the FMC.  I will now compare each approach in FD Pro to the FMC when arriving at a unfamiliar airport as I never want to be trapped in this situation again.

column121: I was conducting a training flight and was headed to N40. Approximately 4 miles south of the airport; I made a radio call on UNICOM announcing I was 4 miles south at 2;000 feet descending to pattern with intentions to join the left downwind Runway 25 using a standard 45 degree entry. As I continued to approach the airport; I was scanning in front; to the left; and to the right of me while listening to the radio for relevant traffic. When I was about to make my entry to join a left downwind; I made a radio call announcing that I was joining the downwind leg of Runway 25. Prior to turning; I made a quick scan to the left; center; and right. I did not see any traffic. As I started my turn; I scanned left again and saw a Piper on downwind approximately 200 feet horizontally to the left of my aircraft and 60 feet vertically beneath me. Simultaneously; I heard the Piper make a radio call announcing they were breaking off the downwind and saw the Piper turn away from my aircraft. I judged that the situation was under control so I continued my turn to join downwind and made a radio call to the Piper stating that I had him in sight and apologized for cutting him off in the pattern. The rest of the flight was uneventful. Contributing factors include: 1; Either a lack of an initial radio call from the Piper; or myself missing the radio call from the Piper. 2; I had made a radio call that I was making a 45 degree entry to join the left downwind; but mispositioned my aircraft and made a turn closer to 90 degrees instead. This positioning error may have led the Piper to scan the wrong area. 3; Too fast a scan to detect an aircraft against the backdrop of the ground 4; Busy UNICOM frequency with a lot of radio calls from multiple airports. Regarding human performance considerations; I made a poor judgment to continue to approach the airport directly from the south rather than change course to put the aircraft into a better position for a 45 degree entry to downwind. Additionally; as I was not aware of any radio calls for an aircraft in the pattern at N40; I should have conducted a more thorough scan to look for unexpected aircraft. While I was scanning in all directions; I moved from one sector to another relatively quickly which likely caused me to miss the Piper and reinforce my perception that there were no other aircraft in the pattern. To prevent a recurrence of the situation; I will start thinking about my initial approach direction to an airport further out than I currently do. This should help ensure proper positioning for entry into the pattern and help other traffic locate me. I will also improve my scan and spend more time on each sector. This should greatly improve my ability to spot any unexpected aircraft.

column121: We had been flying on an IFR flight plan with a destination of ZZZ; a non-towered airport just north of ZZZ1.  As usual; we canceled IFR once we got close to the airport; and began our VFR approach to Runway 24.  Upon approach into ZZZ; got a 'Fuel Imbalance' CAS (Crew Alerting System) message with 5 'segments' of difference; without noticing any prior indication of an issue. While dealing with this issue; we also had to deal with other nearby aircraft targets as well (in the practice area right near the traffic pattern for ZZZ; no doubt).  Per the appropriate checklist; we took the action of turning on the fuel pump on the fuller side. In spite of taking this action; the indication of the problem got markedly worse (increased to 6 and then to 7 segments) within only 2-3 minutes; yet we still had no controllability issues. We then ended up getting a 'Fuel Balance Fault' CAS message as well.  In sum; we were close to the ground; in the vicinity of Class B; near a practice area; trying to deal with this issue. We elected to focus primarily on doing the most important things: aviate; navigate; and communicate.  We flew the airplane and began a diversion towards ZZZ1; which we could clearly see.  It has a longer and wider runway; which would be important in landing a bit faster than normal to avoid potential controllability issues; and it also has emergency equipment available as well. Notified ATC since the issue had happened so quickly; and the fuel monitoring device depicted that the problem had gotten markedly worse so quickly as well -- felt it best to get on the ground quickly before a controllability issue developed.  Landed without incident. If we hadn't been so close to landing (instead being enroute at altitude in the middle of nowhere); we could have obviously spent more time analyzing and dealing with the issue in flight.  After landing and thinking about it a bit; we believed it was an indication problem; and not an actual fuel imbalance after all.  Upon further reflection overnight of our actions during the flight; we concluded that we wouldn't do anything different than we did.  In general; we were pleased with what we did.  It was a particularly busy time with the nearby traffic; and we quickly focused on the most important things of aviate; navigate; and communicate.  The notifying ATC might be questioned; but only with hindsight; I believe -- I am comfortable with this judgment call.

column121: I wanted to share an experience on the new RNAV Visual Approach Runway 17L in MCO. There are two fixes that initiate this approach; CELOT and FELUK. On paper they look different; but in the heat of the moment over the radio they sound similar. During a recent approach we requested this procedure. Approaching from the northwest we were given direct FELUK; cross it at or above 3;000 ft. and cleared for the RNAV visual. Expectation bias led me to hear CELOT (the closer of the two fixes and the logical transition from our position) and I initially loaded that into the FMC; but fortunately the First Officer had heard FELUK and after a quick clarification with ATC we loaded and flew that transition. Definitely weird since we passed CELOT to fly to FELUK; which also required what I would call an inefficient turn to final. Not sure why the Controller did this. Perhaps for separation; or perhaps in the heat of the moment he thought that was the closest and most logical fix for us. I'd like to see one of the fixes renamed so they sound distinctly different; perhaps change one of the names such that both are not two syllables in length. Perhaps also add an alert that vectoring to the opposite side (further away) fix is possible.

We were still at ref and on GS fully stable; we landed normally and taxied off at Taxiway F. I will most assuredly use this experience as a learning example of avoiding potential complacency by carefully applying our procedures to prevent any re occurrence going forward.

column121: I was holding short of [the] runway. I thought that the Tower had instructed me to taxi to [the parallel runway] for take off. I began to move the aircraft. At the same time I looked to the left and observed another aircraft on a half mile final. I applied brakes and at the same time the Tower radioed 'stop; stop'. By that time the nose of the aircraft had passed the limit line and I had committed a runway incursion. Since I was in a tail dragger I was able to lock my right wheel; spin around; and return to the correct side of the limit line. The landing aircraft did a go-around.There were two primary factors that caused the runway incursion. First; I should have waited for a response from the Tower before moving the aircraft. That would have given the Tower time to tell me to remain where I was. Secondly; I should have visually cleared the final approach area before movement. I would have seen the aircraft and not gone past the limit line. Another factor was that; since I was in an open cockpit aircraft there was wind noise from the prop wash as well as engine noise that resulted in me mishearing the instructions from the Tower.In the future I plan to 1. before moving the aircraft; give the Tower time to correct any misinterpretation of instructions; 2. check for traffic before moving the aircraft; and 3. allowing a few more feet distance from the limit line as an added safety margin to prevent movement past the limit line.

column123: During descent into RNO landing [Runway] 16L on downwind; the Controller cleared us for the visual to after another aircraft that was on final. In the correction process of getting the airplane back into the wing scraped another sign. Both signs were damaged but; I believe; returned immediately to service. The airplane sustained minor damage to the wings.As I was on final; the wind direction changed from 11:00 o'clock to my 9:00 o'clock; effectively a crosswind or a wind shear that blew my plane to right; which made me lose control momentarily. I recovered and taxied safely to the FBO but not before the collision.Lesson learned... The winds and conditions at this airport are unpredictable. The wind directions can abruptly change. Next time I will pay more attention to the wind socks on the field as I takeoff or from a safe altitude above before choosing a runway to land. I will not [fail to] ask for and validate the advisories before I commit to and line up for landing.

I quickly disconnected the autopilot and climbed to 2;600 feet. We had terrain (the towers) in sight the entire time. The flight continued uneventful and we landed normally.I descended below the MSA for the approach. Though I had the obstacle in sight. column121: I was Pilot Monitoring on Aircraft X. The departure and cruise phases of flight were normal. The Pilot Flying briefed all of the appropriate items for the approach into ROA prior to our descent. We had discussed the mountainous terrain in the area and how we were going to request vectors to final for Runway 24. ATC had no issues with our request for vectors so they gave us turns for a base leg and a descent to 4;000 feet. I cannot remember exactly how far our base leg was from the runway but I believe it was in between HIBAN and PROSE. In anticipation of our base to final turn we had flaps 9 and gear down. At that point we had not left 4;000 feet and had not called the field in sight. As we were crossing over the mountains; the aircraft gave us a 'terrain terrain.' Since it was daytime and all of the terrain in the area was in sight and not a factor; we continued the approach. We then called the field in sight and landed safely. I believe we had over 1;000 feet clearance at the time of the incident.Aural alert of terrain.The aircraft thinking we were too close to terrain.Continued the approach visually without incident.A possible solution could be to provide flight crews a suggested path to follow when arriving into ROA during day VMC. We could have requested vectors for the full RNAV approach but that may have caused us to be placed too far out on the approach unnecessarily. We planned on receiving vectors and delaying our descent from 4;000 feet until we passed the terrain intentionally to avoid an aural alert from the aircraft. Another possible solution would be to cross over that terrain a little higher and either descend faster to reestablish or overshoot final slightly where there is no terrain in order to reestablish on final on glide path.

column121: Briefed the RNDRZ1 approach...and company messages.  After BERBN; aircraft started initial turn to left. Flight crew corrected and turned heading to 079 ...however; at same time; ATC gave us a heading of 080.  Vectors to follow traffic on final.  Called both traffic and field in sight; turned final behind [another airline] traffic; normal approach and landing.  Advised on taxi to gate; to call LAS approach. Had nice conversation with Controller...who advised...that over 300 airplanes have had identical situation occur.  (Fairly new procedure in effect).  Situation notified; and no aircraft deviations had occurred.Our confusion; per the Jeppesen page…says…Expect a heading of 079...Absent any addition ATC instructions... FMC is programmed to turn left; and join the transition to the runway.   Interesting note...if over 300 aircraft have done same...why not have Approach Controller…confirm a heading after BERBN Intersection...as this is a new set up in the approaches to LAS?
column123: Validated the Arrival/CA briefed approach. The company messages regarding LAS were discussed. After BERBN on the RNDRZ 1 arrival; the aircraft FMS initiated a left turn towards the northeast. We caught this at which time a correction was applied; but before correction was commenced ATC initiated a turn to a 080 heading. When the vector was instructed; traffic was called for the other aircraft on straight in final; and at that time visual contact was established and reported to ATC. We were advised to maintain visual separation and cleared the visual approach. A normal approach to landing was made with no traffic advisories (TA) or resolution advisories (RA).We specifically discussed what to expect if cleared for the visual vs an instrument approach after BERBN; yet the automation management threat was not trapped. The LAS STAR is confusing as to what to expect when waiting for an approach clearance.  We never received any additional ATC instructions on what to expect after BERBN and when the aircraft began to turn this created a confusing environment.This procedure at LAS should be simplified and more awareness needs to be presented. It would be beneficial if ATC could provide (for example) 'Fly 080 heading after BERBN this is radar vectors expect the visual approach' OR 'Anticipate BERBN for the instrument approach clearance'. Also; would help if ATC would be willing to give a heads up in the NOTAMs.  Also; include a message on the release when flying into this airport.

column121: On a Visual Approach Tower instructed another aircraft to 'line up and wait' on an intersecting runway.  On very short final I heard [callsign] 'stop' repeatedly from the Controller. I scanned the airport environment and saw what I believe was a small aircraft moving from my right down the intersecting runway toward the intersection to our runway.  While in the landing flare I called for a go-around immediately before the Controller said '... go-around'. Immediately after I heard the Controller; I said 'go-around' 3 times in rapid succession as the pilot flying was simultaneously executing the go-around. Once climbing I responded to that controller that we were going around.  From my perspective we would climb over the other aircraft.  Once we began to climb; I lost sight of the aircraft as it was moving to my left. We coordinated with the tower to make right traffic and remain with him. We made an uneventful visual pattern and landed.There was nothing we could have done to prevent as we were non-offending aircraft. The FAA has repeatedly made incursions a priority for safety information. I don't know how else to ensure people remain vigilant when receiving this type of ATC instruction.
column123: As we were beginning to flare for landing after being cleared to land; we heard the Tower Controller yell repeatedly on the frequency for another aircraft to stop. It was then that we saw a high wing single engine aircraft rolling for takeoff on the intersecting runway and rapidly approaching the intersection. I began pitching up and adding power at the same time the First Officer called for a go-around and simultaneously we heard the Tower Controller calling '...Go-Around.'  As we climbed out; we crossed above and over the high wing aircraft who appeared to be beginning his rotation for takeoff. We then proceeded with a normal go-around and told the Tower we could enter a visual traffic pattern for a return to landing.Prevention would have been out of the control of our crew or ATC. Prevention would rest with the crew of the offending aircraft by being more alert and assuring a proper takeoff clearance prior to departing.
column125: Flight crew on landing flare reported an aircraft took off from an intersecting departure towards their path so they initiated a go-around the same time MMU ATC told them to go-around. The reporters said the other traffic had been instructed to taxi into position and hold.

column121: This flight was the first leg of a four day for the Captain and I. The Captain said he would be the pilot flying; that I would be pilot monitoring and he briefed everything up; including threats. We made each other aware of our lack of recent flying experience and lack of proficiency. I informed him that I only have a little over 700 hours on type; that most of that time was trans-cons prior to the pandemic and that I haven't flown much in the past twelve months. I also informed him to the best of my knowledge this would be my first time flying into ZZZZ [Airport]. We agreed that we would take things slow and that we were doing everything correctly. Our taxi out; take off; departure and cruise were routine and uneventful. Approximately twenty minutes prior to the Top of Descent the Captain briefed our descent and approach into ZZZZ and we ran the descent checklist. Center gave us a descent; followed by descend via the X Arrival. We were handed off to approach and told to expect the VOR [Runway] X; after ZZZZZ [Intersection] to fly present heading for a vector to the VOR approach. Prior to ZZZZZ the Captain called for flaps 1; After ZZZZZ he called flaps 5; slowed to 190 knots and approach gave us the vector and cleared us for the VOR X approach. We were at or near the final approach fix when the Captain called gear down; flaps fifteen followed by flaps 30 landing checklist. I set final approach altitude; we were VOR/LOC and VNAV descending per the approach and slowing to our vref of 156 knots. At 1000 feet we were fully configured; and I believe we were at 165 knots. I called 1000 feet; the Captain was task saturated so I said 1;000 feet; set missed approach altitude and said cleared to land. I noticed VNAV had kicked off; I verbalized it and re-selected VNAV; I thought it went to VNAV PTH; but in our debrief I learned it went to VNAV SPD. Between 1;000 and 500 feet the fastest speed I saw was 170 knots. At 500 feet I called 500 feet and saw 171 knots and almost called a go-around. I called 15 knots fast; the Captain said correcting and I saw the speed slowly begin to decrease. I was focused on airspeed as we were at the limit for a stabilized approach and I was looking at the runway as I was concerned; we were going to be high. We continued our approach and I was beginning to become uncomfortable; at approximately 200 feet I noticed we were at 170 knots (it may have been a couple knots faster) but 170 is what I recall.  At approximately 200 feet I called that we were high and fast; the Captain said correcting and I saw the speed slowly begin to decrease and he pitched down to correct. At approximately 100 feet I wanted to call for a go-around as we were fast; 170 knots or faster; however I was unable to verbalize it. We touched down hard and flat; we may have had a small bounce; but I don't believe that we did. The Captain deployed the reverser's and we made a normal exit onto taxiway and proceeded to parking. Upon parking; we debriefed this event; we both conducted thorough post flight inspections of the exterior. To the best of our knowledge the aircraft was nott damaged. The Captain contacted Maintenance and they wrote up the hard landing. The Captain also called the Duty Manager.Factors that led to this event were both of our lack of recent flying experience; unfamiliar airport; night operation; Runway X PAPI out of service; the change from VNAV PTH to VNAV SPD and I did not call for a go-around.I have learned from this event and will call for a go-around if I become uncomfortable or we become unstable in the future.
column123: We were flying into ZZZZ [Airport] on the VOR [Runway] X approach at night. I had not flown for a year. Currency was a factor.  While on approach I configured on time and landing checklist was called for and completed.  At 500 feet we were 15 knots fast (171 knots) and the FO said we are fast. I said correcting. At 200 feet I was 17 knots (173 knots) fast the FO said we are fast; I tried to correct.  Also; at 200 feet the FO called out high. I over corrected and landed abruptly from 50 feet to touch down. I do not believe this was a bounced landing but may have been. Our pitch was flat; and we landed main gear first. Our descent rate from 50 feet to 0 feet was extreme.  The failure of myself or as a crew to not call a Go Around was the primary mitigating factor.  Also executing an off center VOR approach with a PAPI OTS at night was a factor. I felt I was not high but in reality; it was due to lack of visible surroundings. Furthermore; the VOR approach only gave guidance to 1;054 feet MSL. To further complicate issues the VNAV disconnected. So; in essence we were left minimal visual guidance. Winds were 20 knots reported and was another factor. My FO stated he was about to call for go-around at 500 feet and 200 feet; but neither of us did. After landing we taxied in normally. We both did several post flight inspections. I also notified Maintenance and the Duty Pilot of the events. This was the first leg of a 4 day with an FO I've never flown with. All briefings were completed. The FO and I discussed before departure how infrequently we have flown due to the pandemic. He had never been to ZZZ [Airport]. I had not been in 2 years. All approach briefings were concluded in a timely manner. The STAR in use was the X arrival. After ZZZZZ [Intersection] we were instructed to fly present heading for radar vectors to join the VOR X LOC. The VOR approach X is a very tricky approach. It is offset with limited visual guidance. Flaps 1 was initiated prior to ZZZZZ. Flaps 5 was initiated after ZZZZZ. Best to our knowledge we were configured by the FAF. The FMA was showing VOR/LOC and VNAV path commencement to approach; at some point we lost VNAV path and went to VNAV speed. I accomplished a LAVS check but did not add 2 and 4 mile rings. I may have gotten very close to exceeding flaps 30 speed. I also believe my flare was minimal at best. Having a PAPI would have been beneficial.
column125: Air carrier flight crew reported an unstable approach and a hard landing.  Both pilots reported lack of flying as a contributing factor.

column121: We were flying the ILS 27R to ORD. When cleared for the approach level at 4;000 ft; we twice had to disconnect the autopilot from the aircraft wake ahead of us. Spacing was the usual 3 miles and winds were straight down the path at 40 kts. Other aircraft were radioing similar issues with wake.  I decided to hand fly the approach the rest of the way to better control the speed and avoid the wake. The Final Controller told us to slow to 170 kts and contact Tower. We were cleared to land by Tower and continued the approach at 170 kts. Spacing was holding at 3 miles; but as we approached the marker I noticed our spacing was closing faster than normal. At about 2.5 outside the marker I made the decision to start slowing to avoid going around. (Full disclosure; I have been in the exact situation before and had to go around twice on this approach.) What I misjudged was how fast the 737-700 slows down. We were at approach speed too early and the Tower asked our speed and instructions. We made the aircraft behind us go around from my error in speed control.
column123: While on ILS 27R into ORD; we were experiencing wake turbulence from the aircraft ahead of us and appeared to be quickly closing the distance between us. We had been assigned 170 kts until the final approach fix 'SIBLY' and began slowing 2 miles prior to the fix to stop the closure. The Tower Controller queried our speed; and emphasized we were to hold 170 until the fix.
column125: B737 flight crew reported slowing below assigned airspeed to avoid wake turbulence from aircraft ahead on approach to ORD; forcing traffic in trail of them to go around.

I notified ATC and they said it's a beehive there right now and he can't tell what's happened there.

column121: I was a CFI giving instruction to a first-time tailwheel student in a Aircraft X. The Aircraft X [vintage aircraft] primary trainer that lacks an electrical system and therefore has no radios with which to communicate with other aircraft. The only electronic equipment onboard is a 406 ELT and a battery powered intercom to allow communication between instructor and student. We had finished the airwork/aircraft familiarization portion of our flight and had returned to the traffic pattern for takeoff and landing practice. We operate out of ZZZ; [local city]; just north of downtown. ZZZ is a busy GA airport with heavy flight school traffic; helicopter traffic; and [vintage aircraft] traffic in the form of [vintage military]; aerobatic; and vintage aircraft. The local flight school is well aware of our [aircraft type] operation and it always extra vigilant of possible NORDO traffic because we operate regularly. Worth noting is that Runway XX [north] utilizes a left-traffic pattern while Runway XX [south] uses right-traffic. This is due to obstacles east of the airport and to keep all traffic over the river rather than over the city. The weather at the time of the incident was good VFR; nearly unlimited visibility with northeast wind of 5-10 kts. The windsock unmistakably favored Runway XX [north] all day and was consistent. I had been at the airport since late morning and everyone had been using Runway XX [north] all day. When in the pattern and scanning for traffic; I noted that we were the only aircraft in the area; but a flight school aircraft was taxiing out for departure on Runway XX [north]. On approach to our first landing; I noticed the Aircraft Y holding short and appeared to be waiting for us to land. After we crossed the threshold; the Cessna taxied into position to depart. After our touch and go and on the upwind leg; my student exclaimed 'dude what's that?' I leaned around him (I was in the rear seat) to see an airplane filling our windshield. I quickly took control and aggressively turned into the downwind. While in the turn; I observed an Aircraft Z pass behind us on final to Runway XX [south]. I estimate the horizontal separation to be 1000 feet or less and vertical separation to be less than 50 feet. Once in the downwind; I observed the Aircraft Z that had taxied into position behind us execute an emergency turn off the Runway; presumably as surprised as I was to see an airplane landing on the opposing Runway with a very obvious tailwind.From the downwind; I witnessed Aircraft Z land and clear the Runway. The Cessna was still holding short; so I executed a short approach and put the Aircraft X on the ground as quickly as possible; student and self very shaken. I taxied to parking in front of the hangar and shut down. I noticed the Aircraft Z pilot was walking toward us; presumably to apologize. Instead; he verbally accused my student and me of causing a dangerous situation by not having a radio. I reminded him of the windsock and weather conditions which clearly favor Runway XX [north] to which he replied that he got his weather report from 'Center' and they told him winds were calm. He then informed me that he made several radio calls; including 'left downwind; left base; and final for XX [south]'. I reminded him that Runway XX [south] utilizes a right traffic pattern; so nobody would be looking for him on the east side of the airport. After a short discussion with the instructor in the aircraft involved; he never heard any radio calls from the offending Aircraft Z other than his last call that he was clearing Runway XX [south]. His account of the entire incident was identical to mine; as we discussed it with the event still very fresh in our minds. He has offered to provide a written statement as well.I am 100% confident that we did nothing wrong nor did we violate any FAR's. I am submitting this report purely for the need for such a close call to be documented and hopefully learned from in the future. The negligence of an irresponsible pilot nearly caused a mid-air collision; and did cause a Runway incursion. If it were up to me; some serious remedial training would be required for the offending pilot to continue to use his privileges as a pilot. Everyone makes mistakes or misses a 'RP' [right-hand pattern] note on a sectional chart from time to time; but when that minor error almost gets people killed; something should be done. His defensive and rude 'I didn't do anything wrong; its your fault for not hearing me' attitude certainly didn't indicate to me that he realized the seriousness of his actions. I sincerely hope this report can be of use to others in the future and a harsh reminder to us all of the unforgiving nature of flying at an uncontrolled field.
column125: Vintage aircraft Instructor reported an NMAC event during landing pattern training with Aircraft Z erroneously landing opposite runway in use. Aircraft Z also cause a critical ground conflict with Aircraft Y.

column121: On completion of 9th landing (full stop; 3 point; 30 degrees of flaps) as aircraft rolled out (at approximately 25 mph) a gust of wind lifted the left wing and veered the aircraft to the left. Full deflection of the controls in an attempt to correct the situation had no effect. Aircraft drug the right wingtip; the right landing gear folded and the aircraft departed the pavement; nosing over and damaging the propeller. No injuries sustained by occupants.

column121: I was flying from ZZZ1 to ZZZ just to run the plane. It was a very bumpy ride and turbulent approach on final at this uncontrolled airport. I landed a little too fast and was caught by a gust from my left side; the right wing lifted causing the plane to veer left off the runway and onto the grass where I got it slowed and re entered the runway without any damage or injury. Cause was landing before the plane had lost all lift. I was practicing a wheel landing where you flare just as the wheels touch the runway; however still flying speed which is fine in no wind conditions. Taught me a good lesson to always do stall landings in gusty windy conditions. I stopped and did a preflight before departing to make sure all was fine. I did four more touch and goes after that with no issues.

column121: We were in visual conditions on approach to GEG; when we were cleared by GEG Approach control to descend to an altitude of 3;900 feet; while also being cleared for the visual approach Runway 21. As we began to descend; I as the pilot monitoring; looked outside and noticed some tall radio towers.  I brought the towers to the Captain's attention and he immediately stopped the descent in order to prevent us from coming any closer to the towers. If I remember correctly we stopped the descent at approximately 5;300 feet. We then flew over the towers and after clearing them we continued our descent to 3;900 ft and commenced the visual approach to a successful and safe landing. We were never given any warning about the towers and did not notice the towers on the approach plate until after landing and getting to the hotel. Since this was my first time flying into GEG; and the Captain had not flown to GEG in years; we were both unfamiliar with the area and after this incident would recommend that a better warning be given on the 10-7 pages as well as on the approach plates.These warnings would not only help those who are unfamiliar with the area and specifically the radio towers; but would help remind those that are familiar with the area to possible encounters with the towers.
column123: After being cleared for a visual approach to Runway 21 in GEG; I started a descent to the SIGYOFAF altitude using FLCH with the autopilot on.  During the descent the FO verbalized that he saw towers at approximately our 12:00 o'clock position.  I promptly leveled off until we passed the towers and then continued the visual approach backed up with the ILS for an uneventful landing. It should be noted that there are towers on a bluff a few miles SE of the Runway 21 FAF that are higher than the FAF altitude.
column125: Air Carrier flight crew reported a hazardous situation exist with tall radio towers in the vicinity of GEG airport. Reporters recommends more prominent warnings on approach and airport charts to flight crews .

column121: Coming into ZZZ about 30 miles out in VMC we were assigned at pilots discretion down to 4000 feet; after that we were assigned another heading and what I thought was down to 3000 feet to join the visual approach in a few miles so i descended down and a few minutes after reaching 3000 feet the controller called to let us know just that the MSA for that sector was 3700 feet to which we asked for a climb and climbed back up and let him know that we would file some paper work and told him we thought we heard 3000 feet and he said something about being VMC plus the MSA altitude and he said just to give him a call when we got on the ground and we got his number. After taking down the number he cleared us for the visual in which we were high as well as close to the final approach fix and handed us off to tower. Turning final and finishing up configuration we realized we were still a bit too high  for our comfort level and standards instead of continuing we decided to execute a go around and came back around to land with no issue. Once we got to the hotel we gave the controller a call and he explained that we were in VMC conditions and he thought we might have just taken another planes altitude and that he had just noted we went below the MSA for the alert he got and there was no issues and we let him know we would file an report for our end and he said have a nice day. Misunderstanding or took another planes altitude. Asked ATC to climb to MSA and request accepted and we climbed to MSA. Nothing do exactly as we did.

column121: Knowing that Runway XX would put us on a straight visual; while we were still in cruise; we thoroughly briefed the landing. There is rising terrain NE of ZZZ1 at the approach end of [Runway] XX necessitating close in maneuvering. Airport elevation is at XXXX so we decided to be fully configured with gear down and final flap selection at 7;500 feet 5NM from the Runway XX threshold to approximate a glide path. This would give us X;XXX feet buffer to get down. (Much more was briefed; this is just a highlight.) After we were switched from Center to ZZZ Approach we were told to expect vectors to Runway YY ILS. This was preferable because that would give us an instrument approach without terrain for visual backup; and it's within the 10 kt tailwind limit. However when we got switched to ZZZ Tower we were told they were not using YY and to expect vectors for a visual XX. We requested [Runway] YY again but this was refused by Tower; so we elected to get delayed vectors to allow time to set up XX again (we already had landing numbers and we had briefed it so it didn't take long). As had been briefed; we were fully configured 2;100 feet above field elevation at least 5 NM out and the FMS was used to draw an extended line from the Runway XX threshold to give better situational awareness of runway positioning during maneuvering. Green needles were used with the ILS frequency for Runway YY to give a visual indication of when we were approaching the runway centerline knowing that this would also give us reverse sensing and a false glide slope--this was briefed as well. My First Officer was flying but if it had been my leg I would've transferred controls to him anyway. I had flown with him for three days and seen him perform hand flown approaches and was supremely confident of his skills. And since we were being vectored in the NW sector for a right base to final to XX he would have the best view to make the turn to final. On final we got a visual GROUND PROX caution box on the PFD as well as aural 'glideslope' messages with an associated flashing caution some 3-6 times. Each time I canceled it and said disregard it's a false glideslope which was acknowledged by First Officer. First Officer had me cancel his flight director and he flew the visual raw data with me backing him up on descent rate; speed; and how it looked. He did a nice job and set it down without incident. [Unrelated] runways were NOTAMed closed. Runway XX PAPI was NOTAMed OTS. The reason to bring this attention is so that other crews will have notification that they may be required to land with minimal instrumentation backing them up on a visual. Other flight crews may not have the experience nor may benefit from mild weather condition. Please notify other crews that while [other] runways are closed they may be required to shoot a straight visual to XX near rising terrain; so that awareness may be raised and overall risk mitigated.
column125: EMB/ERJ-145 flight crew reported continuing to an uneventful landing in spite of CFIT indications of multiple GPWS alerts during a visual final approach. Flight crew maintained visual clearance of the rising terrain.

column121: Neither one of us had ever been into ZZZ. We both took the opportunity on the flight to review the company pages. Acknowledging that it is a special qualification airport and after review of company documents; we realized that controls needed to be transferred to the Captain due to my low time in the aircraft. Control of the aircraft was transferred at approximately 13;000 feet MSL. Traffic was landing XXL. We briefed and set up for the RNAV for XXL. Center handed us off to ZZZ Approach. We were asked to advise when the field was in sight. Conditions were VFR and remained so. We had the field and were cleared for the visual. We recognized immediately that we were high. We had a tailwind which made it exceptionally difficult to get configured. The Captain continued to descend and try to reduce speed. He called for gear; however; I was unable to extend the gear because we were not at the appropriate speed. We were switched to Tower. The Captain continued the descent all the while attempting to reduce speed. Best guess we were at approximately a 7 mile final. We received an EGPWS 'TERRAIN TERRAIN'. The Captain shallowed the descent. It was then followed by a 'TERRAIN TERRAIN PULL UP' and I called for the go-around. We followed the escape procedures; were vectored around the pattern; and landed without incident. Neither pilot had been into the airport. We had reviewed documents; however once cleared for the visual we did not comply with the prescribed speeds on the arrival. In retrospect; we should have continued on the RNAV arrival since we were unfamiliar with the airport. Additionally; better awareness of wind may have assisted in getting configured.

column121: We were Filed and Cleared for 'RNDRZ1 Arrival ILS 26L' in LAS. We set and Descended to 8;000 ft. with Managed Descend Mode. Controller then gave us Descend to 6;000 ft. and pointed out traffic. When we leveled off at 6;000 feet; I queried the controller by transmitting Level 6;000 ft. just passing BERBN Fix. He said 'Roger' as we continued to look for our traffic. We were coupled with ILS 26L and the aircraft started to turn after HOGOG Fix to Heading 020. It is only then; the Controller gave us vector to 080 even as he pointed out the traffic. He then told us that we should have picked up 080 heading after BERBN. RNDRZ1 states at BERBN FIX that 'Landing RWY19L/R; 26L/R EXPECT RADAR vectors to final approach course'. Controller never gave us a vector after BERBN and since Aircraft X Connects from BERBN-BISHP-HOGOG-KODSE-HUXAR; AC started the turn to 020.The initial clearance from ATC was too close to Descend Via Cleared for the ILS 26L. Traffic watch was involved. On the Arrival (which is New) the INSET does NOT display a Track of 079 only a small print that says 079 track after BERBN. This is Aircraft X specific when we select the STAR and APPROACH. I suggest that ATC proactively assign a heading prior to or after BERBN just as they to in LAX after SLI Heading 340 on GOATZ1 Arrival. Train Crews to clear the Waypoint after BERBN so there is a Discontinuity which would Default to 079 Heading.
column123: We were filed and cleared for the RNDRZ1 arrival ILS 26L. I set 8;000 ft. in managed descent. Controller then cleared us to 6;000 feet and pointed out traffic. Leveled off at 6;000 feet and the Captain informed ATC that we were at 6;000 feet and passing BERBN and response was Roger. Continued to actively look for traffic. I was coupled to ILS 26L and just as the aircraft at HOGOG started it's turn the Controller gave us a 080 heading and was pointing out traffic at the same time. Then the controller stated that we should have flown a 080 heading out of BERBN. The RNDRZ1 states at BERBN fix that landing runway 26L expect radar vectors.From ATC initial clearance is too close to descend VIA for ILS 26L. Traffic watch was involved. Arrival the INSET does not display a track of 079 after BERBN only in the small print does it show 079. ATC should issue a heading at BERBN. The aircraft did not to link to the approach or to the arrival or remove the linked portion.

column121: On a long base to final due to multi engine aircraft in front of us. Student was turning final and I just happened to glance at my iPad connected with ADS-B sentry (aircraft only equipped with ADS-B out).  I saw traffic turning at us at 200 feet above.  I believe he was on a right base.  I thought it might be a weird ghost reading; but checked to make sure. I reduced power angled right.  While checking I saw just above and slightly above and slightly in front an aircraft in front of us.  We called our final and he asked where we were that he didn't see us. We informed him that we were right behind him. Aircraft on extend downwind informed that we were about a 1/4 mile apart on final. The other aircraft broke off to the right and exited traffic pattern. I had full controls from my student at reduced power setting. We stayed on final and departed the traffic pattern on upwind without landing;  just a low approach so we could breath for a second. I believe the only thing that we could have done is to amend our base call to have said ' extended base' I'm not sure why traffic was on a right base for that runway. Possibly because there was a lot of traffic and they didn't want to try to find a spot between the 4 other planes in the left traffic pattern?  I believe without ADS-B in that I purchased on my own that it would have been a lot closer.

column121: AIRCRAFT 1 was in the downwind. I decided I was going to turn my traffic on to base leg above my traffic to the parallel runway. AIRCRAFT 2 was downwind ahead of AIRCRAFT 1 descending to 7000 feet. I vectored them to join final; no clearance to use altitude leaving. AIRCRAFT 3 was straight in to the parallel runway. I based AIRCRAFT 1 the first time with the wrong call sign XXX ; no response. AIRCRAFT 3 and AIRCRAFT 1 were head to head at same altitude with 8 miles of separation.  I transmitted three additional times for AIRCRAFT 1 feet twice no response before 'say again' and finally got a read back. AIRCRAFT 1 had divergence with AIRCRAFT 3 by about 1/2 mile. I shipped AIRCRAFT 2 to turn heading 050; more panicked each time; finally got a response. Told AIRCRAFT 3 to expedite with a speed restriction; not realizing I had not cleared them for an approach. They said nothing because [of] my near miss seconds before. Local Control called and asked if they were landing; I said I thought they were cleared. AIRCRAFT 2 went around because of this mistake.This was a tight hole to get into. I pushed to get in it because without; AIRCRAFT 1 would have flown 10 more miles. If pilots would have listened; this would be a non-event; but I instead was panicked and forgot to clear AIRCRAFT 2 I feel terrible for the go around; and scared that I barely had separation; head to head; between two aircraft. I think I will be much more hesitant to attempt to get general aviation aircraft their preferred runway. Just a few minutes before this; I made another general aviation land on a different runway because I deemed it not worth the risk. It's not that I don't have cutoffs; in fact; I'm less aggressive than a lot of my coworkers. This exact situation has been completed without incident numerous times by myself and others. Plain and simple; this happened because the pilots didn't hear and then I allowed a distraction to make me forget to clear AIRCRAFT 2. That being said; at least for a while I won't be accommodating general aviation aircraft quite as much. That situation could have been much; much worse and that's going to be playing in my head for quite some time.

While attempting to resolve the situation the reporter shipped another aircraft to the Tower without issuing an approach clearance causing that aircraft to go around.

column121: I was a passenger on a flight to Spokane. I was sitting in seat X. I was traveling with my spouse who was seated on the opposite side of the aircraft in seat Y. The flight enroute was uneventful. On descent; we were again in VMC conditions. A visual approach would be likely as there were no clouds between our position and the airport. The active runway was 21. As an airline pilot I have done the visual arrival into GEG Runway 21 many times from the south and am aware of the several high towers that are southeast of the airport and directly in line with the arrival into Spokane. Two of the towers are nearly 950 high; so this is a significant threat to arrival aircraft; especially if pilots are heads down and not noticing the towers. If pilots are only focused on clearing the terrain by 1;000 ft. that will clearly put the aircraft in near contact with one of these two towers since the towers themselves are nearly 1;000 ft. above ground. Having been a commuter for much of my career; I have flown over these towers as a passenger no less than 100 times. I know what the normal proximity is to those towers should be and look like from both within the cabin and flight deck on this visual approach. From my vantage point in seat 2A; our vertical proximity to the towers southeast of GEG was not normal and appeared to be dangerously close. We appeared to be much lower over the towers than I had ever seen before in nearly two decades of flying into the airport. I was definitely uncomfortable with what I was seeing and even a bit shaken by what appeared to be a 'close call'. Our arrival into Spokane was mostly normal after that event. The landing appeared to be a bit long; perhaps even out of the touchdown; but nothing of great significance. Upon arrival at the gate; my spouse and I deplaned. Once inside the terminal; my spouse asked me 'Did you see that arrival?' I said 'You mean; how close we were to those towers?' My spouse said;'YES! My God I have never seen those towers so close in my life- that wasn't right.' My spouse is also an airline pilot and commutes so he too is a valid witness to what occurred on that flight. We discussed whether or not to say something as we understand that events can happen from time to time. Because of the seriousness of what we saw; we felt it was appropriate to contact ALPA in an effort to highlight this real threat to all airlines and general aviation regarding the visual arrival into GEG. Over the years I have seen certain deviations from standard that were noteworthy but nothing that alarmed me the way this did. My hope is that additional training can be provided to all airline pilots regarding the towers in proximity to GEG. Perhaps a greater emphasis on arrival procedures. Maybe even a bigger emphasis on the Jeppesen chart itself. I also think the arrival gate coming into GEG from the south should not be allowed over the towers; either VMC or IMC. They are a threat in either scenario. Lastly; a possible change to ATC protocol would be for Approach to require visual confirmation of the towers to arriving aircraft when a visual approach is expected.

column121: Runway 16L; the controls were transferred to me by my First Officer (FO).Oakland Center handed us off to Reno Approach; on initial contact we were advised to report the field insight for the visual approach to 16L. After calling the field in sight; I realized that we were a bit high and closer to the field than I had anticipated. Calling for gear down; my first officer called 'speed check I realized that with speed brakes extended; and all efforts to slow the airplane; with a quartering tailwind; my efforts were futile. Still descending and approaching position WORTH on the RNAV approach we were handed over to tower who cleared us for landing on Runway 16L. Being unstabilized at this time and receiving an EGPWS 'TERRAIN TERRAIN' followed by 'PULL UP" my FO and I simultaneously announced "GO AROUND.'We executed the EGPWS escape maneuver and were vectored for another visual approach; followed by an uneventful landing to Runway 16L. Never having flown into the RNO airport; having an FO just off OE (operator experience) and new to the aircraft type; gusty winds; tailwind on initial descent and approach. On landing winds were 260/variable. On arrival at the gate we were told by station personnel other aircraft had also gone around a one diverted. Suggest more emphasis on speed control throughout the descent and approach."

column121: On final approach into ZZZ I was Pilot Flying. After calling for gear down we received a BRK RH FAULT advisory message. We finished configuring for landing and I called for the Quick Reference Handbook(QRH). The QRH procedure had us run the 'Landing Gear/Brake malfunction landing checklist'. At this point I called for a go around so we may accomplish the necessary items and let ATC know. After the go around I made a PA advising the passengers we had a faulty brake indication and had to go-around to run the checklist. I spoke to the Flight Attendants and told them I would update them; shortly but we should be landing in the next 5 to 10 minutes. Immediately after; I sent a free text to Dispatch advising them we had a brake fault and were executing a go around and running the procedure. I do not recall receiving a response from Dispatch.The First Officer completed the procedure as I prepared the aircraft for landing. The procedure called for a flaps full landing and longer roll out. At this point I decided it would be best to [receive priority from ATC] and have the fire equipment standing by as this was a brake issue which would cause asymmetric braking. I also requested ATC to switch our landing runway to a runway that was longer than our planned runway. After the procedure was complete I called the Flight Attendants and told them we had [an urgent situation]; we will be landing in 5 minutes; expect emergency equipment and we will not be bracing. I then made another PA to let our passengers now we had an issue with one of our brakes and out of an abundance of caution I have requested the airport to deploy its safety equipment but I did expect the landing to be normal with an extended roll out. My voice remained calm and I made no mention of an emergency during the PA. At this point ATC vectored us to final and we configured and ran the appropriate landing checklist. We touched down and I used reverse thrust and very gently applied brakes.I immediately felt the right hand brake was not biting. I had to mainly use the left brake with right rudder to maintain centerline. Once the plane slowed below 60 knots I was able to slow with both brakes and used the tiller for directional control. The right brake was working but did not feel as effective. We exited the runway and were able to taxi normal; albeit at a slow speed. At this point I noticed on the STATUS page the right outboard brake temperature did not rise leading to the conclusion that it was indeed that brake which had failed. I elected to taxi with both engines to reduce asymmetric thrust. Parking at the gate was normal. I entered the discrepancy in the logbook; called Maintenance and headed to our overnight.In hindsight I do believe [advising ATC] was the right call even if the issue did not end up being serious. I was worried about asymmetric braking and was also not sure if the brake had failed released or with full pressure. I felt it was best to declare and have the trucks standing by and not need them. The only thing I would have done differently would have been to call Dispatch before talking to Maintenance. After discussing the event with the company it seems Dispatch was not aware of the issue until after the plane arrived at the gate. I usually call them first when we have maintenance issues but In this case since the flight was terminating I figured it would be best to go directly to Maintenance but I can see how the lack of communication can cause confusion with Dispatch.

column121: I was told to cross ZZZZZ at or above 12;300 feet and I was cleared for the RNAV (GPS) Approach. As I was descending past ZZZZZ I set my altitude at 10;000 feet. I should have set 11;800 feet per the approach chart; which is the altitude between ZZZZZ and ZZZZZ1. After ZZZZZ1 it is 10;000 feet until ZZZZZ2. About 10 to 15 miles from ZZZZZ1; Approach asked me to report ZZZZZ1. This seemed like an odd request; so I looked at the chart to make sure where ZZZZZ1 was and realized the error in altitude. As I started to initiate a climb; the TAWS (Terrain Alert Warning) activated and I got the terrain warning. The rest of the approach was normal after that.I think that I had 11;800 [ft.] set before ZZZZZ because the altitude I was descending from and my rate of descent was going to have me cross ZZZZZ well above 12;300 [ft.]. Then after crossing ZZZZZ I set 10;000 [ft.] because I was mentally one step ahead of where I actually was. While I was being proactive and efficient; I did not double check and confirm where I was on the approach. While I wasn't fatigued or distracted; I think complacency played a role due to the nice weather and the length of the flight.

column121: We did night currency at our home airport. There was one Controller for Clearance [Delivery]; Ground; and Tower. It was pretty busy with military aircraft doing night landings and airliners coming in. I had to ask the Controller to clear us to land each time. Usually they say report downwind or base and then will clear us to land or have us extend after we report. He was probably overwhelmed because he was constantly talking while working three frequencies. I wanted to report just in case it was unclear at any point if we were actually clear to land. The second landing he sounded annoyed when I asked if we were cleared to land - I had to ask because he had not cleared us. It didn't help that we were going 150 knots over the ground because of the 40 knot tail wind. It would help if there were separate people working Ground and Tower.

column121: Chain of events. ZZZ Ground cleared me to taxi from FBO. 'Taxi to Runway XX via Taxiway 1; Taxiway 2; Taxiway 3; cross Runway XY.' On Taxiway 3; approaching Runway YX/XY; I was looking down at my checklist; when I looked up; I was startled to see a regional jet on relatively short (1 mile?) final for Runway YX. After I crossed XY/XY; the regional jet landed without incident.Human Performance Considerations. First; when taxiing; one needs to be focused outside the airplane. When approaching a runway; slow to look for landing/departing traffic. If I had seen the regional jet earlier; I may have either increased taxi speed; determined no speed change was required or called Ground and advised that I would wait for landing traffic.With the pandemic; until recently; commercial air travel has been significantly reduced. Tower personnel may have been reduced as well. I believe one Controller was handling Ground and Tower. With activity picking up; ATC needs to be staffed appropriately as well.

column121: Aircraft requested an RNAV Approach. The Radar Controller cleared the aircraft direct to the Initial Approach Fix. The route was updated in the computer to reflect. Aircraft was level at 13;000 feet and told to cross the Initial Approach Fix at 13;000 [ft.]; cleared RNAV Approach. I was talking with the Radar Controller immediately before saying this was a common error point where aircraft descend below the 12;100 [ft.] MEA (Minimum Enroute Altitude) to the intermediate fix. We observed the aircraft cross the Initial Approach Fix and begin descending. The Radar Controller drew a circle around the next fix for reference. When I came back around on my scan I observed the aircraft already below the MEA of 12;100 feet. I do not recall the exact altitude but it was below 12;000 feet. I brought this to the attention of the Radar Controller. Immediately they transmitted 'Aircraft X WHAT ARE YOU DOING?...' The Radar Controller told the airplane they were below the 12;100 [ft.] MEA. The pilot immediately asked for a visual approach and the Radar Controller asked if they had the field in sight. Upon the affirmative response; the visual approach clearance was issued. I inbounded the aircraft to the Tower and a brasher statement was issued. A low altitude safety alert was never issued.First action should have been to issue a low altitude alert and verify they have surrounding terrain in sight. This approach has had multiple MEA violations and we have been briefed that issuing safety alerts is utmost priority. I should have told the Radar Controller to issue the alert but they responded quickly to the aircraft asking 'what are you doing?' and got them cleared for a visual approach. Perhaps the placement of the MEA step downs is not as apparent as required on the approach plate. The other transitions have both caused issues before on this approach.

column121: While being radar vectored to the visual final for Runway XX at ZZZ; Approach Control issued a descent to 6;000 feet MSL. We read back the clearance and proceeded to descend to 6;000 feet MSL. The Captain did observe that we seemed closer to some radio Towers on a hill than what would appear normal; but we received no GPWS Alerts. We continued on vectors and flew an uneventful approach and landing. After a call from Tower a few hours later; it was explained that the descent clearance to 6;000 feet was issued to a (other carrier X) whose readback was on a different Approach Control frequency. Our readback to that clearance as Company A (very similar sounding call sign) must have occurred at the same moment as (other carrier's) reply. Since the (other carrier X) aircraft had been on a different approach frequency; we had yet to hear any other communications to or from that aircraft; and ATC had not issued a caution for the similar sounding call signs. Additionally; ATC did not issue a low altitude warning if we had gone below the Minimum Vector Altitude in the sector we were within.When managing multiple frequencies; ATC should ensure that aircraft with similar call signs are alerted to possible miss interpretations. Furthermore; ATC should minimize the use of simulcasting on multiple Approach frequencies and consolidate traffic to one radio frequency.
column123: We were being vectored for the visual approach to Runway XX at ZZZ from the east. ZZZ Approach Control issued a clearance to 6;000 feet MSL. We were later contacted that the clearance was for (other carrier) X; on another frequency and not for Company A. The clearance apparently took us below the MVA for that sector. The Controller was controlling multiple frequencies (he was busy) and never advised us of similar sounding call signs. We did not receive any terrain cautions from the aircraft or Controller.  The subsequent approach and landing were uneventful.Listening during communications is always the most important.  Being advised of similar sounding call signs is an important step to help alleviate threats and for Crews to be more vigilant.

column121: After being cleared for a visual approach to Runway XXL in ZZZ we received an EICAS 'E2 OIL LOW PRESS' red master warning message on the EICAS display. I cancelled the red warning and advised my First Officer (FO) to reduce the thrust lever to idle on engine number two. I observed the oil pressure for engine two indicating in the red at about 34 PSI; but fluctuating around the top of the red level; and with occasional fluctuations into the bottom on the green range; and which caused the EICAS warning to extinguish; but then reappear once again triggering another master warning for the same condition about 30 seconds to a minute later. Engine number one was still operating within normal engine parameters (green indications); however. The FO was the pilot flying the aircraft; with the autopilot coupled to the [Runway] XXL ILS. As engine number two was still operating at idle thrust; and as I deemed that there was insufficient time to safely compete the required QRH checklist items during the approach prior to landing; I advised the FO to continue the approach. Therefore; rather than perform a go around so as to provide an adequate amount of time to complete the QRH inflight engine shutdown checklist; I made a call to ZZZ Approach and advised the Controller of the situation - that the aircraft had low oil pressure on engine two and that engine failure was imminent. The Controller queried me if I [needed priority handling]? I replied in the affirmative; and subsequently advised the Controller that the we did not require immediate assistance [for the time being]. The ZZZ Approach controller requested the number of souls on board and approximate fuel. I advised the controller that we had XX passengers and X crew for a total of XX souls on board and that we had XXXX lbs of fuel; and approximately 2 hours fuel (endurance - at single engine burn). ZZZ Approach gave instructions to contact ZZZ Tower; who issued a landing clearance for Runway XXL. I advised the FO to disconnect the autopilot and continue to hand fly the approach to landing with the engine number two at idle thrust. The low oil pressure master warning occurred shortly after the glideslope intercept and normal thrust reduction at an altitude of approximately 10;000 MSL; at around the ZZZZZ fix; on approach to runway XXL. The aircraft configuration at the time of the first EICAS warning was flaps 9; gear up; with a speed of 200 KIAS.  As we had already planned to make a normal flaps 22 approach and landing; I elected to continue the approach configure the aircraft for a flaps 22 landing as originally intended. I advised for the aircraft to be slowed to 190 KIAS and the approach to be continued. Landing gear down and flaps 22 was selected at ZZZZZ1 FAP and at which time the before landing items were completed. I then communicated with the flight attendant an abbreviated brief on the CABIN EMER intercom - that we had a low oil pressure indication on engine two; and that we had about 1 minute to go before landing; and to make sure to be seated. A normal landing was successfully carried out; and upon rollout I advised the FO to activate just the engine number one thrust reverser to aid slowing of the aircraft. I took control of the aircraft at the 80 knots and continued to slow the aircraft using moderate braking to a safe exit speed off the runway at the high speed taxiway exit 1. I taxied to a stop just after turning onto Taxiway M. I instructed the FO to advise the tower the we momentarily stopping on Taxiway M to perform checklist items. The tower advised us to contact ZZZ Ground. I then instructed the FO to shutdown engine two and back this up with the QRH and to then perform the after-landing checklist.  A number of ZZZ rescue fire service trucks were approaching the aircraft. Cognizant that the close proximity of emergency service vehicles would cause some concern for the passengers; I made a PA announcement to the explaining the situation and that once the personnel hadperformed a visual check for any smoke emanating from the rear of aircraft fuselage; we would continue the taxi to the gate as normal. Once personnel confirmed that there was no visible fire or smoke present we requested to continue the taxi to the gate as normal. We then taxied to the gate as normal with the exception that I elected to not set the parking braking - due to a possible risk of smoke or fire developing; as the brake temperature on the left inboard brake was indicating in the amber caution range - but requested for the ground personnel to chock the wheels instead to give time for the brake temps to cool down.  A normal shutdown check was performed and with normal passenger disembarkation. I immediately wrote up the discrepancy in the aircraft logbook and contacted the company dispatch to explain what had occurred and requested to speak with maintenance to discuss the write and the event. I advised Maintenance Control of the discrepancy details and what occurred. Maintenance Control then advised that they would need to place the call on hold while they checked if I needed to be transferred to the Maintenance Duty Manager or to receive a callback from a Maintenance Supervisor. I then performed the aircraft terminating checklist and went outside to inspect the aircraft exterior. I then received a call from a company Maintenance Supervisor to needed some additional information about the event. While on the phone call with maintenance I received a call from someone else in Maintenance (possibly the Duty Manager). As no voicemail or text was left by on my phone; I expected another callback from Maintenance Control; but which did not occur; however.In my view; appropriate decisions and actions were made by all crew members. And given the dynamics of the situation; that these decisions and actions were necessary to mitigate the safety risks; to the passengers; to the aircraft; and in the interests of other relevant aviation stakeholders; and which contributed to a safe resolution and satisfactory outcome.
column123: We were cleared for the visual approach to [Runway] XXL; when we received a master warning and EICAS message E2 OIL LOW PRESS. The Captain identified and cancelled the warning and we verified the oil pressure in the red on EICAS as well as checking the oil quantity on the to data page; where we saw only 4 quarts in the number two engine. I was instructed to bring the number two thrust lever to idle to prevent further damage. At this point we were between the IAF and FAF on the XXL around 190 knots with flaps 9. The Captain and I agreed due to the time available before touchdown and severity of the situation to leave the number two engine at idle thrust. The Captain called ATC and advised ATC of our situation; ultimately [requesting priority handling]. The Captain advised ATC of souls on board and remaining fuel onboard; stating we did not need immediate assistance. ATC handed us off to Tower where we got our landing clearance for XXL. The Captain then called the Flight Attendant and gave her a report to prepare for normal landing; advised the situation; gave her time till touchdown; the flight attendant stated cabin was secured. As we neared the FAF I called for gear down and flaps 22 for the final configuration with a landing checklist. We had already briefed to do a flaps 22 landing due to forecasted wind. The Captain had me disconnect the autopilot and hand fly the approach to maintain best control of the aircraft. We performed a normal landing with number two engine at idle thrust; which was successful. At 80 knots; we transferred controls and captain advised me to tell Tower we would need to stop once clear of the taxiway; which I promptly did. ZZZ Tower instructed us to contact Ground and cleared us to stop on the taxiway. The Captain instructed me to shutdown the number two engine; and promptly back it up with the QRH to verify all necessary steps were taken for the E2 OIL LOW PRESS master warning. We completed the QRH items and after landing checklist at this time. Operations vehicles contacted us and the Captain asked them to verify no smoke; fumes; flames etc.; coming from the number two engine; which happened to have no indications. We then contacted ground control to taxi into the ramp area. Prior to movement we noticed the left hand inboard brake was in the yellow arc; in which the Captain elected to leave the parking brake released and have the wheels chocked at the gate. After parking; we accomplished the shutdown checklist; deplaned all passengers and cargo. I stepped outside to do the post flight walk around and found the bottom navel covered in oil; therefore verifying the oil leak. After returning to flight deck the captain was on the phone with Maintenance control; writing up the discrepancy; the Maintenance Duty Manager explaining the situation. We completed the terminating checklists to finish. I believe the decisions by the crew and actions performed; were the best course of action for the dynamics of the situation and time frame we worked with.

column121: Only upon my meeting with the chief pilot was I aware that I had a close range encounter with terrain.  We never received any type of warning.  The Ground Proximity Warning System (GPWS) did not notify us nor did we get a terrain alert from ATC.  A previous report was submitted regarding this flight due to other reasons.  I viewed the flight path in question as a go around (not anything to hide).  The following is what I recall.  We planned and briefed a Localizer Approach.  When transferred to Approach we were informed the Localizer Approach was not available.  We were left with the RNAV Approach. I requested the First Officer to brief the approach.The RNAV Approach was showing in the FMS. Approach gave us a clearance to a fix; the First Officer responded; but I did not understand the fix.  A fix I was expecting to hear was not what I heard.  I asked the First Officer to request the fix again.  Approach replied and again I could not understand what was said.  I am not seeing anything on the approach plate nor FMS what I am hearing.  Approach mentioned that the fix is on the approach plate then spells it out. Now I understood and knew where to go.  We are heading to ZZZZZ and at ZZZZZ I notice the aircraft is not turning.  The FMS had a discontinuity then direct to ZZZZZ.  I turned off the auto pilot and hand fly to get back on track.From ZZZZZ to ZZZZZ1 the course was centering. Inside ZZZZZ1 was going IMC I wanted to go missed [approach].  Somewhere during this time; the flight in which I was following shifted.  The First Officer tried twice to call Approach for a heading and altitude. No answer.  I transferred the controls and called Approach.  Approach responded.  Approach said something about not to continue the descent and gave an altitude and heading.  The second time around we landed. In reviewing the flight; I can see many errors.  I allowed to many little errors become a larger error.  With the little errors I allowed myself to become distracted.

column121: SGU is notorious for near mid-air collisions and RA events.  I was monitoring the CTAF while we were between ETIYR and PIETZ prior to being cleared for the GPS (Runway) 19.  While monitoring I heard an aircraft state that they were having difficulty getting the runway lights to turn on via the CTAF.  I heard multiple series of clicks on the frequency.  After a minute the other plane said he saw the lights and all was normal.  That plane made position reports and was doing a circle to land to a MAP (Missed Approach) for the HIVKA SID.  Everything was uneventful until an aircraft at Mesquite Municipal began trying to cycle the lights at Mesquite.  Hey; what do you know SGU and Mesquite share the same frequency along with Kanab or maybe 2 more fields.  So; inside the FAF our Runway 19 lights cycle through all 3 settings of low; medium; high and turn off 3 times before we land.  We landed with the lights on low with no incident. Too many airports on the same CTAF.  Get a new CTAF for SGU that is it's own
column125: Pilot reported issues with the CTAF pilot controlled runway lights at SGU.  Reporter stated the CTAF frequency is utilized by several airports nearby which allegedly is causing the runway light problems.

column121: On the LIBER 3 arrival; ATC instructed 'after GLOWS fly heading 020 descend 4200 ft. for traffic; vectors for the approach.' Approximately 15 miles from the runway; ATC instructed '5 miles from CIGNI left turn to intercept the inbound course; maintain 4200 until established; cleared approach.' PF (Pilot Flying) tuned heading; drew a line out from WUNDU; set 1700 in the altitude alert and armed the approach.As course came alive and captured we noticed path continuing to drop below us on the vertical guide. As course came alive; PF pushed VS then VNAV. ALT turned to FLCH and the aircraft began to descend. Rather than capture; it descended through the path. PF pushed VS in attempt to regain the path. We heard 'Terrain warning.' PF turned off AP; began a climb and asked PM (Pilot Monitoring) to rest 4;200. ATC questioned the low altitude alert and asked us to confirm we were established on the approach due to the MSA of 4;200. We confirmed due to laterally established and visual contact with terrain. PF leveled at 4;200; engaged AP and we worked to re-engage the APP while continuing; landed and debriefed.After being vectored off the arrival; it may have been a better course of action to load the RNAV Y for 20R due to better altitude protections. Also need to wait for LNAV to be in magenta.
column123: Due to traffic we were on a heading and descended to 4;200. 5 miles from CIGNI turn left maintain 4;200 until established; cleared approach. I turned to the heading; pressed approach and spun the AA down to 1;700 and lined out from WUNDU; entered in the inbound course and activated.Waiting to intercept I noticed VNAV path was dropping below us. I as saw the course come alive I pressed VS and VNAV; ALT turned to FLCH. Aircraft began a descent to capture PATH but we went through it. My first reaction was to press VS and climb back to path. We heard a terrain warning; I clicked AP off and began a climb asked PM (Pilot Monitoring) to spin AA to 4;200. ATC notified us of a Low Altitude alert; he said if we were established we were okay; We were laterally established and had visual contact with all terrain. I leveled at 4;200 and engaged autopilot. We worked to re-engage APP and continued.We landed and debriefed. Mistake was lowering AA to 1;700 until fully magenta.

column121: We were doing pattern operations at Leesburg with a student. We were on the left downwind for Runway 17 at Leesburg. It was busy with at least one other in the pattern and several aircraft waiting for takeoff and I believe two aircraft approaching for landing. About midfield I noticed on my portable ADS-B indicating an aircraft adjacent to us. Looking to the left there was an aircraft passing on our left just below and to our left. We were completely surprised by this. As far as I could tell the aircraft was doing a left downwind departure to the north which was unusual at Leesburg. I do not know if the aircraft had visual contact with us. Doubtful as we were in his blindspot. Most departures from 17 do a right hand departure to exit the pattern expeditiously. I heard nothing from the Tower that approved this and there was no warning from the Tower of the oncoming traffic. I confirmed with a fellow CFI in the pattern that I hadn't missed a call. The other CFI was about a half mile on downwind and witnessed the near collision. I called the Tower about the traffic and was just told he was departing to the North.
