column121: During arrival into HPN; I noticed the PAPI seemed different. After a few moments I realized that the light indications were backwards. The PAPI for 16 is on the left hand side of the runway but the red lights began on the left (farthest two lights from the runway were red).  The FAA requirements per FAA AC150/5340-30G Chapter 7.4 (d) are that the 'light housing assemblies nearest the runway (inboard) must be seen as red and the two farthest from runway (outboard) must be seen as white.' The VGSI (visual glideslope indicator) was at a corresponding angle to the ILS glideslope which we were using as a backup; despite the red and white being 'flipped'.  I wasn't sure the reference and didn't want to address my concern without being certain and without a reference so I did not immediately notify operations. In addition to my hesitation that evening; the Tower closed just a few minutes after we parked at the gate. The next morning when I arrived to the aircraft; I asked for a contact number for the Tower and gave them a call. The Controller who answered seemed helpful and keen on ensuring that the issue would be resolved. Controller said they would send OPS out to check on the system. Address with HPN operations to resolve the PAPI indications.

column121: During the approach briefing for the ILS approach to Runway 15 at LCH; the First Officer (Pilot Flying); informed the Captain that he had problems with the Runway 15 localizer on two previous flights for this runway. The FO (First Officer) said that on both previous flights; the Runway 15 localizer did not stay centered. The FO explained the Localizer Course Deviation Indicator (CDI) had swung left and right of course. After discussing this; the Captain and FO agreed that the FO would remain on GPS guidance while the Captain would monitor on the localizer radio guidance. After being cleared for the approach; The FO intercepted the inbound course on GPS guidance. The Captain's localizer guidance initially appeared stable. Approximately three miles prior to the final approach fix; FIDGA; the FO switched his course guidance to the localizer. The approach guidance was stable. We passed FIDGA; intercepted the glide slope; and began our final descent. At approximately 1;300 feet MSL; the localizer began deviating left and right of course. The autopilot was engaged. The FO disengaged the autopilot and we immediately descended below the cloud ceiling; which was approximately 1;100 feet MSL or 1;200 feet MSL. The Captain and FO both sighted the runway at 1;100 feet MSL. At some point below 1;000 feet MSL; the localizer appeared to be stable. The remainder of the approach was uneventful. During the approach; the winds were 40 knots from approximately 200 degrees; prior to the FAF. After clearing the runway; we reported the problem to the Control Tower. The FO informed the Tower Controller he had problems with the Runway 15 localizer on two previous occasions. The Tower Controller said he had not received any other notifications about problems with the localizer from any other aircrews. At no point was the approach unstable. [Request] inspection of the localizer antenna. A query to the LCH Control Tower to determine if other aircrews have experienced the same problem. A notification in the daily read warning aircrews they might encounter the same problem.

column121: The flight was worked by myself/CA as the PM and the FO as the PF. Both of us are reserve pilots and haven't flown very much this year. Extensive ground delays combined with multiple weather reroutes caused us running 3.5 hours behind schedule. We ended the day with 11.8 hours duty and approximately 8 hours flight time after factoring in a maintenance test run prior to our turn. On the arrival we were assigned delay vectors for Runway XXC due to ZZZ airport's wind shear concerns. We continued to plan accordingly. Visual conditions in the ZZZ terminal area; current speed was 220 knots; approach cleared us down to 3;000 feet and 030 heading. Shortly after they asked if we had the field in sight. We confirmed; then they cleared us for the visual approach Runway XXC and gave additional instruction to slow down to 160 knots. The PF called for Flaps 8 and then Flaps 20 at appropriate speeds. As we continued to slow down; he contemplated a need for flaps 30. Due to it being almost XA PM; the flight deck was dark; so I grabbed the speed cards and turned on my chart holder light to verify if we needed flaps 30 to stay above maneuvering speed. I noted flaps 20 provided a 4 knot buffer above slowest maneuvering speed; however; the flight conditions caused some light turbulence stemming from gusty wind conditions. The PF called for flaps 30 to provide a bigger margin of safety above Va. Unfortunately; as I got distracted with our Va and speed cards previously mentioned; I failed to notice that the APPR mode was not armed. As result; staying in HDG mode and distracted we flew through the Localizer. Approach called and queried us on our flight path. The PF disengaged the autopilot and corrected back without delay while I responded to ATC that we're correcting. The PF landed the aircraft shortly after without any further irregularities. Despite a warranted concern to make sure we're at a safe speed for the given aircraft configuration; I let myself become distracted with said task and should have done better dividing my attention. As soon as the words 'cleared for the approach' are broadcasted; focus should shift to the FMA by both pilots for confirmation that APPR Mode is armed.

column121: We were making right traffic for Runway XX.  We made a normal upwind; turned crosswind at 700 feet AGL.  When turning downwind; we rolled wings level and pilot flying; noticed departing traffic on our right climbing right to left on a near direct course with our aircraft.  The aircraft passed just out ahead of us (a couple to hundred feet out ahead) and just below (50-100 feet vertical separation).  I was the pilot monitoring/instructor pilot) called Tower to inform them of the near miss and we were told we could make a right 360 degree turn to rejoin the downwind which would have taken us over the runway and done nothing to help us avoid the departing eastbound traffic which was now off of our left side. No action was taken to turn the right 360 for consideration of other aircraft departing the runway and joining the downwind behind us.

column121: I was conducting a flight in the pattern following some avionics upgrades. After being cleared for Runway XX; I unintentionally demonstrated a (scary) non-violent; flat ground loop upon landing. No damage to the airplane or the airport environment. The only damage was to my pride! I was fortunate that the grass was as wide as it was adjacent to the runway and that the transition from the runway edge to the grass and the grass itself was as smooth as it was. I am quite glad to have had a passenger (another pilot) in the right seat as we were able to debrief and I was able to assess the errors that created this event.1) I was carrying too much power (throttle) into the approach and upon landing. While my approach speed was correct; I failed to reduce the power to idle over the threshold. My examination of 'why I would do this' is attributed to my very recent multi-day return to flight training in a Turbo Cessna 182 which is very nose heavy and requires a bit of power to avoid sinking on final. This isn't an excuse! It is a reminder to me specifically that different aircraft require different techniques.2) I bounced. Albeit small; I bounced - caused by #1 and the fact that I landed too flat with too much momentum. I should have immediately gone.3) I didn't hold it off and I didn't have the yoke as far back as needed. I was trained to verbally say to myself 'hold it off; hold it off; hold it off' - I have gotten out of the habit and have re-instituted it.4) I wasn't lined up and there was a small crosswind. Crosswind landings are an area for me to work on. My passenger noticed a difference between the windsock at the approach end of the runway which was straight down the runway (my observation) with a calm to slight wind and the mid field sock indicating a 45 degree crosswind at about 7 to 8 knots. I didn't observe this as it was on the right side of the aircraft my view was obscured by the round out and flare (albeit flatter than required)Those are the ingredients in the recipe for inducing a ground loop - plain and simple.As I was able to taxi back following this incident with no physical damage to anyone or anything; there were a few things I did right...1) I did not panic - panicking never makes things better - that is not to say it wasn't serious - one of the scariest moments in my flying career for sure!2) I kept my heels firmly planted on the floor and did not apply the brakes. 3) Once it was clear that the rudder walk was not going to correct this situation; I put my full concentration into managing the energy and keeping the wings level so we could ride it out - the aircraft did end up rotating about 180 degrees in a decent sized arc - thank goodness for ample room and smooth terrain.I am a big believer of getting back on the horse when you fall off (doesn't mean I wasn't nervous or finding it hard not to think about it too much and psych myself out). I took some time to review some videos I have for Tailwheel Essentials. After all; I was bringing the aircraft back the following day and would need to land safely at home.I am glad to report that it was a good landing as I was lined up; on glide slope; with the correct approach speed; idle at the threshold and I held it off and pinned the tail to the runway with full back pressure - a nice three point landing. So I proved to myself once again that I can do it; and can do it safely. I have arranged some additional remedial training with a Tailwheel CFI to reorient my brain and strengthen my muscle memory.I consider myself extremely fortunate to have learned a VERY valuable lesson that I WILL NOT forget; with only a small loss of pride and a degree of embarrassment!

column121: I was coming from the North and was entering the right downwind.  I was given an extended downwind by the Tower Controller and a 'number 3 clear to land'. I had switched to my ADS-B traffic page on my GTN 750 navigator; which I always do to improve traffic identification in the traffic pattern.  Tower Controller mentioned that number 2 is over a VFR reporting point.  I saw the traffic over that point at my 1 o'clock position.  In hindsight; I am not so sure anymore whether this was actually number 2.  The 1 o'clock angle looked right to me and I saw the traffic; a high wing; and pointed it out to my copilot. It was about where the reporting point is located (at least that's what I thought).  I did not verify with my ADS-B traffic page; which would have clearly shown the number 1 and number 2 traffic.I was focused to look outside for the traffic.  I let the traffic pass on my right and continued a little bit further downwind; since my plane tends to be faster; so to give it more separation. Then I turned base; then final.  I was slightly high so extended full flaps. The gear was already down.  All of a sudden on short final; I saw 50 feet left; 200 feet ahead and 100 feet below of me at about 11:30 o'clock position a high wing plane. I immediately turned right and did a 360; and let the Tower Controller know.  Rest of [flight was] uneventful. I am still a little bit unclear about the exact positions of number 1 and number 2 traffic. I was number 3. The most likely case was that I mistook number 1 as number 2; and thought number 1 was on short final. So I did not pay attention to number 1 but only looked for number 2. If I would have paid attention to number 1; I would have recognized that there is no number 1 on short final; and that the plane that I thought is number 2 is actually number 1.Further; I did not cross check with my ADS-B display. This is exactly the kind of situation; where ADS-B helps greatly and in the most important moment I did not cross check; a clear mistake. In summary: When number 3 or number 4 and cleared to land; make sure all ahead are accounted for. Identify number 1; 2; and 3; so to not mistake number 2 for number 1; or number 2 for number 3.  Usually; I do that; but in this case; I was sure; I had identified the position of number 2 correctly. Cross-check with ADS-B traffic page. Contributing factor was that the audio traffic alert from the GTX-345 transponder to my headset does not appear to work. This may have been due to a recent avionics upgrade and I have to fix.

column121: During a VFR flight; I noticed a slight aircraft vibration that concerned me and I made the decision to land and investigate the issue on the ground. While entering a 45 degree entry to left downwind for Runway XX at ZZZ; I became aware that my radio had become inoperative. I visually cleared for traffic and completed a standard traffic pattern and approach to land. I landed slightly long and slightly fast and made the decision to go-around for a second landing attempt. On the crosswind to downwind turn; while visually clearing for traffic; I saw Aircraft Y on climbout in the pattern behind me. I assessed that either the aircraft had taken off immediately after my first landing; or the aircraft had entered the pattern behind me on a straight in approach. While completing a second pattern; Aircraft Y maintained a relatively close spacing to my aircraft. While on the initial phase of my landing roll; Aircraft Y passed me airborne at approximately 100 feet AGL; and offset to the right on either a go-around or low approach; indicating that he had not maintained sufficient spacing in the pattern behind me to conduct his landing option before I had exited the runway. I exited the runway at the departure end taxiway and positioned at a stop in the south ramp area to do a ground run-up and mag check. While I was performing this action; Aircraft Y completed a pattern to a full stop. Aircraft Y was clearing the runway as I was taxiing north to park. I noticed Aircraft Y come to a hold short of the parallel taxiway while I was still approximately 500 hundred feet from the intersection. As I approached his position; I slowed to give way to Aircraft Y to enter the taxiway ahead of me; but I assessed that the aircraft had remained stopped in the same position and the pilot was holding his cell phone up in the windscreen with both hands; as if waiting for me to pass and take a picture of my aircraft. I did not come to a complete stop. While taxiing past; I noticed the pilot; the single occupant of the aircraft; appeared to be taking pictures of my aircraft. Aircraft Y then entered the taxiway and maintained an uncomfortable distance behind me; so much so that I pulled over on the ramp to let the aircraft pass me. While Aircraft Y taxied past me; with his aircraft still in motion; the pilot had both hands holding his cell phone and appeared to be taking more pictures of my aircraft. He continued to do so even as he was past me; leaning over his left shoulder and looking backwards with his cellphone in both hands while his aircraft continued to move forward. I assessed that I had made the correct decision to allow the aircraft to pass me as he was clearly distracted from operating his aircraft. We both taxied to park in the north ramp. Minutes later; the pilot approach me and identified himself. We spoke about the occurrences. I explained my lack of radio calls due to the inoperative radio. I explained that I did not visually see him on his straight in possibly due to distraction from the engine issue; but that I will continue to enforce my own vigilance and VFR lookout. I expressed my concern for the uncomfortable pattern and taxi spacing and he seemed unreceptive. He also expressed significant concern for the fact that I did not allow him to enter the taxiway ahead of me as the aircraft exiting the runway; which I have addressed in this report.
column125: Pilot reported that due to the inoperative radio they made an approach and landing without communicating and had an NMAC with another aircraft. During taxi to parking; the pilot of the other aircraft followed them closely and took photographs of the aircraft.

column121: This event happened While training was in progress on the local one position. Aircraft X was sequenced to follow Aircraft Y in the pattern. The trainee did not observe that Aircraft X was much faster and had turned in too close behind Aircraft Y. Aircraft Y was on short final approximately 100 feet AGL. Aircraft X was off the left wing of Aircraft Y at approximately the same altitude. It was apparent that Aircraft X had offset to the left side of Aircraft Y in an effort not to land on top of or create a collision with Aircraft Y ahead. This being said; Aircraft X never attempted to query ATC. It was not until the ground controller said 'what is happening' That the trainee; trainer and the Controller in Charge even looked out the window. The trainee was staring at the radar; the OJTI and the Controller in Charge were not paying any attention and we're engaged in a non ATC related conversation. Neither one of them were looking out the window. At the time of the incident; the Instructor had his back turned away from the runway threshold. Once the incident was brought to everybody's attention; the trainee who looked out the window but did not make any attempt to send the aircraft around until the Instructor instructed the trainee to send the aircraft around.  I saw the incident; however; I chose not to say anything.After this Incident; I brought it to the Supervisors attention. That said; at the time of the incident the Supervisor was not in the tower cab. I later heard that the Controller in Charge stated to a management official that the situation was 'not that bad.' In fact; the situation was incredibly dangerous. The aircraft probably were only separated by maybe 200 to 300 feet laterally and 0 feet in altitude. I do believe this statement was made by the Controller in Charge in an effort to cover up the situation considering that the Controller in Charge was distracted in a non ATC conversation and did not see the situation developing.  I believe the only thing that needs to change from this incident; is the Controller in Charge and Instructor paying attention. There is too much trust put in a trainee that has a history of dangerous happenings.

column121: Aircraft X checked on from the East and requested the RNAV Approach. I cleared them for the approach over HILIE at 8;000 feet and they read the instructions back correctly. I had the approach plate pulled up initially on the Information Display System (IDS) to verify that 8;000 feet was an appropriate crossing altitude but then toggled back to the normal IDS screen; to keep an eye on the weather. As they got further along on the approach; I noticed the aircraft was at 6;400 feet inside of the 6;500 foot Minimum Vectoring Altitude (MVA).  Unfortunately; I didn't review that section of the approach during their check on and it didn't set an alarm bell off in my head because we do have approaches in our airspace that have published altitudes below our MVAs. I quickly pulled up the approach plate on the IDS to double check.When I started comparing the approach plate and the radar overlay of the approach; I initially thought they were between NAXAF and CUGEV; not outside of CUGEV.  If you look at the approach plate on the IDS; going from the runway out the fixes are: WIVUP; NAXAF; CUGEV; and OZOLA. However; the RNAV Y 26 approach that is available on the radar display is missing the fix 'WIVUP;' so going from the runway out on the radar displayed map you have: NAXAF; CUGEV; and OZOLA).  I then asked the pilot if they were outside of CUGEV and they responded 'Yes'.  I realized they were descending way too early and I issued a safety alert to climb to 6;700 feet. After they climbed; I asked what their intentions were and they wanted the Visual Approach. I cleared them for the Visual Approach.I was not pleased with my recovery time and felt like I should have reacted quicker. We are very rarely on a Runway XX flow and I should have reviewed the radar display map; approach plates; and appropriate altitudes in more detail before and while on position.  I should have just gone with my gut feeling and immediately issued the Low Altitude Alert and asked questions afterwards.  The Low Altitude Alert never triggered which seems like a common theme in these situations. It would be really nice if we could somehow make that thing more accurate.  I think that the fix 'WIVUP' should be added to the radar displayed map so that is correlates exactly with the approach plate. I think it would have helped me recognize more quickly where the aircraft was on the approach and that they were low.My phraseology was not perfect with the low altitude alert but I really think the phraseology needs to rewritten. By the time I got to the 'advise you climb and maintain part' it seemed like they had descended almost another 200.' That small amount of time could be the difference between impacting terrain and not. Maybe something like 'Aircraft X advise you climb and maintain 6;700 immediately; Low Altitude Alert the appropriate altitude for your area of flight is 6;700' I think getting the aircraft climbing quickly should be the absolute priority and in a situation like this.
column125: GEG TRACON Controller reported they issued a clearance that placed an aircraft below the Minimum Vectoring Altitude. The Controller's radar map was inaccurate and the Low Altitude Alert did not function.

column123: While landing to Runway 22 on a visual approach Tower informed crew that they were 500 feet low. Crew was above the minimum for this segment according to the ILS approach plate. column121: While cleared to the initial approach fix for STS; I was vectored 30 degrees right for traffic maneuvering at my eleven o'clock slightly high but erratically. Coincidentally the maneuvering airplane turned in the same direction initially. As we converged my GTN 70 warned me; I then saw the maneuvering airplane slightly above me at about eleven o'clock converging and avoided a potential collision by rapidly descending about 300 or 400 feet until clear when I climbed back to 5;000; all in good VMC. The Controller gave me a number to call about a 'possible deviation'. I called the number and talked with [a Controller]. He wondered why I didn't announce my avoidance maneuver. The reason was the need for an immediate and very rapid descent to avoid a potential collision. I thought the vector unsafe and was going to complain when I got the word to call and did. The key issue in the above seems to me to be who is responsible for traffic avoidance when on an IFR flight. Perhaps the Controller was busy and didn't notice the vector should have changed as I converged. There likely is some latency in the radar depiction. Further; I wonder why the Controller's software didn't supply a warning. Under the relevant FAR I took emergency measures to avoid a possible collision and did. There simply was not enough time to ask first.

column121: It was a very busy day coming into DAL. We were being vectored from the northeast for a Visual Approach. We had Company traffic in sight and were told to follow them and cleared for the Visual Approach. We ensured clearance from them and began maneuvering for the runway with a more direct path to ZZZZZ.  It was about that time I noticed a TCAS target ahead. I cannot remember if we had already been switched to Tower or not at that point; but ATC told us about the traffic ahead. I responded; 'looking for traffic.' Shortly after that we heard ATC tell Aircraft Y [the traffic ] to turn east immediately. We were given no more instruction and weren't sure which way would be best for avoidance. I told the Captain that we may get an RA here and he prepared. We then got a climb RA. The Captain responded immediately and we avoided the conflict.We were able to regain approach stability and land uneventfully. We have no idea why a small aircraft was in Class B airspace between us and our line to the airport while we were cleared for a Visual Approach; or why we were given no instruction by ATC other than a traffic notification. By the time the traffic was noticed by us as a threat; the rate of closure was so fast we only had a few seconds to process what we may needed to do. We never got Aircraft Y in sight; but I noticed on the TCAS indication that he was 300 feet below us at what looked like our position on the 2.5 NM display.I am not sure what needs to happen to prevent this from happening again because I'm not sure why this happened. I'm hoping this report brings some attention to this event so we can find out why something like this happened. I know they were busy but I feel this incident should have been prevented by either keeping us in their control until we were clear of Aircraft Y or keeping them out of Class B at such a busy time.

column121: The flight was about 30 minutes out and we started to have a medical situation. While we did not declare an emergency we were getting priority handling from ATC and short vectors to the ILS to include a rapid descent and a short downwind and base leg to final. Configuration was 190 Knots and flaps 10 to facilitate the rapid descent. Once on final; I called Tower and received clearance to land; the Pilot Flying called for Gear down and flaps 15; then flaps 25 on speed. At the time that we should have extended the flaps to 30; Tower called to ask if we had landing clearance yet. As Pilot Monitoring; I ended up talking to Tower. Either the Pilot Flying failed to call for flaps 30 or I didn't hear him call for flaps 30 due to the radio call from Tower. Between 600 and 700 feet the EGPWS (Ground Proximity Warning) for 'too low; flaps' went off and we immediately put the flaps to 30. Flaps had finished moving before the 500-foot call. The decision to continue to land was based on being on speed; on glide slope; length of runway available and the medical situation in the back of the aircraft. A normal landing was accomplished and the aircraft cleared the runway.
column123: Flying with a medical situation. We did not declare an emergency but we're getting priority treatment for the ILS. Rapid descent with a turn on at the marker. We had gone to flaps ten to lose altitude quickly and slowed to about 190 knots. About 1 mile from the Final Approach Fix the First Officer called Tower and we were cleared to land I asked for gear down flaps 15 and started slowing further to flap 15 speed. Called for flaps 25. I remember looking to make sure we were slow enough and I am not sure if I called flaps 30 and he didn't hear it or if I didn't do it. The Tower had called us at the same time to tell us cleared to land again. Ran the checklist and just about when he got to flaps the too low flaps warning sounded at the same instant we broke out of the clouds. The First Officer put the flaps to 30. They were down about 500 feet. Completed checklist. I elected not to go around because of the medical situation and the 1X;000 foot runway and our speed was on target speed.  Normal landing and we exited as briefed.

column121: On arrival into Seattle ATC advised we were following a heavy 767. They made approach and landing on [Runway] 34R; and we landed 34L. On arrival just past FOOTT intersection I shallowed our descent rate; and re-VTO'ed (vertical guidance to waypoint) with a 3.4 degree glide path to remain above the heavy. Cleared for the visual I disengaged autopilot; and intercepted slightly wide and high on glide path. Wind was out of the east at about 15 kts; which I knew could push their wake into us. At about 4;000-5;000 ft we got substantial roll to the right; and slight pitching. We countered the roll and continued. The FA's (flight attendants) were seated and we heard no passenger complaints; though one of the forward passengers asked what that was; which I told him was wake turbulence. Even with my precautions of trying to stay above and wide; slowing a little more for better separation; and asking for more spacing from ATC; who was trying to keep us fast.

column121: I was watching from the ground near the hangars on the north side of Runway XX.  My student had completed two successful patterns to a full stop.  On his third final approach; he appeared to be on a normal descent until approximately 100-50 feet AGL.  The aircraft then appeared to increase descent towards the runway.  He pulled the aircraft to level flight just before the aircraft touched down.  The aircraft touched and bounced back into the air.  My student lowered the nose as the aircraft started to descend.  The aircraft began to porpoise.  On the third porpoise; the aircraft departed Runway XX on the left hand side; the nose gear collapsed and the aircraft came to a stop just off the runway.I need to re-enforce with this student what to do when the aircraft bounces.  A pitch attitude above the horizon must be maintained throughout the bounce and a go-around must be initiated.

column121: While flying I failed to fully configure by 1;000 feet AGL. Approach was a Visual Approach. Conditions were clear; 10-mile visibility; and light winds.When landing on Runway XX; TRACON had a restriction to cross a point at 2;600 feet. Configuration at that point was Gear and Flaps 15. Began the descent but had trouble initiating the descent due to automation configuration. During this phase of the approach for some reason I thought I had until 500 feet AGL for final configuration during a Visual Approach. Final configuration was accomplished by 700 feet. Later I referred to the Operating Manual and realized my mistake.Causal factors [included] a lack of recent flying. Aircraft was a B737 MAX and this was the first time I flew one in over X years. This aircraft does not slow down and go down like the B737 NG thus adding to configuration delay. Failure to remember that ALL approaches require final configuration by 1;000 feet. Pairing had a XC:10 Sign-In requiring me to get up at XA:30.  Although this occurred at approximately XI:00  I had already been awake for 9 hours; some of it on the back side of the clock. Perhaps have the same criteria for both IMC and VMC as far as being 'in the slot' this would eliminate any confusion at all. Bottom line; I confused the issue and will not allow that to happen again.
column123: Approach Control told us to maintain 2;600 feet until north of a point.  The Final Approach Fix is south of this point and the altitude is 2;600 feet. The requirement to maintain 2;600 feet caused us to be high on approach. Passing 1;000 feet we were not stable and should have gone around. I felt Approach Control had set us up for failure. The Captain had a lack of recent flying experience and hadn't flown the MAX in two years. He thought being configured by 500 feet on a Visual Approach was acceptable. After landing we checked the manual and realized being stable by 1;000 feet is a requirement for all approaches. It's difficult to be stable by 1;000 feet with the restriction approach control gave us. This restriction is impractical and makes our job difficult.
column125: Air carrier flight crew reported final configuration was not accomplished until 700 feet which is not in accordance with the manual.

column121: While on descent in to ZZZ; I the CA was PF.  We received a different runway assignment than anticipated.  We were cleared to 'cross ZZZZZ at 3;000 feet; cleared the ILS [Runway] XXR approach ZZZ.'  The runway change was made in the FMS from YYR to XXR.  While in VNAV path and cleared for the approach we set FAF Glideslope intercept altitude of 2;000 feet in the altitude window.  The last-minute change left us a bit high so we lowered the landing gear and selected LVL CHG in order to re-intercept the path.  ZZZZZ the IF was in the FMS twice and while trying to correct that and heads down and distracted (because we were no longer in VNAV) the aircraft descended through 3;000 feet towards the 2;000 feet set in the ALT window.  I caught the mistake at approximately 2;600 feet MSL and leveled off until intercepting the glide path.  There was no correction from ATC; no known traffic conflict etc.  Normal uneventful landing.  Upon further consideration I determined it was best to file a report.Cause - Short night; last minute runway change; lack of recency of experience...infrequent flying over the last year with the pandemic.PF [should] have better situational awareness and avoid both pilots being heads down even if the PNF is having difficulty correcting the FMS issue.

column121: After being cleared for the ILS XX in ZZZ; I initiated a descent to 1;600 ft. and began slowing from 250 kts. On the left downwind just past ZZZZZ the aircraft made an unprompted right turn away from its present course. The aircraft was in heading mode and I (FO) attempted to correct the course deviation with the heading bug but the autopilot was unresponsive. The GPWS went off with a caution message due to the terrain south of the field. The CA then took manual control and initiated a climb away from the terrain and back on an intercept course. The GPWS and Radar Altimeter (RA) sounded another caution on the intercept back to the ILS even though there was no terrain present. The CA initiated another climb and then resumed course. He asked me to configure the aircraft for the approach and then handed back controls for me. The approach was then completed in a stabilized and safe manner with no further incident. The initial error was a malfunction with the aircraft turning right unprompted but the human error was the PF (FO) not disengaging the autopilot immediately and instead allowing the situation to degrade into a GPWS caution. Much of the situation could have been avoided if the autopilot was disengaged and the approach hand-flown instead of a reliance on automation. The terrain was briefed initially but I lost situational awareness of its location on the downwind. The mistake won't be made again.

column121: Approximately XA:11;  Pilot Flying and I were on the ILS 27R  to OPF. Weather was good; we could see the runway from 10+ miles. I was Pilot Monitoring. About 3-4 miles from the runway; on course and glide path with autopilot engaged; I saw a bright reflection to our left; about 10 o'clock low.  It was obviously not air traffic; nothing on the TCAS. It quickly moved left to right across our flight path. Passed under the right wing. Appeared to be climbing.  It looked no larger than a lunchbox; but was certainly flying; not just blowing in the wind.  It was a shiny metallic color; not a bird.Aggressive enforcement of laws concerning drone flights in vicinity of airports.
column123: I was on an ILS approach to Runway 27R to OPF.  The weather was VFR and we could see the runway from 10+ SM out.  I was the Pilot in Command - flying pilot.  About 3-4 miles from the runway Pilot Monitoring and I saw a bright reflection to our left; about 10 o'clock low.  It was obviously not air traffic; nothing on the TCAS.  It quickly moved left to right across our flight path.  It passed under the right wing.  It appeared to be climbing.    It did not look very large but was certainly flying and not just blowing in the wind.  It was a shiny metallic color and not a bird.  No evasive action was needed on our  part and none was taken.  Pilot Monitoring reported this object to the OPF tower as we continued on the approach.  We landed safely without incident and cleared the runway and taxied to the ramp.  Possibly this was a drone.If this was a drone being flown around and just to the east of the OPF airport; try and find out who was doing this.

column121: I was taking off on my 3rd and final takeoff and landing for my first solo. I had 3 great landings with my instructor and had landed twice solo and was going in for my final takeoff and landing. I was climbing in the pattern and was at 800 feet when I turned right over [road] then leveled the wings. Waited 3 seconds and turned parallel to the runway at 1;100 feet. Powered to 4500 RPM got the clearance to land from Tower and radioed back for clear to land. I pulled the carb heat and when I got to ¾ of the runway I powered to 3000 RPM which is normal for a descent. I put in 2 seconds of flaps and took out the trim. I noticed I was a bit high at 900 feet when I turned over the shopping center so 100 feet above where I normally am. Put in some more flaps to about 20 degrees and turned in to final. I saw 3 whites and 1 red light; so I knew I was a bit high; I reduced power to get the plane to slow and reduce elevation. This gets me to the railroad tracks and descending in normally. Power is reduced to idle over the railroad tracks near Runway XX airplane starts to decent quicker than the last 2 times that I landed solo. As a result; I pitched up to get the glare shield on the horizon as that happened; I touched down and bounced on the runway. As the aircraft rose; I pitched for a decent angle thinking I was high above the runway; as the nose came over; I was surprised how close to the runway I was. Then I pitched for level flight; as I did this the aircraft touched down for a second; I thought It touched flat. But the nose dropped even more; this is when I heard the prop strike the runway. The aircraft went out of control veering to the left; as it departed the runway the nose gear collapses and digs into the dirt. The forward momentum of the airplane has the tail lifted off the ground before aggressively landing on the mains. the aircraft has come to a full stop; after stopping I paused for 3 seconds and then I contacted Tower and let them know what happened. After contacting Tower; I Opened my door and took off the seat belt; took the headset off and stepped out of the plan. I went out of the aircraft for 30 seconds before going back and turning the ignition off and turning off the master switch. After that I was met by airport ops.  After the initial shock and recuperating my thoughts I have found what mistakes I made on this solo and how this should have been avoided. Firstly; there is the initial bounce and when I caught air; I should have pushed into full power and pitched up to have the glare shield on the horizon which allows me to climb and then radio into Tower for a go-around. Definitely do not pitch down like I did and try to save the landing but rather go full power and pitch up for a climb. I was very appreciative of the airport ops and their quick action to make sure that I was safe and getting all the information to see how I didn't land correctly.

column121: After cleared visual approach for Runway 33 in PANC; we descended to 1;300 ft. to catch PAPI. Due to the strong left gusty crosswind; we were a bit off course and Tower gave us altitude alert to check our altitude. We corrected and maintained flight path until we saw PAPI (3 red 1 white) and continued inbound with a normal landing. Correct actions: due to our heavy weight and gusty wind; we tried to maintain lower altitude to catch PAPI. We drifted right of course where terrain side was located and the temperature was low in that day; so the Tower told us we were a little low. In this case; we should consider about maintaining higher altitude for the cold temperature and plan to turn to final earlier due to the strong left crosswind to eliminate the possible low altitude warning and terrain concerns.

column121: Arriving ZZZ on IFR plan.  Initially wanted to perform GPS XX due to rain and some lower clouds scattered around airport area.approximately 10 nm south of field; called TRACON and informed them I had the field in sight and would cancel IFR after acknowledgement contacted ZZZ Traffic via CTAF.Two aircraft were in pattern for [Runway] XX; I called south of field 7 nm and informed traffic that I would remain east of field at 1;600 feet MSL until I had traffic in sight.I flew what amounted to a left downwind for Runway XY and picked up a Cessna departing upwind for crosswind off of XX.  we were in contact with each other when I was alerted to a helicopter below via a radio transmission from that traffic north east bound away from field in a position below me with no alt read out or announced; and a second pattern aircraft a piper on final for XX which i assumed was in the pattern training but never got in sight.While remaining VMC avoiding some lower scud clouds in area and flying through light rain; looking for both the helicopter and piper; I lost visual with the Cessna on downwind for XX; during my maneuvering I lost position awareness believing I was due north of the field and made a base turn calling base to final XX for a full stop landing; on the descent I noticed the Cessna coming towards my position.  he was calling downwind for XX; he was level and I didn't think he could see me due to our closing angle so I descended to remain clear of his path; calling traffic in sight and called final XX continued to the landing...I had inadvertently aligned for XY and was not in the position I announced; I was free from traffic and made a routine otherwise uneventful landing.During my after landing self-critique I realized / reviewed my errors:1)FPL mode on G1000 PFD and MFD displays were not showing real time position due to the cursor mode and Runway XX GPS approach data being left on screen.2) Navigation around some lower clouds and traffic had me looking around and distracted me from dialing in the new runway alignment which is typical practice for our operations.3) Looking for traffic and distractions led to the disconnect between my perceived and actual position.Corrective actions:  will be to enter traffic patterns from more typical locations instead of shorter direct to downwind entry; as well as dialing in correct landing runway orientation or approach guidance to verify against my position when approaching uncontrolled fields.

column121: Aircraft X was handed off to me at 21;000 feet. I had identified prior to the handoff that the descent was probably going to be very difficult for the aircraft considering the ratio between the distance to the airport and number of feet necessary to descend 18;000 feet over about 40 miles. Upon initial contact I told the pilot to descend to 3000 feet which is the crossing altitude for the GPS approach. As the pilot got closer to the airport I decided to vector the aircraft northbound to accommodate the descent. Once the aircraft was in a better position to; altitude wise; make a stable approach; I turned them to a 090 heading. The aircraft was at about 8;700 feet in a 3;600 foot minimum vectoring altitude(MVA); 4.6 miles from a lower 2;100 foot minimum vectoring altitude. At about 7;000 feet; I turned the aircraft southbound on a 170 heading to point them closer to the initial fix for the GPS approach. Within the next couple miles the aircraft descended a lot quicker than I expected. By the time I noticed the aircraft was going to descend below the 3;600 foot MVA to 3;000 feet; it was too late. The aircraft was at 3;600 feet about 1 mile from the boundary; 3;400 feet .75 miles from the boundary; and 3;200 feet .24 miles from the boundary before exiting the 3;600 foot MVA into the 2;100 foot MVA at 3;100 feet.I should have acted quicker to stop the descent; though right up until it happened I thought the aircraft was going to stay high enough. We have seen this at the facility over the last few days. The aircraft are being handed off relatively late and very high. The weather was not very good and the winds aloft were making the aircraft faster than normal; so the descents have been difficult. A recommendation would be to delivery our northern satellite arrivals to us at a lower altitude.

column121: We were landing at ZZZ at XA40 local time after the tower has closed. We requested ILS [Runway] XX and were cleared for the approach from ZZZ Center. Outside the FAF we were switched to advisory freq; the FO made an initial call of an 8 mile final for the runway and adjusted the PCL (Pilot Controlled Lighting). Inside the FAF the FO made another radio call of short final to the runway.  We landed normal and about 90 KTS I saw head lights on Taxiway 1 where I planned to exit; so I decided to roll to the next one. When the FO called 60 that['s when] we saw the truck was crossing the hold short line. I immediately applied full brakes and redeployed and used max reverse as we were only within a few hundred feet. The truck stopped on the runway when the driver heard our reversers; they were almost to the centerline when they stopped. We stopped about one and a half runway stripes from the truck which then reversed off the runway and we continued to our gate.Only thing that might better safety is sharing this experience with other crews for landing at untowered fields.

column121: Normal takeoff; after rotation a loud thump was heard throughout the airframe. Same thump was heard on descent and during landing rollout.  Post flight inspection revealed Hazmat pallet was not secured in locks and was free to slide between 1L and 2L.  Possible aircraft damage and 9G net damage. The cause was the lack of loading personnel/DG (dangerous goods) personnel not ensuring pallet was locked into position. FO (First Officer) is brand new and did not inspect the locks. Reporter recommends more training of load crew/DG personnel to check all locks are engaged. DG person was in a hurry and short handed. Train FO's to inspect locks as well during DG inspection.

column121: When checking in with SEA Tower; Tower told us 'Cleared to land on [Runway] 34L'; and he then asked us which gate we were parking at. We then heard him ask every other aircraft the same thing. Somebody said that's not important right now (which I wholeheartedly agree with); but Tower insisted that it was. I know they want to make their runway exit and taxi plan but asking us which gate we're going to is not necessary; and it's a huge distraction at a critical part of the flight. We're trying to complete our landing check; make sure we're landing on the correct runway; and flying the jet. I don't want my First Officer looking up a gate; or being distracted away from his monitoring duties; and I don't want that for myself when I'm the monitoring pilot. Another thing that SEA Tower is famous for is giving taxi and/or runway hold short instructions while we're slowing through 100 kts. after landing. That also is a critical time; and we're often transferring controls right about then. Tower needs to WAIT until the aircraft is clear or almost clear of the runway and SLOWED down before they start issuing instructions. Nothing further. Educate the tower controllers on critical phases of flight and the their taxi plan is NOT critical. They need to understand that they are there to help the pilots; but instead they're a huge distraction.
column125: Air Carrier Captain reported SEA Tower Controllers were requesting gate information during a critical phase of flight. The reporter stated SEA Tower routinely transmits instructions during critical phases of flight.

column121: Just below 1;000 feet AGL; ATC directed us to go-around; due to previous aircraft still on the runway. I performed the soft go-around procedure. I called for go-around flaps and the FO selected flaps 2; but the correct flap setting for go-around was flaps 3; as this was a flaps Full approach. I noticed the incorrect flap setting when I saw the airspeed was down in the aircraft's amber 'hook' indication. Normally we would be five knots above the top of this hook; the top of which is Vref. I manually selected a higher airspeed and accelerated before commencing the turn away from the runway. The rest of the go-around and subsequent approach and landing were normal.Lack of recency of experience due to short term leaves of absence may play a role. My FO hadn't flown a lot since returning from a six month hiatus. I also had close to a month off; due to COVID. I feel that I could have reduced my approach speed earlier and thereby increased our spacing. However; we had been assigned 160 knots to a 5 mile final. We complied with that ATC instruction.When I am pilot flying; I brief that a go-around is a successful outcome and encourage my FO to call it out if I don't meet stable approach criteria. I think in the future I will include in my approach briefs which flap setting corresponds to the 'go around flaps' call. This aircraft family uses flaps 3 and flaps full for landing; but 'go-around flaps' is a command to go either from flaps full to flaps three or from flaps three to flaps two.  We don't do that many go-arounds; so a quick review might help. Additionally; watching the preceding traffic more closely on TCAS could have prompted an earlier speed reduction and notice to ATC that we were unable to comply with the previous speed assignment.
column123: During go-around I inadvertently selected flaps 2 instead of flaps 3 during the flap retraction sequence. The PF noticed and compensated; and we continued with the cleanup and subsequent climb and re-vector back runway for a landing with no other issues.I inadvertently selected single engine go-around flap setting of flaps 2 instead of full flaps to flaps 3 go-around.Continue training go-around procedures in training and being more mentally prepared for a potential go-arounds in the landing phase.

column121: I departed around XA:30 and was set to arrive at ZZZ1 at XC:35. Upon my descent into ZZZ1 I began experiencing loss in power and noticed my low voltage light turned on. I tried turning the pilot-controlled lighting at ZZZ1 on; but my radio was not transmitting a signal. To make sure it was not just a problem with the lights at ZZZ1; I flew over to ZZZ2 and also could not get the lights to turn on there. With my landing light being inoperable because of power loss; landing at a dark airport was not a safe option. At this point a tried making a call but had no luck transmitting and [advise] by squawking 7600. I started flying to ZZZ3 to see if the airport was illuminated but when I arrived it was dark.At this point I only had 30 minutes of fuel remaining and concluded I needed to start flying towards ZZZ immediately to ensure the safety of myself and my passenger. I flew towards ZZZ at 2;000 feet; stayed clear of Class B airspace for as long as possible; and was closely watching for traffic visually and on my iPad with the ADS-B in device I had connected. After flying above ZZZ4 and seeing the airport was dark I continued flying to ZZZ and concluded that it was my only option to land safely. Since I was low on fuel; when the airport was in sight; I began my descent into Runway XXR. During my approach I saw another aircraft taking off Runway XYL; I immediately turned left and landed on the parallel Runway XXL to stay clear of departing traffic. I taxied off the runway as fast as possible and worked with airport operations to safely secure my Cessna.This experience has taught me a lot of things to prevent [a situation] like this from happening in the future. I have purchased a handheld radio to have as a backup at all times when flying in the future and am actively talking and learning with my mechanic to better understand the electrical systems in my aircraft. I will always fly with a backup radio in the future especially at night to have the ability to turn on lights at uncontrolled fields and have an extra line of communication if necessary.

When the pilot flew to the nearest commercial airport which was open with lighting and approached to land they had to sidestep to a parallel runway due to an opposite direction departure.

column121: Returning to the airport after doing some air work the Tower advised winds were 19 [kts.] gusting to 24 from 190 degrees. Runway in use [would involve a crosswind landing] so I made the decision to ask for [a different runway] as it was slightly more favorable considering the tail wind component.[This] aircraft was manufactured with brakes only on the left side with a stick and rudder pedals only on the right side; where I sat during the flight.Because the passenger said he had never flown [this aircraft] before and after the airwork we agreed I should make the landing...aeronautical decision making failure on my part; should have gone somewhere else or waited for better winds.I briefed the left seater that if I had a problem keeping the aircraft pointed down the runway I would call for rudder and a tap on the brake.I landed; the crosswind overcame the rudder; I yelled for rudder and brake but off we went into the grass; taking out a runway distance marker; ground looped and wiped out both gears.Even though I had practiced a left seat landing four days earlier; it was outright dumb of me to attempt a landing in that kind of wind condition; and secondly; my skill level wasn't up to the situation. No one was hurt; thankfully; but the gear collapsed and the airplane was [damaged].

column121: The operator of Aircraft X visited GA2. The pilot announced his arrival by making a high-speed low buzzing pass of the airfield as he passed the restaurant at the departure end of Runway 13. He then returned; landed and visited the airport; probably the restaurant. Upon departure on Runway 31 at approximately just as he cleared the trees along the departure end of the runway; the pilot rolled right in a steep bank at maybe 200 to 300 ft. and then at an approximately north heading immediately rolled left to make a steep 270 degree turn to return to the airfield in alignment with Runway 13. He proceeded to make a diving high speed approach to Runway 31 landing gear retracted; and reduced altitude to approximately 20 to 30 ft. at the departure end of the runway next to the restaurant. He rolled right to about a 30 to 45 degree bank and steeply climbed out very close to the restaurant. He was definitely not on the extended center line of the runway. The restaurant is only 300 to 400 ft. off the center line of the runway to the south. This is a common 'airshow' performance at this airport; even though we do not have authorized airshows here. The restaurant is a destination for show-off pilots; and this has become commonplace behavior. These are clear violations of the FAR's; and are extremely reckless. Although reports have been made to the FAA; they don't seem to care; and so it continues. It's only a matter of time before someone makes a mistake or has an equipment failure and ends up crashing into the restaurant; or the neighborhood homes.

column121: Captain was the Pilot Flying. On the landing roll-out on Runway XX in ZZZ he noticed a fuel truck attempting to cross the runway on Taxiway XX. I was heads down monitoring the airspeed; around my '80' call out the Captain said...'That's a fuel truck'. I was unable to see it because of the windshield divider. He immediately applied full brakes and full reversers and we managed to come to a complete stop within 100 ft of the truck.The sound of the brakes screeching and reversers caused the driver to slow to a stop. He still managed to make it past the driver side door onto the runway. I made another CTAF call announcing we were on the runway still...there was no acknowledgment from the fuel truck. He backed up allowed us to continue down to Taxiway XY; then he crossed the runway on XX. I continued to make CTAF traffic calls and never heard the fuel truck make a single one while he continued to the side of the runway.I believe the fuel truck was not monitoring the CTAF frequency. We were switched from approach to advisory frequency well beyond 5 miles from the runway landing zone. Even after the event occurred we still did not hear him make any calls as he continued driving across the airport taxiways.Ground personnel should be trained to constantly monitor CTAF; Ground; Tower and ramp frequencies at all times when moving in vehicles around the airport.

column121: We were departing out of ZZZ on runway XXR at [Taxiway] 1. Tower had cleared us to line up and wait. The co-pilot started to move into position as I responded back to the tower. A moment later the tower came back and said for us remain holding short of runway XXR on 1. The co-pilot stopped immediately but we had already crossed the hold short line. I immediately replied back to the tower and stated that we were already across the hold short line and asking for further instruction. The hold short line was still in front of the main landing gear to give an idea of how quickly we stopped and responded to the towers call. The tower then cleared Aircraft Y to land on the same runway. I immediately radioed back again that we were beyond the hold short line and requested instruction. A second [same airline] pilot; on the other side of the runway waiting to take off; then also radioed to the tower that we were across the hold short line and needed further instruction. The tower said they had not heard any response from us and that we were to remain holding short of runway XXR at 1 and then cleared Aircraft Y to land again. The Aircraft Y pilot radioed that they were going around until the situation was figured out; I switched to the other radio and requested instructions again. The tower then gave us instructions to taxi onto runway XXR; turn right onto [taxiway] 2; turn right again to return to 1 and hold short. I responded with the clearance while the co-pilot taxied us on to the runway and back around to hold short at 1. The tower commented that we weren't responding to her transmissions to which both the Aircraft Y pilot; who was around a 10 mile final at the time of our first transmission; and the [same airline] pilot holding on the other side of the runway confirmed that they had heard all of our responses to the towers clearances. It is my belief that there is a radio dead spot in the area of Runway XXR and taxiway 1 as ground control; clearance; and both [other airline] pilots heard us clearly from varying distances and at different points at the airport; but the tower did not while we were at that intersection.Look into why the radio dead zone from that point to the tower while all other aircraft; ground; and clearance could hear us on that radio at different times and/or inform others of the issue so they can be sure to use a radio with a topside antenna for better ground ops/reception of the dead zone can't be addressed.
column123: After taxi to runway XXR at ZZZ for departure the tower cleared us to line up and wait. Controller informed us there was a plane on a 5 mile final. The PNF acknowledged the instruction and began the Before Takeoff Checklist. I began moving to the runway.  As we taxied just beyond HOLD line with our tail above the line the controller issued an instruction for us hold short.  I immediately stopped; knowing that there was an aircraft on final I instructed the PNF to let her know we were past the HOLD line but not on the runway.  Controller never acknowledged our transmission and shortly thereafter repeated her instruction to hold short.  I then attempted to transmit to her we had moved past the hold short line but were not on the runway.  Another aircraft waiting on the other side of the runway let her know we were acknowledging her transmissions to which Controller responded that they had not heard our transmissions.  At that point I realized our transmissions on the #1 radio were not being heard in the tower possibly due to our antenna location and orientation but the other aircraft were hearing us fine.  I instructed the PNF to switch to the #2 radio and to try using the antenna on the top of the fuselage. Meanwhile the Controller instructed the aircraft on final to go-around.  We established contact with her on the #2 radio and were instructed to expedite onto the runway and clear at the first taxiway then taxi back and hold short for departure sequence.  The remainder of the flight continued without incident using the #1 radio.  Not sure why there was a dead spot with our radio at that position on the airport.  We had clear line of sight to the tower cab and antenna array off our nose and had no issues communicating with the ground controller during taxi out except we were oriented 90 degrees broadside to the tower before turning to face the tower upon reaching the end of the taxiway.I am not sure if this would be considered an incursion event since we were in compliance with the Tower clearance when we crossed the hold short line.  It wasn't until after crossing the line and the subsequent counter instruction to hold short that we were inside the clear area.
column125: CE-525 flight crew reported communications failure caused a runway incursion and another aircraft to execute a go around.

column121: We briefed up 26L; new ATIS gave us 1L; and then ATC offered 1R which we accepted.  We briefed the visual to 1R backed up by the RNAV (GPS) 1R. ATC gave us vectors for the visual; and cleared us to intercept the 1R final. ATC kept stepping us down on final and we couldn't quite see the runway.  Our clearance was to 6;600 feet.  As we continued inbound on final; as the Pilot Monitoring; I noticed we were getting slightly high on the VNAV final.  I mistakenly thought we had been cleared the visual and dialed the altitude window down to zero. The FO said he didn't think we were cleared the visual yet; but the command bars signaled him to descend; so he slowly descended; with the terrain in sight; and I queried the Controller. We leveled off at 6;000 feet at 10 miles with the terrain in sight. ATC said our clearance was to 6;600 feet and asked if we had the terrain and airport in sight.  Now; we could see both terrain and airport and were cleared the visual. We flew the visual; backed up by the RNAV (GPS) 1R and landed uneventfully at LAS.As Pilot Monitoring I should have confirmed cleared the approach before dialing the altitude window to zeros. Also; when getting high on final I should have asked ATC for lower.

column121: While briefing the OBRUG 1B Arrival into MMPR (Puerta Vallarta); the crossing restriction at OBRUG on the Jeppesen Arrival Plate was noticed to be different than the crossing restriction in the FMC database. Jeppesen depicts a crossing restriction of at FL 230 while the FMC defaults to a crossing restriction of at or above FL230. We only programmed the OBRUG 1B into the FMC; so it may be worth checking the OBRUG 1A and all the other arrivals for the same discrepancy.

column121: During our approach briefing; First Officer pointed out that there was an antennae approximately nine miles to the southwest of AVL that is 5;049 feet tall and that Approach Control would leave us high until we were clear of that. While approaching the airport; we were given a descent from 6;000 feet to 5;100 feet. It was during this descent that we received our 'Terrain' GPWS caution alert. We were on vectors at this time due to the First Officer requesting vectors for our RNAV 35 approach. This was done because Center had left us at a high altitude until about 15 miles from AVL and we needed time to descend. Our position at the time of the GPWS caution was approximately 2.5 miles to the southwest of the fix ZICZU. Immediately upon receiving the caution; we notified Approach Control; and added that we were now climbing to 6;000 feet. I then reminded the First Officer that there was a note on our Jeppesen REF plates that says that high rates of descent could result in GPWS alerts (our descent rate was approximately 1;500 FPM). The cautions did not stop until we were at 6;000. Approach Control replied that the Minimum Safe Altitude in that region was 5;100 feet and that we were fine. Upon reaching 6;000; we informed Approach Control that we had AVL in sight. We were cleared for the visual and turned towards the final approach course. Once we were established; we descended down to 4;200 feet for the fix ZICZU and continued the approach. We received no further GPWS alerts.We complied with the GPWS caution directive to 'Pull Up' that we received during our climb to 6;000 feet. Perhaps the northwesterly winds had blown us close enough to the fix AVEBE which is about 13 miles to the southwest of AVL that the terrain database triggered the GPWS alerts. The Minimum Safe Altitude in this region is 6;200 feet. In the future; I could not descend as fast as 1;500 FPM and can be more proactive at requesting a lower altitude from Center well before entering the terminal area.

column121: We were descending into HLN; anticipating on a visual approach for Runway 27. The high scattered layer should have allowed for us to get low enough for the visual. HLN Approach gave us descend to 10;000 [feet]. We were just west of the HLN VOR. We still did not have visual on the runway. Approach then told us that he was not able to vector us for the ILS; due to lack of radar. Sane wanted to descend. The GPWS sounded 'terrain' immediately tho now we are at 10;000 ft. and loaded the RNAV Z 27. We put 10;000 [feet] in at the HLN VOR for the approach; which was significantly lower than if we would continue the approach from cruise altitude. Once we went over the large mountain; the ple autopilot was disengaged and we climbed back up to 10;000 feet. The plane was to descend because it was set up to VTO to the FAP. That then caused us to be unprotected. From there we selected a new VPATH; and no other issues were detected. Observing the approach plate; we should have planned a descent segment by segment to assure to proper terrain clearance. The plane only descending around 150 feet when the GPWS alarmed us; with an immediate climb back to 10;000 feet.  We were right at the lowest altitude to start the approach since we got a descent for the visual approach. We were right at the lowest altitude to start the approach since we got a descent for the visual approach. The correct action would be to follow the approach course altitude segment by segment. Which would have been HLN at 10;000 feet until DULCA then 9;000 feet until FAVTA; then 7;200 feet until BOSCA.

column121: It was a routine flight. On approach; the First Officer called for 'Gear Down'. The gear was lowered and showed '3 Green'. Several seconds later; the message 'Steer Fail' appeared. Because of the proximity to the runway and time constraints; a go-around was initiated. Once the gear had been retracted; the message cleared. However; the Quick Reference Handbook (QRH) procedure for 'Steer Fail'; was accomplished. While this procedure was being completed; ATC asked for the nature of the go-around; fuel on board; and souls on board. The Captain provided this information to ATC and also stated that we are not declaring [an urgent situation] as of yet because we were still troubleshooting the problem. It was later discovered that ATC elected to declare [this situation] for our aircraft (ATC informed us that Airport Fire Dept. is standing by). We informed ATC that our procedure had been completed and that there was a possibility that the problem would reappear when the gear was lowered. We further explained that the procedure indicated that the runway centerline would be maintained but we may have an issue turning off the runway. A second approach was performed (the message returned with the lowering of the gear) and a normal landing was the result. Centerline was maintained via the rudder pedals and differential braking. As tiller steering was attempted; there was no response. The aircraft was brought to a stop on the runway. At this point; the flight crew attempted one ground reset per the Ground Reset Guide. This reset was not successful. Because we are unsure of the ability to negotiate turns (verifying smooth radius turns) the decision was made to have the aircraft towed into the gate by high-speed tug. This was proactively put into work by Dispatch prior to our landing. We were in constant contact with the Fire Crew Chief who relayed pertinent information to the tug crew. The APU was started and the engines were shut down. The gear pins were put into place. Maintenance was on scene as well to verify the aircraft was safe to be towed; and the tow to the gate was started. Communication was maintained with the tug crew and they informed us that all tower communication would be handled by them at this point. The aircraft was successfully towed and parked. Appropriate notifications were made; passengers deplaned via jet bridge; and the aircraft was handed over to Maintenance. During the entire approach; go-around; second approach; landing; and tow to the gate; the passengers were kept informed as much as possible. Both flight attendants were updated throughout the incident and were told that this should be a normal landing with a possible tow to the gate. The flight attendants stated that the passengers remained calm and patient throughout the event.There needs to be a clearer definition of 'Smooth Radius Turns.'  Trying to decide what qualifies a 'smooth radius turn' in the midst of this event is somewhat difficult.I was informed well after this incident that the CodeShare Company had asked Dispatch why we had issued a 'Brace' command. I stated that at no such time was a Brace Command issued and I further confirmed with my flight attendants that no Brace Command was heard in the cabin of the aircraft by either Flight Attendant. Passengers were calm and patient throughout the event. Information was presented and updated to the passengers as much as possible.

column121: I had just crossed the threshold for landing on Runway 18 when ATC announced 'Aircraft X on short final; go-around.' I had been cleared for landing as number three; with additional aircraft behind me; and had been directed to follow the Aircraft Y in front of me. In all previous communications; the Tower had used my tail number call sign. I heard ATC's call; but assumed that it was intended for the aircraft behind me since I was already well over the runway and ATC's previous calls had all used my call sign. I landed while the airplane in front of me was still on the runway; about 500-600 feet ahead. I heard ATC's direction; but assumed that it was for the airplane behind me and was concerned that climbing back in the pattern would put me in front of the other airplane that was going around. After landing; ATC gave me a phone number to call them and I did; explaining to them my perspective of the situation. As a student pilot; I had been told not to land when another airplane was on the runway; but failed to recall that during the landing because the round out was in progress and requiring my full attention. The experience of having to call the Tower and be reminded that I cannot land when another airplane is on the runway; irrespective of how far ahead that airplane may be; has burned the lesson into memory; and while I may make other errors; I will not repeat this one.

column121: Called airport upon departure from ZZZ. Winds were said to be calm. Flight was direct to UT25. On final approach it was very bumpy and as I flared and touched down the wind changed from the right and picked up that wing. I lowered the right wing and aligned to the center line and touched down again. As I set down I was pushed left again and the left wheel hit a tumbleweed in the sand and right wheel touched down in the soft sand. I applied right rudder to get back on the runway and then the soft sand grabbed the wheel and pulled me hard left. At that point all I could do is keep straight as possible and brake to a stop in the sand and tumbleweeds.

column121: We were being vectored for a visual approach to BHM Runway 6 from the southeast. We had briefed the visual approach and also made reference to a safety alert for the Runway 6 obstacle threat. This threat is a 2;049 feet MSL Tower just southeast of MCDEN; the FAF for the ILS Runway 6. ATC cleared us direct MCDEN and down to 3;000 feet. This clearance would have put our flight path directly over the obstacle. One would need to cross the tower at least at 3;000 feet; which is a setup for a high approach. Secondly; a crew; who perhaps did not notice the Safety Alert Page would likely get cleared for the visual; descend further and get an EGPWS warning. I realize that this is not intentional on the part of ATC; but by clearing aircraft from the south directly to MCDEN; they are setting crews up for a potential problem; particularly if a crew is busy and/or task saturated. We told ATC about the issue with the routing over the tower and requested direct CEMPO instead. They issued the revised clearance and subsequently cleared us for the visual approach to Runway 6. I believe that ATC should change their policy of clearing aircraft direct to MCDEN; instead clearing them to CEMPO to avoid this hazardous obstacle issue.

column121: During the approach briefing for RNAV GPS 35 at AVL; I had pointed out to the Captain that due to obstacles and terrain characteristics in the vicinity of AVL; ATC would keep us high until very close to the station. As anticipated; Atlanta Center kept us at a high altitude (I do not remember exactly; but well above 15;000) until about 12-15 miles from the station. Upon being handed to Asheville Approach; and considering the terrain; night time; and the need for sufficient airspace to descend to a reasonable altitude in order to execute an effective and stable approach; I requested radar vectors for the RNAV GPS 35 (glideslope for the ILS was out of service). Approach instructed us to descend to 6;000 and to a heading that would put the aircraft on a downwind to Runway 35.  Subsequently; the Approach Controller gave us a heading that put the aircraft on a left base leg to Runway 35; and instructed us to descend from 6;000 to 5;100. During this descent; the GPWS issued an aural TERRAIN caution. We immediately initiated a climb; advised ATC of the situation; and stopped the climb at 6;000; at which point the GPWS silenced. In addition; we advised we had the field in sight; and were cleared for a visual approach to Runway 35.  The minimum sector altitude corresponding to the left base leg to Runway 35 stated 6;200 ft. We should have queried ATC of the validity of the instruction to descend from 6;000 to 5;100.  From a CRM standpoint; The Jeppessen AVL Airport Reference Page alludes to the fact that a high rate of descent in the vicinity of the airport may cause a GPWS activation due to mountainous terrain in the vicinity (We did not exceed 1500 fpm); hence an emphasis on this matter during the approach briefing; in conjunction to minimizing the rate of descent to; perhaps; no more than 700 fpm; more than likely would have avoided the situation.
