column121: We were flying back to ZZZ on a clear dark night; arriving on day four. It was my leg (Captain); PF (Pilot Flying). About 20 miles out we were given a vector to ZZZZZ which is about 6 miles south of ZZZZZ1; the final approach fix for runway XXL. The controller asked us to keep our speed up to 210 or better until final as she had other inbound aircraft that she was sequencing in. We switched to green needles and sequenced the approach according to our normal procedures. When we were about 10 miles from ZZZZZ; approximately abeam ZZZZZ1; we called the field in sight. The controller gave us a heading of 080; cleared us for the approach and switched us to tower. She noted that the other aircraft would be following us and to keep 170 or better to final. At this point we were about 5 miles from ZZZZZ1 at about 90 degrees intercept. The heading the controller gave us would take us inside the final approach fix before we intercepted the final bearing. Seeing this; the FO (First Officer) noted that we had not begun our decent to final approach fix altitude yet. I called for the descent; flaps 20 as we slowed to final approach speed. As PF; I noted that the flight guidance would not give us a good intercept because the intercept was too great. At this moment; the autopilot began the turn to final too soon. I turned off the auto pilot and began hand flying. As we began the turn to final I called for flaps 30; and then flaps 45 as we neared the final bearing. As we were completing the turn; the FO (Pilot Monitoring) noted that we were still high and that we should increase our rate of decent; we were about 2000 MSL; I steepened our decent and he selected 1000 fpm as we rolled out. I called for and we completed the landing checklist. Tower came up and checked us in. We had not called at the FAP (Final Approach Path) as we were busy flying. At this point; we were about 4 miles from the end of the runway at about 1800 MSL. The tower gave us landing clearance and noted that we were low as the PF I called for us to stop our decent; and I leveled the aircraft at about 1500 MSL. I noted that I had not completed the turn correctly and we were not quite on final bearing. I corrected and called for zero VSI (Vertical Speed Indicator) and adjusted to get on final. The FO complied and we continued inbound. At about 3 miles from the runway on final bearing our GPWS alarm went off; PULL UP. As PF; I called for a climb and began a climb at about 5 degrees nose up. As we began our climb; the GPWS alarm went off again; saying PULL UP; I increased the climb; and we climbed about 200ft; as we were very close to glideslope. I was about to call for a missed approach; when I noted that the climb had put us on glideslope; and the PAPI's confirmed us on glideslope. Seeing this; I reestablished our decent and the FO called stabilized at about 3 miles. We landed normally; completed our checklists and shut down the aircraft in accordance with our normal procedures. We have procedures and training guidance that should have prevented this. In this case; there were several errors I made on this approach. As PF; I should not have accepted the vector inside the final approach fix with such a large intercept heading. As Captain; I knew that the flight guidance would be unable to make the turn correctly and we would need more room to make the turn to final. I should have asked to continue our course and give the controller other options. This likely would have prevented the whole series of errors. I should have started my decent to FAF altitude sooner. We were at 3;000 feet; 5 miles from ZZZZZ1 as we turned; and I delayed our decent to 2;400 ft. for several seconds as I turned off the flight guidance and began the turn. If we had been at 2;400 MSL we would not have required a large decent in the turn to final. When the tower checked us in at 4 miles and noted that we were low; I should have called for and established a climb back to glideslope instead of just leveling out. We were very close to glideslope and we should have re-intercepted it first. When the GPWS alarm went off; I should have immediately called for a Missed Approach. We were low (hence the alarm) and there is no space or time for altitude errors on final. Any delay calling for a Missed Approach puts safety at risk. Finally; there was probably some 'get-home-itis' in our minds as we were flying. Arriving late at night; at home field on day four; we were tired and wanted the trip to be over. Going missed would have delayed our landing. Also; we were also trying to satisfy the controller who was sequencing us in front of another aircraft. We needed to focus on our flight; not her decisions or problems.

column121: First Officer; Pilot Monitoring. Captain; Pilot Flying. 3;000 feet MSL inbound to AWLEE 210 KIAS or greater assigned. Cleared for a visual approach approximately 5 miles from AWLEE 170 or greater to 5 mile final assigned. Continue on heading; allow spacing for visual approach at 210 KIAS. Abeam CRDNL given 080 heading and still cleared for visual and switched to Tower. 080 heading gave a perpendicular intercept course for final. Captain armed approach mode. Aircraft began an early turn to final simultaneously the Captain input a command that caused the plane to enter into pitch mode. I called out pitch mode and he turned autopilot off. I advised the Captain that he needed to begin a descent since we were inside the final approach fix and were 600 feet high. I entered vertical speed mode and put in a thousand ft per min rate of descent and dialed in a 45 degree intercept into the heading. Captain began slowing down and called for gear down; flaps 30; and flaps 45 at appropriate speeds I called for level off when I realized we were at 1;500 feet MSL (acceleration altitude). Simultaneously we received a call from Tower advising us that we were low for our area. I informed Tower that we were leveling and continuing our visual approach. At this point I went outside the cockpit and became focused on looking for towers that would pose a danger to our flight. I didn't recognize any threats. I thought that we could maintain this altitude and intercept the glideslope without issue. We then received a call of terrain. I said we needed to climb. No climb was initiated. We then received the audio alert of woop woop terrain. Pull up. At this point I stated we needed to climb. I began pulling back pressure and a climb was initiated. The audible signals ceased and we continued to intercept the glide slope. Once established on glide slope and on course I called out 1;000 feet stabilized and we completed the approach to a landing.  As soon as we were at 1;500 MSL I should have called for a go around. I knew the Captain was disoriented and was confident that I could act as a back stop to get him onto final. I didn't call for a go around after we stopped getting the audible warnings because we were at that point established within the safe and standard parameters of the approach. However; a go-around may have been the pertinent thing to do to increase everyone's situational awareness.

column121: I had just crossed the YRK VORTAC and began a descent from 2;600 feet MSL towards 2;300. I realized that I had descended too quickly and should have held level at 2;600 until reaching the initial fix of MYKEE for the RNAV 12 approach into HTS. Huntington Approach queried me as to my low altitude (I had descended to 2;450 feet MSL). I corrected my altitude and was then asked by Huntington Approach if I could maintain my own obstruction clearance on the approach. I answered in the affirmative and continued on the approach to a landing without any further issue. This was my initial error. I realized I was fatigued on a long flight with a strong headwind and long period of time in IMC. I should have maintained 2;600 feet MSL until crossing MYKEE.

column121: I came close to 2 [helicopters] on departure in ZZZ. When I was taxiing out they called in a flight of 5 inbound for [Runway] XX. I responded that I was taxiing for [Runway] XX and I would hold short for them. While I was holding short; they flew overhead; and I only saw 3 definitive lights. 2 [helicopter] with white lights followed by 2 [helicopters] with barely recognizable dim green lights; and one in the back with a red beacon. They called clear on [Taxiway] XX as I watched the white and red lights clear the runway I could not see the dim green lights after they passed in front of me because they were barely recognizable. I made a radio call that I was departing on [Runway] XX. I taxied onto the runway with all my lights on; did not see any aircraft lights on the runway; and started my departure roll. Just as I was about to rotate I felt wake turbulence and decided to abort. I saw a black silhouette pass on my right; then another one. I cleared the runway. They claimed that they didn't call clear of the runway and that they had all their lights on. There was a pilot at [FBO] that told me they only saw 3 sets of lights and all appeared to have cleared the runway. He saw me abort and was confused as to why I did because he never saw the other 2 [helicopters].

column121: The student and I had taken off of Runway 17 and at 1;500 feet MSL turned on course to the practice area on a heading of about 230. At this point; I had the student put on their foggles to start their simulated instrument time. Tower informed us that there was an aircraft about 6 miles to the South of the airport which was going in our general direction; their path was South to North. I acknowledged the call and told Tower we would keep a lookout for the traffic. The student asked about something on the instrument panels so I looked inside to answer their question. I heard the other aircraft radio that they had us insight. Since I had not yet gotten them in sight; I hoped to now be able to since they had. I looked back outside in the general direction that the airplane was supposed to be at (right side) and saw the airplane in a steep bank at approximately 400 feet from us with no vertical difference. As soon as I saw them; I banked the airplane to the right and kept our climb pitch attitude. I reported to Tower that I had the aircraft in sight.The conditions that caused the problem were: my heads-down time answering the student's question; not having Tower get a distance from the traffic; and not being able to locate them sooner.Actions to prevent/correct the situation: do not have my head down to answer a student's question; have Tower get a distance from the traffic.

column121: I was providing instruction to my instrument student.  He was in the left seat; flying the plane on autopilot; having been cleared by me (simulating ATC) to proceed via direct to the ZZZ VORTAC at 9;500 MSL; there to commence the RNAV XX approach; expecting a circle-to-land.  I obtained approval from the ZZZ tower for this practice approach; with the usual caveats about maintaining VFR; no traffic separation provided; etc.  That approval was granted while we were still about 10 miles west of the VORTAC; inbound to it at 9;500 MSL and perhaps 130 mph indicated. The top of ZZZ Class D is XX00 MSL; and we had no need to enter the Class D until we were final approach fix inbound.Soon after getting approval from the tower for the practice approach; the tower informed us that there was traffic at about our 10 o'clock; southeast bound.  I saw it immediately both visually and on our GPS nav screen.  It appeared to be a white high-wing LSA (Light Sport Aircraft); either a Remos or a Flight Design.  I can't recall if the tower was talking to that plane; but I'd guess not; as the traffic advisory was issued only to our own tail number; as I recall.I watched the LSA carefully as my student (via our A/P (Autopilot); in NAV mode) took us towards the VORTAC.  We seemed to be converging slowly; with only a 20 or 30 degree difference in our headings; and exactly the same altitude.  The LSA was rock-steady in maintaining its heading and altitude; and seemed to be on A/P; also heading for the local VORTAC.We were slowly closing on the LSA; holding a constant bearing about 30 or 40 degrees behind its right wing.  As the distance between us decreased to about a mile; I instructed my student to make a serious power reduction; which he would have done anyway over the VORTAC.  I thought that this would change the equation enough that we would fall behind the LSA.  After half a minute (or less); I realized that we were not slowing enough to make an appreciable difference in our closure; so I told my student to put his heading bug about 45 degrees off to the left of our heading; and punch the HEADING button on the A/P; which he did.  Our 185 then made a nice standard-rate turn to the left; and we passed directly behind the LSA; exactly at its altitude and about 500 feet away.  The LSA never wavered - as if he never saw us.After about a minute on our new heading; I 'cleared' my student from present position direct to the VORTAC to commence the approach.  We heard nothing from the LSA; nor did we see it again.  The practice approach and landing were without incident.As I recall; we were always behind the wing of the LSA; so we were the overtaking aircraft.  I fault myself for waiting too long before starting the turn to break the slow-motion convergence with the other airplane.  There was no chance that we were going to collide with it - I never stopped watching it; and I am an experienced formation pilot; good at judging closure rates and angles; etc. But I did not allow for the possibility that the LSA might make a sudden sharp turn towards us; or that its pilot might look out to his right rear and be unpleasantly surprised to see us there. If such a situation were to arise again; I would make an earlier turn or altitude change to get a good resolution; and try to keep a mile away from the converging traffic.  It makes more sense; doesn't cost any more; and sets a better example for my student pilot.

column121: I was doing an instrument approach in to CVO with a student. RNAV 35. We had made normal radio calls; 10; 7; 5; 2 and 2 miles out.There was one plane in the downwind who had called #2; landing behind my student and I on Runway 35. When we were below the LPV minimums and the student had removed the foggles; somebody on the ground holding short of Runway 35 called out 'there is about to be a mid air collision for the 2 planes on final.'My student and I both were looking out straight ahead and to the sides and saw nothing. Out of nowhere a plane [Aircraft Y] came over the top of us; from behind and to our right and almost clipped our left wingtip.To our knowledge; there was only one plane in the downwind aside from us on short final and 2 planes holding short of 35 ready to depart. My student confirms that they saw Aircraft Y land on Runway 35 and taxi off with no radio calls.We did everything right; making all the appropriate radio calls. Aircraft Y made no calls and we never saw them on ADS-B or out the window. The pilots from on the ground from two different planes both confirmed that Aircraft Y made no radio calls and that I and my student were doing everything correctly.I never saw Aircraft Y visually or over ADS-B. The student was under the foggles for the approach but when he went visual; we still never saw a plane near us.I can confirm nobody was departing Runway 17 at the time because there was already 1 plane doing pattern work for 35; and the plane who called out the near midair collision from the ground; had just completed a taxi from 17 to 35 on the ground because it was the runway in use.This was within feet of hitting our wing from above. Not only was this incredibly unsafe; but the fact that no radio call was made ever is even more concerning.

column121: Initially reported to Tower my position and I was cleared to enter the right downwind. Adjacent to the Class D boundary common with the Class D airspace boundary with the adjacent airport; I was given a traffic advisory of a small aircraft inbound. I had that aircraft in sight and reported so. I was then instructed to 'Turn left and follow that traffic' I refused the left turn as that would have taken me into the adjacent airport Class D; so reported 'negative left turn; I am turning right and following the [small aircraft]'. When approaching there were three planes on the right downwind for the runway. Aircraft Y who was cleared to land short approach with a long landing; the small aircraft I was following and myself who was cleared to land number 3.Shortly after that; something caught my eye in my right peripheral vision. As I scanned it was an airplane broadside to me; northerly heading; at the same altitude as me; in a slight climb. As a collision was extremely possible; I took evasive action by chopping power and nosed over going negative G to miss the aircraft. I contacted the Tower of who was that airplane and why was he in my vicinity and not called for traffic. Controller went into meltdown about who was who and who was where. By this time the small aircraft I was following had landed. I did not follow the previously issued landing clearance to land number 3 behind the small aircraft; but rather followed this mystery plane which was finally identified as an X type plane who had been in the left closed traffic pattern doing touch and goes. Apparently; this plane made a right pattern after takeoff and went right into me on the right downwind. Controller and any supervisors should be watching that aircraft comply with the proper traffic patterns; and if there is a deviation; notify other aircraft that such non-compliance could create a traffic conflict.
column123: Due to a staffing shortage; the other two controllers in the cab were both Supervisors; which one was on Operational Supervisor and the other on Ground Control. [This was] an abnormal configuration at this time of day. Aircraft Y had been in the pattern with minimal traffic before I took the local position. Aircraft A reported inbound from the northwest. Aircraft A seemed unfamiliar and I provided him extra assistance. When Aircraft X checked on; inbound from the northwest; I had him follow Aircraft A as I preferred not to give Aircraft A more instructions than I deemed he could handle. Aircraft Z checked on inbound from the south. TRACON called with a direct for Aircraft B. I instructed Aircraft Y to extend climb straight out to a point and make right traffic to avoid Aircraft B's expected left downwind entry at a high rate of speed.The Supervisor stated that Aircraft C was inbound from the east for a hospital. I asked if Aircraft C was coordinated by TRACON or did I miss the check in. The Supervisor said TRACON had coordinated it. The Supervisor and Ground Control continued to debate with each other the intention of Aircraft C; the background noise can be heard in the background. Traffic on final needed to extend downwind; and thus; created a problem with Aircraft C's trajectory to cross the runway final. I asked the Supervisor to find out Aircraft C's intentions. Aircraft X reported a near mid-air collision with a small aircraft which was not the correct aircraft. This prompted me to verify Aircraft Z's position. Aircraft Z confirmed his position. Aircraft X mistook Aircraft Y; as Aircraft Z. It was at this time that I realized Aircraft Y had turned in front of Aircraft X. I allowed my attention to be drawn away from my pattern. I appreciate other controllers/ supervisors willing to provide assistance; but because all the information was not known it added another distraction to the operation.Aircraft C ended up entering the airspace without coordination. Aircraft C contacted MRI as he exited MRI airspace. Do not expect aircraft to fly the left and right downwind the same. Extend aircraft further if necessary. Tell aircraft to extend further out even if it extends the pattern. Do not allow others in the cab to distract you. Other controllers and supervisors' assistance is needed in the cab and appreciated. If; however; you do not have all the known information; before distracting the local controller; confirm with the appropriate facility/ sector the intentions of all aircraft.

column121: After an uneventful instrument cross-country flight; I listened to the destination airport ASOS; Wind 29 knot; Gusting 38; clouds OC 900 feet and Visibility 4 sm. I set up the FD (Flight Director) for the RNAV approach into the un-towered airport. I had done this approach before in marginal VFR conditions.Approach cleared me for the approach. With the autopilot coupled to the FD I passed the IAF in heavy IMC and turned inbound at the IF. Approach cleared me to switch to the UNICOM frequency; which I did.  I noticed that the autopilot was not maneuvering the airplane as I had intended; it was making a climbing left turn off course.  I decoupled the autopilot and hand flew the airplane while attempting to reset the FD. While being distracted trying to reset the FD I inadvertently got off altitude and heading and found myself in an unusual attitude.  I recovered from the unusual attitude and got the airplane stabilized and hand flew the airplane and landed without further incident.I performed well during my recent IPC; but single pilot instrument flying in heavy IMC is not the same as being placed under-the-hood with flight instructor in the right seat. Also; emphasis should be made on (UPRT) Upset Prevention and Recovery Training. I will seek training before doing single pilot low IMC approaches in the future.

column121: I was working Local Control with our 2 parallel runways combined onto one frequency. There were 3 touch and goes; 2 on Runway XXR; and 1 on Runway XXL. There were 2 other aircraft inbound. I cleared Aircraft X to land runway XXR; sequencing them to follow Aircraft Y on a close in base. Aircraft Z called ready to go at Runway XXR to enter the traffic pattern. At the time; Aircraft Y was rolling out on the runway; so I told Aircraft Z to stand by. Once Aircraft Y became airborne; I cleared Aircraft Z for takeoff to follow Aircraft Y into the right-hand traffic pattern. I forgot about Aircraft X.Airport Ops called on frequency to inform me that he was at the localizer site on foot; and out of the Runway Safety Area. I glanced over to visually see Ops; who was past the departure end of Runway XXR. As Aircraft Z was crossing the threshold onto Runway XXR; Aircraft X was crossing over the top; intending to touch down onto the runway at the displaced threshold. Aircraft Z questioned why an aircraft was landing on the runway; which alerted me to the situation. I extended Aircraft Y downwind so they would not begin a base. I visually watched Aircraft X exit the runway; while Aircraft Z informed me he would wait for another takeoff clearance. Once Aircraft X was turned off the runway; I re-cleared Aircraft Z; and cleared Aircraft Y. I recommend the Tower controller should pay absolute attention to his active runways; and allow his pad to assist him in not forgetting about aircraft.

column121: On a day; VFR approach to RNO just to the north of waypoint URATE as depicted on the RNAV (RNP) Y to 16L; EGPWS alerted 'Caution; obstacle.'  The FO as pilot flying altered his flight path; yet we then received 'Obstacle; pull-up!' which the FO did.  Both the hill and obstacle at 5;101 feet MSL were in sight the entire time.  We then continued for a normal uneventful landing.  Enroute; we had discussed extensively the challenges of RNO; including terrain; single runway; engine failure considerations; and higher than typical descent rates and true airspeeds; and were comfortable with our position and closure rate with the hill and tower.Cause - EGWPS will alert for terrain or obstacles for either proximity or closure rate sooner than I expected.  By being carefully studied on the terrain and obstacles in the vicinity of RNO prior to arrival; we were well aware of the tower and hill located at the location of the EGPWS alert.Suggestions - Give a wider avoidance to high terrain or obstacles both horizontally; vertically; and with respect to closure rate.
column123: Cleared visual approach 16L RNO. Turned to intercept extended final near FAP WORTH. Had briefed and set up for RNAV (RNP) Y 16L and using it as backup to visual. Dialed in 6;400 and began descent to FAP altitude turning base to final. EGPWS alerted 'Caution; Obstacle'. As PF I verified obstacle in site and continued turn to final away from it. Soon after alert 'Obstacle; Pull up' occurred. I disconnected the AP and initiated a climbing turn away from obstacle that we had kept in site the entire time. Alert ceased very quickly and we continued an uneventful and stable approach and landing. Approach briefing had included a good discussion on RNO hazards including single runway operations; EO considerations; mountainous terrain; high descent rates and TAS. We knew where the obstacle/ hill was on the approach and felt comfortable with our position relative to it on the visual approach.Cause - The preceding aircraft was denied the instrument approach. Visuals in mountainous terrain create unique challenges. Being cleared to follow proceeding aircraft and maintain spacing may have also caused a more square pattern than desired which brought us closer to the obstacle.Suggestions - Maintain the practice of following IAP ground track even on a visual approach if not intimately familiar with the terrain. Also; be cognizant of how EGPWS tracks descent and closure rate. The warnings certainly came quicker than we expected. I would give high terrain an even wider berth.

column121: Me and my student are on a cross country training flight from ZZZ to JWG on clear VMC day with light and variable winds. We are coming in from the east to enter the traffic pattern at JWG by flying over head the airport and entering the right downwind for Runway 17 as that runway is right traffic. Approaching overhead I notice that there are multiple aircraft taxiing on the ground on the parallel taxiway. We make multiple position reports on local CTAF about our position on each leg of the traffic pattern and me nor my student hear any other radio calls for our destination. The student is flying the entire traffic pattern and was doing a normal approach and landing. Our last radio call was on short final about one half to one mile final. As soon as we touched down on Runway 17 about 500 feet from the threshold; both me and my student notice that an aircraft [Aircraft Y] had pulled onto the runway from what appeared to be a taxiway that was at the midpoint of the runway and had turned to face north as if the aircraft had intentions to takeoff or back taxi on the runway. As soon as me and my student noticed the aircraft facing directly at us; about two seconds after touching the ground; my student executed a go-around and immediately climbed our aircraft away from the runway and made a slight turn to the left so that we would not overfly the runway. We then exited the traffic pattern back to ZZZ without further incident. This airport is a class G airport so there is no requirement for use of radios in that airspace; but there seemed to be little vigilance on the part of Aircraft Y for noticing an aircraft on short final with strobe and landing lights operable and on.

column121: Prior to making the flight from ZZZ to ZZZ1; I met with my Flight Instructor to review my flight plan and briefing which I created. All parameters were within the confines of my limitations for my student pilot endorsements for a student solo; cross-country operation. This was my first cross-county solo flight in the aircraft. I was to make two full-stop landings at ZZZ1 as part of the flight; then return to ZZZ. Takeoff from ZZZ and cruise to ZZZ1 were uneventful. I flew at 5;500 feet and reported transitioning the airspace above a non-controlled airport along the way. Immediately after reporting this transition; and well prior to entering the Delta Airspace of ZZZ1; I switched my COM2 radio to ZZZ1 and got the latest automated weather report. I do not recall what the letter designation for the report was at the time; but the weather was clear with good visibility of 10+ miles; winds relatively calm; and a clear sky. I then radioed the tower at ZZZ1 on their frequency and identified myself as a student pilot solo flight along with my relative position west of the airport and requested a full-stop landing while approximately 15nm away.The Tower answered saying they were having difficulty hearing my transmission and to restate my position. I spoke more loudly and clearly; identifying my position as west at about 14.6 miles. They advised me to re-contact them when less than a mile from the runway.  I began a descent appropriate to arrive at pattern altitude prior to radio contact. When I got within a mile I radioed the tower and the tower advised me to turn for my base and that I was cleared to land. I executed turns to base and final without issue and extended full flaps incrementally. Upon entering final approach with full-flaps I was just high of the glideslope as indicated by the PAPI system. I entered my flair and 'ballooned' slightly. When I came back down from ballooning; I came down hard and in the process; experienced prop strike; front gear collapse; and damage to my right wing before coming to a stop on the grass to the left of the runway with the help of the grass to arrest my momentum. The plane came to rest in a nose-down fashion at an approximate 45 degree angle to the edge of the runway and in the direction of the landing. After announcing over the radio that they have a crash on the runway; the tower radioed me to ask my condition and I replied that I was fine. I then noticed fuel leaking from my right wing and elected to shut down electrical items as a precaution. When the airport firetruck arrived; the operator sprayed foam on the area leaking as a precaution for the fuel leak. After several minutes; the responding airport staff strapped the nose wheel to the fuselage and were able to pull the plane to a storage hangar with a tractor by keeping the plane on the main landing gear and tugging the plane via the tail tie-down.

column121: [We were] cleared for the visual approach. Pilot in Command hand flew the visual approach. Switched to Tower and was cleared to land. During the descent to land Pilot in Command got too low. Second in Command informed Pilot in Command to climb as aircraft was too low and almost hit terrain Pilot in Command said he was good; Second in Command advised Pilot in Command to immediately climb. Pilot in Command was reluctant to climb. Tower informed us that we were too low for that sector of the approach and to Immediately climb. Pilot in Command climbed back up to 3;100 feet from 2;000 feet; then landed normally. Pilot in Command lost situational awareness during a visual approach. Pilot in Command initially did not react and did not listen to advice from Second in Command that the aircraft was too low on the Approach. It was not until the ATC Tower Controller told crew to immediately climb to 3;100 feet; did the Pilot in Command react and then climb back up to 3;100 feet. Suggest that the company discusses and better trains flight crews in performing visual approaches at day and night and to somehow incorporate improved Crew Resource Management Training so that if a Second in Command tells the Pilot in Command to climb or descend Immediately; he or she will listen and react immediately; and then ask questions later when the risk factor has been mitigated.
column125: First Officer Pilot Monitoring reported the Pilot Flying flew too low on a Visual Approach and did not respond to his instructions to climb to avoid terrain until ATC also instructed them to climb.

column121: While landing in ZZZ it was the worst turbulence ever experienced on landing. The aircraft had shifted on a sharp tilt aircraft left and sharply right. The door to the Portable Oxygen Bottles (POBs) opened and when the wheels/aircraft touched down with excessive velocity and force it catapulted the POB's forward into the isle of the aircraft. But; the grace of GOD; and physics; no one was injured! The latched locked failed. Passenger X was one of many witnesses; his kids were sitting in aft as well! I don't know how but lord I'm so thankful that no one was hit by the catapulted POB bottles. The landing was furious and as the gusts were up to 35 MPH and flaps and speed were notable not in conjunction of safety. It was clear on a not so perfect hard landing the locks failed easily. A very dangerous situation at the very least. As stated thank the lord no one was injured! The locks on cabinet failed and the brackets also failed to keep the POB's secure. It needs complete checks for the safety of the passengers and crew members alike.

column121: Coming in on the OHSEA2 arrival we requested the visual to 20R into SNA. Passing over BHEAR waypoint we were asked if we had an arriving aircraft in site that was inbound from the east; I believe on the Disney4 arrival. We visually acquired the aircraft and was told to descend to 4;000 feet MSL just to the east of BHEAR. We were then told to turn right heading 030 and asked if we had the aircraft in site and we said 'yes; in site. We were told to continue the right turn to 120 and then told we were cleared for the visual approach Runway 20R. When we were coming around at 4;000 feet MSL I realized we had been vectored inside of LEMON. I had to make an aggressive descent in order to capture the Glide Path to be stabilized by 500 feet AGL in order to have a normal descent rate with approach power and approach speed captured. During this aggressive rate of descent to capture glide path (estimated over 2;000 feet FPM); Tower made an advisory call 'low altitude warning; check altitude;' we were actually above glide slope and coming down onto Glide path when Tower made this call but the steep descent rate triggered their warning system and they made a radio call for a 'low altitude warning;' we were never below glide slope/glide path.The most important take away is that this whole scenario started because SoCal approach vectored us high and turned us tight inside of LEMON. This high and tight turn is very hard to capture a stabilized approach. I would recommend that SoCal gives additional downwind distance prior to turning base leg if the requirement is to stay above 2;500 feet MSL when turning near LEMON or HUKEM."

column121: I was the instructor with a student training for a private pilot check-ride. The runway in use was Runway 30. My student entered mid-field left downwind for Runway 30 and made all appropriate calls. For example 10 miles out; 5 miles out; entering mid-field left downwind; left base; and final on the CTAF frequency. As we turned base another aircraft took off and was climbing out as we turned final. When my student was coming in; student was preparing to do a soft field landing. There was also active parachuting going on. When we were extremely short final the plane holding short of the runway made a call to say they were departing Runway 30 as we were coming down crossing over the threshold. They immediately started rolling toward the runway and I said 'Wait! Wait!' on the frequency. My student was starting the rounding out process and they immediately came to a stop just across the hold short line and said something about stopping on the frequency as we were touching down. I do not recall the call sign of the airplane that caused the runway incursion. We full stopped and taxied back and they departed. The aircraft was clearly not paying attention to our calls and either did not see us on final or did not look.

column121: I landed on the newly-resurfaced and painted Runway XXR at ZZZ; although this runway was closed by NOTAM.I was returning to my home airfield after three weeks away. Although I checked NOTAMs for the several stops enroute home from the west coast; I failed to check the NOTAMs for ZZZ; a very familiar airport; for the final leg of this trip. I also failed to catch the note on the ATIS that advised Taxiway 1 was being used as Runway XXR. The 2-3 minute ATIS transmission contained extensive information about no IFR operations and multiple taxiway and access closures.I was cleared to land on 'Runway XXR' and cautioned about the strong and gusty winds. The landing was uneventful and posed no risk to personnel in the airplane or on the ground. There were no workers or service vehicles in the area. I was able to back-taxi on the closed runway under tower supervision and exit at the normal point to my rented hangar on the northeast side of the field.I understand that the runway was closed for good reason--the yet to be completed lighting systems--and that such a landing is always a potential risk to pilots and ground crew. I hope (and believe) I would have noted and aborted the landing had any people or equipment been on or near the runway I landed on.I attribute this incident to complacency on my part. I expected a clearance to land on Runway XXR; based on the winds; and when I saw the newly-resurfaced runway; I went for it. I am embarrassed by the fact that I would make such an error after XX+ years of flying.

column121: The lead Flight Attendant was so uptight with a passenger not complying with the Mask policies; [Flight Attendant] thought it was prudent to chime the cockpit during landing rollout and advise the flight crew to notify a Supervisor to meet the aircraft.  This was a direct violation of sterile cockpit procedures. This is never a good time to chime the cockpit unless it is an emergency. Especially for a mask compliance issue.Relaxed emphasis from Leadership on the tattling on passengers for improper use of masks (never saw this type of behavior from FA when it came to early seat-belt releases.  Reissued sterile cockpit etiquette with a bulletin on scenario that are and are not acceptable for chiming the cockpit during landing rollout.

column121: Once on final for [Runway] XXR; we got a TCAS RA event from what appeared to me an (other carrier) aircraft that overshot the final for XXL. We did not have the transponder in TA mode because we were never given a Traffic Advisory from ATC; and did not have the aircraft in sight as instructed by our guidance. The RA instructed us to Adjust Vertical Speed. It commanded approximately 1500 FPM descent. We were at flaps 10 and 1;500 feet AGL. As we descended per the RA we continued to configure the aircraft. We were below 1;000 feet by the time we got to flaps 30. The aural glideslope warning sounded. We knew we needed to go-around but couldn't because of the aircraft above us. When I looked at the ND (Navigation Display); I saw the red square showing the intruding aircraft right above us at +5. It was at 800 feet when we got the clear of conflict aural message and elected to go around.The (other carrier) aircraft; in my opinion; went through their intended final for XXL. A contributing factor was the lack of notification by ATC.
column123: While descending through approximately 2;000 feet AGL on the visual approach and on the ILS glideslope to Runway XXR in ZZZ; we received a TCAS RA Descend. The conflicting traffic was slightly above and behind us on Runway XXL. We believe the Descend RA extinguished at approximately 800 feet AGL (although a review of the FRM (Fault Reporting Manual) afterwards says it should be inhibited 'below approximately 1;100 feet radio altitude'). After following the Descend command and by the time the command extinguished; we were not in a stabilized position to land. We executed a go-around. We were not informed about the traffic by Approach and therefore did not meet the requirements to switch to TA Only for Runways XXL and XXR in ZZZ. We were vectored around for a normal landing on XXR. As we switched to ZZZ Tower; another aircraft in front of us also experienced a go-around for the same situation.Presumably the conflicting traffic met the requirements to switch to TA Only because they keyed up on the MIC and said they didn't overshoot and we should/can switch to TA Only. However; we didn't meet the requirements to switch to TA Only.  ATC never asked us to report them in sight; presumably because they were behind us! So; what happens to our RA resolution when the conflicting traffic is in TA Only and transgressing on us? Presumably they can't be offered an RA resolution (which would likely have been for them to climb). If they had climbed the Descend RA 'pressure' on us could have been alleviated sooner. Instead we were forced lower and lower. And all of this was close to the ground. Even though the RA inhibits reaching 'approximately 1;100 feet radio altitude' the conflict with the other aircraft still exists; and we were pretty sure we were below 1;000 feet before the RA extinguished (as evidence by being already slightly into the 1;000 feet white tape). When the RA finally went away we executed a go-around from about 600-800 feet; but the traffic conflict doesn't magically go away.This event occurred right as we were configuring to land; so we never had a chance to fully configure prior to 1;000 feet AGL; as maneuvering for the RA was taking up our attention. The time in the RA resolution mode was more than momentary. It seemed long and sustained enough that it felt like it would just RA us into the ground. As I now knew a go-around was imminent there was a slight moment in my mind I thought about going around right then and there; but the RA continued. I can see where a Crew may be spring loaded to go-around even though the Descend RA is still present.The FO recognized that the traffic showed '+500' on the MFD display. The FAA defines a Near Midair as 'an incident associated with the operation of an aircraft in which a possibility of collision occurs as a result of proximity of less than 500 feet to another aircraft...' We were likely very close to meeting that definition.There was a slight tail wind at altitude for aircraft joining final from left base to runway XXL allowing for increased potential for overshoot. Maybe a very slight overshoot by the other aircraft and the tight sequencing by ATC created this incident; but we can't be sure. It's possible ATC was sequencing aircraft too tight.

The First Officer; and pilot flying put ZZZ in the FMS and executed the change.  I thought I saw him select LNAV; but I began looking at the approach plate to review our arrival.  A minute later I noticed we were still in heading.  I immediately told him to turn to ZZZ1 VOR and select LNAV. ATC contacted us at that time and gave us direct ZZZ1 again.  I told them we were in the turn.This was both our first flight in the 737 Max and we were a little distracted at some of the newness of the aircraft. column121: After intercepting the glideslope it was noted by both pilots the aircraft was not slowing down normally.  Despite lowering the gear and deploying speedbrakes we were having a difficult time getting 10 knots below the next flap speed to continue to configure a variable tailwind was noted between 5 & 10 knots which helped explain what we were seeing.  Continuing to investigate however both pilots thereafter realized we were abnormally low and the PF disconnected the AP and leveled off.  Tower called with a low altitude alert shortly after that and we acknowledged it; configured normally and were stable by 500 feet AGL.  Landing and roll out was normal.  There were multiple approach NOTAMs that day and we miscalculated the effective times on the one stating the ILS XXL was unusable.  We were an hour off and were arriving inside the last 30 minutes of the effective time.  It turns out NAVAIDs maintenance was manipulating the glide slope signal at the time; causing the glide slope to appear lower.The ILS to XXL was OTS and NAVAIDs maintenance was transmitting a lower than normal glidepath. It would have been helpful if ILS status had been repeated on the ATIS; as it normally is; but it was not.  During the low altitude call from tower they were even quick to add to the call that it 'was in the NOTAMs'.  Further investigating with my prior position with FAA Flight Inspection it is still in their guidance that NAVAIDs maintenance is not supposed to transmit a localizer signal if they are going to manipulate the glide slope signal to avoid this hazardous; misleading; information.  The localizer and glide slope both were transmitting; with a proper ID; at the time of the incident; we even crossed ZZZZZ at the appropriate altitude of 3;000 and showed on path.  It was after that when the aircraft would no longer slow; as it was chasing a depressed glide path.  In the end we missed the NOTAM but then multiple holes of the swiss cheese lined up; thankfully it was day VFR and we saw the solution with our own eyes in time.  The other layers of protection normally there (ATIS; maintenance procedures) would have helped overcome our oversight.

column121: Unexpected last minute change of runway from RNAV Runway 29R to ILS runway 11L. Winds were calm forecast to pick up out of the west at our arrival time. No digital ATIS; didn't find active runway until listening to ATIS on radio well into the arrival. On downwind vectors advised of pair of military aircraft we would follow on approach. Turned base and cleared for visual approach to Runway 11L. As we turned in towards the airport; we crossed a knoll just north of the final approach course. We were descending and crossed over the top of the knoll at about 1;400 feet AGL. The GPWS activated and announced pull up terrain; the FO (First Officer) executed an escape maneuver as he had been trained.  With autopilot off speed quickly accelerated and still in the flaps 2 configuration we exceeded the limit speed by about 5 knots for 5 to 10 seconds before speed reduced back into normal range. We advised TUS that we were missed approach and were vectored around to another visual approach and landing.Cause - Higher than normal workload; changing runways; keeping traffic in sight and terrain awareness.On calm wind days be prepared for approach from either direction on smaller airports with only one primary runway. Study terrain areas to plan for visual approach altitude you want to be at crossing them.

column121: On flight into MSP. I was PM (Pilot Monitoring) and at 1;200 feet AGL was told by ATC to look for a helicopter at 10:00 o'clock and 600 feet below.  I did not see traffic and responded with 'looking'.  The helicopter reported us in sight and they were told to pass behind the aircraft and he repeated that request.  Shortly after; we got a TA and traffic was 200 feet below and close by at 11:00 o'clock.  Captain deviated slightly from glide path and localizer but not to full scale deflection.  At this time we looked to the left and the helicopter was only 200 feet away and banking hard to the right.  It seemed as if he only just saw us.  We continued and landed with no further incident.  There's not much we could have done different; I believe the helicopter saw different traffic as he should have never been that close to us. Helicopter reporting us in sight and not providing adequate separation.

column121: I was doing training with the Aircraft X in the right hand seat as flight instructor; left hand seat occupied by experienced skydive pilot. During the final of the first touch and go of his session of 3L heard the Aircraft Y on the frequency asking landing information. I gave him 3L in use with right hand circuit and 2 aircraft doing touch and go training. When coming in right downwind again Runway 3L we got a traffic warning on the GPS screen. Traffic appeared to be head on 200 feet above. PIC didn't see it but I spotted the traffic head on almost same altitude and called the conflicting traffic to warn him being opposite in the downwind head on. He replied to join right downwind 3L. As PIC still couldn't see the traffic and a collision was going to happen I took the yoke and turned the aircraft to the right with a slight down input. Conflicting aircraft never saw us neither did any avoidance maneuver.PIC had back the controls and we did a second touch and go. Meanwhile the Aircraft Y joined the right downwind 3L and landed when we where again end of downwind. Turning on final for full stop ( final call was done) the Aircraft Y reported entering back track 3L. Being surprised as we where already on final I saw he could however vacate heaving 3 taxiways ahead. Nevertheless the pilot of the Aircraft Y called us that we better did a go-around as they would not be able to vacate in time. I replied that his behavior was very unprofessional; first positioning his aircraft opposite ours in a downwind leg and then entering and blocking the active runway with an aircraft on final. After we landed and shut down the aircraft I expected at least that the PIC of the Aircraft Y would come to apologize; but no one came to us. When checking the airport register I also noticed he never registered his inbound nor outbound flight.  When checking FlightRadar24 you can clearly see that the Aircraft Y flew opposite the downwind descending through 1;700 feet of the 03L to do a 180 turn to join his opposite track. The Aircraft X is at the same moment at 1;500 feet flying correct downwind position for the 3L. Closing speed was around 250 kts.

column121: While on the ENDEE 6 Arrival to MDW we encountered a near midair collision and the following is a narrative of that event. Time stamps are from 'Flightaware' app and may not be 100% accurate. I added these to recreate the event to the best of my knowledge.I was the Pilot Flying on this leg. At about XX:26Z ATC began to vector us off the arrival for spacing. During these vectors we were cleared to descend to lower altitudes. I had even made the comment jokingly that we were being 'over-controlled' by ATC after several heading; altitude and speed changes. At approximately XX:45Z we were cleared to descend to 5000 feet. Our flight path at the time was approximately over top of Morris Muni airport (C09) about 6.6 miles southwest of STKNY on the arrival. Crossing altitude at STKNY is published is 6000 feet. We were then cleared direct to ALQUE for the ILS 4R at MDW. While still level at 5000 feet at approximately XX:47Z we had a TCAS TA (solid amber) showing an aircraft in front of us and +100 feet above our altitude. I do not remember the scale set at the time on my navigation display but it was very 'close'. This was this first time we had seen a Traffic Advisory of any kind on the display (no open white diamond) and had not been advised of traffic from ATC. One second later ATC told us we had 'glider traffic' ahead and to 'deviate as necessary' to avoid the traffic. I immediately began a right hand descending turn. About two seconds later we received a Resolution Advisory RA to descend and the First Officer told ATC we were responding to an RA. At about 4500 feet; I saw the glider slightly higher and to our left. Once clear of the conflict; ATC cleared us to descend to 4000 feet and stated he was not in contact with the glider and asked if it was clear of clouds. ATC warned aircraft behind us on the arrival about the glider and it's altitude. We estimated the distance from the glider was 150-200 feet as it passed to our left. The event happened in the proximity of Channahon; IL Glider Club (IL59) south of STKNY on the arrival.The takeaway from this event:The TCAS did not operate as we would expect in time regarding a TA caution providing 40 seconds warning and an RA providing 25 seconds warning. It's hard to tell if the glider had just turned on its transponder or if the slow speed of the glider had an impact on our TCAS. The glider was in Class E airspace and outside the 30 mile equipment requirement (transponder) from the ORD Class B. What rules apply to gliders here; to me; is unknown to be honest.ATC had vectored us off the arrival and was assigning altitudes lower than published crossing restrictions by 1000 feet (5000 feet abeam STKNY).Vectors took us almost directly over a glider airpark with no warning about that on any chart we would ever use readily available. Some warning symbology or quick access information would assist considering the low altitudes we fly over these areas.In hindsight; I would have made clear to ATC we experienced a near mid air collision in that language as described in our FOM.

column121: Approach control cleared us for visual approach to [Runway] 14R.  I turned right; towards ZATAR; the final approach fix; and started descending from 4;000 to the final approach fix altitude; 2;400.  We observed the 2;500-foot tower and passed several miles to the north of it.  The tower is approximately 5 nm east of OMA.The transition from the Pawnee City arrival to a visual approach to 14R places the aircraft in a position where the tower is difficult to locate and is in a location where the pilot will naturally be descending towards.  Our Jepp pages do not highlight this as a potential problem.Our Jepp page that shows how the tower interferes with a right traffic pattern to 14R.  The tower is at a normal traffic pattern altitude where a downwind to base turn would be made.  When arriving on the Pawnee City arrival; and landing 14R; a preferred route around the tower for a visual landing should be shown.   Also; approach control could vector the airplane safely North of the tower before giving the clearance for the approach.

column121: There is a group of towers on right downwind/base to 14R and 14L that are a potential hazard when visual approaches are in use. As shown on the approach plates; at least one of these towers is 148 feet higher than the glide slope intercept altitude for these runways. Although the VFR traffic pattern for both runways is left; the PAWNEE CITY 1 and HOWRY 2 arrivals set IFR traffic up for a righthand pattern with the towers situated between them and final. Unlike the approaches; these towers are not charted on either arrival.When flying a righthand traffic pattern; such as from PAWNEE CITY 1; HOWRY 2; or on a visual missed approach; pilots are susceptible to colliding with the 2;548 feet towers while attaining the glide slope intercept altitude of 2;400 feet.1) Add a page in Jeppesen for OMA which calls attention to the towers. 2) Provide easier access to traffic pattern altitudes and directions in Jepp in the event of a visual missed approach. As of right now; this information is buried in a 4;000 plus page document. 3) Obstacles and terrain that are considered significant to approaches should also be charted on arrivals. 4) Pilots should thoroughly review significant obstacles and terrain depicted on procedures and enable ownship on Approach in Jeppesen for increased situational awareness. 5) ATC should ensure pilots see these particular towers; not just the airport; when clearing pilots for the visual from the west. Alternatively; pilots should be vectored well clear of the towers before being issued a visual.

column121: Winds were calm at ROA.  ATIS called Runway 24 as landing and departing using RNAV (RNP) Z RWY 24.  ATC asked us if we wanted the visual.  Given terrain and having never been to ROA I elected to stay with the RNAV approach.   ATC had us cross STEFN at 5;400 feet which had us 400 ft high.  We attained VNAV PATH and 150 KIAS approximately 3 miles after STEFN.   Since we could see the terrain and visibility was greater than 5 miles we planned to maintain 150 KIAS until 5 miles prior to the airfield.   Between DBERD and WILHE there is depicted terrain with a spot elevation 2;058 feet.  Just pass that terrain; still on LNAV and VNAV PATH at 150 KIAS Roanoke Tower gave us a low altitude warning. We verified both our altimeter settings and altitude.  We advised Tower that we could see both terrain and the airfield.  Tower acknowledged and advised us that they could see us clearly; and that they were required to announce the low altitude alert anytime it is activated.  Otherwise; the approach and landing were normal.  Of note there was no GPWS alert with flaps 20 and gear up.  After block-in; I requested a phone number to discuss the event.I spoke with [ATC] and inquired about the altitude alert call.  He indicated they sometimes get an altitude alert activation; but usually on vectors to visual Runway 24. He also indicated he was not as familiar with the Tower sector.   I asked him to look into it a bit more given that the only difference between what I flew and what I would have flown in IMC is I would have been 25 KIAS slower which would have resulted in a descent rate difference of just 133 FPM given a constant wind.  The wind in this case was a light headwind during the approach.Had I been in IMC and/or could not immediately determine by visual reference terrain clearance; the approach would have terminated with an immediate climb and terrain escape maneuver.Recommend the altitude alert system be de-conflicted to ensure aircraft on FMS glide path for the RNAV Z RWY 24 do not get erroneous alerts and/or note on company chart outlining requirements for speed on the approach or other requirements to successfully complete the approach in IMC.No deviations noted by Tower or aircrew.  The low altitude alert system may need to be adjusted or approach procedures may need to be adjusted to avoid erroneous low attitude warnings.De-conflict low altitude warning with proper approach lateral and vertical guidance.

column121: I was making a precautionary return to the airport after experiencing a decrease in fuel pressure on my left engine. In the descent phase to ZZZ the left engine began running worse and worse as I descended. I activated low boost on the left engine to keep it running. I was able to keep the engine running until landing by a combination of low and high boost and manipulating the throttle. On roll out; my right engine idle was low and in an attempt to keep it running I hit low boost and pushed the throttle up; but it died anyway. Managing asymmetric thrust and keeping the left engine running so I could clear the active runway and not shut the airport down; took my full attention. I did not turn the little boost off of the right engine until I had cleared the runway and by then the system was pressure charged and porting fuel overboard.In retrospect I should have feathered and secured the left engine and concentrated on landing and clearing the runway. This would have reduced my workload.

column121: ORD was on a very busy east flow arrival push.  The weather was severe VFR and there were numerous aircraft on 1200 codes flying underneath the ORD finals.  I was working the north final.  A VFR aircraft who I believe departed ZZZ1 climbed up to 3900 feet which is 100 feet below the base of the Class B.  I did not see the aircraft approaching my final because of all of the overlapping data blocks from the south two finals.  When the conflict alarm sounded; I saw the traffic and issued a traffic alert to Aircraft X who climbed 800 feet to avoid a collision.The Class B airspace around ORD does not match the current arrival/departure procedures.  On East Flow; the low-side runway is required to turn final at 4000 feet in accordance with the facility Standard Operating Procedure.  The floor of the Class B airspace is 4000 feet; so there are constantly conflicts with VFR aircraft climbing up to 3500 feet or higher in that area.  When doing simultaneous approaches; there is nowhere to go when there is a conflict. The floor of the Class B airspace west of ORD should be 3;500 feet until 25 miles west of ORD.

column121: The Autopilot A was engaged. The RNAV ZZZZZ3 Arrival and the RNPZ XXL were programmed in the FMC. The  approach was not connected at ZZZZZ; as we were not cleared for the approach.  Approximately five miles prior to ZZZZZ1 [fix] we were told to expect vectors for the ILS. We selected the ILS XXL in the FMC; and upon executing the selection; we received an Autopilot Disconnect message; and an A/P Disconnect Caution light. The aircraft went into Control Wheel Pitch. I; the Pilot Flying; immediately selected Vertical Speed to keep the aircraft in a state towards the impending crossing restriction. I also reset the Altitude Alerter for X;000 feet; as we now were in Vertical Speed and needed altitude protection at ZZZZZ2 [fix].A few seconds later we passed over ZZZZZ2. The Autopilot Disconnect Caution light illuminated; and the Autopilot Lateral Mode switched to Control Wheel Steering; and the aircraft didn't turn and follow the still illuminated and displayed LNAV PATH. I immediately engaged heading mode; and turned to rejoin our intended course. Approach gave us a heading; as the minor deviation from our path was most likely noticeable.  The rest of the flight proceeded uneventfully.  We assumed we programmed the FMC incorrectly; in some way; and we needed to be more vigilant.  ZZZ to ZZZ1 [airport]; we were on the ZZZZZ2 RNAV; Autopilot B engaged; on PATH. We were given an intercept heading to join final for [Runway] XX. Heading mode was selected. The aircraft flew heading and was in VNAV. The final approach course was entered into the FMC; and upon executing; the Autopilot B Vertical mode disengaged; with the A/P Caution light and the aircraft descended in Control Wheel Pitch.  We selected a pitch mode and proceeded without further incident.My concern:  I don't know why these disconnects happened. If they are normal for the -800 MAX; I am unaware of this. I thought all of our fleet types were programmed and responded to Pilot programming the same; in this scenario.  I've never experienced [other model] aircraft revert to CWS Pitch or roll; even with faulty Pilot programming.  We both assumed that perhaps; since neither of us had flown a MAX in a few years that we missed some training; or company notice.  We assumed that the error was a Pilot programming error.   I called the Chief Pilot on Call and told him of the occurrence the first time; and he was unaware of such a thing happening normally. We confessed that we actually had more experience than him in the MAX. He indicated I should call later and talk to the MAX programming expert and he perhaps could explain why the MAX behaves in this way. That is why we flew the aircraft a second leg with the anomaly; I assumed it was Pilot programming error.  A few days later; I was contacted by a Chief Pilot; who informed me that our experience was a malfunction; and that a logbook entry and maintenance resolution should have taken place.  I assumed it was a MAX anomaly; but it was a malfunction.

column121: IOE FO (First Officer) was performing a visual approach into RNO Runway 16L; backed up with RNAV X 16L. We were outside BADPE and were visual with the runway; check airman was monitoring FO and said 'You are too low; shallow out your descent rate.' FO proceeded to do so but not aggressively enough of a correction; Check airman was about to correct him again and we got a single EGPWS aural of 'OBSTACLE.' Under guidance of check airman; FO disconnected autopilot; added power; and climbed a few hundred feet. We were then back on profile and still outside final approach fix of BADPE so continued on approach with FO hand flying. The EGPWS Aural occurred at about 6;500 feet altitude about 4 miles outside of the BADPE fix. Check airman believes terrain clearance was safe; we were observing one white light and three red lights on PAPI; but the EGPWS disagreed with me. We were too low on a visual approach. Check airman had asked for a correction but was not insistent or urgent enough to get through to the FO. Busy arrival with many configuration changes; radio calls; etc. And it was a first day of flight with this particular FO; check airman did not realize that this FO was perhaps a bit overloaded and behind the aircraft; may have overestimated his skill level at this time.Check airman (Me) should have been more insistent about tolerances; as in not accepting one little bit low per arrival altitude guidance on RNAV plate.

column121: While descending into ZZZ; my student elected to make a midfield entry into the downwind. We briefed the approach and announced our position on CTAF at about 4 miles outside of the airport. Following our call; another aircraft asked our location in relation to the airport. We attempted to reply; but severe radio congestion prevented a response. The student and I began scanning for traffic; but it appeared that no other aircraft were in our area. Just after crossing midfield; we began our turn to downwind when the previous aircraft announced a midfield downwind. We spotted the traffic 100 to 150 feet below us and passing underneath us to our left. We took evasive action and initiated a climb; then rejoined a normal downwind behind the aircraft. No evasive action was taken by the other aircraft and it seemed he was not aware of the near miss. The rest of the flight occurred without incident.I believe the cause of the near miss was a result of extreme frequency congestion in the area. Communication in the area was encumbered by heavy usage and several radio calls we made were blocked due to other transmissions; leading to confusion over aircraft positions at the airport we were using. A review of the chart showed a large number of airports with 50NM operating on XYY.Z and several of those airports were surrounding the busy nearby metro areas. My recommendation would be to evaluate the airport usage of that area and assign unique frequencies several busier airports in the area.

column121: I was not present when the event occurred. I am the instructor who signed off this student for solo. The winds were calm; the student was proficient; and to my knowledge; the aircraft was well maintained when I provided instruction to him the previous day. The following is what the student told me. After touch down; the student attempted to make an early exit; and allowed the stick to return to neutral and applied brakes. This caused the tail wheel to lift off the ground and he lost control of the aircraft. He told me that his wingtip touched the ground and there may have been damage to one of the wings. He stated that he will have the aircraft manufacturer or other qualified mechanic inspect; repair and return the aircraft to service. To my understanding; the cause of the event was the student's failure to keep the stick in the full aft position; combined with excessive braking. This appears to be the result of a momentary lapse of judgement on his part. The student was highly proficient in all procedures involving taxi; takeoff and landing of the aircraft. This error was not made on any recent instructional flights. Following the procedures taught to him by his instructors (keeping the stick full aft; minimizing brake usage; and taking the next exit) would have prevented the event from occurring.

column121: We have been made aware of the PKWA (Bucholz Army Airfield Kwajalein Island) runway being shortened from 6;668 feet to a new LDA (Landing Distance Available); ASDA (Accelerate-Stop Distance Available); TORA (Takeoff Run Available); and TODA (Takeoff Distance Available) to 6;058 feet. This is due to what has been called a 'displaced threshold' on both ends of the runway. However; I was expecting pavement from the start of the original Runway 6 with chevrons or similar markings as per the AIM. However; what I saw on final was the first 400 feet of Runway 6 is now coral; gravel; and dirt...not pavement. It is not a displaced threshold; the runway now actually starts 400 feet further down the island. There are temporary lights placed on the pavement to indicate the starting edge of the new runway edge. The approach end of Runway 24 has been displaced 140 feet and still has pavement that is repainted with a temporary threshold and is marked with temporary lighting as well. The performance on the Takeoff Data Message has been adjusted correctly. The PAPI are out of service as well to ensure you are not guided into an early landing as well. Don't expect a displaced threshold on Runway 6 per the NOTAM; expect a new start to the runway surface.

column121: Night VFR RNAV Y XXR approach. Approaching FAF ZZZ tower advised us of Cessna  traffic on downwind turning base for short parallel runway XXL. Traffic in sight. As we passed our FAF; fully configured and stable we saw the Cessna pointing right at us on base and both remarked 'I hope they don't overshoot'. At approximately 800 feet AGL the Cessna was still pointing at us and I could see they were clearly going to overshoot slightly above us as they turned to final. I felt it was way too close and pushed over slightly as I estimated them to be no more than 100-200 feet above us as they crossed right over us and slightly behind. As our approach was now unstable in my estimation I executed an uneventful go-around. We advised tower of the close pass. We returned for a second; uneventful approach and landing to XXR. I called tower controller after landing and he said he would contact the Cessna pilot and talk to him about the incident. Cessna not keeping us in sight and possibly lining up on incorrect runway. Light VFR traffic maintaining better situational awareness with a large air carrier with approximately XXX people on board and all landing lights; fully configured on final approach.

column121: At AVL we had been vectored across final and then back around to the final approach course; near the FREEZ intersection. Conditions were completely clear with great visibility. We had briefed the airport qualification pages; and had talked about the terrain and possibility of GPWS alerts. We were level at 5;100 feet; on autopilot; and received an audible 'terrain; pull up' and had a red 'gnd prox' appear on the PFD. I disengaged the autopilot and began to execute the terrain escape maneuver; and the warning disappeared almost immediately. I had climbed to 5;300 ft during the maneuver. We contacted ATC to notify of the alert and altitude deviation; and the controller replied that the minimum vectoring altitude in that area was 5;100 feet. I slowly returned to 5;100 ft and we continued being vectored onto the visual approach without further issue. Marginal distance from terrain set off the GPWS. It appears that the MVA takes us within 951 feet of the highest obstacle in the area; a small radio tower just north of FREEZ. Executed terrain escape maneuver; per company procedures. It appears the MVA of 5;100 may be insufficient in that area; recommend at least 5;200 feet.

column121: Coming into IAD the errors started when we were intercepting the localizer for Runway 1R. I was the pilot monitoring and the FO (First Officer) was the pilot flying. Approach cleared us to intercept when we were basically already crossing the final approach course so we had to navigate back to the localizer on our own. At the same time we had been getting hit with some minor wake turbulence from the airplane ahead of us which Approach informed us was a Boeing 777. We were then cleared for the visual approach and advised of the caution for wake turbulence and to maintain 170 kts. until 5 miles out. At this time we configured gear down flaps 20. I had just recently had training in the sim for wake turbulence and remembered how dangerous it could be so I advised my FO to use caution and I believe I recommended maybe staying above their path since we had already hit a small portion of their wake prior when we were on downwind. He elected to turn the autopilot off and hand fly slightly above their glide path. We then continued down and around 100 ft. got a terrain warning. Our first reaction was this was because we weren't on glide path and the FO called out; 'visual; landing.' around 50 ft. we got another aural message stating 'too low terrain'; at this point I realized something was wrong and quickly scanned and noticed we were still at flaps 20. At this point I immediately called for a balked landing. The FO restated in a question; balked landing? I said yes and he immediately executed the maneuver. We advised ATC of the go around and they asked for the reasoning and I stated we were unstable. We continued the go around and came back to land on [Runway] 1R after some vectoring without further incident. To add to this and to summarize; we did not complete a landing checklist; we were not fully configured and were at gear down flaps 20 degrees; and our speed bug was still at 170 kts. We received a terrain aural warning that we misinterpreted followed by a second aural warning and executed a balked landing at a medium energy state with lower power setting but high airspeed. Our attention started deviating from standard starting with the poor intercept angle of the localizer. I further complicated the situation by over concentrating on the wake turbulence from the 777 and focusing too much on the FO's glide path and what I would call tunnel focusing on his flying. I believe my statements to him about the wake turbulence also led to distracting him from normal flight operations as well. He was talking through his thought process on staying a half dot high on the glide path and I was responding with statements like I agree; not necessarily breaking sterile cockpit but also causing more distraction on both of our parts. This was the first time that I had not completed a landing checklist. It has made me realize that while being aware of threats like wake turbulence is important; it's even more important to maintain a greater situational awareness and take a step back and not tunnel focus on one threat while other threats could arise. While I am glad we did not try to force a landing. This is a mistake I never thought would be possible or would happen to me. I have definitely learned a lot from this experience.
column125: CRJ-200 Captain reported distractions from possible wake turbulence resulted in failure to fully configure for landing.

column121: Enroute from ZZZ to ZZZ1 noticed oil pressure in right engine had dropped to the low end of the green operating arc and noticed a gradual power loss. Due to this issue I decided to divert from my flight plan and land to evaluate potential oil leak. Chose to divert to ZZZ2 as it had a long enough runway; listed to have maintenance services; and other facilities on the field. Notified ATC of plan to divert and location. ATC approved direct to ZZZ2. Enroute to ZZZ2 oil pressure and engine power on the right engine had diminished significantly. Notified ATC of situation and advised ATC. I was cleared for the straight in visual approach to runway 7. As I switched frequencies from approach to tower the engine began to sputter and the oil gauge was showing in the red arc. I declared to tower that I am shutting down the right engine. Tower cleared me to land. Airplane landed safely and taxied to the FBO. No damage was caused and no injuries were sustained.

column121: I was cleared to land. As I came over the end of the runway; I was getting some weird wind shear that was pushing me around; but my approach felt controlled and so I went through with it. Right before I touched down; I got a strong crosswind gust of wind (from the left) that pushed my super cub sideways (nose pointing to the left) as I touched down. Being a tail dragger; the plane responded accordingly; and I began to skid sideways down the runway. I was already down and too sideways for a go-around; so I pulled the throttle all the way back; stick back and left; and rudder full right. The plane continued to skid to a stop sideways on the runway; but all three wheels remained on the runway at all times; and no damage to the runway or aircraft occurred. After coming to a complete stop; I proceeded to turn the plane around to the left; leaving the asphalt for a brief time on the left side of the runway and taxing to the exit ramp on the other side where the Tower then informed me to stop; and I was later met by a foam truck and a service vehicle. I believe that the main factors in this were the wind gusts from the side; which in turn caused me to land sideways. I could have performed a go-around; though in the moment my approach felt controlled up until the last minute. I believe that I took the correct actions on the ground; and in so doing prevented any further damage to the aircraft or to airport property. My plane did not complete a full ground loop; but rather only turned about 90-120 degrees; and the rest of the 'loop' was me taxiing out of my predicament back on to the runway.

During the observed portion of flight SR22 broke no altitude restrictions however turning off ADS-B broadcast compromised flight safety in this sparsely populated area.FAA should remind pilots that ADS-B is for safety not for enforcement; and is especially effective in avoiding midair collisions.
