column121: My student and I departed on a training flight to the southeast practice area where we were going to run through Private Pilot maneuvers (stalls; steep turns; engine out; ground maneuvers and landings). After climbing to 035 and completing steep turns; we set up to do power on stalls and climbed to 050. We then set up to do power off stalls. After completing 4 power off stalls; we still had significant altitude and I decided to give student an engine out by pulling the mixture. The student set up the aircraft for best glide and selected an airfield that was about three miles away and ran through the restart checklist. Every 1500 feet I would advance the mixture and push forward on the yoke to make sure the engine was staying primed. We then simulated part of the forced landing checklist (ELT; squawk codes; emergency calls) and performed the landing brief.The student chose to try to get to the airfield by going down the downwind then realized that student was too low and close to the runway to safely make it to the field. I asked student what the backup plan was and student selected a field to right of the aircraft that student could easily turn into. Student turned the aircraft to the field and I estimated that student could easily make the landing and that student had the engine back and to do a go-around. I advanced the mixture and the engine sputtered and died and the prop stopped. The student announced that I had the controls and I pointed the airplane to the field that student had selected. I checked the mixture in; shut off valve on; then attempted to restart the engine with the key. After two attempts; I determined that we were too low and shut off the fuel valve and pulled the mixture and landed the plane in the field.There was no damage to the plane; no injuries to myself or my student; and no property damage. Fuel levels were still visible and greater than half way from the top of the tanks (well over 1/2 full; I didn't have a fuel stick to check total actual levels); oil level was still at 5 qts. We were able to later restart the airplane and taxi it closer to the property owner's barn with permission.The biggest thing that I learned was that I will not pull the mixture to simulate an engine out any more. That was the mistake that I feel caused this. In the future; all engine outs will be initiated by pulling the throttle. Though this has been common in my training from private to commercial; it put us in a dangerous situation that I could have avoided.
column123: At the altitude of 4;000 feet my Instructor pulled the mixture in the aircraft to simulate an emergency landing. At that moment I trimmed the plane all the way; pulled the carb heat and pitched the airplane for 60 kts. Next I looked for a place we could simulate the landing and we were just 2-4 miles from [an] airport. We were descending approximately 500 feet per minute. The closer we got I realized that I was too low to make the obstacles. I made the error of not looking at the altitude of the field. Therefore; I looked to my right and there was a large field and I pointed to my instructor that at that moment the safest decision was to do the field on the right. My instructor said that was a good decision and I started my turn to the field.My airspeed was 60; my right hand was on the throttle; which at the time it was all in. My left hand on the controls and my feet on the rudder. A few seconds later my Instructor looked at me and said 'you would have made it' and he decided to end the simulation by putting the mixture all in. However; the engine would not work and my instructor said 'my controls' I restated 'your controls' and he took charge of the plane. He tried turning the engine on a couple times and the propeller would just not move at all. It happened in a matter of seconds. Since I had already lined up the airplane to the field; my Instructor worked hard to fly the airplane until it touched the ground where he applied the brakes after he had landed it safely. We turned all the systems off and removed the key from the starter. We opened the doors and got out of the aircraft. We called the flight school and they came to get us and to see the airplane.

column121: While landing on Runway XX at approximately 70 KIAS and power pulled to idle; after touchdown; plane started to veer to the left. Plane ultimately veered off of runway onto grass; where it came to a near-complete stop. Power was added to keep the plane moving in order to avoid getting stuck in the grass. Tower asked if we were OKAY; which I responded in the affirmative.Tower instructed us to re-enter [Runway] XX; and taxi to the T-hangars (with runway crossing and holding instructions included). We inspected the airplane and there was no apparent damage to the plane; just grass stains on the wheel pants; which we cleaned up. The airport operations reported there was no damage to anything on the runway environment. And there were no injuries to me (PIC) or my safety pilot passenger.The airplane was inspected the following day and was found to be in good operating condition and has been successfully flown since; with no reported issues.I believe the cause was my feet inadvertently slipped up the pedals and activated the toe brakes after landing; with the left side braking more strongly. There were some tire marks on the runway that seem to support this; according to the ground ops personnel.I plan to go up with a CFII as soon as possible; paying special attention to my foot position on takeoffs and landings. I may also fly with the seat farther back to make it less likely that my toes will accidentally be on the brakes.

column121: While on ILS final to Runway XL under visual conditions; the right engine failed. Landing with the left engine only was accomplished after advising ZZZ tower. Aircraft was returning from ZZZ1 with X passengers aboard. It was thought that a fuel pump had failed; but further investigation revealed that I as the PIC had misread my fuel gauge. I had thought that the right fuel quantity was off by 300 pounds because I had flown this aircraft several times before and was going to report the discrepancy to maintenance.On the previous flights; I would refuel the amount of fuel plus reserve I needed for the next flight evenly distributing the fuel load. However; I did not take into account that I would start the right engine first to get air condition operating for the passengers onboard then program the Garmin 1000 for the flight.  I forgot to take into account the extra fuel burned during this procedure; thus producing a cumulative effect of showing a fuel imbalance of close to 300 pounds which I would report to maintenance after this flight. I thought the engine stoppage was the result of a fuel pump failure; instead was probably due to fuel starvation due to the cumulative effect of my failure to account for the additional fuel used during start up and a misguided conclusion that the right fuel gauge was inaccurate.

column121: I was conducting a training flight on Airman Certification Standards (ACS) maneuvers to prepare a student for the practical test to be taken within 4 days.  Initially; we conducted normal; short field; and soft field takeoffs and landings without incident.  Then; we departed to the practice area and practiced unusual attitude recoveries; steep turns; slow flight; approach stalls; and departure stalls from 3;000 feet MSL.  That complete; I directed the student to return to the home airport.  On downwind entry; we were 500 feet above pattern altitude.  I considered this a good opportunity to give my student a simulated engine failure and have him continue to the runway.  On downwind; we were at an altitude lower than the student felt comfortable.  He stated that he didn't feel we could make it.  However; to demonstrate that the landing could still be made if the conditions (engine out) were real; I took the controls made a short approach (a banked continuous base to final turn) and lined the aircraft up with the runway in a landing attitude. Unfortunately; during the maneuver I became obsessed with monitoring our airspeed for proper glide speed and maneuvering during the turn at the neglect of ensuring the landing gear was extended.  We landed with the gear retracted. The flare (round out) was at normal altitude and attitude. Overall; it was not a bad landing.  The student caught the error prior to me when we heard scraping.  The aircraft slid and eventually slid off the runway to a point between the runway and taxiway.One human performance consideration may have been a factor.  I have been conducting training in both straight gear and retractable gear C-172s switching between the two on the same day.  That was the case on this day.  I had flown 0.7 [hours] practicing landings with one student in a fixed gear 172 in the morning; driven to a different airport; and flew in this student's retractable 172 in the afternoon. My mindset was fixated on making the landing and not on the differences between the two aircraft.
column125: Flight Instructor reported a gear-up landing occurred during a training flight. Instructor stated they had flown a fixed gear aircraft earlier and had not focused on the differences with flying a retractable gear aircraft.

column121: We had been cleared the ILS 25L Approach while on the ANJLL Arrival and assigned 250 knots. At about 12;000 feet; we got a call from the cabin that the passenger we had called STAT-MD for earlier in the flight; was feeling worse. I asked the First Officer to call OPS for medical assistance to meet us at the gate. When he returned back to the ATC frequency; we were approaching 10;000 feet and hit wake turbulence sufficiently strong to cause the airplane to go into control wheel pitch and roll. I redirected the aircraft to KRAIN; re-engaged LNAV and VNAV; and asked ATC what we were following.  I was told we were following nothing. This didn't make sense since we had just hit very strong wake turbulence. While established on final; ATC told us to slow to 170 knots. This surprised both of us; because the last speed assignment we recalled being given was 250 knots. 170 knots would be a huge speed reduction and we were still about 25 miles out. ATC then asked us if we were at 210 knots. We told the Controller no; and that we had been assigned 250 knots. He told us we had been instructed to slow to 210 knots back when we complained about the wake turbulence (approaching 10;000 feet). We were now overtaking a B777 ahead; so our approach clearance was canceled. We were given vectors for spacing and vectored back onto the localizer and then re-cleared the ILS 25L. Neither the First Officer no I recall hearing the 210 knot speed assignment. We had only heard 250 knots and read that back. The 210-knot speed assignment probably occurred about the time the Flight Attendant called us about the passenger feeling ill again and just as we hit the wake turbulence.
column123: A bunch of things happened at once which led to us missing a slower speed assignment. On the arrival to LAX; cleared the approach to 25L and told to maintain 250 knots. The flight attendant called up and said that our previous medical issue in the back (we had earlier talked to STAT-MD and gotten clearance to continue) was not feeling well again; and asked for paramedics. As the Pilot Monitoring; I called OPS at LAX; to arrange for emergency personnel - meaning I was off frequency. At the same time; we encountered wake turbulence; strong enough to knock the aircraft out of both LNAV/VNAV. The Captain; Pilot Flying; reported the wake turbulence to Approach; after getting the aircraft established on profile; apparently ATC told us to slow to 210 knots. Neither of us heard or read back the speed change. A few miles later Approach canceled our approach clearance and said we were too fast. We responded that we were assigned 250. They said no; we were given 210.
column125: B737-700 flight crew reported missing an ATC speed assignment on arrival into LAX when they were distracted by a wake turbulence encounter in trail of a heavy B777.

column121: I am a little rusty because I haven't flown much over the past 12 months.  We had a tailwind on final; but as ZZZ frequently does; they asked us to keep our speed up and maintain 180 knots until ZZZZZ (the final approach fix on XL ILS).  I had configured to flaps 10 and gear down before ZZZZZ.  After we crossed ZZZZZ I finished configuring but the plane did not want to slow down after flaps 25; possibly because we hit a gust or went through a shear zone.  I was so distracted by the amount of difficulty I was having getting the plane to slow down so I could call for flaps 30 that I didn't realize we were lowering the flaps to 30 just as we passed through 1;000 feet.The tailwind on final;  ZZZ's request for us to keep our speed up; my relative lack of proficiency and my not paying close enough attention to our altitude were the causes for my error.I will start configuring even earlier when contending with a tailwind on final.
column123: After fully configuring the aircraft for landing; I realized that we were below 1;000 feet.A tail wind; ATC assigning 210 knots on final; and a lack of recent experience all combined played a role in my late recognition of configuring below 1;000 feet.Configuring the aircraft earlier; and paying closer attention to procedures.

column121: I am a Private Oilot who currently lives in ZZZ; and I was in ZZZ1 for the weekend. I am currently working on my instrument rating; and I do not have a complex endorsement. I was looking to book a plane for that day; and I decided that it would be best to look at renting a complex aircraft and hiring a CFI as PIC so I could get my first lesson in flying complex. This way; the time would count towards my 10 hour requirement for complex or TAA aircraft for my commercial license; which I plan on getting after my instrument rating. I knew that I would be required to hire a CFI as PIC since I do not yet have my complex endorsement. We arrived on the morning of Date and took off from ZZZ2 at roughly XA:00am after a preflight with the CFI; who is employed by Company. The lesson took place in the school's 172RG. Our flight consisted of an intro complex lesson where we flew down to ZZZ3 for a touch and go. Following this; we flew back to ZZZ2 and prepared for landing. We were cleared for Runway XR by Tower and made a straight in approach. The before landing checklist was completed; and we extended the landing gear as required. We noted that the light on the landing gear instrument had turned green as we were on approach; indicating that the landing gear had been deployed. There was a crosswind out of 070 with winds at 17kts; gust 22kts as reported by Tower. We performed a side slip to correct for the crosswind; and approached the runway at normal speed. The side slip was performed well and the airplane touched down straight and in the middle of the runway at appropriate touchdown speed. Once the main wheels touched down; the plane started to wobble on the left side; and it became clear that the left main landing gear had not locked in place. The left landing gear then folded back into the plane during the rollout. By this time our ground speed had slowed considerably; but the plane was supported partially by the left fuselage. While the main right gear and front gear had deployed; the main left gear was now back inside the plane; and the aircraft veered off to the left and into the grass. Informed Tower; and the airport closed the runway and deployed emergency services to come meet us.Nobody was hurt during the event; and we safely exited the aircraft where we waited for the airport personnel. I did not notice any damage to the airport; and no runway lights were hit. The only damage to the plane was minor; and from what I could see it was limited to the landing gear and possibly the left wingtip; which was touching the ground when the plane came to a stop to the left of the runway. The engine; prop; and the rest of the plane seemed undamaged.The airport informed the FAA of the event; and eventually I was dismissed after giving the airport workers my pilot certificate. I made a quick call to inspector. We briefly spoke; and I gave a brief oral statement. In my opinion this was a clear mechanical failure and was not due to pilot error. Also; this event does not fit the definition of an accident or reportable incident to the NTSB; per NTSB part 830.
column123: Take off at ZZZ2 at XA:40 AM local time Eastbound after clear ZZZ2 Delta airspace; right turn southbound direct to ZZZ3 at 1;500 feet MSL.  Joined left downwind runway seven and performed one touch and go; no issues. After the touch and go; we flew out of the traffic pattern to the west and proceeded directly over the bay towards ZZZ2 at 1;500 feet MSL. At mile 10 southwest from ZZZ2 we proceed to check with ZZZ2 Control Tower and be clear to join direct final Runway XR for a full stop. Around XC:46 we performed a normal landing. After touchdown we proceeded to press brake pedals and the left landing gear slowly collapsed rearward and up. Left wing tip touched the runway's surface around 40 knots and by that time directional control of the plane was lost even with use of opposite brake; aircraft slowly slid left towards the grass and off the runway and fully stopped on the left side of runway nine right. No hard Landing; no injuries; no propeller strike; no fire.

column121: For years I have seen student pilots overshoot final when landing on the parallel runway at Centennial airport. More then a few close conflicts have occurred during the landing phase while I was on final approach. The recent mid-air collision at that airport was overdue; and it will happen again. A staggered spacing controlled by the tower controller would make things much safer.  At least staggering small aircraft to turn base after passing traffic on final would end the inherent danger.

column121: On a coupled RNAV XX LPV approach to ZZZ the aircraft overshoot the turn to final. It started the turn but not fast enough to intercept. After correcting and intercepting final the aircraft began a descent as if following the glideslope. There was heavy rain between me and the airport and my attention was diverted to that and I lost situational awareness. It is obvious now that the descent was not following the glideslope when the terrain alert activated I climbed to a higher altitude and shortly broke out of the rain and landed in visual conditions. We were in contact with approach control until just before the turn to final; then switched to UNICOM. My mistakes were ones of complacency. When I had an abnormal turn to final I should have executed a missed approach. Something was not right. I allowed my attention to be diverted at a critical time from the task of monitoring the approach. I misinterpreted the weather situation. I used ASOS reports that may have been 5 - 10 minutes old in a rapidly changing weather situation. I should have requested the RNAV XY and accepted the tailwind even though the minimums were higher. The weather was moving west to east and conditions in the quadrant turned out to be much better.

column121: During TO out of ZZZ thrust levers anomaly- TO thrust setting as I remember was 84.6; however; when First Officer selected TOGA the thrust increased to over 90% N1 before reducing. Discussed with First Officer after climb out after verifying First Officer had as I expected acted properly (setting thrust) continued to ZZZ1. Executed the visual approach to Runway XX at less than 50 feet thrust lever(auto throttle) anomaly - executed go around. ATC cleared us to 2;000 - during the Go Around I feel it's safe to say we were in the yellow and mitigating any transition into the red by both actively flying/monitoring aircraft instrumentation and following SOPs and yes very deliberately. There was a surprise effect from the aircraft not acting as directed (thrust levers/ auto throttle) as the approach itself was stable. Then we were vectored in visual conditions back around for a second approach to Runway 31. After parking at the gate in ZZZ1 Maintenance was notified of the irregularities with thrust levers (auto throttle).The aircraft was removed from service by Maintenance.Thrust lever / auto throttle anomaly that added a startle effect and divided attention or required additional attention. We could have after the missed approach disconnected the A/T but felt better about closely monitoring/ guarding the thrust levers.
column123: On the take off from ZZZ when I pressed the TOGA button; the throttles rapidly advanced to near full forward; and then slowly retarded to the selected takeoff thrust as we were rolling down the runway.  The throttles came forward very rapidly and went further forward then I would expect.  We were set for a 22K takeoff with an assumed temperature reduction. After takeoff the Captain and I discussed the anomaly.  It was very unusual. The rest of the flight; up to the landing flare; was normal.  I was flying the new visual approach. The approach was loaded into the FMS. The autopilot was engaged. I think I remember disengaging the autopilot if the final turn to final.  In the flare; below 50 feet the throttles began to advance by themselves. I remember being right on speed prior to this. I felt the throttles move forward; and I remember trying to hold them back.  I think the autothrottles were attempting to go around.  I felt the aircraft 'ballooning' and I saw that I was now going to fast (I remember seeing 155 knots). At this point I said go around and pressed the go around button. We climbed out and tower gave us a visual downwind to return. The go around was a normal go around. After cleaning up; I went right back to flaps 1 for the downwind. I was trying to process what just happened and the Captain and I discussed it. We were both surprised by the auto throttle. While turning base; the auto throttles were doing a poor job of holding command bug. They were very slow to respond. I turned off my flight director and visually flew a visual pattern (I had no lateral guidance from the FMS; the approach had not been resequenced. I know the airport very well could see where I needed to go.  I left the auto throttles engaged but I had a firm hand on the throttles. While on base; the air speed got 5 knots slow and I manually pushed the throttles up to speed up. I completed a visual approach and normal flaps 40 landing with flight director off and auto throttles engaged. The auto throttles functioned normal after I had manually pushed them up; but in retrospect I should have disconnected them and flown manually. They were definitely suspect at this point. The flaps 40 approach and landing was normal and smooth. The auto throttles worked as they should.  We had a mechanic at ZZZ1 meet us and we wrote up what had happened. The next morning while eating breakfast; I was 'bothered' by what had happened and I called Name to discuss the anomaly.  We discussed the anomaly in detail. He didn't have any answers either; but said he would keep an eye on the aircraft; X. Additional note; the approach was stable prior to the auto throttle anomaly. We were following an [aircraft] that was going very slow. We were minimal space behind them so we were right on speed throughout the approach to flare. I believe the autopilot was engaged until the turn to final.I am not sure if this was a fault with the airplane auto throttle; a 737 Max anomaly or a coding issue with the new visual approach? Thinking about what had happened on the takeoff; I suspect something was wrong with the auto throttles. I am fairly new to the Max. [No suggestions]; however in retrospect I should have turned off the auto throttles when it became clear they were not working correctly after the go around.

column121: I departed with one passenger to ZZZ on an IFR flight plan. The flight was uneventful until on a 5-mile final when I heard the Tower clear a 'Heavy' cargo plane to take the runway for departure. As we got closer to the runway; we noticed that the heavy cargo plane was just getting off the runway as we were on short final. With traffic behind us in the pattern; I continued the approach opting to land slightly below the glideslope on the first third of the runway so as to avoid any potential wake turbulence put off by the departing aircraft beyond its rotation point. While in the landing phase of flight; approximately 1' off the ground and near stall speed for a normal landing; we encountered sudden turbulence that caused the plane to destabilize resulting in a brief touchdown followed by a balloon and a subsequent stall that caused the aircraft to bounce on the runway. As I saw we were losing directional control; I added full power to initiate the go-around maneuver; regained stability; and completed a left traffic pattern. We came in shortly after another Cirrus on short final and had a normal smooth landing. It was not until after taxiing to [FBO] and performing engine shutdown that I noticed the tips of the props were frayed as the obvious result of a prop strike on our initial attempt.It is my opinion that the cause of the turbulence encountered on the runway was the jet blast from the departing heavy jet rather than wake turbulence as we landed well before its point of rotation. While I do not have a copy of the Tower's audio; I do not recall any warnings from the Tower. Apparently; the 'heavy' jet took an extended amount of time to take the runway after being cleared and departed less than a minute before our touchdown. Since we could not see the plane delay taking the runway from a distance due to haze; the Tower should have warned us to avoid wake turbulence and jet blast during our approach. This would likely have prompted a decision to break off our approach at a safe altitude to allow the jet blast to dissipate; and follow trailing traffic to an uneventful landing.At 120 kts. approach speed; our ETA to land on a 5-mile final was approximately 2.5 minutes. Had the Tower held the 'heavy' cargo jet for the additional few minutes; substantial loss of property and the potential for loss of life could have been avoided. In hindsight; as PIC I could have made the decision to break off on short-final and abort the landing when I noticed the heavy cargo plane had departed so shortly before we were to land. I trusted the Tower to provide safe and adequate separation for our arrival and it could have cost us a lot more than a prop strike. I will not make that mistake again.

column121: Called landing intentions on CTAF frequency on approach to airport and prior to entering traffic pattern. No other traffic heard. On downwind leg; observed another aircraft nearby flying  approximately same heading but heard nothing on radio. I called my position and intentions on my radio but heard no response. I watched other aircraft continue on course and I landed after flying base and final legs of the pattern and calling my position on the radio. While taxiing on ground after landing; discovered volume of my radio was turned down. Turned volume up and heard the other aircraft calling landing intentions at the airport. They had apparently been flying the pattern too; and extended their downwind while I landed.Factors involved were my inadvertently turning volume down too far; and distraction of passengers (the radio was active with lots of faraway traffic and passenger asked question; so I turned radio volume down to hear better). As a side note; did not see a traffic alert on ADS-B (Automatic Dependent Surveillance - Broadcast); suggesting other aircraft was not ADS-B equipped.

column121: I was Pilot Flying.  The right thrust reverser was inoperative and disabled by maintenance; the left thrust reverser was available for use.  After touching down and derotating; I deployed the left thrust reverser.  When I attempted to move the thrust lever backward from idle reverse to increase reverse thrust I found that the thrust lever would not move and my hand position somehow felt different from usual.  In that same moment I realized that instead of lifting the thrust reverser unlock lever I had lifted the shutoff lock and moved the thrust lever into shutoff; shutting down the left engine.  I called out the problem to the Captain and told him I would be using only brakes to stop the aircraft.  There was plenty of runway remaining and heavy braking was not required.  We taxied to the gate using normal single engine taxi procedures.After performing several thousand landings in this aircraft I have a well developed muscle memory for engaging reverse thrust.  I also have a well developed muscle memory for shutting down the left engine during taxi.  With the right thrust reverser disabled; thrust reverser use required touching and moving only the left thrust lever instead of both thrust levers.  I am fairly certain that this interaction with only the left thrust lever subconsciously triggered the muscle memory motions for a left engine shutdown instead of those for activating the thrust reverser(s) which usually involves moving both thrust levers.  I had landed the same aircraft the day before with the same inoperative right thrust reverser and properly deployed only the left thrust reverser.  I have no other explanation for how I managed to perform the motions for an engine shutdown instead.I will be much more aware of this possibility for inadvertent incorrect hand motions possibly caused by muscle memory triggers when operating an aircraft with a deferred thrust reverser.
column125: First Officer reported during landing roll out; he shut off the left engine fuel lever instead of deploying the left thrust reverser and used wheel brakes only to slow the aircraft to a safe stop.

column121: Arriving in San Diego; we had been cleared for the RNAV Z Runway 27 approach. We checked in with the Tower and were cleared to land. I heard Tower instruct a [heavy jet] to; 'Line up and wait; traffic on an 8 mile final'. Checklists and callouts may have masked additional communications on the frequency because I can't recall whether the [heavy jet] flight was cleared for takeoff; or not. We broke out of IMC at approximately 1400 feet. The Pilot Monitoring called 'Runway in Sight - Landing'. Shortly thereafter; the First Officer said; 'Is that an airplane on the runway? I looked closer and honestly could not tell. The navigation and beacon lights were blending into the approach lights and it was difficult to distinguish the aircraft. We had to get closer before the actual structure of the airplane was apparent. We had already determined that a go-around/missed approach was necessary; but before we could communicate that to the Tower Controller; she issued the go-around instruction; 'Fly the published Missed Approach; and contact Departure'. I had observed that the offending aircraft was stationary on the runway; and not in the midst of a takeoff roll; so I knew that the published missed approach or runway heading was appropriate; and that a sidestep departure for spacing was not necessary. So we complied as instructed. We had already determined our BINGO fuel enroute and knew that we could accept one more approach before diverting to our alternate; so we accepted vectors back for another approach. The subsequent approach and landing was uneventful. The [heavy jet] had been removed from the runway and was still holding short when we landed. No mention of the reason for the go-around was given by the Tower; nor did we ask. Because we knew. There was an airplane on the runway.     Cause: [The] decision to work a Maintenance Irregularity while on an active runway. The Tower's decision to put the [heavy jet] on the runway when they were not ready to depart; and then forgetting about them. And our loss of situational awareness based on the radio communications that were; or were not; taking place on the Tower Frequency.In the future; if the First Officer questions the security of the runway; or the landing clearance; I will instruct them to simply; and without delay; to ask the Tower. This would NOT have prevented the necessity to go-around; but perhaps would have initiated the maneuver a little sooner."
column123: While on the RNAV Z 27; we were handed off to Tower and were cleared to land. This occurred at approximately SAYAE intersection. The approach continued normally and we broke out about 1;400 feet and then called 'runway in sight'; 'runway in sight landing'. Sometime after the 1;000 feet stabilized cleared to land call; I noticed what appeared to be an airplane on the displaced threshold of Runway 27 and said; 'Is that an airplane on the runway? The darkness of the displaced threshold area against the runway lights and the fact that the aircraft was in position pointed away from us made it difficult to tell. However; by the times the words were spoken; we were close enough to see. At the very same moment we began our go-around; the Tower issued instructions to go around and fly the published missed approach; which we did since the aircraft on the runway did not appear to be moving. We proceeded with a normal go-around and returned for a normal landing since we had sufficient fuel. In the simplest terms; we executed a normal go around due to an aircraft on the runway. Why was there an aircraft on the runway? I am not sure. Shortly after we were cleared to land; we heard the tower instruct [another aircraft] to line up and wait; traffic 8 mile final. I honestly don't recall hearing a take-off clearance; perhaps due our being engaged with our tasks and callouts during the approach. We did see the aircraft short of the runway when we returned. It wasn't until later that we read that they had a mechanical issue.Maintaining better awareness of traffic utilizing the runway particularly when we can't see it yet. Better awareness would have allowed us to question if the runway was indeed clear. That may not have prevented the go around this time; it would've depended on how soon we became aware."

column121: [Pilot] was flying her Aircraft X as Pilot in Command at the ZZZ airport; landing Runway XX. I was monitoring from the right seat. [Pilot] was cleared for the option on Runway XX by the ZZZ Tower from the left downwind of Runway XX approximately midfield. She initiated proper airspeed and flap configuration for a stabilized approach from pattern altitude. She applied correct airspeed; pitch; bank; and flap configurations while flying base and final approach. The aircraft maintained proper airspeed; pitch; and crosswind correction while on final maintaining the centerline of the runway as it flew a stabilized glide angle down to the runway. When transitioning into the landing phase; she began a flare and proper attitude to land the airplane. As the airplane was about to land; the attitude of the airplane changed suddenly in a way that was consistent with large rapid change in the characteristics of the wind; [Pilot] attempted to initiate a go-around with my immediate assistance. However; the plane lost directional control; veering to the right and eventually coming to a stop with a nose down attitude.

column121: I conducted a routine pre-flight of the aircraft and no anomalies were noted.  I requested that the aircraft be fueled; and that was done.  I also checked weather and NOTAMs for my solo flight.  I noted some wind indicated in the forecast - clear; unlimited visibility; wind variable 6 G 13; 30.01 altimeter - but well within aircraft and pilot limitations.  Actual weather at take-off was clear VFR and unremarkable.I then had a routine flight for approximately one hour focusing primarily on use of the Garmin avionics system.  I did an approach and landing which was uneventful.I returned to [home airport].  My initial approach was stabilized; but I was alert because of wind guests.  On touchdown; the aircraft became airborne after a wind gust. I executed a balked landing procedure as the aircraft settled on the runway and went around.On my second approach to land; the aircraft was again on a stabilized approach through flare and touchdown.  I was conducting a no-flaps approach because of the wind. After touchdown; there was an exceptional gust of wind which caused the aircraft to veer sharply to the left.  I immediately applied full right rudder and heavy braking to maintain control of the aircraft and avoid exiting the runway.  Despite my best efforts; the aircraft exited the runway.  I immediately released hard braking to avoid flipping the aircraft and focused on maintaining control; which I did.The aircraft came to rest adjacent to the intersecting taxi-way.  I could tell that the left tire was flat so I stopped the engine and exited the aircraft to inspect it and assess the situation.

column121: On the downwind I was in a Cessna Skyhawk with a student when Tower told us to look out for traffic joining the downwind on a 45º; it being a Piper Seminole.  We had them in sight; however it appeared they were on a continuous course direct to us. Tower clearly told the Seminole they were #2 following us to land however the course they took seemed to be as if they wanted to overtake us in the pattern. When I looked over seeing them directly abeam us; off our 3 o'clock while banking right; I knew they not only were unable to see us while banking. They were extremely close; 400 feet horizontal distance being a hopeful estimate of distance at that point. I requested a short approach to land from Tower to get away from the Seminole and operate the aircraft at a safer distance away from the Seminole.

column121: This was not a good flight because of compounding errors that happened and the negative effect that they had.  The good part is that the flight remained safe; but here below are several things that I learned.  The events started when on decent into ZZZ when the lights on the autopilot mode selector began to flicker erratically; however the autopilot appeared to perform normally.  This was in IMC descent thru layers with occasional moderate turbulence.  (The was second flight after the power supply to the EFIS system failed resulting in loss of the primary attitude indicator and HSI which was repaired and return to service.  I mention this only because it attributed to my increase lack of trust in the flight instruments.) The autopilot was on altitude preselect with a vertical speed selected.  During the decent I was evaluating the autopilot issue and I think; after the fact; I pushed the altitude capture twice; resulting in me disengaging the capture and descending below the desired altitude.  At the time I attributed this to a malfunction autopilot and I decided to disconnect the autopilot and manually flew the plane.  This increased the workload.  Weather was also a significant factor.  There was a line of thunderstorms that were moving over ZZZ (destination) and I was watching the satellite radar utilizing a portable GDL52 communicating to Garmin pilot on my I pad. For this flight I was using ForeFlight for navigation/charts and Garmin Pilot for weather; because the GDL would not communicate satellite radar information to ForeFlight.  I have been using ForeFlight for charts and weather in previous aircraft for many years; but this aircraft has only ADSB-out and is scheduled to have an avionics upgrade to a GDL69a (sat WX) and Garmin 345 (ADSB IN/Out) installation in Month.  I was also utilizing the onboard radar; but am learning to use this system.  Bottom line was too many nav / weather systems in use that resulted in unnecessary and additional workload. Another factor was that I had transitioned from a piston to a turboprop and had about 80 hours in the TBM time at the time of this flight. So I was still low on the TBM experience curve.   Based on the weather I requested GPS XX and activated the approach on the 530s. However; as I proceeded to ZZZ GPS XX; the weather changed and the IAF for GPS XX was near the line of thunderstorms and center offered ILS XY and left turn to heading for the IAF - I made a right turn to that heading.  I should have recognized that I was starting to make small mistakes. During this time I also was varying from assigned altitude due to increase workload.   I entered ILS XX in to the 530S and activated and Center vectored me to the approach.   I began the approach and was able to see the ground occasionally.  I was handed off the Tower and I waited to capture the glideslope. I continued the approach; my position and altitude were not cross checking; and I began try to determine why.  I was high when I was on a 2-3 mile final.  While making this evaluation; I was able to see the runway lights and able to make a visual decent and landing.  I was not confident after this flight; there were too many mistakes in this flight.  When I was on the ground I contacted my flight instructor and went over flight and discussed the avionics and my reaction/ decision I made.  We made a plan of how to determine the effect of the flickering light on the mode selector and insure that it was safe for flight.  I altered my plans so that weather would have a minimal impact and returned back to ZZZ1.  To learn and avoid these errors in the future; I did a full debrief with my instructor when I return home.  This focused on both the avionics and cross reference that he recommended to determine status of the navigation system.  We also spent a considerable time on how I responded to the apparent situation and decision making.  It was at this time; I realized how much some minor errors in my part had a large impact onthe flight.  The avionics have also been evaluated by Company in ZZZ2 and determine that the malfunction was limited to the mode selector display and that the autopilot is functioning normally. It is schedule to be repaired.   Further I have also flow approaches in VFR conditions to verify operations of the navigation and autopilot systems and apply some of the procedures that my instructor and I discussed.

column121: While doing pattern work on Runway 30L; we requested a full-stop on Runway 3. Tower instructed us to 'make left downwind.' My student and I turned left to enter the left downwind Runway 3; at this time; Aircraft Y traffic was mid-field downwind Runway 30L. Traffic was cleared touch-and-go Runway 30L. Without further instruction; my student and I proceeded with a standard left pattern Runway 3. On 0.25 nm final; I see the traffic landing the parallel is still rolling on the runway; and I did not anticipate them being clear of my runway by the time my student and I crossed the threshold Runway 3; so I instructed my student to execute a go-around. This resulted in our Aircraft X directly overflying the Aircraft Y right as they rolled across Runway 3 on Runway 30L. They were not yet airborne.In hindsight; I believe Tower's instruction to make left downwind may have been intended as extend downwind; or they would call my base. Since this instruction was not given; we made a normal pattern and did not anticipate a conflict until short final.Earlier on the same flight; we had been doing full-stop-taxi-backs; and we landed Runway 30L; and exited left onto Taxiway B. The same controller gave us taxi instructions 'Runway 30L taxi via Delta' and I asked if I needed 'cross Runway 3.' During this same flight; I had prompted the previous controller a couple of times; and gotten the instruction to cross each time; but only after prompting for it. The next controller volunteered; 'we don't normally do that.' She did give me the crossing; but I am wondering why we do not volunteer that crossing since it is necessary to cross the full-hold bar for Runway 3 in order to turn left onto Taxiway D from Taxiway B; or the other way to turn right onto Taxiway B from Taxiway D.

column121: While in route to Burlington Airport (BUY) descending from 11;000 feet to 4;000 feet and approximately 20 miles to the south of the field on a very clear VFR day; the Co-pilot and I both had the Burlington airport in sight and informed ATC (Greensboro Approach). We were then cleared for the visual approach for BUY and told to contact the advisory frequency 122.97; we then descended to 3;000; we were on an extended downwind for Runway 24 in BUY; I had spotted traffic off my left wing which was indicating 200 feet below our altitude. In an attempt to ensure there would not be a conflict with the traffic; I promptly climbed up to 4;000 momentarily until the conflicting traffic was no longer a factor. At that point descended back down to 3;000 feet and began our final approach into Burlington (BUY).

column121: During approach to landing at RNT; winds were light; 360 at 3kts. We chose to land on Runway 34; during our landing I noticed bright lights at the end of the runway heading towards us. I immediately turned the aircraft towards the nearest taxiway and made a radio call. I got no answer and Aircraft Y took off from Runway 16 above us apparently oblivious to the fact that we just landed on the opposite runway at the same time they departed. Tower was closed. I went back and listened to the ATC audio from the landing and you can hear Aircraft Y make a departure call that I didn't hear. Then you hear all my calls; downwind base and final. The Aircraft Y makes another radio call again that we did not receive then we make a call from the ground once I saw the Aircraft Y rolling towards us. The Aircraft Y started it's takeoff roll on Runway 16 as we landed on Runway 34. I checked the radio after and our radio is functional; able to transmit and receive.

column121: Runways 8/15 in use. Arriving Runway 8 and departing Runway 15.I was on CIC at the time of this event. I was monitoring the LC (Local Control) frequency as required; but there are often times when I'm monitoring the frequency and not hearing everything since I am performing other CIC duties such as phone calls; sector assignments; coordination with the airport authority about NOTAMs; weather briefings; facility log updates; etc.At the time this situation started; I was talking to the training team on GC (Ground Control) so I did not hear the clearance given to Aircraft X; who was taxiing up Taxiway A and had been given an IFR release off Runway 15 from TRACON. After I finished talking with GC; I sat back down at the CIC position. Aircraft Y was on about a 1.5 mile final to Runway 8 as I saw Aircraft X taxiing onto Runway 15. This is completely normal to see; as we often LUAW (Line Up and Wait) our Runway 15 departures so that we can clear them for takeoff as soon as the Runway 8 landers roll through the intersection.When Aircraft Y was just about to flare for Runway 8; I noticed that Aircraft X appeared to be starting his roll. I asked LC; 'Is Aircraft X rolling? Did you LUAW him or clear him for takeoff?' LC said he didn't think he had cleared him for takeoff. I told him to cancel Aircraft X's takeoff clearance immediately; which he did. The aircraft had rolled about 1;000 to 1;500 feet by the time they aborted.After this incident; a supervisor listened to the tapes and confirmed that LC had indeed cleared Aircraft X for takeoff. I'm not sure whether it was inadvertent and he meant to give him a LUAW clearance or whether he just forgot about Aircraft Y on final.Don't fall into a sense of complacency.Don't allow distractions to take your focus away from your primary duties. I am often teased about it; but when we are busy with many critical situations happening at once; and especially when we have CIC combined with GC; I will not answer the outside phone line until our traffic isn't as busy. I don't want to take my eyes off the busy operation in order to get a phone call from the airport authority about a NOTAM for a taxiway light bulb that's going to be OTS (Out of Service) during the mid-two weeks from now. (CIC was not combined in this situation)LC's scan should have been focused more heavily on making sure that Runway 15 traffic was not moving when Runway 8 traffic was on short final. When I brought it to his attention seemed to be the first he noticed the situation.

column121: While level at 5;500 ft. and at 170 kts. and on the RNDRZ 1 RNAV; ATC asked us to fly heading 090. ATC gave us a left turn toward Runway 25L. ATC asked us if we had Runway 25L in sight. We responded that we had Runway 25L in sight. We were cleared a visual approach for Runway 25L and made a normal landing. As ATC noted on my phone call to them regarded a possible navigational deviation error; ATC admitted that they are having trouble with their new arrival charts. The ATC person told me that there have been many errors by pilots and agreed the chart is confusing to pilots and this problem needs to be re-examined. After reviewing the arrival chart at the gate; my First Officer and I noted that what is depicted on the arrival chart is not what is depicted on our PFD. On the chart; a small box is depicted at intersection 'BERBN' to look for another box for the runway transitions and its very confusing and can be easily missedThis RNDRZ 1 RNAV arrival needs to be examined and rectified so as to not confuse pilots as was noted by our ATC Specialist during our phone conversation.

column121: Flew Korry 4 arrival and proceeding visually up the Hudson River at 4;000 feet. Cleared to descend to 3;000 feet. Descending out of 3;500 feet about abeam the museum; noticed an object at our 12 o'clock; flying very stable; and appeared to be flying towards us; at the time our speed was 250 knots. We made no corrective action with our aircraft and continued on descent and heading as there was very little time for it to come and go. It passed approximately 100 feet under the aircraft and was flat oval in shape and a very shiny black in color. I have seen many Mylar Balloons and it definitely wasn't one of them. It had the appearance and actions of a DRONE. We immediately reported it to Air Traffic Control and followed up with law enforcement.We were at the end of a 4 leg day all flown in high density airspace of DCA and LGA. Luckily it was a beautiful weather day and we were both looking out the window at the river and city when the flying object appeared and flashed under the nose.Vigilance is always required as is protection of airspace.

column121: Supervising a requalification of a Captain who has not flown in over 14 months. He was Pilot Flying. Given clearance to 'cross ZZZZZ at 8;000 [feet]; cleared ILS [Runway] XXC'. We had briefed ZZZ note about not flying glide slope until Final Approach Fix. Captain set 2;200 feet Final Approach Fix altitude and instead of pushing managed descent; pulled open descent. We were outside of ZZZZZ1 and I noticed the Flight Mode Annunciator and that aircraft was not going to level at 8;000 feet. I announced LEVEL OFF three times then intervened with Push to Level about 7;500 feet. I instructed Captain to climb to 8;000 feet as ATC said; '...I see you are going to 7;000; re-cleared to cross ZZZZZ2 at 7;000'; re-cleared ILS XXC.'Not having flown in 14 months; the muscle memory may or may not be there. We must always VERIFY what input we make and MONITOR aircraft for compliance; being ready to intervene if necessary.

column121: After a normal flight from ZZZ we descended for the visual approach to ZZZZ; due to no response from anybody on the ground at ZZZZ; I elected to do an over flight of the airport at pattern altitude of 1;500 ft. with the autopilot disconnected; crossing over midfield from north to south on the east west runway to enter a left hand crosswind for the left pattern landing east; we started configuring the aircraft for landing by selecting flaps to the #2 position; on the left downwind leg we selected the gear handle down; at that point we received an amber CAS message of GEAR MISCOMPARE R The nose gear and left main indicated down(GREEN) the right main gear was an AMBER X. While the First Officer (FO) looked for the message in the Emergency Procedures checklist I engaged the Auto Pilot and Auto throttles and climbed to 2;000 and stayed in an extended racetrack pattern around ZZZZ. At this point we informed the passenger of the situation and was thinking that the procedure should work and we would land at ZZZZ. We followed gear will not extend checklist; pulling the blow down handle; no change; the pulling the main gear uplock handle; no change the last procedure in the checklist was to try and yaw the airplane. We informed the passengers what to expect; and tried the yaw left then right twice; no change. At this point I made the decision to divert to ZZZ1 again informing the passengers what the plan was; they were fine with that because they had used a local company at ZZZ1 to take them to ZZZZ before. We pickup a clearance to ZZZ1 with ZZZ2 Center; and informed them of our situation. After that hand off to ZZZ2 Approach Control; the controller asked us if we would like a fly by for visual verification of the gear status. We chose to do this option; and he coordinated with the tower. I felt very confident that the gear was down because when the FO pulled the uplock handle he felt very little resistance; from my past experience that is a good indication the gear was not in the uplock. With blow down in the system the gear only has one place to go; so I felt with a very high degree of certainty that the right main was down and locked. The weather at ZZZ1 was VMC 10 miles 5;000 ft. Scattered wind was 110/18 gusting 24. After being vectored for the visual low pass over Runway XXR at ZZZ1 Tower informed us that Airfield Operations was in a position to observe our low pass; we had the aircraft fully configured for landing and made the low pass at 300 AGL and 140kts. Airfield Ops confirmed that he saw three gear. We executed the climb out instructions cleaned up the aircraft except for the gear remaining down; then we were vectored for another visual approach to land Runway XXR at ZZZ1. During our patterned we went ahead and [received priority]; to have the trucks just in case. Also the FO radioed Company ZZZ1 if they could have a van meet us to take our passengers to their requested destination on the field; and if they would call the Company1 maintenance facility and let them know we were inbound. We reconfigured the aircraft for landing while on a 3mile final we slowed to final approach speed and with Airfield Ops and the fire trucks standing by we made a normal landing with an extended rollout; confirming the gear was indeed down. We cleared the runway and taxied to the Company1 maintenance facility. We deplaned the passengers and got them on their way immediately. I informed the maintenance staff of the issue and they pulled the aircraft in the hanger and did a visual inspection of the gear. They found that the uplock switch; which is a plunger type; was stuck in the gear up position making the computer think the gear was still in the well.

column123: Encountered a UAV drone at 2;700 feet while flying an intercept to the 36R localizer between LANSR and CIKDU for a visual approach to 36R at CLT. Someone was flying a drone close to the ILS flight path; co altitude; for 36R at CLT. I originally called out 'bird 12 o clock;' then it maneuvered to our right; where I watched it pass off of our right wing; co altitude.  I notified the Captain it was a drone; and we immediately made a PIREP to CLT 36R Tower. Notify other pilots and attempt to inform the general; drone flying public how dangerous and illegal it is operate drones in Class B airspace.

column121: I was on a multi-engine add on training flight with my instructor in Aircraft X. Upon landing at ZZZ I experienced what appeared to be a complete left brake failure. I used right brake to slow down but the brake barely worked. Towards the end of the runway we still had a lot of speed. I tried pumping the left brake which resulted in a slight left turn. My instructor said 'I will help you with the brakes;' which I understood to mean that he will work the brakes so I took my feet off the brakes. He said that he had no brakes at which point I stepped back on the brakes. At this point; the left turn was more aggravated and we were on a trajectory to run over the side of the runway and into the runway lights. My instructor applied right engine power to avoid the lights and stay on the runway. At this point we were near the intersection to the intersecting runway and swerved onto that runway without clearance.We were able to slow down and stop and immediately exited the runway at the nearest intersection. Tower advised us of possible pilot deviation. Contacted Ground. Copied the Tower's number. We called the Tower and advised of the situation. I was later informed that my instructor had been aware of a brake issue with the plane; had the part in hand; but had not wanted to fix it yet so the plane can still be used to finish up instruction for a few students. He said the brakes on the right seat had not worked in weeks; which I was not aware of. The left seat brakes felt soft on previous flight but still allowed adequate braking. I did not notice any anomalies during the pre-flight.
column123: Upon landing at ZZZ field on Runway XX pilot advised me that the brakes on his side were soft as were my side on the copilot side; so we both started to pump the brakes to try and regain brakes on either side. As we were pumping brakes we were getting to the end of the runway so I tried to use the hand brake which had no pressure in it either so I checked what my best option was. A taxiway was coming up to fast; not enough room to regain brakes in time before we ran off the taxiway; straight ahead the runway end lights; so getting onto the intersecting runway was that option so I told my student that I had the throttles and I skid the airplane with differential power onto the intersecting runway. By the time we had the airplane pointing down the runway my student had got his brakes back and was able to get the airplane stopped. I did not ask for permission to taxi onto the runway due to the time I had to make a split second decision on where to continue letting the aircraft roll so I looked down the runway and nobody was on final so I wanted the best chance to get our aircraft slowed down without running off and hitting any signs or buildings or hangers; so I used differential power to turn the airplane onto the intersecting runway.

column121: Two Controllers in the Tower. I was on Ground Control. Aircraft X landed long on Runway 24 and appeared to be able to exit at [Taxiway] H. Aircraft X was instructed to exit at [Taxiway] H but delayed for a reason that was not communicated to the controllers. Aircraft X was then instructed to exit onto [Taxiway] A at the end of the runway; as it appeared; they were making a turn that was too tight to exit at [Taxiway] H in a timely manner. Aircraft X then began a high-speed taxi to [Taxiway] A; at which point tower sent Aircraft Y traffic on final around. I was scanning the runways and observed Aircraft X make a turn and clear the runway edge lines with all parts of the aircraft. At that time; the Local Controller cleared Aircraft Y for takeoff; while Operations called the Tower on our high frequency radio asking if Aircraft X required assistance. Aircraft X was still taxiing at this time; but had not contacted Ground; as they were not observed beyond the hold short lines. Aircraft X was not visible due to a hangar west of the tower obstructing the view of '[Taxiway] A' that Aircraft X was using. Local Controller issued a cancel takeoff instruction out of abundance of caution because Aircraft X was not observed in a timely manner by GC (Ground Control) or LC (Local Control). The instruction was stepped on; and it was decided that it would be less safe to reissue the instruction as Aircraft Z was now rotating and Aircraft X had been observed clear of runway. When Aircraft X contacted Ground; Ground Control instructed Aircraft X to expedite taxi past the hold short lines for Runway 24 and then observed the aircraft again visually.The hangar west of the tower presents an imminent safety concern that diminishes ATC's ability to provide safe; orderly; and expeditious service to the users of POU. The hangar needs to be lowered; or the tower needs to be raised.

column121: I was working arrival and departure for the JAX north area. We were on Runway 26. FHB airport is about 15-mile final for JAX [Runway] 26. We are constantly having to vector air carriers and business jets around 1200 codes traffic over and around that airport. Their altitudes and courses are erratic. The air carriers are audibly uncomfortable with it. Just today I had three 1200 codes from 1;500 feet to 3;500 feet MSL orbiting around my final while I'm trying to get jets into JAX. That sector also has multiple Victor routes that are busy with low level GA overflights. So; we have to get these jets as low as we can to miss the overflight traffic but the 1200 codes are blocking our low altitudes. It takes all our focus because we have to watch the tracks and altitudes of these VFR airplanes and constantly update the jet traffic of where they are. We don't like it. The pilots don't like it. It is a wildly unsafe situation there.Our Class C needs to be extended for the final for [Runway] 26. That is the only solution. No one should be flying around our final at crossing altitudes without talking to us. They just opened a flight school at FHB recently so the problem is only going to get worse. It's almost a daily problem now. One air carrier pilot said this is the only airport he flies into where he is dodging Skyhawks on the final. It is the most unsafe operation I have seen in my career.

column121: Approach control cleared us direct to WESEN for ILS [Runway] 08 [approach] at SJU. Captain was Pilot Flying; First Officer was Pilot Monitoring. This was the second leg of the night for this three day pairing of a long duty day. Level at 3;000 ft. (pilot selected) then cleared descent to 2;500 ft. (pilot selected) accomplished in vertical speed. Aircraft pilot selected speed was 180 kts. Controller assigned heading 110 to intercept and then cleared us for ILS [Runway] 08 approach. Approach/Land was armed and 'Land Armed' was displayed on PFD. The localizer remained identified and aircraft turned to intercept inbound course. Glide slope was above. Halfway through the intercept; the aircraft made an abrupt pitch-up followed by a thrust increase. This was very unusual as I have never experienced or seen an aircraft climb to capture a glide slope. While this was happening the aircraft exited approach/land mode and reverted to heading mode and solid bow tie altitude (2;500 ft.) All FMAs were 'white'. During the automation the aircraft pitched up and altitude was approximately 3;000 ft. in an attempt to capture the glide slope. The FO made a quick analysis and quick calls to the pilot flying while I was simultaneously disconnecting the autopilot and manually retarding the throttles to correct this anomaly. The aircraft was stabilized and a hand flown ILS was accomplished without autopilot assistance. This aircraft was scheduled for an 'auto land' for currency requirements and subsequently not accomplished. The First Officer monitoring that was accomplished and the verbal inputs from the FO were timely and appropriate; solid CRM. A note in our company aircrew briefing guide notes: Safety Alert Runway 08 Intermittent Glideslope Weak or intermittent glideslope signals have been reported when using ILS Runway 08. Exercise caution and intercept glideslope at the altitude depicted on the approach chart to guard against following a false glide slope signal. If the crew experiences any anomalies while conducting the approach; report the incident to tower and file an event report.

column121: I rented the Cessna 172; Aircraft X from FBO in ZZZ1 to ZZZ at XA:00 on Date. I did a checkout on the day before. Before takeoff; I followed the checklist to perform the pre-flight and everything was normal; both brakes worked fine.At around XB:10; Aircraft X entered the final leg for Runway XX on ZZZ with 240 at 9 knot wind. I did a short-field landing and touched down smoothly on the second white stripes after the number XX with power idling. After the touchdown; the aircraft was rolling on the runway. I pulled out the yoke and stepped on both pedals to slow down the aircraft. The aircraft slowed down a little bit when taxing near the first exit. However; the airplane was still rolling when it approached the second exit. I checked the pedals and found that the left pedal was lost with no response and the right pedal was OK. So I reached out my right foot to the left pedal on the passenger side to make sure if that one was working; but it was not. In the meantime; my hand turned the yoke to the left subconsciously because the taxiway was on the left of the runway. After I turned the yoke; I saw the aircraft was going left slightly so I decided to turn the yoke all the way down to the left and finally the aircraft turned 180 degrees to the left from the runway to the dirt road. The right-wing struck the fence causing damage when turning. I pulled out the parking brake and the nose turned to the right slightly and stopped. I saw the hydraulic fluid was leaking from the left side of the brake's leg after I got out of the aircraft.

column121: Aircraft X was on Final approach after calling out turning Final Runway 21; Second call Aircraft X on Short final Runway 21. After first call on Final I saw Aircraft Y pull slowly onto the runway and stop; that's when I cancelled Short Final. Just as I was preparing to go-around he started moving and in my judgement I could land if he kept going. He lifted off as I touched down with full brakes and speedbrakes out. I was gaining on him as he climbed to 100 feet and by the time I made the second to last turn off he was 200 feet above and maybe 300 feet in front of my aircraft.Additional realities information. I never heard Aircraft Y announce anything; entering Runway; or even my call to him after I landed close to him. The heavy cross winds were a very big issue for me on the approach to this downhill runway. I know it well and with 160 at 22 knots on a 21 Runway I had 50 degree left crosswind and yaw angle for lineup. I knew I would get a major downdraft at the end of this runway so I elect to land at the 1000 feet marker. My landing speed is normal 100 knots plus 1/2 the wind makes 110 knots approach speed. That is the reason my ground speed and distance is long and fast. I was gaining on him even at his 100 to 200 feet. I also want to point out that as a NON controlled airport no one should pull on the runway and stop for even 10 seconds with an aircraft on final at 110 knots. Since his information indicates he is a commercial pilot I'm sure he realizes that there is no excuse to be on the wrong Frequency XYZ.00 or to stop as in Position and HOLD like you might get at a Towered airport.

column121: On a training flight with a commercial single engine student around ZZZ. After performing some airwork north of the airport we came back in for a full stop landing. A pipeline patrol aircraft was patrolling a pipeline just north east of the extended runway centerline. At around 1;400 feet the student and I were on a 2 mile final. ATC was busy with a sudden increase in workload including the Pipeline aircraft. My student and I had the traffic in sight the entire time though ATC never asked us if we did. Instead the Pipeline finished his work inspecting the line and requested to exit the airspace to the southwest. This took him right through the final approach course of our runway.At that moment we saw him turn on a collision course with us. ATC would ask the Pipeline twice if he had us in sight to which he responded yes; but made no course correction. He did not hit us because my student and I made the prudent move to forget our approach profile and level off to avoid descending into this aircraft. He went under out aircraft at maybe 75 feet. After the aircraft passed we had to use a large amount of forward slip to get down to our proper glide path because of that incident and the fact that we had to level off to avoid collision. ATC allowed this aircraft to fly through the final approach course with an aircraft on approach and as a result this aircraft was too close to us. The Pipeline aircraft that called us in sight did not show up on my ADS-B; either due to altitude or other reasons; but if he saw us he intentionally passed very close to us.

column121: At ZZZ we were notified by the maintenance technician as part of the maintenance briefing that the autoland system was 'still' deferred and that a part was to be installed when the airplane returned in a few days. We noted that the previous crew had performed an autoland to Cat II standards; and had not written up the airplane with an items. An inspection of the logbook showed multiple issues with the First Officer (FO) raw data glideslope (G/S) indication and other failures over the last few landing cycles. During the flight we received messages from dispatch asking us to perform an autoland in ZZZZ1 to recertify the autoland system as that is how the airplane was deferred; i.e. the aircraft could be recertified by a satisfactory autoland being performed. We thought this strange as the previous crew performed an autoland to cat II standards and did not write anything up. The captain needed a landing for currency anyway; so a few messages were exchanged to that effect with dispatch; which led us to be directed to do an autoland in ZZZZ because the weather at the destination that the airplane would be flying were cat III. We said we would and performed the autoland. On approach we had a no land 3 message on the EICAS and a status message cue. The airplane then indicated the CAT II only on the PFD. We also saw that; as per multiple write ups; the FO's side G/S indication for the raw data portion was not in view. At about 1500 feet above the ground; the FO side glideslope came into view; but the aircraft showed slightly high on glide slope according to the raw data indication (note very slightly high; not out of any parameters; but still a bit unusual for the indication while on autopilot). Atmospheric conditions were VFR and the runway was in sight during the whole approach sequence. At about the time that the FO's raw data for the G/S reappeared; the Captain's side G/S raw data indication disappeared from view; though there was no indication of fault noted on EICAS with either of these issues. We continued the approach and the captain let the airplane land and it landed normally and tracked the centerline normally. The autoland was written up as unsatisfactory due to the loss of both raw data indications during the approach; the FO side data came back; the Captain side never did once it was gone. It appears to me that the autoland system deferral perhaps was not deferred properly as multiple failures over weeks of time suggested a problem with the system; and in the case of our monitoring of the autoland during our flight resulted in the same G/S issues on the FO side as well as a new failure on the Captain side of the raw data indications.

column121: This was a flight [to] ZZZ for pickup of an item. I monitored the AWOS and listened to UNICOM for traffic. I announced my intention to enter the traffic pattern for Runway XX and land at ZZZ with a FULL STOP. It was observing that several aircraft were in the pattern in close spacing on the downwind with an estimated speed of approximately 70 Knots. My pattern entry speed was reduced to 130 KIAS for landing gear deployment and spaced about a 1;000 feet to right; outside of these aircraft. Again; I announced my intention to make a Full Stop landing without interfering with the numerous aircraft doing Touch & Go landings. I reduced the speed 110 Knots on base and 100 Knots on Final Approach. Expecting these aircraft to extend downwind about 30 seconds to give me room; I was surprised when one of them continued on a tight base and turn to final directly in front my plane. At 100 Knots approach speed; I easily overtook the 70-knot aircraft and passed successfully at about 500 feet right of ZZZ Runway XX centerline. I accelerated my plane while retracting the gear and flaps and maintained 180 knots for the climb and turn to crosswind. Again; I observed several aircraft on close spacing downwind and I entered a downwind slightly outside the pattern. For the fourth time; I announced my intention to make a Full Stop landing and turned the normal base for my plane's speed. For a second time; an aircraft made a turn to base and turned to final directly in front of me instead of extending their downwind about 30 second to allow much-faster airplanes to make a Full Stop. On this pattern; I was grateful the other aircraft decided to go-around when the pilot observed the rapid CLOSURE from behind. I said 'Thanks' on the radio and completed a safe Full Stop Landing.After my safe landing; I proceeded to the FBO to pick up the item. Upon reaching the FBO desk; I heard on the FBO radio that Runway XX is now CLOSED for an airplane stopped on the runway. From the FBO windows; it appeared to be an aircraft was sitting on Runway XX without its Landing Gear. The poor soul and retractable aircraft were still out there when I departed. If these aircraft belong to a Flight school; perhaps the instructors should talk to their students about allowing faster; dissimilar airplanes into the traffic pattern for a single full stop landing. There are MANY corporate airplanes that land at ZZZ Airport with approach speed in the 100 - 120 knots range. This would be appreciated by many and certainly may save unwanted distraction during landing. Perhaps the instructor input may save some distracted soul from making a gear-up landing.

column121: While serving as PF (Pilot Flying) into ZZZ on the ZZZZZ arrival; I was flying the aircraft with autopilot; autothrottle; and VNAV. We had programmed the STAR along with a transition to the ILS XXR into ZZZ and reviewed all the fixes and block altitudes on the Pro Line Fusion MFD screen. The aircraft flew the STAR without incident from FL280 all the way down to about 13000. The VNAV and autothrottle were working. I became distracted when the autothrottle failed to reduce 'FMS speed' for ZZZZZ1 to 250. I manually selected 250 and slowed. I reactivated the FMS speed function and verbally announced that we would monitor the speed and to make sure it made the next speed slowdown proactively at ZZZZZ2. What I failed to properly notice is the VNAV set a floor altitude of 13000 and leveled off. I did not think much of it since the next fix was an 'at or above' 9000 so I thought it was a level-off in lieu of an overly shallow descent.While being distracted in over-monitoring the FMS speed reduction; we got caught in an undesirable aircraft state. To make the alt window at ZZZZZ3; 9000-8000; I manually selected VS and adjusted the descent rate and flight spoilers in order to make that fix's alt window. I believe it was too close to notice if we made it or not and we did not seek relief with ATC since the Captain seemed unsure how to query ATC since he too was interested in watching the VNAV and FMS speed since he had little time on this Pro Line Fusion avionic suite. I flew the remaining alt windows in VS mode since that higher level of automation was not trustworthy or compatible with my state at that time. Later in the arrival; before ZZZZZ4; ATC gave us relief off the arrival and gave a descent to 3000. I dialed 3000 into the FGCP and the Captain confirmed the ALT. Continued down in VS. Since the previous UAS - Undesired Aircraft State - was around 10000; it impeded on the 'in-range check' being completed. We were at about 6500 when the in-range check was started and it distracted us from noticing the aircraft was still in VNAV and had captured the floor of the arrival; 6000; and did not continue [the] descent to 3000. ATC would query us shortly and again I would notice that the aircraft in a UAS. I noticed VNAV still active and disabled it; and continued down. We intercepted the approach and landed without further incident.In hindsight and a word of caution; this is the first avionics suite I have seen where altitudes on a STAR can be superseded by altitudes on an approach. In this example; the ZZZZZ STAR with the ILS XXR ZZZZZ5 transition. There was a discontinuity between ZZZZZ6 and ZZZZZ5. I want to know why in what world should VNAV look ahead of a discontinuity and block the VNAV windows on the STAR. All the stepdowns on the ILS XXR were there for reference as there no way knowing how far in the downwind ATC would take you before a base turn. I don't have a lot of VNAV-capable aircraft experience and I see tremendous issues with not being properly trained in how the aircraft will respond based on what is programmed into the FMS. In general; the avionics differentiation training was lacking and laughable and the differences from the standard avionics in the CL604. It seems half-finished and very retrofit. Having to train on-the-line with the suite to new crew members is frustrating at best and dangerous at worst. I will continue to step up and down levels of automation as my comfort and knowledge adjusts and will try to look for any potential red flags in FMS programming beyond the basic comparing to the approach plate.

column121: I was on approach to land Runway XX at lake hood ZZZ with an 8 knot cross wind; I decided to carry about 5 knots of extra airspeed into my landing and land long. I touched down with the main landing gear first; parallel with the runway with proper cross wind correction. My trouble came when I set the tail down it began to swing to the right and I gave all of the right break and rudder I possibly could with no avail. I ended up sliding sideways into the left ditch just north of taxiway going roughly 15 kts. The ditch I slid into was very marshy and muddy which caught my right tire making the plane stop abruptly causing the plane to tilt over far enough for right wing tip to make contact with the ground and then settle back on both tires. I first checked my prop to ensure it didn't strike anything and that the motor was still running; I then immediately shut down the engine and contacted Tower to notify them of the situation. I told Tower I would need a minute on the runway to push the plane out of the way and they responded with 'OK'. There were no injuries in the incident and the only damage that occurred was minor to the right wingtip.
