column121: According to my student: While he was doing solo touch/goes at ZZZ; on his second landing; he touched down left of centerline. While rolling with the throttle in the idle position; he turned off the carb heat and began to retract the flaps. The nose of the plane began to veer right. When he applied left rudder he said the plane felt as if it would skid; spin or drop the nose onto the ground. Therefore; he applied just enough left rudder to straighten the nose wheel and rolled onto the grass adjacent to the runway. No contact was made with any runway signage or lights. There was no damage to plane; runway; or grass area; and there were no injuries. Tower cleared for continued movement to the Alpha taxiway. He continued to the transient parking area and called ZZZ Tower per their instructions.As the instructor; my student called me to inform me he had run off the runway; no damage had occurred; and he had spoken with the tower and FAA. I did not know he was flying by himself that day and had received no notification from him. I asked him to recount what happened to me several times trying to understand what happened. I called the Tower and confirmed that the winds were variable at 3 knots and visibility was very good. Based on his recount; I do not fully understand how he thought going off the runway was his safest option. I have not offered him any training or instruction that would indicate that was good judgment. He has never landed far off centerline with me in the plane and has never demonstrated that he would ever act in such a way. He and I have logged extensive dual hours at ZZZ doing pattern work; and he has never flown in a way that indicated he would act or make a decision in this manner. He had demonstrated consistently with me that he was able to maintain directional control of the airplane both in the air and on the ground. I have informed him that all of his solo endorsements are no longer valid. I was planning to spend an extensive amount of time with him on the ground and in the air addressing what happened yesterday through re-training; but he has decided to discontinue his flight training at this time. As a result of this incident; I have re-clarified to all students that they must inform me of any time they plan to solo and must receive my permission before doing so. I have also implemented a strict rule of no solo touch and goes. Solo pattern work will from now on only be full stop landings.
column123: While doing solo touch/goes at ZZZ; on my second landing; I touched down just left of center. While rolling with the throttle in the idle position; I turned off the carb heat and began to retract the flaps. The nose of the plane began to veer right. Applying left rudder the plane felt as if it would skid; spin or drop the nose onto the ground and I was too far toward the edge of the runway. I felt it best; to reduce the chances of injury or damage. I applied just enough left rudder to straighten the nose movement and rolled onto the grass adjacent to the runway. No contact was made with any runway signage or lights. No damage to plane; runway or grass area and there were no injuries. Tower cleared for continued movement to the taxiway. I continued to the transient parking area and called ZZZ Tower per their instructions. As a result of this occurrence; training has been suspended for evaluation and scheduling with CFI.

column121: On left base for visual Runway (RWY) XX; slowing to full configuration and turning final; flaps were extended to 45 degrees while flight spoilers where deployed at 1;500 feet. Speed trend vector was decreasing although thrust setting was at expected thrust setting for approach. Captain called for increased power and crew realized that the flight spoilers were not fully retracted while turning final. The aircraft speed was approx. 150 knots which was below the SOP maneuvering speed of ref + 17 knots with spoilers deployed. At this time the stick shaker activated for approx. 2 seconds and the angle of attack was lowered. The flight spoilers were immediately retracted and the stick shaker stopped. Continued to join the localizer and were on a stabilized approach by 1;000 feet and landed without any incident.While slowing to configure for final approach; Pilot Flying (PF) believed the flight spoilers were fully retracted and called for flaps 8 and 20 at 200 knots. Then the call for gear down and flaps 30 at 180 knots. PF was approaching the localizer course; called flaps 45 at 160 knots. To reduce automation workload the autopilot was disconnected and was preparing to make a left turn to intercept the localizer and glide slope. The stick shaker activated at this time because the flight computer detected a pending stall with the flight spoilers deployed. Immediately after the flight spoilers were fully retracted; the stick shaker stopped and the aircraft was on speed with flaps at 45 degrees. A left turn was made intercepting the localizer and the aircraft was on a stable approach by 1;000 feet.ATC gave direct to the final approach fix for the visual to RWY XX while still descending out of 6;000 and needing to slow to configure for landing. PF could have anticipated needing more distance to reduce airspeed; possibly without flight spoilers; and fully configure the aircraft by 3 miles of the final approach fix by descending sooner and leveling off earlier. This would have reduced pilot workload and automation management allowing for a more thorough scan of the aircraft and its systems before deploying flaps and slowing to final approach speed.

column121: We were given an ILS clearance into ZZZ in VMC conditions. A VFR airplane was at our altitude and seemed to be tracking to cross the final approach course. We both had the aircraft in sight and changed to a visual approach to maneuver behind the aircraft. At that point the airplane made a sharp right turn and we had to turn further right (away from the final approach course) to maintain separation. It seemed like the plane was on a long final for ZZZ1 and we were then at 90 degrees off of the final approach and unable to make a stable approach into ZZZ. We called the go-around with Tower and were given a heading and altitude toward the east before switching to Approach Control to come back and try it again.The main issue was the VFR traffic no one was communicating with. The crew did not fully brief the location and potential for conflicting traffic for ZZZ1 when approaching from the south. Had the crew not changed to a visual approach and been given ATC vectors; we may not have had the same issues. A go-around may not have been avoidable due to conflicting traffic but a more thorough brief of the airspace and threats may have increased situational awareness and avoided confusion. We should also have not changed the type of approach at the last minute.
column123: We were given a heading to intercept the localizer to Runway XXL and given a traffic call that was not on frequency. Controller then had us maintain heading and fly through the localizer to avoid traffic which was about 500 ft. above; crossing our path and that of the localizer track. We decided as a crew that if they gave us clearance for the visual; we could maintain separation from the aircraft into ZZZ. We were given just that and made the right turn back towards the runway and began to descend for landing. The general aviation aircraft ahead of us descended into our path and made an unexpected turn as we were continuing. I disconnected the autopilot and continued the turn towards the east to avoid ZZZ3 airspace and aircraft. Once clear of the aircraft; we elected to scrap the approach and were given vectors for another uneventful approach. We were originally on a 5 mile final to Runway XYR when ZZZ swapped runway configuration and we were then rerouted to the south to land north. We briefed the new approach and had the approach loaded and armed. With the change of runways; perhaps there was not enough time to fully brief the new approach and surroundings to point out a runway to the east that could cause some confusion. Some cumulative fatigue was present; as well as it being our third of four legs for the day added to the stress of the situation. I do think the spacing given into ZZZ was a partial factor with a GA aircraft that wasn't talking to anyone. There are steps to help mitigate issues like this; like ensuring complete situational awareness of self and the possibility of others; but ultimately there might always be general aviation aircraft out there operating around controlled airspace's not on frequency.

column121: Landed without a locked nose gear. Heading into ZZZ; I did the before landing flow and put the gear handle down. The gear lights only indicated 2 green mains and the red unlocked light with no nose gear indication. Seconds after the lights illuminated for both the Left and Right Hydraulic Flow. I informed Tower I needed to break off my approach for a gear indication issue and troubleshoot. They gave me a heading and altitude then sent me to approach. Approach gave me vectors while I troubleshot. I performed the landing gear will not extend hydraulically checklist. Part of the checklist is to pull the emergency gear extension handle; I did so briskly and without twisting the handle. The indications did not change and I did not hear or feel a thump. I then pulled even harder on the emergency gear handle and braced myself by putting my feet on the floor then tried again and still no response. I finished the checklist then read through the next checklist to land with defective gear. I called approach and said my checklist was unsuccessful. I asked to return to ZZZ and told them there was no indication for the nose gear; and [advised ATC]. They brought me around for [Runway] XX; I ran through the checklist multiple times to ensure I had the procedure down; then left the book open. I landed softly and held the nose off for as long as possible. Performed the ground evacuation checklist then shut everything off and got out.

column121: Taxiing out of ZZZZ we called the advisory frequency; we were told there was no inbound traffic and we were cleared to back taxi. It's about a 3;500 foot taxi to the end and roughly 1;000 feet from the end my First Officer points out what seems to be a Citation on a visual left base. We advise the VFR tower that there was an aircraft on final for our runway. Both the tower and ourselves attempted to tell the aircraft to go-around and failed. At this point there is brush and trees lining both sides of the runway and there was no turn off. I pull the aircraft to the right edge of the runway in event that he continued the landing. At an estimated 100 feet; the aircraft was seen adding power and retracting the gear. We advised the tower that we were clear of the conflict and we continued our departure.Suggestions: VFR island flying training or VFR plates to advise of traffic flows to certain airports.

column121: First Officer's landing. After touchdown and roll out to the end; we were issued taxi instructions while still on the runway and during transfer of control from FO to Captain for taxi. I heard taxi Alpha to the gate which I read back. As we turned across the hard stand for gate X from Alpha the controller informed us we had been instructed to taxi Alpha Fox. [The cause was] Task saturation. Controllers should not issue taxi instructions until clear of runway. Busy airports should have a standard and understood initial taxi route so that you have time to clear the runway and listen to further instructions. Maybe they could be depicted on the approach plate or in the airport diagram or the controller could issue with landing clearance.
column123: On the landing roll out while we were transferring controls we were issued a taxi clearance to gate X. The Captain still had the radios as we were decelerating through about 80 knots. He responded to the radio call and I can clearly remember him saying taxi to the gate via Alpha. There was no correction from ATC so we taxied to the gate. As we crossed the ramp area the Controller advised us the instructions were Alpha Echo Foxtrot. We apologize and proceeded to the gate with no further issues.The inability to hear the controllers instructions clearly at a busy time in the landing roll out combined with the lack of correction by ATC when our read back was incorrect. In the future I will restate the instructions from ATC once I have the radios on the ground after the roll out is complete. I will also plan not to respond the ATC during task saturation like this.

column121: On arrival at ZZZ; winds were variable and began to favor Runway XX for landing. We were set up for landing on Runway XY. I; acting as Pilot Monitoring for this leg due to special qualification airport; suggested to Captain that we swap our planned arrival runway due to winds and additional available landing distance (approximately 500 feet) for landing on Runway XX. The FMC was reprogrammed correctly; briefed; and we accepted a heading to intercept final Runway XX. With approximately a 30 degree intercept; approach control cleared us for the ILS XX. Captain selected approach mode early. This caused the aircraft to capture a false glideslope and the aircraft began a descent prior to capturing the localizer. I called this to the Captain's attention; of which he was also aware.Prior to localizer capture I recommended a discontinued approach. Altitude was well above Minimum Safe Altitude and the approach was salvageable but due to my experience level in the jet; I suggested a soft go-around. The Captain elected to continue; to which I agreed and remarked that our subsequent protected altitude would be 2;600 feet; which we were currently above; and that I would monitor. The Captain; slightly flustered about the false glideslope capture began to fiddle with Auto Pilot disconnect; Flight Director reset; and approach mode was re-armed. The new capture mode now is LOC but with a failed Glideslope. The aircraft has defaulted into the current vertical speed. I call slightly below glideslope; Captain again resets Auto pilot; Flight Director; and re-arms the approach mode. Localizer captures; Glideslope has been armed; and we are currently in a 700 to 800 FPM descent; very slightly low on Glideslope; at a safe speed; and safe altitude.I call attention to our protected Final Approach Fix altitude and glideslope intercept at ZZZZZ 2238 feet. If I remember correctly; we were approximately 3 miles from ZZZZZ when I remarked that at our current Flight Path Angle would have us slightly below our target altitude without any correction at ZZZZZ. Let me be clear; the aircraft was; based on our current descent rate; projected to be only slightly below 2;300 feet at the Final Approach Fix. A minor correction would have rectified the approach path angle and captured the glideslope. I called our altitude to the Captains attention once more approximately 1 to 2 miles prior to ZZZZZ; where we had now reached 2;300 feet; the charted approach value. Right as I was going to call for the Go Around at ZZZZZ; the tower controller issued a Low Altitude Alert; the Go Around was called and initiated; and the aircraft was at approximately 2;100 feet; a little less than 200 feet low at the charted glideslope intercept altitude; as well as above 1;000 feet above ground.The Go-Around was accomplished without error and a subsequent ILS was flown to Runway XX without any additional complications. Upon debrief; I became aware that the Captain was unaware that he had not captured the Glideslope. I re-framed the situation for us to consider alternate action and explained my reasoning for a requested discontinued approach prior to localizer capture. I am still low time and in consolidation. I had no reason to expect that we could not become stable with glideslope capture prior to Final Approach Fix. Hindsight is exactly that and this event began and ended all within 4 miles. The Go-round was completed to qual standards with safety and stable approach criteria in mind.False glideslopes capture prior to localizer capture due to not allowing the aircraft to capture in LOC or LNAV prior to arming the approach mode. Again; I believe I was very clear about our impending altitude deviation; however I don't believe I heard any response from the Captain from 3 miles(my initial call) and 1 to 2 miles(my second que). I could be wrong. The whole event spanned a time frame of less than 3 minutes from 'looks good' to 'doesn't look so good' so I could NOT have fit many more altitude callouts in that span. Due to my low experience in the jet; I incorrectly expected a correction that I did not receive. The go around was initiated at or just seconds after the Final Approach Fix. An assumption of understanding is not the greatest way to handle approaching a go around. I do not believe I was hinting and hoping but I will be more aggressive with my descent planning awareness callouts so as not to enter into a [undesirable situation] in the future. However; the correct decision was made concerning the safe operation of the aircraft and the proper response was executed.
column123: Trip was third leg of the day.  Landed from second leg at around XA:40 local and got to sleep room at XB:10L.  Awakened 30 early of show time at approximately XB:40L.  Weather was marginal regionally and at ZZZ. ZZZ1 was filed alternate but also monitored weather at ZZZ2 for second option.  First Officer has less than 75 hours and ZZZ is Special Qual field. Identified the CFIT (Controlled Flight into Terrain) hazard; short runway; marginal weather; etc.  Pre briefed the ILS XY enroute as winds were favoring that runway.  Also talked about EFVS (Enhanced Flight Vision Systems) and auto land contingencies.  Upon contacting ZZZ approach; they asked if we wanted ILS XX due to a wind shift.  Initially decided to stick with XY but reversed the decision seconds later due to shorter usable runway on XY due to displaced threshold and winds favoring XX slightly.I was flying the approach while the First Officer set up and briefed the ILS XX.  We re-accomplished the approach checklist and accepted a clearance direct to the Final Approach Fix to cross at 2;600 feet and maintain until established; cleared for the ILS.  We put the 2;600 foot hard restriction at the Final Approach Fix and were navigating LNAV/VNAV.  Just prior to Final Approach Fix I mistakenly selected the approach mode.  The aircraft immediately intercepted what I believe was a false glide slope and begun to descend before intended.  I turned off both flight directors and attempted to get the aircraft out of approach mode. The aircraft stayed in approach mode because I did not disengage the autopilot.  So; after re-engaging the aircraft tracked the localizer in LOC mode and Vertical Speed with Glideslope Flight Mode Annunciation struck through as a failed mode.  Realizing the error; I disconnected both flight directors and autopilot and immediately re-engaged and re-automated.  At this point I believe the aircraft momentarily intercepted a glide path; and almost immediately and simultaneously with the Tower Controller realized we were about 250 feet low of the glide slope intercept altitude.  We immediately performed a discontinued approach and climbed to 3;000 feet.  We received vectors for another ILS XX and auto landed uneventfully.Fatigue was undeniably a root cause although not the only issue here.  Flying two legs and then getting only 30 minutes opportunity to nap between legs was just a little too much for me despite 'feeling pretty good' ahead of the flight involved. In retrospect I should have used LNAV/VNAV to get solidly on the ILS final before selecting approach mode.  And; also; I should have and will in the future decide to discontinue an approach as soon as any serious mode switching starts happening on the final approach. In the light of day and after a nap I know this solidly; but it is much more difficult with a foggy brain at XF:00.  So; in the future; I'm going to use LNAV/VNAV more and not use APP until later in the approach. Revision of the flight pairing to allow for more rest time...even an hour nap; would help.  Also; making sure to use LNAV/VNAV and protect GSIA (Glide Slope Intercept Altitude) at 1 NM prior to the intermediate fix.

column121: On approach to land in LAS from vectors; and cleared for the visual my Captain had just turned final at approximately 3;800 feet and was on a stable descent to land. At about 1;150 feet our RA dinged 'traffic' which indicated about 200 feet below us. We couldn't see the traffic. At 940 feet to land (3;000 AGL); we had an RA warning go off due to a helicopter. The warning was to our right. We still couldn't see the traffic; but the warning soon turned into an announcement from our aircraft systems to 'pull up' at 940 feet on the RA. My Captain immediately decided to call for a go-around and we executed the maneuver appropriately; were radar vectored back to land; chose the left runway to allow for some additional distance; and landed safely without incident.Cause - A busy weekend post COVID time when the helicopter industry was seeing a lot more activity. This particular situation was out of our control to avoid. We don't generally have issues with helicopter traffic; and in this situation at such a busy airspace; we could not disregard the RA instructions so low to the ground and at such a critical phase of flight. Considering the busy nature or the weekend and the idea we are experiencing such high post-COVID activity in Las Vegas; perhaps we as a crew could have suggested the left runway for some extra separation; but it's never been an issue in my time flying before.
column123: On visual approach fully configured turning final at approximately 3;700 feet; when rolled out on final an RA was enunciated and a subsequent go-around was performed. We returned to fly visual to the left runway with no further issues. Helicopter traffic operating too close to the active runway approach path. Either keep helicopter traffic further west or make the left the primary runway for south landing traffic.

column121: I was operating a flight from ZZZ to IWS with two passengers on an IFR flight plane and talking with Approach. Approach cleared me for the Visual Approach into West Houston Airport; untowered field and approved my frequency change. Before I switched to CTAF; I heard a traffic alert as Approach asked if I was still on the frequency. I heard the traffic call for crossing traffic 500 feet below me; I'm not sure what lateral separation was with the initial call. I acknowledged the traffic alert and stayed with Approach instead of flipping to CTAF.I saw that I had a nearby ADS-B target converging with my flight track and was scanning furiously for that aircraft but could not see him. The other aircraft was climbing; and was converging vertically as well as laterally. I have a low-wing aircraft and dipped the left wing and slipped to try to make visual contact but did not find it; as it was already quite a bit inside the wing in my line of sight at that point. As I saw the ADS-B track continue to converge and pulled up and right sharply to evade the target. As I did that; the other plane unmasked from behind my wing and I was able to maintain visual separation for the 10 or 15 second we were closest to each other. Approach called the traffic as no factor at the same time and I cancelled IFR with West Houston in sight as we were in VMC and set up for downwind entry overflying midfield.I explained to my passengers that we had to move the plane away from another aircraft which had failed to yield and that we were not in danger. On reflection; I should have positioned the aircraft more aggressively to drop the left wing to see the other plane earlier. I also could have immediately asked for a vector from Approach to keep me clear; although this traffic appeared to be climbing out of Dan Jones (T51) or Dry Creek (TS07) and presented a late radar contact for the traffic call.

column121: I was returning to my home base of ZZZ from ZZZ1. I am flying my newish Aircraft X; of which I have about 19 hours in. It should be noted that most of my flight hours are from 25 years ago. I knew ZZZ had some crosswinds but I thought they were manageable and I felt I needed to push myself a bit. If I remember; the winds were about 13 at 260 (260 at 13). Part of my challenge was that they were not reporting winds on METAR only ATIS. Upon approach the winds started gusting to 19. This would give an 8.4 kt. crosswind gusting to 12 knots; something I should be able to handle. So; on the first attempt; I think my approach was OK; but I flared high; bounced and quickly realized a go-around was the best option. Not perfect but decent decision making.The second attempt is where things went very wrong. This time I came in with 20 degrees of flaps. My approach was probably a bit fast; but seemed OK as I got closer to the airport. First of all; I flared too high again; bounced; but then made the mistake to try and salvage the landing. On the second bounce I recognized that the plane was proposing and I'd have a prop strike if I let it drop again. So; I started another go-around; but the plane would not climb; more importantly I wasn't facing down the runway but more towards the left side. At this point the helicopters parked left of the runway are getting bigger in my windshield but I know I've got to gain air speed. The one correct decision I made was to keep the nose down until I gained some airspeed. Eventually I got the speed and was able to climbout. At some point in this I realized why I couldn't climb; I had raised all of the flaps! I then came around and successfully landed the plane on the third attempt. It was bad enough that after I parked; the fire department came over to 'congratulate' me on my recovery; but I really think it was to make sure I was OK.I learned the following on this landing attempt. I need more time with my instructor with landings; especially crosswind landings. I need more practice on Go-Arounds focusing on flap management and right rudder.

column121: While being vectored for the visual approach at ZZZ; approximately while descending through 5;000 feet; the Amber CAS message (ENG CTRL SYS (L-R)) appeared as well as the Master Caution System light; I referenced the Manufacturers Emergency/Abnormal Checklist which dictated the first step is to reset the FADEC to the affected side; which in this case was both sides; the checklist goes on to say if the CAS message clears then no further action is required; or if the CAS message remains; to land as soon as practical. The checklist also goes on to advise of the possible effects that may be encountered; they are:- Degraded or lack of response to throttle movements including possible surging or flameout.- Possible inability to restart.- Possible inability to achieve ground idle.- Loss of automatic Interstage Turbine Temperature (ITT) limiting during ground idle.- Loss of automatic ITT limiting during ground or windmill starts.- Loss of ITT indication.The message did not clear after the reset; and given the possibility of a flameout; I chose to land ASAP; I notified ATC and requested to proceed directly to the final approach course for landing. ATC complied with my request and gave me priority. The landing was uneventful; but on the landing roll out; the L Engine Fail Warning CAS message came up; it distinguished about 15 seconds later; then appeared once again about a minute later during taxi and stayed on for 10 seconds then went away.Once I arrived to the ramp at the FBO I shut the engines down and powered down the aircraft with no further incident.

column121: Departing from ZZZ. Initial Climb on runway heading with intention to remain in pattern. Aircraft Y was crossing ZZZ VOR before our takeoff and had announced on CTAF that he was going to cross midfield; make a teardrop entry to the left downwind for the runway. He specifically stated that he was going to enter downwind behind us. When climbing through 600 feet; I received a traffic warning from ADS-B stating that traffic (Aircraft Y) was nearly overhead with 500 feet vertical separation. Upon reaching 800 feet the traffic (Aircraft Y) was directly overhead with 300 feet vertical separation. This event caused us to temporarily suspend the climb until Aircraft Y had passed. Aircraft Y was below pattern altitude; 1;200 feet; by 100 feet when he was closest to us. This aircraft flew nearly the entire teardrop maneuver off the departure end of the runway at pattern altitude and below. Then proceeded to cut across the upwind portion of the pattern to join the downwind from the inside; rather than the intended 45-degree entry.

column121: We began the day at XA:00; the plane had 13 gallons of fuel on the pilots side; and 11 on the other. Generally we plan for 5-6 gallons per load of jumpers. After releasing jumpers I descended down from 9;000 feet AGL a couple miles east of the airport. Under 3;000 feet AGL on a 2/3 mile straight in approach to Runway XX @ ZZZ; I continued losing altitude as plane with the throttle fully out to lose airspeed. (It was put in every couple thousand feet descending to 'clear' the engine.) When the desired speed and altitude was reached; I pushed the throttle in with no response. I enriched the mixture and pumped the throttle; and that also made no change. Checked fuel selectors; mags; etc; all with no luck. I realized the aircraft would not make the runway fairly shortly. Continuing straight in; I would have hit the side of an adobe hill.Right was rougher terrain; so left was the best option. I made an announcement on CTAF but may have been too low at that time. I navigated to the smoothest looking area i could find given the very short amount of time I had to deal with the situation. Dropped full flaps; and slowed down as best I could. The plane touched down; bounced over a few hills; and came to rest slightly upward on another hill. I exited the aircraft thru the pilots door; and looked over the plane; then called Person X to inform him of the situation and was able to text him GPS coordinates While waiting I checked the fuel level in the aircraft; and the passenger side was empty; while the pilots seemed to have about 7 gallons; however the plane was at a slight angle so could have been inaccurate.Burning 17 gallons of fuel for 3 flights is about normal. Especially considering the slightly lengthy times to climb today.

column121: After making a left base at ZZZ call over CTAF; the R66 stated over communications they were '3 miles NE of the field'; after hearing this we asked if they could see us. They replied they saw us right over them. After hearing this we decided to take evasive action and discontinue our approach and continue straight west of the field; away from the other aircraft. We did not see the aircraft until they were short final of the field. They did not adhere to standard taxi pattern procedures and came in right underneath us on our base leg. The pilot of the Robinson did not state they saw us and were number 2 for the runway or anything so we just decided to get out of their way and try again later after they were no longer a safety factor for us.

column121: Upon safely landing I noticed that the battery had become partially dislodged during flight. Using Google search words of 'dji mavic pro 2 battery popping up' I found that multiple people have had problems and crashes due to the batteries coming out during flight. I believe this is a design flaw in the battery attachment method that could easily be corrected if the battery clip design was changed slightly; such that it provided a more captive interlocking engagement. I believe that a crash during flight; which according to various forums many people have had as a result of a battery becoming dislodged; represents a significant design safety flaw that should be corrected.

column121: We were cleared for the Visual Approach. The FO (First Officer) was PF (Pilot Flying) and briefed a visual backed up with the ILS. ATC brought us in on a 4 mile final from a left base which was inside the final approach fix on intercepting the final/Localizer we were slightly low but as it was a visual I told him he was a little low but I let him continue since we were stable and spooled up just below the Glideslope. At about 800 ft. the FO was still descending and we got a Glideslope aural Emergency Ground Proximity Warning (EGPWS) to which he stated correcting and he leveled off to get back on profile. We continued to a landing.I noticed he was low and called it out and then the EGPWS called it out as well. [The] cause was the First Officer misjudged his height and continued descending. I assumed he would correct expediently but he only shallowed his descent rate slightly. The Pilot Flying took corrective action. I'll be more assertive next time and suggest the FO go outside the final approach fix while doing the visual so he has time and isn't rushed.
column125: An Air Carrier Captain reported ATC vectored them inside the Final Approach Fix and cleared them for a Visual Approach. The First Officer descended early below the glideslope and the aircraft received a Ground Proximity Warning but continued to a successful landing.

column121: On a visual approach to Runway 16L while descending to 7000 ft. we received a GPWS warning. I leveled off around 7;100 ft. and had the terrain in sight the entire time. After passing the hill I started a descent and was unstable so we went around.We were expecting the RNAV Y 16L but were taken off because of departing traffic on Runway 34R. This lead to an extended downwind and base to final. We were much farther out than we briefed or expected. When we finally turn final I used the RNAV 16 X approach plate for distance and altitudes. Over 'BADPE' fix the altitude is 6900 ft. I set 7;000 and started to descend my rate was too much and we received the GPWS warning. [Pilot flying suggests] when operating in mountainous terrain on an RNAV approach and you are taken off that approach see if there is another approach that is better suited for your new location and situation; and familiarize yourself with it before even starting a visual approach.

column121: I was [the] pilot flying; headed into ZZZ. It was VMC; severe clear; and in the afternoon. We were being vectored toward Runway XX in ZZZ. I was in green needles; with the [Runway] XX ILS frequency active. My FO made a comment [that] we weren't picking up the right identifier; but because it was visual; and we had just called the field in sight; I was not going to reference the glideslope anyway. I could clearly see the PAPIs and was going to reference that since I had a visual. We were then cleared for the visual for [the] runway; so I kicked off the autopilot; and began a stable descent. We had noticed the glideslope and the PAPIs were not lining up; so I made a comment I was just going to fly it visually and use the PAPIs as the guide; not the glideslope. After all; it was only being used as a back up. Well; as we proceeded along; even though I was 2 white/2 red on the PAPI; the glideslope annunciator started yelling at us (later; we found out the ILS was OTS; which explained why it was not working correctly). Instead of asking the FO to switch his and my frequencies back to standby; I elected to fly higher on the PAPIs to shut the annunciator up. I ended up being 4 white about at about 300 ft. Because the winds were calm; the runway was clear and dry; and I knew a safe landing could be accomplished; I elected to continue the approach. I still landed softly and safely in the touchdown area; and stopped in the usual amount of runway required. It was an uneventful landing.When we taxied clear of the runway; I asked Tower and asked about the frequency and asked the FO to pull up the current ATIS to see if there was a NOTAM about the ILS frequency; and sure enough; it was OTS. In hindsight; I'm not sure how I missed that on the ATIS; but I should have read it more carefully and I could have avoided this situation altogether.
column123: I was the First Officer and the Pilot Monitoring for this flight. Having received ATIS for ZZZ and setting up the approach the CA (Captain) briefed the Visual Approach Runway XX to be backed up by the ILS and I concurred and had no questions on the briefing. When going to green needles on the approach I noticed the identifier for the ILS Runway XX did not match with the identifier on the LIDO chart. I mentioned to the CA that I did not have the proper identifier and I double checked the frequency to ensure that it was correct and it was. The CA acknowledged and [said] that we had been cleared for the visual approach. During the approach the CA noticed that the GS (Glideslope) and the PAPI were not matching and informed me he was strictly using the PAPI for the visual approach. While continuing on the approach the GS annunciator was sounding a notification and the CA ended up high on the PAPI at about 100-200 ft. The CA called he was high and correcting and continued the approach into ZZZ and landed in the landing zone with no issue and was able to slow the aircraft and exit on taxiway. Once on the ground the CA asked the Tower to confirm the frequency for the ILS and the Tower responded it was released to Maintenance. At that point the CA asked me to bring the ATIS back up and that is when we both noticed the ILS was NOTAMed OTS. We discussed the approach and landing and decided to both submit reports.

column121: We were flying the ZZZZZ RNAV arrival into ZZZ inside of the ZZZZZ1 waypoint when ATC instructed us to descend via except cross ZZZZZ2 at 10;000 ft.; 2000 ft. higher than published. We informed ATC that if we comply with the assigned crossing restriction that we will have trouble making ZZZZZ at 6;000; [which was] only 8.2 miles from ZZZZZ2. ATC then replied; 'If you will be high on the arrival let me know.' We then decided to slow down the aircraft to prepare for making the crossing restrictions and recalculate our descent inside of ZZZZZ2 to advise ATC if we will be high or if we can comply with the ZZZZZ restriction.Once the Honeywell FMS reached waypoint passage over ZZZZZ2 and cycled to the next fix; we started down to make the next crossing restriction. Within a few seconds of starting our descent; we received a TCAS 'TRAFFIC' alert followed by a TCAS RA. I immediately disconnected the autopilot to comply with the RA and resolve the conflict. After the resolution advisory was resolved; [the Pilot Monitoring] noticed that the FMS had cycled to the next waypoint early and found that we were still a few tenths of a mile from ZZZZZ2 during the event. The assigned altitude at ZZZZZ2 was 10;000 ft.; we leveled off during the RA around 9;750 - 9;800 ft. There was no communication from ATC to us regarding the event. The rest of the arrival and approach was flown successfully and we landed safely.The Honeywell FMS cycled waypoints early; leading us to believe that we had reached waypoint passage. [I suggest] finding a solution to prevent the Honeywell FMS from making premature smart turns/waypoint passage cycles.
column123: Operating [the] flight as SIC (Second in Command) and PM; we were assigned to descend via the ZZZZZ [arrival] but cross ZZZZZ2 at 10;000. We leveled off at 10;000 prior to the fix and set the next appropriate altitude. At about 2.5 miles prior to the fix; the Honeywell FMS prematurely sequenced to the next waypoint. We began an initial descent and quickly noticed the discrepancy in distance between current position and waypoint. [There was] an RA to 'monitor descent' annunciation. The Pilot Flying immediately climbed back to 10;000 and we waited until the correct DME was displayed. [The cause of the crossing altitude deviation was the] Honeywell FMS [working improperly]. I have noticed this problem in almost all company aircraft containing Honeywell FMS. The airplane will sequence to the next waypoint prior to the actual position. This can; and does lead to; altitude deviations and potential RAs and possibly worse. I would like to either see the problem itself fixed or the company to implement an SOP or training that covers this issue.

column121: After canceling IFR on a visual approach into ZZZ; we switched to CTAF and began making position reports. Center had advised us of 2-3 aircraft in our vicinity as we approached the airport. We had one aircraft in sight and were looking for the others. There were several aircraft on the CTAF and no immediate conflicts were noted until a departing aircraft was shown on the TCAS display at 12 o'clock and climbing towards our altitude followed by an automated 'traffic' callout. We could not see it immediately or infer its exact course so started a slight right turn away from the airport to avoid [it]. After several seconds I spotted the other aircraft on a converging course from the left and advised my copilot to increase descent to increase separation as he continued flying and searching out my window. It was difficult to spot due to very little relative movement and it was more difficult to spot from the copilot's seat. During the correction I saw that we were on a collision course and directed a climb/level off before taking [the] controls and beginning a more aggressive climb. My copilot spotted the aircraft immediately after the control transfer and we passed approximately 500 feet above the other plane. I had the opportunity to discuss with the crew of the other aircraft on the ground. After checking in with Center and receiving a traffic advisory; it seems they saw us before our initial ('blind') right turn and began a left turn to increase separation; then a level off to allow us to pass above. The mirrored lateral and vertical corrections contributed to the closure. Our 'blind' turn was initiated due to the TCAS return; which displayed their position and climb; but no lateral movement information. Lateral movement information may have provided better situational awareness in this case. Spotting the aircraft sooner would have also helped prevent the conflict.

column121: My wife and I were flying to IOW. The nearest airport to IOW with a TAF is CID; 16 NM to the northwest. The CID TAF at our estimated time of arrival was... 4SM -RA BR OVC015We planned on flying the flight VFR with only CID being a little marginal VFR. About 30 NM from IOW we encountered light rain and the OVC015 ceiling started to drop. We maneuvered right and left as we made our way toward IOW. The IOW ASOS at the time was reporting the ceiling 1100 scattered. At 15NM west of IOW at 500-600 feet AGL skimming the cloud bases; I turned around to get back to better weather; but found that the weather had closed in behind me; or my route in could not be duplicated out. I knew there were antenna towers in the area and got a visual on one of the towers which appeared to be near my altitude. The safety of the flight was now in question. I called CID Approach and requested an IFR clearance to IOW. The Controller cleared me to the IOW airport via radar vectors and gave me a heading and instructions to climb to 2;500 feet. I advised the Controller that I wanted to shoot the RNAV (GPS) 30 approach into IOW circle 7. We shot the approach; broke out around 1100 AGL; circled; and safely landed on Runway 7. I hold an ATP certificate; however my last instrument proficiency check I had was a 135 check [several years ago]. Therefore; I was not instrument current.Our airplane is equipped with a Garmin 400W and a Garmin 335 ADS-B out transponder. The 400W had a current procedures data base and the pitot static encoder check was completed [two years ago]; making [my aircraft] legal for IFR operations. Additionally; I had flightplan.com app loaded onto my phone; which allowed me to have current IFR low altitude charts and approach plates for IOW; as well as the surrounding area.My brief IFR flight and approach was performed to ATP standards and did not alarm ATC in anyway. I have reviewed my ADS-B track and found that the lowest I got on that flight was 422 feet AGL; which is lower than I recall. The antenna tower I saw is at 320 feet AGL. The low flight occurred in Class G airspace during the day. Complacency on my part was definitely a factor and I have taken steps so as to not repeat this occurrence. I have scheduled an Instrument Proficiency (IPC) check.

column121: During a training event; my student was learning to judge height above the runway; and the landing went long due to our duration in ground effect. We were asked by Tower to expedite our taxi down the runway and clear [the runway].  During the turn; my foot became entangled in my headset cord. In an effort to manage the aircraft; I pulled my headset plugs from the ports and dislodged my headset on my head. It is a very confined area and the ports are under the center portion of the instrument panel. I made every effort to stop the aircraft prior to the hold short intersection and crossed the hold short line that guards the runway. Factors that affected my performance include a small cockpit environment leading to cord entanglement. Small area that does not favor any ability to clear a runway; and stop the aircraft if the pilot finds them self in any kind of predicament. Faded hold short markings on taxiway. Plan of action is to review this scenario with local instructors. Incorporate this situation into 'real world' training for my students. Renewed priority for 'hotspot' training.

column121: As PM; the Captain and I were nearing BERBN intersection on the COKTL 1 Arrival into LAS. After receiving the ATIS for LAS; and before beginning the descent on the COKTL 1 Arrival; the Captain briefed the COKTL 1 Arrival; along with the visual approach; backed up with the ILS to Runway 26L. Conditions at the time were VMC DAY (15;000' few/10SM vis). Prior to reaching BERBN intersection; Approach Control asked us if we had the ILS loaded. I looked over to the Captain and we both nodded; at which time I stated that we did indeed have the ILS 26L LAS loaded into the FMC (from the direction of PRINO intersection). The Approach Controller cleared us to fly the ILS 26L with a clearance to cross BERBN at or above 7;000 feet MSL. I then queried the Approach Controller about whether or not they would like me to call our base leg for Runway 26L.  The Controller replied that our base leg would be visible on their radar; and that a call was not necessary. Not realizing that there was INDEED a transition from the BERBN intersection to HUXAR on the ILS 26L; we proceeded turn north whilst descending to intercept the Runway 26L localizer just outside of RELIN intersection (D7.2 IRLE) Runway 26L.Shortly thereafter; we were told by the Approach Controller that we had initiated the turn too soon; and we were given a heading to the East in order to avoid traffic (other carrier) that was already established on final for Runway 26L.  We did receive a TCAS TA (no RA); and after completing the turn to the East; the TCAS TA was resolved. After the turn to the East; and with proper separation between our aircraft and Aircraft Y; Approach Control vectored us back to the north to join the localizer and clear us for the visual to LAS Runway 26L. We were then handed-off to LAS Tower. Landing was uneventful. After landing; we were provided a phone number to call LAS TRACON. The COKTL 1 Arrival and the latest ILS 26L charts for LAS are dated (date). I can tell you that I was certainly not familiar; nor had I ever been assigned the ILS 26L from the COKTL 1 Approach (I wasn't even aware that there was a transition from BERBN intersection; as that transition is located on the new ILS 26L Approach chart). My apologies to the LAS Approach Controller for our misunderstanding.As an added measure; I would prefer to see more information added to the COKTL 1 Arrival; like a ball note referring Pilots to the BERBN transition on the ILS 26L LAS.  An added note within our company pages may help bring attention to this transition as well.

column121: While on approach into ZZZ; we were descending from 6;000 ft to 5;000 ft on the runway localizer tracking inbound to runway; and were cleared for the ILS . While descending through approximately 5;300 ft; on the Localizer; a larger sized; square 'Quad-Copter' drone suddenly went past our right wing; (from aircraft front to back) missing the right wingtip by; in my estimations; about 50 ft. The event happened so quickly that there was no manual reaction to the event (autopilot / auto-throttles stayed engaged; etc). I immediately told my Captain that we just had a near miss with a drone since he did not see it; and we immediately notified Air Traffic Control as to the exact location; altitude; and description of the drone/event. We proceeded on the ILS as normal; for landing. The Captain contacted the Chief Pilot once parked at the gate to report the matter.

column121: I was the PF (Pilot Flying) on this flight from ZZZZ1 to ZZZZ. On arrival into the ZZZZ terminal area; we were cleared direct to ZZZZZ and cleared for the ILS XXL approach. Weather at ZZZZ was reporting winds 280 at 12; few clouds at 3;000 ft.; altimeter 29.62; and no other significant weather. The initial approach was uneventful and we were fully configured and stable by about 1200 ft. AGL. At approximately 800 ft.; I turned off the autopilot and began hand flying. Then at approximately 700 ft. we received an audible wind shear warning that lasted for 3-4 seconds. This caught us both by surprise as there were no other conditions that would be associated with wind shear. There was no convective activity in the area; the weather was good; and the wind readout showed stable at 12 kts. The aircraft showed no airspeed fluctuation nor an increasing or decreasing trend vector; the thrust setting was normal; pitch attitude and vertical path were stable. During the alert; the weather radar showed a lot of red radar return that wasn't previously there; and did not correlate with what we were seeing visually out the windscreen. We both assessed the situation; and agreed that the alert was erroneous. At this point we were at about 300 ft. AGL; and the Captain told me to continue. I agreed with this assessment and decision; and continued to land normally. Not once did we receive any other indication that wind shear was present; it wasn't even 'choppy'. It was smooth all the way through landing. After reflecting on the event; I still believe that the alert we received was false. With that being said; as a crew; we probably should have discussed making a logbook entry and discussing the alert with the mechanic once we were parked at the gate in ZZZZ. Going forward if I encounter something like this again; I will handle it differently for a couple of reasons. First; the FOM does state that if a wind shear warning is received; to follow wind shear escape maneuver or to execute a normal go around. Secondly; I do not want to get into a habit of second guessing the warning systems on board the aircraft. Regardless of a suspected error; and as long as safety is not compromised; follow procedure then once at the gate; make a logbook entry for a suspected error.
column123: We were operating this flight from ZZZZ1 to ZZZZ arriving approximately XA:30 UTC. Captain was PM (Pilot Monitoring) and FO (First Officer) was PF (Pilot Flying). We were set up for the ZZZZZ1 RNAV arrival for the ILS XXL. The aircraft (a B767) was empty and our landing weight was 198;100 lbs. VApp was calculated at 122 and VRef at 117. The weather was VFR. There was no convective activity; a few clouds at 3000 ft.; winds 280 at 12 reported on the field; and altimeter at 29.62. We were cleared to the fix ZZZZZ to begin the ILSZ XXL at 4000 ft. We intercepted the localizer and armed the APPCH. We continued to configure the aircraft for landing maintaining VFR conditions. With the aircraft fully configured for landing (Stabilized Approach criteria met) at approximately 700 ft. AGL we received a wind shear alert. The weather was VFR; and the winds at our altitude were showing stable at 12 knots. There was no airspeed fluctuation (including the airspeed trend line); the throttle position did not change; the thrust setting did not change; and the aircraft pitch attitude did not change. As PM; I did not elect to call 'Go Around' as the aircraft was fully stabilized; fully configured for landing and we were passing through approximately 300 ft. AGL with the PF hand-flying the aircraft. Given the situation with all of the present conditions and the state of the aircraft; I believed the alert was erroneous. I should have made a logbook write up on the EPGWS (Enhanced Ground Proximity Warning System) to check for errors. There were no reports of any wind shear activity in the area and the weather conditions were VFR. Also given the location and state the aircraft was in; I thought it would create an unnecessary 'non-normal' situation that could have potentially caused more problems than just continuing the approach and landing the aircraft. As I stated above; the aircraft exhibited no indications for us to believe that there were any type of wind shear occurrences at the present time or in the immediate time prior to landing.

column121: We were on a visual approach to Runway XX at ZZZ.  During the approach we received a GPWS Caution 'Terrain' or 'Too Low Terrain' just after the '500 ft stable' call. As the PM (Pilot Monitoring); I failed to call for a go-around.  The PF (Pilot Flying); Captain completed the approach and landing uneventfully. Everything about the approach was normal and I have seen it many times in my 3 years here. PF was stable at 100 ft above field elevation as well as at 500 ft above field elevation; the only exception was that the winds that had recently shifted and gusting were causing the airspeed to deviate by plus or minus 10 knots; but not dramatically.  The fact that we were visual with the runway; on path and speed and properly configured; made the GPWS caution surprise me. After hearing it; I reverified that we were within all our approach criteria and it appeared safe so I did not call for the go around. However; being that it was not day VMC; I should have called it. In the future I will be sure to comply with the GPWS alert immediately and call for the go-around.

column121: Original First Officer (FO) called out from the trip. I was assigned a new FO; he informed me that he was low time FO and hasn't flown much during the past few weeks. I took the first leg to ZZZ1; and I let him [have] the leg back from ZZZ1 to ZZZ. He flew really well during the entire flight. We were cleared for the visual approach Runway XXL with great weather. FO did a great job during the approach and he was properly configured and stable at all times. It looked like he was going to have a great landing because he was doing everything by the book. Unfortunately; when it came time to do the flare he barely flared and took the power out a bit too quickly and the main landing gears made contact with the runway firmer than normal. Everything happened too quick; I saw him initially starting to flare but then at the end he let the nose drop a bit and the mains contacted the runway. I didn't get a chance to note the VSI at the time. As soon as we arrived at the gate I called Maintenance and informed them of that I was writing the aircraft for a possible hard landing with minimal flare. We originally had a plane swap; while on the swap I grabbed some dinner. And eventually received a call from the Chief Pilot confirming that there was damage to main landing gear and he wanted to hear from me what had happened. I explained to the Chief Pilot what had happened; and Chief Pilot took notes and said to follow up with an Ops report.A firmer landing than normal due to minimal flare that caused the main landing gears to come into contact with the runway quicker than normal due to minimal flare. FO barely flared during a visual approach at Runway XXL in ZZZ that ended up causing a hard landing situation.[Reporter recommends] possibly rotate brand new FO's and make them fly more often. FO informed me he had not flown in a while and was still low time FO. He did a great job on our leg to ZZZ1 as Pilot Monitoring (PM); and then a great job on his leg from ZZZ1 to ZZZ until his landing flare where everything happened to quick and he ended up barely flaring and making contact with the main landing gear a bit firmer than usual; which prompted me to write up the landing.

column121: During arrival into ZZZ we received 4 runway changes in about 5 minutes time. We were then broken off the arrival and vectored around for a visual approach to XXL. Reaching the ZZZZZ fix we received a fuel imbalance caution message. We then switched the fuel selector to the correct side to balance fuel and landed within limitations for a fuel imbalance. We felt rushed and focused on flying the visual correctly. Upon receiving the caution; we felt like going around was a more unsafe decision than continuing on the approach; due to the distraction and complexity that were that we were experiencing. Always go around whenever a caution message appears. Run the QRH as directed.
column123: After a rushed departure due to company operations. The aircraft was initially over fueled (may have played a role). Descending on the ZZZZZ arrival we were assigned multiple runway changes and arrival transition changes in sterile. This caused task saturation. Finally we were assigned RNAV visual for [Runway] XXR. On the approach we received a fuel imbalance message turning onto final at ZZZZZ1. We were able to promptly balanced and corrected the issue. We landed with the selector off and a imbalance within limitations. Cause was gusty wind condition; multiple runway/transition changes; rushing because we were 'leading the pack'; and no clear reason why the fuel imbalance was caused (pilot error; mechanical; etc.) I feel we should have considered a go-around. I was behind the airplane and task saturated. I should have verbalized this to the captain. We could have requested delay vectors and potentially broke of the approach to fix and trouble shoot the issue.

column121: The incident happened during our descent on the ZZZZZ arrival into ZZZ. As far as I remember; we had been cleared by the previous Controller and told to do the XXL transition. I had already programmed the FMS for that specific arrival and pre-loaded the ILS XXL. The weather was not an issue with a clear sky and great visibility. I briefed a visual approach for XXL; backed up with the routing of the ILS for that same runway; and made sure we had the discontinuity between the last point on the arrival - ZZZZZ1 and the first point on the approach - ZZZZZ1. We were then told to contact the next Controller and assigned different altitudes on the arrival while maintaining the routing. For some reason; at one point; I thought/assumed we were cleared for the visual and got rid of the discontinuity by linking the first point on the approach with the last point on the arrival - ZZZZZ1. I turned to the Captain to ask him to 'confirm' and proceeded by removing the discontinuity. Because we were not cleared for the approach yet; the aircraft proceeded past ZZZZZ1 on a XXX as expected; but because it was now programmed to fly the approach it started turning at ZZZZZ2. The Controller asked us if we were flying the RNAV; we responded that we were doing the XXL transition and as the Captain said these words we realized we had made a mistake by leaving the XXX. The Controller kindly reminded us what we were expected to do and gave us a heading to intercept the localizer for the visual XXL. What led to this mistake is mostly my fault. I was fixated on flying the visual; and I forgot to actually make sure we had been given the clearance before getting off the arrival. My mind was too focused on the preconceived idea of what was expected rather than what we had actually been cleared for that I failed to properly communicate with my Captain. I was complacent; and this led to confusion in the cockpit and with the Controller.
column123: During our descent on ZZZZZ arrival into ZZZ; we were cleared to descend via ZZZZZ ; XXL transition. We programmed the FMS for that particular arrival and transition and preloaded the ILS XXL. The weather was good with clear sky and great visibility. Pilot Flying; briefed the visual approach XXL back it up by ILS. We made sure the discontinuity between the last point on the arrival ZZZZZ1 and the first point on the approach ZZZZZ1. We were told to contact the next Controller and assigned us different altitude on the arrival while maintaining the routing. I contacted the next Controller and for some reason we thought; we were cleared for visual via ZZZZZ1 transition. As we past ZZZZZ1; we continue to fly heading XXX as we approached ZZZZZ2 the plane was about to transition to the new fix. At this point ATC assigned us a heading 350 and asked us if we are flying the RNV transition? Which responded yes and I realized the error. At this point he assigned us 310 heading to intercept the localizer and cleared us for visual to XXL.We were both fixated and our mind was set on the preconceived idea of what was expected rather than what we had actually been cleared for; especially when the proceeding traffic receives that clearance. I (we) failed to confirm and communicate with each other and ATC. I got complacent and side tracked; the combination of these two led to the confusion in the cockpit and with the Controller.As a (Captain/Pilot Monitoring) I should have communicated and confirmed that we are both on the same page and understood the clearance and when in doubt ask the ATC for clarification. I want to make sure and get the confirmation before removing the discontinuity and not to rush. Do not assume if previous aircraft receives particular clearance we will get the same and avoid complacency. I will emphasize this on my future arrival briefings to ensure this doesn't happen again; FOOLISH ASSUMPTION/MISTAKE.

There was an FDC NOTAM for this approach (to disregard a note for night operations); but was N/A.  Company was contacted.

column121: Received a runway assignment change from Runway XX to Runway XYR after initial Runway XX briefing.  Re-briefed the Runway XYR ILS and began approach.  Between ZZZZZ and ZZZZZ1 intersections PF began descending to 1;500 feet.  Altitude deviation was noted and a correction was made around 1;600 feet; shortly thereafter the tower issued a low altitude warning.  Altitude was corrected back to 2;000 feet; approach and landing was accomplished without further incident.  During debrief; crew agreed that expectation bias was a major contributing factor to the deviation.  The PF was still thinking about the altitudes for the Runway XX ILS.  It was the last leg of a three-day pairing so the PM did not properly back up the PF due to Pilot Flying's strong performance during the entire pairing.  PM should not allow previous performance or experience to impact critical backup role.
column123: Initially set up and briefed for the ILS XX. New ATIS was obtained with runway change to XYR just prior to runway transition point on the ZZZZZ Arrival.  Re briefed and set up for ILS XYR. Approach Control gave us a  vector to intercept the Localizer XYR and then to descend to 2;000 feet from 4;000 feet outside of ZZZZZ [intersection] and then cleared us for the approach. I armed the localizer and set 2;000 feet; localizer captured and upon reaching 2;000 feet at ZZZZZ; I reverted to my previous briefing of the ILS XX and set 1;500 feet and proceeded to descend to 1;500 feet. Several hundred feet into the descent; Approach Control told us that we were low. Upon realizing what I had done I initiated a climb back to 2;000 feet from now 1;600 feet prior to ZZZZZ1. Reaching 2;000 feet prior to ZZZZZ1 and being stable again; we now continued to configure and resume the approach to land with no further incident.I should have verified my actions as I was accomplishing them and verified them with the PM. I was still in the mind set of what I had originally briefed and should have transitioned to the new approach even though we were rushed with the late runway change.

column121: The following narrative is due to a complete lack of threat and error management and operation performance.   This was the first leg of a 4 day trip and I would be flying with a First Officer for the first time.   We were heavily delayed to start our day and weather was terrible.   High winds and windshear were reported throughout the whole Company system with several crews diverting.   Yet my release for this flight indicated nothing other than I should evaluate the use of Runway XX for ZZZ departure.   When the aircraft arrived I met the arriving Captain and he explained to me the auto pilot master warning EICAS message occurred at 1;000 feet on the coupled ILS approach into ZZZ.  The Captain stated maintenance refused to take the write ups as it was going to ZZZ1 and they would casually look at it there.   I immediately called maintenance and asked why they wouldn't take write ups and said from what it sounded like a write up was not warranted.   The maintenance controller asked if I wished to MEL the autopilot and I stated how can I?  At this point I should have immediately refused the plane; but at Company; refusing to fly gets us an immediate phone call from the Chief Pilot office.   We Were now operating in the red of the threat and error management philosophy.   On the way to ZZZ1 we experienced moderate turbulence as expected and when we turned base for Runway XY the field was surrounded by virga.  The FO and I briefed a go around and the windshear escape maneuver once more.   On final at about 1;200 feet the Autopilot Fail master warning EICAS  message appeared and the Autopilot disconnected.  I continued to hand fly the aircraft and remained stable to about 300 feet where I encountered increasing performance and the approach became unstable.   A normal go-around was executed and we called positive rate gear up; we received the windshear caution.   Immediately after we hit severe turbulence.  We diverted to ZZZ without further incident.  The remainder of the flight was performed without the autopilot.Company maintenance did not take a write up from a Captain. I wrote up the AP failure and the encounter with severe turbulence. All write-ups must be taken by maintenance.

column121: I acted as the pilot-in-command and sole occupant of a Cessna 402 aircraft on a flight in visual meteorological conditions. Prior to departure; I conducted a preflight inspection in accordance with the manufacturer's recommended procedures; and I followed the preflight checklist. All systems appeared to operate normally during takeoff; climb; and cruise flight.During the approach to landing; I monitored the UNICOM frequency when another pilot approximately 4-5 miles ahead of me; approaching the same runway; advised that there was turbulence on final approach. While on short final approach; I experienced turbulence and added power to maintain a proper glide path.  At that point; I noticed that the left (i.e.; critical) engine was no longer producing power. As a result; the aircraft nose yawed left (i.e.; toward the dead engine) and I applied full right rudder to maintain directional control.Since I was on a short final approach to the runway; I reduced power in order to land while maintaining my airspeed above Vmc (i.e.; minimum controllable airspeed with critical engine failure). However; the gusty wind drifted the airplane to the left of center line. Since I had applied full right rudder to maintain runway centerline while in flight; upon touchdown the airplane aggressively spun to the right and departed the runway; which caused the landing gear to collapse.When the aircraft came to a complete stop; I shut down the right engine; secured the aircraft; and exited through the cabin door without incident.

column121: Myself and my Captain were flying our final leg from ZZZ to HWD. Conditions were night VMC and we had the Hayward city and airport location in sight. The Captain was pilot flying and I was pilot monitoring. The Captain was manually flying and our clearance was to maintain 4;000 until established on the localizer. I went into my iPad to look at my charts; a few moments later I heard the Controller tell us; 'low altitude alert; check altitude immediately; MVA is 3;900.' When I looked over at the Captain's PFD; I saw we had descended to between 3;300-3;400 feet. The Captain took over the radios and corrected the altitude and continued uneventfully.

column121: I was in the traffic pattern doing touch and go landings with a student at ZZZ (one of five aircraft in the pattern).  We were approaching mid-field right downwind for Runway XX; and tower advised that we were following another aircraft on base; which we advised we had in-sight (which was number two; making us number 3.) As we approached our base leg; tower told us the base turn was our discretion; and we began turning base.  As soon as we did so; the traffic we were following (Aircraft Y) called tower and advised that they needed to do a 360 for spacing (it appeared they had turned their base leg too soon; and were too close to the traffic they were following.  Tower approved and notified them that once they completed their 360 to watch out for us; and that they would be now following us (making us now #2 for the runway; and them #3).My student turned final and we were on an approximately 1.5 nm final; descending through 700-800 feet; when my student stated; 'I think they're turning right at us!'Simultaneously Aircraft Y had apparently just completed their 360 turn and Tower inquired if they had us in sight and were to follow us; they stated they were looking for traffic.I looked up to suddenly see the other aircraft heading directly across our final approach path; and almost on a head-on course; coming at us approximately from 45 degrees off of our left nose.  As I looked; I also simultaneously received an ADS-B traffic alert from Foreflight on my iPad; indicated the other traffic; heading directly at us and at the same altitude.I immediately stated 'my controls'; took over control of the aircraft; and quickly began a descending turn to the right to create distance from the other traffic; I looked up again and saw the other aircraft also start a sharp bank to their right; while simultaneously telling the tower that they now had us in-sight.  I remained in control of the aircraft and executed an (otherwise) uneventful landing.Tower did not acknowledge the event.  It seemed that the controller was very task saturated with the five aircraft in the pattern.  It seemed they were also distracted by a King Air 350 that was handed over to tower from ZZZ Approach and setup for a right downwind for Runway XX. Initially tower had told them to enter left downwind; but they were adamant that they wanted right downwind; which I believe both added to the confusion; as well as tied up the tower frequency unnecessarily with their continued back and forth communications. It did not help that the King Air had requested and was then doing a 360 turn for spacing of their own; right on the upwind leg of Runway XX when there were multiple other aircraft in the traffic pattern.It should be noted that while we were on short final; the Aircraft Y involved in this incident had once again turned base too soon; and I noticed that they were less than 0.5nm behind us.  They were so close that they in fact were ordered to go-around by the Tower because we had just touched down and were still rolling out when they crossed the runway threshold behind us.I believe contributing to the incident were: The pilot of Aircraft Y failing to maintain situational awareness resulting in inadequate spacing thus requesting multiple 360 turns; their failure to adequately scan for traffic and maintain a proper rectangular course in the traffic pattern causing them to cross our final approach course at a 45 degree angle; tower controller being task saturated with multiple aircraft in the traffic pattern; and the King Air traffic adding to the confusion by adamantly requesting to enter the right downwind (which conflicted with the other aircraft already in the traffic pattern for Runway XX; which is right traffic.)

column121: It was a busy day in the pattern at LMO and BJC was closed for pattern work. I had already done one lap in the pattern at LMO and was in the downwind for another lap. In the downwind; the skydiving operation announced a few times; 'Jumpers away'. I saw them and told my student to make a slightly wider pattern to accommodate them. Just as we made a slight deviation; an aircraft; Aircraft Y; entered the pattern mid field downwind and my ADS-B indicated they were the same altitude as us. Aircraft Y announced they had 'downwind traffic in sight' but it was likely another plane. They cut us off not only in the downwind; but also headed straight for the jump zone. The jump plane announced frantically 'parachutes have been deployed'. I was careful not to climb or descend while I did not have Aircraft Y in sight because ADS-B indicated they were same altitudes. We did not see them until they were passing just under us. Once I saw them; I asked their intentions with no response. I decided it was best to depart the airport back to BJC. Upon returning to BJC; I spoke with many instructors from different flight schools in the area and it seems like the traffic pattern at LMO has become increasingly dangerous. Many instructors have had near misses with other pilots not making calls or entering the pattern however they wish.

column121: The event happened at night on taxi out from gate to Runway XXL. There were significant amounts of thunderstorms surrounding ZZZ requiring the airport to only have Runway XXL for takeoff and XXR for landing. Upon leaving the ramp area; we were given clearance to taxi to XXL via Taxiways '1' then [Taxiway] '23.' We turned north on [Taxiway] '1' and followed a B737. The B737 aircraft stopped holding short of [Taxiway] '23.' After about 30 seconds it proceeded and turned onto [Taxiway] 23 and we followed. It was as we turned onto [Taxiway] 23 that both the Captain and I heard the ATC Ground Controller give excited directions to 'hurry up/go fast.' The key point here is that we never heard our call sign used for any of these calls and assumed he was talking to someone else. As we advanced to a position abeam Runway XXR on our right; the Ground Controller called our call sign and I responded. He then told us; using our call sign; to 'go fast; aircraft on short final to Runway XXR.' We saw the conflict and tried to move faster but it was too late and the aircraft was forced to execute a go-around. The Controller told us the aircraft had to go-around and chastised us for missing several calls. I responded that we 'never heard it.'Ultimately; I believe one of two things happened--either the Controller made several excited transmissions either omitting our call sign or used an incorrect call sign; or both the Captain and I missed several radio calls made to us until a conflict happened.
column123: We had been reassigned after a long weather delay and misconnect out of ZZZ.  We had to consult with dispatch to confirm our clearance and routing. ZZZ ATC had reconfigured airport and were landing and departing runways XXL/R. At spot X we were instructed to taxi to XXL; [Taxiway] 1; [Taxiway] 23. As we approached [Taxiway] 23 a B737 was stopped ahead of us. That aircraft then turned onto [Taxiway] 23. We followed; no instructions to hold short of the XXR approach was given. The Ground Controller then told the B737 to hurry; then told us to hurry and cross XXR approach; saying that we were expected to hold short of [Taxiway] 23.  We were not instructed to hold short of Taxiway 23.

column121: We were cleared for the visual approach to [Runway] 28R from the arrival. I set the altitude to 6;000 for the FAF of ZIXIP. My original plan was to fly towards the FAF at 6;000 [feet] then turn base leg then final just inside of it to start my descent after being fully configured. I decided to turn earlier and mistakenly followed what I thought to be my glide slope; therefore; I believed myself to be too high and needing to descend for a stabilized approach. Turning base to final; we got an audible alert of 'obstacle' to which I was searching outside but did not see. Then at the Captain's prompt suggestion I tried to level off but was not aggressive enough. The slow descent resulted in a 'pull up' alert and I was able to maintain altitude but the Captain noted the RA of being around 650 AGL. I was able to continue my turn base to final stabilized and had no other incidents with the event. Lack of situational awareness on my part and last-minute decision to keep a tighter traffic pattern which would require compressed time to configure the aircraft. Mainly; my mistake in believing/following a false glide slope which lead to my confusion. Following the ILS profiles even in VMC conditions for terrain/obstacle clearance. Keeping my situational awareness outside of the cockpit more & remaining vigilant especially in VMC conditions.

column121: I was Pilot Flying. I triggered a stick shaker/pusher at the Final Approach Fix. I recovered per SOP.[There was] a northbound squall line west of the field. A last minute wind shift caused us to switch runways. Although it was fast; we were able to set up and brief before intercepting course. ATC forgot to turn [us] on the ILS; so we went through the localizer course. This led to reactionary vectors and the aircraft doing S turns until we passed the final approach fix. At this point we were flaps 30 with the power reduced. I called for the go-around; but when I pressed the autopilot disconnect button; I became fixated on the lack of a missed approach course. (I didn't press the TO-GA button.) While troubleshooting; the airspeed continued to decrease leading to a simultaneous stick shaker and pusher. I lowered the nose and recovered per the training; paying special attention to the radar altimeter. I briefly oversped flaps 8 on recovery.Stall recovery training works. I was able to accomplish the recovery quickly without having to think about it. However; it shouldn't have gotten that far. It was my first go-around since OE. I was task saturated and in the moment couldn't remember where the TO-GA button was. We were able to handle weather; ATC error; runway changes and an instrument approach at the same time. However; because our go around was triggered at a relatively untrained aircraft state/position; I missed a critical aspect of the maneuver. I don't believe this is a common problem; but incorporating go-arounds during recurrent in various positions may be beneficial.
