column121: We were operating with MEL 27-XX; Pitch Control Disconnect Advisory Message; which requires other MELs; including MEL 22-XX Autopilot System. Approach Control turned us onto final for a visual approach to Runway XX and instructed us to contact Tower. After tuning the #1 com radio to the Tower frequency; we began configuring the aircraft for landing when we received the autopilot warning along with the AP (Autopilot) Fail and Pitch Control Disconnect EICAS messages. This was a bit distracting because the autopilot was not in use nor had it been used the entire flight due to [the] MEL. After determining all systems were okay to continue; we then started receiving the EGPWS glideslope warning. I determined this was due to ATC having the ILS for Runway XY turned on. The FO (First Officer) had established a normal visual approach angle so I told him to continue. I then reset the frequencies in both nav radios to silence the errant warnings. After landing; the Tower issued taxi instructions and said that next time we should contact Tower for a landing clearance.[The cause was] multiple distractions. Don't let minor distractions dominate the flight deck.

column121: Arriving in TUS we had briefed the RNAV RNP Y Rwy 11 approach.  Our clearance was for the ZONNA 1 arrival which took us from SSAND to OTUKE.  Just outside SSAND we were cleared to fly the lateral course for the RNAV Y.  We cleaned up the box and turned on to the course.  The next fix was LUYEG; our clearance was to cross at 11;000 feet cleared for the RNAV Y approach.  We slowed to 240; went flaps 1; did the LAVS procedure.  The FMA went to PATH and looked normal.  We read the Approach checklist.  I checked the FMAs again; it indicated SPD not PATH.  There was no VNAV scale to indicate high or low on the path.  I selected V/S and slowed the rate of descent.  Before I could enter the next stepdown altitude in the MCP; ATC called with a low altitude alert.  There was no EGPWS warning.  I disconnected the A/P and initiated a climb.   We were in VFR conditions.  We reengaged the A/P and selected 9;500 feet for a crossing altitude at HODPU.  We did the LAVS procedure outside of HODPU.  The FMA went to PATH and looked normal until it crossed the fix and then it went to speed.  After that we used the A/P to fly the lateral course put each crossing altitude in the MCP.  On final I disconnected the automation [and] flew a visual approach.  First Officer did an excellent job monitoring; calling out deviations; and analyzing the problem.  We delayed reading the approach checklist until the FMS was correct for the approach that we intended to fly.  I was distracted by the checklist reading and that caused a delay for me to recognize that the FMA for the vertical mode was no longer in PATH.  We had asked the ABQ Center Controller to clear us onto the approach [but] he told us to make our request to Approach Control.  It meant that we were making changes to the FMS just before turning on to the approach.First Officer and I discussed the FMS setup; the approach; our procedures in the debrief.  We were unable to determine what error caused the airplane to not fly the RNAV approach correctly.  It would have been helpful to do everything earlier; complete the changes to the FMS and read the Approach checklist well before we were cleared onto the approach path.  That would free both pilots to monitor the automation and catch deviations sooner.

column121: With just 0.9 hrs of PIC time YTD; I arrived at my flying club's towered home field; ZZZ; rested and eager to shake off some rust. My first sign of how much rust I had came when I accepted a Ground instruction to RWY XX but taxied to the nearer RWY XY. After completing my runup; Ground advised me to taxi to RWY XX. I thanked Ground for 'that catch' and received a double-click 'you're welcome.' I paused; ensured I was focused and not letting myself feel rushed; and proceeded to RWY XX. I completed four takeoffs and landings; and after reviewing LiveATC recordings I believe that my radio communications were professional and crisp. Ground did request that I confirm one hold-short instruction; and Tower asked that I confirm a 'give way to helicopter' instruction as part of a 'hold short' read back. However; I acknowledged and complied with all instructions promptly; including hold-shorts; midfield reports; extended down winds; go-arounds; no delay etc.; using proper phraseology. A midair near-miss occurred when two Cessna's with 'XXXX' in their call signs began to turn base at the same time -- Aircraft Y from Right Downwind; and I; Aircraft Z; from Left Downwind. I mistook a 'cleared for the option' instruction for Aircraft Y as being intended for me; and radioed my acceptance for a full stop. I believed Tower had cleared me to land. The conflicting traffic had been advised of 'that Cessna on Left Downwind;' from which I further inferred my own landing priority over this newcomer to the pattern. Upon review of the recordings; it is clear to me that Tower attempted to clarify that the landing clearance was for Aircraft Y; and that the call out to my position was purely advisory for Aircraft Y -- and not a reaffirmation of a clearance for Aircraft Z to land. However; someone stepped on the frequency; and I missed the clarification. It was only after Tower instructed me; 'You need to turn right now' that I saw the conflicting traffic approximately 1/2 mile turning right base. Tower then instructed me for the first time that I was #2 for the field. I attribute this near-miss primarily to my own lack of recency; but secondarily to additional factors. I lost situational awareness of other similarly-[call] signed Cessna's entering the pattern from the opposite direction. ATC could have clarified our landing sequence earlier; or verbally emphasized the differences in our call signs. (A minute later; Tower itself briefly confused two call signs; instructing me to turn Right Base when I was already on Short Final from Left Base; Tower quickly corrected itself when I advised of my position.) It was also a very busy pattern in which to shake off rust -- for pilots and ATC alike. Simultaneous radio transmissions obscured instructions that might otherwise have been sufficiently clear. Stress was apparent: After landing; I complied immediately with Tower's instruction to exit without delay -- only to be told urgently to 'stop right now' to avoid a jet on its takeoff roll. (I appreciate Tower's urgency: I had acknowledged the hold-short instruction and was already preparing to stop; but to their eyes I was still in 'no delay' mode as I transitioned from RWY to Taxiway.) Tower similarly expressed frustration with another pilot for requesting 'a turn around a point;' a term that Tower did not recognize; they doubtless would have preferred 'a 360' or 'a circle.' When I was finished with my pattern work; I briefly expressed my appreciation to Tower and received a friendly 'no problem' in response. I think we all did what we could to be as clear as possible (urgently if the situation demanded it); and to correct our mistakes. Although it will be frustrating at times; on balance I think this is a good set of attitudes to have; especially as the entire National Airspace System shakes off varying degrees of rust coming out of the pandemic. The dangers here were addressed; but a slightly more intransigent attitude; or a slightly more congested frequency; might have tipped the balance to a midair collision.
column125: A Single Light Aircraft Pilot; with low flight time because of the pandemic; reported a NMAC turning final after getting confused because of similar call signs. The Tower controller separated the aircraft and a normal landing was accomplished.

column121: On above time and date at SJC I taxied from FBO to 30L at intersection C for departure.  Upon completing the run up at about a 45 degree angle to runway facing departure end; I switched frequency to Tower frequency and immediately heard tower call me asking if I was ready.  I responded in the affirmative.  Tower's response was partially stepped on but I thought I was given take off clearance with an advisory of traffic on 2 1/2 mile final.  Since with Tower's initial call to me asking if I was ready I sensed possible urgency and in my attempt to assist in that perceived urgency I quickly ran through pre take off flow and turned aircraft perpendicular to runway which placed the aircraft partially over the hold short line.  Upon becoming perpendicular to runway I looked for the said traffic on final and saw the traffic not on 2 1/2 mile final but on short final.  I immediately stopped the aircraft at which time Tower urgently advised me to hold short.  I believe the tail of the aircraft was approximately at the hold short line with of course the rest of the aircraft beyond the hold short line.  The Tower did advise the landing traffic to go around.  After take off I was given a phone number to call.  Immediately upon landing I called said phone number as requested and discussed occurrence with Tower.

column121: The instructor and I were on a checkout flight in a flight school C172. After the training flight; we returned to ZZZ for 1 touch and go and 1 full stop landing. It was VFR conditions; but hazy with scattered clouds about 2000 feet above the airport. The airport was very busy with multiple training aircraft in the pattern using both runways. We were assigned Runway XXR. We completed our touch and go and were instructed to fly runway heading. After several minutes; we advised ZZZ Tower that we were still runway heading; at which point the controller advised us to turn right crosswind for right closed traffic to Runway XXR. While on the downwind; the controller advised us to extend downwind and he would call our base. We read back the instruction.When we were approximately .75 statute miles northwest of the airport; the controller changed our clearance to fly a close approach and we were cleared for the option. We read back the clearance and turned right base. Because we were high; we extended flaps to increase our descent rate. As I was about to turn final; the controller (a different voice this time) advised us to extend downwind; which we read back. I began an immediate climbing left turn. The controller immediately followed that clearance by clearing Aircraft Y to fly an immediate left 360. Aircraft Y was on final to either [Runway] XXL or XXR. There were multiple flight school aircraft with similar call signs in the pattern. Aircraft Y mistakenly made a right 360. I gained a visual contact on Aircraft Y; who was at my one o'clock low. I continued my left turn and climb to avoid collision with the aircraft. We extended downwind and were then given clearance to land. We landed without further incident. I believe that the hazy conditions; busy controllers; controller change or controller training; and multiple aircraft from the same flight school and similar call signs created confusion leading to this incident.

column121: I had Aircraft X in LUAW (Line Up and Wait) Runway XX waiting for Aircraft Y to complete a Touch-N-Go Runway XXR. While the Aircraft Y was around short final Runway XXR; a Helicopter (Aircraft Z) called ready to depart from west side of the airfield. I also had 2 helicopters (Aircraft A / Aircraft B) inbound who were 2 - 2.5 miles out to land. I quickly responded to Aircraft Z to depart and proceed outbound.Based on memory; I don't recall giving Aircraft Z any traffic about Aircraft X in LUAW. My memory feels like about 1-2 minutes now have passed and Aircraft Y is now upwind Runway XXR and crossing the intersection of Runway XX. I tell Aircraft Y to continue upwind. I then clear Aircraft X for take-off Runway XX. As I'm scanning the runways watching Aircraft X start his departure rotation; I noticed a helicopter going east about to cross the departure end of Runway XX. I thought that helicopter was Aircraft A which was supposed to land and according to our Letter of Agreement (LOA); would make him restricted west of Runway XX. Finally; I reached out to Aircraft A and told them to turn base now to land. Aircraft A then responded that they were still 1 mile west. I then realized my mistake and that the helicopter crossing the departure end was my helicopter outbound. I became flustered and a bit panicked and tried to reach out to that Aircraft Z; but accidentally called them the 3rd helicopter's call sign; Aircraft B; adding even more confusion to the whole scenario. At this point Aircraft X passes below Aircraft Z by maybe 50 to 100 ft. I apologize to Aircraft X about the close aircraft; he responded that he had the helicopter in sight. I then later reach out to Aircraft Z and apologize; and they said it was OK. Aircraft X did not report a RA and neither aircraft reported having to take evasive maneuvers.

column121: Descending in to SNA on DSNEE 4 arrival between CARZZ and KADYN somewhere between 13;000-15;000 ft. we had a near miss with a glider. I was flying; never saw it. We got a primary target only; no mode C; 30 seconds or less from when the CA actually saw the glider; he guesses maybe 100 ft. vertically and 500 ft. laterally. The glider was right on the arrival path. It startled him; and even though I never saw it his reaction startled me. Great CRM; professionalism; training for us to get our focus back and not get distracted and become an unstable approach statistic in to the challenging SNA; especially after having hot brakes climbing out of ZZZ1 and having to lower the gear; it felt like a true multiple threat simulator session! Great scenario for an IOE.
column123: Operating flight from ZZZ1 to SNA. Cleared to descend via the DSNEE 4 Arrival. Between CARZZ and KADYN Intersections at approximately 13;500 ft.; we received a 'Traffic; Traffic' TA with only a primary yellow circle popping up as a target with no altitude or trend information. The TA occurred about 20 seconds prior to us and the target converged. Center called 'traffic at 12 o'clock; altitude and type unknown'. The weather was VFR; but it was hazy. We scanned the immediate area above and below and did not see it.Then I looked to my left and I saw a white colored glider passing less than 500 ft. laterally and within 100 ft. vertically. I did not pick it up until it was at our 8 o'clock position and as we were descending on the arrival. We must have NEARLY HIT HIM! He was heading southeast bound.This occurrence made the hair stand up on my neck and it really shook me up. Just as I reported the near mid-air; ATC gave us a frequency change to Approach. I was so taken-aback by what had just happened; I had to ask Center to repeat the frequency. Just as I was trying to re-establish contact with Approach; I heard Approach calling us on Guard; which I thought was strange as we had only been off of Approach for less than 30 seconds. There seemed to be either a late hand-off from Center and they were looking for us; or they saw the near miss and were trying to call us. I mentioned that we had just missed a glider and we were trying to regain our composure but we had switched over in a fairly timely manner.After a busy; but uneventful approach and landing to 20R at SNA; during the taxi-in to Gate XX; SNA Ground alerted us that Approach was requesting that we call them once we parked.I called and spoke to a Supervisor. His first question was am I going to file a NASA report. I stated that I would. He took my name and number in the event that this occurrence needed further investigation. That glider was sailing on the main ridge-line just prior to entering the area. HE HAD NO MODE C transponder at an altitude that should have required him to have it. The glider was sailing on the direct arrival path of the STAR; and we are lucky that we hadn't arrived at our point of convergence 10 seconds sooner. When I saw him he was about 100 ft. higher than us at 8 o'clock as we were descending.

column121: Air Traffic Control requested that Aircraft X fly a heading of 110; and maintain 3;000 ft. until established on the localizer for Runway XXL ILS approach. The autopilot was immediately disengaged; and the advisory was followed to climb; then updated to bank left. Final deviation altitude was 3;200 ft. MSL; and was quickly corrected to the assigned 3;000 ft. when traffic was no factor. Air Traffic Control was advised of the deviation.
column123: We were on a heading to intercept approach path for Runway XXL into ZZZ. Approach gave us heading 110 degrees to intercept and maintain 3;000 ft. until established on the ILS XXL approach. Once we were established on heading received Traffic Advisory warning advise to turn and climb at approximately 400 ft. closing to 200 ft. I disengaged autopilot as I turned and climbed following traffic advisory warning. My First Officer advised ATC about traffic advisory and deviation. We then returned to 3;000 ft. and established on approach.

column121: Flying Pilot thought he was cleared for visual approach.ATC then gave us a low altitude warning and instructed us to climb back up to 3;000 feet.During descent the Flying Pilot asked the Monitoring Pilot to ask the ATC Controller how to cancel our IFR flight plan. The Controller then gave the Pilot Monitoring instructions on how to cancel our IFR flight plan. Upon hearing this; the Flying Pilot mistook it for cleared for a visual approach. The altitude alerter was then changed and no communication was exchanged to the other Pilot about the change.We complied with instructions.Suggestions - Verify you heard what you thought you heard. Always keep other Pilot informed of altitude change in altitude alerter if self changed or have the other Pilot change it.

column121: Flying from ZZZ to SLC IFR; I was given the Cargo 1 Visual approach to Runway 32 (over the top) by SLC Approach 135.5. After my descent from 11;000 ft. to 7;000 ft.; while leveling off at AMENR way point; Tower called traffic at my 12 o'clock and 5 miles at 7;000; Aircraft Y. I assume the pilot was doing a VFR transition north over I-15 which runs directly below the approach path. I did not see the aircraft; however; he reported to see Aircraft X. Tower told me to stay on the profile and he would call my base turn for Runway 32. The profile has you level at 7;000 until DUECE way point. In-between AMENR and DUECE I spotted Aircraft Y at my 1 o'clock slightly lower within 400 ft. heading for a collision course. I immediately performed a climbing left hand turn to avoid the traffic; while he did a left turn at the same time. We narrowly missed each other. I called Tower in my turn to advise them of the deviation for traffic. Finished the flight with no other incident.I would advise that VFR transitions north while the Cargo 1 approach is being used stay below 6;500 ft. MSL; or refused entirely.

column121: During an initial flight lesson with a student; I was landing on Runway XX with a slight crosswind. Upon touching down; right at the point of adding brake pressure; a gust of wind picked up the left wing. The wind also caused us to drift aggressively to the right of the center line and pointed the nose off of the runway. I felt that I did not have enough energy to go-around; but also felt the aircraft was moving too fast for an aggressive left rudder input. I decided that the best option was to apply maximum braking in hopes of stopping before reaching the runway edge. Unfortunately; I was unable to stop the airplane before it struck a runway edge light and rolled approximately 10 ft. off of the asphalt. The aircraft nor anyone on board received any damages or injuries. Upon reflection of the event; I suspect that my failure to apply full opposite aileron for proper crosswind correction once touched down may have been a leading factor. If I had made that input; the initial uplifting left wing may not have occurred; which would have kept the aircraft pointed in the right direction. I do not regret my judgement in solving the problem after it had occurred. I believe that an attempt to go around would have caused us to strike multiple objects off the side of the runway; and an attempt to steer back towards center line could have causes the aircraft to skid/flip.

column121: Prior to entering the traffic pattern at UAO; I could hear over the radio that it would be very busy with all the air traffic and could sense the air traffic controller was not taking his job too serious because he had had a comedic inflection in his voice; which I've never heard any other controller use. I was told to enter left downwind for 'A' taxiway and report when abeam the tower. I entered the left downwind and reported when abeam the tower. Nothing was heard from the controller. After thirty seconds had passed I made another call to the tower and the controller instructed me to pass behind the traffic that was on right base for Runway 35. During the downwind approach I got multiple 'Traffic' advisories. This became a distraction and I reported back that I had the traffic on final in sight because I had been focused on that aircraft. This was not the correct aircraft that I was supposed to pass behind and my transmission was not corrected for by the controller so I turned for left base. As I'm turning I am getting another 'Traffic' annunciation and look out my right door. Down below I see Aircraft Y 500 feet under me. By the time the controller comes up on the radio and questions my intentions it is already too late. Luckily the Cessna saw me and began descending or else we would have been a much closer incident. I maintained my altitude and requested a descending right 360 because I had overshot 'A' taxiway. This in turn caused a fixed wing that was departing to the south from the right downwind to make a left 360 to avoid me. I proceeded down to the taxiway to park on the company ramp. I should have not got so caught up on the aircraft on final that caused me to not read back the proper clearance back to the tower. I was also distracted from the multiple 'Traffic' annunciations while I was trying to locate where these aircraft were in particular. I feel that had the controller also done his part in correcting my incorrect read back that the situation could have easily been avoided.

column121: I was flying Aircraft X. I was incoming to SQL from the north for [a] full stop landing; exiting the SFO Bravo; heading south towards the airport. SQL Tower initially asked me to enter a left downwind for Runway 30; keeping 101 freeway to my left; and they would call my left base for Runway 30. A couple of minutes later they asked me to turn left; overfly the runway and enter a right base for Runway 30. I crossed the runway at 1200 feet and began to descend to pattern altitude. SQL Tower told me to follow Aircraft Y that was on an extended right base for 30. As I was searching for Aircraft Y; I began to turn right downwind for 30 right as I noticed Aircraft Z cross in front of me....both of us executing an evasive maneuver. I told Tower that I had a near miss with Aircraft Z....and Tower responded that this aircraft was not on frequency. I was able to land safely.   Upon reviewing ADS-B data and ATC recording of the event; it appears that Aircraft Z took off from Runway 30 and was asked to follow Aircraft A; Aircraft A was told to proceed to KNBR [radio station] antenna and then proceed on course; which they did. Aircraft Z however did not follow Aircraft A and turned too early into downwind. The tower also gave Aircraft Z a very early frequency change so they were not on frequency at the time of [the] near miss. It seems to me that the failure of the Tower to verify that Aircraft Z had Aircraft A in sight was a factor in this near miss; as well as the Tower's early frequency change for Aircraft Z.

column121: On this aircraft; annual inspection is current and the most recent oil change had happened less than 2 flight hours prior; with the mechanic noting that the filter cut check was normal. I had flown the aircraft uneventfully for about 0.9 hours previous to this. Preflight was normal and showed good fuel quantity in all tanks and no evidence of water or sediment in any fuel cell or the main sump. Engine oil was full and run-up checks were normal. I executed a straight-in approach [planning on a touch-and-go]. I conducted my descent check in accordance with the checklist along with a 'GUMPS' check. Final approach and landing were normal. I pushed up power for the 'go' portion and the aircraft initially responded normally.  Shortly after takeoff I heard the RPMs drop and felt the airplane experience a partial power loss. I checked mixture; prop; and throttle all in the full forward position (they were) and maintained landing attitude until touchdown.  During this time I announced (incorrectly) 'engine failure' on Tower frequency. Once the aircraft was down; the engine was still running and I was able to taxi clear of the runway.  At this point; after continued power manipulation the engine stopped. I maintained radio contact with Tower until a tug arrived to taxi the aircraft back to the hangar. I documented throttle/mixture settings; fuel quantity indicators; and checked the sumps. There was no apparent damage to the aircraft or any property; and no injuries.

column121: During a training flight with a student who was preparing for solo operations; the student made his second approach to the runway in calm conditions. During the flare; the aircraft yawed to the left and he attempted to correct it using right rudder. Shortly thereafter the aircraft touched down; at which time it immediately exited the runway to the right. The aircraft crossed a grassy area; then an adjacent taxiway and another grassy area; before coming to rest. As the Instructor; I had taken control once it began exiting the runway. Once the aircraft had stopped; we exited the grassy area back to the taxiway and held short of the runway. I exited the aircraft and took a visual review of the aircraft for damage. Having seen none I asked the student if he would like to continue and while he said he was shaken; he was OK with continuing. I then took control of the aircraft and proceeded to demonstrate an approach and landing. Upon finishing the lesson and reviewing the aircraft; I noticed a slight nick in the prop. In hindsight; I should have shut the aircraft down and suspended operations immediately; to do a more thorough review of the aircraft as well as allow my student to gather his thoughts and focus.

column121: I was flying RNAV 24 into LKR with a student. We both each made 2-3 radio calls on CTAF once we were 10 miles out to make sure our locations were clear on approach; and we checked to make sure we were on the correct frequency multiple times. Winds were calm; so we planned to land on runway 24; and suddenly I saw a plane taking off of 6 while we were 3 miles out on the approach. The plane never made any calls; and only slightly broke off to our left on departure. I took controls and broke off the approach to the right some while he was passing by. It seemed he knew where we were; but still decided to take off the opposite runway and squeeze past us.

column121: Shortly after touchdown on Runway XX the plane began drifting to the left; controllability was deteriorating rapidly. The SIC was attempting to correct the drifting; I (the PIC/PM) stated we're drifting left and applied Full; Authoritative; Firm pressure to the right rudder.  The plane began to straighten out and correct right. The left tires were on the edge of the runway next to the grass and water runoff; causing hydroplaning. As the plane decelerated I was able to regain control and corrected back to the center of the runway. The SIC further assisted in maintaining control of the aircraft as we could feel the tires slipping and hydroplaning. With team work and crew coordination; we regained full control of the Aircraft again and we're able to stop and clear the runway onto a taxiway.Contributing factors include; but not limited to: Weather; rain; winds/crosswinds; darkness/night; unfamiliar airport; wet runway; uncontrolled airport; fresh grass clippings and water pooling along edge of runway; long duty day greater than 12 hours;  consecutive long duty days; multiple flights in a duty day (4th);  human factors; pilot/crew exhaustion; crew dehydration due to extreme heat and humidity throughout the day; high stress loads throughout the day; get there itis; external pressure from passengers;  crews desire to succeed and make everyone happy by completing the mission.
column123: After touchdown the aircraft began to drift to the left. Control correction was applied as the aircraft was drifting but with very little effect. As we neared the left edge of the runway the Pilot Monitoring (PM) assisted the Pilot Flying (PF) and applied full right rudder.  Due to the water collecting on the runway the aircraft did not respond very well to the rudder input until we decelerated.  At that point we had drifted slightly off the runway edge onto the grass. With crew coordination we were able to recover the aircraft back to the runway center line. We excited the runway without further incident.Contributing factors:Weather; fatigue; hot temperatures and humidity throughout the day; dehydration; lack of nutrition; end of a long duty day (arrived at ZZZ on hour 13) preceded by multiple long duty days.This event has opened my eyes a little wider to the complexity of risk assessment. In the future more emphasis will be placed on assessing external pressure; fatigue; and physiological needs.  Also more assertiveness towards these areas of risk assessment. ADM is a very complex system of checks and balances and awareness to even the slightest deviation can prevent future contributing factors to this and other events. A lot could be benefited from a personal assessment matrix to pair with a flight risk assessment matrix to help with mitigating a go/no go decision.
column125: The crew of a King Air 300 reported loss of control during landing but were able to regain control. The crew cited weather; including wet runway and cross winds; also sighting fatigue and a long duty day as contributing factors.

column121: Landing at ID24 (short grass runway) to the North which meant there is a large hump in the middle of the runway- about 15 feet high but with a short but smooth run up to it. Asked student if I could do the landing as this was the second time I had landed on a grass strip and the first time on a runway with a pronounced hill in the middle and that hill was only about 700 feet past the threshold- I knew we would be rolling over it. If you rolled over too fast it was possible for the plane to want to become temporarily airborne. There were some very tall trees on final and it was a good idea to touch down as close to the threshold as possible. The idea was to fly a very stabilized approach as low as possible over the trees and then get into the flare just as we were crossing the shore of the lake where the runway began. Set up a long straight-in over Garfield Bay; crossed the shore when I realized I did not recognize the landmarks at the lower altitude we were at; and was already descending to what I thought were the tops of the trees on final.  I think we were below the legal altitude limit a few times when considering how far from the airport we were so I climbed a little so as not to bother residents and get a better view.  Then my student who knows the area well spotted the landmarks I needed. Set up the steep approach and the rest went better than expected. Contributing factors were [the] challenging approach and landing in the type of plane we were flying had me anxious- would have been really easy in a Aircraft X. More challenging to me than landing at Telluride with a 25 gusting 35 crosswind during light snow (Aircraft Y). I did not pick out my landmarks for the approach with the idea I could not orient so well when down low. Did not do a low approach to check out the runway where I wouldn't be so worried about getting stabilized on the approach early- we just overflew the airport at pattern altitude having taken off from Timber Basin about 40 minutes earlier. Did not just do a left downwind/base/final because I was worried about being super-stabilized for the short/soft field landing.  I could have just done a longer downwind and my orientation would have been very good. The high terrain on the left downwind had something to do with my decision. How to avoid [is to] do a better job of picking out landmarks for making an approach plan to challenging airports. Fly the pattern as long as it is safe. At short unprepared strips with tall trees all around it do a low approach and check things out; ensure safety and make a good plan.

column121: FO Flying / AP on in Nav mode. Just as we were making our turn to transition from the arrival to the approach. FO selected APP mode on FCU the LOC & GS  captured simultaneously . Initially the GS indicated below us but as soon as the aircraft went to capture mode the GS indication jumped above the 1/2 scale deflection up. The aircraft followed the GS as it moved up to 1/2 scale high until the aircraft pitch was approximately 20 degrees nose up. Thrust had been at idle so the speed vector showed a negative trend while the engines were trying to accelerate.  The power finally caught up and the nose came back down . We were able to continue the approach and land. I talked to the crew from the day before and they had a similar thing happen to them.  There is an airport very close to this intersection. I wonder if there maybe some interference from the [other] airport. If there is some interference we need to add a note to the 10-7 pages to warn the crews this situation could occur.

column121: On descent into ZZZ; we set up for the visual to XX backed up by the ILS. I was the pilot flying. We were given a runway change from Runway XX to XY. We made the necessary changes and briefed them. The ILS frequency for XX and XY at ZZZ are the same. After we were not receiving a good signal on approach to XY; we asked Approach if they had switched from XX to XY. He responded roger and sent us to Tower. He sounded busy and both the Captain and I agreed that he had not really processed the question.Once with Tower; we asked the same thing and they advised us to let them know if we ever got a good signal while on approach. We didn't have good ILS guidance and the Captain suggested I click off the autopilot and hand-fly it down. I did so and was working to maneuver myself on final. I overshot final while continuing to descend. I was worried that this would trigger a GPWS warning as I could see that I was near high terrain to the south of the field. I adjusted my vertical speed as I corrected toward final; but still got a GPWS warning. I responded to the warning as I continued correcting back to runway centerline.We were immediately out of the GPWS threat and elected to continue the visual approach but we were left high after the response. It was here that I should have seen we were not in a good position to continue and should have gone around. As we continued down final; we were at idle thrust and flight spoilers were out as we continued to configure the aircraft. I don't believe that we were fully configured below 1000 ft.; which was outside of stabilized approach criteria; and we still had the flight spoilers extended - which; after review; was against company limitation. By 500 ft. we were fully configured but still fast and slightly high. Another opportunity to conduct a go-around. The power remained in idle for almost the entire approach. We landed on Runway XY.Due to poor maneuvering to final while on visual approach; an overshooting final resulted in a terrain warning. Rather than make the decision to terminate the approach there; we continued; which resulted in an unstabilized approach that continued to a landing. [The cause was also] failure to call a go-around when that was the appropriate course of action. When you think you should go around; go around. Don't wait for someone else to call it and don't continue an approach that isn't stable. Follow SOP and your training.
column123: GPWS warning followed by an unstable approach into Runway XY in ZZZ. We were descending into ZZZ and were set up for Runway XX. Approach advised that winds had shifted and asked if we would like Runway XY. We accepted and began to set up for XY. When we had the airport in sight; we were cleared for the visual. As we armed the approach for the autopilot; we were told by Approach that the localizer and glideslope may not be working. My First Officer turned off the autopilot and began hand-flying the visual. He became distracted by configuring the airplane and was flying through the localizer when I informed him he needed to start his turn. As he began the turn he started descending; which caused a GPWS warning for a couple seconds due to terrain. We were confident of terrain clearance but leveled off until we were back on the localizer. Once we were on the localizer; we were high. We began our descent and continued configuring. We weren't fully configured until about 800 ft. AGL. We proceeded with the landing without further event.I think fatigue played a factor in our poor decision-making to continue the approach. We had a [before-dawn] wake-up call that morning and this was our 4th leg of the day. We had been dealing with thunderstorms and delays all day long.
column125: CRJ-900 flight crew reported receiving a GPWS warning during an unstabilized approach and elected to continue the approach instead of safely executing a go-around.

column118: Flight Crew Took Evasive Action
column121: ATC slowed us to 210; 190; then 170 KIAS; and then vectored us at 4;000 feet on a base leg for the visual approach to [Runway] 34L. As we rolled out on our base leg vector heading; a B737 was 3 miles ahead; at our 11 o' clock and about 1;000 feet above. ATC had us follow that traffic; cleared visual approach 34L. In the flight deck; we discussed the options to increase our separation; and reduce wake turbulence; behind the preceding aircraft. We decided to make the turn as square as possible without blowing through the localizer (visual approaches were also being conducted on the parallel Runway 34R); and stay one dot high on the glide path. We made a square turn to intercept the localizer; but the aircraft was still only about three miles ahead. As we began our descent below 4;000 feet; we encountered wake turbulence. Significant control inputs were required to correct for the resulting 'Dutch Roll' motion (wing lateral roll/rocking motion coupled with tail wag directional motion). The turbulence lasted for approximately 15-20 seconds; as we raised our glide path to about one dot above; which allowed us to exit the wake turbulence by about 3;000 feet. We maintained a higher glide path for the rest of the approach; keeping us clear of the turbulence for the rest of the visual approach to landing. Although the aircraft remained under positive control throughout the turbulence encounter; and within stable visual approach parameters for the entire approach; the motion was very uncomfortable and noticeable to everyone on board. Checking with our flight attendants after the event; no one in the cabin was injured; but a lot of passengers were understandably giving the flight attendants weird looks. Both flight attendants had recognized the motion as wake turbulence as it was occurring.A busy airport can present challenges with aircraft separation and wake turbulence avoidance; especially on visual approaches. ATC requested a specific speed for sequencing; presumably to fit us in behind the B737 and in front of other arrivals. Under other circumstances where the preceding aircraft is at the same altitude or lower; three miles of spacing behind a B737 may not have resulted in wake turbulence. It is a larger aircraft but not a heavy. However; in this case; the higher altitude of the preceding aircraft guaranteed that we would start out below its glide path; at least until we could cross its glide path to become established on a higher glide path. Therefore; the spacing really was not adequate for wake turbulence avoidance; since additional time should have been allowed for the vortices to dissipate prior to our crossing of the preceding aircraft's glide path (and those vortices). We suspect ATC was not accounting for the higher altitude of the preceding aircraft when they stacked us in so close behind the preceding aircraft. In the future; we will be more assertive and ask ATC for a lower speed; or for delay vectors; prior to the visual approach; even if that means ATC has to work harder to fit us in somewhere else in the sequence.

column121: We were flying ZZZ1 to ZZZ; a XA00 report in ZZZ1. It was the First Officer's (FO) initial trip back from a 10-month furlough. We were on the ZZZZZ RNAV arrival from the northeast. I was the PF. ZZZ was landing north. ZZZ airport had an overcast layer obscuring the airport. At the appropriate time; I briefed the ILS XXR approach. We set up the FMS and tuned/identified the NAV radios for XXR; the normal approach for this arrival. When we checked in with ZZZ Approach; we were advised to expect the ILS XXL. I briefed the approach and we tuned/identified the NAV radios. We were being vectored to intercept the localizer. It looked like we overshot the localizer on the NAV display; so I turned to intercept. ATC advised us; 'It looks like you are lining up for the wrong runway;' and turned us off of the approach. There were no conflicts with any other aircraft. They then told us to expect the ILS XXR. We flew that approach successfully to a landing. While we were setting up for the XXR approach; we discovered we never set up the FMS for the ILS to XXL. [I believe the cause was] early sign-in. Overcast skies obscuring airport. [It was also the] FO's first trip after furlough. [I suggest] verification that both the NAV radios are tuned and identified and the FMS is set for the proper runway/approach.
column123: Planned and briefed approach for ILS Runway XXR and was given ILS Runway XXL. The CA (Captain) set up and briefed new approach. I verified the frequencies and inbound course but neither of us set the new approach into the FMS. Was getting vectors to the ILS XXL [and] we had the correct ID for the ILS. When the CA looked at the ND and saw himself flying through the course for what was ILS XXR; the wrong approach; he corrected the heading to go back to intercept the course on the ND. The Controller said you are turning to line up for wrong approach and told us to go around.I have not flown since [last year]. I also do not have much experience in the aircraft. Was first flight since furlough. [I plan on] getting more flying in and becoming more comfortable in the aircraft; getting back into the rhythm of the flights.

column121: On approach on the ILS for 18R at 1;500 feet we passed a drone hovering about 50-100 feet off the left side of the aircraft. We did not have to deviate to avoid it; but it was close by. It was black and white with four propellers. It did not move over as we went by. It just hovered in the same spot. We notified ATC tower controller. Upon landing the controller asked us more in depth questions about the drone. Being on localizer and glide slope was key here because a deviation to the left may have hit the drone. Suggestions: Not sure what to do to stop this action; except for strict enforcement of the law when these operators are caught. Had I been left of course a bit a half dot; this could have been worse.

column121: On approach into [Runway] [27C] in Chicago it was by all accounts a normal day. A storm had passed about 2 hours prior giving way to the benign conditions usually experienced after the passing of a storm. The wind was hardly noticeable I had nearly no crosswind correction in on the approach. As we approached the final approach fix Tower cleared a 777 to cross our landing runway. We continued; the aircraft was fully across the runway by the time we reached 1;000 feet AGL and parked on Taxiway TT. I assume he was waiting for a clearance from Ground to continue further. We continued on the approach and right as I was bringing the thrust levers back to idle a sudden jolt of firm left aileron and right rudder were needed to continue a safe ground track and land the aircraft. I am not sure exactly what bank angle was achieved but the proximity to the ground made it seem like quite a lot. My guess is around 10-12 degrees of bank at 10 feet off the ground. Some comments from passengers sounding the alarm that things where not correct when they got off the airplane make me think things where perhaps worse than I know due to the speed at which this all happened. The incident all happened approximately 10 ft AGL. Due to the suddenness and nature of the incident the best course of action was to land the aircraft. I had plenty of runway available to safely execute a full stop landing. Had the event happened at say 50 or 100 feet; a go-around would have certainly been necessary. The landing overall was completed without further incident. We taxied to gate; deplaned. And other than a few comments from Flight Attendants and passengers it was a normal operation. The cause of this sudden change and necessity for rudder and aileron was due to the 777 parked on Taxiway TT attempting to taxi right as I was landing. He was headed out for takeoff so I assume he was all loaded up and needed a lot of thrust to get moving. This is what caused such a dramatic necessity for correction very close to the ground. I hope out of this pilots and Controllers are made aware of the possibility of incidents like this in the future if we're not operationally prepared in some way. The cause of the event was the new taxi procedures and runway configuration due to the addition of 27C in Chicago. When cargo carriers taxi on TT they're putting anyone landing at risk of jet blast. At least a note could be added to our company pages alerting pilots about the possibility of this happening. But maybe even a change in how Chicago does taxi operations.
column123: There is a significant safety threat to aircraft landing on Runway 27C in ORD; due to jet blast from aircraft crossing 27C on taxiway TT.  This taxiway is approximately 1;100 - 1;200 feet from the departure end; so it is perfectly situated to impact aircraft in the landing flare.  To make matters worse; aircraft that cross 27C are instructed to contact Ground on TT and are therefore not on frequency when given an instruction to continue their taxi. In this particular case; a 777 had crossed 27C; then stopped on TT; presumably awaiting further taxi instructions from Ground.  They were not on our frequency.  At under 10 feet we were hit with a really bad amount of jet blast from the 777.  The First Officer did a fantastic job of getting the roll event back under control for a safe touchdown.  We advised Tower of the event. Something with regard to the above needs to change.  Either; aircraft should cross somewhere else; Controllers should advise aircraft crossing 27C at TT to use minimal power when resuming taxi; Controllers advise pilots of the jet blast potential; or at the very least; there should be a note on a chart.
column125: B737 flight crew reported encountering jet blast from B777 on Taxiway TT while landing on ORD RWY 27C.

I estimate that it was 50-75 yards away. I took a time stamp on my phone about 10 seconds later. It was XX37Hrs Eastern Time. The pilots had left the aircraft before I could make my way to the front of the plane.

column121: I was instructing a Student Pilot on traffic patterns and full stop taxi back landings this morning at IYS off of Runway 04. We had been working in the pattern for over 30 minutes; making all radio calls: 'upwind; crosswind; downwind; base; and final'. For over 30 minutes we were the only ones in the traffic pattern and landing at the airport. We made frequent position radio calls the entire time we were there. We were on our 4th landing; on the final leg for Runway 04; when a multi engine aircraft overtook us from behind and underneath. Not only had we made a 'turning final' call but I also personally made a 'short final' call. We were already established on a stable approach and on glide slope. We had not heard any radio calls at all from any other aircraft for PAWS or the surrounding area while on that lap in the pattern. We believed we were the only ones in the traffic pattern or attempting to land at the airport.After I made my short final call; I saw them at my 2 o'clock position underneath us. They heard my call and finally made a radio call with their position; and made a hard right 360 to avoid traffic. We started a shallow climb on our present heading; responded over the radio and informed them we would continue our final heading to also avoid conflict. We came to a full stable stop on Runway 04 and taxied to the parking area where I shut down the plane and had my student and myself take a break. The other aircraft continued to fly multiple approaches; only ever making a call when on their own short final. We waited for them to leave the area before starting the plane up again; taking off; and leaving that airport.

column121: This was a practice approach to ZZZ RNAV XX.  I was the Instructor in the right seat.  We had requested the practice approach from ZZZ Departure and was cleared for the approach and had changed to the ZZZ CTAF frequency. We had made several radio calls of our location. Our airplane has a Garmin 530W with ADSB and Traffic. Also onboard was an iPad with Foreflight and a Status 3. At no time did we get any traffic warning or observe Aircraft Y in the pattern at ZZZ. We were on short final at about 1/2 SM and 500 MSL. When my Pilot (Student) spoke out of the near aircraft; as I looked out the right window I observed Aircraft Y within 100 feet.  I immediately took the flight controls and made a descending right turn towards the south away from the aircraft.  I then transmitted that we were turning to avoid the aircraft that had just flown over us.  There was a long pause when a female voice transmitted that they didn't see us.  We circled around and re-entered the pattern to land.  The other aircraft had continued their approach and landed.  After we landed and taxied around for another takeoff; I observed the plane parked at the fuel with two individuals standing near the aircraft doors. When the aircraft departed; our equipment showed the aircraft but no display of a tail number.  Prior to this incident; our equipment had been reporting other traffic.

column121: Weather was very good; a few scattered clouds at 6;000 feet with no real ceiling; visibility was over 6 miles. I was north of the field and called my position at 10 miles. I heard some aircraft that were landing and taking off and was looking for them. About 2 miles north I think I recall one of the aircraft declaring crosswind for [Runway] XX and was still looking for him. I got a little distracted with setting up for the downwind and my visual search was a little interrupted. I still could not make visual contact and thought maybe the other aircraft was close to base or had left the pattern. I called entering downwind [Runway] XX and the other aircraft responded that they were on downwind as well. At that point I responded that I would leave the pattern and circle and re-enter. It turns out the other aircraft was just below me and I couldn't see them. A later conversation with the pilot after we landed was that he saw me just above him probably less than 100 feet and pitched his plane down just as I turned out. I normally won't enter the pattern or will leave the pattern if I don't have visual contact with other known aircraft; but maybe cause of other distraction I did not this time.

column121: I was on a midfield downwind left for Runway XX when the Tower called out traffic for me to follow as a Jetprop on a 3 mile final and asked me to report when the traffic was in sight.I could see the traffic on ADS-B; but did not acquire visually until I was just past a point parallel with the end of the runway. I called traffic in sight and the tower replied with '#2; cleared to land Runway XX'. I waited a few moments until the Jetprop was off my left wing; then commenced my base turn.I was aware of two other aircraft in the pattern; both military trainers (later determined to be T6 Texan II's). I was also aware that at least one of the aircraft was on a right downwind for Runway XX.Shortly after we were cleared to land; the tower called to one of the Texans that his traffic to follow was an experimental aircraft and asked him to report traffic in sight. The Texan replied that he had the Experimental in sight and the tower instructed the Texan as #3 to follow the Experimental.On final; at approximately 350 feet AGL and 80 knots airspeed; I was concentrating on the Jetprop in front; as I was following somewhat closely and wanted to ensure my touchdown point was beyond his (for wake turbulence avoidance). At about this point; my passenger said 'Airplane; Airplane!' I looked right and saw the Texan at my 5:00 position; approximately 10 feet higher and approximately 150 feet lateral distance; on a collision course and overtaking us. At the same moment; the Texan pulled up and turned left; as I pushed the stick forward and dove down. The tower then transmitted: '[Texan]; I believe you were looking at the wrong traffic. Traffic to follow is an experimental on final'. The Texan replied that they agreed with the Tower.In retrospect; I should have made an attempt to visually acquire the Texan while he was on the downwind right. However; in my defense; I was concentrating on landing and separation from the Jetprop I was following and did not have much time or feel it was a high priority to be acquiring traffic which had declared me to be in sight and which the tower was monitoring. I did not make allowance for the potential that the Texan in fact did not have me in sight or that the Tower may not have visually confirmed that the Texan was following the correct traffic.In my opinion; this was an oversight between the three of us (myself; Tower and Texan) that had a happy ending; but which could have been a much different outcome if our evasive actions had been delayed by just a couple of seconds.

column121: We were making a visual approach to Runway XXR in ZZZ. The airport was is sight; while the downwind leg and convective activity was present south and west of the airport. After receiving landing clearance from Tower; a wind check was issued and landing speeds adjusted accordingly. The First Officer (FO) was flying and called for landing Flaps 30. The Before Landing Checklist was accomplished and I did a second wind check. We discussed the new speeds and continued the approach. Shortly thereafter; we received a Too Low Flaps Warning. Upon inspection I discovered that the flaps were in the 25 detention. The flaps were moved to position 30. I considered a go-around; but elected to continue the approach as we were on speed and path and a go-around would have been into the convective activity.Obviously; this was a failure to read and verify the checklist items. While debriefing the event with the FO; we both agreed that the monitoring of the wind/ weather was a factor as we considered our performance on final. Also being in a MAX 8 was a factor as the location of the flap gauge is different than the NG; possibly affecting the visual flow of the checklist items. Other factors were the weather deviations on arrival and non-standard arrival into a visual from the north side of the airport and terrain monitoring. Passenger issues requiring Dispatch coordination and the aircraft being met by Law Enforcement at the gate. It was a very busy flight at the end of a long day.
column125: B737 MAX8 Captain cited fleet differences; and checklist compliance contributing to a Too Low Flaps warning during a visual approach with convective weather also being a distraction.  Procedural deviation was corrected; stability evaluated and approach and landing completed.

column121: I was the Line Captain in the First Officer's seat. After numerous delaying vectors which cut into our planned landing fuel; we were finally cleared for the ILS Runway 35L. Passing through 9;000 feet we noticed an aircraft approaching our aircraft from the east at 8;000 feet. As we descended on the glideslope; passing 8400 feet; the TCAS generated an RA on the aircraft; which was still at 8;000 feet. We came within 400 feet. of merging with this aircraft! We circled back for another approach; cleaned up to conserve fuel; and advised Approach that we were now at a minimum fuel state. We circled back for another ILS to Runway 35L; which we completed normally. I called the TRACON Supervisor on my cell phone after parking. He said the other aircraft was supposed to intercept the final for Runway 35R but neglected to do so; which resulted in a rather severe loss of separation. According to the TRACON Supervisor; the other pilot blamed his automation for not capturing the 35R localizer.
column123: Working Final Radar. Because of weather; it was necessary for me to use the ILS approach for my runway; 35L. The parallel final for Runway 35R was able to use visual approaches. I had cleared Aircraft X for the ILS to Runway 35L at Denver International. I switched communications to Tower. A few seconds later; the pilot of Aircraft X called on my frequency that he was responding to a TCAS RA and climbing. Aircraft Y was cleared for a visual approach to Runway 35R at 8000 ft. but was crossing his localizer. Aircraft Y ended up turning virtually underneath Aircraft X before heading back northeast to Runway 35R. Aircraft Y was at 8000 ft.; Aircraft X was indicating 8400 ft. and climbing. I cancelled the approach clearance for Aircraft X; turned west; and climbed to 9000 ft. Aircraft X was re-sequenced and landed safely on Runway 35L.North landing configuration is the preferred operation. This morning; the wind was out of the south at 8 to 12 kt. Although pilots were complaining about tailwinds; we maintained the north operation. In addition; convective weather was south of the airport; sitting on the final approaches. This affected [the] pilots' ability to see the airport. The only other weather in the airspace was a small area east of the airport that had no impact. In a north configuration; the runways; 35L/35R; are direct parallel runways. If we had landed south; as the wind and weather favored; we would have used a widely spaced configuration and landed on runways 16R and 17R. We also could have easily used visual approaches and the aircraft would have been in clear weather all the way to the airport and had a headwind. Landing north unnecessarily was a major contributing factor in this event. Also; the parallel final for Runway 35R was training and the Developmental forgot to turn Aircraft Y in a timely manner resulting in the overshoot. This could have also been avoided in a south operation because of much more spacing between the runways.

column121: Aircraft X was holding short for Runway 7 at MBJ while [other airline flights] were on the approach. As Aircraft Y approached the runway (approximately 1 mile final); I heard a voice on the Tower frequency say cleared for takeoff" No callsign was given; and the voice did not match the Tower controller who had cleared us to hold short. We held our position. If we had taxied onto the runway; we would have created a collision threat with the aircraft on short final. We did not respond to the call or mention it to the controller because to do so in that moment would have created confusion on the busy frequency. Operations continued as normal with no additional threats. It is my belief that one of two things happened. Either a person with a transmitter outside the airport attempted to get us (Aircraft X) to taxi onto the runway in front of a landing aircraft; or someone in the Tower spoke too close to an open microphone while discussing a takeoff clearance. I do not think the second scenario is as likely; since the tone was not conversational; the voice sounded young; and no other words were spoken other than "cleared for takeoff"."

column121: Working arrival wall combined. Aircraft X inbound to ZZZ was vectored around weather to get the Visual Approach for ZZZ. I failed to make a point out to Departure Controller for the inbound aircraft as required and he was cleared for a Visual Approach and shipped to ZZZ Tower. I issued traffic for a VFR aircraft off the field before shipping to Tower. Departure Controller approved automated release for Aircraft Y off ZZZ. Aircraft departed ZZZ but was not on an IFR Beacon but instead on a 1200 code. Aircraft remained on 1200 code and was shipped to Departure Controller. This was in fact the VFR aircraft that I issued traffic for to Aircraft X prior to switching to Tower. Departure Controller assigned new beacon code and then saw the IFR inbound I had already switched to the Tower. Departure Controller immediately issued an Eastbound turn away from the other aircraft. I completely forgot to make the point out or hand off to the Departure Controller. For the last 15 months [the] schedule we worked combined so often I just didn't remember at that moment that ZZZ was not in my sector at the time. I needed to make the point out or hand off and be more aware of the room configuration and this event could have been avoided. This was just an accidental mental lapse in realizing the configuration of the radar room at the time.

column121: BHM ATIS advised runways 6 and 36 were active; with visual and RNAV approaches in use to both. Conditions were VMC with good visibility. Upon initial contact with BHM Approach; we were advised to expect Runway 6. The First Officer briefed a visual approach to be backed up with the RNAV Runway 6. As we approached the terminal area; we observed a single heavy rain shower cell located directly on the final approach course for Runway 6; very near the final approach fix. This cell was approximately 5 NM wide and ATC had advised two other aircraft that this weather contained 'light to heavy' precipitation. It seemed to us that if we were to continue to pursue the approach for Runway 6; we would most likely fly through this weather.After brief discussion; the First Officer and I determined that an approach and landing to Runway 36 would keep us clear of that weather. BHM Approach agreed and vectored us for an RNAV approach to Runway 36. The First Officer briefed the RNAV 36 approach and I entered the landing data for Runway 36; including current ATIS reported winds - 110 at 6 kt. The approach proceeded normally with the aircraft configured normally for landing and in a stable condition. The First Officer had switched off the autopilot at just below 1;000 ft. and maintained the proper glide path with the 'snowflake' showing on glide path. At some point just below DDA; 1;530 ft.; and MDA; 1;480 ft.; very near the ridge just south of the airport; we received a terrain caution with [a] 'too low terrain' audible message. The First Officer adjusted pitch and we went very slightly above glide path momentarily. We received no additional warnings or cautions and the First Officer readjusted pitch to resume normal glide path. Touchdown on Runway 36 was normal and within the touchdown zone. At no time did we receive any 'pull up' or 'obstacle pull up' alerts; only the one 'too low terrain' alert. At the time of alert; the FO (First Officer) responded in accordance with the note in [the] POH Bulletin XX-XX; X.X.X. Conditions during the event were day VMC with good visibility; 10 SM.I believe this terrain caution could have been avoided by not performing an approach to Runway 36 in the first place and by maintaining a profile slightly above glide path. I did not observe us ever being below glide path. In thinking about the event afterwards; I realize that while the charted procedure guarantees obstacle clearance down to the MDA; it is the pilot's responsibility to maintain visual clearance from terrain and obstacles once below MDA. In the case of this particular approach; doing that requires one to fly a profile above a normal glide path. I had always known about the ridge and antennas south of the airport; but had never flown an approach to Runway 36 in BHM. Despite it being a charted procedure which is permissible in day VFR conditions; I'm not convinced that it is an approach that I would use in the future - due to the need to maintain a higher than normal glide path in order to avoid the terrain alert. In my opinion; flying a steeper approach could potentially lead to other issues such as landing long or past the touchdown zone if not managed very carefully. In the future; I would likely only use Runway 36 if in an emergency if Runway 6/24 was not available.

column121: I flew a night operation into PTKK.  While I was aware of the NOTAM'd PAPI being out of service; and the flight was flown in accordance with SOP; I would like to make the [company] aware of the potential risk associated when conducting a night  RNAV(GPS) 4 PTKK approach under these conditions.  I believe this reduction in safety at night is due to several factors:1) Lack of visual reference on short final (black hole effect).2) RNAV(GPS) 4 approach lacks vertical guidance to the runway; resulting in the FD's initiating a missed approach at HAMAX.3) Reaching the MAP 300 ft. higher than published DDA.  If not briefed and aware; the FD will command a left turn and level flight prior to the 'expected' minimums.4) Lack of outside and inside references to determine if you meet the stabilized approach criteria once you are in visual conditions.5) PTKK is one of shortest runways in our system.6) Numerous threats associated with preloading an electronic glide path while still on the published approach; especially in IMC conditions (a technique many use to increase the level of safety) 7) Junior and less experienced pilots typically fly this trip.

column121: After taking off of Runway XXL at ZZZ; I turned 15 degrees left of the runway (per policy at ZZZ) and continued climbing. ZZZ Tower began giving me instructions to enter the pattern for left downwind XXL and I requested to return to ZZZ1. ZZZ Tower instructed me to fly a 150 heading. As I was flying on the 150 heading (I believe that I may have drifted 10 degrees off- closer to 160 heading) I saw a Piper Cub roughly a mile out and it appeared to be turning to my right; but coming straight at me still. After watching the plane; and still continuing my climb; I saw it coming directly at me (as far as human factors; the last image I remembered of the plane was it turning to my right and coming at me) so I chose to dive left immediately instead of right. Looking out the right window I was able to see the plane very clearly Turing left as well; so I steepened my dive down and to the left (after talking to a ZZZ Tower Supervisor named NAME; I found that the plane was in a 45-degree turn to join the left downwind for Runway 20L).As my adrenaline had kicked in upon seeing the plane coming at me and being left with no choice besides taking evasive action; I had missed a radio call from ZZZ Tower to turn right 20 degrees. After flying under the Piper Cub; tower explained that I was off my 150 heading instead of making a comment on the near miss; so I exclaimed that I was off my 150 heading because the plane almost hit me. Tower seemingly dismissed my comment and reiterated that I was to comply with the 150 heading immediately and I apologized and returned to the heading.Regardless of my adrenaline (which was very high for several hours after my landing at ZZZ1) I took what information I had; as I had previously seen the plane turning to my right and had to take evasive action and dive down and to the left.The inaction of ZZZ Tower in providing me or the other plane with information about each other is the most astonishing part of this event. In any pattern at a towered airport; even my times at busy ZZZ; I have always been advised if I was even within two miles of another plane in the pattern. Therefore; not being warned that another plane was on a close collision course with me would have led to a mid-air collision had I not been looking for a plane heading my direction. Even if I had been precisely on a 150 heading or if I had been 30 degrees off of the heading; I would have been on a course for a mid-air or a near miss like I had today. Upon talking with the ZZZ Tower Supervisor; he admitted that the cub had performed a non-standard pattern entry with the sharp 45-degree bank entry into it. While I do not blame the pilot of the cub; I do believe that if sharply banking into a pattern is enough to cause a near miss event; that plane and my plane should NEVER have been that close to each other.I do; however; blame the Controller in a large part; as it becomes clear that he catches his mistake when he exclaims to the cub that the traffic is one mile away and quickly tries to offer separation; but it is too late and I am too focused on trying to avoid this plane coming at me/across my field of view.Ultimately; I believe that the Controller's inaction and lack of communication with both planes is what allowed this event to occur.
column123: Aircraft X was instructed to fly heading 150; vectors for traffic. Prior to this instruction being given; Cessna 172 has requested flight following which was on a East bound heading. The reason that this vector was given was because of a Piper Cub; Aircraft Y; was inbound making left traffic for Runway XXL.I was busy issuing doing other things such as issuing wake turbulence calls and getting visual separation between aircraft on final for the parallel runway; I realized that Aircraft X was veering to the left; turning East bound. I immediately tried to correct the situation by instructing Cessna 172 to turn right 20 degrees.By this point it was too late and the Cessna had turned right into the path of the Piper Cub that was descending. I immediately issued traffic to the Piper Cub. I inquired Aircraft X about his heading to which he replied 'Yeah I was flying heading 150 but there was a plane there so I turned left to avoid.' The 150 heading exists to keep aircraft away from these exact situations. Aircraft X was an unfamiliar pilot and needed extra attention. I was busy engaging in other duties to notice that he had turned left East bound.

column121: Skydiving was occurring at the field. I heard the jump plane on CTAF occasionally. The pilot who took off ahead of me called the jump plane on CTAF asking for the status of the skydivers and received no answer. I then heard that a group was being released from 14;000 feet. I had to do my run-up and checks; but I never saw them. While doing this; I heard another call for release of skydivers from 14;000 feet. I called and asked if the first group was on the ground and someone (not the jump pilot) answered that the only ones up were the ones that had just released. I thought I had a couple minutes before they would be down; so I looked around; didn't see any; and rolled onto the Runway to takeoff. As I was doing this; I heard the jump plane call. It was a very rushed transmission; so I couldn't make out exactly where he was; but I heard the Runway number and gathered that he was coming in to land. I expedited my takeoff; and as I started rolling I saw a skydiver appear from behind my wing. He seemed to be off to the side of the Runway and traveling parallel to it so I did not abort the takeoff as I wasn't sure where the jump plane was and wanted to get out of the way. Well; shortly after I lifted off; the skydiver turned right in front of me. I made a sharp turn to the right; just clearing the trees and ended up buzzing a neighborhood. I got away from the field; re-established a normal climb to altitude; and took a minute to breathe. During this; I ended up too close to final; looked up; and saw the jump plane filling my windscreen. More evasive action.

column121: While piloting my aircraft at Northern Colorado Regional Airport (FNL); I was holding short of Runway 33 at Taxiway A1; and I contacted the Tower and requested to takeoff Runway 33 and remain in the pattern for landing Runway 33.  Tower cleared me for takeoff; advising me of an aircraft just departing ahead on Runway 33.  I was instructed by the Tower to remain in left closed traffic for Runway 33.After climbing to pattern altitude; I turned crosswind; and then downwind for Runway 33.  While mid-field downwind for Runway 33; I noticed several aircraft targets ahead of me to the south were all converging toward runway 33 on my ADS-B display; one from the east and one from the west; both apparently on base for Runway 33 from opposite directions; and a third target (Aircraft Y) approaching from the southeast.  Spacing among all 3 appeared inadequate.  The Controller told Aircraft Y from the southeast to report 3 mile final.  I was scanning the sky and acquired visual contact on all three aircraft.  Aircraft Y was unable to turn final due to inadequate spacing with the other two aircraft.  I was unable to continue in the standard traffic pattern; as I needed to maneuver east to avoid Aircraft Y as it crossed through final from the southeast and across the downwind to the west; which I estimated to be 500 feet in front of me and 300 feet overhead.  Turning east was the safest alternative to remain clear of Aircraft Y and the other two aircraft; as I had them all in sight. The Controller began shouting to all of the pilots 'I don't have radar!  I see two targets converging to the south!'  The Controller sounded very anxious.  I immediately informed the Tower that I was maneuvering to avoid Aircraft Y now in front and above me.  I recall that the Controller then called one of the other aircraft and said loudly to 'leave the pattern!'; but his instructions were not clear.  He then asked where everyone was located.  I clearly and calmly replied 'Aircraft X; turned left base to avoid the traffic conflict' with Aircraft Y.  The Controller then asked seconds later; 'Who is that on left base?'; and I again replied calmly 'this is Aircraft X on mid-left base.'  He then cleared me to land Runway 33; told the traffic ahead on final to turn east; and then instructed Aircraft Y (now west of the field) to return to land on Runway 33. There was inadequate traffic spacing for incoming traffic entering the pattern; and incomplete awareness by the Controller of the four aircraft positions relative to one another; followed by his distraught demeanor and very exasperated tone as he complained of having no radar feed.  It is also possible that the Controller forgot about my aircraft position in the pattern and that he had instructed me to maintain closed left traffic for Runway 33.  I did my best to remain clear of any traffic conflict; communicate calmly and clearly; and defuse the Controller's anxious situation; while maintaining safe spacing from all traffic in the pattern.  I was cleared to land Runway 33 and did so safely; and taxied to parking as directed by the controller.  No other communication took place between me and the controller.As a recommended solution; the temporary Tower at FNL needs a radar feed installed as soon as possible for better situational awareness for the Controllers.  The Controller's shouting and anxious tone heightened the tension of the situation for the other pilots; which was not helpful in this scenario.  A professional demeanor by the Controller with clear; yet rapid instructions would have been more effective at resolving the traffic conflicts.
column125: Pilot reported experiencing a NMAC in the traffic pattern at FNL and suggested that a radar feed be installed so Controllers can better track aircraft operating at and nearby this remote Tower airport.

column121: After being cleared the ILS PRM Y to Runway 10R at ORD and being cleared to land; Tower issued a go-around instruction to our aircraft.  The ILS PRM Y has a 2.5 degree offset course to the runway due to the close nature of the two Runways 10R and 10C. At approximately 500 feet; we gained sight of the runway and began maneuvering to stabilize the approach on runway centerline by approximately 200 feet. The go-around command was issued at approximately 300 to 400 feet.  The go-around instructions were to climb to 2;500 feet and fly the localizer course. The CRJ-200 procedure for a go around is to TOGA; advance power and set the flap to 8 degrees and initiate a climb. This was accomplished uneventfully; however; we were already approximately 0.5-1 dot left on the localizer proceeding visually toward the runway centerline. Once the TOGA is initiated; the command bars will raise to command a climb and also capture the heading the aircraft is currently on when the button is pushed.  This all happened as we were transitioning back into IMC.  The localizer was still displayed on both displays; however due to the sensitivity of the localizer beam at that distance from the antenna; I am not certain of our actual deviation in terms of distance to the left of course.  The Tower Controller informed us we were supposed to be following the localizer course and issued a right turn to heading 140 degrees; followed by a new heading of 150 degrees.  We complied and were vectored around a radar pattern to an uneventful landing on 10R.Although we could have possibly corrected faster to the localizer course to prevent the left of course deviation; I think this is an unnecessarily complicated go-around instruction.  The 'button pushes' required; and the delays in the automation system before it appropriately reacts essentially rule out even using it; especially in low energy state while transitioning back into IMC.  Even though we had immediately pressed the NAV button after the TOGA; the command bars did not capture the course because we had been maneuvering visually and were far enough away from the localizer course so it defaulted to 'roll' mode.  This added an element of confusion and additional button pushing as we were executing the go around.  Also; I have personally never been issued a clearance to rejoin a localizer course during a go-around in my aviation career; so it was definitely unexpected.  I would recommend a non-breakout go-around clearance on a PRM be limited to something very simple like a heading and an altitude to climb to.  This would simplify the communication and lower the overall workload in the cockpit during a critical phase of flight.
column125: Air carrier Captain reported confusion with a go around procedures directing they fly the localizer outbound when they were offset from the localizer on a visual portion of the ILS PRM Y to Runway 10R at ORD.
