column121: Conditions were VMC and more that 10 statute miles visibility within the area surrounding ZZZ. Upon seeing and confirming the ZZZ airport to our Northwest; ATC cleared us for the visual approach. After clearance was provided I entered the downwind and both descended to and leveled off at approximately 2300 to 2400 feet.  Now this altitude is about 145 to 245 feet higher than traffic pattern altitude.  This additional altitude decision was due to the understanding of elevated terrain in the area as described in our company published page that was current and wanting to have what I felt was an adequate but slight additional safety buffer between the aircraft and this terrain. In level flight the aircraft had a GPWS (Ground Proximity Warning System) warning occur one time for a moment and then disappear nearly immediately.  When the warning occurred both pilots were already heads' up and eyes out with a clear visual of the area and the airport. At such time when the warning occurred it was determined that the best course of action was to hold altitude for the moment and the warning disappeared.  Upon the disappearance of the warning and the CRM (Crew Resource Management) of the situation; the Visual Approach was continued to a successful landing and positive result.Now as I do not recall the complete details of the event I can only speculate on a possible cause for this while in level flight. It may be due to not being on a perfect line for the downwind and sporadic changes in terrain within proximity of the aircraft for the safety system catch that change and to alert. Our company page does advise caution when conducting visual approaches into ZZZ due to elevated terrain and that GPWS warnings do occur in early descents and if one does occur to take appropriate action to ensure the safe completion of the flight. I believe the CRM with this situation was handled appropriately with respect to this publication. The company page for ZZZ states that the safety department has noticed a high rate of GPWS warnings with respect to landing in ZZZ. With respect to this a possible suggestion could include a little more detail on the pages for the area of the airport with recommended altitudes; or possibly change approach procedures in to the airport entirely to help combat these occurrences.
column125: Air Carrier pilot reported receiving a momentary GPWS warning while on downwind conducting a visual approach. The flight crew were in visual conditions with the airport in sight so they continued the approach.

column121: My student pilot and I had just entered the pattern from a 45 degree leg to left downwind when a call on CTAF addressed us by call sign; asked our intentions; and said we were 'all over the place'.  I looked to my right and saw another airplane about 500 feet from us; also on left downwind. The pilot said on CTAF that he had just turned from crosswind to downwind and was aware of our entry from the 45 based on our position reports. He said he would extend downwind to allow us to land ahead of him. I can think of three factors that contributed to my failure to see the traffic until so late.First; the CTAF frequency for ZZZ is shared by numerous airports in the area. While I was talking my student through the pattern entry; we were hearing calls for at least four different airports without any silence; so we had to talk to each other over these calls. In such a situation it was easy to miss the one call we needed to hear.Second; the frequency was congested shortly before the traffic conflict by a lengthy and non-standard position report ending with; 'could any traffic in the area please advise'.  This may have been the pilot of the aircraft that conflicted with me; or it may have been an unrelated call at another airport that blocked the frequency.  A colleague who had just landed at the same airport later told me that the same pilot had also called him on CTAF while he was on final; asking his 'intentions after landing'. Third; my student had become disoriented while making a teardrop turn to the 45 degree entry; and I was distracted by helping her get reoriented to the airport and make a correct pattern entry.
column125: Flight Instructor reported a NMAC when they were assisting their disoriented student enter downwind at an uncontrolled airport and failed to see another aircraft on downwind. The instructor reported CTAF congestion due to several airports sharing the same frequency was also a factor.

column121: Potomac TRACON Chesapeake Sector always issues the BWI altimeter; regardless of the destination of the IFR flight within the sector. This occurs whether the pilot is calling with an initial check-in or being cleared for an approach. TRACON always issues the BWI altimeter; even though they are provided the ESN weather data (Tower/ASOS). Note that BWI and ESN altimeters rarely are the same. When cleared for an instrument approach to ESN; a pilot should be given the ESN altimeter and not BWI's. As a local pilot; I am keenly aware of the discrepancy in altimeter settings provided by the TRACON; therefore; I ignore the altimeter setting given to me by the TRACON. However; transient pilots may overlook the discrepancy and subsequently descend below minimums on an IAP into ESN. I have talked to Chesapeake Sector supervisors about the problem but haven't been successful in convincing TRACON management that their procedures should be changed.
column125: Designated Pilot Examiner believes pilots should always receive destination airport altimeter setting when checking in to the next TRACON controller instead of receiving a common altimeter setting to maintain separation in that sector.

column121: While supporting a Metro pilot as a non-acting crew member on the ZZZ-ZZZZ run who was unfamiliar with the specifics; we were returning to ZZZ and found the airplane in a near-miss with VFR Traffic. The pilot; was instructed to fly direct to the IAF. The controller alerted us about VFR traffic; later instructing to expedite descent to 4;300 feet. I continued to watch for traffic as it was on my side of the aircraft. I found the traffic and alerted the Captain that it was at the same altitude; heading in our direction of flight. Being unfamiliar with the aircraft I'm not sure if there is proper TCAS; but we received an alert and immediately descended to avoid the near-miss with VFR traffic. The pilot flying then told the Approach Controller his action and intent; and the controller repeated twice 'below MVA (Minimum Vectoring Altitude) climb to 4;300 feet but we were unable due to the VFR traffic we were avoiding. After successfully avoiding the traffic the Captain climbed to 4;300 feet and continued the approach. I saw the VFR/colliding traffic at the airplane's 3 o'clock; the side of the airplane I could visually see from my observation seat. Lack of vectoring instruction from ATC for two airplanes on a collision course. Captain immediately descended to avoid collision with VFR traffic. IFR traffic and VFR traffic needs better observation from ATC. Though I was a non-acting crew member on this flight the Captain and I worked as a team and executed proper CRM to avoided a mid-air collision.
column123: I was operating Aircraft X to ZZZ; as the Captain and Pilot Flying. Onboard I had a Brasilia First Officer; who flew down to ZZZZ with me to assist with customs and all the paperwork associated with the run. ZZZ-ZZZZ was his regular run; and I was covering it for the day. The additional crew member was not an active crew member during the leg; but he was on headset with me helping to monitor the flight. It was towards the end of the flight; and we were being vectored by Approach. We were flying towards the Initial Approach Fix for the ILS Approach and descending to 4;300 feet. During the descent; ATC called out VFR traffic to our 3 o'clock position several miles away; at an altitude of 4;500 feet. During the descent; ATC has also told us to expedite our descent. Initially; we saw traffic in that direction and called 'in sight' to ATC; but I quickly realized we had identified a 737 climbing; not the VFR traffic we were looking for. I decided to use the First Officer and asked him to continue to look for the VFR traffic. He told me he had it in sight; and he helped me locate it. At about that same time; the transponder gave an aural alert 'traffic; 1 mile; same altitude'. When I saw the traffic; we were very close and a couple of hundred feet below it. The other aircraft had little relative motion on the windscreen; so I knew our flight paths would cross very closely. At that moment; I made a split-second decision to exercise Pilot in Command authority; and I deviated from our altitude by initiating a descent. I immediately advised ATC that I was deviating due to traffic; and I was descending to 3;500 feet. The weather was VFR; so I felt like descending was the safest option for the flight. The controller responded that I was below his Minimum Vectoring Altitude and needed to return to 4;300 feet. I told him that we would go back to 4;300 feet when able. I stayed around 3;500 feet until I felt like we were clear of the traffic. The controller asked if I was responding to an RA. I told him; no. I'm not sure if was explained that the aircraft was not equipped with a TCAS. Eventually; we returned to 4;300 feet; got a visual on ZZZ; flew a Visual Approach to the field; and continued the flight without further abnormalities. We were aware of the event the moment it occurred through active monitoring. I think the instructions we received from ATC were the primary factor in this event. I deviated from our assigned altitude to create a safe amount of separation between our aircraft. I should have queried the controller more about the traffic at 4;500 feet as he was having us expedite our descent. That could have given him an opportunity to change his instruction for us. I thought his plan was to get us further below the traffic.

column121: While maneuvering near ZZZ airport; another aircraft was on a similar flight in the same general vicinity. My instructor and I made at least six radio calls to the other aircraft by its tail number to attempt to determine its intentions; but received no response to any of them. Said aircraft did make occasional radio calls; but they did not reflect its actual flight path or maneuvering intentions. On two occasions; the aircraft maneuvered directly into our immediate vicinity; although we were able to remain clear of it in the course of our maneuvering. On the third instance; we had announced our intention to follow the other aircraft as we were both returning to ZZZ airport. We had maneuvered well outside of the traffic pattern because of other traffic in the area; continuing to follow the other aircraft. Said aircraft then announced a right turn to increase its clearance from us and other traffic; but instead initiated a descending left turn directly into our path of flight. At this point we were approximately 1;000 feet; and were forced to initiate an evasive descent of about 350 feet. We estimate the lateral distance between our aircraft and the other to be approximately 150 feet; as its registration was clearly legible. Prior to these events; our aircraft was on a short final approach as part of a practice landing; at which point the other aircraft in question cut in front of us to take off. We had announced our position and the other aircraft had responded that it was holding short of the runway; but did not do so. Throughout the duration of our flight; the aircraft in question appeared to operate without regard for us or other aircraft in its vicinity; and had we not taken the evasive action that we did; a collision would have been highly likely.

column121: Chain of Events:Advised by Tower upon approach that a runway inspection was being conducted; cleared to overfly the runway at 800 feet MSL; then make left pattern until runway inspection was completed. I descended to the 800 feet but; upon level off; was subsequently blinded by the sun (I was flying west and it was approaching evening). As a result; I was sun-blinded and didn't see the altimeter for a few moments -- when I could see it again it was unwinding toward 450 feet as I approached the runway. I immediately added power and pulled up to regain the assigned 800 feet I was to overfly the runway. No violation occurred as I did cross the threshold (and did overfly the runway) at the assigned 800 feet. Apparently the tower controller concurred; remaining cheerful; friendly; and very helpful in cutting short my pattern to expedite my arrival as the runway inspection was concluded (I was allowed to turn downwind; cleared to land; after only needing to overfly a few feet of the runway).Human Performance Considerations:I was sun-blinded upon level off; as the sun then appeared suddenly in my flight path after being shielded by the roof line in the descending approach. I had not yet trimmed the aircraft for level flight; so it seems the manual pullback on the yoke was relaxed without my awareness while being distracted/blinded by the sun. I was aware of the hazard and tried to look to the side for orientation while reaching to block the sun with the visor and my hand; whereupon I regained sight of the altimeter and corrected.What can be done to prevent recurrence:Remember to preemptively pull down visor in such an approach. (It is my habit to do so; but didn't this time because it didn't seem quite late enough to worry about it.) I'm sharing this to alert others to this potential problem.

column121: It was my second day on the line. This was a repositioning  flight with two other crew members in the back who I was taking to ZZZ to fly their lines. I was on standby for the first half of the day and I had just flown ZZZ to ZZZ1 then to ZZZ2 and was in IMC from take off to landing on each flight. I was unaware upon leaving ZZZ1 that they had scheduled me to fly these other pilots to ZZZ after reaching ZZZ2. So I operated the flight and all went according to plan except I had an unstable approach into ZZZ. Using the auto pilot for the approach I ended up descending below minimum altitude for the final approach fix and continued well below glide slope until I was approximately 1200 AGL at which point I disconnected autopilot; increased power to help arrest the descent rate; caught up to the glide slope and hand flew the rest of the approach. I relied too heavily on the autopilot and lost situational awareness. I also could have asked one of the other pilots to act as copilot.I think it would be safer to not have new hire Captains fly without an FO (First Officer) in hard IMC for multiple legs even if it is just a reposition flight. And if there is a crew member on board they should sit right seat.

column121: Aircraft X departed ZZZ1 for ZZZ behind schedule due to a deferred APU and excessive cabin temps. Prior to descent crew performed arrival brief discussing the landing on Runway 26 at ZZZ. As part of the brief crew extended a 3.5 NM extended final at approx 1;000 feet and programmed the FMS for the corresponding VNAV guidance. Flight was being vectored for approach and landing when crew was taken off arrival into ZZZ. Crew had airport in sight and proceeded visually to airport with FMS back up. The plan was to be fully configured; on speed at the created waypoint at 1;000 feet AGL. During vectors it was determined the flight was going to be heavy for landing; crew extended drag to help with landing within the maximum safe landing weight. At that time; crew became distracted with weight; terrain and visually flying to the final approach course when crew descended prematurely below our briefed 1;000 foot AGL waypoint. Crew was still approximately 3 miles on an extended base leg. Crew maintained visual separation with terrain. It was identified that we descended below 1;000 AGL down to 800 AGL. Crew initiated corrective action and climbed back to 1;000 AGL. Upon reaching 1;000 AGL; crew proceeded to turn final and land without further issue.In this scenario; crew became distracted with managing landing weight; flying a non standard visual approach without guidance in the vicinity of terrain. For the next time; I would've fixed the landing weight issue prior to beginning the approach. In this case the amount was so small that a simple delay vector would've solved our issue. The crew could've then focused on the visual flight path to the runway. Also for the future I would; I'd also incorporate more of the autopilot until at a safe altitude to help with workload management. For the future maintaining the altitude and autopilot until approaching a base to final turn could resolve premature descents. In the future I could also be more specific and clear about which altitudes and how soon I expect to descend to those altitudes and waypoints when transitioning below 10;000 MSL building a better shared mental model.

column121: Busy day at the airport in the ZZZ traffic pattern. I heard a slightly garbled message about a Piper Cub entering a tear drop for the 45 to Runway XX as I was turning crosswind. I continued looking for the cub but did not see it and made my downwind turn and radio call. About halfway through downwind TCAS alerted me that there was something closer than a mile about 300 feet below me even though I was at TPA (Traffic Pattern Altitude). Worried I was over top of another aircraft I turned left and then right aggressively to check under the wings and nose for an aircraft and I did not see anything. Passed the numbers on downwind and started my descent to base. Called my base turn; heard something garbled. Then heard someone calling 'the Diamond' to which the pilot said I had gotten within 100 yards of him and that I should watch for traffic better. I did look; I had been in the pattern practicing all morning; I made clearing maneuvers to make sure there was nothing in a blind spot but at the end of the day I did not see him. While radios are not required; he obviously had one. Some of the hardest things to see while in the air are airports and other planes. It also would have been beneficial had the Piper Cub been at published TPA and even though not required; when within a very busy non towered airport; clearly announcing his position.

column121: I experienced a ground loop. Wind was 260 at 8kts; and tower had me land on Runway 16. My plane has conventional gear. I got my right wheel down; then the left wheel; then the tail wheel; retracted flaps. Suddenly the plane turned right into the wind; despite my pressing left brake.We ended up in the weeds of the runway safety area; about 50 feet from the runway but facing towards it. I did not drag a wing tip or strike the prop. Neither I nor my passenger suffered any injury. Got grass between the left tire and the rim; and a shallow gouge in the tire's inner sidewall where it took off the brake bleeder valve. No other damage to the aircraft. Could start up; but could not taxi straight. Operations / Fire towed me back to my hangar. I called Tower with a report as requested; and gave a report to the Operations / Fire agent. I suspect I failed to keep the control stick into the wind on roll-out.

column121: We were on a repositioning flight to ZZZ on an IFR flight plan in VFR conditions. Upon being handed off to Approach; ATC instructed us to descend to 2000 feet and fly a westerly heading to join a right downwind for Runway 9 at ZZZ. We reported the airport in sight for the visual. ATC said that we would fly for several more miles to allow for a departure. ATC then instructed us to fly a heading of 360; cleared us for the visual approach; and to contact ZZZ tower. We complied with all instructions. As we turned a 5-mile base to final; we attempted to contact the tower three times as we continued our descent. At 900 feet; the tower finally responded and told us to immediately turn to a heading of 120 and climb and maintain 2;000 feet. As we began the turn and initiated a climb; tower announced several times in the same radio call that we needed to expedite our climb as there was traffic at 1;000 feet at our 12 o'clock; less than a mile. We aggressively climbed from 1;000 feet to 2;000 feet and was instructed to contact Approach for further vectors. ATC vectored us to a 9-mile right base and once again cleared us for the visual approach. We landed without further incident. After clearing the runway and asking ground control for a phone number to speak with the manager. The controller asked if it was about what just happened. We replied yes. She stated that it was Approach that gave the suggested heading of 120 that put us in direct conflict with the GA traffic that was at traffic pattern altitude on the right downwind for Runway 9. She said that it was Approach's fault and offered the TRACON phone number if I wanted it. I declined and informed her that I would file a NASA report and that the midair was entirely too close.
column125: Captain reported taking evasive action and climbing aggressively to avoid a near mid air collision.

column121: I am the owner of a Mooney M20E and have >300 hours in this plane. The flight in the late afternoon was a demonstration flight for a friend of mine who is considering beginning pilot training. He has done informal training many times over several years including with me; and has excellent instincts and innate skills that indicate he will be an excellent pilot. In addition; he has significant knowledge about airplanes; airplane systems and general aeronautical/ aviation information. Weather conditions were VFR and not challenging in any way. There were some mild clouds and precipitation in the area but we had no difficulty avoiding them throughout the flight.I flew left seat and handled all engine controls and critical phases of flight; takeoffs; landings; radio calls; etc. He had controls for much of the enroute time and legs of traffic patterns. We did standard maneuvers; mostly practice traffic patterns and landings at ZZZ1; which is close to ZZZ and is the most commonly used training field for many pilots out of ZZZ; including myself. We did three touch and goes and one full stop/ taxi back at ZZZ1. There was no indication of a gear problem for any of these several extensions or retractions. I have very recently done solo training flights with similar or greater numbers of gear extensions and retractions in a similar span of time with no indication of problem. After close to an hour of flying; we returned toward ZZZ for our final stop. We entered a long right base for Runway XXR; and at the appropriate time put the gear switch down. For the first time ever in this plane that I know of; there was a gear extension problem. The gear motor activated and could be felt extending the gear. It seemed mostly normal; but perhaps a little louder/ more forceful than usual. It stopped abruptly; it seemed to have taken a usual amount of time; but the gear on this plane do normally extend quickly and I could not be certain the motor had run as long as usual. The two gear down lights (one on the panel and one on the floor) did not illuminate. The gear actuator circuit breaker had popped out. I tried resetting the circuit breaker twice; but it did not stay in. The gear switch had no effect at this point; as expected with the circuit breaker tripped. The feel of the plane in flight felt as though the gear were down with the expected amount of drag.I advised ZZZ tower that we were breaking off the approach for troubleshooting of a gear problem; and headed generally north of the field in coordination with the controller. I pulled out the Owner's Manual; and my passenger assisted in reviewing the manual gear extension procedure; with which I am very familiar. I have practiced this on the ground a few times over the years at the time of annual inspection with the plane on jacks. I have also practiced it in the air at least once in this plane.With my passenger reviewing the steps out loud for me; I extended the gear manually. The hand crank did not reach a stopping point and the gear down lights did not illuminate. I checked the visual floor indicator; which is there as a backup in case of electrical failure; and the 'GEAR DOWN' sign was visible as normal when the gear extend; with the alignment bars lined up normally as per my memory of how they typically line up. Many additional cranks on the manual extension handle on several more tries had no further effect on anything that I could tell. We were concerned we might have a broader electrical problem of some type leading to failure of the indicator lights; even though the gear appeared to be fully extended. I checked another indicator light on the panel in the same cluster as the 'Gear Down' light (the 'Ram Air' light); which was working properly. I could find no other signs of electrical failure on board. There was no smell or sign of smoke.At this point; I contacted ZZZ tower and coordinated a low pass down Runway XXR to get a visual check of the gear. The tower controller was ableto see the gear well and reported all three appeared to be fully extended. The plane was performing well and maintaining control was not an issue. I was able to aviate; navigate and communicate easily and without difficulty; and to go through the appropriate manual gear extension procedure with plenty of time and focus. My passenger remained very calm; focused and helpful throughout. Neither of us became anxious or nervous; and we were able to communicate well and work together well. He was an asset to me functioning as part of the crew; even without formal pilot training. The ZZZ controller(s) likewise were extremely responsive; helpful and professional.I did not seem to have any additional options to try to get the 'Gear Down' lights to illuminate. I was fairly confident that the gear were fully extended. We were in no immediate danger; and there seemed to be no other emergency reason to land prematurely; although the possibility of some larger electrical system problem remained in my mind. As such; I decided that spending extra time in the air for additional attempts at troubleshooting was no longer wise and that we should land safely while conditions were good.The landing on Runway XXR was smooth; well controlled and uneventful. The wind was nearly straight down the runway and was light. I most often land with no flaps; and chose to do so in this case as well. I made sure to land with excellent technique; touching down gently just above stall speed; well aligned with the center line and with minimal control inputs. I made sure not to use the brakes; and I elected not to use the elevator for aerodynamic braking; as I wanted to avoid any unnecessary forces on the gear. I allowed the plane to roll out long on the runway; and all appeared to be going well. I let the tower know that I intended to roll out long due to the unknown status of the gear. Runway XXR is 7;500 feet long; I touched down not far past the numbers; and rolled out nearly to taxiway 1 for a total rollout distance of about 3;500-4;000 feet.After we had dissipated most of our forward speed without incident or sign of additional problem; I began a very gentle right turn toward the 'high speed' exit for taxiway 1. At that point; the plane began to settle toward the ground toward the left; with the front and right settling down very quickly after. The prop struck the runway and the engine quit; the plane skidded about 30 feet and came to a stop at a mild angle off center line. The impact with the runway was surprisingly soft from our standpoint. I quickly shut off the master switch; magneto switches; and fuel selector; and we exited the plane quickly and easily. There was no fuel spillage; smoke or fire. Neither of us sustained any injury of any kind.Using a heavy boom truck; we were able to gently lift the plane off the runway via the engine mount. I was able to easily manually extend the gear fully; and again was unable to get the 'Gear Down' lights to illuminate despite the gear being verified fully down; and the manual floor indicator properly aligned. While being towed away; the plane again settled to the ground toward the left when initiating a fairly gentle slow turn to the right. This time; only the left gear folded.At the time; shortly after; and for the next day or two I believed that I had properly pushed the crank forward to engage the manual gear extension mechanism and allow the handle to work properly. This is one of the basic few steps needed in the manual gear extension procedure; and I had done it many times. It is a small handle and lever located at approximately the left side of the left pilot's knee; and is easily reachable with your left hand. After reflecting on the events and going back to visit the plane; in fact I had not properly pushed that lever all the way forward. It appears that the manual gear extension mechanism will apply enough lowering force even when not properly engaged to allow the gear to fully or nearly fullyextend; but without the crank properly engaged it appears that you cannot get enough force to fully lock the gear in place.At the time of the [event]; although I and my passenger were under good control and responding well to the situation; I was approaching or beyond 14 hours since I had woken up and gone to work early that morning. I believe that pushing the end of my duty day; as well as familiarity with the procedure and not double checking myself that I had followed every step properly were contributing factors in my failure to fully lock the gear. I believe I could have prevented the gear collapse had I followed the checklist and double checked every step.We have not yet jacked the plane and examined all of the relevant components. Whether or not there is some other structural damage to the gear extension mechanism or any of the components involved; and whether or not we are able to ultimately demonstrate that the gear down lights were or were not in fact working; it does not change the fact that I did not properly complete one of the necessary steps on the checklist for manual gear extension. I also failed to catch this omission with a thorough review of the procedure and circumstances after the gear lights failed to come on. I allowed myself to give too much weight to the possibility of an indicator failure and did not continue to troubleshoot more when I have the ability to do so. I also believed that my familiarity with the procedure was sufficient that I did not consider that I might have omitted or failed to properly complete a step.As I reflect on this; it gives me a sense of humility and a reminder of my own fallibility; and reinforces that I need to double check checklists and critical steps at all times. My belief that my left hand had done what it was supposed to with respect to the one missing step was simply inaccurate. I could have and should have caught the error with a careful review of the circumstances; and there was not an emergency pressing need to get the plane on the ground immediately. I will carry this lesson for the rest of my life. Fortunately; no one was injured; and the mechanical and financial damage to the plane are the only costs. But it is still a humbling fact that I did a basic part of managing my aircraft incorrectly and caused damage as a result. In another circumstance; I or someone else could have been hurt with such an error. I will redouble my efforts to be meticulous for procedures; checklists; and review of critical items at all times. In addition; I will be more careful with respect to aircraft operations beyond 12 or 13 hours since waking up in the morning on any particular occasion.

Returned and landed uneventfully on 28L.

column121: I was flying with a private pilot student to practice landings at ZZZ1. We departed ZZZ; and arrived midfield at ZZZ1 at XA:40 local time according to my ADSB track log. Other traffic in the pattern was using Runway XX; so we made right traffic for XX. The student and I completed two laps in the traffic pattern for XX. On our third lap in the pattern; we were flying in front of another Skyhawk in the pattern; Aircraft Y. At XA:57 we turned base to final for runway 26 and made a radio call on CTAF. We heard Aircraft Y call base behind us. At XA:58; just past the numbers of Runway XX; I saw Aircraft Z low and climbing towards us. I could see his tail number. I called my controls and executed a go around and a turn to the right. I attempted to raise Aircraft Z on the radio; but had no answer. Aircraft Y responded to my calls and advised me that the aircraft appeared to be making a left crosswind turn and exiting the pattern to the north. I attempted to raise the pilot on 1XX.X; in case he maybe just had the frequency wrong; but this did not work to raise a response. My student and I exited the traffic pattern to the north to gain altitude. Then; re entered right traffic for [Runway] XX with a 45 degree entry to the downwind. We continued to make our radio calls for the traffic pattern. As we turned base for XX; I observed on my ADSB that Aircraft Z had made a 180 degree turn from his heading and was now headed directly for us; 300 feet high and descending. I took the controls and called a go around; extending the base. The aircraft was now at my altitude; close and lining up for the base to final for Runway XX. I had not heard any radio calls from the aircraft. We exited the ZZZ1 area and departed to another area until we could contact approach to get clearance back in to ZZZ for a landing. After the incident; I called the pilot of Aircraft Y to confirm what had happened. The pilot mentioned that they had landed at ZZZ1 and talked to a mechanic who witnessed the incident and talked to Aircraft Z after he had landed. According to the mechanic; the pilot of Aircraft Z said that they had a radio failure. It is unknown to me what transpired with the aircraft after that.

column121: Planned flight for the day was to ZZZ1 (for fuel) then to ZZZ. Preflight planning conducted with assistance from ForeFlight mobile application. Departed Runway XX. Climb to 1500 MSL; 14 min cruise to ZZZ1 with landing. Landed at ZZZ1. The flight was conducted satisfactorily. The aircraft was then fueled as necessary.Secondary preflight planning was completed. Satisfactory takeoff and climb out to 7;500 MSL. Approximately 29 minutes; started to notice engine roughness with a significant drop in tachometer RPM. The Pilot in command (PIC) took over as sole manipulator of control and emergency checklist for engine failure in flight initiated. Looked for suitable landing area. Increased in engine RPM occurred and became within glide distance of ZZZ. Engine roughness and drop in RPM began again and ZZZ Tower contacted; advising of need for precautionary landing due to engine roughness. Decision was made to not [get priority handling]. Smooth and satisfactory landing made at ZZZ. After slowing and attempting to turn off Runway; the aircraft did not have any right turning authority. Airplane would only turn left in circle when power applied. Suspected right tire or brake malfunction. Contacted the Tower and asked for permission to shut down aircraft on runway to inspect aircraft.Aircraft was satisfactorily shut down on Runway XY with airport crew arriving for assistance. On post flight walk around; it was found that the right tire became flat; no other external damage grossly observed. Also found to have fluid leak within right pilot side inside firewall. Aircraft was towed to FBO for diagnostics of engine and repair of right tire. When asked if needed to report anything to Tower or FAA; the ZZZ airport crew stated nothing was needed. Furthermore; the Tower did not mention of any need to provide any post flight report during communications. No accident or serious incident occurred.There were a few factors found on post-flight that may have caused the problems:1. Engine Roughness: The engine was new with approximately 28 hours on the engine. Engine during break in period was using Aeroshell 100W. Furthermore; this flight was conducted with cruise altitude at 7;500 MSL. Miscommunication and ill-information about any special procedures regarding aircraft break in were not discussed between aircraft owner and PIC. PIC was told to use 100W oil from previous aircraft owner (box in back of aircraft). Furthermore; the aircraft owner did not provide engine overhaul information or procedures needed for aircraft. On mechanic review; it was found that the left magneto was rotated 17 degrees causing the engine to overheat; an oil line by the firewall was damaged causing a leak; cylinders were glazed; induction air leak; and some crankcase oil leak. Overall a combination of these factors is what probably lead to engine roughness. Post flight testing; the engine performed within specifications 2250 static RPM; 600FPM climb; 2350 dynamic RPM. Engine oil consumption has increased; likely due to glazed cylinders; and is being sent to Company1 for re-honing. Furthermore; the magnetos are being sent in to [Company] Magneto to be re-checked.2. Right Flat Tire: No noted gross evident issues on pre-flight inspection for tires; struts; fairing; or braking systems. Landings was smooth and satisfactory. Theory for tire damage could be due to stuck break after landing and prior to right turn out to taxiway; in conjunction with weak spot on tire and/or a foreign object on runway and/or other. Tire was satisfactorily repaired at ZZZ. Reviewing this scenario there are a few factors to consider. There needs to be improved communication between aircraft owner and operator (PIC); especially during critical times where specific procedures are needed (such as a new engine break in). The right tire flat is a chance occurrence; and I do not think that the flight could have been handled different to prevent. The pilot did all in power to safely shut down the aircraft and immediately check out airplane to prevent any further damage.I have had a clean pilot record; commercial license and CFI. I will use the lessons from this flight for myself and all future students for the purpose of discussing complete airplane maintenance with airplane owners (especially new airplanes); what to do when engine becomes rough; and procedures to follow with a flat tire.

column121: While on right downwind at Traffic Pattern Altitude (1000 MSL) to land runway XX at ZZZ; I reduced power abeam the arrival end of runway XX and started my descent. I was momentarily looking at the airport runway as I reduced my power. I then returned my vision to straight ahead and spotted a high wing airplane towing a banner approximately 100 feet below me; coming directly at me from my 11-12 o'clock position. He was less than 1/4 mile away when I first spotted him. At that time my altitude was approximately 950 feet. I immediately added power and pulled up as he passed no more than 100 feet below me and no more than 100 feet to my left. After he passed under me; he turned out to the west and departed the area at the same altitude. I made a radio call to the aircraft telling him he nearly hit me but he did not respond. Prior to that time; I had made 5 radio calls announcing my position and intentions beginning 8 miles north of the airport.The banner tow plane flew right through the ZZZ traffic pattern; directly opposing downwind traffic on the right downwind and between 100 and 150 ft. of traffic pattern altitude. He should not have been flying through the traffic pattern and was not communicating with aircraft in the area on the CTAF. He was initially in my forward blind spot; just under my nose. I spotted him when I lowered my nose to start my descent for landing on the downwind leg. He was a high wing aircraft (fabric body; silver colored) and likely did not see me above and slightly to his left. The approaching banner tow aircraft was not spotted until he was less than 1/4 miles ahead. Contributing factors were:1. His position in my forward blind spot below the aircraft nose. 2. A second factor is that the banner towing aircraft was approaching head on and provided a small profile for both the aircraft and the banner.3. He was flying through an active traffic pattern with aircraft actively communicating on the CTAF.4. He was not communicating his position or intentions on the CTAF while operating in the airport traffic pattern5. He was flying a non-standard traffic pattern leg; i.e.; opposed to downwind traffic.6. He was approaching from a position that was at first spotting; slightly outside the traffic pattern. Aircraft in the traffic pattern are dividing attention between the traffic pattern and the runway environment. My attention was diverted toward the runway for a few seconds just prior to the time the aircraft was first spotted.7. The banner towing pilot has a dangerous habit of flying through the traffic pattern at this airport as described in the additional incident below which occurred earlier on the same day. Additional Relevant Information:Earlier that day another pilot had a near miss with the same aircraft at the same location. He also had to take evasive action to avoid the aircraft. In his case; he was on right base for runway XX and the banner towing aircraft was just above his altitude.

column121: While returning to ZZZ from the south after an instructional lesson; we made a radio call approximately 10 miles out reporting our intentions; around the same time I heard a non flight school aircraft report they were departing but the call was very weak and had a lot of interference with it (I believe this to be the conflicting aircraft but did not hear any type/callsign). We continued to report our position as we got closer to the airport and reported our intention of entering a 45 to downwind for Runway XX at ZZZ and while we entered and were on the 45 to downwind. While on around 3 NM I noticed an ADS-B target departing Runway XX and was climbing so I tried to look for them in case they were remaining in the pattern. After about 5-8 seconds of looking and not seeing them I returned my focus to ahead of the aircraft; it was at that time that a small high wing aircraft (appeared to be a piper cub); was at our 12 o'clock and level at the same altitude heading straight towards us. I immediately took the controls from the student (who was flying at the time) and entered a rapid descent and began banking the aircraft to the right. Well after we began to maneuver away from the aircraft I noticed them start to climb and turn (to their right). It appeared that the other aircraft did not have us in sight until our maneuver.I did not hear any radio calls from the other aircraft at any point before (except possibly as mentioned above) nor after the event.ZZZ is in need of a Control Tower; it is an unsafe airport with the type of flight operations; how much activity there is. There seems to be close calls for various types of events nearly every day.

column121: On the RNAV Stadium Visual to runway 29 we were vectored behind an Airbus 321 on the ILS to 22R.  We joined the visual at GIMEE with the airport and the [A321] in sight.  We had been cleared to land prior to crossing GIMEE.  Tower reported winds calm; ATIS had winds 200/6.  Prior to SLIMR we were configured and slowing to approach speed.  At approximately 1;500 ft AGL I called for the landing check and called to the FO that [the A321] appeared to be crossing the numbers and spacing looked good.  Continued in LVAV/VNAV till the autopilot rolled us out on final at about 1;000 ft AGL.  At this point PAPI showed slightly high and corrected to visual glide path.  A rapid right roll was encountered just prior to touchdown causing contact of the right main. This slowed the roll but aircraft was still rolling right.  Immediate left aileron was applied along with deployment of the auto speed brakes quickly caused the left main to touch down.  Roll out was normal from this point.  Wing drop was so rapid that I thought the right gear leg had collapsed.  The event occurred so quickly that there was not even time to consider a go-around.  The application of power and lifting off again would likely only have exacerbated this situation placing us at a bank angle at low speed with an at the time unknown problem.  During debrief we determined that the event occurred just prior to the intersection with Runway 22R and was likely a wake turbulence event.  An ACARS generated report showed a max bank angle of 6.9 degrees with a predicted wing tip strike at a bank angle of 7.5 degrees.  The FO inspected the aircraft for possible damage; none was found.  The event was reported to Tower; but they did not seem concerned.  I contacted Clearance Delivery to get more information on the preceding aircraft.  CD took more interest and sounded like it would be reported.
column122: Reporter stated intersecting runway wake encounter events were more unusual in his experience; but this one was quite severe.

column121: En route from ZZZ1 to ZZZ2 we were directed by the physician on call to divert to ZZZ which we had just passed at FL350. The Captain gave me controls of the aircraft and radios and began the fix strategy. We were given a heading direct to ZZZ and began a descent to the airport. We began the approach on a visual right downwind as there was a cell to the north and west of the airport we wanted to avoid. Selected speed was chosen as we slowed down; however; when managed speed was selected the aircraft accelerated because I had not activated the approach. We captured the error; however; these few seconds caused us to be high and fast. Given the already high-altitude airport and compressed diversion time for the medical issue; we elected to do a visual left 360 and rejoin final. We had selected the final approach fix altitude; however; when we did our left 360 turn it took us away from the airport. The Tower Controller alerted us that he had an altitude alert and we climbed back to the 7;200 feet altitude for the fix we were now joining the ILS. We continued the approach without further issue.Cause - 1. Compressed; high pressure with the medical emergency of passenger with 60/40 blood pressure.2. Out of sequence normal approach and landing with the diversion that greatly compressed our time from top of descent to landing. 3. Choosing ZZZ - high altitude/high terrain as a divert airport.4. Loss of situational awareness as we made the turn away from the airport.Suggestions -1. Take vectors out for a longer final approach.2. If possible; choose diversion airports that have less threats. 3. If a turn for an airport is needed to descend or slow - make them towards the airport not away as altitude is less likely going to be an issue with regards to terrain avoidance.
column123: During a medical divert we were high and fast.  On approach we accomplished a left 360 to descend.   We initially had 7;300 feet set and when we turned inbound and had the field insight we began a descent to 6;300 feet the final approach fix altitude.  Approach received a low altitude alert and informed us.  We climbed and continued the approach visually.  We never descended below 6;300 feet before the FAF.Visual approach; high and fast resulted in loss of situational awareness.  Avoid visual approaches when in unplanned situations; get vectors for an instrument approach.

column121: Experienced a partial power loss and expedited landing beginning at about 800-1200 AGL. I had turned a high final and noticed that engine RPM was at approximately 1;000. I advanced the throttle in an attempt to add power but the engine did not respond. I pitched for best glide and tried to troubleshoot. I attempted to engage the starter again with no response. I noticed that the display on my comm 2 had disappeared. I tried to declare an emergency but I believe that I was not able to reach ATC. On my comm 1; I requested priority on my CTAF frequency; not having time to dial a new frequency. I landed on the Runway without incident. Maintenance determined that the starter wiring/switch had an issue and was fixed in such a way that it was permanently on. Therefore; whenever the master was on; it would engage the starter.

column121: After conducting an aerobatic practice flight; I entered the pattern on the 45 degree leg to downwind behind two other aircraft; a Cherokee and a C182.  The Cherokee was operated by the local flight school and was in front of the C182.  The C182 was flying a wider than normal downwind; 3 times the size recommended by the airport.  The Cherokee did the same and flew a 1.7 mile long final with no other traffic in front of him.  This caused me to perform two 360 degree turns to increase spacing.  I also performed S-turns on final but got too close to the C182 so I went around.  During the go-around the C182 announced that he was disabled on the runway.  I orbited and then started to divert to ZZZ1 but realized that I did not have enough gas to get there.  I returned to the ZZZ pattern; declared a fuel emergency on CTAF and landed without incident on taxiway A.  My low fuel state was caused by two factors: the excessively large traffic patterns flown by flight schools causing two 360 deg turns; s-turns; and a go-around; and the limitation of the Extra 300/S to prohibit aerobatic flight with fuel in the wing tanks limiting the amount of fuel on board to 12.9 gallons.The FAA must push flight schools to fly tighter traffic patterns.  The ZZZ airport requests GA traffic to fly a 1/2 to 3/4 mile downwind and flight schools typically fly 1 to 1.5 mile down winds.

column121: [Personnel were brought to] PTKK to finally fix the PAPI lights that have been inoperative for months. The Runway 4 PAPI was no longer NOTAM'd out. However; upon arrival [to] PTKK; we tried multiple times to turn them on to no avail. We asked TKK Radio to try and he was unable to turn them on either. Then arriving PTSA; the PAPI was inop for Runway 5 with no NOTAM. Then arriving [to] PKWA the Runway 6 PAPI is also NOTAM inop with shortened runway construction. So now we have the three shortest runways PTKK 6;000 ft.; PTSA 5;700 ft.; and PKWA (now down to 6;000 ft. with construction) having inoperative PAPI lights for the prevailing wind runway. Add this to PGSN not having PAPI/VASI installed even though the Jeppesen Approach chart indicates there are VASI; and we have 4 airports in the Guam region with no visual guidance. Poor trend; especially for night time operations and new crews coming on board. Unsatisfactory trend. [We're] lucky we have light and empty airplanes with very few passengers allowing for short landing rolls.
column123: I write this not relating to a specific flight but relating to a safety issue at PTKK airport. For the past 2 years; the PAPI at PTKK Runway 4 has been out of service. Sometimes this is noted on the NOTAMs and sometimes it is not. The safety issue is that PTKK is the proverbial 'black hole' airport which gives very limited visual reference. This makes flying a visual approach very challenging. In fact; an article 'Black Hole Approach' concludes with the statement 'The best way to combat these often subtle and insidious factors (relating to the Black hole effect) is to avoid straight-in; visual approaches at night without glide slope guidance; especially when overflying the infamous black hole.' Engineers conducted an extensive study of the problem. The results were published in a report entitled; 'Flight Deck Work Load and Night Visual Approach Performance' They concluded that the problem is exacerbated by many factors but the factors that stood out to me are those that are present on nearly all PTKK approaches. They are:1. A runway length/width combination that is unfamiliar to a pilot. PTKK is 6;000 ft. long with absolutely no overrun on either end. It is shorter than most runways and that effect is amplified by the fact that there is no paved safety area on either end. SNA is famed for being short but it is 5;700 ft. long PLUS over 1;000 ft. of paved safety area on the approach end to Runway 2L. It actually has almost 7;000 ft. of pavement. PTKK only has 6;000.2. Substandard runway and airport lighting. PTKK has had broken PAPI for at least 2 years. The only other airport lighting is the runway edge lights and some taxi lights. There are no MALSR lights. There is no big city lights to help with visual reference. 3. Peering through a rain-soaked windshield can convince a pilot (because of refraction) that the aircraft is too high and can result in an error of as much as 200 ft. of altitude per nautical mile from the runway. It is very common to have an island induced rain shower right off the approach end of Runway 04 at PTKK. Now we are flying this approach late at night; windshield covered in rain; poor lighting; and absolutely no backup source of glide path information as I will explain later due to the way the RNAV approach is built. 4. An airport that is situated at a slightly lower elevation and on a different slope than the surrounding terrain. This is PTKK. The runway is on the northwest side of the island at sea level. The island rises quickly to the southeast to an elevation of 2;700 ft. in less than 1 mile. 5. An approach over water or unlit terrain means that the visual reference points are at a distance where altitude and sink rate are more difficult to judge. This is PTKK. Absolutely no ground reference from the IF of FIGBY until the runway nearly 10 miles away. The problem is further exacerbated by the fact that the only approach we have to Runway 4 (most commonly used because of the prevailing trade winds from the NE) is the RNAV (GPS) Runway 4 approach. Everything works fine with VNAV and LNAV until we reach the missed approach point of HAMAX. Notice that HAMAX is 2.2 miles from the end of the runway. So here you are; flying the approach as usual in VNAV and LNAV and you have the usual rain shower between FASPO and the runway. You are tired because it is now after midnight and you are over the dark ocean with no ground lights in sight and the PAPI's are not working as usual. You approach the waypoint HAMAX and the flight director bars (your only vertical guidance at this point) suddenly command a pitch up and a turn to the left. Why?? Because you have hit the missed approach point and the guidance tells you to go around. This isn't common for most approaches. It throws off even seasoned pilots. You can see the runway lights through the rain soaked windshield but again with no PAPI; you have now lost all vertical guidance. You are now at 660 ft. above the ocean and continue based on a fuzzy view outof the windshield. Are you stable for that 500 foot callout? I guess so; hard to tell if we're on the vertical path...Is this safe? I would submit that in my opinion that this is the most dangerous flight we operate. Why do we do it in the middle of the night? I am not talking about the hopper. That is daytime and is doable. We don't currently have an aircraft availability problem like we did when we were busy. Why not do this flight in the daylight? Why not get those PAPI's fixed? They've been broken for 2 years! Why not get the RNP approach finished. That would solve the whole problem if we actually had vertical guidance to the runway instead of losing it right at 660 ft. where we really need it.

column121: While on our first attempt to land at COD we were vectored onto the RNAV to Runway 4 visual conditions prevailed and we were using the RNAV primarily for additional terrain clearance continuous moderate turbulence and windshear persisted on the arrival. ATC vectors and my attempts to maintain an airspeed that buffered against the rapid ups and downs of the green line and the rapid fluctuations of the indicated airspeed left us high and fast on our first attempt so we initiated a go-around missed approach. There was a significant tailwind down the valley that sheared to a headwind at the surface this contributed to us being high and fast.While we were climbing out we were instructed to climb and maintain 15;000 feet and were given vectors back to TOCUD. Approaching TAKBE the Captain requested a turn direct to TAKBE from ATC. Being at 15;000 [feet] we couldn't lower the flaps [so] the Captain instructed me to set 12;200 [feet] for TAKBE and start down. I complied with these instructions and as we dropped below 15;000 [feet] we began lowering the flaps and configuring.Shortly after starting our descent ATC issued a low altitude alert and stated that his minimum vectoring altitude was 15;000 [feet]. We were visual with both the mountains and airport in sight but failed to get proper approval from ATC for the visual approach before descending. As a side note we were being shook pretty good by the continuous moderate turbulence and I was really having to work the thrust levers to maintain a safe airspeed. We informed ATC that we were visual and we were cleared for the approach to Runway 4. We successfully landed on our second attempt. At no time was the safety of the fight or terrain clearance an issue and no EGPWS cautions or warnings were issued.Confusion on my part of the Captains instruction to 'sneak / start down'. It was the last leg of long hot sweaty day with multiple Captain swaps delays and MX issues; heat fatigue and dehydration were setting in and the turbulence and windshear created a very challenging flight environment.Once we goofed up and should have made sure that we were cleared for the visual prior to initiating a descent from a clearance altitude. The clearance to turn direct to TAKBE was not a clearance to descend even though we were visual. Mountain airports and strong winds are a difficult mix and other than adopting overly restrictive limits these difficult approaches will continue to be part of the job.

column121: I am a Private Pilot working on my commercial ticket. I need dual time in complex so my Instructor and I decided to fly from ZZZ1 to ZZZ; do a full-stop taxi-back and return to ZZZ1. I picked up VFR flight following en route and talked with ZZZ2 Approach and ZZZ3 Approach. Approximately 10 miles from ZZZ; ZZZ3 switched me to advisory so I called ZZZ Tower. Tower instructed me to enter a right base for Runway XX and report a 2 mile right base. There was one other aircraft also intending to land at ZZZ; who was also going to be reporting a base.I don't recall exactly what Tower said when I called my 2 mile right base; but was instructed to look for traffic. I assumed the traffic would be closer to the airport than me; considering that they had entered the pattern prior to my arrival in the Class Delta airspace. They had reported a 2 mile base before I reported mine. I looked 12:00 to 3:00 (straight ahead and along to the numbers) but could not see any traffic. I glanced at the ADS-B traffic on my iPad but did not see any markings there to indicate traffic (aircraft do not require ADS-B in the area at ZZZ). Finally; I saw traffic a few hundred ft. away from me; at my 11:00; at just a slightly lower altitude. This traffic was flying a significantly wider base than is standard (more than 1/2 to 1 mile from the runway threshold). I initiated an immediate 90 deg left turn; then flew a 270 right turn to enter the final approach.When Tower asks me and other traffic to report a 2 mile right base; I am assuming that I will have a 1/2 to 1 mile final; and that I am just on an extended base leg. I'm wondering if the other pilot flew to a 2 mile final; and then called that as their 2 mile base. I know ZZZ is a Class Delta; but I don't believe they get any supporting radar from ZZZ3. If they were using their eyes to sequence us; then their advisories were unhelpful at best. I don't believe that ZZZ Tower had any accurate idea of where the traffic in the air was at any moment. ZZZ was unaware of how close we came to a collision; and didn't seem to care when I told them I was turning onto a downwind; and then doing a 180 to final. I am the Pilot in Command so [I] take full responsibility for my actions; which were correct in that moment to avoid a collision; but I received no further instructions; which would have been an issue if there had been anybody on a straight-in.In the future; I will be more cognizant of scanning left and right when looking for traffic ahead on a right base. I will be less dependent on Towers with no radar backup to provide me with accurate traffic advisories; or any at all. I believe that Tower Controllers need to be more cognizant of where the traffic is in the air so they can provide better traffic advisories. If they aren't sure where an aircraft is; they should be telling the number 2 airplane to keep a wide scan; rather than acting like everybody is flying a standard pattern.

column121: On approach in to ZZZ; we were cleared direct ZZZZZ intersection and to descend to 3;000 feet There was a thunderstorm cell between ZZZZZ and ZZZZZ1 (Final Approach Fix) on the approach to Runway XXR to ZZZ. We asked ZZZ TRACON (Approach Control) for direct ZZZZZ1 to avoid the thunderstorm cell. We were anticipating a visual approach to Runway XXR because the most recent ATIS (weather report) was reporting only Few and Scattered clouds at the airport with adequate visibility for a visual approach. The TRACON Controller asked if we would be able to 'get the airport?'My First Officer and I were slightly confused by his phraseology and tried to discuss what the Controller meant. At that time; we were within a few miles from ZZZZZ and just about to level off at 3;000 feet. The Autopilot Mode Control panel was in Vertical Speed (Altitude Acquire) and LNAV to ZZZZZ. I was anticipating going to ZZZZZ1 and was ready to change the altitude to 2;800 feet in MCP for the autopilot. We were then cleared on a 160 degree heading to join the final approach course of 128; and cleared for the approach. I didn't hear the Controller say 'maintain 3;000 feet until established.' I selected VOR/LOC and dialed 2;800 feet into the autopilot Mode Control Panel. Because we were in a descent and in Altitude Acquire; the autopilot continued to descend towards 2;800 feet.My First Officer said we were supposed to maintain 3;000 feet. I had missed that part of the clearance so I dialed 3;000 feet into the Mode Control Panel; but by that point the aircraft had descended below 3;000 feet and was continuing to descend and would not ever capture 3;000 feet. I was also trying to slow the aircraft and get it to capture the localizer. Seeing that we weren't going to level off; I tried to use Vertical Speed to make the aircraft climb back to 3;000 feet; but it was continuing to descend. Our descent was exacerbated by the fact that I still had the speedbrakes deployed almost to the flight [descent]. ZZZ TRACON (Approach Control) said he he had a low altitude alert and his MVA [Min Vector Altitude] was 2;800 feet. We were now at about 2;300 feet. I disconnected the autopilot and autothrottles; started a climb and we decided to go missed approach. I finally stowed the speedbrakes; climbed to our clearance altitude and we then got re-vectored by ZZZ TRACON for another approach to Runway XXR and landed safely.
column123: We were being vectored around some weather coming into ZZZ and were given a descent to 3;000 feet and direct ZZZZZ outside of ZZZZZ1 on the ILS XXX. There was some confusion as ATC asked if we would be able to get the airport. I wasn't sure if he meant visually or altitude wise. After a pause; as I pondered his meaning; I responded that we were IMC. At the time; I didn't think we were high; so I assumed he meant visually. Just a couple miles outside of ZZZZZ; Captain asked for a turn to ZZZZZ1. I paused because I was anticipating a vector shortly to intercept the final approach course. I then asked ATC for the turn inbound; and ATC responded that he was just about to give us a vector and then provided the clearance. We were given a 160 heading to join and maintain 3;000 feet until established and cleared for the ILS to [Runway] XXR. This extra communication ultimately caused a delay in getting turned in. Because it all got really busy; I didn't see that Captain dialed in 2;800 feet. So; I don't think it acquired 3;000 feet and was now descending below it. Then I think 3;000 feet was reset into the MCP and yet the aircraft was still descending. All of this was happening while I was replying to ATC and I didn't realize what happened until after we talked about it later. We triggered a low altitude alert; and ATC informed us that we were to maintain 3;000 feet until established. I responded that we were correcting. Captain was climbing back up to 3;000 feet and we were slightly south of the localizer. I figured by the time we got back to 3;000 feet; we would then be high for the approach and not being established on the localizer; I said we should go-around. Captain agreed; and we did. This whole thing took me out of the Green; made me feel rushed and got behind in my responsibilities. A go-around was the best decision.

column121: During approach to Runway 2 into BMI; there are four bright lights approximately 0.5 miles from the approach end. They appear to be part of a sports complex. The lights appear to be PAPI lighting and in IMC conditions; such as low ceiling and/or visibility; and may cause spatial disorientation. Due to the proximity to the airport; we momentarily thought the runway environment was in sight. After cross checking flight instruments; we continued down another approximately 200 feet to where we saw the actual PAPI lights and runway environment; at approximately 800 feet. We landed following a stabilized approach and without further incident.There are four bright white lights approximately 0.5 from the approach end of runway 2 that appear to be PAPI lights on the same side as the real PAPIs. Suggest a NOTAM; remark; comment; or other publication should be made available to all airmen wishing to use the ILS; LOC; or RNAV (GPS) to Runway 2 at BMI.

column121: About 1-1:15 hours before landing into Country I smelled a strong pungent smell and therefore called my co-worker to come up and smell the odor. She smelled odor; we contacted Captain and we then continued to check first class passengers electronic devices for overheated batteries etc..and asked passengers to check their bags in the overhead compartments.We checked ovens and coffee makers and odor was not concentrated in that area. The odor was concentrated in fwd aircraft left jump seat area and lavatory. As we were descending the odor got stronger. The odor was not electrical more like an overheated odor sweet but irritating to the eyes; throat. I had a headache very quickly and nauseous. Very pungent on descent. Couldn't breath had to get on oxygen. At descent; odor entered cockpit. During time in Country; mechanics were looking for problem. They discovered catering papers behind coffee makers that looked brown and smelled liked burned paper this could have caused a fire midair however that was NOT the odor I smelled. This would have been another issue.

column121: I was the Pilot Flying on the ZZZZZ 1 Arrival into ZZZ. ZZZ TRACON asked us what approach we wanted to [Runway] XXR into ZZZ. We requested the RNAV visual to [Runway] XXR. We were cleared to 7;000 feet and cleared to intercept the final approach course for [Runway] XXR after ZZZZZ. We thought we were cleared for the approach so we set the next altitude of 4;000 feet in past ZZZZZ.  After crossing ZZZZZ the aircraft started descending to 4;000 feet. ZZZ TRACON informed us of a Low Altitude Alert and had us climb to 7;000 feet; then he Immediately cleared us for the visual. Told us to take down the phone number for possible pilot deviation.
column123: Both our first time to ZZZ.  We were cleared to intercept final segment. We said to each other; 'ZZZZZ is IAF;' and then we determined it was the final course we were supposed to intercept.  We should have asked ATC to repeat; but we put 4;000 feet in the autopilot; as in VNAV; to continue published approach; but 7;000 feet was still our clearance limit.  On the published segment it continued down; inside ZZZZZ; below 7;000 feet.  ATC queried us at 6;000 feet and told us to climb.  We had the field in sight and ATC then cleared us immediately for the visual.I was so focused on terrain and being in VNAV. As Pilot Monitoring; I should have questioned the clearance and any confusion we had. I am sure what we heard; thought we heard and what ATC said were all different.

column121: New Captain was PM; LCA (Line Check Airman) was flying from right seat on arrival into IAH. Approach kept us so high the entire time we had to get approval to miss the altitudes. Aircraft on other arrivals were holding and we were expecting the same. We were cleared to descend via the GUSHR arrival; and had to use full speed brakes the whole time but we made 10;000 at 250kts; and were able to make the bottom altitude at GUSHR at 6000 and 210 kts. Big rain shower at 15 miles out on the final of 8L. By the time we went by the rain shower it was mostly north of our course but there was rain and lightning coming out of the shower. TCAS showed traffic on 8R behind and above us. We could see him out of our window.  We were told to hold 200 knots till about 10 mile final. At this time; traffic somewhere; behind us we think; said they needed to go around because of rain and lightning. Approach gave us a frequency and switched us to tower and told us to tell them our speed.  We switched to the Tower frequency but must have put it in wrong; and when we tried to check on to what we thought was tower; another approach told us it was the wrong frequency. To go to the Tower; we looked up on the Jepp chart and the Tower frequency for 8L was 120.725. We called Tower on 120.725 and they cleared us to land. On final after that; a monitor came on and told us to change to 119.82. We switched to 119.82 and it was the same Tower Controller as 120.725 and she cleared us to land a second time. After landing we were told to contact ATC for possible pilot deviation. We had no idea why. We contacted the ATC Ops Manager who said there was a possible conflict with the traffic on 8R as that traffic was so close that had he not seen the airport and been given a visual we would have been too close and had a separation issue.1. We were cleared for an ILS. There was no PRM operations being used or advertised.2. The tower frequency they were using; 119.82 is nowhere on the IAH 70-9 page; dated 16APR21; effective 22APR21. ATC Ops Manager said Houston has been using frequency 119.82 as Tower for a few weeks because coverage was poor on the other frequency in some areas; but that frequency (119.82) is not published anywhere. 3. After talking with ATC Ops Manager; we verified in the NOTAMs there was nothing about 119.82 being used anywhere.4. Why is approach cramming the planes on approach so close that a momentary loss of communication results in loss of separation?

column121: Aircraft X had landed [Runway] XXR and stopped on the runway to question the exiting instructions. This delay in exiting caused the next [Runway] XXR arrival to go around. Then; an airport vehicle requested permission to inspect the runway because there's some local airport procedure to sweep the runway behind a [type aircraft]. There was a full sequence of arrivals to [Runway] XXR and [Runway] XY. Then; while the Local Controller was sorting out the Aircraft X arrival; the succeeding go-around; and the airport vehicle; Aircraft Y ([Runway] XXR) and the Aircraft Z ([Runway] XY) tied at the intersection. ASDE-X (Airport Surface Detection Equipment) alarmed; Local Controller issued a late go-around.Actual time on position may not have been a factor; rather the lack of staffing to open other positions and reduce the Local Controller's workload. There were only two of us in the tower at the time; simply because no one else was available. We had asked; and been told that we would be getting more people at XA:30z. Positions in the TRACON were also combined; and despite the fact that management had kept 3 tower-certified controllers down [in the TRACON] to 'get time'; one controller was working both the runways Arrival position; and vectoring the final to Runway [Runway] XY having to sequence in with the intersecting [Runway] XXR arrivals. The search for more controllers is on-going. What else can one do; when asking for more staffing; and being told to just wait?
column123: I was working Local Controls combined. I had Aircraft X land Runway XXR. I told the aircraft to exit the runway at taxiway. An airport vehicle asked to check the runway behind the Aircraft X as per our SOP (Standard Operating Procedure). Aircraft X then stopped at taxiway on the runway an asked if I was sure I wanted him to clear there as he is a [type aircraft]. I said affirmative. It was then too close for the next arrival to land safely so I sent him around. I then got the airport vehicle on to the runway but had to have him exit early as more arrivals were too close. It was then that the ASDE-X (Airport Surface Detection Equipment) alarmed informing me that the next [Runway] XXR arrival and [Runway] XY arrival were in conflict. I sent the [Runway] XXR arrival; Aircraft Y; around. Aircraft Y was about to touch down when he executed the go around. I had become distracted by the situation with Aircraft X and vehicle. By the time I was made aware of the tie between the [Runway] XY arrival and [Runway] XXR arrival the ASDE-X had alarmed. I would give more space behind Aircraft X to do the runway sweep and maybe have local controls split during the push.
column125: Tower Controllers reported a landing aircraft on short final was sent around when the ASDE-X alarm alerted the Local Controller to conflicting traffic landing on an intersecting runway. The Local Controller working both Local positions combined due to a lack of staffing was distracted by a previous arrival stopping on the runway causing the Controller to have to send a subsequent arrival around.

column121: Wind was gusting to 15 knots at ZZZ; and the Citation seemed unsure on if they wanted to land at ZZZ or go somewhere else. They got all the way to about 3 minutes from the field before they stated that they had the field in sight and wanted the Visual Approach. They had been assigned present heading and to maintain 9 thousand; but then I cleared them for the Visual Approach. Shortly after the aircraft informed they were VMC and were just going to do local maneuvers while they 'come up with a plan'. They had not begun their descent; and I asked if they were cancelling IFR; and they said no. I told them 'approach clearance cancelled; maintained 9 thousand' and I neglected to give them routing. A hit (radar update) or two goes by and they make a slight left turn and I assign them a heading for terrain. A few hits later; they made a turn into the 9700 foot MIA (Minimum IFR Altitude) at 9 thousand feet. The pilot was cleared for the Visual; and the last thing I expected them to do was to turn about 160 degrees away from the airfield. They're allowed to climb; descend; and turn; on a Visual Approach; but they're required to keep the field in sight; which they obviously can't do on that easterly heading; so I'm not sure why they turned that far east. Additionally; I don't think a Visual Approach clearance enables an aircraft to just hold and 'come up with a plan'. I get updates every 12 seconds; and I kept waiting; and hoping; that the pilot would fly their assigned heading. I'm not sure how long it took for him to be established on the turn; but if I had updates every second; I would have known he wasn't taking my turn; and climbed him. This airspace area needs an Approach Control. I believe that if there had been faster updates on the data block; I would have taken different action.

column121: In VFR conditions and clear of clouds on final approach to Runway XXL in ZZZ. ATC vectored us behind two other aircraft on final for the same runway and assigned us a speed of 160 kts. The aircraft was already configured with approach flaps and slowing. Tower advised that the aircraft in front of us had slowed further and advised us to slow to minimum approach speed. We fully configured the aircraft for landing and continued to slow. Tower advised we were still 60 kts faster than the preceding aircraft and requested us to do an 's' turn to the north to provide further spacing. The Pilot Flying; using the autopilot; initiated a 90 degree turn to the north (right) and continued to descend. We were clear of any traffic. The parallel runway was closed. About 80 degrees perpendicular to the approach course; using the autopilot; the Pilot Flying turned the aircraft back towards the runway. I; the Pilot Monitoring advised him to slow his descent rate; we were continuing to descend while maneuvering; using the autopilot the Pilot Flying slowed the rate of descent. At about the same instant at 2.5 mi from the end of the runway. Tower stated that he had a 'low altitude alert' on us and he advised us to 'initiate a climb'. The altimeter showed 860 ft above the water. The electronic glide path showed one dot low. The Pilot Flying immediately disengaged the autopilot pilot and applied back pressure to the controls. The aircraft immediately recovered to 1;100 ft agl. The Pilot Flying continued the turn towards the runway and descended on the glide path. At 1;000 ft agl; I; the Pilot Monitoring; reported 'stable'. I confirmed our landing clearance. At 500 ft agl; I reported 'stable'. We continued and landed. There were no TAWS warning triggered by the event. No alarms 'glide slope' or 'terrain to low' triggered.Less automation. Had the Pilot Flying turned off the autopilot for the maneuver and hand flown the aircraft; responding to the rate of descent would have happened in a more timely manner and an altitude low waning would have probably not been triggered. Hand flying would have managed the rate of descent better.

column121: ALTR FAIL master warning occurred for approximately 1 second during the downwind vector setting up for the ILS XX into ZZZ. Approximately 10 minutes later while approaching minimums on the ILS XX ZZZ; the ALTR FAIL master warning came on continuously. I instructed the student to go-around and fly the missed approach procedure as normal. After a positive rate of climb was established; I silenced the warning and started working on the checklist. The alternator amps were positive when I checked them. The checklist failed to bring the alternator back online without a master warning activation; so I deactivated the alternator and completed the recommended load shedding procedures with the exception of the lighting bus due to being in close proximity of ZZZ1. BATT AMPS was -7. We flew the ILS XYL into ZZZ1 due to poor inflight visibility. The flight was completed without incident; [company] was notified; and aircraft was written up.Reviewing the POH; my best guess as to the cause of the alternator failure is an alternator voltage between 30.5 and 32. The alternator was still providing power with a positive amperage indication while the master warning was active; which leads me to believe that the over-voltage relay was not tripped. Something else caused the master warning to activate. There was no burning smell or other indication of trouble.
