column121: Student and I were doing landings on [Runway] 27L before our departure to the practice area at GFK. On one of the patterns; (I believe on the downwind); Tower told us that we needed to keep it a touch and go for jet traffic. We acknowledged but no information was given to where the jet was or what runway it was inbound for. I was looking for the traffic as we moved to short final and did not see it visually or on ADS-B. After completing a shaky soft field landing with a new student; we kept it rolling and at rotation; Tower asked if we could turn crosswind at airport road. It would be right at 30 degrees bank but I felt it was safe so I told them it was possible. At about 2-300 AGL just as I was starting the turn over the road and finishing the reply for the above radio call; I saw an air carrier over the approach lights on short final for [Runway] 35L at near co altitude. I took controls immediately and had to bank nearly 45 degrees to the left in order to avoid a collision with the air carrier. Tower did not seem like it was important to let us know where the traffic was or which runway it was coming in on.Lack of separation and information provided to both aircrews.Better procedures for separating jet and prop traffic especially when using crossing runways. Better communication between ATC and pilot of possible traffic conflicts. Pointing out traffic to aircrews that may be landing close time-wise on intersecting runways.

column121: IFR flight plan filed TVL - ZZZ. 2 pilots. I was the designated SIC and the Pilot Flying for this flight. We elected to depart VFR and obtain our IFR clearance once airborne. We announced our taxi intentions for Runway 36. We noted a few aircraft in the area either landing or flying by the airport. As we held short of Runway 36 at intersection Bravo; we saw an aircraft on our TCAS at 1;700 feet AGL about 4 miles south of the airport heading toward the airport. I visually acquired the aircraft soon thereafter. After hearing no further traffic advisories from other aircraft; the PIC and I agreed to depart Runway 36. The PIC announced our intentions to depart Runway 36 and no further calls (from other aircraft) were initially heard. As we crossed the hold short line; the aircraft that we visually acquired earlier stated their intention to land Runway 36 and began descending. We immediately stopped the aircraft and told the other aircraft we were past the hold short line; but not on the runway surface. The other pilot acknowledged our location and continued their approach to landing. The PIC reiterated that we were already past the hold short line and the other pilot just acknowledged our location. No attempt to increase spacing through S-turns; a 360 or go around was made by the other pilot as we were stopped between the hold short line and the left edge of the runway surface (displaced threshold). Not knowing how fast the aircraft was going to approach the runway; we elected to stay put and maintain visual contact with the landing aircraft. As that aircraft landed; another aircraft (PC-12) announced their intentions to land while on a left base to final. Again; we announced we were past the hold short line but not on the runway surface. They acknowledged our location and continued to land while showing no attempts to increase spacing for us to move our aircraft. After the second aircraft landed; we departed Runway 36 without further incident.

column121: On initial approach to Runway XXL; vectored to position ZZZZZ (IAF) 2;600 ft.; and reducing speed to180 Kts. ATC cleared us for the approach Runway XXL. Pilot Flying descended below the required altitude to 2;420 feet; While the Pilot Monitoring and I (Observer seat) were giving corrective call outs; the Pilot Flying disconnected the autopilot and corrected the altitude and simultaneously ZZZZ Approach Control gave us an advisory call; which was responded appropriately by pilot monitor. Rest of the approach and landing were uneventful.
column123: While being vectored for ILS approach to RWY XXL at ZZZZ; the Pilot Flying momentarily began an early descent through required altitude of 2;600 feet.  I; the pilot not flying; and the jumpseat pilot gave immediate verbal corrections. Aircraft descended to approximately 2;420 feet as the Pilot Flying regained situational awareness and applied corrective action to include resetting the MCP altitude; and then reverting to manual control.  The recovery was smooth and controlled.  As the aircraft was returning to 2;600 feet; ZZZZ Radar Control called to direct climb to 2;600 feet.  I responded to ATC while the Pilot Flying completed the recovery.  Approach and landing were completed uneventfully.   This was the final segment of a 2 leg international flying pairing nearing 17 hours of duty.  All crew members were not at their best state of alertness.  Original arrival setup was for a STAR coupled to an ILS approach.  Flown arrival was very non standard. The aircraft was vectored off arrival path and given clearance to disregard many published mandatory altitudes.  This complicated arrival descent planning and may have led to the Pilot Flying losing some situational awareness.  ATC Traffic on approach ahead was a potential conflict.  This diverted my attention during the approach.Delays in positive corrective action which could have lessened/prevented the altitude deviation were potentially caused by distraction due to traffic call; lessened cognitive performance due to overnight/long duty period; lack of urgency due to VMC weather with runway in view.

column121: Early descent and low speed condition resulting in go-around. I was the Pilot Flying. Very short flight. Visual approach to RWY 15 at CHS. I was expecting the ILS approach. I missed that the GS for RWY 15 was out of service. We had briefed the OSPRI7 STAR. ATC recleared us direct to the airport and eventually direct MAMAE intersection to join the localizer for [Runway] 15 at 3;000 [feet] ft. Airport was in sight and we were cleared for a visual approach. I armed the approach and when we went into altitude capture at 3;000 [feet] I incorrectly set an altitude below 3;000 [feet] to allow the descent to continue in order to join the glide slope. I then realized we were not receiving the glide slope and were below the FAF crossing altitude at 1000ft. I disconnected the autopilot and stopped the descent. Unfortunately I did not also call for the flight directors off initially. I am relatively new to the equipment. Since we were in landing configuration of course the speed dropped rapidly. We were in landing configuration with the airport and PAPI's in sight but since the approach was unstable I decided to go around. We then flew the RWY 15 RNAV (GPS)Y to a normal landing. It was the third leg of a busy day with thunderstorm avoidance on the first two flights. It is my practice to carefully review the NOTAMs but somehow I missed the GS OTS. I haven't been into CHS as yet and the flight is very short and our ATC clearance had us high from the top of descent.Direct to a new airport on a time compressed flight for a visual at night is never in my opinion a pleasant way to conclude a long day of flying. Upon honest reflection I fully acknowledge I had a strong expectation bias for the ILS to 15. The lack of the GS and the unexpected consequences placed us in a reactionary position which is unacceptable.Our training is clear on how to capture and correct erroneous situations should they occur and we did our best to stop and correct an inadvertently entered unstable position. This was a clear example of the value and necessity of our human factors training. I know these things have happened before and I consider it my professional responsibility to not allow it to happen to me. And yet it did. Recognizing; and fully appreciating how higher than expected workload situations can present themselves is an essential aspect to our work. An increased diligence regarding the review of NOTAMs is a resounding suggestion I'm making to myself for one. Also; the need for particularly increased vigilance when in situations such as this is something I'll never forget.

column121: On a flight landing in EWR; the ATIS advertised the Stadium Visual to Runway 29.  Although the environment was busy due to the airspace congestion; everything was working out smoothly and well.  Approach mentioned to us a few different aircraft along the way to use caution for due to wake turbulence and we did use caution for the several large aircraft we saw during the terminal phase of the approach.  I do not recall; however; my Pilot Monitoring later said the Tower upon clearing us to land issued another caution for wake turbulence for a 747.  I did not see any flying aircraft that would pose any wake turbulence threat.  The approach was normal and I was right on my target speeds.  I closed both throttles all the way during the flare; and both engines settled all the way to idle.  The tires were maybe an inch or two from the surface; the yoke back bumping the stop; all the flying air gone from the wings; touchdown imminent; [when] a large gust from the left shoved the aircraft aggressively to the right.  The gust raised the left wing up and also the whole aircraft up into the air several feet. When the gust reached the tail; it shoved the vertical stabilizer right moving the nose left.  The centerline of the runway went from centered on the nose gear to well outside the main gear near the left wing tip.  There were two gusts one right after the other; both the same and having the same effect on the aircraft.  I immediately shoved both throttles all the way forward and countered with the controls to maintain orientation.  I don't recall calling the go-around; but my Pilot Monitoring says I called it right away. The air was smooth after the two gusts; the plane settled some towards the ground as it gained momentum.  Once everything was straightened out and the airplane with some flying speed now climbing from the runway instead of closing toward it; I realized my right arm was still holding the throttles against the stop.  Knowing that could be very bad for the engines; I looked inside to see how badly they were over revving and simultaneously pulled them back off the stop some.  I never got the chance to see because I noticed the left engine sitting at idle while the right engine was making full power.  That's when I realized my right leg was holding about 80% right rudder travel in.  The gear was up and the flaps sitting at 15 degrees and we were making only 125 knots.  Then after several more seconds; the left engine began to slowly make its way through the 50%'s; 60s; 70s; 80s; and finally into the low 90% of N1 speed.  With everything back to normal; we cleaned up the aircraft and settled in for the next approach.  ATC again offered us the Stadium Visual to 29 but said the ILS 22R was available.  We took the ILS 22R.  ATC never asked us why we went around.  We did not make any communications to our team in the cabin until after shutdown.  The origin of the gust was immediately very clear to both of us in the cockpit.  It was jet blast from jet engines pointed at our flight path during our touchdown; departing aircraft on 22R beginning their takeoff roll were blasting the landing zone of 29 with their engines.  Perhaps the cautionary statements we received from ATC for wake turbulence were referring to jet blast from airlines beginning to roll on the ground and not from wake turbulence from aircraft in flight.  Had I been aware this was the case; I would have never accepted 29 as a landing option.  The 22R & L runways cross Runway 29; so I envisioned aircraft departing across our landing path as they took off on the 22s.  So I would see them and be aware of the timing for jet blast and wake from landing aircraft.  However; instead; airlines were making intersection departures after the 29 intersection; shortening their takeoff distance.  So instead of crossing our path; they were starting from our left and starting their takeoff roll; and moving away to our left.  I never looked left during my landing; so I don'tknow what was there blasting us; I'm guessing something big.  Of note; due to high fuel price in EWR; we were heavy; just a few hundred pounds under max landing weight.  I would first suggest not landing small jets on [Runway] 29 at EWR when 22s are departing in the way they were.  I wasn't familiar enough with EWR to know how dangerous the circumstance would be; I've landed on 22s in years past.  I would also suggest the left engine acceleration be repaired to an acceptable level.  Waiting 15 - 20 seconds for an engine to give power in fight is not acceptable.  If neither engine had given power for that amount of time; I do not believe a touchdown without major damage would've been possible.  Even still; it took everything the aircraft had in it plus everything humanly possible I could do to prevent an accident.
column125: CE-560 Captain reported executing a go-around at touchdown on Runway 29 at EWR when jet blast from  air carrier aircraft on Runway 22 had a destabilizing effect on their aircraft.

column121: Captain [was] Pilot Flying. He briefed the OSPRI arrival and ILS to [Runway] 15. ATC kept us high; then Cleared for the straight in visual. Gear; flaps and speed brakes out to get down. We had left the speed brakes out. Retracted them. Disconnected the FD's. Captain called for a go around. Preformed the GA and returned for the RNAV 15. Busy day flying around storms. Last flight at night. Short flight. ATC keeping us high. Expectation bias to the ILS. I had recently been to CHS at night and the ILS was working. New Captain to [aircraft]; all his other time was in Boeing aircraft. My failure to trap our errors earlier. I will create more effective barriers going forward. For example: Request ILS/RNAV approaches vs visual at night. Get delay vectors when ATC keeps us high.

column121: I was making a normal approach to Runway XXR on a solo flight. Winds were about 220 @ 6 mph at the time. After I had turned to base; ATC inquired if I had turned to base. I informed ATC that I had turned to base and at that point immediately needed to turn to final. ATC asked the plane at taxiway to hold position. Glide slope was normal. I was on centerline and airspeed was 85 mph. Final approach continued normally and as I crossed threshold; I reduced throttle to idle. The airplane landed firmly but did not bounce. When the nose wheel came down; I perceived a violent shaking of the nose wheel. The airplane began heading to the right side of the runway and I attempted to correct as normal. However; my corrections started a series of oscillations that grew in amplitude. The airplane was now headed in to the grass safety area after taxiway and I let the plane continue on its track for fear of flipping the plane. The airplane went into and out of the drainage ditch and came to a stop about 2-3 yards from taxiway X. I believe that one of the contributing factors to the event was task overload. This was my second solo flight and the additional load of ATC's inquiry; the follow on instructions to the other plane near my intended runway; and my focus toward the other aircraft may have used more attention than intended. I believe also that a greater emphasis overall on safe after landing roll would be good. In the FAA 'Airplane Flying Handbook'; after landing roll safety is addressed but does not receive the emphasis it deserves.

column121: ATC advised we would be following a heavy aircraft on approach into ORD. While on short final; I observed the aircraft we were following on Taxiway P; headed eastbound. We continued on the approach and did not experience any effects of wake turbulence. I heard ATC instruct the aircraft to turn left onto Taxiway EE and taxi onto Runway 28R; which requires a turn of more than 90 degrees to complete. As the heavy aircraft negotiated the turn; I was just beginning to flare for the landing. I suddenly experienced what seemed to be a heavy crosswind from the right (much heavier that what was being reported); associated with a higher than normal pitch attitude to arrest the descent rate just before touchdown. Approximately 1-2 seconds before touchdown; the stick-shaker activated until the main landing gear made contact with the runway. A normal touchdown and roll-out was performed and we taxied to the gate without incident. The First Officer and I both agreed during the debrief that the approach and landing was stable and on proper speed throughout.I believe the cause of this event was a result of ATC giving the instruction to the heavy aircraft to make the left turn; causing its jet blast to cross directly over the approach end of Runway 28C. It appeared to create a crosswind/tailwind from the 5 o'clock position of our aircraft. I believe this is what contributed to the higher than expected crosswind from the right and the stick-shaker to activate just before touchdown. If ATC would have had the heavy aircraft hold short until the turn could be made in between arrivals; I believe this situation could have been avoided.

column121: I was leading a flight of two [warbirds] to land at ZZZ on Runway XX right which is the north runway. Runway XX left is the south parallel runway. Tower cleared us to land. I was a straight in and Aircraft Y was a go-around. Aircraft Y announced that he was breaking to land from the overhead. Tower instructed him to break left which is non-standard when landing on the right runway since it would be across the left runway and we are always instructed to break right when landing on the right runway. As I touched down I heard tower instruct Aircraft Y to break left and immediately heard tower repeat break left as the tower operator noticed that Aircraft Y was breaking right. I looked straight up through my bubble canopy and saw Aircraft Y make an immediate correction and enter the left break as instructed. At no time did Aircraft Y make more than a 10-20 degree turn before correcting.  The following day I was notified that there was a CFI with a student in a Cessna 172 in the right downwind to Runway XX right who was startled and was claiming that there was a near miss between herself and Aircraft Y although it is hard to imagine how that would be possible given that the Cessna would have been in downwind offset from the runway by quite a distance.  Since I was leading the flight of 2 aircraft and was carrying the squawk and ADS-B for the flight; I am concerned that a complaint may be incorrectly filed against me even though I had landed at the time the incident occurred.

At this time the student pilot may have become disoriented and misheard a communication from ATC where he began to turn left base where the near miss happened with a Lear Jet on Final for Runway XX. ATC told the Learjet to go around and evasive action was taken to avoid a collision. After the Learjet passed over head ATC gave the student pilot a right base to final for Runway XX and that was to a full stop. I believe ATC didn't really treat my student as a priority when the number of aircraft on the ground and in the airspace increased. I would have liked ATC to have taken more consideration for a student pilot. Having a student pilot extend upwind to almost outside the Delta followed by asking him to make multiple right 360s on downwind; followed by the making him to extend downwind all in one traffic pattern could throw a seasoned pilot off and cause a problem to stir.

column121: As we were in the flare on Runway 29 at EWR with power at idle or being reduced towards idle; maybe 10-20 ft off the ground;  we were hit by jet blast from a departing heavy aircraft on the intersecting Runway; 22R. EWR was landing 29 and departing aircraft from [Runway] 22R at intersection [Taxiway] W or Y. The aircraft weather vane into the jet blast; the aircraft was pushed right of center line; we experienced moderate turbulence; and the aircraft rolled slightly to the right. I was able to control the aircraft as if [it was] landing in a crosswind and land safely. The aircraft never got into an unsafe situation. Needless to say; this caught us completely off guard. Maybe EWR needs to reevaluate this practice when heavy aircraft are departing. In the landing flare is not a great time to get hit with jet blast. Crews should also be aware of this hazard.

column121: On a visual approach to runway 19 at MKC; we were flying on a extended (wide) right base for runway 19 in a descent and were about to start leveling out when ATC (MKC Tower) gave us a minimum safe altitude warning (MSAW). Tower advised us to level out or climb and to check our altimeter setting to the local. I leveled the airplane and began a shallow climb while checking my altitude and surroundings. The altitude we were at wasn't too low for the local terrain. Nor was our altimeter setting off. The aircraft that was being flown is equipped with a terrain awareness and warning system (TAWS) that wasn't triggered at any point in the flight. We continued our approach; joined the visual glide path; and landed without incident. Later; after some research; I learned that MSAWs can be triggered based on descent rate. Our descent rate may have been high during the visual approach. In the future; I will do better to monitor and control my descent rate; especially in wide patterns to urban runways.

column121: My First Officer (Pilot Monitoring) and I (Pilot flying) flew to Bogota; Columbia (BOG).  This was the first time for either of us to fly into Bogota.  During our 3+ hour transit to BOG; the FO (First Officer) and I had a very long discussion about the specifics of the airport.  About an hour from landing; ATC said that BOG was using Runways 13L/13R ILS approaches; which we were happy to hear; because we had a few questions explained below about the non-ILS approaches for Runways 31L/31R.  No ILS approaches were available for Runway 31. Once we checked in with Bogota; we were told to fly the ISVAT 3W Arrival for Runway 31L; which surprised us because we had been told to expect Runway 13.  The Jeppesen approach charts have 5 approaches for Runway 31L; although 3 of those approaches (RNP Z/Y/X Rwy 31L (AR)) are specifically not allowed to be flown by [our aircraft type].  Of the two remaining approaches listed in the Jeppesen; pages; only one;'RNP Visual Flight Proc RNP C Rwy 31L'; was available to us in the FMS (Flight Management System).  So our primary question was is the 'RNP Visual Flight Proc RNP C Rwy 31L' an instrument approach; or is it a visual approach?  Based on a simulator training event several years ago; I distinctly remember a Check Airman telling me that [company] pilots are not allowed to fly visual approaches at night.  I searched the Flight Operations Manual (FOM) for 'night visual approaches' and found nothing.  While on the arrival; a search for just 'visual approaches' yielded 28 results; a daunting number; given we were two pilots who had never been to BOG; it was late at night and we were descending on the arrival into mountainous terrain to a field at 8;400 foot elevation.  In an effort to decide whether IMC or Visual Approach rules applied; I decided that this had to be classified as a visual approach; not an instrument approach; since page 10-7B-1 lists our only available approach to 31L under the category of Visual Approach Procedures.  Finally; in the FOM; I found a one-sentence reference to visual approaches at night which states: 'At night; since obstacles may not be visible; do not accept or request visual approaches unless the airport is clearly in sight and the flight crew is certain of remaining clear of obstacles and terrain while maneuvering for landing.'  During the time we were flying the arrival; we were above an overcast and the field was not in sight; so we quickly discussed the possibility of having to divert. At some point well after we transitioned from the arrival to the approach at AMVES; the runway environment came into sight.  At that point I thought we could continue the approach; as we were down in the valley and could maintain sight of the field.  As we approached the 31L Final Approach Fix (BO480); we were fully configured for landing and Tower asked us if we would prefer to land on Runway 31R.  We said no; since we were already set up for Runway 31L.  Then Tower told us that Runway 31L was 'now closed' (and the runway edge lights and centerline lights were turned off) and that we had to land on Runway 31R.  My concern now was that we would no longer be on the coded purple FMS path to landing because we couldn't reprogram the FMS in such a short time; and I was unfamiliar with the terrain in the area.  The FO and I quickly referenced the terrain on our cockpit displays and decided that we would be clear of any terrain while I transitioned to 31R.  So I disconnected the autopilot and flew over to the 31R center line.  Another challenge was that we were initially on the PAPI glide path for 31L; and once we transitioned to 31R PAPI; were instantly high because 31R is offset about 4;000 feet closer to us than 31L.  But I was able to get the airplane stabilized on PAPI glide path before we reached 1;000 feet AGL; and we landed successfully on 31R.Of the many lessons learned from this flight; two things stand out to me. First; the 10-7 pages need a better discussion of visual approaches at night.  The fact that the title of the approach is 'RNP Visual Flight Proc RNP C Rwy 31L' (which sounds like an RNAV approach since RNP is listed twice in the title).  Second; the actions of Bogota Tower to close 31L Runway at such a late stage in our approach was totally unnecessary and inexcusable. Insufficient discussion in 10-7 pages regarding Runway 31 visual approaches at night.  Also; why Bogota Tower closed Runway 31L and shut off runway lights when we were in vicinity of Final Approach Fix and required us to transition to Runway 31R. Improve discussion regarding night visual approaches in 10-7 pages and query Bogota Tower why they closed the runway on us passing Final Approach Fix; requiring us to deviate from coded FMS path in mountainous terrain.

column121: On approach into Reno we set up for and requested the RNAV RNP Y 16L. ATC denied our request and extended our downwind until we could sight another aircraft to follow on final. With the airport; surrounding terrain; and traffic in sight; we were cleared the visual approach to 16L. Once on final after maneuvering; descending; and configuring; our aircraft ground proximity warning system alerted us of the rolling terrain below which we could also clearly see. We leveled off and even started a climb when the warnings ceased. We then continued our visual approach to an uneventful landing on 16L.Being cleared for an unexpected visual approach while on an extended downwind behind traffic in mountainous terrain at an unfamiliar airport. Perhaps briefing alternative approaches in the event ATC should deny our pre-planned approach.  Exploring and discussing other potential approach possibilities may have helped prepare us for the ATC visual clearance and to possibly avoid the terrain warning.

column121: On taxi-out to Runway 5 we made a radio call stating aircraft type and runway of intended departure. Upon reaching the hold short line we contacted Air Traffic Control for IFR clearance; and waited for release time for takeoff. Hold short line faces approach end of Runway 5; but does not allow pilot to view departure end of runway for on-coming traffic. After copying clearance we switched back to CTAF frequency and continued to monitor the frequency. Upon moving the aircraft we announced taking Runway 5 for takeoff and intended direction of departure. As we took the runway and started takeoff roll we encountered an aircraft on short final for Runway 23. This aircraft had not made any traffic calls and was difficult to see on the hazy background of the sky. Aircraft executed a missed approach and re-entered the pattern for Runway 5. I failed to make an abort decision at the appropriate time and continued with the takeoff and departure. Jeopardizing safety in the process.The cause was inadequate communication used by both aircraft; white aircraft on similar background and poor illumination of landing aircraft to be seen. Inadequate decision making by Captain of departing aircraft and failure to abort the departure. Ways to avoid this would be for all participating aircraft to be communicating effectively and for all aircraft to monitor the frequency and flow of traffic. Other avoidance measure would be to have an ATC facility present at the airport and better positioning of the hold short line for both sides of the runway to be seen.

column121: Everything was normal coming out of ZZZ. The original flight was ZZZ-ZZZ1. Takeoff; climb; level off and descent were normal. Enroute there were slight deviations for weather but nothing major. It was a little smoky into ZZZ1 so we started the ILS 15 then circle to land [Runway] 33 into ZZZ1. Everything was going as briefed and we picked up the airport inbound on the ILS and decided to do a visual [Runway] 33 from there. The Captain was flying since it is a Captain airport. We were flaps 20 and breaking off the ILS to do the 'published' circling maneuver. I loaded the visual into the box to have better situational awareness. Once done with that; the Captain called for gear down flaps 30 in order to get configured. Shortly after I put the gear handle down; there was an aural warning and a caution/warning message. I believe it was nose door open; gear disagree and hyd 3 lo press. We both looked at the messages and the Captain called for a go around. We went through the go around procedure rather smoothly and got the autopilot on and continued a climb to 15;000 ft. MSL. As we were climbing we read the messages and the Captain called for me to [advise ATC] and that we were diverting to ZZZ2. Once we were established in the climb; the Captain transferred the aircraft controls to me so he could run the QRH. The Captain kept the entire crew and passengers informed throughout this entire flight. There was contact made with appropriate personnel; including Dispatch; ATC; Maintenance and the Company. I relayed information to ATC while the Captain ran the QRH. I believe he ran the QRH for the gear disagree message first. That cleared the disagree message and we were left with hyd 3 lo press. There was 0% fluid in hyd 3 reservoir. We continued to ZZZ2 at 15000 as directed by ATC; they eventually handed us off to Approach where we asked for a hold over ZZZ2 VOR. This helped us with time management so the QRH could be ran properly. After going through all the steps in the QRH; we finally got the gear down with three green indicators. Fuel was being monitored this whole time and there was more than enough fuel to make it to ZZZ2 and hold for an appropriate amount of time. Once the gear was down and the QRH ran we; asked for the RNAV 27 into ZZZ2. We descended in the hold and then continued to approach as normal. Eventually the approach became a visual and we landed safely following the QRH procedure. We made a complete stop on the runway and made the decision to be 'tugged' off of the runway due to lack of steering. There was a large puddle of hydraulic fluid under the nose when parked and inspection was completed.  I believe that this was an outstanding crew and a phenomenal job was accomplished by the entire crew and all personnel involved. Required actions were accomplished in a; safe; timely and professional manner.

column121: Landing Runway 28 GDL; night VMC with bright moonlight. Set up to land Runway 28 using the ILS DME 2 with the ILS DME 2 transition from IKBAN. At the last minute; Control told us to descend via the LIVRI 1D Arrival.  We quickly put this arrival into the FMC; but did not change the approach. Shortly after starting the arrival; we were given direct to PLADE with continued descent to the mandatory altitude of 9;000 ft at PLADE which is the point leading into the ILS Approach to Runway 28. There was a discontinuity between PLADE and CI28; which I closed up. Once we reached PLADE; I asked my FO (First Officer) to set 7;100 ft. in the mode control panel which is the minimum altitude at CI28 and started a VNAV descent with the intention to arm the ILS just prior to CI28. At approximately 7;600 ft. we received a warning 'too low terrain'. We could visually see the terrain and started to level off; then; very quickly received 'terrain; pull up' with amber showing on the terrain display.  I immediately complied by going into the CFIT recovery procedure. We climbed to approximately 8;000 ft. and leveled off seeing that we were well clear of all terrain (no more warning and terrain indicator showed us to be clear). We then reassessed. At that time; we were very close to CI28 and had plenty of light from the city and a very bright moon. We were also in a good position to continue to the airport; so I continued to an uneventful landing.
column123: We were landing Runway 28 in Guadalajara. Night VMC conditions with moonlight provided adequate visual references. We were set up to land Runway 28 using the ILS DME2 or localizer Runway 28 with the transition for the ILS DME 2 with the IKBAN transition. At the last minute; Control told us to descend via the LIVRI 1D arrival. After changing to this arrival in the FMS; we realized that we needed to descend quickly. To complicate matters; Control give us directions to go direct to PLADE. I estimate Control shortened our arrival by 40 to 50 miles in giving us the arrival change and direct PLADE which greatly compounded our decent plan. This caused us to have to execute an expedited decent while having to re-program the FMS and plan for this new routing. I verbalized terrain that was to our right as we were turning direct PLADE and both the CA (Captain) and I correlated this to what we were seeing on our terrain displays. Prior to reaching PLADE; Control cleared us for the ILS Zulu Runway 28. However; we heard cleared for the ILS Two. I attribute this miscommunication to language barriers. CA asked me to set 7;100 ft.; which would've been correct for ILS 2 but 7;700 ft would've been correct for ILS Z. We got the 'too low; terrain' oral warning followed by the 'terrain; pull up'. CA commanded auto pilot off; stow speed brake (Even though they weren't in use); pitched up and added full thrust. We had the terrain in sight the whole time; as well as the Runway environment. We climbed approximately 500 ft before determining that we could safely continue the approach without incident. We landed on Runway 28 without incident.

column121: We were doing a late night flight to Evansville on the visual approach for Runway 22 and the FO (First Officer) (Pilot Flying) drifted below the glide slope as he went to full visual not cross referencing a glide slope. I told him he was sinking below it and about that time we received the glide slope warning as he was correcting. Pilot Monitoring stated the Pilot Flying was too low and below the glide slope while shortly after the glide slope GPWS went off.I believe the cause of this was improper scan of the Pilot Flying while transitioning to the visual part of the approach and also it being late at night and being tired on the last leg of the day.The Pilot Flying climbed the airplane back on to glide slope and proceeded with the visual approach and a good landing.Keep a continuous scan in and out of the cockpit while on a visual approach; especially when there are no PAPIs.
column123: While hand flying the approach around 500 ft. to Runway 22 in Evansville; I allowed the aircraft to drift below glide slope. The Captain; who was PM (Pilot Monitoring) noticed and informed me I was getting low. I also received a 'glide slope' aural warning. I quickly corrected pitch and power to get back on glide slope and landed without any further issues. The Captain informed me I was getting low; and the 'glide slope' GPWS aural alert. I believe the cause was my insufficient scan of the flight instruments; as well as transitioning from the glide slope to scan the runway environment. Since it was nighttime; and since Runway 22 in Evansville has no VASI or PAPI; I believe my sight picture was incorrect; and I judged the aircraft to be higher than it was. I corrected pitch and power and returned to the glide slope for a normal landing. I believe I can do a better job of scanning the flight and navigation instruments while on approach; especially to an airport with no VASI or PAPI.

column121: I was attempting a landing at ZZZ; Runway XX; at approximately XA:45 in a 1976 Cessna 172M with a 160 hp Lycoming engine. I was the sole occupant. It was a hot day and density altitude was about 4;950 ft. at the time. As I flared for touchdown over Runway XX; the wings lost their lift in the thin air and heat emanating from surface and the plane dropped 5+ feet causing it bounce even higher due to the spring steel landing gear. At first I tried to salvage the landing on the amount of runway I had left but after a successive bounce and drop it was clear that I was experiencing a severe oscillating (porpoising/bouncing) effect that would not end well for me or the airplane. Coming off that bounce I then applied full power for a go-around. On the subsequent landing I held the nose up as long as possible after the mains touched down. The nose wheel was indeed flat but I was able to taxi from the runway and exit the aircraft. There was no prop strike but apparently the bounce(s) left a wrinkle in the firewall. The airplane was subsequently flown; repositioned; by a pilot/mechanic to ZZZ1 and is awaiting repair at an airframe repair shop there. I was not hurt in this incident.

column121: I was approach the ZZZ from the north; preparing to perform a touch and go at the airport before flying northbound back towards ZZZ1. Before beginning my VFR descent; I obtained the current weather at the airport; and I found that the wind would be favoring Runway XX at the airport. From there I determined that in order to conduct a standard pattern entry; I should overfly the airport at 1;700 ft. MSL or 500 ft. above pattern altitude; and then perform a 225 degree turn to enter the left downwind from a 45 degree angle to Runway XX. At 10 miles; I began to announce my intentions regularly at intervals of approximately 2.5 miles.At a distance of around 5 miles from the airport; a Piper Pawnee preparing to tow a glider announced that they would be making a right downwind departure from Runway XX; towards the east. This would be the only transmission I noted from this aircraft until I would be touching down on Runway XX. I continued approaching the airfield and I had the two aircraft in sight. As the Pawnee turned downwind; I noted that my flight path would create a collision hazard; should I have continued my descent. As such; I immediately applied full power and climbed to 2;800 ft. MSL. I continued to watch as the Pawnee; with its glider in tow passed underneath me by approximately 500 ft. Climbing was likely the best evasive action in this case because I was in a high wing Cessna; while he was in a low-wing Pawnee. Had I descended; we both would have lost visual contact with each other and no additional action could have been taken should it have been necessary. The factors which appear to have contributed to this event appear to be the Pawnee pilot's lack of communication when departing; thus preventing me from restructuring my arrival plan so that I may safely avoid him. Additionally; he utilized a non-standard right downwind departure that was not charted at the airport for Runway XX. I believe that the Pawnee pilot could have extended their crosswind leg so as to remain well clear of me. On my own part; I should have taken earlier action to increase separation such as maintaining 3;000 ft. over the airport; as I was indeed aware that he would be making right traffic and climbing out of the airport. I had misjudged the climb rate of the Pawnee; and as such suspected that I could pass in front and above them before there would have been a collision hazard.

column121: In an extremely busy pattern on both sides of ZZZ's Runway XX; 2 planes (Aircraft Y and another- Aircraft Y might have been the lead flight in a flight of two; which I thought were normally not allowed at ZZZ) are given instructions by ATC to extend their right downwind and not turn base due to incoming straight in traffic (my airplane; Aircraft X). Aircraft Y seemingly acknowledges instruction; reports myself in sight; then turns base contrary to instructions. Aircraft Y is at this point 100-200 ft. above Aircraft X; and enters Aircraft X's field of view high and crossing to the left.ATC spots the deviation right as I did; and issues instructions to Aircraft Y to proceed and enter a left downwind.Aircraft Y then turns right- and begins entering final when ATC again contacts Aircraft Y to issue corrective action.During this time I have expedited my descent by going engine idle and adopting a slightly nose down attitude. Due to low AGL; nearby towers and already having 20 degrees of flaps in; I am hesitant to enter a forward slip and descend faster (aircraft type placards state to avoid forward slips with flaps). It's possible that I'm also reaching the end of my endurance- coming back from an hour of pattern practice at ZZZ1 where conditions were gusty. My flight time at this point was around 1.6 hours; and my flights up to that point at typically max of 1.8.I have debated if I should have immediately turned right or even left. While I had the right of way (a lower craft engaged in landing); the right of way isn't going to help me if I end up in a midair. In the end I think it was correct not to turn- the pattern was extremely busy; there were higher towers to the right and planes on extended down wind to both sides. I could have caused another NMAC by turning suddenly. ATC was aware of the situation; issuing instructions to Aircraft Y and I was going to pass safely- although close- below.While I pass under (300-400 ft. estimated?) Aircraft Y either completes a right 360 or an S-turn; and ends up in an extended left downwind.Because I am below the ideal approach path to Runway XX; I put power in and arrest my descent. I actually end up too high for the runway and decide to do a go around. The go around is uneventful and I land safely. I believe Aircraft Y actually landed in between my go around and actual landing.
column123: Moderately heavy traffic relative to ZZZ; training in progress; everything under control of the Trainee until I noticed an aircraft turning base without any sequence or landing clearance yet. This aircraft was initially part of a flight of two who had just separated in the downwind; because we have to issue separate landing clearances at ZZZ; the AID (Aircraft IDentification) is Aircraft Y. Multiple inbound aircraft on either downwind and on straight in.I key up and say 'Aircraft Y and flight; both of you guys; Don't come in on base; there is traffic at the [VFR reporting point]; so don't come in on base right now; you are going to follow the guy on a 3 mile final' The [VFR reporting point] is 3 mile final; Aircraft Y is starting a 1 mile right base so this is plenty of time to catch and correct this pilot. The second aircraft from the original flight of two responds traffic in sight and immediately corrects back to the downwind. I respond to 2 other calls then scan back to ensure Aircraft Y was correcting; he wasn't; I say 'Aircraft Y it looks like you are doing exactly what I said not to do; unless the radar is deceiving me here.' Pilot responds 'Aircraft Y was already on base'; I respond 'OK; well I just said don't go on base; you missed that part; so let's go right turn back to the north downwind for both you and the other guy (his flight wing man; who was following instructions)' Pilot responds 'Affirm; right turn; north downwind; Aircraft Y'. I tell the original wing man to now turn base; he correctly responds; I then scan back and see Aircraft Y not following instructions; again the pilot does the opposite and starts a left turn towards final before correcting right and continuing for the north downwind. I then say 'Aircraft Y are you literally on final right now cutting someone off?' Pilot responds 'No; I'm trying to get out of here; we're turning; Aircraft Y'I advise the traffic on final 'Aircraft X; the guy in front of you is moving out of your way; Runway XX cleared to land' His response seemed to be partially blocked but I heard 'Aircraft X'.All aircraft eventually land; brasher warning was not issued. The pilot of Aircraft X called the tower a couple hours later and said he wanted to file a NMAC. He said he was aware of the situation developing and heard the controller (me) several times tell the other aircraft not to turn base and issue instructions which the pilot of Aircraft Y repeatedly disobeyed. I should have issued a traffic alert to both aircraft; but at the minimum to Aircraft X.

column121: Near mid-air collision at the approach end ZZZ. It was almost a perfect storm that could have put us in a nearly unavoidable accident had the timing been a few seconds different. I was number one on final (RNAV Approach; VFR conditions; approximately 1/4 mile from threshold; visual landing) ahead of Aircraft Y on a survey mission landing for gas. The other aircraft was a Airtractor crop duster operating from a semi-trailer full of chemicals parked on the ramp. The pilot reported that his radio was marginal; but they had no replacement. His ground crew reported that he was using a hand-held radio. He made no calls before getting to the runway; and may not have even had the radio on until taking the runway. He certainly wasn't listening to it. He says he made a call taking the runway; but the FBO said it was more like a call after he was on the runway and rolling; and I didn't hear it or recognize it as there were also two aircraft operating same runway alignment; same frequency. I believe he took off from the midfield intersection just short of the midpoint of the 6307 ft. runway opposite direction to the one with two aircraft on final who had been making multiple position reports on CTAF. Winds were being called as calm until I was on three-mile final when they went to about 9 kts. crosswind. He obviously didn't visually clear the final approach course as he took the runway because I had all lights on (three strobes and three landing/taxi lights) and would have been quite visible had he looked.He was likely concentrating on centerline (tailwheel aircraft with a crosswind); and I was concentrating on the aim point on short final (end of the runway) so I could turn off at midfield with Aircraft Y behind me. When I was about ¼ mile on final; he came into my field of view just above the pavement less than 1000 ft. from the end of the runway. I went around and broke right; but he didn't deviate before he passed out of my view under my wing as I banked away. We passed abeam one another just south of the approach end of Runway XX. I doubt he saw me before I was wing-up passing him on his left.

column121: During the RNAV X 16L approach into RNO; we passed the KNACK approach fix at 6;900 ft.; as depicted; then set 6;200 ft. for WARAX and descended. The weather was IFR and our aircraft was not equipped with vertical guidance for this approach; so we descended quickly to see if we could get below the smoke layer. Reno Tower advised us to check altitude and said that we had a Low Altitude Warning. We double checked the approach charts and our altitude settings and found no error. We continued with the approach with no further Altitude Warning and did not need to adjust our altitude. The approach terminated with a missed approach due to low visibility and a diversion. Possibly a higher descent rate than what would have been suggested by an aircraft equipped with vertical guidance.Possibly a slower rate of descent or a navigation system with vertical guidance and would be very helpful.Upon arriving at LNAV minimums on the RNAV 16L approach into Reno; we were unable to get the runway or runway environment in sight due to smoke and low visibility. We decided to go around at the missed approach point and divert to ZZZ because it did not seem that conditions would improve for a second attempt at a landing. Low visibility; inaccurate meter report.

column121: The runway incursion occurred when a new CFI and myself were doing a night flight for the said CFI to become night proficient again in small GA aircraft after not flying GA in a long time. ZZZ Tower had closed 30 minutes prior and we were the only aircraft working in the pattern. I said that we should practice a power off 180 when the incursion occurred. The situation that occurred was that Airport Operations was doing sweeps of the runway and either they did not announce they were on the runway or we did not hear them due to being focused on the power off 180 maneuver. As we turn from base to final and get closer to the runway; we did not see anything on the runway that would have warranted a go-around. As we start to line the aircraft up with the runway a few hundred feet above the ground we see on the very edge of the runway two sets of stationary lights that were on the edge that we initially mistook for runway edge lights; but by the time we were about to pass; we saw it was a vehicle. Being low to the ground; still in a bank; low energy; and with nothing in front of us I determined going-around was not the optimal decision to make since the vehicle was now behind us. After seeing it was a vehicle of some sort I initially believed that the sweeper had moved off into the grass; however the driver came over after we landed and said he was still on the runway. We landed 300 ft. past the vehicle to the best of my knowledge so there was no immediate concern for a collision.I believe that the situation would not have occurred had several things happened. A breakdown of communication had occurred in the chain of events between the aircraft and the Ground Operations in terms of knowing the position of where everyone was. We made a downwind call but did not make subsequent calls due to the high workload of the power off 180 maneuver. I cannot confirm if the ground vehicle did or did not make a call; but to the best of my knowledge they did not make the call saying they were on the runway.

column121: Upon arrival at ZZZ; A normal landing was set up. Engine and flaps were in landing configuration. Landing gear was extended and confirmed down by green indicator light and by visual (pilot side main gear by viewing out pilot window; co-pilot side main and nose gear by wing located mirror). I landed on both main gear as normal. As the nose settled during deceleration; the nose fell to the runway. The plane slide to a stop on the center line of the runway. I turned off electronics; master switch and fuel. I exited the plane. I did not notice any fluids leaking from the engine compartment or any other location. I contacted the Manager of the airport and the Manager of the FBO. I also spoke briefly with the FAA. The FAA authorized removal of the aircraft from the runway. Upon lifting the nose of the plane by a front end loader; the nose gear limply extended. Upon kicking the nose gear a few times; the gear appeared to lock down. The plane was towed from the runway.

column121: On the way back from the practice area with a student after practicing steep turns; slow flight and stalls; I called ZZZ Tower 12 miles out asking for full stop taxi back intending to do some landing practice with my student. We were advised to report our position when we are inbound over the landfill. As we are about to report the landfill inbound when my ADS-B traffic lit up and advised me of traffic same altitude opposite direct less then half a mile. Looking up I immediately saw the Piper twin on a collision course and immediately began a hard right turning decent. At the time I was aware of a TBM aircraft behind and faster then me on an GPS approach for an avionics check; as well as another aircraft 500 ft. below me passing right to left. Tower was extremely busy at the time and I was unable to report the near miss immediately due to frequency volume and traffic. Adding to the confusion there was another aircraft in the area with the abbreviated tail number being Aircraft Y; while my tail number being Aircraft X. Tower told me to turn base which did make sense to me considering I was 5 miles out. I reported to Tower that there were two [similar N-numbers] on frequencies. He then told Aircraft Y to turn base followed by an instruction to me to turn to a heading of 070. I read back 070 but confirmation bias; and still rattled by the near miss; I turned to a heading of 170 thinking that he wanted me to turn an extended base to final; essentially cutting off the faster TBM closing on me. Tower asked me to read off my current heading and I reported flying 175 only then realizing the mistake. I was advised to make a right turn to intercept the final of Runway XX and report turning through 270 degrees. Already heading south this happened in less than 30 seconds. Now inbound on final for landing I was advised to copy a phone number on the ground for a possible pilot deviation. Finally on a half mile final I asked Tower twice if I was cleared to land Runway XX; and they advised me that I was 'still cleared to land XX' when I had not previously been given a landing clearance. Once on the ground I contacted Tower via phone and we discussed the situation; agreeing that parties were at fault a that a lot of events contributed to the situation.I believe that there were 3 main human factors causes that led to this incident.First the Tower was overloaded with inbound; transit; and outbound traffic and was unable to properly give traffic advisories to inbound and departing traffic.Second that Tower instructed me turn base when an aircraft with a similar call sign was the intended recipient even though it didn't make sense to me and at the time. Lastly that because of confirmation bias I expected to receive a base to final clearance when in reality he wanted me to intercept a left downwind entry into the pattern.

column121: Complete GPS Signal loss for 2 minutes without NOTAM or other notification. I have 5 devices with built-in GPS devices connected to 3 GPS antennas.  Additionally; I had a portable GPS; with its own GPS antenna and power supply.  All GPS devices reported a complete lost of signal.  I covered about 3.5 miles while on the visual approach.  Signal was restored; but the virtual glideslopes from the GPS's were not available. I reported the event to the tower. Since I was in visual condition; I was able to maintain control of the aircraft and was hand flying for the landing.  Since I had been navigating with GPSS and the autopilot; this could have created a surprise in the loss of GPSS steering.  Additionally; had this been in IMC; I would have lost the glideslope and needed to make changes.  I do not know how the coupled autopilot would have responded. I do not know where else to report this sudden loss of GPS signal.

column121: On an RNAV 34 approach into 11R; once landed on Runway 34; an aircraft landed on the opposing Runway 16. I had been switched from ZZZ Center 13 miles out and had made a 10 mile; 5 mile; 3 mile final and short final call out for [Runway] 34. No traffic was heard on the frequency until after the incursion. My aircraft could not have done a go-around as the aircraft was already on the ground and the configuration had already been changed; as well as the distance was gauged to be too close to gain the airspeed needed. I swerved to the left side of the Runway to avoid the traffic; while the other traffic swerved to the right side; relative to me. I pulled back on the yoke to provide drag to slow down; and applied the brakes heavily. We passed each other without the aircraft touching; and we were both still in motion when we passed. When my aircraft stopped I proceeded to taxi off on the next available taxiway. While doing this someone came onto the frequency asked me if I was all right and gave me their tail number. I then taxied back to the run-up area for Runway 34. Upon stopping; another aircraft came onto the frequency and gave me their information as well; the aircraft that was believed to have been the opposing traffic. With that I departed the active Runway of 34 to the north.

column121: An aircraft in crosswind for Runway 29. Aircraft Z is 4 NM to the South West 1;000 feet above pattern altitude; announcing intentions to enter the downwind. My aircraft (Aircraft X) is 2 miles to the south at pattern altitude; announcing intentions to enter on the 45 for midfield downwind [Runway] 29; have the crosswind and 4 mile traffic insight. I announce to the now downwind traffic that I have them insight and will sequence behind them at the midfield downwind Runway 29. Aircraft Y departs Runway 29 and announces crosswind to perform touch and goes on Runway 29 (CFI and student). Aircraft Z announces performing a 360 for spacing. I believe that Aircraft Z was facing away from the field and did not see Aircraft Y in crosswind. I hear a radio call that Aircraft Z has cut off Aircraft Y; I look at my MFD and see the two aircraft appear very close to each other. The CFI in Aircraft Y announces that they will be performing a 360 for spacing behind the 'cutting aircraft'. I am closer than I would like to the aircraft in front of me and stay 100ft higher and slightly to the right to keep the aircraft insight. Aircraft in front announces intentions to full stop land; and I announce that I will extend downwind for spacing. Aircraft in front calls base; I continue on downwind. Aircraft in front calls final; I call and turn base. I call and turn final upon seeing the Aircraft on short final and deem spacing to be appropriate for the Aircraft to vacate the Runway. Reviewing the radio calls; my call to Final was stepped on. Aircraft Z calls base; I'm expecting him to be behind me on base. Aircraft in front calls that they are on the runway and will be vacating; I verify that spacing looks good. My passenger (2nd GA flight) calls for traffic to the left; I look and see the Aircraft Y in downwind. 5-10 seconds later the broadside of Aircraft Z passes in front of the windshield at 100ft or less; same altitude. I call 'go around sidestep left' as I can see the shadow of Aircraft Z on the right of me. I announce intentions to depart crosswind to the south. Not hearing any calls from Aircraft Z I become concerned. Using my MFD; I identify the call sign of the aircraft involved and ask if they are OK? They respond they are OK and that they are in Downwind. I believe that Aircraft Z confused my radio calls for that of the Aircraft in front of me. I believe that the near miss with Aircraft Y after his 360 for spacing caused him to lose situational awareness. I believe that my call for final being blocked did not help Aircraft Z's SA. I believe that the lack of availability of pattern work at ZZZ/ZZZ1/ZZZ2 is making non towered fields like LMO busier than they should be.

column121: During a normal flight lesson in the traffic pattern with my student; my student landed the plane with full brakes and the brakes locked up. With barely any directional control left I was able to keep the plane on the runway long enough to to make a taxi way. We took the taxiway at a fairly high speed and then I was able to regain directional control and bring us to a full stop. No damage was caused to the plane; runway or taxiway; or any signs or lights on the airport. Tower asked if we needed any assistance and I said negative just to taxi back to the ramp.

column121: I was pilot flying; my CA (Captain) was pilot monitoring. After receiving instructions from Approach to slow down to 170 KT until a 5-mile final; we got an IDG (Integrated Drive Generator) fault message. The CA pulled out the QRH and tried to address the issue. Shortly after; we got a multitude of issues. We notified ATC and we agreed that we would be safe to land. Slat flap fail; AT/AP (Autothrottles/Autopilot) disconnect; and more. Suddenly we lost all screens and everything went black. The RAT (Ram Air Turbine) deployed and I had to continue flying using the Captains PFD (Primary Flight Display). The CA [requested priority handling] and was cleared by Approach to land and swap to Tower.CA told the FAs (Flight Attendant) we were making a precautionary landing. Tower said they had the trucks rolling and landed as best we could normally. We dropped the flaps on schedule and noticed that the Slat Flap Disagree persisted. The CA then took over the controls with ample time before the 1;000 feet call and I swapped roles to PM. We notified ATC that we could not verify [if] the gear and flaps were down. Luckily; Tower notified us that another plane verified they were down. We landed uneventfully and taxied to the gate under our own power. No passengers were hurt; only the first row or two knew something was abnormal due to the RAT vibrations; the rest of the passengers were unaware of the incident. [The cause was an] internal electrical malfunction at a time of high workload. Potentially go-around and troubleshoot If issues happen on final.

column121: Landing at ZZZ with about 40 minutes of fuel estimated upon arrival. Upon entering traffic pattern; I experienced some delays due to faster aircraft on final for [Runway] XXR. Cleared to land number two behind a military F-16 and ahead of another military jet; I believe an F15. Tried keeping my speed up; but it wasn't enough and told to go-around. Upon going around and entering the traffic pattern a second time; I was now at minimum fuel and declared minimum fuel. Tower told me they will get me down and that I'm number two behind a Company jet. Following behind the Company jet; I noticed they were taking a long time to get off the runway; so I slowed down as much as I could so I could avoid another go-around. The Company jet began to exit the runway as I was getting close to touching down and then I was told a second time to go-around. I replied 'Unable' and landed. By the time of touchdown the jet was clear of the runway completely. Tower and I both exchanged apologies as I was taxiing to the FBO and went on our way. I wanted to report this since I did land without a landing clearance; but I was uncomfortable with doing another lap in the pattern with such low amount of fuel. I felt the safety of flight would be compromised and decided to land rather than risk a worse situation.

column121: I was vectoring Aircraft X into ZZZ for an ILS Approach. He was vectored through final and left high for VFR traffic on the localizer. He called the field in sight over the Final Approach Fix; was cleared for a Visual and switched to ZZZ Tower. He turned towards ZZZ1.  VFR aircraft above 1500 feet near the localizer are a conflict because 2400 feet is the crossing altitude for the Initial Approach Fix ZZZZZ. VFR aircraft departing ZZZ or ZZZ2 Class D southbound are in a head on conflict with any aircraft on the localizer into ZZZ. IFR aircraft often end up being too high for a localizer approach because they can't descend because of VFR traffic on the localizer and they get a Visual Approach instead. It's also not uncommon for aircraft to be vectored through the final approach course to avoid traffic or descend. Both of these courses of action can disorient pilots; but there is nowhere to break them out without creating another conflict somewhere else. A few miles east of the localizer is higher terrain. An aircraft at 3000 feet within 10 miles of the airport will not be able to climb to the Minimum Vectoring Altitude in time. If the aircraft continues on a vector north to parallel ZZZ1; they could conflict with a departure off of ZZZ1; likely a prop on a 020 heading climbing to 3000 feet.  ZZZ1; ZZZ; and ZZZ2 are so close to each other that it can be confusing which airport they see when they aren't able to fly a straight path inbound because of VFR conflicts.ZZZ localizer approach course need to be controlled airspace. VFR aircraft should not be able to fly on or through the localizer without talking to ATC. It's impossible to predict the actions of a VFR aircraft not on frequency. They climb; descend and maneuver around traffic they do see; sometimes making 360's.
column123: About XA:25 PST I observed Aircraft X; who was on an ZZZ ILS Runway XXL approach; heading westbound about 2 miles east of ZZZ1.  They had gone through the ZZZ final and appeared to be flying towards ZZZ1 and not ZZZ.  I told the Local controller and the Cab Supervisor then called ZZZ1 Tower on the shout line advising them of the traffic and that ZZZ Tower was attempting to turn Aircraft to the north towards ZZZ. Aircraft X's data tag was then changed to Visual Approach; ZZZ VA XXL. Aircraft X landed ZZZ Runway XXL without incident.

column121: Was instructing in owner's aircraft. He wanted to practice takeoff and landings in the pattern at ZZZ. We did three three pointers. They were acceptable. Not greasers but nothing fundamentally wrong. We then did two wheel landings. Again; acceptable but not greasers but no warning flags. It was getting hot and thermal activity was affecting the landings so we decided to do one more and then call it quits. He did an acceptable wheel landing and at first everything was OK. Straight on the runway and wheels down. He then put the tail down - prematurely in my judgement as we still had a lot of speed (not excessive). The plane immediately yawed to the right. I intervened and applied full opposite rudder (I think he did as well) but it did not stop the yaw. This aircraft has no brakes on the instructor side so unable to apply brakes. The pilot did not use brakes to control the yaw. We ended up in the grass next to the runway. The pilot was pretty shaken up so I taxied back onto the runway and to the tie down. We determined there was no damage to the aircraft. I recommended to the pilot to have the tail wheel inspected in case there was some issue with it.I was surprised that we had the yaw to the right after what appeared to be an acceptable wheel landing. There were no wind gusts or other factors that I was aware of. It was either a result of lowering the tail too quickly (gyroscopic precession) or there is an issue with the tail wheel mechanism.

column121: Aircraft X was assigned the unadvertised XYL for arrival (advertised as a departure only runway) to accommodate for the required runway inspection by airport ops after this type of aircraft arrives or departs. It's easier to stop traffic and inspect the departure runway.  Aircraft Y checked on 10 to 15 miles out and I cleared them to land and advised that traffic would Line Up and Wait/depart prior to their arrival. I was very focused on my sequence because we were in weather reroute program and there were numerous in-trail restrictions over most (all?) departure fixes. I got too focused on the departures and did not appropriately scan for the arrivals progress. When I instructed Aircraft Y to Line Up and Wait; Aircraft X advised that they were going around. I responded with a subsequent go around and maintain 3000 feet shortly after followed by a 300 heading after coordinating with Local Control. Aircraft Y crossed the Hold Short Lines but not the edge line. I will add a memory aid to my personal technique to prevent this from occurring again. I plan to use an empty strip holder to conservatively predict where the arrival will fit in with my departures. For example; if the arrival is 10 miles away I would put the blank strip 2 departures back and not depart beyond the blank strip without reassessing the situation. This will force me to periodically check on the progress of the arrival to my departure runway.

column121: We were holding short of [Taxiway] 1 on Taxiway 2 at ZZZ. ATC cleared Aircraft Y into position and hold Runway XXL. In the meantime Aircraft Z was on short final to the same runway at approximately 1;500 ft. AGL (just a wag). Aircraft Y turned left on Taxiway 1 and crossed the hold lines for [Runway] XXL and continued till he came to a screeching stop as I assume he then saw the Aircraft Z coming down the ILS. Aircraft Y was well across the hold lines but had not crossed onto displaced threshold portion of the runway. ATC nor anybody else said anything. There was this weird silence; and without hearing anyone else saying anything I keyed the microphone and said 'Hey Aircraft Z go around;' and Aircraft Z immediately started a Go Around. Then finally ATC came on the radio and gave Aircraft Z; go around instructions; and told Aircraft Y that it'll be a few minutes for take off due to wake turbulence. Eventually Aircraft Y was cleared for take off and then we were cleared onto the runway. As we crossed the [Runway] XXL hold lines we saw fresh tire skid marks; approximately 20 ft. in length from Aircraft Y. Interesting thing is Aircraft Y never queried ATC during this event. We happened to have a jump seater commuting home and he provided me some data from the website Flight Aware. Flight Aware shows Aircraft Z at 475 ft. when they started to climb/Go Around; and ATC had previously cleared Aircraft Y to depart. On departure ATC said 'Sorry to Aircraft Z' and Aircraft Y acknowledged with 'Yeah we were confused.' I told/instructed Aircraft Z to 'go around;' after nobody else would say anything and the fact the two planes were on what appeared to be a collision course. It seemed as though ATC was unaware they had two planes going to the same runway.

column121: On the final phase of the Visual Approach; backed up by the ILS Runway XY to ZZZ; with the intention to capture the glide path from above; the Auto Pilot (AP) and Auto Throttles were disengaged. Prevailing conditions were Good Visibility and varying Tail Winds of up to nine (9) Knots. During the final portion of the descent to touchdown; with a visual transition to the PAPI reference already established (PAPI were showing 3 RED-1 WHITE); the Aircraft descended below the referenced visual glide path; with the tendency to fly over the Runway XY overrun area at an altitude (AGL) of less than 50 ft. The moment the Captain; having the Pilot Monitoring (PM) duties; mentioned his estimation for being low on the glide path; the aircraft was overflying a point short of the RWY by about 500 feet. He consecutively called for a Go Around; which was immediately executed by the PF (Pilot Flying). The Flight landed after a RADAR pattern on the same runway uneventfully.[The cause was] Human Factors / Pilot Flying error on visually estimating the projected Glide Slope to Runway XY; with the current Tail Wind conditions. Root cause is the initially higher angle of descent / rate of descent to capture the glide slope from above; that ended up to an over correction - reverse situation of flying finally lower than the projected/correct slope. [I suggest] earlier Aircraft configuration for the Final Approach Phase and timely request from the ATC for lower altitudes / earlier descent to the recommended altitudes that lead to a better projected sequence of flying tasks.
column123: FO was flying pilot. Performed visual approach and FO had a hard time trying to catch the glide slope from above because he was trying to slow down and put flaps down while on OPEN DESC. FO called for 'the bird'; which caught me off guard because it is the first time anyone has asked for the flight path vector while trying to intercept the ILS and while disconnecting the AP. He not only disconnected AP but also eventually the auto thrust without previously briefing it. I told him to never disconnect the auto thrust again while trying to save a visual approach. Finally he was established on the ILS. Below 500 feet he said he was going to follow the PAPI. I glanced down at the VSI to make sure we were stable and when I looked backed up we had 4 red on the PAPIs and at the same time we received a GS GPWS warning. Became unstable. I loudly and sternly called 'unstable; go-around.' Performed go-around as RA called 50 feet. FO performed a soft go-around. I overrode his decision after he brought back TL (Thrust Lever) to CLB and I pushed TL to TOGA since we were in ZZZ (high airport elevation) with high OAT. I honestly could not tell you the altitude when the FO brought back the TL to CLB. I wanted to make sure we were safe since the go-around was performed at 50 feet from an unsafe position. At 50 feet RA; aircraft was not anywhere near the approach end of the runway. Came back for an uneventful landing.FO descended below glide slope and PAPI showed 4 red.Soft Go-around procedure should be changed or at least highlight the need to take into account airport elevation and OAT when deciding to make a soft go-around from a low altitude with an obvious performance deterioration.Soft Go-around procedure should be changed or at least highlight the need to take into account airport elevation and OAT when deciding to make a soft go-around from a low altitude with an obvious performance deterioration.

column121: Arriving SEA; ATC cleared us to follow aircraft ahead to visual approach 34L.  We were given 170kts to final fix due to ATC spacing us just over 3 miles behind a B737.  I was Pilot Monitoring; the First Officer the Pilot Flying had our plane on autopilot using heading select and vertical speed to navigate onto the localizer and glideslope.Just as the plane was joining the localizer; a mild buffeting was felt followed by a rapid roll in direction of the turn. Aural warning 'Bank Angle' annunciated.  The First Officer immediately disconnected the autopilot and reduced the bank angle to normal parameters.  The rest of the approach was uneventful.On visual approaches; especially with close spacing; fly above the path of the preceding aircraft.
column123: While on dogleg to final for RWY 34L; descending to 2;400 feet MSL following preceding B737 by 3.5 NM encountered wake turbulence while on autopilot.  As autopilot initiated turn to join the localizer the aircraft encountered the wake turbulence causing a rapid roll to the right (in the direction of the turn).  The aircraft rolled past 30 degrees of bank and an aural warning 'bank angle' sounded.  The Pilot Flying recognized the excursion and disengaged the autopilot and reduced the bank angle to normal range.  Pilot Flying continued the approach and landed uneventfully.Consider staying above preceding aircraft's altitude/flight path to avoid wake turbulence or increase range between aircraft.

column121: On Runway XX approach into ZZZ; I attempted to lower the gear by placing the landing gear handle down to extend the gear. During the extension process; both main gear green lights came on but neither the red or green light came on for the nose gear. I told ZZZ Tower that I needed to go around due to a system malfunction. After completing the go around; I followed the QRH procedures. The QRH procedures did not correct the unsafe nose gear condition. I then asked for priority handling and I contacted Tower for permission for a low pass to have them see if they could confirm the status of the nose gear. They said it appeared like all my gear was down; but they were not certain as it was night and it was difficult for them to have good visibility of the nose gear. After the low pass; I went back through the QRH checklists again to try to correct the condition. Once it was determined the nose gear would remain in an unsafe condition; I prepared for a potential nose gear up landing. At that time; I took the time to brief the Jump Seater and we reviewed the egress checklist. Since the Jump Seater was an experienced pilot; I handed the egress checklist to the Jump Seater and had him read through it. We discussed egress options in the event I became incapacitated. We briefed the scenario several times to ensure we had a clear understanding of what to expect and what actions we may need to take. After all appropriate checklists were ran and briefed and the approach reviewed and briefed; I elected at that point to land. I flew the ILS for Runway XX. I followed QRH guidance to land on the mains and hold the nose off the runway while gently lowering it as appropriate. In accordance with the procedures; I pulled the stop and feather pressed the stop button and turned the fuel and hydraulics off. After safely coming to a stop on the runway center line; I asked the Jump Seater if he was ok or if he was injured he said he was not injured and was safe. I assessed myself and reported to ATC that we were both safe. I completed the remaining items of the egress checklist and we exited the aircraft.Nose wheel door failure.Run the checklists; keep the Jump Seater informed and remain calm.Metro nose gear door inspections more often.

column121: Upon configuring flaps from 25 to 30; Trailing Edge Flaps indicated a split and stopped tracking at 27 degrees. LE FLAPS TRANSIT light was also illuminated (amber light). A go-around was accomplished to enable time for non normal checklists to be run. LEADING EDGE FLAPS TRANSIT checklist was run which led us into TRAILING EDGE FLAPS DISAGREE checklist. Flaps were left at 27 degrees with flap handle at 25 setting. Vref15 +5 was the approach speed selected and an uneventful landing and taxi to the gate was accomplished.
column123: On Final Approach to XXL in ZZZ and configuring from flaps 25 to flaps 30 at about 1;500 feet the trailing edge flap needle indicators displayed a split and the flaps stopped at approximately 27 degrees. The LE FLAP TRANSIT light was also illuminated. After taking a few seconds to realize what was happening and coming through approximately 1;000 ft the Captain called for a go around. I executed the go around; however upon attempting to retract the flaps we realized they were stuck at 27 degrees and would not retract at all. I continued to fly the plane and the Captain advised ATC and then ran the non-normal LEADING EDGE FLAP TRANSIT QRH followed by the TRAILING EDGE FLAP DISAGREE QRH. We received vectors from ATC and took our time setting up for a flaps 27 landing using the VREF15+5 knot wind additive. While being vectored back to the final approach course the Captain TEST and the FA's and made an announcement to the PAX. We landed and taxied to the gate uneventfully.
