column121: On a VFR flight from HTL to 48D in day VMC with unrestricted visibility and high ceilings; with a right seat Private Pilot passenger. Briefed that Runway 09/27 at 48D was NOTAMed closed; and elected to land Runway 22 given westerly winds and NOTAM.  I made multiple callouts on CTAF including base and final.  During rollout; the pilot passenger noticed and informed me of a remote-controlled RC aircraft ahead; apparently above Runway 09/27 but overflying the extended centerline of Runway 22; about 600ft from the end of Runway 22. I did not myself see the RC aircraft. Upon safe landing and shut-down we became aware that there was an RC aircraft group operating at a series of RVs south of Runway 09/27 and that this was the reason for the NOTAM. While it is completely acceptable to have RC aircraft operations in an airport environment given NOTAMs; RC operators should be briefed to avoid overflight of an extended centerline for an active runway. Alternatively; if RC operations require the overflight of an extended centerline of another runway; that runway (or the airport) should be NOTAMed closed as well.If we were either performing a touch-and-go; or had to perform a go-around; the RC aircraft would have been directly in our path and would have posed a collision risk.

column121: I took off from ZZZ in my 2008 CAW SportCruiser; an aircraft that I had recently purchased. This was my first flight from ZZZ where I had based the aircraft. I flew around the local area to familiarize myself with landmarks in a 15 NM area near ZZZ. When I returned to land; an aircraft that was returning to the airport announced their intention to land on runway XX. I set up to enter the pattern for that runway about 5 NM away and went over the approach in my mind. Then; when I was about 3 NM away; I heard a pilot announce he was entering a left downwind for runway XY. I was surprised and concerned and made changes to my power setting and flaps setting from downwind settings. I changed my approach to enter a crosswind for runway XY. I was still a little shaken by the sudden change in approach for landing. The downwind and base legs were uneventful. The final approach started well; but as I neared the runway threshold; the PAPI system indicated that I was below the glide slope. I added a little power to get back on the glide slope; but I was too far into the landing. The aircraft hit hard and then the nose gear collapsed. The aircraft slid about 250 feet on top of the nose gear fairing and cowling. The prop struck the asphalt and one blade snapped off. Fortunately; I was thoroughly strapped into the four-point restraint harness and was uninjured.In retrospect; I should have abandoned the approach when it became unstable; and initiated a go-around. This will be a very expensive lesson. I am grateful that I emerged from the experience physically unscathed.

column121: Close call event on short final to Runway 13 and unstable approach at ZZZ after finishing a long cross-country home from ZZZ1. Event occurred after being cleared for the visual approach to Runway 13 from Approach and switching to the local advisory frequency. I made a call on the advisory frequency on a 5 mile initial to the left downwind for Runway 31; intending to enter the traffic pattern. The aircraft was descending and I was very fast; 130 KIAS; with the intent to slow the aircraft in the pattern. Switching back to Approach frequency to cancel the IFR flight plan I decided on a straight in approach to Runway 13 to save time; while on Approach I missed the local traffic call of an aircraft taking Runway 31 to depart. After switching back to the local advisory frequency I made an advisory call for a 3 mile final to Runway 13 and I was switching the landing runway. The ADS-B data link immediately showed a traffic target directly ahead and climbing from Runway 31; passengers in my airplane spotted the traffic; and the other aircraft and I communicated to ensure we would avoid each other. Being distracted by the traffic close call; handling the radio swaps; and changing our approach I did not slow the aircraft down or add full flaps. Attempted to land with two notches of flaps and very fast; floated the aircraft most of the runway with braking in the final 1000 ft. Factors and Chain of Events:1. Long day of flying; and being tired from week at ZZZ1 contributed to wanting to be home. Additional time pressure of needing to pick up the family dog from the boarder affected decision making on speeds to fly and ultimately the runway selection.2. Concerns about forgetting to close the IFR flight plan; especially when tired; under time pressures; and distractions of passengers (kids); etc; contributed to cancelling in the air very close to the local airport operations. I should have stayed on the advisory frequency whenever within 5 miles of the airport that is known to be busy. Without ADS-B I would not have known about the departing traffic.3. Long two weeks of very good; challenging; and very safe flying supported an overconfidence in flying the airplane outside of SOPs; which contributed to flying way too fast on short final and almost floating the entire runway. Established patterns; speeds; altitudes; and power settings are practiced and used for good reason...to take variability out of flying and allow for handling of non-routine issues. Flying outside of SOP adds variability and multiplies risks that could overwhelm the pilot.4. Inaction of sticking with the originally stated plan to enter the downwind for Runway 31; the radio call is not just an advisory of what I am doing; but provides information to other operators on how the airport is flowing. Changing the plan adds risk to all of the other operators.

column121: While practicing short field landings; I came in on final at 70 kts. for Runway XX at ZZZ. I touched down on the runway with a slight right crosswind as I had done on the landing 5 or so minutes before; where I felt the same thing had happened; being only two of the three wheels initially touched. On the first landing this was not a problem and we took off again without issue; but during this approach I attempted to fix the problem by putting in right rudder as my Instructor told me to; which would have been fine if I had not also hit the right brake as well; causing the Aircraft to turn sharp to the right and drift sideways into the ditch between the runway and the taxiway. Both me and my Instructor were ok; and the plane seemed intact. My Instructor took the controls swiftly after we veered off course and maneuvered the plane gently into the ditch. After this we turned the engine off.
column123: I am a Flight Instructor and was practicing landings with my student. We were practicing short field landings and were inbound for the second landing at the airport. On final the student had made the remark we were coming in slightly fast and began correcting for the correct airspeeds. The approach to landing itself was not abnormal.  It was on centerline and at a proper glidepath; just slightly fast. Due to the fact that we had been practicing short field landings; the student was aiming to flare at a point that likely should have been extended. As we flared; a little bit of crosswind caused there to be a slight sideload on the left wheel and at the time it was the only wheel touching the ground. As the Instructor; I had mentioned right rudder to get us realigned with centerline and reduce the sideloading. The student used right rudder but accidentally had his foot on the brake as well as the rudder. This caused the plane to brake solely on the right side and begin skidding down the runway. Out of fear; I believe the brake was pushed slightly more as opposed to less. At this time I started calling that I had the flight controls and the student took his hands off of the controls. At this point; the aircraft was perpendicular to the runway. Momentum had the plane still rolling slowly. I was not sure at the time if there was any damage to the tires due to side load or skidding and did not want to apply brakes in an effort to reduce damage. At this point I treated the plane as if it was a soft field landing; I used minimal braking and let the plane come to a stop in the grass directly next to the runway. I have a very small feet and know for myself that if my heels are on the ground I am on the rudder. In order to touch the brake; my leg needs to be lifted. This is not the case for most other people. In the future; I have now learned that to reduce the chances of this happening again; I will verify on short final that the students have their feet only on the rudder and not touching the brakes. Instead of teaching 'heels on the ground' I will now specify that 'balls of feet should be on the rudders'

column121: Aircraft was on final approach segment of GPS36 at M41.  Conditions were hazy with scattered clouds at 3000 ft MSL (2500 ft AGL).   Subject aircraft was VMC prior to FAF and had cancelled IFR and switched to CTAF.  At two miles from touchdown end of Runway 36; with aircraft at approx 1000 ft AGL; pilot not flying (PNF) called traffic off right wing.  PF noted rapidly approaching target and banked left.  Both PF and PNF noted a large UAV (drone) pass within 1/4 mile of right wing.  PF switched to Memphis approach frequency and reported near miss.  A missed approach was not required and PF was able to recover to stabilized approach and land without further incident.  Subject aircraft had ADS-B in/out capability (L-3 Lynx NGT-9000 transponder).  The UAV did not show on display (Traffic Information Services).Operating a drone on final approach course to airport; in MVFR conditions is extremely careless and reckless.  Due to haze conditions; the unlighted drone was difficult to spot and subject aircraft was fortunate to have rated pilot in right seat watching for traffic.  Due to conditions; PF was focused on maintaining stabilized approach in marginal VFR conditions.

column121: Prior to taxiing onto Runway XX/XY at ZZZ I observed the pattern and both approach ends for landing or departing aircraft. None in sight; I radioed back taxiing Runway XY for departure. Approximately 500 ft from the threshold of Runway XY; I noticed Aircraft Y banking hard onto the final of Runway XY about 45 degrees to the left of my nose. The pilot appeared to have entered on a right base in what is otherwise a left traffic pattern. I called again stating back taxiing XY; assuming they would hear and also see me during their base turn. No response and they were lined up for XY head-on with me at this point so I applied 70% power to accelerate to a turn out at the threshold area. Aircraft Y proceeded to land shortly after I had pulled out of the way. A private jet [Aircraft Z] was holding short of Runway XY at the ramp at this point and also likely observed the situation. Given the size of the jet (wouldn't have fit in the small turn out at the end of Runway XY) and how the pilot never attempted a go-around; this could have been a real incident if it were the jet on the runway instead of me which is what concerns me. I received communications from the jet (jet that is based at ZZZ) that was holding short as well.After later talking with the operator; they did state the aircraft has no radios but insisted they were in the right.While landing aircraft have the right away; this aircraft was not yet in the pattern when I had began to back taxi. I believe they entered the pattern directly on a right base; making a right turn to final which was unexpected given this is a left traffic pattern. Furthermore; I was in a position where I could not easily turn my aircraft around and exit the runway which should've been clear from their view point; making a go-around the best option for the landing aircraft. Instead they proceeded to land; needlessly making a dangerous situation for the both of us.Ultimately nobody was injured but this entire scenario could have been mitigated by Aircraft Y following the established landing pattern; making himself more visible and giving any aircraft on the ground or the landing aircraft to take the appropriate evasive action.

column121: We were on final for Runway XX; in Aircraft X; and my student accidentally put in flaps 40 on about a mile final (this is pretty easy to do as the flaps are really touchy and the indicator is above the students eye level). This caused him to get low and slow. I asked if he wanted to go around and he decided to continue. His focus was mainly on that and he failed to notice he was coming in crabbed to the right. I told him to use left rudder to straighten; which he did about 10 ft above the ground but at that point we were extremely left of center line and he panicked banked to the right; very hard; I told him to go around but he did not respond and we touched down completely side loaded and fairly hard. I took the controls and went around; but with all the flaps in it took a bit to get off the ground and the tail dragged on the runway. Once we got off the ground; I told the tower we were going around and that we were making this a full stop because we had a hard landing. [They] responded by telling us to set up for a left downwind for Runway XX1; and that we were cleared to land. In the meantime I did hear the tower send maintenance to inspect the runway; and they found no damage. This landing was fine and we taxied back and had maintenance (Name) inspect the tail. He said there was nothing but cosmetic damage to the left elevator and tie down. The plane was flown again.

column121: Visual approaches to Runway XXR were in use at ZZZ at the time. Due to heavy traffic in the vicinity of the airport; and terrain to the southwest; ATC kept us on a vector that aligned us with a roughly 2 mile final approach to XXR. The airport was extremely difficult to obtain visually; and neither the FO nor myself had it in sight until about 4 miles out. We called the airport in sight; and were cleared for visual approach. At that time; I turned the aircraft to the right to align the aircraft for a downwind in a left hand traffic pattern; and descended the aircraft to 6;500 feet; the appropriate altitude for the 5 mile visual fix. Reaching 6;500 feet; we began to feel uneasy; as the terrain south of the airport forms a bowl; with terrain reaching 8;000 feet in places. Once at the 6;500 feet altitude; we could visually see that there wasn't any room to maneuver the aircraft in the valley to align it with the final approach course (we had about a 3 mile wide; terrain-free swath to work with). At about the same time; ATC issued us a low altitude warning; with the accompanying 'check altitude immediately' instruction. At that time; while on a heading corresponding with base leg; we executed a climb to 7;500 feet; and told tower we were aborting the approach. We overflew the airport and executed a right hand turn to set up for right traffic for XXR; a visual approach which was completed successfully. [Cause was] ATC's vector that led us into a valley not wide enough to maneuver the aircraft on approach. Poor charting of terrain on VFR maps in company publications. I'm filling out a separate safety concern report recommending an additional alert page on the company charts advising pilots of the difficulty of shooting a close-in visual approach to runway XXR from the West.
column123: ZZZ was conducting visual approaches to runways XXR and XXL at the time of the event. ZZZ Approach was descending us for arrival and told us to expect the visual approach for Runway XXR. At about 20 miles from the airport; we were told to proceed direct to the airport and report the field in sight for the visual. At this time we were also told to maintain 9;000 feet for terrain and traffic in the area. We did not obtain visual contact with the airport until about 5 miles away; at which point we informed ZZZ Approach and they cleared us for the visual and told us to contact the tower. At this point the approach controller has us on a vector to intercept approximately 2 mile final for Runway XXR. The Captain made a turn to the right and began a descent to traffic pattern altitude of 6;500 feet to maneuver the aircraft for a stable approach and landing. This turn brought us toward gradually rising terrain northeast of the airport. As we continued to maneuver the aircraft; it became apparent that there was not enough room to safely align with the runway. We initiated a climb and turned back toward the airport. At this time tower issued a low altitude warning and told us to 'check altitude immediately' (we were climbing through 7;000 feet at this time). We responded and said that we were climbing and turning back toward to the airport and that we would need to abort the approach and turn right to re-enter right traffic for XXR. A safe approach and landing was then completed without further incident.ZZZ Approach kept us high due to terrain and traffic and issued a tight vector for a visual approach that only allowed for a 2 mile final for the landing runway. Contributing factors included a busy GA traffic environment and inability to see the airport visually until about 5 miles away.A better way to conduct this approach when arriving from the west would be to join the RNAV approach from vectors or an initial approach fix (especially if this were to be conducted at night) or to vector the aircraft over the airport and down the valley to enter a right downwind for XXR.

column121: [We] encountered two wake turbulence events while on the downwind at 8;000 feet with Atlanta approach control. First Officer maintained control of the aircraft on both occurrences. After the second sudden right roll we observed the large aircraft we were in trail of and now was below our altitude. There was no 'bank angle' aural. Perhaps ATC separation issue.

column121: We were flying RNAV Stadium Visual to Runway 29. We were on speed and fully configured by the approach gates. I was hand flying. As we were rolling out on final; [a] B777 was holding in position on Runway 22R at intersection Y. As our aircraft was approaching Runway 29; [the] B777 was cleared for takeoff on Runway 22R. Flight conditions were VFR; there was about a 9-knot tailwind on final. Up to the point of the incident; approach was normal. As I started to bring the airplane into a flare; we experienced a pretty significant upset. To the best of my recollection; we first experienced a significant yawing to the left; followed by a wing drop. I cannot say with any certainty whether the left or right wing dropped. I applied multiple aileron corrections to recover and the touchdown was normal. As we rolled out; it became very obvious that the upset was caused by the B777 applying takeoff thrust as we crossed 22R behind that aircraft. As we rolled to a taxi speed; the Captain advised the Tower of the incident. Also; one of the aircraft awaiting departure on 22R made a comment about what they observed; making us believe that the upset was quite significant to be noticed by another aircrew. Upon arrival at the gate; both Captain and I made a post flight aircraft walk around. We did not observe any type of damage or scratches on either wings; nor the engines nor the tail.
column123: On short final to Runway 29 at EWR. At approximately 100 ft noticed a 777 was cleared to Takeoff from 22R at Y. I noticed we had a crosswind of about 8-9 kts from the left. While in our flare; approximately behind the 777 we experienced a large jet blast of 50 to 60 kts from left. Aircraft got displaced to right. Due to large wind addition; left wing briskly rose due to added lift. Once jet blast hit vertical stabilizer aircraft yawed strongly to the left as well. The aircraft also ballooned up about 50 ft due to all the added wind/lift. I considered a go around but reasoned that any asymmetric thrust application to go-around thrust from a slow spooling engine in an older -800 would prove disastrous in such a low energy state and may possibly have resulted in a cartwheel as we had minimal controllability. This was evidenced to me by the large control wheel applications by the First Officer resulting in only minor flight path modifications. To be frank; if I were to apply full go-around thrust and one engine spooled right up and the other lagged; I did not feel that I would have adequate aileron and/or rudder to control the airplane from it's present state. The First Officer did a masterful job (15 years in present seat) of adding just enough power and slowly bringing aircraft back to centerline. We had a firm but safe landing and stopped with about 1000 ft of runway left. Again; the First Officer showed his experience and did a masterful job recovering. I never even for a second doubted the outcome.

column121: I was performing touch and goes at the local airport ZZZ; I was in the landing phase of flight and flaring. A gust of wind came up as my left main hit the runway and bounced. This made the aircraft swerve right and into the grass. The aircraft was not in control when it suddenly turned right. I applied brakes and the aircraft came to a stop and then I turned left back onto the runway. I made my way to the hanger area and inspected the aircraft from front to back and top to bottom. No damage just some grass blades. Prop was not struck and was clean and free of any indication of prop strike.Looking back after a couple of days leads me to believe that I became too slow at touch down which caused the bounce and temporary loss of control. My perception of my speed was incorrect; it was too slow. My personal minimums were considered prior to flight as I always do; and they were in check and I was mentally and physically ready to fly.I am currently scheduled for some recurrent cross wind training with an instructor as of date.

column121: I took a handoff on Aircraft X; east of ZZZ1 landing ZZZ at approximately XA45Z.  As he got closer to ZZZ I verified that he had the weather and NOTAMs and what approach he wanted.  He requested the RNAV Runway XX from ZZZZZ; the right base T-fix.  I gave him a pilots discretion descent to 7;000 feet and pointed him out to ZZZ2 approach; who was still open at the time.  They accepted the point out; and I issued a crossing restriction of 6;600 feet at ZZZZZ and approach clearance once the aircraft passed the ZZZ2 Runway XY departure corridor.  I switched the aircraft to CTAF once I ensured he was ZZZZZ1 inbound.  A few minutes later (XB13Z approximately) the aircraft cancelled IFR while still airborne passing 3;000 feet (approximately). I stated 'Roger; IFR cancellation received; squawk VFR; freq. change approved'.I then moved on to do a couple other things.  Approximately XB20Z I received a call that an aircraft was 'missed approach at ZZZ; I couldn't get the lights on'.  It took me a few moments to realize who was missed as I had removed strips on Aircraft X when they cancelled IFR.  I asked 'calling say again'; he repeated his prior statement at which point I grabbed the strip I had just marked to get the call sign.  I issued Aircraft X a code; and radar identified him.  It seemed to me that the pilot still thought he was IFR because he was flying the missed approach procedure; but I still had to climb him to my Minimum IFR Altitude of 6;500 feet. Again; he didn't seem to grasp this fact but eventually he did so.  During this he asked me if there was a way to get the lights on at ZZZ; I quickly reviewed NOTAMS to see if they were U/S (they weren't) and also reviewed the Chart Supplement to make sure there weren't any 'tricks' associated with getting the lights on (different frequency than CTAF; lights only on for part of night; for example).  There were none; they should have come on by clicking the mic on CTAF as expected.  I told the pilot this; and he asked me if I had any way to turn the lights on; I responded in the negative.He then stated that he would try the approach one more time and if unsuccessful go to ZZZ2; 23 miles west of ZZZ. At this point; ZZZ2 ATCT had closed for the evening at XB00Z and I had assumed control of the airspace.  ZZZ2 had a NOTAM for a runway XX/XY closure with 30 min PPR (Prior Permission Required) from XB00Z-XC30Z; and I had a strip in the bay stating as such.  Runway XZ/XA was open and available.  He then stated he had about 20 gallons of gas on board.  I issued 'maintain 6;500 until ZZZZZ; cleared RNAV-Z Runway XA approach ZZZ'.  When the aircraft was procedure turn inbound; I switched him to CTAF.  My 'spidey sense' started to kick in at this point; so I went and grabbed the emergency airport binder and the portable phone in case I needed to call ZZZ2 airport ops if Aircraft X diverted - this happens regularly on the mid shift and ZZZ2 ops personnel are very responsive to any request.Around XA35z; Aircraft X reported airborne and requested clearance to ZZZ2 as he could not get the lights on.  I radar identified him; and told him to execute the published missed approach; climb and maintain 6;500 feet.  He was at approximately 3;000 feet.  His voice seemed stressed and said he wanted to go direct ZZZ2.  I told him unable as my Minimum Vectoring Altitude is 6;500 feet; when you level off I will have direct ZZZ2 for you.  He kept tracking westbound towards ZZZ2 in a slow climb.  I told Aircraft X that direct ZZZ2 is not a good idea; you are 1;000 feet below the terrain in the area; fly the published missed; maintain 6;500.I am familiar with the area; the aircraft was tracking directly at a  mountain 4;393 feet tall.  It is not depicted on our scopes; but ZZZ direct ZZZ2 takes one dangerously close to it; especially on a dark moonless night. I told the aircraft again I couldn't give direct ZZZ2 until he got to 6;500 for terrain; and he said 'well I'm gonna [request priority handling]'and continued tracking straight at the mountain.  Again; I told him he was well below the mountaintop.  At this point since [priority handling was requested]; I called the Supervisor over from the watch desk.  Eventually Aircraft X did climb; got to 6;500 feet and over the terrain - for the moment. I asked Aircraft X for fuel; souls on board and intentions. Again; he stated he wanted vectors for an approach to ZZZ2.  I advised him Runway XX/XY was closed; but I am working on getting it open; and that any instrument approach with vectors would result in him going out to an 18 mile final due to our vectoring requirements.  Meanwhile; the Supervisor showed up; I quickly explained what was going on and asked him to call ZZZ2 ops and open up Runway XX/XY for the inbound.  While the Supervisor was doing this; I asked him if he needed any information for ZZZ2.  He told me to standby.Meanwhile; Aircraft X had started to descend again and was getting below the Minimum IFR Altitude (MIA). The area in question is on the boundary of a 6;500; 6;000; and 4;100 MIA area.  I succeeded in getting him the ZZZ2 weather; but other than that the pilot was in his own world; doing whatever he wanted to do below the MIA. I asked the pilot 'is there any information I can get you for ZZZ2 so you don't have to look it up?' I was fully prepared to give him elevation; runway; lighting; CTAF; etc data so he didn't have to be heads down looking at his [tablet] for the info.  He stated firmly 'NO'.  He then passed into a 4;100 foot MIA area and I issued a vector for a right base entry for Runway XY.  I also issued the position of the airport.At this point there wasn't much else I could do as the pilot was charting his own course; literally.  I advised the MIA was 4;100 feet and he just kept descending.  The Supervisor was successful in getting a hold of ZZZ2 ops and opening Runway XX/XY.  I advised Aircraft X that Runway XX/XY was open and again gave the position of ZZZ2.  He could not see the airport. Aircraft X in his haste to get to ZZZ2 did not realize the tower was closed and the lights were not on.  The Supervisor was still on the phone with ZZZ2 ops; they saw the aircraft circling; and activated the lighting for him.  It appeared that the aircraft circled ZZZ2 and landed Runway XX.  He cancelled IFR on the ground on my frequency. I don't know what could have been done to prevent this. The pilot painted himself into a corner; and it almost led to his demise.I did a bit of research; and the aircraft had originally departed ZZZ3 destination ZZZ4.  Somewhere he changed his destination to ZZZ.  Looking at his ICAO flight plan info; ZZZ was his alternate for his flight plan to ZZZ4.  Per the FARs he should have had enough fuel to fly ZZZ3-ZZZ4; then to ZZZ and for 45 minutes thereafter.  Being a pilot myself; this seems a bit of a stretch for [that make/model aircraft].  There were plenty of viable other airports along his route to ZZZ if fuel was going to be a factor.  I don't know why the pilot didn't/couldn't want to climb to get over the mountain. If one is concerned about engine stoppage due to fuel; altitude is your best friend. I think the pilot was task saturated in a mountainous; dark environment and was used to being provided approach control services in flatter terrain.  He just picked out a path and did whatever he wanted to do; without listening to ATC; below the MIA.  If one chooses to operate in the area ZZZ is located in it deserves more planning and caution than a typical flight.The pilot also declined my offer to provide him with ZZZ2 airport information.  Had he stopped for a minute to listen; he may have gained some useful information rather than thinking 'hey; I got this'.
column123: I was Pilot In Command of Aircraft X in on Date and Date1 and I had to [request priority handling] due to the possibility of fuel shortage at approximately XA:00 AM; Date1. My account below describes the circumstances that triggered my decision to [request priority handling]; the steps that resulted in the safe; uneventful landing of Aircraft X; and my post-flight review of trip and fuel planning. I filed an IFR flight plan from ZZZ3 to ZZZ4. Given forecasted IFR conditions at ZZZ4; I filed ZZZ as an alternate which had excellent VFR weather forecasted; an LPV approach; and is a field with which I'm familiar and experienced.I monitored weather at ZZZ4 real-time during the flight via the onboard G1000 and ADSB on my iPad Foreflight app. I noted conditions at ZZZ4 were deteriorating and consistently coming in worse than they were forecasted. As I approached I coordinated with ATC to divert to our alternate ZZZ. I was given direct to ZZZ and the flight continued to proceed unremarkably until approaching ZZZ. Upon diverting; I noted fuel quantities onboard were well in excess of requirements for ZZZ and IFR reserves. Getting closer to ZZZ; I made my requested approach (the RNAV XX an LPV approach); picked up weather at ZZZ (excellent VFR conditions and calm winds); and noted that the G1000 predicted 40 gallons of fuel upon landing at ZZZ (in excess of 2 hours of fuel). Everything was feeling good and looking good at this point.I shot the RNAV XX and pilot-controlled lighting did not activate. More specifically; a PAPI light was on; but no runway lighting came on. Having previously flown into ZZZ at night; I had a reasonable expectation of what the lighting would look like; as well as I had an expectation of bright lights that leave the pilot no uncertainty about the runway location and comfort in landing. I retried the pilot-controlled lighting a few times as I was descending with no success; and ultimately decided the PAPI light alone was insufficient; did a go around; and flew the published miss. Given climb out of ZZZ and the published hold for RNAV XX; I had a modest amount of time to replan and coordinate with ATC; which I took advantage of. I first ruled out that pilot error as a factor in activating the pilot-controlled lights; asking ATC to confirm I was using the correct frequency; that pilot-controlled lighting wasn't down via NOTAM; etc. ATC confirmed as much. I should also mention that I had a phone call with the ZZZ Fixed Base Operator Manager earlier that day. The runways at ZZZ have been under construction and only recently opened; so as an extra precaution I wanted to confirm with them that pilot-controlled lighting was operational; which they did.I then expressed to ATC my plan was to retry the RNAV XX and to go directly to ZZZ2 in the event I could not activate the lighting at ZZZ and land successfully. ATC expressed vigorous agreement. I noted with ATC that according to the fuel gauges I had about 1 hour--while lower than I expected; it is well known that the fuel quantity gauges in this type aircraft can be unreliable (especially when fuel sloshes around in descents and climbs); were likely under-reporting fuel onboard; and in any event 1 hour of fuel was still sufficient to shoot the RNAV XX and to make an approach at ZZZ2 if needed. The second approach into ZZZ resulted again in pilot-controlled operated lighting not activating. I climbed out and began heading directly to ZZZ2 on a heading of roughly 280. I noted that fuel quantity gauges were continuing to read lower than expected (at this point I had at least one tank showing a reading 'in the red range'). For the same reasons as noted above; I had good reason to believe that the fuel gauges were reading lower than their actual levels; but given the risks involved with low fuel or fuel starvation; I decided to treat these low readings at face value and act accordingly; which I did.I reestablished contact with ATC and immediately [requested priority]. ATC asked me to fly the published miss at ZZZ; but noting [my request] and my right to deviate; I elected to continue direct to ZZZ2. I loaded the RNAV XY (vectors) and intended to use its guidance displayed visually on my G1000 to aid in getting well aligned for a straight in landing on Runway XY; an 8;000 plus foot strip that was only a slight right turn from my present heading.  A negotiation ensued with ATC about climb altitude (he wanted me higher; I knew from experience that we were clear of terrain; the minimum altitudes for waypoints on RNAV XY suggested I was already high enough; as well as I had synthetic vision and TAWS (Terrain Alert Warning System) onboard to ensure safety; direction of flight (I would have liked wider to get better established on XY; ATC wanted me on a heading tighter to the airport; presumably to account for his concerns about altitude); and selection of runway (XY was my preference; but that choice was briefly in doubt until ATC coordinated on the ground with ZZZ2). Complicating matters further; I expected that with clear VFR the Class C ZZZ2 airfield would be 'lit up like a Christmas tree' but it turned out that the tower was closed and pilot-controlled lighting was in effect. ATC was able to coordinate with ZZZ2 staff to turn on the lights; however; at the time lights were switched on I was very high about 2;500 feet AGL and right on top of the runway. I attribute being too high and too close to the factors named above--kept too high by ATC and vectors too tight to the runway from ATC--but in any event that was where we found ourselves.At that moment; I made a split-second calculation that I could throw out all available drag (gear; flaps; and speed brakes) and make a steep descent for [Runway] XY and given the length of the runway I'd have time to level off; slow down; and land successfully. This proceeded successfully until I was leveling off over Runway XY. I noted that my airspeed was too high (began leveling off at about 150-160 knots) and I had already eaten up about a quarter of the runway. For context; I like airspeeds in the 80s as I'm coming over the fence; so 150-160 knots is way too fast. I made another split-second decision that while it was probable that I would be able to land the plane in those conditions it was unlikely I could do so without running off the runway; damaging the aircraft and property if not worse. I peeked at the fuel gauges and noted I was showing enough fuel to make a quick traffic pattern for Runway XA and that the conditions for a safe landing on Runway XA were ideal (I could be assured of no other traffic in the pattern; the weather was severe VFR; the runway was now lit up like a Christmas tree; and I was perfectly established for an easy left traffic pattern). Landing on XA proceeded with great ease and uneventfully.While we were taxiing off the runway and to the Fixed Base Operator (FBO); I noticed as expected that the purportedly low fuel readings were returning to higher ranges more in line with my expectations. I noted fuel quantities in excess of 10 gallons in both the left and right tank when I shut the aircraft down; which is in excess of one hour of fuel. After getting a few hours of sleep; I returned promptly to ZZZ2 the next morning. I turned on the G1000 to get a fuel reading. It showed 11 gallons in the left tank and 9 gallons in the right tank; well in excess of an hour of fuel. I had the FBO top off the aircraft's main tanks (60 gallons capacity each for 120 gallons total; I did not have them top off the two 10 gallon extended range tanks). The top off resulted in 94.3 gallons; meaning that the aircrafts tanks held 25.7 gallons (120 gallons - 94.3 gallons) when it landed at ZZZ2. All plausible readings of actual fuel onboard; in other words; vigorously confirm that the aircraft landed with well over one hour of fuel onboard (exceeding regulatory required minimums) and point to inaccuracies in the fuel quantity measurement system as the likely root cause of the apparent fuel [situation] we experienced.Furthermore; I reviewed the flight planning and fuel expectations for the flight. We took off with approximately 140 gallons of fuel from ZZZ3. With conservative assumptions (normal cruise speeds; no extra helpful pushes from big tail winds; etc.); it takes a little under 90 gallons of fuel to get from ZZZ3 to ZZZ4. It takes another 30 gallons to fly all the way from ZZZ4 to our alternate ZZZ. That implies a full 20 gallons of fuel (in excess of an hour's worth of fuel). And; of course; under real-world conditions; outcomes are likely to be better. For instance; if the pilot begins to anticipate (s)he may need to divert (s)he can begin leaning the aircraft to economy settings. I've seen going to an economy setting take my normal cruise fuel burn down from about 22 gallons of fuel per hour to as low as 14-15 gallons per hour. Or; as I did; diverting to the alternate simply by paying attention to the conditions on the ground at ZZZ4 while I was enroute no doubt helped me bank some fuel that might otherwise have been misapplied.Altogether; I think the lessons from this challenging but ultimately happy and successful outcome include: Discussions with my aircraft mechanic. I understand that the aircraft's fuel quantity measurement system while well understood to be inaccurate have a few after-market upgrades that can improve reliability. Continued or perhaps additional rigor in flight and fuel planning. For instance; I keep a log on each flight of any meaningful distance of the fuel onboard. Every time I switch from one tank to the other (which I do every half hour); I note the new fuel levels and whether those new levels are matching my expectations. For instance; if I'm burning 20 gallons per hour; I look to see that the most recently used tank has burned about 10 gallons in the previous half hour. While this is a reasonable process; I think I can explore ways to harden it further. For one; I can assign another person onboard on the flight to duplicate this process with me to reduce the possibility of human error. I'm also interested to see if there are products on the market that provide additional telemetry on fuel readings; fuel estimation; etc. with which to triangulate against the readings I get from the current system. Finally; in the very unlikely event I find myself in a similar position where I have a robust quantity of fuel onboard but know that if the fuel readings 'act up' it could make the situation look like an emergency; I'll make sure to over-communicate with ATC and earlier in the process to get several steps ahead of any unlikely issues.

column121: Had a near miss with a drone while on final into MSP.  On the Date about XA30 we were on final to runway 12L at MSP and I noticed something fly past the left side of the aircraft.  I did not see it clearing and asked if anyone else on the flight deck saw that.  We had a airline pilot in the jump seat and he stated that he saw it and it was definitely a drone.  We were on the visual for 12L at MSP at about 2;800 feet just inside WASHY (the final approach fix); just left (north) of the final approach course.  The drone was fairly large and yellow.  I did not see the exact configuration as it passed by so quickly.  The drone went passed us on the left side of the aircraft from nose to tail.  I did complete a post flight walk around to verify we did not hit it and found no indication of any kind of impact on the aircraft.Drone operating at altitude and in airspace not authorized.

column121: We were cleared for the visual approach 33 and the FO (First Officer) was hand flying. Initiated a descent to 2;700 ft. and a base leg turn. We got an obstacle caution aural on the base leg of the visual approach Runway 33 into SYR at 2;700 ft. Upon getting the obstacle alert; ATC told us MSA was 3;700 ft. and I initiated a climbing right turn to gain obstacle clearance; while keeping the obstacle in sight the entire time. I misread the approach plate for the RNAV33 SYR and thought that we were still clear of obstacles at 2;700 ft. coming from the west outside of the FAF. Be more thorough on briefing an approach; even if the conditions are for a visual approach to an airport you have been to before.
column123: Landing Runway 33 in Syracuse. FO (First Officer) was hand flying in visual conditions. We got cleared for the visual 33 and started a descent down to 2;700 ft. and turned toward the final approach course. As we made our base turn we received an obstacle GPWS caution. We did a climbing right turn and had the obstacle in sight. Should have stayed at the MSA until closer to the final approach course; also referencing the approach plate better.

column121: This event was in my opinion a near miss. We were at 3;000 feet on a visual approach to runway XXR. Saw on TCAS a target 100 feet below us off left side of Aircraft. ATC said traffic alert turn right 150. Just then we got TCAS RA alert descend; descend now immediately followed by climb climb now. Followed SOP and complied with alert we never visually acquired traffic. After talking with ATC supervisor; he informed me it was Aircraft Y transitioning Class B to ZZZ2.  He was at 2;500 feet initially; then for some reason as he approached us he pulled up to 2;800 feet (our TCAS showed him at 2;900 feet; 100 feet below us) once he saw us he descended back down to 2;200 feet and turned west. I do not know if this classifies as a near miss; but it was way too close for my comfort.  Aircraft Y never entered Class B; so now [no] rules were broken; but I think the other pilot used questionable judgement being so close to arriving ZZZ traffic and climbing to within 100 to 200 feet of Class B airspace.There must be another area to transition to ZZZ1 from the west that doesn't involve flying so close to ZZZ arriving aircraft.  Maybe a 2;000 feet ceiling under the approach corridor for ZZZ?

column121: Midway through the flight from ZZZ to ZZZ1 we received 'HYDR #1 PUMP 3' CAS (Crew Alert System) message. The QRH states that system operating times will take longer than expected and that no other action was required. We continued the flight after notifying home base of the situation. Quantity showed 100% on the synoptic page with a pressure stabilized at 3;000 psi.On the arrival to ZZZ1 we were given a level off altitude and 'HYDR #1 PUMP 1' CAS message appeared. Soon after pressure fluctuated between 0 and 3;000 psi. causing CAS messages to appear then extinguish when pressure was high. Due to sequencing into ZZZ1 we had extended vectors for the ILS XX circle to land runway YY. During this time The PIC and I decided we would configure early so I could get a feel of the aircraft and allow extra time for gear extension; flap extension; ect. After being established on a long final for runway XX circle YY; I asked for gear down. We received 3 red down arrows but heard what I would consider normal gear down. We then overflew the airfield so tower could view if the gear was down then executed the missed approach for the ILS XX.The tower reported the gear was down but our indication was three red arrows. After being handed off to ZZZ2 we received delay vectors to trouble shoot the gear issue. At this time hydraulic quantity on system 1 was zero. Because we had a red gear indication; PNF followed the checklist that included pulling the gear T-handle. We still had three red gear indications so we followed the checklist for the gravity free fall. Because the checklist required 190 knots for gravity gear drop I commanded the aircraft to clean wing. This set off additional 'inboard slat fail' CAS message and we added the landing distance penalty required by the checklist.The PNF then followed the checklist for the gravity gear deploy checklist. He pulled the left main gear pull-handle and I applied full left rudder per the checklist; resulting with a left main gear down green indication. As I returned the aircraft to rudder neutral the nose gear indicated down and locked. The PNF then pulled the right main gear pull handle and after applying a slight right rudder the right main gear appeared down and locked. The PNF finished the checklist and we had a three green gear indication.We determined because of the hydraulic/gear issues it was in our best interest to declare an emergency and return to ZZZ and have crews standing by in the event the gear would collapse. Airport conditions were VFR and favored runway XX. We landed the airplane as a non-event and were towed to the FBO due to the loss of nose wheel steering.

column121: On date around XA:25 local time I was entering the traffic pattern at ZZZ airport. I had been making radio calls announcing my intentions to over fly the airport at 6;500 feet and enter left traffic for runway XX. After crossing the airport midfield I announced entering a left downwind pattern for runway XX descending to 6;000 feet while looking for traffic in the area. After passing the numbers I started my approach into ZZZ and about 1.25 away I announced turning left base runway XX while scanning for traffic. Then turning base; I looked for traffic and announced that I was on a 1 mile final for runway XX.    About 20 seconds after landing on runway XX I saw Aircraft Y over fly the runway that I just landed on. I cleared the runway and taxied to the ramp area to investigate what happened. During this whole time I didn't hear any announcements from any other traffic in the area. After getting to the ramp I went back through everything to investigate what happened. During which time I noticed that the aircraft wasn't showing up on my ADS-B (Foreflight) until it turned downwind after going around. During the whole time in the pattern I never saw them show up on foreflight. Then I noticed that I was transmitting on 123.7 and not the CTAF frequency 123.0.

column121: I was doing take offs and landings at ZZZ using runway XX. I announced departing runway XX and remaining in the pattern. As I was turning downwind a Cessna 182; Aircraft Y announced he was taking off on runway YY. I looked at the runway and he was really departing on runway XX; not YY. I didn't correct him because I was the only one in the pattern and I thought he just miss-spoke. I had him in sight the whole time. After I did several landings I heard Aircraft Y report 9 miles southwest of the airport and he said he was going to land on runway YY. I told him that XX was the runway being used; and he replied that he would enter a downwind for XX.I did another landing and as I reported turning downwind Aircraft Y reported that he would enter the downwind for XX on the 45. I was wondering how he was going to do that if he was southwest of the airport. As I was on the downwind approaching abeam the numbers of XX; I saw Aircraft Y pass in front of me about 100 feet above and 200 feet horizontally. I watched him pass in front of me and then he reported turning downwind for runway XX. I told him he just flew a crosswind for Runway YY and cut across the downwind for XX. I turned base and final for XX and asked Aircraft Y for his location. He reported crosswind for XX; which I was able to confirm. He was no factor for my landing at that point.The guy in Aircraft Y; obviously doesn't know what runway he is using and needs additional training. How does a 182 driver not know which end of the runway is in use? Guys like that are dangerous.

column121: Given RNAV XX approach. Changed to visual approach when near final approach fix. Changed to advisory frequency and called entering downwind leg for Runway XY as wind was now favoring XY. Glider called entering downwind for Runway XX. After doing so; he passed opposite direction to me at same altitude about 100 feet laterally.  He then proceeded to land on XX. I continued downwind; base and final for XY and landed while watching for the glider landing on the intersecting runway. I passed the intersection of XY and XX just as he touched down on XX. Due to the stress of the near midair; I did not close my IFR flight plan until after I had parked the aircraft. This delayed a departing business jet on an IFR flight plan. Having fixed wing and gliders using intersecting runways seems dangerous with the amount of jet traffic into ZZZ.  I should have closed my IFR flight plan immediately after landing. I do not usually close my IFR flight plan in the air as problems can arise (like a midair!) in the traffic pattern. The glider did not show up on my ADS-B traffic alert nor was it mentioned by ATC. I suspect it had no transponder so it would not show up on TCAS either.

column121: While on a descent into ZZZ1 field I inadvertently dipped into ZZZ Class D airspace. Although I am acutely aware of the area airspace and very familiar with ZZZ; a moment of distraction due to very smokey/hazy conditions continued my descent into ZZZ Class D airspace. Upon realizing my mistake I immediately climbed up and clear of their D airspace. My best estimate is 30 seconds inside their airspace.The flight involved 2 passengers whom I invited for a flight and lunch at ZZZ2. It is clear to me that the hazy and smokey conditions contributed to my inattention. However; a much more egregious event occurred upon landing in ZZZ2 and more than anything preoccupied my thoughts. Upon arriving in the pattern at ZZZ2; there were 4 aircraft operating in a left hand pattern using Runway XX. I landed following an aircraft doing touch and goes and pulled off at the intersecting runway. I came face to face with a landing C180 who decided to use the cross runway and very narrowly avoided a collision. In the process of avoiding the landing aircraft I scraped my wingtip on a runway light. In 40 years of flying I have never dented or put a scratch in any airplane and although the damage to my wingtip is minor the event clearly preoccupied my thoughts on the way home and most likely contributed to my inattention to ZZZ's Class D airspace. In most cases when I operate around or especially under the veil our Class B airspace I set my altitude hold thus allowing me to pay more attention to traffic and other tasks. The smokey/hazy condition required hand flying to avoid pockets and areas of lesser visibility. Waiting for a better day would have been a better option.

column121: I was on an IFR flight plan from ZZZ to ZZZ1. I began to experience engine surging. I attempted to troubleshoot; but it continued to worsen. I informed Approach that I needed to divert to ZZZ2. That controller asked if I was declaring an emergency; and I said no; but he took my souls on board and fuel remaining. Shortly thereafter he switched me to another frequency. I checked in with that controller and continued to ZZZ2. The condition continued to worsen; so I attempted to contact Approach to let them know I was in an emergency and needed priority into ZZZ2. I attempted to contact Approach multiple times with no response. It sounded as though he was busy on other frequencies. In hindsight I should've squawked 7700; but didn't think of it at the time. I was primarily concentrating on flying the airplane and managing the engine.At this point I was inside ZZZ2's surface area and had reached the last altitude I had been cleared to. I needed to continue my descent to land; so after another failed attempt to contact Approach; I proceeded with my descent and switched to ZZZ2 tower. They were expecting me; and they contacted Approach and informed me I was cleared for the Visual Approach. I landed uneventfully otherwise. Police and fire were waiting for me.I was uncomfortable continuing the descent without being cleared and switching frequencies without being told; but felt both actions were necessary for the safe outcome of my flight. I should have officially declared my emergency when the first controller asked; and squawked 7700. It seems to me the second approach controller forgot about me; or perhaps he was busy making space for me; but either way the lack of communication did add a bit of stress to the situation.

column121: ILS XXR with circling to Runway XY with tailwind of 10 knots. Briefed the approach for the ILS with the special ops note for the circling approach to Runway XY. Fly the ILS and turn left at 2 miles from Runway XXR threshold to parallel Rwy XY with flap 3 and initially 160 knots till we have the threshold XY at about 45 degrees of shoulder before turning in and going flaps full when somewhat aligned with the landing Runway. We discussed the wind issue. It didn't work out as planned. ATC late speed reduction and clearance; Captain's confusion of 'cleared for the approach' with the 2;500 foot altitude constraints and the resulting delay in descending and speed reduction despite gear down before the FAF kept us higher and faster then ideal. Autopilot was deselected once below the cloud layer while descending to 1;000 feet MSL. I did hand fly the turn to 110 degree before reversing to align myself with the landing Runway. Just before turning in we got the altitude alert. Crew communications could have been better while the Captain wanted full flaps before the turn to align myself with Runway XY. This could have been avoided by a more thorough briefing to the to refresh the procedure. Final Approach to XY was continued safely to an uneventful landing.ATC Issued Low Altitude Warning

column121: A training flight was conducted to ZZZ. We entered on a 45 to left downwind for runway XX at ZZZ. Heard a call that there was an aircraft 6 mile final just before we entered the downwind. We kept it in mind; but in the process of trying to configure to land; we forgot to look out for him. Upon turning base; we saw him on final directly 12 o clock ahead of us about 150ft above us and about 200ft lateral. We immediately turned right and exited the pattern to come back in for a 45 to a left downwind again. We heard a call that the aircraft on final is going around and offset to the right to rejoin the downwind again. We called and said we were rejoining the pattern again. We ended up rejoining and following traffic on base and came in for a full stop.We did not have traffic alert or traffic displayed because we only had ADS-B out. There was definitely lack of awareness and some complacency on our part. We will be extra vigilant from now on to look out for traffic and keep separation in the pattern.

column121: Flight was deviating off the ZZZZZ arrival due to thunderstorm activity. Subsequently cleared direct ZZZZZ and told to descend to 5;000 feet. column123: On arriving into ZZZ; we were taken off the ZZZZZ4 arrival due to thunderstorms in the terminal area. Upon contact with Terminal Radar Control we were issued radar vectors. The Air Traffic Controller stated that we would rejoin the arrival at ZZZZZ. We were told to fly heading 090 and cleared to descend from 9;000 to 6;000. The clearance was read back and confirmed by both of us. Shortly thereafter the controller gave us a further descent down to 5;000. The clearance was read back and confirmed by both of us. After the clearance was read back; ATC never intervened on the altitude that we read back. ATC then told us to fly direct to ZZZZZ. Whilst we were level at ZZZZZ at 5;000; ATC stated that [the next fix] had an altitude of 6;000 ft. I stated that we were cleared by him down to 5;000. There were no traffic conflicts.

column121: The [SQL] ATIS recording is almost always very hard to understand. Many times it cuts in and out when it is recorded. This means that pilots may not be able to clearly hear critical information such as the altimeter setting or the wind. This has been reported to the tower controllers but the situation hasn't been improved. Pilots often times have to report they don't have the WX and ask the Controller to read it. This defeats the purpose of having an ATIS. The recording sounds like the person is not always speaking into the microphone.

column121: SWAP night at ZZZ (Severe Weather Avoidance Plan). I had just arrived at work for the mid and taken over the two local positions from the outgoing controller. I settled in and started working the traffic and Aircraft X was one of the first few aircraft I cleared to land after taking over the position. It was VFR conditions after weather that had impacted the airport earlier had moved well out of the area. Everything seemed routine except it was busier than usual due to the weather delays as traffic was landing and departing on parallel runways. Ground Control was open and working the outbound departures to runway XXL. At ZZZ; traffic must cross runway XYR in order to access runway XXL because the runways are staggered. Ground control was taxiing Aircraft Y on taxiway 1 and I learned afterward that the aircraft was instructed to hold short of runway XYR. As Aircraft X was approaching runway XYR (our arrival runway) I noticed the taxi light on Aircraft Y was still illuminated and the aircraft seemed to still be in motion. I quickly scanned the ASDE-X (Airport Surface Detection Equipment) and as I looked up; Aircraft Y's data tag started to flash. I issued a go around clearance to Aircraft X as soon as I saw that Aircraft Y was not going to hold short. The ASDE-X did issue a go-around alert; but I had already been issuing the go-around clearance. I climbed Aircraft X to 3;000 feet on runway heading. As Aircraft X was starting into a stable climb; I issued a turn to a 300 heading away from the traffic departing runway XXL that had been cleared on a 245 heading prior to the go around and was already on departure roll. Aircraft X was then switched back to approach control for resequencing and landed about 10 minutes later without incident. Aircraft Y was then issued a runway crossing clearance by Ground Control and instructed to contact the Supervisor in the TRACON once the aircraft was parked on a taxiway.No recommendations. In a telephone interview with the flight crew by the Supervisor; it was learned that the crew was confused by the lights (ZZZ has wigwags at all the hold short lines and it is unmistakable where to stop for the runway) and failed to stop at the hold short line on taxiway 1.

column121: Aircraft X arrival to ZZZ1 and Aircraft Y ZZZ departure were in direct conflict.  An IFR release was received from ZZZ2 approach for Aircraft Y and after Aircraft Y was airborne Aircraft X's data tag was in view on the edge on my radar screen.  I advised ZZZ tower on the situation immediately then passed traffic to Aircraft Y. As the collision advisory appeared a traffic alert was issued where the pilot immediately got the traffic in sight and asked what direction to go.  Aircraft was on an IFR departure climb below the MVA (Minimum Vectoring Altitude) so I advised the pilot left or right turn at pilot's discretion. Aircraft Y turned to avoid.I don't know the current visual aids used by approach for release availability to each facility but I'd advise that their visual aids should be made more effective. A radar software update to where ZZZ1's instrument final turns red if someone has been cleared for the approach would give ZZZ [Tower] better situational awareness and be a home run in terms of ensuring this doesn't happen again.  ZZZ controllers standard radar setting does not allow for view of ZZZ1 instrument arrivals much farther out then 10 mile final but we can always see the final and a change in its color would be noticed.

column121: During approach into ZZZ while receiving vectors was cleared to 9;500 feet MSL and told cleared for the ILS XX and to maintain 9;500 feet until established. Selected APP and aircraft captured GS and LOC and was descending on the app with GS and LOC locked on. Got a call from approach at 8;100 feet letting us know we were low and to climb up to 8;600 feet I immediately disconnected the auto pilot and initiated a climb while maintaining LOC. After crossing ZZZZZ I then recommenced my descent and flew visually using the PAPI as my guidance. Landed the aircraft within the touch down range and stopped on the runway. CA taxied aircraft to gate. During taxi we were informed by approach that the GS had issues and they thought it had been fixed. We initially intercepted the GS and LOC approximately 20 miles out. At approximately 2 miles out the GS was back in line with the PAPI.
column123: We were doing the ILS Z Runway XX into ZZZ. Night time; good visibility; no clouds. ATC had us at 9;500 feet; turned us to intercept final outside of ZZZZZ and cleared us for the approach. Autopilot was on. We were flaps 1 about 190 knots at the time and about 22 miles on final. We intercepted the localizer and the glide slope at about the same time. At which point we were busy setting touch down zone; putting flaps down and gear. A moment later ATC gives us an altitude low warning; climb immediately to 8;600 feet. The flying pilot ( first officer) immediately disconnected auto pilot and initiated climb. We were around 8;100 feet; on the glide slope; auto pilot on when this was initiated from what we could tell. We had just passed ZZZZZ (FAF; but 17 miles out). The controller mentioned they had some glide slope problems just a week or two prior; and asked us if we were ok to proceed visually. Still being over 10 miles out; runway and PAPI in site; and glide slope still showing slightly below us; we proceeded visually with reference to PAPI and GS. The problem may have been from the auto pilot intercepting the LOC and GS at the same time and possibly overshooting the GS as it sometimes does when far out and possibly exasperated by configuration changes. This all happened about 15 to 20 miles from touchdown. Or it could have been from a faulty GS signal that they have had problems with previously.
column125: Flight crew reported descending below minimum altitude on approach and climbed back to correct altitude. ATC advised flight crew that there had previously been problems with the glide slope.

column121: During the approach to landing at ZZZ on runway XX; the TIS traffic warning on the GPS came on. I was at 500 ft and descending to the runway.  I believed it to be an 'echo' of my transponder; which sometimes happens; so I reset it and continued with the approach and landed (The conflicting traffic was showing +200 ft).  An observer on the ground came into the terminal office and asked to see the pilot of the flight that had just landed.  I introduced myself and he asked me if I had seen the helicopter over the airport. I explained that I could not see them as they were in the cloud layer and could not hear them; of course. I had made three position reports during my approach to the airport; but none were made by the military helicopter; which is not unusual.  It is my estimation that the crew of the helicopter turned on their transponder to let me know of their presence as they saw me on their traffic awareness system.  While it is not a common practice for the military to have their transponders on during operations; it would be invaluable for them to do so while on training missions that would get them close to civilian aircraft and airports.

column121: Aircraft X was 12 miles Northwest of ZZZ on a base heading 220 at 3;000 feet being vectored to the ILS Runway XXR at ZZZ airport. I observed an unidentified VFR target depart ZZZ1 airport climbing out of 1;700 feet southbound. At first the the target was flying away from Aircraft X but then I observed the target start a a turn to the east. I then assigned Aircraft X heading 250 anticipating the VFR target would make a turn to the north east to avoid the immediate incursion into the ZZZ Class C airspace. This caused Aircraft X to go through the final for Runway XXR and I climbed Aircraft X to 3;100 feet because the new projected flight path would be into a higher Minimum Vectoring Altitude. I then anticipated the VFR target would turn east bound once it got north of the Class C. So I took Aircraft Y off the arrival who was 10 miles north west of the airport on the downwind. I stopped his descent at 5;000 feet when I would normally descend aircraft at this phase of flight to 3;000 feet. It then became apparent the altitude adjustment wasn't going to be enough so I turned Aircraft Y to a 220 heading. The VFR target did make a turn to the east once north of the Class C climbing opposite direction into our arrival corridor at ZZZ. The VFR target did eventually contact another controller for an IFR pickup in my airspace.This event only had a positive outcome because this happens so frequently I have become overly cautious and I guessed correctly what the non participating aircraft was going to do every step of the way. I could have easily guessed wrong and could have had a dangerous situation develop into a crash. I recommend an airspace change that would extend controlled airspace over ZZZ1.

column121: I set up for the visual to Runway XX backed up with the ILS into ZZZ. The approach met stable approach criteria and landed at approximately 85 knots. Upon landing with all three gear down; the left gear began shaking. I assumed I had a flat tire as we started to drift left slightly. I applied brakes and right rudder; neither of which rectified the situation. The aircraft then continued to roll off to the left of the runway. We hit the grass and the gear caught and we spun around 180 degrees. I notified ATC and shut down the engines. I then called company to inform them that I was off the runway and needed the FBO to bring their passenger van. I asked the passengers if they were okay and explained I thought we had a flat tire. A passenger then informed me my left tire was on the runway about 500 feet further down. I helped the passengers get off the aircraft and told them to stay behind the wing. They all said they were uninjured and were brought to the Gate XX where our agents met them. I stayed with the aircraft and got in contact with Maintenance. When I returned to the terminal I wrote up the aircraft for the tire coming off and the aircraft departing paved surface. I was asked to go back and take more photos of the tire. FBO mechanics came awhile later and were able to get a spare tire on. They said half of the rim was missing so operations and fire went back to the landing spot where they found part of the rim and a handful of pieces of the landing gear all over the runway. The aircraft was towed to the hangar and I left the pieces with the log book with operations in ZZZ.

column121: This event was a near midair collision in ZZZZ. On Aircraft X ZZZ to ZZZZ. Weather at ZZZZ was not a factor even though there was a significant storm cell over the final approach fix to Runway XX. ZZZZ was reporting 10 miles visibility. While making approach into ZZZZ we were told to go around due to previous aircraft reporting a pothole with gravel on approach end to Runway XX. Tower immediately closed the runway. We then went around and did published missed procedure. We entered holding at 8;000 feet at ZZZZZ per instructions. After we exited holding (now at 6;000 feet) the controller gave us instructions to go direct to ZZZZZ1 fix and begin RNP approach into Runway YY. We loaded the approach to the opposite Runway YY since the pothole was on the approach end of the Runway XX and proceeded to ZZZZZ1 on about a 200 heading. Then unexpectedly; the controller gave us a vector of 090. In hind sight; we think this vector was meant for another aircraft. The new heading of 090 ended up sending us directly for another aircraft at same altitude. TCAS began with a TA then an RA. At same time controller frantically gave us a new vector. After properly executing the resolution advisory; we narrowly missed the conflict aircraft and proceeded back to ZZZZZ1 and did the RNP to Runway YY for normal landing. We never saw the conflict aircraft however they did have us in sight; and I don't recall who the conflict aircraft was. There were two jets in holding. We think the controller got our call sign confused with someone else for the 090 vector. English as a second language may have been a factor in this event. Both the F/O (First Officer) and myself remember hearing our call sign in the 090 vector. In the end it was TCAS that saved the day.

column121: While being vectored on a base leg to final; approach requests us to call the field in sight. It was a night approach with good weather and we had the field at 10 miles but I initially declined to call the field because of our angle and unfamiliarity with the area. After the second (or third?) request from ATC we accepted the visual at around 6 mile dog leg. They had directed us to keep speed up so now I was gauging the energy state while working toward a straight in final. As we descended I scanned the radar altimeter and noticed it fluctuating between 1;300-900 feet. We were configured at flaps 25 and I verbalized this to my FO (First Officer) as I temporarily leveled our descent and checked our distance from the runway. DME was NOTAMed out of service so I put [the] runway on fix page during brief and it showed around 4-5 miles. Bar altimeter was 2;300 with 800 ft elevation so we determined that hilly terrain was causing the spike. By this time radar altimeter normalized above 1000 ft and we had selected flaps 30 so we continued approach to landing.Approach brief covered obstacles but I did not take into account hilly terrain in the area. Also; our desire to facilitate ATC requests to keep speed up and accept visual approach.At unfamiliar airports during night operations it is advisable to let ATC vector to the initial or final approach fix.

column121: As we were being vectored to get established on final; we received an IDG FAULT Caution message; I decided to run the QRH; while we were in a high [workload situation]; lots of communication with ATC as we were being vectored; descended; and slowed; lots of radio chatter caused a breakdown in our communication within the flight deck and that resulted in turning the wrong IDG off; which caused a momentary loss of all AC and subsequent deployment of the RAT; at this point with emergency power only I decided to [request priority handling]; accept the longer runway; and continue to an uneventful landing; after landing we continued to the gate; as normal power had been restored.The cause of this event can be contributed to the fact that we were rushed in completing the QRH in a very busy environment; while being vectored and slowed and while starting to configure for landing.Based on what I experienced; I would not run the QRH that close to the runway for a caution message; in a high [workload situation]; rather I would wait and deal with it on the ground.

column121: I was doing touch and go's at ZZZ and Runway XX was in use. After my first touch and go; I remained in the pattern; and I was on left downwind for Runway XX when I heard another aircraft with the '[FBO]' call sign calling on a 3 mile straight in for Runway XX. I decided to extend my downwind to let him pass; and I said it on the frequency that I was still on the downwind for Runway XX and would extend my downwind for the traffic on the straight in Runway XX. Aircraft Y said that he was going to the left downwind for Runway XY. I was quite confused but continued to Runway XX as it was the best runway given the wind; and everyone else was using [Runway] XX. When I turn final; I asked 'Is there anybody on final for [Runway] XY?' And I also said that I was on final for [Runway] XX; the opposite runway. Aircraft Y answered me but he was unclear. After he finished talking I said once again that I was on short final for Runway XX. I touched down; and while I was braking; I finally spot Aircraft Y; right in front of me; on short final for Runway XY. I expected him to go around as I was still on the runway but he did not; so I expedited my turn to vacate the runway. Shortly after I crossed the hold short line; he touched down on the opposite runway I just landed on. Also; in the pattern was company flight. He was leaving the area during the event but heard the situation over the radio.After the investigation it was clear the other pilot violated right of way rule and landed in reduced safety standards.

column121: On Date about XA45 PM local time; during roll out after landing on Runway XX in ZZZ in Aircraft X; the plane veered to the right and exited the runway surface and came to rest in the soft mud just to the right of the runway. We departed ZZZ1 enroute to ZZZ; at XB25 local time on a part 135 passenger charter flight with passengers; their luggage; and groceries for ZZZ; and after a nice 2 hour and 20-minute VFR flight we arrived at ZZZ. We made a pass overhead of Runway XY to check the windsock; and then flew out about 3 miles and made a teardrop back to final to Runway XX. When we did our wind check I observed the windsock to be showing about 5 kts. or less of wind out of about 070 degrees. When we were on final to Runway XX; I again looked at the windsock and observed it to be showing about 10-15 kts. of wind at about a 30-degree right crosswind to the runway. After a normal approach and touchdown; during rollout while the tail was still in the air; I could feel the rudder starting to become less effective and the wind wanting to push the tail to the left; so as quickly as I could I got the tail wheel planted firmly on the ground to help with directional control. As the plane was slowing down through an estimated 35-40 kts. the plane started to veer to the right; I immediately applied full left rudder and pumped the left brake to make sure that we were getting maximum effectiveness out of the left brake. After rolling about another 100 ft.; I realized that it was not likely that we were going to be able to get the plane straight again; so then I applied maximum right brake also and was just trying to get the plane stopped before it went off the side of the runway. When we were about 100-150 ft. from going off the runway the Copilot also applied maximum braking; but nothing we did was able to keep the plane on the runway; we went off the right side of the runway about 1;000-1;200 ft. from the end. The plane stopped quickly in the soft mud that was just off of the runway. We then did a quick assessment of the passengers and after none of them reported any injuries we evacuated the passengers and then secured the aircraft. There were no known pre-accident mechanical issues with the aircraft. Of the passengers and 2 crew members on board; there were no reported injuries. After the incident was over as I was back on the ramp; I was able to observe the windsock for a while. I noticed that the wind would be anywhere between 5 and 15-20 kts. and the direction would shift between approximately 060 and 150 degrees. At the time we landed I would estimate that the clouds were about 2;000-2;500 ft. broken-overcast and greater than 10 miles visibility. The ceilings and visibility did deteriorate throughout the day.

column121: Started our flight in ZZZ2 to ZZZ1; ZZZ; ZZZ2 for our commercial cross country requirement. Had flight planned around XA:00am with the student for our departure at XH:30 pm. Flight up to ZZZ1 was uneventful; burned we burned 21 gallons going up; leaving us with 29 gallons to get to ZZZ. The flight planned showed we required 19 gallons to land at ZZZ where we planned to refuel before returning to ZZZ2. This left us with 10 gallons reserve; meaning over 1 hour of flight time at cruise. Roughly 50 miles out from ZZZ we had engine roughness and realized we had pulled the tank dry; so we swapped tanks again with no issue. We knew we had a reserve left and headed directly for ZZZ. Upon entering the pattern and turning base (2 miles) the engine sputtered again and we knew we had run out of gas. Knowing we would not make the runway we notified ATC and landed uneventfully on highway. Fire responded with no injuries or damage. ZZZ ground crew towed the airplane to the airport we had an A&P check everything over; added fuel; did a ground run; everything checked out good and we continued the flight to ZZZ2. Coincidentally when we fueled at ZZZ knowing the airplane has a 50 gallon capacity and the POH says all 50 gallons usable; from dead empty the plane only took 48.2 gallons. In conclusion; during the flight back from ZZZ1 to ZZZ we knew the head wind was more than we had factored for so we recalculated midair and that is where our mathematical error came into play. A simple addition mistake caused us to land just short of the runway that night.

I would estimate the drone had passed underneath the right wing at less than 100 feet separation but far enough away to feel reasonably confident that it had not struck the airplane. The co-pilot agreed and then alerted Newark Tower of the presence of the drone on final approach so that they could pass this information on to the succeeding aircraft on approach. They requested that after landing we provide them with any additional description we could; which we did with Ground Control after clearing the runway and beginning our initial taxi to the gate.

column121: A student that I signed off to solo ran off the runway during pattern work; took out a PAPI light and had a prop strike. The weather conditions were fantastic with calm winds and unlimited visibility. The student had completed 7 previous touch and go's with no issues but on the 8th lost directional control after touchdown and ran off the runway. The student in question had 51 hours of total time and approximately 3 hours of solo time before the occurrence. The lack of ADM (Aeronautical Decision Making) and physical motor skills to keep the plane on the runway led him to have an excursion. Corrective action is to make sure that he is not challenged with directional control and talk about decision making if something just doesn't seem right.
