column121: Running practice approach for Runway XX at ZZZ. Hand flying; drifted high on glide path at about 2 miles out. Pitched to return to glide path and failed to pull throttle enough and airspeed increased. I didn't notice it until I crossed the threshold and was still doing 118kts. Normal approach with 10% flaps is 100. There was also a gusty crosswind and I was focused on maintaining alignment and centerline. Ended up landing almost 2;000 feet down the 5;000 feet runway. Continuing to maintain centerline; I saw the end of the runway coming up quickly and braked hard; speed then was 49 kts. Crossed the end of the runway into the grass at approximately 20 kts; continued to slow; executed a hard left turn and rejoined taxiway. Subsequent inspection showed no taxi light damage and no damage to gear. I need to maintain a closer watch on airspeed even when dealing with gusty crosswind; don't get fixated.

column121: Student and instructor were on a night cross-country. Traffic pattern was entered and to verify wind direction we did not descend on downwind but continued on an upwind leg to ensure we would be ready for the landing. We turned crosswind and downwind and did our before landing procedure. Student was slow to configure the aircraft which extended the downwind leg further than desired and upon turning base we noted we were low on the VASI. The student made a correction to get back on/above the glide path which was too little. I as the instructor was too late and too slow to inspire a more aggressive correction until the top of the tree came into view. I took control of the airplane and landed uneventfully and taxied to parking. There was no indication of flight control malfunction nor any indication of a loss of engine RPM. Subsequent inspection revealed slight damage to the right main landing gear fairing and damage to the left horizontal stabilizer fairing. I suspect that the propeller may have been impacted but there was no sudden stoppage of the prop. Of note; when the runway lights were activated by the CTAF; the lights on the north side of the runway from midfield to the departure end did not illuminate. What did come on was all of the lights on the south side of the runway and half the lights on the north side from the approach end to midfield. Also; I'm aware that the VASI glide slope angle is set higher than a three degree glide slope because of obstructions (trees) and the angle used to be published in the Chart Supplement; but that information is no longer available.

column119: Aircraft; Airport; Procedure; Environment - Non Weather Related; Human Factors
column121: Received vector for initially an ILS to Runway 8R.  Intercepted LOC just outside JELLI at 5;000 feet.  Upon interception; glideslope captured aircraft autopilot began a 'substantial' descent (1;500 feet/min).  At first I didn't think anything about it. Shortly after; we were cleared a visual approach.  We were fully configured before MATON; and passing MATON I felt low on the approach; it was too shallow and looked wrong; and then I noticed our altitude was 1;200 feet MSL (1;100 feet AGL) when it should have been about 2;000 feet MSL.  Flight Director still showed us on glide path.  I immediately called out we were 800 feet low for some reason.  Suspecting an instrument issue; I disconnected the autopilot; turned off the flight director; and began hand flying.  We had been cleared a visual shortly after LOC capture and were visual; so I completed the final segment flying purely visually.  I even took the precaution of having the First Officer deselect the ILS in the nav radio tuners.  At approximately 1 mile from the runway threshold; I noticed 2 aircraft holding short on the north side of the approach end of 8R waiting clearance to cross.  A B737 was holding short on Taxiway NE; and a E-170 was holding short at NR.  It appeared those aircraft were causing interference of the glideslope signal resulting in the indications we were receiving on our instruments.  Our manual recommends backing up a visual approach with the ILS; when available.  However; good airmanship and situational awareness must always be maintained. An additional item: ATC gave us no call about our low altitude on the approach.  I couldn't find it above anywhere on this form; but I could also consider this an incident which was successfully recognized and resolved in time with no incident. I have seen aircraft waiting to cross untold number of times; but never have I seen glideslope interference such as I saw during this approach.
column125: Pilot reported they were low on glide path during approach and thought they might be having an equipment problem.  Pilot observed two aircraft close to the runway which may have interfered with the ILS glide slope signal.

column121: I was First Officer (FO) on Aircraft X operating to ZZZ. We were conducting the ILS XXR circle to XY approach when we received an abnormal landing gear indication. When I lowered the landing gear; the annunciator panel showed 3 red lights along with 3 green lights. The overhead panel showed 3 green as well. We briefly discussed continuing the landing as there was some confidence the gear was down. The discussion continued until around 500 ft. and I felt strongly that we shouldn't be making such consequential decisions that close to the ground without running a checklist. We performed a go-around; ran the checklist; got the gear down without the red lights; and landed successfully.
column123: ILS XXR circle XY at ZZZ. Lowered gear just prior to FAF (Final Approach Fix). 3 red; 3 green illuminated. After evaluating with First Officer (FO); executed go-around on XY due to gear disagree. Standard missed. Ran checklists. Vectored for longest runway... ILS XXR. Extended gear on 10-mile base. All indications normal. Executed normal landing. Taxi to gate to find puddle [of] hydraulic fluid at left main. Possibly unrelated.

column121: On visual approach to Runway 01L Tower Controller called an 'altitude alert'. We were cleared for the visual to Runway 01L to Tampa. The glideslope to the ILS 01L was NOTAM'd Out of Service.  Tower instructed us to maintain 2;600 feet until north of Macdill AFB. I had briefed that this would cause us to be high and that I would configure the aircraft for landing at 2;600 feet and prior to Macdill.  I had also briefed that we would need a high rate of descent to get us on glide path.  Once north of Macdill; and runway in sight; I disconnected the autopilot; and started to hand fly the visual approach. At approximately 1;000 feet the First Officer stated we were approaching the (visual 3:1) glidepath.  At that time; I had started to reduce the rate of descent.  At about the same time the Tower called an altitude alert for Aircraft X.  I started to pick up the PAPI and saw we were below glidepath.  I continued reducing descent rate to intercept the PAPI glidepath.  Aircraft was on glidepath and stable at 500 feet. Landed normally. [Suggest] Brief the visual in more detail on 3:1 glidepath and required rate of descent to intercept from above.

column121: I received a phone call from a student saying that he had gone flying solo (which I was not informed on until after the flight. I tell all my students to text me before going to fly and once they are on the ground) and when he came in for a landing with a light crosswind coming from the right; he recognized he was slightly left of the centerline so he attempted to correct. After touchdown; due to the right crosswind; my student had left rudder in due to the sideslip situation for the crosswind; and the plane then pulled to the left. The plane then veered to the left and my student overcorrected with the right rudder resulting in him ending up in the grass. While the aircraft was moving in the grass off the right of the runway; my student attempts to stop in the grass and reports 'the aircraft pulled hard to the left causing me to cross back over the runway and coming to a rest on the left side of the runway'.This report is all [the] words from my student; as I was not at the airport due to me not knowing that the solo was happening. My student has submitted his own NASA report as well.
column123: I'm student pilot and I was doing a student solo. I started my flight with a take off on Runway XX and did some practice ground reference maneuvers south of ZZZ. After working ground reference maneuvers at about XA:50; I headed back to the airport to land for the day. I was on final for Runway XY and had a right crosswind; and was correcting with left rudder and right aileron. When about 10-15 feet off the runway; I was slightly left of center line and as soon as I touched down; my wheel was turned left because of the rudder so my plane was jerked slightly to the left; I attempted to use right rudder to straighten the aircraft and lost control and went off the right side of the runway. I then attempted to slow down my aircraft in the grass off the runway; but the aircraft pulled hard left causing me to cross the runway; coming to a rest on the left side of the runway. During the time that I was off the runway my engine had shut down. I then turned the keys off and pulled mixture all the way out and exited out of the aircraft.

column121: Myself and an Instructor flight planned to do a commercial long cross country going ZZZ1-ZZZ2-ZZZ-ZZZ3. The flight plan read that we would have used up less than half our fuel (50 useable per POH) going to ZZZ2. We departed ZZZ1 having a ground speed much higher than expected of 125-131 using up less fuel than expected. Other than deviating around clouds and cells it was an uneventful flight. I have done this flight before and last time made the smart decision of fueling up in ZZZ2 to go back to ZZZ1. When we landed this time in ZZZ2 during day light; It was shown that we had used about 21 gallons. Leaving 29 gallons remaining. Then once our preflight was done in ZZZ2 we jumped in the plane and started it up. At this point it was night time. The plan was still to go to ZZZ. So we departed and start to head in that direction.  We found ourselves deviating around storms and making sure we are of course not running into airspace. We found that we had a greater headwind than expected and our ground speed ending up being around 90. When we were en route to ZZZ the engine began sputtering south of ZZZ4. I thought we were having an engine failure because throughout the flight I had constantly been checking the fuel gauges to show we had fuel. I turn the fuel pump on and switch the tanks from left to right. Once I was complete the engine ran normally. It was after this that I said to the Instructor that I wanted to get on the ground as soon as possible. He reassured me that we had plenty of fuel based on the aircraft fuel gauges and the secondary JPI. The left fuel gauge read a quarter of a tank of fuel when we ran out. We were still going toward ZZZ and continued. We contacted ZZZ Tower and they instructed us to enter left base and report a 3 mile final for Runway XX. We enter the base to final and report a 3 mile final. Followed by the Tower's instruction that we were number two and cleared to land on Runway XX. On about a 1 mile final we had the engine sputter again. I turn the fuel pump on and switched tanks from right to left hoping there was a possibility we have a little bit more fuel in there to keep the propeller going. Unfortunately; there was no more fuel even though the gauges read there was and the JPI indicated we had 8.8 gallons remaining when the engine died. The Instructor [advised] Tower and we then landing on [the] highway.

column121: I was the Pilot Flying. The weather in ZZZ was deteriorating quickly. Thunderstorms; heavy rain and wind were moving towards the airport. On downwind we could see the airport through the spotty rain showers and were number 3 for the RNAV XX approach. The previous two aircraft reported light turbulence; heavy rain; negative windshear; and they broke out right at minimums. The weather radar depicted a large cell just to the south of the airport moving in quickly and one at the very end of the runway moving towards the airport. Lightning was also depicted on the weather radar. We broke out right at minimums with heavy rain on short final however the runway was visible and I continued with the remainder of the approach visually. At about 300 ft. I encountered a wind gust that caused the aircraft to go above the VASI glidepath slightly. I recovered by lowering the nose of the aircraft and also being cognizant of the increase descent rate that would occur. Even though there was heavy rain; the runway was still visible. With the heavy rain and wipers on max rate; it was difficult to hear anything else. My First Officer informed me that he heard an alert through the aircraft speakers that sounded like 'Don't Sink' but it could have been 'Sink Rate.' He wasn't sure. I glanced at the VSI and it was indicating about a 1;200 fpm descent rate. Speed was about 10 kts. Fast. I arrested our descent rate and got back on the VASI glidepath. I removed my headset from my ear to hear any further aural warnings. At about 100 ft. with a VSI of 850 FPM I also heard 'Don't Sink' in conjunction with the 100 ft. aural call. The descent rate had been stabilized and it was evident that a safe landing could be made within the Touchdown Zone. We were both quite certain there was no question that a safe landing could be made and also; if we initiated a go-around we would have gone directly into the thunderstorm at the end of the runway. I decided that the safest course of action at that given time was to land. I elected to land the aircraft rather than follow the Company requirement of initiating a go-around when a 'Don't Sink' Alert was received. The landing was uneventful. We touched down in the Landing Zone as briefed. Deceleration was smooth and as we were taxiing towards the gate the ensuing thunderstorms enveloped the field but the wait for our gate was not that long. We arrived at the gate a bit behind schedule. Company policy is quite clear on what is expected and required when encountering this; or any other EGPWS Alerts. I think our situation was a little precarious due to the weather; heavy rain; wipers and also a lack of direct communication as to what was heard. A prevention in this situation would be to not be in this situation. Hold; wait out the storm. Divert as necessary. Company aircraft send Aural Warnings through the speakers. There are some aircraft where the speaker volume is not that loud. Better planning and briefing would also be a factor in preventing this from happening.
column123: I was Pilot Monitoring during RNAV XX Approach to ZZZ. Last weather report from tower was 10 miles visibility and winds 150 degrees at 23 kts. gusting to 28 kts. with no reported wind shear however it was evident visually as well as on radar that a thunderstorm was rapidly approaching the field from the south.  Because of this we were vectored north of the field and cleared the approach. We discussed the published missed approach would take us toward the incoming storm and how to mitigate the risk if we had to go missed approach. At the DA; the runway was in sight (despite a quick to develop rain shower between the FAF and DA) and would remain so throughout the landing; thus we continued the approach. However; on the descent from the DA; the gusty conditions resulted in the aircraft being above glideslope and in an effort to correct glidepath; the aural 'Sink Rate' sounded on short final; followed by a sink rate call from me. The PF (Pilot Flying) made appropriate corrections; maintaining a stable airspeed despite the gusty conditions; and landed in the Touchdown Zone without incident. The post flight report noted a max sink rate of 1;215 fpm. Although the sink rate aural caution sounded; I feel the safest action was to continue the landing considering the runway remained in sight from the DA to touchdown; airspeed remained stable and a go-around would result in flying the aircraft toward the fast moving thunderstorm enveloping the airfield.

column121: During a morning approach; BJC was busier than usual. It took three calls to establish two-way communication with Tower; during which a right 360 was completed for spacing. On approach; ATC cleared us straight in for a left base on Runway 12L; then with about 2 miles to go; handed us off to Tower Frequency 2 and changed us to Runway 12R. After changing; we continued the southbound approach 'behind Aircraft Y'. about a half mile from the airfield; over US 36; we saw not one but two [aircraft]; same altitude and 1/4 mile apart; heading for us west-to-east up US 36. I felt that this was loss of separation and far too close for comfort; and uncommanded (By ATC) evasive action was taken including a sharp right turn and an arrested descent; to allow the aircraft to pass under and behind us. No traffic advisory was given (possibly because the other two were on Tower 1 frequency); and it felt as though Tower had no idea where we were. After nearly missing; Tower cleared us No. 1 for Touch and Go on Runway 12R; and we completed our touch and go in front of Aircraft Y on right traffic behind us for the same runway. During the climb-out; the Controller gave us a traffic alert for a 'helicopter on your left' (he was on our right). It felt to me as though the Controller was over-saturated and had no idea where the aircraft he was in control of were located. I also believe there was a lack of communication between Controller staff in the tower during the hand offs from Tower 1 to Tower 2; as the receiving Controller seemed to have no idea where we were; what we were doing; or what we were previously cleared to do. To have the FAA breathing down ZZZ's throat for recent mid-airs (both of which were pilot error and had nothing to do with ATC); and then to be directed into a near-collision on base by BJC Tower; is unacceptable; and tells me that the FAA doesn't care until something makes the news.

column121: After we landed in INL we had a runway excursion with a GA aircraft. While on approach we heard the GA traffic make radio calls about being inbound about 3-5 miles north of the field. We notified them of our location of about a 10-mile final for Runway 32 and they said they would wait for us to land before landing themselves. They also called us; 'In sight'. After we landed we advised on the radio we were back-taxiing; and as we were turning around we saw the GA traffic landing while we were still on the runway. They stayed well clear of us and were able to exit the runway so we could back-taxi with no issues; and no evasive maneuvering was required at any point.GA traffic apparently not being aware of the risk of landing on a runway we were still on. While he had us in sight I did not have him in sight or on TCAS; and therefore did not know exactly how close behind us he was. Construction in INL requiring back-taxiing and therefore a longer landing time than usual that the GA traffic was probably used to. Try to advise GA traffic earlier if a need to back-taxi is required.
column123: We were flying into an uncontrolled airport and made two-way radio contact with another aircraft north of the field coming into land on the same runway we were. He told us that he would let us go first since we were much faster than him and that he would follow us and be #2 to land. Normal radio calls occurred after that and we landed without incident. Because of construction there are no taxiway turn offs available on the runway and a180 degree turn and back taxi is required. After coming to almost a stop and just about to turn around I made a radio call stating that we would be back taxiing on the runway. Once we just about completed our 180 degree turn we saw the other aircraft about 50 feet off the ground at the other end of the runway in his landing flare. So we pulled as far to the side of the runway as we could just in case he made it down to us. We stayed still until he landed and pulled off on the taxiway about 2;000 feet down the runway where we needed to exit. Once the other aircraft was clear we continued our back taxi to the exit point without incident.The other aircraft stated he saw us on about a one mile final and would be number 2 to land. A contributing factor could be that he never actually saw us or he just disregarded us. Being that he was in aircraft type he might have just assumed he had plenty of runway to land and turn in before us without it being an issue on his end. Poor communication on his end as well; very few radio calls almost all of them were non standard; no tail number; no position reports at all outside of being north of the field; and simply stating 'I'll follow the jet'. A pilot with little regards to aviation safety more or less.The only thing I could think of is maybe more communication. We could have tried getting back on the radio real quick and attempting to have the other aircraft go around. By the time we saw it however; he was basically on the ground

column121: Outside the marker [Runway] XXR Captain received call from purser about chemical type smell moving forward from aft cabin. Noticed it on extreme short final but took measures to alleviate; if possible; the stated smell and monitored for reduction or increase.  The landing in gusty conditions required focus there. On landing APU selected to start on final was on line and directed APU bleed air to packs and selected recirculate [fans] off immediately causing cessation of the smell and acknowledged alleviation in command.  Smell was barely noticeable but a tad acrid then removed on taxi in.  Recirculate [fans] back on blew it out completely. Never a need nor concern for use of cockpit oxygen. No persons affected nor did anyone use O2.  No injuries. Event terminated at gate with a write up and briefing of mechanics plus filling out of required maintenance 4 page review by Captain.
column123: On approach in ZZZ the Captain received a call from the lead Flight Attendant about an acrid chemical smell moving forward from the aft cabin.  By that time we could smell it in the cockpit as well.  We turned off the recirculate fans to stop the spread of the smell; which helped; but then focused on the landing.  We started the APU on short final and turned off the engine bleeds after exiting the runway; using the APU to power the packs.  After turning off the engine bleeds the smell dissipated and we turned the recirculate fans back on. No oxygen was used or needed by the crew or passengers.  We parked safely at the gate and briefed me and made a maintenance write up.

column121: About 15 miles west of ORD on arrival we were exposed to a laser beam from the left front of the aircraft.  I was Pilot Monitoring; my First Officer (FO) was flying; and I reported the location to ATC.  About 5 minutes later I experienced an intense nauseous feeling.  I thought I was going to be sick.  The intense nauseous lasted maybe 5 minutes and then I started feeling somewhat better. My FO [advised ATC]; we landed and taxied in without incident.  Medical personnel met our flight and checked my vitals; all being normal.  The upset stomach was still present but not as bad as it was in the air.  Side note...The flight was X hours and 10 minutes; about 90 minutes into the flight we ate a crew meal.  I'm not sure whether the nauseous feeling could be food related or if it was because of the laser.
column123: Experience a laser event followed by a pilot incapacitation followed by an inflight [urgency situation] on approach into ORD. Arrived at the gate and was evaluated by EMS. Had to wait an hour for crew transportation to the hotel. Arrived at the hotel after midnight. Stayed up to record the details of the flight. Called [operations] around XA:00 to say I was not rested physically or emotionally to operate the flight tonight that had a hotel pickup time of XB:00 followed by a X hour flight.

column121: I was approaching the ZZZ airport for landing on Runway XX and observing other arriving traffic. I entered the traffic pattern and started my prelanding checklist. I observed another aircraft ahead of me making a nonstandard approach. I adjusted my approach to sequence behind the other aircraft and ran the prelanding checklist a second time. On final; I was sure the gear was down and indicated by three lights; but perhaps the other aircraft's close proximity caused me to overlook the gear handle not being fully locked in the down position and the lights on. Also; the gear warning horn was not heard.To eliminate the possibility of this happening again; a go-around and better sequencing along with maintenance to double-check gear indicators will be practiced in the future.

column121: I was conducting a routine cross-country flight to ZZZ1; then back to my departure location ZZZ. The flight to ZZZ1 went as planned. We landed on Runway XX and were asked to back-taxi on the runway by ATC in order for us to depart back to ZZZ. We departed from Runway XX and headed back southwest. We climbed to 4500 MSL enroute back to ZZZ. Approximately 20 miles out I began my initial descent. As I began this descent; I also tuned into the CTAF frequency as Tower was closed at this time. Approximately 10 miles out I made my first position report. I made a secondary one at 6 miles. Upon reporting at 6 miles; I was under the impression that I was the only one in the airspace as there was no other plane making reports. Upon this; I began to set up for a 6-mile final straight into Runway XX at ZZZ. I made another position report at 4 miles and then another 2 miles. Once I reached 1 mile out; I made the announcement that I was on short final. I continued my descent until I had reached the numbers and started my round out. Upon doing this; my ADS-B announced that there was traffic on short final at 6 o'clock. This traffic had not made any position reports and gave nobody in the airspace an indication for what he was doing. Upon hearing this; I made the decision to land in order to avoid a possible midair collision with this traffic.Continuing my flare; I was dealing with a 15 kt. headwind which I had flown with through my entire approach. Upon flaring; I decreased power and the wind shifted from a headwind to a crosswind that became in excess of 17 kt. As a result of this; I side-loaded my right main landing gear. As an attempt to keep the nose gear off for as long as possible; I increased power to aid in my attempt. After doing this the crosswind sheared to a tailwind; which as result forced my nose straight into the ground. With the increase in power and the direct nose impact; this resulted in the aircraft bouncing. As a result of the bounce; I initiated go-around procedures. As a I began to gain altitude; the aircraft who was not talking to anyone came to a stop on Runway XY. As a precautionary measure I flew 10 miles out and climbed up to 2500 ft. I soon turned towards the airfield and began making position reports every mile as an attempt to make contact with this aircraft. Unable to make any sort of communication with this aircraft; I overflew the field to try and see his exact position. I observed that he was off the active runway and on a taxiway. Upon noticing this; I made the decision to go in and land. This decision was based upon the fact that I was unaware of any possible damage to the aircraft and I had no exact way of telling what this other aircraft's intentions were. Upon making my base to final turn for Runway XX; this aircraft turned off the taxiway and departed Runway XY. I made a landing on XX and was able to taxi back to the hangar.As I taxied up to my hangar; this aircraft was finally able to make contact with me as he reported radio difficulties. Upon performing a normal shutdown; I got out and examined the aircraft and the only direct observation that could be made was that the tube of the front nose gear tire had gone flat. It was only once the cowling was removed that the damage to the firewall was observed. A learning experience that I will take away from this is to be more aware of potential traffic surrounding me and in my airspace. The most important one I believe is to be more aware of the possibility of windshear on final. This better preparation and awareness I believe; would help me to respond better if a situation like this were to occur again and I was called upon to take action. Having this type of experience will benefit me as before; I did not have the chance to be forced to react in a situation like this. Having gone through this will help me to learn from my mistakes and allow me to correct them and perform an appropriate response to these less-than-ideal landing conditions in the future. I also believe that I need to learn to be more hesitant about adding power in a situation like this and learn to make more accurate or precise adjustments that might be necessary.

column121: We were seven miles behind a 747 on approach to Runway 9 in Miami; flaps 5; 170 knots on glidepath; when the aircraft abruptly went into 30 degrees left bank. We commanded full right aileron and there was no response for an unknown amount of time (possibly three seconds). We regained wings level flight only to encounter another abrupt 30 degree roll to the left with lack of aircraft control. After much discussion; my First Officer and I were worried that after such a 'minor incident' (seven miles behind a 747) that the event should cause such a severe loss of aircraft control. We both agree that the incident should be investigated.
column123: We were 15 miles out on the localizer to Runway 9 at 5000 feet. We were seven miles in trail of a B747. We encountered wake turbulence and the aircraft yawed to the left 30+ degrees for 3-5 seconds. We inputted right aileron with no results. The aircraft returned to level flight on its own and immediately went into a 30+ degree left bank turn for 3-5 seconds.
column125: B737 NG flight crew reported momentary loss of control during a wake turbulence encounter on approach to MIA in trail of a B747.

column121: Assigned ZZZZZ Visual to Runway XX at ZZZ. Briefed use of VNAV in accordance with Approach Briefing Guide. Prior to ZZZ1 VOR; instructed to descend from 4;000 to 3;000 ft. MSL and FLCH was utilized. Upon crossing ZZZ1; pilot not flying; while also saturated with LCA (Line Check Airman) duties; set 1;500 ft. for ZZZZZ1 and then touchdown zone elevation while slowing and configuring aircraft. We did not notice FLCH was still engaged until ATC provided an altitude alert at 900 ft. MSL. Pilot not flying/LCA took command of aircraft and performed a momentary level-off before giving aircraft back to original pilot flying for a stabilized approach and landing.
column123: Pilot not flying is an LCA (Line Check Airman) conducting an Initial Operating Experience flight for pilot flying; who is a transition First Officer from the other aircraft builder.Assigned to fly the ZZZZZ Visual Approach to Runway XX at ZZZ. Installed the RNAV V XX procedure in FMC and briefed RNAV/visual approach procedures. ATC cleared us direct ZZZ1 and cross ZZZ1 at 3;000 ft. LNAV was engaged to fly direct ZZZ1 and FLCH was engaged to descend to 3;000 ft. Crossing ZZZ1; pilot not flying entered 1;500 ft. in MCP altitude window for ZZZZZ1 crossing altitude. While descending on profile; aircraft was slowing to 180 kt. and configuring for landing. PM was tracking the configuration changes and verifying on course with LNAV; and then selected TDZE after passing ZZZZZ2 intersection. Pilot flying was having difficulty finding the runway which was out his right window behind [of] his shoulder line. Pilot not flying looked outside to acquire runway to direct pilot flying to look in the right direction. At that moment; ZZZ Tower Controller called 'Altitude Alert' for our flight. Pilot not flying looked inside and noticed altitude was 900 ft. crossing ZZZZZ1; below the 1;500-ft. altitude that was programmed in the FMC. Pilot not flying then also noticed that VNAV was not engaged but the aircraft was descending in FLCH; so was therefore not on the VNAV descent path. Pilot not flying took control of the aircraft; leveled the descent; and continued to follow the LNAV course to line up with Runway XX. Once aligned and in proper position to land; pilot not flying gave control of aircraft back to pilot flying; who conducted a stable visual approach to a safe landing.

column121: Landing XXR ZZZ during gusty conditions; received too low terrain while on short final. Low-level windshear reported on east side of field. Wind gust took airspeed up to 175. Neither pilot flying or pilot not flying saw airspeed go above flaps 30 speed of 175; or any movement in flap retraction due to airspeed. Original flap 30 speed was 147. Added 15 for gust. Reported stable at 500 ft. AGL with 1;000 FPM descent. Gusty conditions from above FAF to 100 ft. AGL; but under control. Largest gust after 500 ft. AGL stable call; approximately 150 ft. AGL. On short final; I thought 'too low terrain' was an erroneous warning. Visual conditions from FAF inbound. Touchdown uneventful. Wrote up maintenance discrepancy for possible overspeed due to unusual GPWS warning.

column121: We were given a late hand off by the previous controller to Salt Lake Approach control controlling the LEEHY arrival. On check in Approach Control contacted us first and it was apparent that he was task saturated and that the sector was extremely busy. He told us he needed to give us a 'box pattern for spacing' and gave us a heading westbound over Utah Lake. We complied with this heading and the instruction to descend to 14;000 ft. and reduce speed to 190 KIAS. We were then given another heading turning us to our left and back towards the approach course. It was at this time that we got a traffic advisory of an aircraft that was in the direction we were now turning towards with the latest vector from ATC. The aircraft was climbing towards us as we were descending and turning as per the ATC instructions. Almost simultaneously ATC realized the situation and gave us a traffic alert at the same time the aircraft gave us an RA to descend. The Captain immediately disconnected the autopilot and began to comply with the instruction to descend. At this time I was looking outside doing what I could to see and avoid the traffic. At this point we were in a layer of smoke and visibility was low. I was able to spot the traffic as the aircraft was passing above us. It was very close; only a few hundred feet. It is hard to judge distance without anything to base the measurement on. Just prior to me seeing the traffic the TCAS system made the annunciation to monitor vertical speed and as the aircraft was passing off our right we got clear of conflict. It took us a minute to get back with the controller and he gave us a new altitude and heading to join the final approach course into Salt Lake. We made an uneventful landing on Runway 35 in Salt Lake and once safely parked contacted Company to figure out next steps. I believe the cause of this event mostly had to do with improper instructions from ATC. I obviously did not have the big picture of the traffic environment in that sector like the controller did. However I could tell he was task saturated and had more aircraft to control than he could safely handle. We were not aware of any potential conflict until beginning the turn to the heading the controller gave us which put us in conflict with the other aircraft. In the future I will attempt to be even more vigilant at situational awareness. I believe this event has only renewed my sense of importance on this matter. Furthermore I would suggest that the controller of this sector not be controlling more than one sector as it seemed he was. I believe that the controllers task saturation was potentially the largest contributing factor in the event. I am glad that we responded so promptly to the resolution advisory and were able to resolve the conflict. The training we had at Company prepared us well for this situation and I am glad that I have practiced it many times in training.

column121: Departing Runway XX at ZZZ.  Had approaching Aircraft Y on ADS-B while they were executing a practice approach to Runway XY.  Pattern traffic was using Runway YY.  After rotation; I had a visual on the other aircraft and started an early turn to pass behind.  At that point; my glare shield mount for my GPS moving map came loose and dropped into the control stick arc dangling by the data/power cord.  I grabbed it with my right (stick) hand and began controlling the aircraft with my left hand.  I was holding the stick below the grip and unable to reach the push-to-talk trigger.  If I had turned left to runway heading; I would have gone wing up to the other aircraft and lost sight. I focused on flying the airplane first and after passing the aircraft was able to move my left hand into position on the grip to reach the push to talk trigger.  By this time the pilot in Aircraft Y was calling me and expressed; understandably; his alarm. The early turn was an effort to clear the pattern quickly for the training aircraft that use this field.  However; I believe continuing the upwind further would have minimized the conflict that is a part of practice approaches opposite to the traffic patterns in use. Opposite direction approaches are common at this airport and other uncontrolled fields in the area. Secondly; I am re-engineering the bracket for my GPS moving map to preclude a recurrence.  I will also add a 'bracket secure' check to my pre-takeoff flow.

column121: I am a pilot who hasn't flown GA in XY+ years. I hired this pilot to re-familiarize me in the GA world. Instructor is very capable and in my opinion did nothing incorrectly. We had been doing touch and go's at ZZZ1 airport; and we were returning to ZZZ to land. The runway being used was XX. The instructor was flying back and made all the required calls on the Unicom frequency. I was in the left seat; watching instructor fly and listening to instructor speak on the radio. I saw a plane turn toward us as instructor started to make base turn and I told instructor to roll out and stay downwind. Instructor lifted wing and saw the traffic coming right at us. He had taken off on Runway YY. He never made any calls. He was turning upwind when we were turning base. He finally saw us and passed behind us. He made a comment on the radio; admitting to making 'a huge mistake' and apologized for it. The runway in use was definitely XX with a 10 kt head wind. I shudder to think what would have happened if I hadn't seen him.

column121: The Heli Pilot was not talking on the radio; but had it on.  I found out later in the day he was unsure where he was.  He heard my radio calls but didn't know how to process them.  Didn't understand N1ame & Name 2 Beach or where they were.  I have T-CAS & ADS-B that are working perfectly; he didn't have transponder turned on & I'm not sure if he had ADS-B.  My system would have warned me he was there.  I was descending through 500 feet on base-leg 1/2 mile off shore to land on the Name 1 Beach to pick up clients.  I checked the 3 planes on the Name 2 beach to my left to make sure they were not departing; then focused on my landing paying close attention to the two Bears sleeping on the beach and folks taking pictures; maneuvering around and beyond them.  Then suddenly there was a Heli at my altitude 100 feet off my right wing. At the end of the day I have discussed this with Company that own the Heli & the Pilot.  I believe it to be resolved.

column121: The DFW 17C/35C Glideslope on the ILS and the VNAV on the RNAV is not reliable! Both approaches for both runways; are showing aircraft 100 to 300 feet low all the way down final including the FAF (Final Approach Fix)! Management says it's been flight checked; and it checks out fine. Company said it was something to do with certain aircraft with a FMS update. All aircraft are low on the final. I don't want to be working aircraft down those runway finals during IMC weather when an aircraft is too low to the runway.Even though Management knows it's an issue; they say it's not an issue. They say continue using the approaches; but they want us to take aircraft out further on final; so we don't lose vertical separation with the aircraft next to them. I don't understand how that's an issue if there's 'not an issue.'First; I recommend no Instrument Approaches should be conducted to these two runways ASAP! I know this will delay the airlines; but this is a safety issue! I also recommend the equipment Maintenance Tech get out to the ILS/RNAV equipment; and find out what the problem is. It has to be an equipment issue. It's unsafe and needs to be fixed ASAP!

column121: I was on Center [sector] ZZZ. Aircraft X was handed off to me from ZLA [sector] ZZZ1 on the ARLIN3 arrival; assigned a 070 heading left of course for weather. When the aircraft checked on; I called the weather [and] told them I would need to vector them north of the weather but south of the GLADDEN MOA/ATCAA (Air Traffic Control Assigned Airspace) that was active. I turned them to a 085; trying to aim them towards the 2-mile gap between the north side of the weather and the 3 miles I needed to keep from GLADDEN. The last vector; I think a 100; I needed to clear GLADDEN by 3 miles; the aircraft refused and I ended up 2 miles from GLADDEN. On their refusal; they said they were in weather and the chop was pretty bad. The pilot was pretty willing to work with me on vectors as I was trying to front-load them with information on the military airspace and the need for expedited compliance on the vectors. I think I even mentioned the two-mile gap I had to play with.Prior to all of this and taking over Center [sector] ZZZ; I took over the area as CIC. As I took the area; the FLM told me that he needed to go to breakout briefly; but was going to stop by the northwest area to have them recall GLADDEN because the weather was building north of the R2306/8 restricted areas  which were hot FL800 and below and GLADDEN and YARNELL; and we were running out of room for the arrivals. Soon after; the Area X FLM at ZLA called me to ask about the weather and said they had one SDL and one PHX arrival heading our way. We still had room at the time to wedge them north of the weather and get into the terminal area without risk of hitting the 3-mile buffer of GLADDEN; but I was worried about weather building as the R-side on the sector at the time had told me that the weather had doubled in size in about ten minutes. Aircraft up as high as FL410 were also deviating. I told the Area X Supervisor that we still had room and that we could vector nort;  plus it was too late for them to move them as they were out of room.Soon after; the STMC (Supervisor Traffic Management Coordinator) called me to ask me about the weather and I mentioned that the FLM had tried to get the northwest area to recall GLADDEN; a recently changed local procedure about recall of airspace. The STMC said that they needed GLADDEN - I think that was what he mentioned - and that they would be shutting off the IZZZO/KEENS departure because of the weather and that they would also be turning off the HYDRR arrival. He asked about the FYRBD departure because of weather over MOHAK and the J2 corridor - a single corridor that goes between two restricted areas - and said that aircraft were getting through so long as they were above FL280.After that I took over Center [sector] ZZZ; and I alerted the FLMs. The swing Supervisor had just arrived; and the previous FLM had just shown up from breakout to a IWA on the IZZZO departure. ZZZ3 sector; south departure; called the D-side Trainee on the sector to ask about them since they said the IZZZO was shut off; and had asked about a 270 heading. Swing Supervisor said put them over IZZZO since; at the time; we still had some room to go north of the weather. I had a subsequent SDL aircraft in addition to an aircraft at 110; Aircraft Y I think was the callsign; that I had to vector in the same basic spot. Bottom line; someone; or multiple someones; missed at least three aircraft at higher altitude on these routes - seemed to be the satellite airports. Additionally; there were three aircraft; one Aircraft Z; on routes below 140 that were also missed. As I was leaving the sector; there was Aircraft A on the BLH arrival going to PHX; that was attempted to be handed off to me with a DL (Departure List) in the 4th line. I tried to call ZLA ZZZ2 about them twice; they didn't answer; and then I saw the aircraft turning north; so I believe they finally turned them north go to north of all the ATCAAs. When I left the sector; the entire gap between GLADDEN and R2306/8 was fully blocked with weather and was completely impassable.First; R2306/8 was hot FL800 and below for a solar powered UAV at FLXXX; operating in various altitudes. I'm not really sure why the restricted areas needed to be blocked off at such a large altitude for one aircraft. We could have deviated to the right into the restricted areas if ZLA was more dynamic in scheduling the restricted areas; something like FLZZZBDDD.Second; TMU at both Center and ZLA have been behind the power curve this year in being dynamic and proactive in trying the move aircraft for weather. Arguably; our monsoon weather this year has been particularly brutal; and our traffic is up at our highest levels; 10% above our norms according to our ATM. However; moving aircraft in a dynamic manner due to how fast our monsoon weather develops is vital; and it has been absolutely abysmal this year.Third; recall of military airspace and the ability to change military airspace in an unorthodox manner has been something of a disappointment this year. Our Safety Representative has been trying to effect change through a process that would better coalesce procedures for recall of airspace into one unified procedure and one point of contact per area.Fourth; moving aircraft is good for weather; but TMU missed all of the satellite airports - at least that is how it appeared - and all of the Category C aircraft. When ZLA [sector] ZZZ1 called about Aircraft X; the D-side OJTI told them that the arrival was shut off; and ZLA [sector] ZZZ1 said they hadn't even been told.

column121: We were being vectored onto the ILS approach Runway 26 into PHX. There was an Airbus that was being vectored on to the parallel approach for 25L into PHX. Because we were both intercepting our respective localizers at the same lateral point from the field; we got an RA to descend. The TCAS did exactly what it was supposed to do- it doesn't know that we are intercepting a localizer; and so it sees two airplanes that are opposite direction and converging and issues the RA command. The Captain turned off the autopilot; turned away slightly from the traffic; and descended approximately 300 feet. We had the traffic in sight the entire time; and we were able to stabilize the aircraft before the '1;000 ft above the field' call out. Why does ATC keep on having aircraft on parallel approaches intercept parallel localizers at the same lateral point? If they would space it out by a mile; this wouldn't happen. And if it's happened to me twice now in the last 6 months; it must be happening to other people as well.
column123: While on the visual approach to Runway 26 we received a Descent TA.  Another aircraft was on approach to 25L and also joining final.  We had the aircraft in sight and we also complied with the TA and started a descent.  Our position was just outside PRUNN on approach to Runway 26.  We were at 3;000 [feet]; and descent was to approximately 2;700 [feet].  We then leveled off and joined the glide slope and landed on Runway 26. ATC was notified of the TA.

column121: We were coming back to ZZZ (our base) from ZZZ1. We were assigned ZZZZZ Arrival. Shortly after crossing the shoreline; Tower told us to maneuver toward a landmark to follow the traffic. After we made our turn; Tower asked us to if we have the light transport traffic in sight; my student pointed out a traffic to me and I radioed the tower that I had the traffic in sight. Tower then told us to cross behind the light transport traffic and cleared us for Runway XXL. After the traffic my student pointed out passed in front of us we angled toward the base leg. then I realized that traffic is not a light transport. Then I inspected the final for Runway XXR and saw a light transport about 250 ft. below; and about 500 ft. north east of us. I took over the airplane and maneuvered to avoid flying in front of the light transport. [The] maneuver was successful and we flew behind them. Shortly after the evasive maneuver Tower asked us again if we have the light transport traffic in sight; I told them 'We do have [light transport] in sight' We proceeded to land on Runway XXL.In retrospect; I shouldn't have told the tower we have the traffic in sight without positively identifying the light transport. A good radio transmission  would be 'we do have a traffic over X location please confirm that is our traffic.'

column121: Landed at ZZZ; Runway XXL; full flap landing. Touched down on centerline; short of the 1;000 feet marker. As plane rolled-out; plane deviated to right. Applied left rudder pedal to turn nose wheel back to center; but airplane continued to the right; in almost a skid motion. Released left rudder due to perceived unsafe condition. Checked to confirm feet not uneven braking. Began to apply even braking; but plane continued to [go] right. Released brakes; and confirmed feet off brakes. At [the] same time; confirmed throttle idle.Assessed situation for potential go-around; but speed was reduced and quickly approaching runway edge without noticeable correction to centerline. Determined go-around not feasible or safe. Once I assessed the plane was going to exit the centerline and enter the grass; I then confirmed throttle idle and attempted to once again straighten nose wheel and traversed through the grass between Taxiway XX and XY off XXL. Plane then exited grass onto Taxiway XZ and I was then able to stop safely; having been slowed by the grass. Fire services were dispatched to inspect the plane and Operations to inspect area and runway. Aircraft had no noticeable damage and no leaks. Operations did not note any parts on runway. Aircraft was able to taxi safely back to parking and nose wheel directional control was adequate.Unknown cause to incident. Maintenance was called to inspect plane. Inspection not complete. Consideration is being given to right main wheel pants catching the tire and creating a speed differential that induced the uncontrolled turn to the right that could not be corrected prior to exiting into the grass.

column121: EWR ATIS from yesterday makes reference (and has for a while now) to a crane sticking up in relation to the Holland Tunnel. Now; I've been through the tunnel on limo trips to and from EWR to our downtown hotel; but that's been fairly rare. But; after I land I relax in the ride rather than keeping a close eye on the navigation. The point being; EWR is referencing something that I have no real idea where it is. And I'm an American...what about all the foreigners who fly into EWR...does EWR really think every pilot knows where that tunnel is?

column121: We were vectored for the Visual approach 13R ZZZ.  (ILS Out of service).  We were cleared for the visual approach a few miles NE of ZZZZZ.  The aircraft was slowly descending through 2;200 ft. when we received a GPWS alert (obstacle alert) for a tower 1605 feet between us and ZZZZZ.  We immediately climbed back to 2800 feet. (Glide slope verification altitude). Climbing back to 2800 ft through ZZZZZ put us high on the approach. We configured quickly and ran checklist. Through 1000 feet we were still high on VASI but agreed must be stable by 500 in visual conditions. I realized through 800 feet we had not contacted the tower; tried to contact tower twice and realized we had the incorrect frequency; finally contacted tower passing 400 ft and was cleared to land.  Normal landing.The ILS was out of service. The pilot flying was new to the airbus (3 months) and not totally confident yet with the airplane. I let myself get a little complacent;  the weather was clear and I had flown many legs in the last 7  days. As pilot monitoring; I should have seen that we were drifting low outside ZZZZZ.  After getting high on the approach; should have requested vectors back around for a new approach to give us more time to reorganize.

column121: A lot going on that culminated into a low altitude caution on approach to Runway XXR. The RNP approach was loaded into the FMS in the normal preflight planning. I did not catch this error and thought it was the RNAV XXR that we were flying during the initial descent and approach; which is the approach we were cleared for. On the base leg prior to the ZZZZZ fix; we noticed the error and the box was not sequencing correctly. I instructed the FO to 'handfly' to make the 90 degree turn to final while I went heads down to get the correct approach in the box. The FO started a turn but also a descent and we received a caution obstacle aural warning from the aircraft. I instructed the FO to immediately correct and to climb to the correct altitude. At that time we also received a query from the control tower for a low altitude alert and we were 800-1000 feet below the minimum vectoring altitude at that point on the arrival. We corrected the altitude error and climbed to be at ZZZZZ1 (FAP) at the correct altitude.   I neglected or misread what approach was actually loaded into the FMS. The turn to  final should have been a turn only and not a descending turn. I was removed from monitoring while heads down reloading the approach so I did not monitor the hand flying properly. It was the end of a long day; and the last 45 minutes of the flight was dodging weather in and around ZZZ. This all happened very quick and was a bit of a wake up call to me how quickly it went from normal to a situation that had the aircraft in a low altitude situation. In hindsight; I should have just asked ATC for a vector to buy time and get reestablished on the approach instead of trying to save the approach and fix it while asking the FO to hand fly. The safest course of action would have been to get a vector.
column123: On the late downwind leg of the approach; of the second leg for the day; after multiple deviations due to weather; the CA & I realized the approach loaded in the box differed to that which was briefed. That is; RNAV GPS instead of RNAV RNP . Although this was recognized; changed & executed just after the IAF; the aircraft did not respond accordingly; and as PF I initiated hand flying to correct the base to final turn; of which we momentarily overshot. Whilst hand flying & commencing a 30 degree turn in the descent at the IF; insufficient back pressure was held & the aircraft lost approximately 700-800 ft before establishing on final. This was immediately recognized and corrective action was taken to reestablish the correct altitude; whilst an aural 'Obstacle' warning was heard & moments later; an ATC advisory was received. The approach was stabilized before 1000 ft; we broke out around 900 ft & landed safely. This event was initially due to a combination of complacency & distraction... where we were dodging multiple cells en route; and failed to amend the previously entered approach in the box (*as loaded prior to departure). We held off amending the box; until we were in range to receive the latest ATIS; and at that time; became busy avoiding weather; and then once closer to the field; there was multiple traffic & very busy ATC communications. To ensure this is not repeated; I will also be thoroughly going over the box during the approach brief; and verify my intentions regarding when I shall be arming the approach & what I then expect to see on the FMS.

column121: While conducting a power-off 180 precision landing on Runway XX; an aircraft that had initially indicated they were entering the pattern for the opposite Runway XY crossed in front of my aircraft as I was beginning my base to final turn. I did not hear this aircraft specify they were on final straight-in; nor see them as I was clearing final prior to my turn. My co-pilot also did not hear or see them.This aircraft had earlier stated they were going to enter on the downwind left traffic for Runway XY; the opposite direction of Runway XX; which was in use by myself and two other aircraft. Estimated distance is easily at or less than 500 ft. horizontal separation when they crossed in-front. I aborted the maneuver and elected to remain in slow flight configuration to gain separation. The aircraft landed 2/3 down Runway XX and took back off again; departing to the south of the field.

column121: On visual approach into ZZZ Runway XX during day VMC with unrestricted visibility; we received a GPWS caution at 2;300 ft. MSL while flying to ZZZZZ to follow GPS guidance. We did not see any obstacles which posed an immediate threat but we climbed to 2600 ft. to stop the GPWS. There are numerous towers in the area along with rising terrain that could have triggered the GPWS. Once assured clear of obstacles; on course/glideslope and configured; we continued the approach with no further incident. Upon debriefing; the crew agreed the safest course of action would be to join the course farther out from a safe altitude. Other factors that may have contributed were the good weather contributing to confidence/overconfidence in terrain clearance.

column121: During the afternoon; my student and I were practicing touch-and-go landings at ZZZ. While we were on the departure leg of the third touch-and-go; I heard Aircraft Y make his first call that he was about to enter the pattern. My student made her crosswind turn and made a radio call; self-announcing her position in the crosswind over CTAF. Upon making the downwind turn; we noticed that Aircraft Y was also quickly approaching the 45-to-downwind turn via the ADS-B In function on our MFD.Visibility was hindered due to smoke in the air as well as the sun being in the same direction as the oncoming traffic. I looked to my right side to spot traffic and noticed him approaching our aircraft. I took control of the aircraft and made a bank to the left to avoid the traffic coming from the right. I estimate that Aircraft Y passed behind our aircraft at a distance of 300-400 ft. It appeared that Aircraft Y was descending into the traffic pattern at a higher-than-normal speed for the aircraft. We estimated his ground speed on the 45 entry to be 120-140 kt. After he passed behind us; we re-established on the downwind and executed another touch-and-go before departing the pattern.

column121: We were approaching the airport from the west on an IFR flight plan. We called the airport in sight roughly 13nm from the field and Approach Control handed us off to Tower. Our company aircraft was ahead of us and already talking to Tower. They were told to enter a left base for Runway 2. We then checked in and gave our position and informed Tower we were on a visual approach for Runway 2. We were told to enter a left base for [Runway] 2. We then saw that our company was just ahead of us by maybe 2 miles and I turned right (to the south) and pulled the power; lowered the landing gear and configured to slow to our final approach speed to ensure separation between us and our company aircraft. Shortly after we were cleared to land on Runway 2. On about a 3-4 mile final and fully configured; we heard tower tell a small aircraft to enter left base; Runway 2. To our surprise; there was a small aircraft just ahead of us and to our left on the base leg. I told the PNF (Pilot not Flying) with me that the small aircraft was very close and was going to hit us. I had to push the nose down and turn to the right while the PNF simultaneously told the Tower Controller about that other airplane. At the same time; the small aircraft began turning final; right into our path. Within a matter of seconds the Tower controller told the small aircraft to go around and he started climbing above us. Since we were faster and below him; I turned back onto the extended centerline and stabilized the approach. This happened on about a 2 mile final. The PNF and I were both in shock that this had happened; especially since the tower was supposedly going to alleviate close calls in the pattern. Upon landing; I called the Tower to speak with a supervisor to discuss what had happened. The supervisor had gone home for the day but the person who answered the phone was the controller who was working tower position at the time of the incident. I told him that we were upset that another plane had come so close to us. I told him that in my XX years of flying; I had never come so close to another airplane in the air and that it shook me up. He said that the small aircraft had reported both us and the company small transport in sight; but I don't remember hearing the small aircraft call us in sight until he pointed us out and told us to go around.I think a major factor in this incident was the fact that the new tower had just begun operations that morning. I also think the controller; not being familiar with the local terrain and area contributed as well. Lastly and what I think is a major factor and was a total surprise to me; was the fact that the tower does not have a radar feed from BNA. In other words; it's a matter of the controllers asking for position reports and trusting that airplanes are where they say they are. From a safety standpoint; this should be added very soon.

column121: This occurrence happened after maneuvers. We were practicing normal takeoffs and landings at ZZZ which had a crosswind component of 10 kt. After a first attempt and go-around; we decided to try again. On the second approach in; we proceeded to come in slow; 50 kt.; and I prompted my student to add in power. However; he was frozen and too fixated to notice the low airspeed. I should have promptly issued a go-around and should have taken the controls. However; I was not forceful enough and was not able to take the controls from my student to go around; resulting in a rough landing and a tail strike. We ended up going around and did not feel there was any difference in controls and continued back to ZZZ1.Later after pushing the plane back; we noticed the damaged tail piece and the bent tail strike protection. This could have all been avoided with just a simple go-around. At the slightest feeling of something not going right - for example; not being lined up or being too slow on final - a go-around will ultimately save a bad landing from happening. The student and the instructor should always be communicating and be aware of the situation that is happening. A positive exchange of controls should be practiced frequently so that the exchange can be flawless and can prevent situations where a student may be fixated or task saturated. The reaction to go around was not quick enough and resulted in the tail strike. Task saturation is a real thing; especially if a student pilot is being introduced to a new airport and also uncontrolled airport operations as well. To wrap things up; going around is your best option and positive exchange of controls should be practiced not only when braking; but also while in the air; even in critical phases of flight.

column121: I was training on LCL. There was a line of thunderstorms to east with moderate to extreme precipitation. Runways XXR/XXC/XXL in use. Aircraft X was on an ILS Runway XXR approaching ZZZZZ the final approach fix when he checked on the frequency. I issued traffic to Aircraft X at his two o'clock and 2nm; same altitude moving south toward him type unknown suggest you descend. I then called ZZZ sector and asked him if he was talking to the unidentified aircraft in the Class C and he said negative. I went back to Aircraft X and updated him on the traffic at his one o'clock and 1nm southbound type unknown and Aircraft X reported him in sight. Aircraft X went underneath the unidentified aircraft by about 400 ft. and landed without incident. I asked Aircraft X on arrival what type aircraft it was and he stated some sort of Cessna. Traffic [should be] issued sooner to Aircraft X via the approach sector.

column121: I was the First Officer and pilot monitoring on our flight from ZZZ1 to ZZZ. This was the last leg of a three-day trip. We taxied out on time and parked to wait out a ground stop. ZZZ was just starting to get the worst of a tropical storm. Convective SIGMETs for embedded thunderstorms covered much of the mid-Atlantic states. The ground stop lifted and we were able to depart 2 hours and 2 minutes after pushback.The first part of the flight went fine; and we were clear of weather until contacting Approach Control. The controller gave us good vectors; but we still had to penetrate some moderate rain showers. Our aircraft was an older 321 with CRT screens and the old radar display; and it was night by this point. This made it difficult to know the intensity until we were very close to the depicted weather. We were able to keep clear of magenta turbulence indications on the display; yet we still encountered moderate to severe turbulence with speed indications +/- 20 kt. below 8;000 ft. Upon turning base; we were in a clear area and could see one more cell crossing the final approach course. Traffic ahead that had just flown through the same cell reported moderate rain and smooth ride. Our ride was worse than that; and after configuring we got an aural predictive windshear warning; 'GO AROUND; WINDSHEAR AHEAD.' We initiated a go-around. The ride was moderate and there were big airspeed fluctuations; but we didn't get a reactive windshear alert. Approach vectored us back for another ILS. The downwind took us around more moderate intensity rain and turbulence. Approach informed us that traffic behind us had landed; and that we were the only one to go around so far. I believe two aircraft landed between our first approach and our second attempt.Our second approach also resulted in a go-around. When we encountered the heaviest part of the rain; airspeed loss was unacceptable and we initiated the go-around. We did not get a predictive windshear alert. I don't recall if we had a reactive windshear warning; but almost instantly we had multiple ECAM warnings and cautions. The autopilot disconnected and Captain assumed manual control - he was pilot-flying throughout the event. The ride felt very similar to how the simulator presents a windshear event. Recovery felt like it took forever. With no autopilot and multiple F/CTRL ECAM messages; we were fully caught up in recovering to a normal state. We climbed up to 3000; which was [the] missed approach altitude. Flight director guidance was fluctuating a lot; and we turned off FD 1 and 2.Re-configuring the aircraft after the approach was delayed; the airspeed trend arrow indicated a very imminent flap over-speed condition. I asked the Captain if he wanted flaps up; and then raised them when he indicated in the affirmative. My perception here was that we were in level flight and accelerating rapidly. In retrospect; I misjudged our energy state and failed to follow the prescribed schedule for flap retraction; retracting them too quickly. As the flaps were retracting; we were somewhere around 3;100 ft. correcting for 3;000; in moderate turbulence. These factors all compounded; resulting in a low-energy state and stall warning indication. I'm not sure what the auto thrust was commanding at this point; or if it had reduced thrust for us to descend back to 3000. I believe I yelled; 'Nose down!' and Captain responded that he was full forward stick. I momentarily pushed my flight stick forward and was corrected by the aural 'dual input' alert. Captain recovered the aircraft. I gave ATC a quick description of our flight control computer problems and they climbed us to 5000 on vectors. We began the process of going through the ECAM malfunctions; with me working the ECAM. The autopilot was inoperative for the rest of the flight. F/CTRL ALTN LAW was next. There is no follow-up for this. I believe the next was F/CTRL ELAC 1 PITCH FAULT and F/CTRL ELAC 2 PITCH FAULT. I know that's the checklist I went to; but after the fact I can't say whether it was the ELAC PITCH FAULT or a generic ELAC FAULT. The pitch fault follow-up prohibits resetting an ELAC in flight; and so we did not attempt a computer reset. The ELAC FAULT reset procedure does allow a reset in flight if you do it one at a time and there are no uncommanded maneuvers happening.After follow-ups; I called Dispatch to see if we could go to ZZZ2 or ZZZ3 or ZZZ4. We had around 12;000 lb. of fuel. ZZZ4 appeared to have storm cells at the field but ZZZ2 and ZZZ3 were clear. I expressed that we were hand-flying with degraded flight controls and needed a clear airport. Dispatch gave us a minimum fuel of 8.0 to go to ZZZ4 if we could not get into ZZZ. Ultimately; ZZZ updated us that winds were shifting straight out of the north; and they could turn the airport around for us. The non-normal section of the [manual] confirmed we had enough runway landing distance. The final for XXR was clear of storms; and we were able to shoot the ILS and land.So many factors were at play here. At the time we entered ZZZ airspace we had been on duty for 10 hours. We were navigating around severe weather in a tropical storm with the old-style radar. It was working well; but overall; it just doesn't provide the clarity that the new radar presents. We were in continuous turbulence of varying intensities; occasionally severe; before our first approach. Our picture of what the weather was doing was not as clear as the situational awareness that can be achieved in cruise flight. We were relying on ATC vectors through areas that other aircraft had just previously flown. Reports from other aircraft indicated acceptable conditions; or conditions that we could plan for by following severe weather avoidance guidance. The predictive windshear system did its job the first time and we had a clean go-around. Other aircraft landed after our missed approach and conditions were ok. Startle factor played a huge role on the second attempt. Windshear plus multiple ECAMs and autopilot failure created a complex and dynamic situation. We were getting bounced all over the place. We were struggling to get the airplane under control. The monitoring challenge was huge because of the turbulence and the degraded flight controls. I was making FCU inputs in between running checklist items. I had sent an ACARS to Dispatch asking where we should go and listing our system failures; and got no response. The crew phone app worked well enough; but I was frustrated that the feedback we got was that it would be preferred that we hold for ZZZ or go to our listed alternate. I'm sure the Dispatcher could hear my frustration. I am glad that we were able to safely continue to ZZZ; but I wanted to get the plane on the ground without dealing with any more threats to the flight. Holding between thunderstorms with no autopilot in alternate law is an unnecessary risk. I'm not sure why the ground stop for ZZZ was lifted. After the flight; we learned that there had been 7 confirmed tornadoes in the area right around ZZZ. These storms were moving very quickly. It looked like it was possible that ZZZ would clear up before we got there; but there was a good chance it would not. Our decision to go was based on having a lot of fuel; and two alternates. Our second alternate was ZZZ3; which remained clear. This meant that if at any point the path ahead looked bad; we could turn around and have an escape plan. The weather moving in from the south across the final ended up being the worst that we would encounter. If we had delayed this flight by another hour; I think we would have been better off.
column123: On first approach; got a windshear warning and went around. On second approach with moderate to almost severe turbulence; we hit a heavy rain shower. From what I remember we were configured for landing when we hit turbulence. Right then from what I can remember; the autopilot kicked off with the nose pitching up and the speed tape coming up. I pushed the power to climb and tried pushing the nose down. At first; nothing was happening. We did get a low-energy warning too. The plane did recover and started to respond to control inputs. On the ECAM we had F/CTRL ALTN LAW [and] ELAC 1&2 PITCH FAULT. I was flying; the FO (First Officer) did the ECAM and follow-ups and communication. No resets in the air so we looked at going somewhere I could shoot a visual approach. ZZZ Approach had us flying a box pattern until we figured out what we wanted to do. ZZZ Approach then gave us the option to land on XXR and visibility was adequate for a visual. We made an uneventful landing in normal law and gusty crosswinds. I'm guessing we hit the rain at the wrong time.ATC did a good job vectoring us between. The aircraft in front of us just had heavy rain and a smooth ride. Cells were building very fast. I'm thinking that one of the probes froze up momentarily; giving us the speed tape fluctuations. One thing that bothered me was that we couldn't get a hold of Dispatch right away; even after [communicating our situation] over ACARS. The FO did a great job with all the ECAM and checklist procedures.

column121: We got a low altitude alert on arrival to Runway XX in ZZZ. We had to hold due to thunderstorms in the area. That caused a runway change close to airport. We also were put behind an emergency aircraft. I got high and a little fast on extended approach. As we got closer glide slope was below our path. Being new to the airplane I was getting busy. And when I felt we were high I turned off autopilot to descend to the glide slope. We captured glide slope but I descended one dot thru it before correcting. That's when we received the low altitude alert.
column123: This was the last leg of a six day pattern. Fatigue was not the primary cause; but certainly a contributing factor. This was an OE flight with the FO (First Officer) being the PF (Pilot Flying) and trainee. The PM (Pilot Monitoring) was a Captain LCA (Line Check Airman) and two other FO's were onboard. The flight was being vectored around weather for a visual approach to Runway XX. The autopilot was engaged; we were above the glideslope; and approximately 5 miles from ZZZZZ our altitude was above 8;000 feet. I mentioned that we seemed high; and soon after the gear and speedbrakes were extended. I also spoke of the pitfalls of getting ALT before G/S capture and that using vertical speed instead of FLCH was the correct way to solve that problem. We did get ALT and the FO disconnected the autopilot which significantly increased the work load. I switched to Tower; and shortly after we went from above glideslope to approximately 1 dot below. Speedbrakes were extended; flaps were going to 25 and then 30. Tower issued a low altitude alert; and the FO responded back to glidepath. We never heard a GPWS glideslope warning. After securing the airplane at the gate; all four of us thoroughly debriefed the event. We emphasized automation management and specifically the result of turning off the autopilot which significantly increased everyone's workload. Another emphasis was situational awareness and how the 787 does not slow and descend as quickly as the 777 the FO had previously flown. We also talked about monitor crosscheck and the need to speak up earlier to address an approach that is becoming high and fast. Finally; I talked about leadership effectiveness and my lack of being more assertive instead of allowing the student to continue into that situation. Additionally; during the debrief for my own knowledge and clarity; I asked the other pilots if the speedbrakes had been retracted by 1;000 feet; the autothrottles had remained engaged; the glideslope was captured and if we had met the stabilized approach criteria. The others agreed that was the case. Finally; perhaps the training center could include a training scenario where the glideslope needs to be captured after the FAF with ALT engaged.
column125: Air Carrier Crew reported an Low Altitude Alert issued by ATC as the crew was maneuvering around weather for a visual approach. The First Officer (PF); was new to the aircraft and corrected the flight path after the alert.

column121: Initially set for ZZZZZ ONE arrival to ZZZ. Runway XX active so they had to re-clear us on the ZZZZZ1 TWO. Center asked us to go direct to ZZZZZ2 and keep 290 or greater as we were out front of other traffic. Approach told us to go direct ZZZZZ2; cross ZZZZZ2 at 11;000 feet and cleared for RNAV GPS X XXR. The clearance of DIRECT ZZZZZ2 put us 90 degrees off the outbound course from ZZZZZ2. ZZZZZ2 is a fly-over fix. We arm the approach and cross over the top of ZZZZZ2 at 11;000 feet and the plane turns north and begins decent to next altitude. Approach calls us and says they have a low altitude alert on use as we were now below the minimum vector altitude for that area.  She asks if we missed the turn and I said 'No'; that the aircraft has a turn radius as we are turning to the north. She said minimum vector altitude was 10;900 feet and we were at 10;300 feet. We stopped our descent and the aircraft dropped out of the approach mode. Approach asked if we had the terrain insight; luckily for us it was clear with 10 mile visibility and we could see cultural lighting on the hills and knew terrain was not an immediate factor. None of the Enhanced Ground Proximity Warning System annunciated and we were always in either the black or hashed green on the ND. Back on the inbound course; we selected a lower altitude and armed the approach. Nothing further.Center gave us a speed and we didn't get relief until we asked Approach. This higher speed resulted in a larger turn radius than had we been at the mandatory 210 knots restriction at ZZZZZ2. They also gave us direct to a Fly-Over fix with a 90 degree intercept; which set us up to fly through our final approach course; and asked us to cross ZZZZZ2 at 11;000 feet instead of the mandatory +13;000 feet restriction.   This was all done to keep the other aircraft from getting a vector or slowing down. Don't give direct to a fly-over fix with 90 degrees of turn required at the fix; especially at night; at lower than published altitude; and in mountainous terrain.
